<ide_opened_file>The user opened the file [repo]/Makefile in the IDE. This may or may not be related to the current task.</ide_opened_file>

Hi Claude! In this repository, we'll be attempting to design and implement a parallel hash table that can run efficiently on an Nvidia GPU. 

We want:
- A header-only CUDA library
- The ability to insert many elements in parallel
- In theory, utilize 100% of device memory bandwidth

Let's start by brainstorming a possible design into a Markdown file in the notes directory.

## Assistant — 2026-05-13T17:13:29.574Z

**→ Tool call: `Write`** (id `tool-6`)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "file_path": "[repo]/notes/design.md",
  "content": "# GPU Robin Hood Hash Table — Design Brainstorm\n\n## Goals\n\n- **Header-only CUDA library.** A single `.cuh` (or a small set) that users `#include` and\n  template-instantiate for their key/value types. No separate compilation of the table itself.\n- **Massively parallel insertion.** A batch of `N` keys is inserted by `N` (or more) GPU threads\n  cooperatively. Worst-case behaviour must remain bounded even under heavy contention.\n- **Memory-bandwidth bound.** On large tables, the table is much bigger than the L2 cache, so\n  performance is determined by how many useful bytes each DRAM transaction delivers. The aim is\n  to come as close to peak HBM bandwidth as the access pattern allows.\n\n## Why Robin Hood?\n\nOpen-addressing with the invariant *\"the entry in slot `i` whose probe distance is smaller gets\nevicted by an entry whose probe distance is larger\"* gives:\n\n- A tight, low-variance distribution of probe distances → small worst-case lookup length even at\n  load factors of 0.85–0.9.\n- A natural early-exit rule for lookups (stop as soon as the slot's probe distance is smaller\n  than the probe distance of the key we're searching for).\n- Backward-shift deletion that avoids tombstones.\n\nThese properties matter even more on a GPU than on a CPU because the warp's tail latency is set\nby the longest probe sequence among its 32 lanes.\n\n## Slot layout\n\nA slot is the unit of an atomic update. The simplest design that admits lock-free CAS on a single\nmachine word is:\n\n```\nstruct Slot {                       // 8 bytes total — fits in atomicCAS<uint64_t>\n    uint32_t key;\n    uint32_t value;                 // or a 32-bit payload / pointer index\n};\n```\n\nEmpty slots use a reserved sentinel key (e.g. `0xFFFFFFFF`); users are forbidden from inserting\nthat key. For 64-bit keys or larger payloads we have two options:\n\n1. **128-bit slots** with `__int128` CAS via `atomicCAS` on `unsigned long long` pairs — supported\n   on sm_70+ but with reduced throughput.\n2. **Separate parallel arrays** (`keys[]` and `values[]`) with the key array being the source of\n   truth for CAS, and values being written only by the thread that successfully claimed the slot.\n   This halves the bytes-per-CAS but requires care: a finder must re-read the key after reading\n   the value to make sure the slot wasn't reused mid-read.\n\nWe will start with the 64-bit packed slot and parameterise later.\n\n**Probe distance.** We do *not* store probe distance in the slot. It is derivable as\n`(slot_index - hash(key)) mod capacity`. Storing it would cost bits we'd rather give to the\npayload, and the derivation is a single subtract once we've read the slot.\n\n## Capacity, hash function, load factor\n\n- Capacity is a power of two so `mod` becomes a mask. Users specify a target load factor\n  (default 0.7); we round capacity up to `next_pow2(N / load_factor)`.\n- Hash function is a template parameter; default is a finalizer-style mixer (e.g. the\n  splitmix64/Murmur3 finalizer) that is cheap on GPU and decorrelates structured keys well.\n- Robin Hood tolerates higher load than linear probing, but on a GPU the warp tail-latency\n  argument pushes us back toward 0.7–0.8 rather than 0.9.\n\n## Parallel insertion algorithm\n\nEach thread owns one `(key, value)` pair. The thread walks the probe sequence and, at every slot,\nperforms one of three actions:\n\n1. **Empty slot, CAS succeeds.** Done.\n2. **Same key, CAS succeeds.** This is an update; done.\n3. **Occupied by another key.** Compare probe distances. If the resident is \"richer\" (its probe\n   distance from *its* home is smaller than ours from *our* home), we attempt to CAS our pair\n   into the slot. On success, we adopt the displaced pair and continue probing from this slot.\n   If the resident is \"poorer\" or equal, we move on.\n\nA CAS failure is treated as \"the world changed under us\": we re-read the slot and re-evaluate\nwithout advancing the probe index. This is the standard lock-free retry.\n\n### Why this is correct under contention\n\nThe Robin Hood invariant is maintained by every individual CAS because each CAS only swaps in a\npair with probe distance strictly greater than the one already in the slot (or the slot was\nempty). Two concurrent inserts targeting the same slot serialise through CAS, and whichever\nloses retries with the freshest state. There is no global ordering requirement: as long as every\nwrite respects the local invariant, the final table is a valid Robin Hood layout for *some*\npermutation of the input.\n\nCaveat: the *value* portion of an update (case 2) needs care. If two threads insert the same key\nconcurrently and the user wants \"last write wins\" semantics, we need an additional CAS loop on\nthe value, or we tolerate non-deterministic resolution.\n\n## Achieving bandwidth saturation\n\nThe 64-bit slot is small relative to a 128-byte L2 cache line: one transaction brings 16 slots.\nThe bandwidth strategy follows from this:\n\n- **Warp-cooperative probing.** Within a warp, threads whose home slots land in the same cache\n  line should service their probes from one load, not 32 loads. We have the warp ballot/shuffle\n  primitives to do this cheaply.\n- **Bucketed probing.** Treat the table as an array of *buckets* of 16 slots (one cache line).\n  A probe inspects an entire bucket at a time — one coalesced 128-byte load — and uses warp\n  intrinsics (`__ballot_sync`, `__match_any_sync`) to find the candidate slot. Insertion still\n  uses CAS on a single 8-byte slot inside the bucket, but the read traffic per probe is exactly\n  one cache line. This is the same idea as `bucketed_cuckoo` and Bucketized Cuckoo Hashing.\n- **Probe length capping.** Cap probes at, say, 32 buckets. If a thread overruns the cap during\n  insertion, mark the batch as failed and rehash. With Robin Hood at 0.7 load this happens with\n  vanishing probability for reasonable `N`.\n- **Avoid divergence.** All threads in a warp execute the same probe loop. The \"I'm done, others\n  aren't\" case is handled with masked operations rather than early-return-then-divergence.\n\nIf we get this right, an insert of `N` keys at load factor 0.7 should cost approximately\n`(1/(1-α)) * sizeof(slot)` bytes per insert in expectation, which is the information-theoretic\nfloor for an open-addressed table with this layout.\n\n## Lookup and deletion\n\n- **Lookup.** Same probing rule, no CAS. Stop as soon as we find the key, an empty slot, or a\n  resident whose probe distance is smaller than ours. Lookup is fully read-only and trivially\n  parallel — no synchronisation required.\n- **Deletion.** Backward-shift: starting from the deleted slot, while the next slot is non-empty\n  and its probe distance is positive, shift it back by one. On a concurrent GPU this is the\n  hardest operation to make correct without locks. **Initial scope: defer deletion**, support\n  insert + lookup only, and add it once we have benchmarks in place.\n\n## API sketch\n\n```cpp\nnamespace gprh {\n\ntemplate <class Key,\n          class Value,\n          class Hash = default_hash<Key>,\n          Key   EmptyKey = Key{} - Key{1}>            // sentinel\nclass HashTable {\npublic:\n    // Host-side construction allocates device memory.\n    explicit HashTable(std::size_t capacity);\n\n    // Bulk operations. Stream-aware, non-owning input buffers.\n    void insert(const Key* keys, const Value* values,\n                std::size_t n, cudaStream_t stream = 0);\n\n    void find(const Key* keys, Value* values_out,\n              std::size_t n, cudaStream_t stream = 0) const;\n\n    // A device-side, copyable handle for use inside user kernels.\n    struct View {\n        __device__ bool  insert(Key k, Value v);\n        __device__ bool  find  (Key k, Value& out) const;\n    };\n    View view();\n\nprivate:\n    Slot*       slots_;\n    std::size_t capacity_;\n};\n\n} // namespace gprh\n```\n\nThe `View` is the centrepiece for header-only use: a user kernel that already has its keys in\nregisters can call `view.insert(...)` directly without a separate kernel launch.\n\n## Open questions / things to settle before coding\n\n1. **Slot width.** Start with 64-bit packed `(uint32 key, uint32 value)`, or go straight to a\n   128-bit slot for 64-bit keys? The 64-bit case is the canonical micro-benchmark and is what\n   most published GPU hash tables use.\n2. **Bucket size.** 16 (one 128-byte cache line of 8-byte slots) is the obvious choice. Worth\n   measuring 8 and 32 as well.\n3. **Failure semantics on full table.** Return a count of failed inserts, or `assert`? A\n   bulk-insert API can naturally return the number of survivors and let the caller resize.\n4. **Resize.** Out of scope for v1. The table is allocated at a fixed capacity. Users that need\n   growth allocate a bigger one and re-insert.\n5. **Multi-GPU.** Out of scope for v1.\n6. **Testing.** A small CPU reference implementation (sequential Robin Hood) plus a randomised\n   differential test against the GPU implementation, plus stress tests at 0.5/0.7/0.9 load.\n\n## Suggested next steps\n\n1. Sketch the `Slot`, hash, and probe primitives in a single `.cuh`.\n2. Implement the insert kernel without bucketing, just to validate correctness and the CAS loop.\n3. Add the bucketed (cache-line-aligned) variant and benchmark against the naive one.\n4. Add `find`, then benchmark against `cuCollections` / `WarpCore` as external baselines.\n"
}
```

</details>

Looks reasonable. I agree with:

2. The decision to use bucketed warp-cooperative probing, for exactly the cache line reasoning you say.

Given these, the table's structure should be parameterized by a set of constants, including GPU cache line size, warp size, and related values. We'll want to add a note on what these should be.

But to address that, we first need to make the algorithm slightly more concrete. How should the work necessary to insert elements be split up among the GPU - to what unit (warps, threads, blocks, etc) should elements be allocated?

## Assistant — 2026-05-13T18:13:06.350Z

Good question — this decision constrains everything downstream, so let me lay out the alternatives explicitly before recommending one.

## The four candidate mappings

| | **Thread / key** | **Sub-warp tile / key** | **Warp / key** | **Block / key** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Threads cooperating on one key | 1 | B (= bucket width) | 32 | 128–1024 |
| Probe = one coalesced load? | **No** — 32 unrelated lines | **Yes** — exactly one cache line | Yes, but lanes wasted if B<32 | Yes, but lanes wasted |
| Keys in flight per warp | 32 | 32/B | 1 | <1 |
| Wasted lanes per probe | 0 | 0 (if B divides 32) | 32−B | huge |

- **Thread-per-key** is the obvious-looking choice and is what naive GPU hash tables do. It throws away our entire bandwidth strategy: 32 threads in a warp probe 32 unrelated buckets, generating 32 cache-line loads per probe step instead of one. Coalescing happens only when home slots accidentally collide. Fatal for our stated goal.
- **Block-per-key** is too coarse — there's no useful work for 128+ threads on a single key.
- **Warp-per-key** is the canonical "warp-cooperative hashing" design, but it wastes lanes whenever the bucket holds fewer than 32 slots. With 8-byte slots and a 128-byte line, the bucket is 16 slots, so half the warp idles.
- **Sub-warp tile per key**, where the tile width equals the bucket width B, is the design that exactly matches one tile-probe to one cache-line transaction with no wasted lanes. It's what NVIDIA's `cuCollections` and Awad et al.'s "WarpCore" / "SlabHash" converge on.

## Recommendation: cooperative-group tile of width B = bucket size

Concretely:

1. A **bucket** is a contiguous run of B slots, cache-line aligned. For our default 8-byte slot and 128-byte line, B = 16.
2. A **cooperative tile** of B threads (`cg::thread_block_tile<B>`) owns one in-flight key at a time. Each tile thread holds one slot of the current bucket in a register, so one bucket probe = one coalesced 128-byte load.
3. **Tiles per warp = 32 / B**. For B = 16 that's 2 keys per warp in flight, which is healthy parallelism per SM.
4. **Block** = some multiple of the warp (e.g. 128 or 256 threads = 8 or 16 tiles for B = 16). Chosen for occupancy, not algorithmic reasons.
5. **Work assignment**: tile-strided loop. Tile index `t` (across the whole grid) processes input keys `t, t+T, t+2T, …` where T is the total number of resident tiles. Naturally load-balanced and survives skew.

## What the tile does on each probe

1. The B lanes cooperatively load one bucket (each lane reads its own slot, fully coalesced).
2. `tile.ballot()` over a predicate identifies empty slots / matching keys / Robin-Hood-displaceable slots.
3. The lane corresponding to the chosen slot performs the CAS; the result is broadcast to the rest of the tile with `tile.shfl()`.
4. On displacement, the displaced `(key, value)` becomes the tile's new in-flight pair and probing advances by one bucket.

Every step of this is a warp-intrinsic operation on a single cache line, which is what makes the bandwidth argument work.

## Falls naturally out of this

The parameters you mentioned now have specific meanings, not free knobs:

- **`SlotBytes`** — chosen by key/value packing (8 by default).
- **`CacheLineBytes`** — 128 on every NVIDIA arch we care about.
- **`BucketSize` = `CacheLineBytes / SlotBytes`** — derived, not chosen. 16 for us.
- **`WarpSize`** — 32 (constant per arch).
- **`TileSize` = `BucketSize`** — derived.
- **`TilesPerWarp` = `WarpSize / BucketSize`** — derived.

The only genuinely free parameter is `BlockSize` (occupancy tuning) and the *target load factor* (Robin Hood quality tuning). Everything else is forced by the architecture.

---

Shall I append a "Work decomposition" section and a "Parameters" section to [notes/design.md](notes/design.md) capturing this, and then move on to fleshing out the per-tile probe state machine?

{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/notes/design.md",
  "old_string": "## Lookup and deletion",
  "new_string": "## Work decomposition: how insertions map onto the GPU hierarchy\n\nBefore discussing parameters, we need to commit to a unit of work — what GPU resource cooperates\non a single key insertion. There are four candidates:\n\n| Mapping              | Threads / key | Probe is one coalesced load? | Keys in flight / warp | Wasted lanes / probe |\n| -------------------- | ------------- | ---------------------------- | --------------------- | -------------------- |\n| Thread per key       | 1             | No — 32 unrelated lines      | 32                    | 0                    |\n| **Sub-warp tile**    | **B**         | **Yes — exactly one line**   | **32 / B**            | **0 (if B ∣ 32)**    |\n| Warp per key         | 32            | Yes, but ≤ B lanes used      | 1                     | 32 − B               |\n| Block per key        | 128–1024      | Yes, but lanes mostly idle   | ≪ 1                   | huge                 |\n\n- **Thread-per-key** is the naive choice and exactly the case our bandwidth strategy is designed\n  to avoid. Each warp issues 32 cache-line loads per probe step instead of one, and we lose any\n  coalescing benefit. Fatal for our stated goal.\n- **Block-per-key** is too coarse: there is no useful work for 128+ threads on a single key.\n- **Warp-per-key** is the classic \"warp-cooperative hashing\" design but wastes lanes whenever\n  the bucket is narrower than the warp. With 8-byte slots and a 128-byte line on NVIDIA, the\n  bucket holds 16 slots, so half of every warp sits idle.\n- **Sub-warp tile per key**, with tile width equal to the bucket width, is the one design where\n  *one tile-probe = one cache-line transaction* with no idle lanes. This is the design we adopt.\n\n### The structure that follows\n\n1. A **bucket** is a contiguous, cache-line-aligned run of `B` slots.\n2. A **cooperative tile** of `B` threads (`cooperative_groups::thread_block_tile<B>`) owns one\n   in-flight key at a time. Each tile lane holds one slot of the current bucket in a register;\n   one bucket probe is one coalesced load.\n3. **Tiles per warp** = `WarpSize / B`. For NVIDIA with 8-byte slots that is 2.\n4. **Block size** is purely an occupancy knob — some multiple of `WarpSize`, tuned empirically.\n5. **Work assignment** is a tile-strided loop: tile index `t` (across the whole grid) handles\n   input keys `t, t+T, t+2T, …` where `T` is the total number of resident tiles. Naturally\n   load-balanced and robust to input skew.\n\n### Per-tile probe step\n\nOn each probe:\n\n1. The `B` lanes cooperatively load one bucket — each lane reads one slot, fully coalesced.\n2. `tile.ballot(...)` over a predicate identifies empty slots, key matches, or\n   Robin-Hood-displaceable slots in a single warp intrinsic.\n3. The lane corresponding to the chosen slot performs the CAS; the outcome is broadcast to the\n   rest of the tile with `tile.shfl(...)`.\n4. On displacement, the evicted `(key, value)` becomes the tile's new in-flight pair and the\n   tile advances by one bucket.\n\nEvery step is a warp-intrinsic operation on a single cache line, which is what makes the\nbandwidth argument hold.\n\n### Parameters: what is forced by the architecture, what is free\n\nOnce we commit to the sub-warp tile design, almost every constant is *derived* from the\nhardware, not chosen:\n\n| Parameter         | Value (default)              | Source                                   |\n| ----------------- | ---------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |\n| `SlotBytes`       | 8                            | Key/value packing choice                 |\n| `CacheLineBytes`  | 128 (NVIDIA)                 | Hardware                                 |\n| `BucketSize`      | `CacheLineBytes / SlotBytes` | **Derived**                              |\n| `WarpSize`        | 32 (NVIDIA)                  | Hardware                                 |\n| `TileSize`        | `BucketSize`                 | **Derived** (the central design choice)  |\n| `TilesPerWarp`    | `WarpSize / BucketSize`      | **Derived**                              |\n| `BlockSize`       | tuned (e.g. 128 or 256)      | Occupancy — empirical                    |\n| `TargetLoadFactor`| tuned (e.g. 0.7)             | Robin Hood quality — empirical           |\n\nSo the only genuinely free knobs are `BlockSize` and `TargetLoadFactor`. The rest should be\nexpressed in the code as compile-time constants derived from `SlotBytes`, `CacheLineBytes`, and\n`WarpSize`, with the latter two being template parameters of the table (with sensible defaults)\nrather than hard-coded.\n\n### Why the abstraction matters — an AMD aside (out of scope for v1)\n\nAMD GPUs (CDNA, RDNA) have a 64-byte cache line and a 64-thread wavefront. With 8-byte slots:\n\n- NVIDIA: `BucketSize` = 128 / 8 = 16, `TilesPerWarp` = 32 / 16 = **2**.\n- AMD:    `BucketSize` =  64 / 8 =  8, `TilesPerWarp` = 64 /  8 = **8**.\n\nThe same algorithm runs unchanged; only the derived constants differ. We are not targeting AMD,\nbut it is the reason we want `CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` to be parameters of the table type\nrather than hard-coded `128` and `32`. Anything that ends up baked in as a literal in the\nimplementation is a portability bug waiting to happen.\n\n## Lookup and deletion"
}

Worth\n   measuring 8 and 32 as well.\n3. **Failure semantics on full table.** Return a count of failed inserts, or `assert`? A\n   bulk-insert API can naturally return the number of survivors and let the caller resize.\n4. **Resize.** Out of scope for v1. The table is allocated at a fixed capacity. Users that need\n   growth allocate a bigger one and re-insert.\n5. **Multi-GPU.** Out of scope for v1.\n6. **Testing.** A small CPU reference implementation (sequential Robin Hood) plus a randomised\n   differential test against the GPU implementation, plus stress tests at 0.5/0.7/0.9 load.",
  "new_string": "1. **Slot width.** Start with 64-bit packed `(uint32 key, uint32 value)`, or go straight to a\n   128-bit slot for 64-bit keys? The 64-bit case is the canonical micro-benchmark and is what\n   most published GPU hash tables use. Note that changing this also changes the derived\n   `BucketSize`.\n2. **Failure semantics on full table.** Return a count of failed inserts, or `assert`? A\n   bulk-insert API can naturally return the number of survivors and let the caller resize.\n3. **Resize.** Out of scope for v1. The table is allocated at a fixed capacity. Users that need\n   growth allocate a bigger one and re-insert.\n4. **Multi-GPU.** Out of scope for v1.\n5. **Portability across vendors.** Out of scope for v1 but informs the abstractions — see the\n   AMD aside above. `CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` should be table-level parameters, not magic\n   numbers in the implementation.\n6. **Testing.** A small CPU reference implementation (sequential Robin Hood) plus a randomised\n   differential test against the GPU implementation, plus stress tests at 0.5/0.7/0.9 load."
}
```

</details>

## User — 2026-05-13T18:28:26.651Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-15`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
1	# GPU Robin Hood Hash Table — Design Brainstorm
2	
3	## Goals
4	
5	- **Header-only CUDA library.** A single `.cuh` (or a small set) that users `#include` and
6	  template-instantiate for their key/value types. No separate compilation of the table itself.
7	- **Massively parallel insertion.** A batch of `N` keys is inserted by `N` (or more) GPU threads
8	  cooperatively. Worst-case behaviour must remain bounded even under heavy contention.
9	- **Memory-bandwidth bound.** On large tables, the table is much bigger than the L2 cache, so
10	  performance is determined by how many useful bytes each DRAM transaction delivers. The aim is
11	  to come as close to peak HBM bandwidth as the access pattern allows.
12	
13	## Why Robin Hood?
14	
15	Open-addressing with the invariant *"the entry in slot `i` whose probe distance is smaller gets
16	evicted by an entry whose probe distance is larger"* gives:
17	
18	- A tight, low-variance distribution of probe distances → small worst-case lookup length even at
19	  load factors of 0.85–0.9.
20	- A natural early-exit rule for lookups (stop as soon as the slot's probe distance is smaller
21	  than the probe distance of the key we're searching for).
22	- Backward-shift deletion that avoids tombstones.
23	
24	These properties matter even more on a GPU than on a CPU because the warp's tail latency is set
25	by the longest probe sequence among its 32 lanes.
26	
27	## Slot layout
28	
29	A slot is the unit of an atomic update. The simplest design that admits lock-free CAS on a single
30	machine word is:
31	
32	```
33	struct Slot {                       // 8 bytes total — fits in atomicCAS<uint64_t>
34	    uint32_t key;
35	    uint32_t value;                 // or a 32-bit payload / pointer index
36	};
37	```
38	
39	Empty slots use a reserved sentinel key (e.g. `0xFFFFFFFF`); users are forbidden from inserting
40	that key. For 64-bit keys or larger payloads we have two options:
41	
42	1. **128-bit slots** with `__int128` CAS via `atomicCAS` on `unsigned long long` pairs — supported
43	   on sm_70+ but with reduced throughput.
44	2. **Separate parallel arrays** (`keys[]` and `values[]`) with the key array being the source of
45	   truth for CAS, and values being written only by the thread that successfully claimed the slot.
46	   This halves the bytes-per-CAS but requires care: a finder must re-read the key after reading
47	   the value to make sure the slot wasn't reused mid-read.
48	
49	We will start with the 64-bit packed slot and parameterise later.
50	
51	**Probe distance.** We do *not* store probe distance in the slot. It is derivable as
52	`(slot_index - hash(key)) mod capacity`. Storing it would cost bits we'd rather give to the
53	payload, and the derivation is a single subtract once we've read the slot.
54	
55	## Capacity, hash function, load factor
56	
57	- Capacity is a power of two so `mod` becomes a mask. Users specify a target load factor
58	  (default 0.7); we round capacity up to `next_pow2(N / load_factor)`.
59	- Hash function is a template parameter; default is a finalizer-style mixer (e.g. the
60	  splitmix64/Murmur3 finalizer) that is cheap on GPU and decorrelates structured keys well.
61	- Robin Hood tolerates higher load than linear probing, but on a GPU the warp tail-latency
62	  argument pushes us back toward 0.7–0.8 rather than 0.9.
63	
64	## Parallel insertion algorithm
65	
66	Each thread owns one `(key, value)` pair. The thread walks the probe sequence and, at every slot,
67	performs one of three actions:
68	
69	1. **Empty slot, CAS succeeds.** Done.
70	2. **Same key, CAS succeeds.** This is an update; done.
71	3. **Occupied by another key.** Compare probe distances. If the resident is "richer" (its probe
72	   distance from *its* home is smaller than ours from *our* home), we attempt to CAS our pair
73	   into the slot. On success, we adopt the displaced pair and continue probing from this slot.
74	   If the resident is "poorer" or equal, we move on.
75	
76	A CAS failure is treated as "the world changed under us": we re-read the slot and re-evaluate
77	without advancing the probe index. This is the standard lock-free retry.
78	
79	### Why this is correct under contention
80	
81	The Robin Hood invariant is maintained by every individual CAS because each CAS only swaps in a
82	pair with probe distance strictly greater than the one already in the slot (or the slot was
83	empty). Two concurrent inserts targeting the same slot serialise through CAS, and whichever
84	loses retries with the freshest state. There is no global ordering requirement: as long as every
85	write respects the local invariant, the final table is a valid Robin Hood layout for *some*
86	permutation of the input.
87	
88	Caveat: the *value* portion of an update (case 2) needs care. If two threads insert the same key
89	concurrently and the user wants "last write wins" semantics, we need an additional CAS loop on
90	the value, or we tolerate non-deterministic resolution.
91	
92	## Achieving bandwidth saturation
93	
94	The 64-bit slot is small relative to a 128-byte L2 cache line: one transaction brings 16 slots.
95	The bandwidth strategy follows from this:
96	
97	- **Warp-cooperative probing.** Within a warp, threads whose home slots land in the same cache
98	  line should service their probes from one load, not 32 loads. We have the warp ballot/shuffle
99	  primitives to do this cheaply.
100	- **Bucketed probing.** Treat the table as an array of *buckets* of 16 slots (one cache line).
101	  A probe inspects an entire bucket at a time — one coalesced 128-byte load — and uses warp
102	  intrinsics (`__ballot_sync`, `__match_any_sync`) to find the candidate slot. Insertion still
103	  uses CAS on a single 8-byte slot inside the bucket, but the read traffic per probe is exactly
104	  one cache line. This is the same idea as `bucketed_cuckoo` and Bucketized Cuckoo Hashing.
105	- **Probe length capping.** Cap probes at, say, 32 buckets. If a thread overruns the cap during
106	  insertion, mark the batch as failed and rehash. With Robin Hood at 0.7 load this happens with
107	  vanishing probability for reasonable `N`.
108	- **Avoid divergence.** All threads in a warp execute the same probe loop. The "I'm done, others
109	  aren't" case is handled with masked operations rather than early-return-then-divergence.
110	
111	If we get this right, an insert of `N` keys at load factor 0.7 should cost approximately
112	`(1/(1-α)) * sizeof(slot)` bytes per insert in expectation, which is the information-theoretic
113	floor for an open-addressed table with this layout.
114	
115	## Work decomposition: how insertions map onto the GPU hierarchy
116	
117	Before discussing parameters, we need to commit to a unit of work — what GPU resource cooperates
118	on a single key insertion. There are four candidates:
119	
120	| Mapping              | Threads / key | Probe is one coalesced load? | Keys in flight / warp | Wasted lanes / probe |
121	| -------------------- | ------------- | ---------------------------- | --------------------- | -------------------- |
122	| Thread per key       | 1             | No — 32 unrelated lines      | 32                    | 0                    |
123	| **Sub-warp tile**    | **B**         | **Yes — exactly one line**   | **32 / B**            | **0 (if B ∣ 32)**    |
124	| Warp per key         | 32            | Yes, but ≤ B lanes used      | 1                     | 32 − B               |
125	| Block per key        | 128–1024      | Yes, but lanes mostly idle   | ≪ 1                   | huge                 |
126	
127	- **Thread-per-key** is the naive choice and exactly the case our bandwidth strategy is designed
128	  to avoid. Each warp issues 32 cache-line loads per probe step instead of one, and we lose any
129	  coalescing benefit. Fatal for our stated goal.
130	- **Block-per-key** is too coarse: there is no useful work for 128+ threads on a single key.
131	- **Warp-per-key** is the classic "warp-cooperative hashing" design but wastes lanes whenever
132	  the bucket is narrower than the warp. With 8-byte slots and a 128-byte line on NVIDIA, the
133	  bucket holds 16 slots, so half of every warp sits idle.
134	- **Sub-warp tile per key**, with tile width equal to the bucket width, is the one design where
135	  *one tile-probe = one cache-line transaction* with no idle lanes. This is the design we adopt.
136	
137	### The structure that follows
138	
139	1. A **bucket** is a contiguous, cache-line-aligned run of `B` slots.
140	2. A **cooperative tile** of `B` threads (`cooperative_groups::thread_block_tile<B>`) owns one
141	   in-flight key at a time. Each tile lane holds one slot of the current bucket in a register;
142	   one bucket probe is one coalesced load.
143	3. **Tiles per warp** = `WarpSize / B`. For NVIDIA with 8-byte slots that is 2.
144	4. **Block size** is purely an occupancy knob — some multiple of `WarpSize`, tuned empirically.
145	5. **Work assignment** is a tile-strided loop: tile index `t` (across the whole grid) handles
146	   input keys `t, t+T, t+2T, …` where `T` is the total number of resident tiles. Naturally
147	   load-balanced and robust to input skew.
148	
149	### Per-tile probe step
150	
151	On each probe:
152	
153	1. The `B` lanes cooperatively load one bucket — each lane reads one slot, fully coalesced.
154	2. `tile.ballot(...)` over a predicate identifies empty slots, key matches, or
155	   Robin-Hood-displaceable slots in a single warp intrinsic.
156	3. The lane corresponding to the chosen slot performs the CAS; the outcome is broadcast to the
157	   rest of the tile with `tile.shfl(...)`.
158	4. On displacement, the evicted `(key, value)` becomes the tile's new in-flight pair and the
159	   tile advances by one bucket.
160	
161	Every step is a warp-intrinsic operation on a single cache line, which is what makes the
162	bandwidth argument hold.
163	
164	### Parameters: what is forced by the architecture, what is free
165	
166	Once we commit to the sub-warp tile design, almost every constant is *derived* from the
167	hardware, not chosen:
168	
169	| Parameter         | Value (default)              | Source                                   |
170	| ----------------- | ---------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
171	| `SlotBytes`       | 8                            | Key/value packing choice                 |
172	| `CacheLineBytes`  | 128 (NVIDIA)                 | Hardware                                 |
173	| `BucketSize`      | `CacheLineBytes / SlotBytes` | **Derived**                              |
174	| `WarpSize`        | 32 (NVIDIA)                  | Hardware                                 |
175	| `TileSize`        | `BucketSize`                 | **Derived** (the central design choice)  |
176	| `TilesPerWarp`    | `WarpSize / BucketSize`      | **Derived**                              |
177	| `BlockSize`       | tuned (e.g. 128 or 256)      | Occupancy — empirical                    |
178	| `TargetLoadFactor`| tuned (e.g. 0.7)             | Robin Hood quality — empirical           |
179	
180	So the only genuinely free knobs are `BlockSize` and `TargetLoadFactor`. The rest should be
181	expressed in the code as compile-time constants derived from `SlotBytes`, `CacheLineBytes`, and
182	`WarpSize`, with the latter two being template parameters of the table (with sensible defaults)
183	rather than hard-coded.
184	
185	### Why the abstraction matters — an AMD aside (out of scope for v1)
186	
187	AMD GPUs (CDNA, RDNA) have a 64-byte cache line and a 64-thread wavefront. With 8-byte slots:
188	
189	- NVIDIA: `BucketSize` = 128 / 8 = 16, `TilesPerWarp` = 32 / 16 = **2**.
190	- AMD:    `BucketSize` =  64 / 8 =  8, `TilesPerWarp` = 64 /  8 = **8**.
191	
192	The same algorithm runs unchanged; only the derived constants differ. We are not targeting AMD,
193	but it is the reason we want `CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` to be parameters of the table type
194	rather than hard-coded `128` and `32`. Anything that ends up baked in as a literal in the
195	implementation is a portability bug waiting to happen.
196	
197	## Lookup and deletion
198	
199	- **Lookup.** Same probing rule, no CAS. Stop as soon as we find the key, an empty slot, or a
200	  resident whose probe distance is smaller than ours. Lookup is fully read-only and trivially
201	  parallel — no synchronisation required.
202	- **Deletion.** Backward-shift: starting from the deleted slot, while the next slot is non-empty
203	  and its probe distance is positive, shift it back by one. On a concurrent GPU this is the
204	  hardest operation to make correct without locks. **Initial scope: defer deletion**, support
205	  insert + lookup only, and add it once we have benchmarks in place.
206	
207	## API sketch
208	
209	```cpp
210	namespace gprh {
211	
212	template <class Key,
213	          class Value,
214	          class Hash = default_hash<Key>,
215	          Key   EmptyKey = Key{} - Key{1}>            // sentinel
216	class HashTable {
217	public:
218	    // Host-side construction allocates device memory.
219	    explicit HashTable(std::size_t capacity);
220	
221	    // Bulk operations. Stream-aware, non-owning input buffers.
222	    void insert(const Key* keys, const Value* values,
223	                std::size_t n, cudaStream_t stream = 0);
224	
225	    void find(const Key* keys, Value* values_out,
226	              std::size_t n, cudaStream_t stream = 0) const;
227	
228	    // A device-side, copyable handle for use inside user kernels.
229	    struct View {
230	        __device__ bool  insert(Key k, Value v);
231	        __device__ bool  find  (Key k, Value& out) const;
232	    };
233	    View view();
234	
235	private:
236	    Slot*       slots_;
237	    std::size_t capacity_;
238	};
239	
240	} // namespace gprh
241	```
242	
243	The `View` is the centrepiece for header-only use: a user kernel that already has its keys in
244	registers can call `view.insert(...)` directly without a separate kernel launch.
245	
246	## Open questions / things to settle before coding
247	
248	1. **Slot width.** Start with 64-bit packed `(uint32 key, uint32 value)`, or go straight to a
249	   128-bit slot for 64-bit keys? The 64-bit case is the canonical micro-benchmark and is what
250	   most published GPU hash tables use. Note that changing this also changes the derived
251	   `BucketSize`.
252	2. **Failure semantics on full table.** Return a count of failed inserts, or `assert`? A
253	   bulk-insert API can naturally return the number of survivors and let the caller resize.
254	3. **Resize.** Out of scope for v1. The table is allocated at a fixed capacity. Users that need
255	   growth allocate a bigger one and re-insert.
256	4. **Multi-GPU.** Out of scope for v1.
257	5. **Portability across vendors.** Out of scope for v1 but informs the abstractions — see the
258	   AMD aside above. `CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` should be table-level parameters, not magic
259	   numbers in the implementation.
260	6. **Testing.** A small CPU reference implementation (sequential Robin Hood) plus a randomised
261	   differential test against the GPU implementation, plus stress tests at 0.5/0.7/0.9 load.
262	
263	## Suggested next steps
264	
265	1. Sketch the `Slot`, hash, and probe primitives in a single `.cuh`.
266	2. Implement the insert kernel without bucketing, just to validate correctness and the CAS loop.
267	3. Add the bucketed (cache-line-aligned) variant and benchmark against the naive one.
268	4. Add `find`, then benchmark against `cuCollections` / `WarpCore` as external baselines.
269	
```

</details>

## Assistant — 2026-05-13T18:29:13.140Z

**→ Tool call: `Write`** (id `tool-16`)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "file_path": "[repo]/notes/design.md",
  "content": "# GPU Robin Hood Hash Table — Design Brainstorm\n\n## Goals\n\n- **Header-only CUDA library.**\n  A single `.cuh` (or a small set) that users `#include` and template-instantiate for their key/value types.\n  No separate compilation of the table itself.\n- **Massively parallel insertion.**\n  A batch of `N` keys is inserted by `N` (or more) GPU threads cooperatively.\n  Worst-case behaviour must remain bounded even under heavy contention.\n- **Memory-bandwidth bound.**\n  On large tables, the table is much bigger than the L2 cache, so performance is determined by how many useful bytes each DRAM transaction delivers.\n  The aim is to come as close to peak HBM bandwidth as the access pattern allows.\n\n## Why Robin Hood?\n\nOpen-addressing with the invariant *\"the entry in slot `i` whose probe distance is smaller gets evicted by an entry whose probe distance is larger\"* gives:\n\n- A tight, low-variance distribution of probe distances → small worst-case lookup length even at load factors of 0.85–0.9.\n- A natural early-exit rule for lookups (stop as soon as the slot's probe distance is smaller than the probe distance of the key we're searching for).\n- Backward-shift deletion that avoids tombstones.\n\nThese properties matter even more on a GPU than on a CPU because the warp's tail latency is set by the longest probe sequence among its 32 lanes.\n\n## Slot layout\n\nA slot is the unit of an atomic update.\nThe simplest design that admits lock-free CAS on a single machine word is:\n\n```\nstruct Slot {                       // 8 bytes total — fits in atomicCAS<uint64_t>\n    uint32_t key;\n    uint32_t value;                 // or a 32-bit payload / pointer index\n};\n```\n\nEmpty slots use a reserved sentinel key (e.g. `0xFFFFFFFF`); users are forbidden from inserting that key.\nFor 64-bit keys or larger payloads we have two options:\n\n1. **128-bit slots** with `__int128` CAS via `atomicCAS` on `unsigned long long` pairs — supported on sm_70+ but with reduced throughput.\n2. **Separate parallel arrays** (`keys[]` and `values[]`) with the key array being the source of truth for CAS, and values being written only by the thread that successfully claimed the slot.\n   This halves the bytes-per-CAS but requires care: a finder must re-read the key after reading the value to make sure the slot wasn't reused mid-read.\n\nWe will start with the 64-bit packed slot and parameterise later.\n\n**Probe distance.**\nWe do *not* store probe distance in the slot.\nIt is derivable as `(slot_index - hash(key)) mod capacity`.\nStoring it would cost bits we'd rather give to the payload, and the derivation is a single subtract once we've read the slot.\n\n## Capacity, hash function, load factor\n\n- Capacity is a power of two so `mod` becomes a mask.\n  Users specify a target load factor (default 0.7); we round capacity up to `next_pow2(N / load_factor)`.\n- Hash function is a template parameter; default is a finalizer-style mixer (e.g. the splitmix64/Murmur3 finalizer) that is cheap on GPU and decorrelates structured keys well.\n- Robin Hood tolerates higher load than linear probing, but on a GPU the warp tail-latency argument pushes us back toward 0.7–0.8 rather than 0.9.\n\n## Parallel insertion algorithm\n\nEach thread owns one `(key, value)` pair.\nThe thread walks the probe sequence and, at every slot, performs one of three actions:\n\n1. **Empty slot, CAS succeeds.** Done.\n2. **Same key, CAS succeeds.** This is an update; done.\n3. **Occupied by another key.**\n   Compare probe distances.\n   If the resident is \"richer\" (its probe distance from *its* home is smaller than ours from *our* home), we attempt to CAS our pair into the slot.\n   On success, we adopt the displaced pair and continue probing from this slot.\n   If the resident is \"poorer\" or equal, we move on.\n\nA CAS failure is treated as \"the world changed under us\": we re-read the slot and re-evaluate without advancing the probe index.\nThis is the standard lock-free retry.\n\n### Why this is correct under contention\n\nThe Robin Hood invariant is maintained by every individual CAS because each CAS only swaps in a pair with probe distance strictly greater than the one already in the slot (or the slot was empty).\nTwo concurrent inserts targeting the same slot serialise through CAS, and whichever loses retries with the freshest state.\nThere is no global ordering requirement: as long as every write respects the local invariant, the final table is a valid Robin Hood layout for *some* permutation of the input.\n\nCaveat: the *value* portion of an update (case 2) needs care.\nIf two threads insert the same key concurrently and the user wants \"last write wins\" semantics, we need an additional CAS loop on the value, or we tolerate non-deterministic resolution.\n\n## Achieving bandwidth saturation\n\nThe 64-bit slot is small relative to a 128-byte L2 cache line: one transaction brings 16 slots.\nThe bandwidth strategy follows from this:\n\n- **Warp-cooperative probing.**\n  Within a warp, threads whose home slots land in the same cache line should service their probes from one load, not 32 loads.\n  We have the warp ballot/shuffle primitives to do this cheaply.\n- **Bucketed probing.**\n  Treat the table as an array of *buckets* of 16 slots (one cache line).\n  A probe inspects an entire bucket at a time — one coalesced 128-byte load — and uses warp intrinsics (`__ballot_sync`, `__match_any_sync`) to find the candidate slot.\n  Insertion still uses CAS on a single 8-byte slot inside the bucket, but the read traffic per probe is exactly one cache line.\n  This is the same idea as `bucketed_cuckoo` and Bucketized Cuckoo Hashing.\n- **Probe length capping.**\n  Cap probes at, say, 32 buckets.\n  If a thread overruns the cap during insertion, mark the batch as failed and rehash.\n  With Robin Hood at 0.7 load this happens with vanishing probability for reasonable `N`.\n- **Avoid divergence.**\n  All threads in a warp execute the same probe loop.\n  The \"I'm done, others aren't\" case is handled with masked operations rather than early-return-then-divergence.\n\nIf we get this right, an insert of `N` keys at load factor 0.7 should cost approximately `(1/(1-α)) * sizeof(slot)` bytes per insert in expectation, which is the information-theoretic floor for an open-addressed table with this layout.\n\n## Work decomposition: how insertions map onto the GPU hierarchy\n\nBefore discussing parameters, we need to commit to a unit of work — what GPU resource cooperates on a single key insertion.\nThere are four candidates:\n\n| Mapping              | Threads / key | Probe is one coalesced load? | Keys in flight / warp | Wasted lanes / probe |\n| -------------------- | ------------- | ---------------------------- | --------------------- | -------------------- |\n| Thread per key       | 1             | No — 32 unrelated lines      | 32                    | 0                    |\n| **Sub-warp tile**    | **B**         | **Yes — exactly one line**   | **32 / B**            | **0 (if B ∣ 32)**    |\n| Warp per key         | 32            | Yes, but ≤ B lanes used      | 1                     | 32 − B               |\n| Block per key        | 128–1024      | Yes, but lanes mostly idle   | ≪ 1                   | huge                 |\n\n- **Thread-per-key** is the naive choice and exactly the case our bandwidth strategy is designed to avoid.\n  Each warp issues 32 cache-line loads per probe step instead of one, and we lose any coalescing benefit.\n  Fatal for our stated goal.\n- **Block-per-key** is too coarse: there is no useful work for 128+ threads on a single key.\n- **Warp-per-key** is the classic \"warp-cooperative hashing\" design but wastes lanes whenever the bucket is narrower than the warp.\n  With 8-byte slots and a 128-byte line on NVIDIA, the bucket holds 16 slots, so half of every warp sits idle.\n- **Sub-warp tile per key**, with tile width equal to the bucket width, is the one design where *one tile-probe = one cache-line transaction* with no idle lanes.\n  This is the design we adopt.\n\n### The structure that follows\n\n1. A **bucket** is a contiguous, cache-line-aligned run of `B` slots.\n2. A **cooperative tile** of `B` threads (`cooperative_groups::thread_block_tile<B>`) owns one in-flight key at a time.\n   Each tile lane holds one slot of the current bucket in a register; one bucket probe is one coalesced load.\n3. **Tiles per warp** = `WarpSize / B`.\n   For NVIDIA with 8-byte slots that is 2.\n4. **Block size** is purely an occupancy knob — some multiple of `WarpSize`, tuned empirically.\n5. **Work assignment** is a tile-strided loop: tile index `t` (across the whole grid) handles input keys `t, t+T, t+2T, …` where `T` is the total number of resident tiles.\n   Naturally load-balanced and robust to input skew.\n\n### Per-tile probe step\n\nOn each probe:\n\n1. The `B` lanes cooperatively load one bucket — each lane reads one slot, fully coalesced.\n2. `tile.ballot(...)` over a predicate identifies empty slots, key matches, or Robin-Hood-displaceable slots in a single warp intrinsic.\n3. The lane corresponding to the chosen slot performs the CAS; the outcome is broadcast to the rest of the tile with `tile.shfl(...)`.\n4. On displacement, the evicted `(key, value)` becomes the tile's new in-flight pair and the tile advances by one bucket.\n\nEvery step is a warp-intrinsic operation on a single cache line, which is what makes the bandwidth argument hold.\n\n### Parameters: what is forced by the architecture, what is free\n\nOnce we commit to the sub-warp tile design, almost every constant is *derived* from the hardware, not chosen:\n\n| Parameter         | Value (default)              | Source                                   |\n| ----------------- | ---------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |\n| `SlotBytes`       | 8                            | Key/value packing choice                 |\n| `CacheLineBytes`  | 128 (NVIDIA)                 | Hardware                                 |\n| `BucketSize`      | `CacheLineBytes / SlotBytes` | **Derived**                              |\n| `WarpSize`        | 32 (NVIDIA)                  | Hardware                                 |\n| `TileSize`        | `BucketSize`                 | **Derived** (the central design choice)  |\n| `TilesPerWarp`    | `WarpSize / BucketSize`      | **Derived**                              |\n| `BlockSize`       | tuned (e.g. 128 or 256)      | Occupancy — empirical                    |\n| `TargetLoadFactor`| tuned (e.g. 0.7)             | Robin Hood quality — empirical           |\n\nSo the only genuinely free knobs are `BlockSize` and `TargetLoadFactor`.\nThe rest should be expressed in the code as compile-time constants derived from `SlotBytes`, `CacheLineBytes`, and `WarpSize`, with the latter two being template parameters of the table (with sensible defaults) rather than hard-coded.\n\n### Why the abstraction matters — an AMD aside (out of scope for v1)\n\nAMD GPUs (CDNA, RDNA) have a 64-byte cache line and a 64-thread wavefront.\nWith 8-byte slots:\n\n- NVIDIA: `BucketSize` = 128 / 8 = 16, `TilesPerWarp` = 32 / 16 = **2**.\n- AMD:    `BucketSize` =  64 / 8 =  8, `TilesPerWarp` = 64 /  8 = **8**.\n\nThe same algorithm runs unchanged; only the derived constants differ.\nWe are not targeting AMD, but it is the reason we want `CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` to be parameters of the table type rather than hard-coded `128` and `32`.\nAnything that ends up baked in as a literal in the implementation is a portability bug waiting to happen.\n\n## Lookup and deletion\n\n- **Lookup.**\n  Same probing rule, no CAS.\n  Stop as soon as we find the key, an empty slot, or a resident whose probe distance is smaller than ours.\n  Lookup is fully read-only and trivially parallel — no synchronisation required.\n- **Deletion.**\n  Backward-shift: starting from the deleted slot, while the next slot is non-empty and its probe distance is positive, shift it back by one.\n  On a concurrent GPU this is the hardest operation to make correct without locks.\n  **Initial scope: defer deletion**, support insert + lookup only, and add it once we have benchmarks in place.\n\n## API sketch\n\n```cpp\nnamespace gprh {\n\ntemplate <class Key,\n          class Value,\n          class Hash = default_hash<Key>,\n          Key   EmptyKey = Key{} - Key{1}>            // sentinel\nclass HashTable {\npublic:\n    // Host-side construction allocates device memory.\n    explicit HashTable(std::size_t capacity);\n\n    // Bulk operations. Stream-aware, non-owning input buffers.\n    void insert(const Key* keys, const Value* values,\n                std::size_t n, cudaStream_t stream = 0);\n\n    void find(const Key* keys, Value* values_out,\n              std::size_t n, cudaStream_t stream = 0) const;\n\n    // A device-side, copyable handle for use inside user kernels.\n    struct View {\n        __device__ bool  insert(Key k, Value v);\n        __device__ bool  find  (Key k, Value& out) const;\n    };\n    View view();\n\nprivate:\n    Slot*       slots_;\n    std::size_t capacity_;\n};\n\n} // namespace gprh\n```\n\nThe `View` is the centrepiece for header-only use: a user kernel that already has its keys in registers can call `view.insert(...)` directly without a separate kernel launch.\n\n## Open questions / things to settle before coding\n\n1. **Slot width.**\n   Start with 64-bit packed `(uint32 key, uint32 value)`, or go straight to a 128-bit slot for 64-bit keys?\n   The 64-bit case is the canonical micro-benchmark and is what most published GPU hash tables use.\n   Note that changing this also changes the derived `BucketSize`.\n2. **Failure semantics on full table.**\n   Return a count of failed inserts, or `assert`?\n   A bulk-insert API can naturally return the number of survivors and let the caller resize.\n3. **Resize.**\n   Out of scope for v1.\n   The table is allocated at a fixed capacity.\n   Users that need growth allocate a bigger one and re-insert.\n4. **Multi-GPU.**\n   Out of scope for v1.\n5. **Portability across vendors.**\n   Out of scope for v1 but informs the abstractions — see the AMD aside above.\n   `CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` should be table-level parameters, not magic numbers in the implementation.\n6. **Testing.**\n   A small CPU reference implementation (sequential Robin Hood) plus a randomised differential test against the GPU implementation, plus stress tests at 0.5/0.7/0.9 load.\n\n## Suggested next steps\n\n1. Sketch the `Slot`, hash, and probe primitives in a single `.cuh`.\n2. Implement the insert kernel without bucketing, just to validate correctness and the CAS loop.\n3. Add the bucketed (cache-line-aligned) variant and benchmark against the naive one.\n4. Add `find`, then benchmark against `cuCollections` / `WarpCore` as external baselines.\n"
}
```

</details>

  "content": "# CLAUDE.md\n\nProject context for Claude Code.\nThis file is loaded automatically at the start of every conversation in this repository.\n\n## What this project is\n\nA header-only CUDA library implementing a parallel hash table for NVIDIA GPUs, using Robin Hood open-addressing with bucketed (cache-line-aligned), sub-warp-cooperative probing.\nThe design target is bandwidth-bound performance on large tables.\n\n## Repository layout\n\n- `notes/` — design documentation, written for humans.\n  These files are the canonical record of *why* the implementation looks the way it does, and are intended to be readable as standalone documentation by anyone picking up the project.\n  Read these before touching code.\n  - `notes/design.md` — top-level design: Robin Hood rationale, slot layout, parallel insert algorithm, work decomposition (sub-warp tile per key), parameters, API sketch.\n- `Makefile` — build entry point (placeholder; not yet populated).\n\nWhen new design decisions are made or revised, update the relevant note in `notes/` rather than scattering rationale across commit messages or code comments.\n\n## Conventions for working in this repo\n\n- **Notes are documentation, not scratchpads.**\n  Keep them prose-first, structured, and publishable.\n  Avoid jargon shorthand that only makes sense inside one conversation.\n- **Markdown line-break convention: one sentence per line.**\n  Do not wrap at a fixed column width.\n  End every sentence with a newline; long sentences may span the screen.\n  This keeps diffs sentence-scoped and makes prose edits easy to review.\n- **Architecture-derived constants are derived, not chosen.**\n  `BucketSize`, `TileSize`, `TilesPerWarp` are all functions of `SlotBytes`, `CacheLineBytes`, and `WarpSize`.\n  The latter two should be table-level template parameters, not literals embedded in the implementation.\n  See [notes/design.md](../notes/design.md#parameters-what-is-forced-by-the-architecture-what-is-free).\n\n## Out of scope (for now)\n\n- Deletion (insert + lookup first).\n- Resize / dynamic growth.\n- Multi-GPU.\n- AMD / non-NVIDIA backends — but the abstractions should not preclude them.\n"
}
```

## Assistant — 2026-05-13T20:09:40.393Z

Good time to think through this systematically before any test code gets written. Let me organize by the failure modes each category is designed to catch.

## The meta-strategy: CPU reference + differential testing

A sequential Robin Hood implementation on the CPU is easy to write correctly (a few dozen lines, no concurrency, well-studied algorithm). Treat it as a *trusted oracle*: every interesting test becomes "run the same input through both, compare."

This single pattern subsumes most ad-hoc tests, because the oracle defines correctness for you. The only thing differential testing *can't* check directly is non-deterministic resolution (e.g. two threads inserting the same key with different values — the winner is implementation-defined). For those, the property is weaker: "value is one of the inserted values," not "value equals the oracle's value."

## Categories of tests, by what they catch

### 1. Sequential correctness — does it work at all?
- Insert one key, find it.
- Insert N distinct keys, find them all; find a key that wasn't inserted, get not-found.
- Inserting a duplicate key updates the value (or whatever our chosen semantics are — pin this down).
- Inserting the sentinel/empty key is rejected or documented as UB.
- N=0 and N=1 bulk inserts.

These run the GPU code path serially (one thread, one key) and catch single-threaded bugs without concurrency noise.

### 2. Robin Hood invariant — is the structural property actually maintained?
Walk the table after a batch of inserts and assert: for every occupied slot, the resident's probe distance is ≥ the probe distance of any displaced entry that has passed through. Equivalently, if you scan left-to-right, probe distances form a near-monotone pattern.

This is the property that makes lookups correct. Test it explicitly rather than only via end-to-end lookups, because a broken invariant can still pass lookups for the keys you happen to query.

### 3. Concurrent correctness — does CAS handle contention?
- N threads inserting N distinct keys. After: key set matches input, every key findable.
- K threads inserting the *same* key with K different values. After: exactly one entry exists; value is one of the K inputs.
- Synthetic high-contention case: thousands of keys whose home slot all hash to the same bucket. The CAS retry loop and Robin Hood displacement chain get exercised maximally here.

These are the tests that would be impossible to write correctly without the CPU oracle for the first case and a property-based check for the others.

### 4. Boundary conditions specific to the bucketed layout
- Keys whose home slot is at the *end* of a bucket — displacement chains cross bucket boundaries.
- Keys whose home slot is near `capacity - 1` — probe sequences wrap around to slot 0.
- Capacity is a power of two — verify behavior is correct at e.g. 16, 1024, 1<<20.
- Bucket of size B = 16 vs. instantiations at B = 8 or 32 (different `SlotBytes` template parameters).

Bucketed designs are particularly prone to off-by-one bugs at boundaries; these tests pin them down.

### 5. Adversarial / structured inputs
- Identity hash on consecutive integers — forces every key to its own bucket, no collisions. Tests the trivial path.
- Identity hash on keys all hashing to slot 0 — forces a single linear probe chain. Worst-case displacement.
- Keys that are multiples of `BucketSize` — exercises bucket-aligned home slots specifically.
- Real-world skewed distributions: Zipfian, keys with shared high bits, etc.

If the default hash function is good these should all pass. If it isn't, this is where you'll find out.

### 6. Capacity and load factor
- Fill to load factors 0.5, 0.7, 0.85, 0.9 — all keys findable; observed max probe length is within Robin Hood's theoretical bound (roughly O(log N) at 0.9 load).
- Try to insert past capacity — graceful failure with an accurate count of survivors (per the design's API), not silent corruption.
- Insert into a table where one slot has been pre-populated to the sentinel — does the empty check still work?

### 7. Memory safety
Run the entire suite under `compute-sanitizer --tool memcheck`, `--tool racecheck`, and `--tool initcheck`. These catch out-of-bounds slot accesses, undefined memory orderings between CAS and surrounding loads, and uninitialized reads. Cheap to wire in, catches a class of bugs that unit assertions never will.

### 8. Compile-time / API surface
- `static_assert` that `BucketSize == CacheLineBytes / SlotBytes` and similar derived-constant identities.
- Instantiations for `<uint32, uint32>`, `<uint32, uint64>`, `<uint64, uint64>` all compile.
- `View` is trivially copyable and passes by value into kernels.
- The header parses standalone (which the current smoke test already exercises).

### 9. Determinism on the read path
Find is read-only. Two identical runs of a find batch must produce bit-identical outputs. This is cheaper than it sounds and catches uninitialized-output bugs and race conditions that accidentally read mid-write.

## Explicitly *not* unit tests

- **Throughput / bandwidth benchmarks.** Belong in `benchmarks/`, run separately, compared via Nsight. They tell you about performance regressions; they don't tell you whether the table is correct.
- **Probe-length histograms at load factor 0.9.** Useful for tuning, but a statistical claim, not a pass/fail. Belongs in a diagnostic script.

---

If this looks roughly right, I can write it up as `notes/testing.md` — same prose style as `design.md`. Or we can prune first: a v1 might reasonably skip categories 5, 6, and 7 and rely on differential testing (category 1 done in bulk against random inputs) plus invariant checks (category 2) plus the concurrent oracle (category 3) plus compute-sanitizer.

Which scope feels right for v1, and want me to write it up?

## Assistant — 2026-05-13T20:53:23.825Z

Here's a sketch following your constraints — the header carries the device-side primitives and the host side does only allocation, no data movement.

## Assistant — 2026-05-13T20:53:49.534Z

**→ Tool call: `Write`** (id `tool-27`)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "file_path": "[repo]/include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh",
  "content": "#pragma once\n\n// gpurhh — GPU Robin Hood Hash table.\n//\n// Header-only CUDA library implementing a parallel hash table for NVIDIA GPUs,\n// using Robin Hood open-addressing with bucketed, sub-warp-cooperative probing.\n//\n// See the notes/ directory for the design rationale behind this layout.\n//\n// The hash table itself lives in device memory. Host code constructs and\n// destructs a HashTable, which allocates and zero-initialises the slot array\n// on the device. All operations on the contents — insert, find — are device\n// code, exposed through the View handle which is meant to be passed by value\n// into user kernels. Data movement between host and device (e.g. building the\n// input arrays, reading back results) is the caller's responsibility.\n\n#include <cstddef>\n#include <cstdint>\n\n#include <cuda_runtime.h>\n\nnamespace gpurhh {\n\n// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------\n// Defaults: empty-key sentinel and hash function.\n// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\n// Reserved sentinel signalling an empty slot. Users may not insert this key.\n// Default is the all-bits-set representation of Key (e.g. 0xFFFFFFFF for uint32).\ntemplate <class Key>\nstruct default_empty_sentinel {\n    static constexpr Key value = static_cast<Key>(~Key{});\n};\n\n// Default hash: splitmix-style 32-bit finalizer. Cheap on the GPU, decorrelates\n// structured keys well in practice. Users can supply their own via the Hash\n// template parameter.\ntemplate <class Key>\nstruct default_hash {\n    __host__ __device__ inline std::uint32_t operator()(Key key) const noexcept;\n};\n\n// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------\n// HashTable: host-side owner of the device-resident slot array.\n// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\ntemplate <\n    class Key,\n    class Value,\n    class Hash         = default_hash<Key>,\n    Key   EmptyKey     = default_empty_sentinel<Key>::value,\n    int   CacheLineBytes = 128,\n    int   WarpSize     = 32\n>\nclass HashTable {\npublic:\n    using key_type   = Key;\n    using value_type = Value;\n    using hasher     = Hash;\n\n    // Packed (key, value) slot. One Slot is the unit of an atomic CAS.\n    struct Slot {\n        Key   key;\n        Value value;\n    };\n\n    // ---- Architecture-derived constants ----\n\n    static constexpr Key empty_key        = EmptyKey;\n    static constexpr int slot_bytes       = sizeof(Slot);\n    static constexpr int cache_line_bytes = CacheLineBytes;\n    static constexpr int warp_size        = WarpSize;\n    static constexpr int bucket_size      = CacheLineBytes / slot_bytes;\n    static constexpr int tile_size        = bucket_size;\n    static constexpr int tiles_per_warp   = WarpSize / bucket_size;\n\n    static_assert(slot_bytes <= CacheLineBytes,\n                  \"slot does not fit in a cache line\");\n    static_assert(CacheLineBytes % slot_bytes == 0,\n                  \"cache line must be a whole multiple of slot size\");\n    static_assert(WarpSize % bucket_size == 0,\n                  \"warp size must be a whole multiple of bucket size\");\n\n    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------\n    // View: lightweight device-side handle. Trivially copyable; pass by value\n    // into user kernels. Holds only a device pointer and a couple of integers.\n    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------\n    class View {\n    public:\n        // Cooperative single-key insert.\n        // Called by a tile of exactly tile_size threads in lock-step. Returns\n        // true on success, false if the table is full (probe length cap hit).\n        template <class Tile>\n        __device__ bool insert(const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value);\n\n        // Cooperative single-key lookup.\n        // Returns true and writes the stored value to `out_value` if the key\n        // is present; returns false otherwise.\n        template <class Tile>\n        __device__ bool find(const Tile& tile, Key key, Value& out_value) const;\n\n        __host__ __device__ std::size_t capacity() const noexcept { return capacity_; }\n\n    private:\n        friend class HashTable;\n\n        Slot*       slots_         = nullptr;\n        std::size_t capacity_      = 0;   // power of two\n        std::size_t capacity_mask_ = 0;   // capacity_ - 1, for modulo via AND\n        Hash        hash_{};\n    };\n\n    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------\n    // Host-side resource management.\n    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\n    // Allocates a slot array on the current device sized for at least\n    // `min_capacity` slots, rounded up to the next power of two, and\n    // initialises every slot to (empty_key, _).\n    explicit HashTable(std::size_t min_capacity);\n\n    ~HashTable();\n\n    HashTable(const HashTable&)            = delete;\n    HashTable& operator=(const HashTable&) = delete;\n    HashTable(HashTable&&) noexcept;\n    HashTable& operator=(HashTable&&) noexcept;\n\n    // Returns a device-side handle for use inside user kernels.\n    View view() const noexcept;\n\n    // Actual capacity (input capacity rounded up to a power of two).\n    std::size_t capacity() const noexcept { return capacity_; }\n\nprivate:\n    Slot*       slots_    = nullptr;\n    std::size_t capacity_ = 0;\n};\n\n// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------\n// Definitions.\n// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------\n//\n// All function bodies are TODO. They live in the header because the class is a\n// template and instantiations need full definitions visible at the call site.\n\ntemplate <class Key>\n__host__ __device__ inline std::uint32_t\ndefault_hash<Key>::operator()(Key /*key*/) const noexcept {\n    // TODO: splitmix32 / Murmur3 finalizer.\n    return 0;\n}\n\ntemplate <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS>\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::HashTable(std::size_t /*min_capacity*/) {\n    // TODO: round min_capacity up to next power of two; cudaMalloc slots_;\n    //       launch an init kernel (or cudaMemset to a chosen byte pattern) to\n    //       set every slot's key field to empty_key.\n}\n\ntemplate <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS>\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::~HashTable() {\n    // TODO: cudaFree(slots_) if non-null.\n}\n\ntemplate <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS>\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::HashTable(HashTable&&) noexcept {\n    // TODO: steal slots_ and capacity_ from the moved-from instance.\n}\n\ntemplate <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS>\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>&\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::operator=(HashTable&&) noexcept {\n    // TODO: free current state if any, then steal from RHS.\n    return *this;\n}\n\ntemplate <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS>\ntypename HashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::View\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::view() const noexcept {\n    // TODO: populate a View with slots_, capacity_, capacity_ - 1, and a\n    //       default-constructed Hash.\n    return {};\n}\n\ntemplate <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS>\ntemplate <class Tile>\n__device__ bool HashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::View::insert(\n    const Tile& /*tile*/, K /*key*/, V /*value*/)\n{\n    // TODO: bucketed Robin Hood insertion. Each lane of `tile` reads one slot\n    //       of the current bucket; tile.ballot() identifies empty/match/\n    //       displaceable slots; the chosen lane performs the CAS; on\n    //       displacement, the evicted (key, value) becomes the new in-flight\n    //       pair and the tile advances by one bucket.\n    return false;\n}\n\ntemplate <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS>\ntemplate <class Tile>\n__device__ bool HashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::View::find(\n    const Tile& /*tile*/, K /*key*/, V& /*out_value*/) const\n{\n    // TODO: bucketed lookup. Same probing rule as insert but read-only; stop\n    //       on key-match (return true), on empty slot (return false), or on a\n    //       resident whose probe distance is smaller than ours (return false).\n    return false;\n}\n\n} // namespace gpurhh\n"
}
```

</details>

## Assistant — 2026-05-13T20:54:04.839Z

All informational spell-checker noise on standard C++/CUDA keywords (`constexpr`, `noexcept`, `nullptr`, `cuda`, `cudaMalloc`, `cudaMemset`) and the usual GPU/British-English terms (`splitmix`, `finalizer`, `displaceable`, `initialises`, `decorrelates`). Nothing to fix.

Sketched in [include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh):

**Host-side surface (minimal — just resource management):**
- `HashTable(min_capacity)` — rounds up to next power of two, allocates and initialises the slot array on device.
- Destructor frees device memory; non-copyable, movable.
- `view()` returns the device handle; `capacity()` reports rounded capacity.

**Device-side surface (where the algorithm lives):**
- `View::insert(tile, key, value)` and `View::find(tile, key, out)` — cooperative methods called by a tile of `tile_size` threads in lock-step. `Tile` is a template parameter so the header doesn't need to commit to a specific cooperative-groups header.
- `View` itself holds only a device pointer, capacity, capacity mask, and a default-constructed Hash — trivially copyable, passes by value into user kernels.

**Compile-time scaffolding:**
- Template parameters: `Key`, `Value`, `Hash`, `EmptyKey`, `CacheLineBytes` (default 128), `WarpSize` (default 32).
- Derived `static constexpr` constants: `slot_bytes`, `bucket_size`, `tile_size`, `tiles_per_warp` — exactly the parameter table from the design notes.
- `static_assert`s for the divisibility invariants (`CacheLineBytes % slot_bytes == 0`, `WarpSize % bucket_size == 0`).

**All bodies are TODO stubs** so the header parses and the test/example smoke files keep compiling, but nothing functional happens yet. The TODO comments describe what each body will do, mirroring the design notes.

The header has zero `cudaMemcpy` calls — host code does no data movement. Tests and examples will be the ones to allocate device buffers and call `cudaMemcpy` themselves before passing the View into a user-written kernel.

A few decisions baked into the sketch that we might still want to revisit:

1. **Sentinel as a template non-type parameter** (`Key EmptyKey = ...`) rather than a runtime constructor argument. Compile-time gives faster comparisons and lets us check it in `static_assert`s, but it complicates the type signature.
2. **No bulk `insert(K*, V*, n)` host method.** Per your guidance, callers write their own kernels using the View. We can add a convenience kernel later if the boilerplate gets repetitive.
3. **`Tile` as a template parameter** on the View methods rather than a concrete `cooperative_groups::thread_block_tile<tile_size>`. Avoids dragging `<cooperative_groups.h>` into the public header and lets callers pass any tile-like type, at the cost of less specific type checking.

Want to adjust any of these before we start filling bodies in?

## Assistant — 2026-05-13T20:57:48.091Z

In the current sketch, the answer is split across two pieces:

**The slot array** — i.e. *the actual table contents* — lives in device memory. It's allocated by `cudaMalloc` inside `HashTable::HashTable(min_capacity)` and freed by `cudaFree` in the destructor. The pointer `slots_` is a device pointer.

**The `HashTable` C++ object itself** lives on the **host**. It's just metadata: a `Slot*` and a `std::size_t`. None of its members are annotated `__device__`, so by default they're host-only — the constructor/destructor/move/`view()` are all called from host code. It's a host-side handle that owns a device-side allocation.

**The `View`** is what crosses the host-device boundary. It's trivially copyable (pointer + two integers + a default-constructed Hash) and is passed by value into kernel launches. The kernel's parameter copy is what ends up in device memory.

```
              host                     device
              ----                     ------
   HashTable { slots_*, capacity_ } --> [ Slot, Slot, Slot, ... ]
        |                                       ^
        | view()                                |
        v                                       |
   View { slots_*, capacity_, mask_, hash_ } ---'
        |
        | passed by value into kernel<<<...>>>(view, ...)
        v
   (copy of View now resident on each thread's stack inside the kernel)
```

This is the standard host-handle / device-buffer pattern (the same shape as `thrust::device_vector`, `cuCollections::static_map`, or `std::vector` with a device allocator). I believe it matches what you described — "table lives on-device, memory allocated host-side" — where "the table" means the slot data.

The alternative would be to put the `HashTable` object itself in device memory (or unified memory) so kernels can dereference it directly without needing a separate `View`. That works but means the constructor can't be a normal C++ ctor, RAII becomes awkward, and users pay for one indirection per kernel access. The View-handle pattern is strictly cleaner for the device-side hot path.

Did you have something else in mind, or is this what you intended?

---
