```json
  "content": "// Tests for gpurhh::HashTable.\n//\n// Basic correctness tests for the public API: construction, insertion, and\n// retrieval. Each test allocates device buffers, copies inputs across the\n// host/device boundary, and runs a small driver kernel that calls\n// view.insert / view.find on each input pair. See notes/ for the broader\n// testing plan.\n\n#include <cassert>\n#include <cstdint>\n#include <cstdio>\n#include <cstdlib>\n\n#include <cooperative_groups.h>\n#include <cuda_runtime.h>\n\n#include <gpurhh/hash_table.cuh>\n\nnamespace cg = cooperative_groups;\n\nnamespace {\n\n// Concrete table instantiation used throughout these tests.\nusing Table = gpurhh::HashTable<std::uint32_t, std::uint32_t>;\nusing View  = Table::View;\n\nconstexpr int kBlockSize = 128;\nstatic_assert(kBlockSize % Table::tile_size == 0,\n              \"block size must be a whole multiple of tile size\");\n\n// --- CUDA error checking -------------------------------------------------\n\nvoid cuda_check(cudaError_t e, const char* what) {\n    if (e != cudaSuccess) {\n        std::fprintf(stderr, \"CUDA error (%s): %s\\n\", what, cudaGetErrorString(e));\n        std::exit(1);\n    }\n}\n\n// --- Driver kernels ------------------------------------------------------\n//\n// One cooperative tile of Table::tile_size threads handles one (key, value)\n// pair. Tiles are tile-strided across the grid so batches larger than the\n// grid still work.\n\n__global__ void insert_kernel(View view,\n                              const std::uint32_t* keys,\n                              const std::uint32_t* values,\n                              std::size_t n)\n{\n    auto block = cg::this_thread_block();\n    auto tile  = cg::tiled_partition<Table::tile_size>(block);\n\n    const std::size_t tiles_per_block = blockDim.x / Table::tile_size;\n    const std::size_t tile_id =\n        blockIdx.x * tiles_per_block + threadIdx.x / Table::tile_size;\n    const std::size_t total_tiles = gridDim.x * tiles_per_block;\n\n    for (std::size_t i = tile_id; i < n; i += total_tiles) {\n        view.insert(tile, keys[i], values[i]);\n    }\n}\n\n// For each input key, writes (value, 1) on hit and (_, 0) on miss. We assume\n// view.find broadcasts the located value to every lane in the tile, and have\n// lane 0 perform the store to keep the store count to one per key.\n__global__ void find_kernel(View view,\n                            const std::uint32_t* keys,\n                            std::uint32_t* values_out,\n                            int* found_out,\n                            std::size_t n)\n{\n    auto block = cg::this_thread_block();\n    auto tile  = cg::tiled_partition<Table::tile_size>(block);\n\n    const std::size_t tiles_per_block = blockDim.x / Table::tile_size;\n    const std::size_t tile_id =\n        blockIdx.x * tiles_per_block + threadIdx.x / Table::tile_size;\n    const std::size_t total_tiles = gridDim.x * tiles_per_block;\n\n    for (std::size_t i = tile_id; i < n; i += total_tiles) {\n        std::uint32_t v = 0;\n        const bool ok = view.find(tile, keys[i], v);\n        if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) {\n            values_out[i] = ok ? v : 0;\n            found_out[i]  = ok ? 1 : 0;\n        }\n    }\n}\n\n// --- Host-side driver helpers --------------------------------------------\n\nvoid bulk_insert(Table& table,\n                 const std::uint32_t* h_keys,\n                 const std::uint32_t* h_values,\n                 std::size_t n)\n{\n    std::uint32_t *d_keys = nullptr, *d_values = nullptr;\n    cuda_check(cudaMalloc(&d_keys,   n * sizeof(std::uint32_t)), \"malloc d_keys\");\n    cuda_check(cudaMalloc(&d_values, n * sizeof(std::uint32_t)), \"malloc d_values\");\n    cuda_check(cudaMemcpy(d_keys,   h_keys,\n                          n * sizeof(std::uint32_t), cudaMemcpyHostToDevice),\n               \"memcpy d_keys\");\n    cuda_check(cudaMemcpy(d_values, h_values,\n                          n * sizeof(std::uint32_t), cudaMemcpyHostToDevice),\n               \"memcpy d_values\");\n\n    const int tiles_per_block = kBlockSize / Table::tile_size;\n    const int grid_size =\n        static_cast<int>((n + tiles_per_block - 1) / tiles_per_block);\n    insert_kernel<<<grid_size, kBlockSize>>>(table.view(), d_keys, d_values, n);\n    cuda_check(cudaGetLastError(),       \"insert_kernel launch\");\n    cuda_check(cudaDeviceSynchronize(),  \"insert_kernel exec\");\n\n    cudaFree(d_keys);\n    cudaFree(d_values);\n}\n\nvoid bulk_find(Table& table,\n               const std::uint32_t* h_keys,\n               std::uint32_t* h_values_out,\n               int* h_found_out,\n               std::size_t n)\n{\n    std::uint32_t *d_keys = nullptr, *d_values_out = nullptr;\n    int* d_found_out = nullptr;\n    cuda_check(cudaMalloc(&d_keys,       n * sizeof(std::uint32_t)), \"malloc d_keys\");\n    cuda_check(cudaMalloc(&d_values_out, n * sizeof(std::uint32_t)), \"malloc d_values_out\");\n    cuda_check(cudaMalloc(&d_found_out,  n * sizeof(int)),           \"malloc d_found_out\");\n    cuda_check(cudaMemcpy(d_keys, h_keys,\n                          n * sizeof(std::uint32_t), cudaMemcpyHostToDevice),\n               \"memcpy d_keys\");\n\n    const int tiles_per_block = kBlockSize / Table::tile_size;\n    const int grid_size =\n        static_cast<int>((n + tiles_per_block - 1) / tiles_per_block);\n    find_kernel<<<grid_size, kBlockSize>>>(\n        table.view(), d_keys, d_values_out, d_found_out, n);\n    cuda_check(cudaGetLastError(),       \"find_kernel launch\");\n    cuda_check(cudaDeviceSynchronize(),  \"find_kernel exec\");\n\n    cuda_check(cudaMemcpy(h_values_out, d_values_out,\n                          n * sizeof(std::uint32_t), cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost),\n               \"memcpy h_values_out\");\n    cuda_check(cudaMemcpy(h_found_out,  d_found_out,\n                          n * sizeof(int), cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost),\n               \"memcpy h_found_out\");\n\n    cudaFree(d_keys);\n    cudaFree(d_values_out);\n    cudaFree(d_found_out);\n}\n\n// --- Construction / allocation -------------------------------------------\n\nvoid test_construct_with_capacity() {\n    Table table(1024);\n    assert(table.capacity() >= 1024);\n    // Capacity should be rounded up to a power of two.\n    assert((table.capacity() & (table.capacity() - 1)) == 0);\n}\n\nvoid test_destruct_releases_memory() {\n    // Smoke test: repeatedly construct and destruct to surface any CUDA\n    // errors from the (de)allocation paths.\n    for (int i = 0; i < 4; ++i) {\n        Table table(1024);\n        (void)table;\n    }\n    cuda_check(cudaDeviceSynchronize(), \"after repeated construct/destruct\");\n}\n\n// --- Insertion ------------------------------------------------------------\n\nvoid test_insert_single_key() {\n    Table table(1024);\n    const std::uint32_t keys[]   = {42};\n    const std::uint32_t values[] = {100};\n    bulk_insert(table, keys, values, 1);\n\n    std::uint32_t got_value = 0;\n    int got_found = 0;\n    bulk_find(table, keys, &got_value, &got_found, 1);\n    assert(got_found == 1);\n    assert(got_value == 100);\n}\n\nvoid test_insert_many_distinct_keys() {\n    Table table(1024);\n    constexpr std::size_t N = 100;\n    std::uint32_t keys[N], values[N];\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < N; ++i) {\n        keys[i]   = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i + 1);     // avoid 0 to keep keys clear of any defaults\n        values[i] = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i + 1000);\n    }\n    bulk_insert(table, keys, values, N);\n\n    std::uint32_t got_value[N] = {};\n    int got_found[N] = {};\n    bulk_find(table, keys, got_value, got_found, N);\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < N; ++i) {\n        assert(got_found[i] == 1);\n        assert(got_value[i] == values[i]);\n    }\n}\n\nvoid test_insert_duplicate_key_updates_value() {\n    Table table(1024);\n    const std::uint32_t key = 7;\n    const std::uint32_t v1  = 111;\n    const std::uint32_t v2  = 222;\n    bulk_insert(table, &key, &v1, 1);\n    bulk_insert(table, &key, &v2, 1);\n\n    std::uint32_t got_value = 0;\n    int got_found = 0;\n    bulk_find(table, &key, &got_value, &got_found, 1);\n    assert(got_found == 1);\n    assert(got_value == v2);\n}\n\n// --- Retrieval ------------------------------------------------------------\n\nvoid test_find_inserted_key() {\n    Table table(1024);\n    const std::uint32_t key   = 5;\n    const std::uint32_t value = 99;\n    bulk_insert(table, &key, &value, 1);\n\n    std::uint32_t got_value = 0;\n    int got_found = 0;\n    bulk_find(table, &key, &got_value, &got_found, 1);\n    assert(got_found == 1);\n    assert(got_value == value);\n}\n\nvoid test_find_missing_key() {\n    Table table(1024);\n    const std::uint32_t key = 9999;\n    std::uint32_t got_value = 12345;   // poison\n    int got_found = 1;                 // poison\n    bulk_find(table, &key, &got_value, &got_found, 1);\n    assert(got_found == 0);\n}\n\nvoid test_find_after_many_inserts() {\n    Table table(1024);\n    constexpr std::size_t N = 50;\n    std::uint32_t keys[N], values[N];\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < N; ++i) {\n        keys[i]   = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i + 1);\n        values[i] = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(2 * i + 1);\n    }\n    bulk_insert(table, keys, values, N);\n\n    // Query a strided subset.\n    constexpr std::size_t M = 10;\n    std::uint32_t query[M], got_value[M];\n    int got_found[M];\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < M; ++i) query[i] = keys[5 * i];\n    bulk_find(table, query, got_value, got_found, M);\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < M; ++i) {\n        assert(got_found[i] == 1);\n        assert(got_value[i] == values[5 * i]);\n    }\n}\n\n} // namespace\n\nint main() {\n    test_construct_with_capacity();\n    test_destruct_releases_memory();\n\n    test_insert_single_key();\n    test_insert_many_distinct_keys();\n    test_insert_duplicate_key_updates_value();\n\n    test_find_inserted_key();\n    test_find_missing_key();\n    test_find_after_many_inserts();\n\n    std::printf(\"All tests passed.\\n\");\n    return 0;\n}\n"
}
```

## Assistant — 2026-05-13T21:30:51.079Z

All informational spell-checker noise on standard C/C++ headers (`cassert`, `cstdint`, `cstdio`, `cstdlib`), CUDA terms (`cuda`, `CUDA`, `cudaMalloc`, etc.), C++ keywords (`constexpr`, `noexcept`), and `gpurhh`. Nothing functional to fix.

Filled in [tests/test_hash_table.cu](tests/test_hash_table.cu). The structure:

**Driver kernels (top of file):**
- `insert_kernel` — one cooperative tile per `(key, value)` pair, tile-strided across the grid.
- `find_kernel` — same pattern; lane 0 writes the result to avoid `tile_size` redundant stores.

**Host-side helpers:**
- `bulk_insert` / `bulk_find` — handle device allocation, host→device copy, kernel launch, sync, and (for find) device→host copy of results. These are the test-file equivalents of bulk operations; they live here rather than in the header so the header stays free of `cudaMemcpy`.
- `cuda_check` — error-checking helper.

**Test bodies (the eight stubs from before):**
- *Construction* — `test_construct_with_capacity` checks rounding to next power of two; `test_destruct_releases_memory` is a smoke loop.
- *Insertion* — single key, 100 distinct keys, duplicate-updates-value.
- *Retrieval* — find-after-insert, find-missing-returns-not-found, find-after-many-inserts on a strided subset.

**Assumptions baked into the kernels (worth confirming before we implement):**

1. **`view.find(tile, key, out)` broadcasts the located value to every lane** of the tile (e.g. via `tile.shfl`). The `find_kernel` reads `v` from lane 0, so any other broadcast convention would need either a different kernel or a different View contract.
2. **All lanes of a tile pass the same `(key, value)` arguments** to `view.insert` / `view.find`. The driver kernel naturally arranges this since `keys[i]` and `values[i]` are the same value for every lane in the tile.
3. **Duplicate-key insert is an update** (case 2 of the design notes). The duplicate-key test does two sequential bulk_insert calls so the order is well-defined; we don't test what happens when duplicates appear in the same batch.

**What happens against current stubs:**
- The constructor sets `slots_ = nullptr, capacity_ = 0`. So `test_construct_with_capacity` will fail at the capacity assertion.
- Kernels run safely against the nullptr View because the stub `insert` / `find` return false without dereferencing.
- All insert/find tests will fail at their assertions (assertions trip, program aborts).
- `test_destruct_releases_memory` should pass (it only constructs/destructs, which both no-op).

So nothing crashes — the tests are a working spec that flips from failing to passing as the implementation lands.

I'll refactor to a postfix `>> CUDA_CHECK` operator. Capturing `__FILE__:__LINE__` at the call site via a macro removes the need for context strings entirely.

## Assistant — 2026-05-13T21:59:42.473Z

**→ Tool call: `Edit`** (id `tool-29`)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/tests/test_hash_table.cu",
  "old_string": "// --- CUDA error checking -------------------------------------------------\n\nvoid cuda_check(cudaError_t e, const char* what) {\n    if (e != cudaSuccess) {\n        std::fprintf(stderr, \"CUDA error (%s): %s\\n\", what, cudaGetErrorString(e));\n        std::exit(1);\n    }\n}",
  "new_string": "// --- CUDA error checking -------------------------------------------------\n//\n// Usage:    cudaMalloc(&p, n) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n//\n// The macro captures the call site so errors are reported with file:line and\n// individual call sites do not need to carry a context string.\n\nstruct CudaCheckLoc {\n    const char* file;\n    int         line;\n};\n\ninline void operator>>(cudaError_t e, CudaCheckLoc loc) {\n    if (e != cudaSuccess) {\n        std::fprintf(stderr, \"CUDA error at %s:%d: %s\\n\",\n                     loc.file, loc.line, cudaGetErrorString(e));\n        std::exit(1);\n    }\n}\n\n#define CUDA_CHECK CudaCheckLoc{__FILE__, __LINE__}"
}
```

</details>

## Assistant — 2026-05-13T22:00:19.042Z

Spell-check noise, ignoring.

All call sites converted. The pattern is now:

```cpp
cudaMalloc(&d_keys, n * sizeof(std::uint32_t)) >> CUDA_CHECK;
cudaMemcpy(d_keys, h_keys, ..., cudaMemcpyHostToDevice) >> CUDA_CHECK;
kernel<<<...>>>(...);
cudaGetLastError()      >> CUDA_CHECK;
cudaDeviceSynchronize() >> CUDA_CHECK;
```

The `CUDA_CHECK` macro evaluates to a `CudaCheckLoc{__FILE__, __LINE__}` at every call site, so when a check fires it reports the exact line of the failing call — no need to hand-author "what" strings, and no risk of the strings going stale when code is refactored. On success the `operator>>` is a trivial branch that gets eliminated; the cost is identical to the old function form.

---

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "file_path": "[repo]/tests/common.cuh",
  "content": "#pragma once\n\n// Shared infrastructure for gpurhh tests: a concrete table instantiation, a\n// CUDA error-check operator, the small driver kernels that read keys/values\n// from device buffers, and host-side helpers that wrap allocation, copy,\n// launch, and result-readback around those kernels.\n\n#include <cassert>\n#include <cstdint>\n#include <cstdio>\n#include <cstdlib>\n\n#include <cooperative_groups.h>\n#include <cuda_runtime.h>\n\n#include <gpurhh/hash_table.cuh>\n\nnamespace cg = cooperative_groups;\n\n// Concrete table instantiation used throughout the test suite.\nusing Table = gpurhh::HashTable<std::uint32_t, std::uint32_t>;\nusing View  = Table::View;\n\ninline constexpr int kBlockSize = 128;\nstatic_assert(kBlockSize % Table::tile_size == 0,\n              \"block size must be a whole multiple of tile size\");\n\n// --- CUDA error checking -------------------------------------------------\n//\n// Usage:    cudaMalloc(&p, n) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n//\n// The macro captures the call site so errors are reported with file:line and\n// individual call sites do not need to carry a context string.\n\nstruct CudaCheckLoc {\n    const char* file;\n    int         line;\n};\n\ninline void operator>>(cudaError_t e, CudaCheckLoc loc) {\n    if (e != cudaSuccess) {\n        std::fprintf(stderr, \"CUDA error at %s:%d: %s\\n\",\n                     loc.file, loc.line, cudaGetErrorString(e));\n        std::exit(1);\n    }\n}\n\n#define CUDA_CHECK CudaCheckLoc{__FILE__, __LINE__}\n\n// --- Driver kernels ------------------------------------------------------\n//\n// One cooperative tile of Table::tile_size threads handles one (key, value)\n// pair. Tiles are tile-strided across the grid so batches larger than the\n// grid still work.\n\n__global__ inline void insert_kernel(View view,\n                                     const std::uint32_t* keys,\n                                     const std::uint32_t* values,\n                                     std::size_t n)\n{\n    auto block = cg::this_thread_block();\n    auto tile  = cg::tiled_partition<Table::tile_size>(block);\n\n    const std::size_t tiles_per_block = blockDim.x / Table::tile_size;\n    const std::size_t tile_id =\n        blockIdx.x * tiles_per_block + threadIdx.x / Table::tile_size;\n    const std::size_t total_tiles = gridDim.x * tiles_per_block;\n\n    for (std::size_t i = tile_id; i < n; i += total_tiles) {\n        view.insert(tile, keys[i], values[i]);\n    }\n}\n\n// For each input key, writes (value, 1) on hit and (_, 0) on miss. We assume\n// view.find broadcasts the located value to every lane in the tile, and have\n// lane 0 perform the store to keep the store count to one per key.\n__global__ inline void find_kernel(View view,\n                                   const std::uint32_t* keys,\n                                   std::uint32_t* values_out,\n                                   int* found_out,\n                                   std::size_t n)\n{\n    auto block = cg::this_thread_block();\n    auto tile  = cg::tiled_partition<Table::tile_size>(block);\n\n    const std::size_t tiles_per_block = blockDim.x / Table::tile_size;\n    const std::size_t tile_id =\n        blockIdx.x * tiles_per_block + threadIdx.x / Table::tile_size;\n    const std::size_t total_tiles = gridDim.x * tiles_per_block;\n\n    for (std::size_t i = tile_id; i < n; i += total_tiles) {\n        std::uint32_t v = 0;\n        const bool ok = view.find(tile, keys[i], v);\n        if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) {\n            values_out[i] = ok ? v : 0;\n            found_out[i]  = ok ? 1 : 0;\n        }\n    }\n}\n\n// --- Host-side driver helpers -------------------------------------------\n\ninline void bulk_insert(Table& table,\n                        const std::uint32_t* h_keys,\n                        const std::uint32_t* h_values,\n                        std::size_t n)\n{\n    std::uint32_t *d_keys = nullptr, *d_values = nullptr;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_keys,   n * sizeof(std::uint32_t)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_values, n * sizeof(std::uint32_t)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMemcpy(d_keys,   h_keys,   n * sizeof(std::uint32_t),\n               cudaMemcpyHostToDevice) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMemcpy(d_values, h_values, n * sizeof(std::uint32_t),\n               cudaMemcpyHostToDevice) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n\n    const int tiles_per_block = kBlockSize / Table::tile_size;\n    const int grid_size =\n        static_cast<int>((n + tiles_per_block - 1) / tiles_per_block);\n    insert_kernel<<<grid_size, kBlockSize>>>(table.view(), d_keys, d_values, n);\n    cudaGetLastError()      >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaDeviceSynchronize() >> CUDA_CHECK;\n\n    cudaFree(d_keys);\n    cudaFree(d_values);\n}\n\ninline void bulk_find(Table& table,\n                      const std::uint32_t* h_keys,\n                      std::uint32_t* h_values_out,\n                      int* h_found_out,\n                      std::size_t n)\n{\n    std::uint32_t *d_keys = nullptr, *d_values_out = nullptr;\n    int* d_found_out = nullptr;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_keys,       n * sizeof(std::uint32_t)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_values_out, n * sizeof(std::uint32_t)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_found_out,  n * sizeof(int))           >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMemcpy(d_keys, h_keys, n * sizeof(std::uint32_t),\n               cudaMemcpyHostToDevice) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n\n    const int tiles_per_block = kBlockSize / Table::tile_size;\n    const int grid_size =\n        static_cast<int>((n + tiles_per_block - 1) / tiles_per_block);\n    find_kernel<<<grid_size, kBlockSize>>>(\n        table.view(), d_keys, d_values_out, d_found_out, n);\n    cudaGetLastError()      >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaDeviceSynchronize() >> CUDA_CHECK;\n\n    cudaMemcpy(h_values_out, d_values_out, n * sizeof(std::uint32_t),\n               cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMemcpy(h_found_out,  d_found_out,  n * sizeof(int),\n               cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n\n    cudaFree(d_keys);\n    cudaFree(d_values_out);\n    cudaFree(d_found_out);\n}\n"
}
```

</details>

```json
{
  "file_path": "[repo]/tests/test_construction.cu",
  "content": "// Construction / allocation tests for gpurhh::HashTable.\n\n#include \"common.cuh\"\n\nnamespace {\n\nvoid test_construct_with_capacity() {\n    Table table(1024);\n    assert(table.capacity() >= 1024);\n    // Capacity should be rounded up to a power of two.\n    assert((table.capacity() & (table.capacity() - 1)) == 0);\n}\n\nvoid test_destruct_releases_memory() {\n    // Smoke test: repeatedly construct and destruct to surface any CUDA\n    // errors from the (de)allocation paths.\n    for (int i = 0; i < 4; ++i) {\n        Table table(1024);\n        (void)table;\n    }\n    cudaDeviceSynchronize() >> CUDA_CHECK;\n}\n\n} // namespace\n\nint main() {\n    test_construct_with_capacity();\n    test_destruct_releases_memory();\n    std::printf(\"test_construction: all tests passed.\\n\");\n    return 0;\n}\n"
}
```

```json
{
  "file_path": "[repo]/tests/test_insert.cu",
  "content": "// Insertion tests for gpurhh::HashTable.\n\n#include \"common.cuh\"\n\nnamespace {\n\nvoid test_insert_single_key() {\n    Table table(1024);\n    const std::uint32_t keys[]   = {42};\n    const std::uint32_t values[] = {100};\n    bulk_insert(table, keys, values, 1);\n\n    std::uint32_t got_value = 0;\n    int got_found = 0;\n    bulk_find(table, keys, &got_value, &got_found, 1);\n    assert(got_found == 1);\n    assert(got_value == 100);\n}\n\nvoid test_insert_many_distinct_keys() {\n    Table table(1024);\n    constexpr std::size_t N = 100;\n    std::uint32_t keys[N], values[N];\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < N; ++i) {\n        keys[i]   = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i + 1);     // avoid 0 to keep keys clear of any defaults\n        values[i] = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i + 1000);\n    }\n    bulk_insert(table, keys, values, N);\n\n    std::uint32_t got_value[N] = {};\n    int got_found[N] = {};\n    bulk_find(table, keys, got_value, got_found, N);\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < N; ++i) {\n        assert(got_found[i] == 1);\n        assert(got_value[i] == values[i]);\n    }\n}\n\nvoid test_insert_duplicate_key_updates_value() {\n    Table table(1024);\n    const std::uint32_t key = 7;\n    const std::uint32_t v1  = 111;\n    const std::uint32_t v2  = 222;\n    bulk_insert(table, &key, &v1, 1);\n    bulk_insert(table, &key, &v2, 1);\n\n    std::uint32_t got_value = 0;\n    int got_found = 0;\n    bulk_find(table, &key, &got_value, &got_found, 1);\n    assert(got_found == 1);\n    assert(got_value == v2);\n}\n\n} // namespace\n\nint main() {\n    test_insert_single_key();\n    test_insert_many_distinct_keys();\n    test_insert_duplicate_key_updates_value();\n    std::printf(\"test_insert: all tests passed.\\n\");\n    return 0;\n}\n"
}
```

```json
{
  "file_path": "[repo]/tests/test_find.cu",
  "content": "// Retrieval tests for gpurhh::HashTable.\n\n#include \"common.cuh\"\n\nnamespace {\n\nvoid test_find_inserted_key() {\n    Table table(1024);\n    const std::uint32_t key   = 5;\n    const std::uint32_t value = 99;\n    bulk_insert(table, &key, &value, 1);\n\n    std::uint32_t got_value = 0;\n    int got_found = 0;\n    bulk_find(table, &key, &got_value, &got_found, 1);\n    assert(got_found == 1);\n    assert(got_value == value);\n}\n\nvoid test_find_missing_key() {\n    Table table(1024);\n    const std::uint32_t key = 9999;\n    std::uint32_t got_value = 12345;   // poison\n    int got_found = 1;                 // poison\n    bulk_find(table, &key, &got_value, &got_found, 1);\n    assert(got_found == 0);\n}\n\nvoid test_find_after_many_inserts() {\n    Table table(1024);\n    constexpr std::size_t N = 50;\n    std::uint32_t keys[N], values[N];\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < N; ++i) {\n        keys[i]   = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i + 1);\n        values[i] = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(2 * i + 1);\n    }\n    bulk_insert(table, keys, values, N);\n\n    // Query a strided subset.\n    constexpr std::size_t M = 10;\n    std::uint32_t query[M], got_value[M];\n    int got_found[M];\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < M; ++i) query[i] = keys[5 * i];\n    bulk_find(table, query, got_value, got_found, M);\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < M; ++i) {\n        assert(got_found[i] == 1);\n        assert(got_value[i] == values[5 * i]);\n    }\n}\n\n} // namespace\n\nint main() {\n    test_find_inserted_key();\n    test_find_missing_key();\n    test_find_after_many_inserts();\n    std::printf(\"test_find: all tests passed.\\n\");\n    return 0;\n}\n"
}
```

## Assistant — 2026-05-14T17:13:31.032Z

Split done. New layout under [tests/](tests/):

- **[tests/common.cuh](tests/common.cuh)** — `Table`/`View` aliases, `CUDA_CHECK` operator, `insert_kernel`/`find_kernel` (marked `inline __global__`), `bulk_insert`/`bulk_find` (`inline`).
- **[tests/test_construction.cu](tests/test_construction.cu)** — `test_construct_with_capacity`, `test_destruct_releases_memory`.
- **[tests/test_insert.cu](tests/test_insert.cu)** — single-key, many-distinct, duplicate-updates.
- **[tests/test_find.cu](tests/test_find.cu)** — find-inserted, find-missing, find-after-many.

Each `test_*.cu` has its own `main` that prints `test_<name>: all tests passed.` on success, so a test runner can produce per-suite output. The old [tests/test_hash_table.cu](tests/test_hash_table.cu) is removed, and [.claude/CLAUDE.md](.claude/CLAUDE.md) is updated to describe the new layout.

`inline __global__` and `inline` host helpers ensure no duplicate-symbol problems if we ever switch to linking the tests into a single binary later — but each one also works fine as a standalone executable, which is the assumption the build will use first.

```json
{
  "file_path": "[repo]/tests/utils.cuh",
  "content": "#pragma once\n\n// Small CUDA utilities shared across tests (and, eventually, examples).\n//\n// CUDA_CHECK provides a postfix error-check operator:\n//\n//     cudaMalloc(&p, n) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n//\n// The macro captures the call site so errors are reported with file:line and\n// individual call sites do not need to carry a context string.\n\n#include <cstdio>\n#include <cstdlib>\n\n#include <cuda_runtime.h>\n\nstruct CudaCheckLoc {\n    const char* file;\n    int         line;\n};\n\ninline void operator>>(cudaError_t e, CudaCheckLoc loc) {\n    if (e != cudaSuccess) {\n        std::fprintf(stderr, \"CUDA error at %s:%d: %s\\n\",\n                     loc.file, loc.line, cudaGetErrorString(e));\n        std::exit(1);\n    }\n}\n\n#define CUDA_CHECK CudaCheckLoc{__FILE__, __LINE__}\n"
}
```

```json
  "old_string": "// Shared infrastructure for gpurhh tests: a concrete table instantiation, a\n// CUDA error-check operator, the small driver kernels that read keys/values\n// from device buffers, and host-side helpers that wrap allocation, copy,\n// launch, and result-readback around those kernels.\n\n#include <cassert>\n#include <cstdint>\n#include <cstdio>\n#include <cstdlib>\n\n#include <cooperative_groups.h>\n#include <cuda_runtime.h>\n\n#include <gpurhh/hash_table.cuh>\n\nnamespace cg = cooperative_groups;\n\n// Concrete table instantiation used throughout the test suite.\nusing Table = gpurhh::HashTable<std::uint32_t, std::uint32_t>;\nusing View  = Table::View;\n\ninline constexpr int kBlockSize = 128;\nstatic_assert(kBlockSize % Table::tile_size == 0,\n              \"block size must be a whole multiple of tile size\");\n\n// --- CUDA error checking -------------------------------------------------\n//\n// Usage:    cudaMalloc(&p, n) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n//\n// The macro captures the call site so errors are reported with file:line and\n// individual call sites do not need to carry a context string.\n\nstruct CudaCheckLoc {\n    const char* file;\n    int         line;\n};\n\ninline void operator>>(cudaError_t e, CudaCheckLoc loc) {\n    if (e != cudaSuccess) {\n        std::fprintf(stderr, \"CUDA error at %s:%d: %s\\n\",\n                     loc.file, loc.line, cudaGetErrorString(e));\n        std::exit(1);\n    }\n}\n\n#define CUDA_CHECK CudaCheckLoc{__FILE__, __LINE__}\n\n// --- Driver kernels ------------------------------------------------------",
  "new_string": "// Shared infrastructure for gpurhh tests: a concrete table instantiation, the\n// small driver kernels that read keys/values from device buffers, and\n// host-side helpers that wrap allocation, copy, launch, and result-readback\n// around those kernels. CUDA error checking lives in utils.cuh.\n\n#include <cassert>\n#include <cstdint>\n#include <cstdio>\n\n#include <cooperative_groups.h>\n#include <cuda_runtime.h>\n\n#include <gpurhh/hash_table.cuh>\n\n#include \"utils.cuh\"\n\nnamespace cg = cooperative_groups;\n\n// Concrete table instantiation used throughout the test suite.\nusing Table = gpurhh::HashTable<std::uint32_t, std::uint32_t>;\nusing View  = Table::View;\n\ninline constexpr int kBlockSize = 128;\nstatic_assert(kBlockSize % Table::tile_size == 0,\n              \"block size must be a whole multiple of tile size\");\n\n// --- Driver kernels ------------------------------------------------------"
}
```

</details>

Moved. New layout:

- **[tests/utils.cuh](tests/utils.cuh)** — `CudaCheckLoc`, `operator>>`, and the `CUDA_CHECK` macro, plus the docstring. Depends only on `<cstdio>`, `<cstdlib>`, and `<cuda_runtime.h>`.
- **[tests/common.cuh](tests/common.cuh)** — `#include "utils.cuh"` at the top; everything else (table alias, driver kernels, bulk helpers) unchanged. Dropped the now-unneeded `<cstdlib>` include and the inline CUDA-check section.

The three test `.cu` files don't need any changes — they include `common.cuh`, which transitively pulls in `utils.cuh`. If we later want `CUDA_CHECK` in `examples/`, those files can include `tests/utils.cuh` directly (or we can promote it to a shared location).

```json
}
```

```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/tests/common.cuh",
  "old_string": "// For each input key, writes (value, 1) on hit and (_, 0) on miss. We assume\n// view.find broadcasts the located value to every lane in the tile, and have\n// lane 0 perform the store to keep the store count to one per key.\n__global__ inline void find_kernel(View view,\n                                   const std::uint32_t* keys,\n                                   std::uint32_t* values_out,\n                                   int* found_out,\n                                   std::size_t n)\n{\n    auto block = cg::this_thread_block();\n    auto tile  = cg::tiled_partition<Table::tile_size>(block);\n\n    const std::size_t tiles_per_block = blockDim.x / Table::tile_size;\n    const std::size_t tile_id =\n        blockIdx.x * tiles_per_block + threadIdx.x / Table::tile_size;\n    const std::size_t total_tiles = gridDim.x * tiles_per_block;\n\n    for (std::size_t i = tile_id; i < n; i += total_tiles) {\n        std::uint32_t v = 0;\n        const bool ok = view.find(tile, keys[i], v);\n        if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) {\n            values_out[i] = ok ? v : 0;\n            found_out[i]  = ok ? 1 : 0;\n        }\n    }\n}",
  "new_string": "// For each input key, writes (value, 1) on hit and (_, 0) on miss. We assume\n// view.get broadcasts the located value to every lane in the tile, and have\n// lane 0 perform the store to keep the store count to one per key.\n__global__ inline void get_kernel(View view,\n                                  const std::uint32_t* keys,\n                                  std::uint32_t* values_out,\n                                  int* found_out,\n                                  std::size_t n)\n{\n    auto block = cg::this_thread_block();\n    auto tile  = cg::tiled_partition<Table::tile_size>(block);\n\n    const std::size_t tiles_per_block = blockDim.x / Table::tile_size;\n    const std::size_t tile_id =\n        blockIdx.x * tiles_per_block + threadIdx.x / Table::tile_size;\n    const std::size_t total_tiles = gridDim.x * tiles_per_block;\n\n    for (std::size_t i = tile_id; i < n; i += total_tiles) {\n        std::uint32_t v = 0;\n        const bool ok = view.get(tile, keys[i], v);\n        if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) {\n            values_out[i] = ok ? v : 0;\n            found_out[i]  = ok ? 1 : 0;\n        }\n    }\n}"
}
```

</details>

```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/tests/common.cuh",
  "old_string": "inline void bulk_find(Table& table,\n                      const std::uint32_t* h_keys,\n                      std::uint32_t* h_values_out,\n                      int* h_found_out,\n                      std::size_t n)\n{\n    std::uint32_t *d_keys = nullptr, *d_values_out = nullptr;\n    int* d_found_out = nullptr;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_keys,       n * sizeof(std::uint32_t)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_values_out, n * sizeof(std::uint32_t)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_found_out,  n * sizeof(int))           >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMemcpy(d_keys, h_keys, n * sizeof(std::uint32_t),\n               cudaMemcpyHostToDevice) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n\n    const int tiles_per_block = kBlockSize / Table::tile_size;\n    const int grid_size =\n        static_cast<int>((n + tiles_per_block - 1) / tiles_per_block);\n    find_kernel<<<grid_size, kBlockSize>>>(\n        table.view(), d_keys, d_values_out, d_found_out, n);\n    cudaGetLastError()      >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaDeviceSynchronize() >> CUDA_CHECK;",
  "new_string": "inline void bulk_get(Table& table,\n                     const std::uint32_t* h_keys,\n                     std::uint32_t* h_values_out,\n                     int* h_found_out,\n                     std::size_t n)\n{\n    std::uint32_t *d_keys = nullptr, *d_values_out = nullptr;\n    int* d_found_out = nullptr;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_keys,       n * sizeof(std::uint32_t)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_values_out, n * sizeof(std::uint32_t)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_found_out,  n * sizeof(int))           >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMemcpy(d_keys, h_keys, n * sizeof(std::uint32_t),\n               cudaMemcpyHostToDevice) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n\n    const int tiles_per_block = kBlockSize / Table::tile_size;\n    const int grid_size =\n        static_cast<int>((n + tiles_per_block - 1) / tiles_per_block);\n    get_kernel<<<grid_size, kBlockSize>>>(\n        table.view(), d_keys, d_values_out, d_found_out, n);\n    cudaGetLastError()      >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaDeviceSynchronize() >> CUDA_CHECK;"
}
```

```
7	void test_insert_single_key() {
8	    Table table(1024);
9	    const std::uint32_t keys[]   = {42};
10	    const std::uint32_t values[] = {100};
11	    bulk_insert(table, keys, values, 1);

20	void test_insert_many_distinct_keys() {

22	    constexpr std::size_t N = 100;
23	    std::uint32_t keys[N], values[N];
24	    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
25	        keys[i]   = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i + 1);     // avoid 0 to keep keys clear of any defaults
26	        values[i] = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i + 1000);
27	    }

29	
30	    std::uint32_t got_value[N] = {};
31	    int got_found[N] = {};
32	    bulk_find(table, keys, got_value, got_found, N);
33	    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
34	        assert(got_found[i] == 1);
35	        assert(got_value[i] == values[i]);
36	    }
37	}
38	
39	void test_insert_duplicate_key_updates_value() {
40	    Table table(1024);
41	    const std::uint32_t key = 7;
42	    const std::uint32_t v1  = 111;
43	    const std::uint32_t v2  = 222;
44	    bulk_insert(table, &key, &v1, 1);
45	    bulk_insert(table, &key, &v2, 1);
46	
47	    std::uint32_t got_value = 0;
48	    int got_found = 0;
49	    bulk_find(table, &key, &got_value, &got_found, 1);
50	    assert(got_found == 1);
51	    assert(got_value == v2);
52	}
53	
54	} // namespace
55	
56	int main() {

58	    test_insert_many_distinct_keys();
59	    test_insert_duplicate_key_updates_value();
```

226	The `View` is the centrepiece for header-only use: a user kernel that already has its keys in registers can call `view.insert(...)` directly without a separate kernel launch.
227	
228	## Open questions / things to settle before coding
229	
230	1. **Slot width.**
231	   Start with 64-bit packed `(uint32 key, uint32 value)`, or go straight to a 128-bit slot for 64-bit keys?
232	   The 64-bit case is the canonical micro-benchmark and is what most published GPU hash tables use.
233	   Note that changing this also changes the derived `BucketSize`.
234	2. **Failure semantics on full table.**
235	   Return a count of failed inserts, or `assert`?
236	   A bulk-insert API can naturally return the number of survivors and let the caller resize.
237	3. **Resize.**
238	   Out of scope for v1.
239	   The table is allocated at a fixed capacity.
240	   Users that need growth allocate a bigger one and re-insert.
241	4. **Multi-GPU.**
242	   Out of scope for v1.
243	5. **Portability across vendors.**
244	   Out of scope for v1 but informs the abstractions — see the AMD aside above.
245	   `CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` should be table-level parameters, not magic numbers in the implementation.
246	6. **Testing.**
247	   A small CPU reference implementation (sequential Robin Hood) plus a randomised differential test against the GPU implementation, plus stress tests at 0.5/0.7/0.9 load.
248	
249	## Suggested next steps

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T15:54:21.965Z

Three new `static_assert`s on `tile_size`, plus a grouping comment:

1. **`tile_size > 0`** — catches the `slot_bytes > CacheLineBytes` case at the tile layer too. Strictly redundant with the existing slot-fits-in-line assertion, but it states the tile-side intent explicitly.
2. **`(tile_size & (tile_size - 1)) == 0`** — power-of-two requirement from `cooperative_groups::tiled_partition<N>`, which only accepts N ∈ {1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32}. This is the one that would actually fire today for an unusual slot/cache-line ratio (e.g. `SlotBytes = 12` would give `bucket_size = 10` and `tiled_partition<10>` wouldn't compile, but the failure message would come from deep inside the cooperative-groups header).
3. **`tile_size <= WarpSize`** — equivalent to "`tiles_per_warp >= 1`" but more directly named. Catches misconfigurations where someone picks a `CacheLineBytes / slot_bytes` ratio larger than the warp.

I deliberately stopped short of `WarpSize <= 32`: that would be a portability bug per the AMD aside in the design notes, where wavefront = 64 is the whole point of keeping `WarpSize` parameterised.

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T18:35:10.391Z

The standard choice for GPU hash tables is the **MurmurHash3 32-bit finalizer** (commonly called `fmix32`):

```c
uint32_t fmix32(uint32_t h) {
    h ^= h >> 16;
    h *= 0x85ebca6b;
    h ^= h >> 13;
    h *= 0xc2b2ae35;
    h ^= h >> 16;
    return h;
}
```

**Why this:**
- **Industry standard for GPU hashing.** cuCollections, RAPIDS, WarpCore — all use either this or the closely related `fmix64`. Picking the same default makes our results directly comparable to theirs.
- **Cheap on GPU.** Eight ALU ops: 2 multiplies, 3 xors, 3 shifts. No branches, no memory access, fully SIMT-friendly — every lane does the same work in lockstep.
- **Strong avalanche.** Flipping any single input bit flips roughly half the output bits, which is what Robin Hood needs to perform well on structured keys (consecutive integers, all-multiples-of-N, etc.).
- **Well-studied.** MurmurHash3 has been audited in countless production systems since 2011. We're not gambling on a homegrown mixer.

**Alternatives considered and rejected:**

- **Multiplicative / Fibonacci hash** (`x * 0x9E3779B1u >> shift`). Half a cycle cheaper, but the low-order output bits depend only on low-order input bits — bad for our modulo-by-capacity-mask which selects the low bits as the home slot. Would force us to either use the high bits (extra shift) or accept clustering on structured keys.
- **xxHash / wyhash.** Designed for hashing variable-length byte streams. Overkill and slightly more expensive for fixed 32-bit keys.
- **Stafford's mixer variants.** Slightly better statistical scores than fmix32 on some test suites; not enough difference to deviate from the MurmurHash convention everyone else uses.

**Forward path for other key sizes:**

When we add 64-bit keys, we want `fmix64` (MurmurHash3's 64-bit finalizer with constants `0xff51afd7ed558ccdULL` and `0xc4ceb9fe1a85ec53ULL`) and to truncate to a 32-bit output for our hash interface. For now we'd specialize `default_hash` for `uint32_t` and leave the 64-bit specialization as a follow-up.

Want me to implement `fmix32` as `default_hash<uint32_t>` (specialization), or as a generic that accepts any 32-bit-or-narrower type via implicit conversion? The specialization is cleaner; the generic is one fewer line of plumbing when we add new key types.

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T18:40:28.819Z

Two new asserts at the top of the slot / cache-line invariants block in [include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh):

1. **`slot_bytes == 8 || slot_bytes == 16`** — the permanent set of supported slot widths. Matches the design notes' "64-bit packed slot now, 128-bit slot for 64-bit-key cases later."
2. **`slot_bytes == 8`** — temporary v1 restriction with a comment explaining when and why to remove it (when 128-bit slot support lands, which requires the 128-bit CAS path on sm_70+).

Strictly speaking the second assert makes the first redundant *today*. I kept both because they serve different purposes: the first documents the design's intended set of allowed widths permanently, the second tracks a temporary implementation limitation that will be deleted. When the deletion happens, the first assert remains and continues to do useful work.

```json
  "new_string": "// Default hash: MurmurHash3 32-bit finalizer (fmix32) for 4-byte keys; an\n// 8-byte specialization is reserved for when 128-bit slot support is added.\n// Both are cheap on the GPU, branch-free, and have strong avalanche. ## Assistant — 2026-05-15T18:47:00.078Z
```

Hash function in place. Summary of what's now in [include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh):

**`gpurhh::detail` namespace:**
- `fmix32` — full MurmurHash3 32-bit finalizer (2 multiplies, 3 xors, 3 shifts).
- `fmix64` — stub (returns input unchanged) with a TODO note and the canonical fmix64 constants written down in a comment so they're not lost.

**`default_hash` template — two compile-time specializations:**
- `default_hash<Key, 4>` — bit-casts the key to `uint32_t` via `std::memcpy` (optimises to a no-op for matching sizes; works for any 4-byte trivially-copyable type — `uint32_t`, `int32_t`, `float`, `enum`, etc.) and feeds it through `fmix32`.
- `default_hash<Key, 8>` — empty stub that returns 0, with a TODO describing what the eventual implementation does. Since the `slot_bytes == 8` assert in `HashTable` currently prevents 16-byte slots, this specialization is unreachable from the table; it exists so the type compiles when 8-byte keys are paired with smaller values.

The old `default_hash<Key>::operator()` out-of-class definition (with the "splitmix32 / Murmur3 finalizer" TODO) is gone — the new specializations are defined inline.

Tests can now do something meaningful: once we wire up `View::insert` and `View::get`, the table will actually hash keys instead of always returning 0.

## User — 2026-05-15T18:47:46.302Z

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T18:48:09.772Z

<em>Thinking…</em>

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T18:48:17.202Z

It's a long-standing C++ convention (Boost, fmt, the std library's internals) to put "implementation details that are not part of the public API" into a `detail::` sub-namespace. It signals to users "don't depend on these directly; we may rename, refactor, or delete them without a deprecation period."

For our case it's mostly habit — there are arguments against it:

- **The library is header-only and small.** Everything is visible anyway, and `detail::` doesn't actually hide anything from determined users.
- **`fmix32` is a useful building block.** A user writing their own `Hash` functor might reasonably want to call it. Burying it in `detail::` discourages that.
- **The name is generic.** If we ever add more utilities (probe helpers, slot encoding, etc.), they all end up in the same `detail::` bucket and the namespace loses meaning.

A few alternatives:

| Option | Looks like | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| `gpurhh::detail::fmix32` (current) | `detail::fmix32(x)` | Conventional, signals "private", but uninformative. |
| `gpurhh::fmix32` (flat) | `fmix32(x)` | Promotes it to a public utility; users can reuse it. |
| `gpurhh::hash::fmix32` | `hash::fmix32(x)` | Topical grouping; scales if we add `hash::fmix64`, `hash::splitmix64`, etc. |

If you have a preference, happy to switch. The topical `hash::` is my preferred next-best — it's a self-documenting grouping rather than a generic "stuff lives here."

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T19:09:26.069Z

Real trade-off, not just style. The `memcpy` is acting as a `std::bit_cast` (C++20) — it's there to handle types where the value-cast you'd get from `static_cast` differs from the bit pattern.

Cases where `static_cast<uint32_t>(key)` does the wrong thing:

| Key type | `static_cast<uint32_t>(k)` | `memcpy` bit-cast |
|---|---|---|
| `uint32_t`, `int32_t` | same bits ✓ | same bits ✓ |
| `enum class : uint32_t` | needs an explicit underlying-type cast first | works ✓ |
| `float` | **value-converts** — `3.14f` becomes `3u`, losing the bit pattern | preserves the bit pattern ✓ |
| 4-byte trivially-copyable struct | won't compile | works ✓ |

For the 8-byte spec it's worse:

| Key type | `static_cast<uint64_t>(k)` | `memcpy` |
|---|---|---|
| `uint64_t`, `int64_t` | same bits ✓ | same bits ✓ |
| Pointer (`T*` on 64-bit) | **won't compile** (needs `reinterpret_cast`) | works ✓ |
| `double` | value-converts | preserves bits ✓ |

So the choice is really "which key types do we want `default_hash` to handle by default?"

- **Integer-only** (your proposal): cleanest code, `static_cast` works, but users with `float`/`double`/pointer/struct keys must write their own `Hash`.
- **Anything trivially copyable** (current): `memcpy` covers everything, compiler optimises it to a no-op for matching types — but the code looks like it's doing work when it isn't.

The compiler will fold `std::memcpy(&bits, &key, 4)` into a single 4-byte load (or eliminate it entirely if `bits` is already in a register). It is genuinely zero overhead. So the actual cost is readability.

My recommendation: **keep `memcpy`**. The project will plausibly want to hash pointer keys (very common — interning, cache keys) and possibly float/double for scientific use cases. Forcing those users to supply their own `Hash` just to do the bit-cast we'd already write here is a poor default. And the cost of `memcpy` is exactly zero.

That said, if you'd rather keep the code maximally explicit and restrict `default_hash` to integers, the change is two lines:

```cpp
static_assert(std::is_integral_v<Key>, "default_hash<Key, 4> supports only integral keys");
return detail::fmix32(static_cast<std::uint32_t>(key));
```

Your call. The `memcpy` reads "weird" but it's the standard C++ idiom for portable bit-cast in pre-C++20 code, and most of the GPU hash-table libraries (cuCollections, WarpCore) use the same pattern for the same reason.

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T19:11:11.856Z

Honest answer: we haven't pinned a version anywhere, because there's no Makefile yet. The code I've written so far uses features through C++17 — `inline constexpr` variables in `tests/common.cuh`, default template arguments after a template parameter, etc. — but not anything C++20-specific.

CUDA toolkit support:

- **C++17** is fully supported by NVCC and has been since CUDA 11. Safe default.
- **C++20** is supported from CUDA 12.0+ but with caveats — `<concepts>`, ranges, `std::bit_cast`, and `std::source_location` mostly work, but you sometimes hit `__host__`/`__device__` annotation gaps in libstdc++ that need workarounds.

If we pinned C++20, the relevant cleanup for hashing would be:

```cpp
#include <bit>

template <class Key>
struct default_hash<Key, 4> {
    __host__ __device__ inline std::uint32_t operator()(Key key) const noexcept {
        return detail::fmix32(std::bit_cast<std::uint32_t>(key));
    }
};
```

— one line, no temporary, intent obvious. The `<cstring>` include goes away too.

`std::bit_cast` is `constexpr`, has the right type system semantics, and `static_assert`s internally that source and destination are the same size and trivially copyable. It's strictly better than `memcpy` here.

My suggestion: target **C++17** until we have a reason to bump. If/when we want `std::bit_cast`, concepts on `Hash`, or `std::source_location` to replace the `CUDA_CHECK` macro, that's the natural moment to move to C++20. For now, the `memcpy` pattern in two places is a small price.

When we get to setting up the Makefile, we'll bake the chosen `-std=c++17` flag in so this stops being implicit. Want me to flag this in `notes/design.md` so the decision is recorded?

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T19:16:00.260Z

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T19:16:13.297Z

Sensible policy. "C++20 standard, C++17-equivalent idioms unless C++20 demonstrably wins" gives us a clean baseline without dragging the project into the weirder edges of C++20 (concepts, ranges, coroutines in CUDA) that don't pay for themselves yet.

Things in our existing code that would actually move:

- **`default_hash` memcpys → `std::bit_cast`.** Clear win, one line, intent explicit, no temporary, no `<cstring>`. This is the only spot today.
- **`CUDA_CHECK` macro → `std::source_location`.** Possible but I'd leave it. Macros that capture `__FILE__:__LINE__` are still the lighter-weight pattern; `source_location` saves no characters and adds a parameter to the operator. Doesn't pay for itself.
- **Hash interface via concepts.** Tempting (`template <class T> concept Hasher = ...`) but the `static_assert`s we'd want aren't pressing yet. Defer.

So in practice, switching to C++20 means: change two `memcpy`s to `bit_cast`. That's it for now. Everything else stays C++17-shape.

One thing I want to flag rather than assert: **I'm not 100% sure CUDA 13 is the exact requirement for 128-bit CAS**, and I'd want to verify before we bake that into the design notes. What I do know:

- 64-bit `atomicCAS` (our v1 target) has been around since sm_60.
- 128-bit atomics via libcu++ (`cuda::atomic_ref<__int128>`) have been getting steadily more robust through CUDA 12.x.
- Single-instruction hardware 128-bit atomics on Hopper/Blackwell are a separate path.

If the deciding factor for choosing CUDA 13 is "the version where 128-bit CAS is reliable across our target GPUs," we should pin that down explicitly when we get to 128-bit slot support. For now, **the 8-byte slot path uses 64-bit CAS only**, so the CUDA version we actually need for v1 is much lower — probably CUDA 11 or 12. Bumping to CUDA 13 is then a forward-looking choice ("get the toolchain ready for 128-bit work"), not a v1 necessity.

Want me to:

1. Apply the `bit_cast` change to `default_hash` now, and
2. Add a short "Language and toolchain" note to `notes/design.md` recording: target C++20 / CUDA 13, use C++17-shape code by default, with the caveat about verifying the 128-bit CAS version requirement?

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T19:21:01.034Z

Looking at what's still stubbed in [include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh), there are two distinct chunks of work left:

**Host-side resource management** (5 TODOs, all small):
- `HashTable(min_capacity)` — round up to next power of two, `cudaMalloc`, initialise slots to the empty sentinel.
- `~HashTable()` — `cudaFree`.
- Move constructor + move assignment — steal pointer/capacity.
- `view()` — package the device pointer, capacity, and capacity mask into a `View`.

**Device-side algorithm** (2 TODOs, hard):
- `View::insert` — bucketed Robin Hood with CAS, displacement, retry on stale read.
- `View::get` — read-only bucketed probe with the smaller-probe-distance early exit.

My recommendation: **host-side first.**

Reasons:
1. **It's a prerequisite.** Nothing in the device path can be meaningfully tested with `slots_ == nullptr` and `capacity_ == 0`. Once it lands, `test_construction.cu` flips from "all assertions fail" to "all assertions pass," giving us a working end-to-end smoke test for free.
2. **It's small and self-contained.** Maybe 30 lines of straightforward CUDA. No CAS, no probing, no warp intrinsics — just `cudaMalloc`, a one-line init kernel (or `cudaMemset` since our sentinel is all-bits-set by default), `cudaFree`, and a struct copy.
3. **It clarifies one open question.** While writing the constructor we need to decide how initialisation happens: `cudaMemset(0xFF, ...)` works only when the empty sentinel is all-ones — which is the default but a user-overridable template parameter. So we either launch a tiny init kernel unconditionally (one line of CUDA), or branch on whether the sentinel is `~Key{}`. Worth deciding explicitly, and that decision goes into the design notes.

After that, between the two device-side pieces, I'd then tackle `View::get` second (simpler, read-only, no CAS), and `View::insert` last (the algorithmic core).

Want me to proceed with the host side, or did you have a different next step in mind?

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T19:24:59.712Z

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T19:25:13.066Z

**How it works today:**

```cpp
template <class Key>
struct default_empty_sentinel {
    static constexpr Key value = static_cast<Key>(~Key{});  // all-bits-set
};

template <class Key, class Value, ..., Key EmptyKey = default_empty_sentinel<Key>::value, ...>
class HashTable {
    static constexpr Key empty_key = EmptyKey;
    // ...
};
```

Three pieces: a trait (`default_empty_sentinel`) that yields all-bits-set for any integral type, a non-type template parameter (`EmptyKey`) on `HashTable` so users can override it, and a `static constexpr` alias (`empty_key`) exposed on the class.

Mechanically: an empty slot has `slot.key == empty_key`. Insert treats such a slot as available. Get treats encountering one as proof the key isn't in the table (the probe sequence terminates). Users are forbidden from inserting `empty_key`. The default of all-ones is convenient because it means we can initialise the entire slot array with `cudaMemset(slots_, 0xFF, ...)` — a single memory-bound DMA — rather than launching an init kernel.

**Why "sentinel" instead of "tombstone":**

These are two genuinely different concepts in the hash-table literature, and conflating them causes subtle bugs:

| | **Sentinel / empty marker** | **Tombstone** |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | "This slot has never held a key, or the table was just initialised." | "A key used to live here; it was deleted; keep probing further." |
| Lookup behaviour | Probe terminates — key is not in the table. | Probe continues — the key may still be further down the chain. |
| Needed when... | Always (you need to recognise empty slots). | Open addressing **with lazy deletion**. |

Robin Hood hashing with backward-shift deletion — the design.md plan — **does not need tombstones**. When you delete a key, you shift the trailing entries back by one slot until you hit either an empty slot or an entry with probe distance zero. There are no gaps; there are no tombstones. So in our table the only thing we ever need is the empty marker.

Naming-wise, "sentinel" is the standard term for "value that means 'this isn't a real key.'" Other things in the same conceptual family:

- C's `NULL` is a pointer sentinel.
- `std::optional`'s "empty" state is a sentinel.
- `std::string::npos` is a sentinel.

If you don't love "sentinel" either, the alternatives I'd consider:

- **`empty_key`** — what the class actually exposes already. Could rename the trait to `default_empty_key<Key>` to match. Most direct.
- **`null_key`** — by analogy with null pointer.
- **`vacant_key`** — a bit literary but unambiguous.

I'd vote `empty_key` and rename the trait to `default_empty_key<Key>` — it removes the only "sentinel" word in the codebase and matches the existing public name. Want me to do that rename, or did you have something else in mind?

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T19:29:18.478Z

**Yes, it does — for any integral key type.**

Walking through `static_cast<Key>(~Key{})`:

1. `Key{}` is value-initialisation, which for integral types is `0`.
2. `~Key{}` is the bitwise NOT, which yields all-bits-set in whatever type the operand has after integer promotion.
3. The `static_cast<Key>(...)` puts it back into the original type.

The `static_cast` is doing real work for *narrow* integer types. For `uint8_t`:
- `Key{}` is `uint8_t(0)`.
- `~uint8_t(0)` undergoes integer promotion to `int`, becoming `~0` = `-1` = `0xFFFFFFFF` as `int`.
- `static_cast<uint8_t>(-1)` truncates to `0xFF`. ✓

For our actual key types — `uint32_t`, `uint64_t`, `int32_t`, `int64_t` — no promotion changes the value, and the result is bit-for-bit all-ones (`0xFFFFFFFF`, `0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF`, or `-1` in the signed representation, which has the same bit pattern). The cast is a no-op but harmless.

The relevant non-yes-case is **non-integral keys**: `~Key{}` doesn't compile for `float`, `double`, scoped enums, or structs. So users with those key types must explicitly supply `EmptyKey` (or, more likely, supply their own `Hash` *and* `EmptyKey`). Given our current `slot_bytes == 8` restriction limits us to 4-byte integral key + 4-byte integral value anyway, this isn't reachable today.

Now the rename:

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T19:36:48.098Z

Good points. For signed integers, `std::numeric_limits<T>::min()` (`INT_MIN`-like) is the conventional alternative — much rarer in user code than `-1`. Trade-off: it breaks the `cudaMemset(0xFF)` init path for signed types (the bit pattern `0x80000000` doesn't memset cleanly), so the constructor will need an init kernel when the sentinel isn't all-bytes-the-same. We'll handle that when we get to the constructor.

```json
  "new_string": "// Reserved value signalling an empty slot. Users may not insert this key.\n//\n// Default value depends on the signedness of `Key`:\n//   - Unsigned integer types: the maximum representable value (all-bits-set).\n//     E.g. 0xFFFFFFFFu for uint32_t.\n//   - Signed integer types: the minimum representable value (e.g.\n//     -2147483648 for int32_t). We deliberately avoid -1, which is a very\n//     common legitimate key in user code (loop counters, error codes,\n//     sentinel returns), whereas INT_MIN-like values are rarely used as\n//     data.\n//\n// Compiles for: any integral type. Does not compile for floating-point\n// types, scoped enums, or user-defined structs — for those, supply an\n// explicit `EmptyKey` template argument to `HashTable` (and probably your\n// own `Hash` functor too).\n//\n// For unsigned keys the all-ones bit pattern lets `HashTable` initialise\n// its slot array with a single cudaMemset(0xFF, ...). ## Assistant — 2026-05-15T19:37:20.414Z
```

Three changes:

- **[include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh)** — `default_empty_key` now dispatches on signedness: unsigned types keep all-bits-set (via `~Key{}`); signed types use `std::numeric_limits<Key>::min()` (INT_MIN, LLONG_MIN, etc.). Comment rewritten to explain both branches and to note the cudaMemset implication. Added `<limits>` and `<type_traits>` includes, plus a `static_assert` inside the struct ensuring the type is integral.
- **8 `static_assert`s** moved out of the test file to namespace scope right after the trait definition, in the header itself. They always run when the header is parsed; if any default value drifts, the header fails to compile.
- **[tests/test_empty_key.cu](tests/test_empty_key.cu)** — deleted. The asserts now live with the trait they're checking.

Tradeoff to remember when we implement the constructor: the init path needs two branches now. For unsigned-default tables, `cudaMemset(slots_, 0xFF, ...)` works. For signed-default tables (or any custom `EmptyKey`), we need a small init kernel that writes `empty_key` into each slot. We can pick the branch at compile time using `std::has_unique_object_representations_v` and/or a comparison against `~Key{}`, or just always launch the init kernel and accept the marginal cost — that's an actual decision to make when we write the constructor.

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T19:42:40.548Z

Good point. We can pick a byte value for signed types whose repetition gives a sensible empty key. I'd suggest **0x80** for signed: every byte has the high bit set, giving a very negative value — for `int8_t` it's exactly `INT_MIN`, for wider signed types it's `0x80808080…` which is close to but not exactly `INT_MIN`. The "very negative with weird bit pattern" property is what we want for a sentinel.

Other candidates ruled out:
- `0xFF` → `-1` (rejected earlier)
- `0x7F` → `0x7F7F7F7F` (very positive, less unusual than very negative)
- `0x01`, `0x55`, `0xAA`, etc. → random-looking small/medium values with no semantic intent

Let me update the trait to expose both the byte pattern and the resulting value, computed from the type's signedness:

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T19:43:01.852Z

**→ Tool call: `Edit`** (id `tool-76`)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh",
  "old_string": "// Reserved value signalling an empty slot. Users may not insert this key.\n//\n// Default value depends on the signedness of `Key`:\n//   - Unsigned integer types: the maximum representable value (all-bits-set).\n//     E.g. 0xFFFFFFFFu for uint32_t.\n//   - Signed integer types: the minimum representable value (e.g.\n//     -2147483648 for int32_t). We deliberately avoid -1, which is a very\n//     common legitimate key in user code (loop counters, error codes,\n//     sentinel returns), whereas INT_MIN-like values are rarely used as\n//     data.\n//\n// Compiles for: any integral type. Does not compile for floating-point\n// types, scoped enums, or user-defined structs — for those, supply an\n// explicit `EmptyKey` template argument to `HashTable` (and probably your\n// own `Hash` functor too).\n//\n// For unsigned keys the all-ones bit pattern lets `HashTable` initialise\n// its slot array with a single cudaMemset(0xFF, ...). For signed keys the\n// constructor falls back to an init kernel since INT_MIN is not\n// representable as a repeated-byte pattern.\ntemplate <class Key>\nstruct default_empty_key {\n    static_assert(std::is_integral_v<Key>,\n                  \"default_empty_key only supports integral types; supply an \"\n                  \"explicit EmptyKey for other key types\");\n\n    static constexpr Key value = std::is_unsigned_v<Key>\n        ? static_cast<Key>(~Key{})\n        : std::numeric_limits<Key>::min();\n};\n\n// Compile-time verification for the standard integer types.\nstatic_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint8_t >::value == 0xFFu);\nstatic_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint16_t>::value == 0xFFFFu);\nstatic_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint32_t>::value == 0xFFFFFFFFu);\nstatic_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint64_t>::value == 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFull);\nstatic_assert(default_empty_key<std::int8_t >::value == std::numeric_limits<std::int8_t >::min());\nstatic_assert(default_empty_key<std::int16_t>::value == std::numeric_limits<std::int16_t>::min());\nstatic_assert(default_empty_key<std::int32_t>::value == std::numeric_limits<std::int32_t>::min());\nstatic_assert(default_empty_key<std::int64_t>::value == std::numeric_limits<std::int64_t>::min());",
  "new_string": "// Reserved value signalling an empty slot. Users may not insert this key.\n//\n// The default is a byte pattern — the same byte repeated across the full\n// width of `Key` — chosen so the slot array can be initialised with a\n// single cudaMemset. The byte depends on the signedness of `Key`:\n//\n//   - Unsigned integer types: every byte is 0xFF, so `value` is the\n//     maximum representable value (e.g. 0xFFFFFFFFu for uint32_t).\n//   - Signed integer types: every byte is 0x80, so `value` has the high\n//     bit set in every byte (e.g. 0x80808080 = -2139062144 for int32_t).\n//     For int8_t this is exactly INT_MIN; for wider signed types it is\n//     close to but not exactly INT_MIN. We avoid 0xFF (which would give\n//     -1) because -1 is a very common legitimate key in user code.\n//\n// Compiles for: any integral type. Does not compile for floating-point\n// types, scoped enums, or user-defined structs — for those, supply an\n// explicit `EmptyKey` template argument to `HashTable` (and probably your\n// own `Hash` functor too).\n//\n// The trait exposes both `value` (the key value itself) and `memset_byte`\n// (the single byte that, repeated, produces that value). `HashTable`'s\n// constructor uses `memset_byte` to init the slot array with one\n// cudaMemset call.\ntemplate <class Key>\nstruct default_empty_key {\n    static_assert(std::is_integral_v<Key>,\n                  \"default_empty_key only supports integral types; supply an \"\n                  \"explicit EmptyKey for other key types\");\n\n    static constexpr unsigned char memset_byte =\n        std::is_unsigned_v<Key> ? 0xFFu : 0x80u;\n\n    static constexpr Key value = []() {\n        using U = std::make_unsigned_t<Key>;\n        U bits = 0;\n        for (std::size_t i = 0; i < sizeof(Key); ++i) {\n            bits = static_cast<U>(bits << 8) | static_cast<U>(memset_byte);\n        }\n        return static_cast<Key>(bits);\n    }();\n};\n\n// Compile-time verification for the standard integer types: every byte is\n// 0xFF for unsigned, 0x80 for signed.\nstatic_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint8_t >::value == 0xFFu);\nstatic_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint16_t>::value == 0xFFFFu);\nstatic_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint32_t>::value == 0xFFFFFFFFu);\nstatic_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint64_t>::value == 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFull);\nstatic_assert(std::bit_cast<std::uint8_t >(default_empty_key<std::int8_t >::value) == 0x80u);\nstatic_assert(std::bit_cast<std::uint16_t>(default_empty_key<std::int16_t>::value) == 0x8080u);\nstatic_assert(std::bit_cast<std::uint32_t>(default_empty_key<std::int32_t>::value) == 0x80808080u);\nstatic_assert(std::bit_cast<std::uint64_t>(default_empty_key<std::int64_t>::value) == 0x8080808080808080ull);"
}
```

</details>

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T19:43:14.645Z

All spell-check noise on `memset`, `cuda`, `Memset`, `signedness`, `initialised`, ignoring.

Summary of what's now in [include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh):

- **`default_empty_key<Key>`** exposes two static constexpr members:
  - `memset_byte` — `0xFF` for unsigned, `0x80` for signed. This is what `HashTable`'s constructor will pass to `cudaMemset`.
  - `value` — the actual key value, computed by repeating `memset_byte` across `sizeof(Key)` bytes via a constexpr lambda.
- **8 namespace-level `static_assert`s** verify the resulting bit patterns: `0xFF…` for unsigned (compared directly), `0x80…` for signed (compared via `std::bit_cast` to unsigned of the same width).
- **Comment** explains the trade-off (every byte same → memset-able; 0x80 rather than 0xFF for signed → avoids the `-1` footgun) and notes the int8_t-is-exactly-INT_MIN coincidence.

Net effect: `HashTable`'s constructor stays a single `cudaMemset` call for all integral key types, no init kernel needed. The byte argument is `default_empty_key<Key>::memset_byte` rather than the literal `0xFF`.

The byte depends on the signedness of `Key`:\n//\n//   - Unsigned integer types: every byte is 0xFF, so `value` is the\n//     maximum representable value (e.g. 0xFFFFFFFFu for uint32_t).\n//   - Signed integer types: every byte is 0x80, so `value` has the high\n//     bit set in every byte (e.g. 0x80808080 = -2139062144 for int32_t).\n//     For int8_t this is exactly INT_MIN; for wider signed types it is\n//     close to but not exactly INT_MIN. We avoid 0xFF (which would give\n//     -1) because -1 is a very common legitimate key in user code.\n//\n// Compiles for: any integral type. Does not compile for floating-point\n// types, scoped enums, or user-defined structs — for those, supply an\n// explicit `EmptyKey` template argument to `HashTable` (and probably your\n// own `Hash` functor too).\n//\n// The trait exposes both `value` (the key value itself) and `memset_byte`\n// (the single byte that, repeated, produces that value). `HashTable`'s\n// constructor uses `memset_byte` to init the slot array with one\n// cudaMemset call.\ntemplate <class Key>\nstruct default_empty_key {\n    static_assert(std::is_integral_v<Key>,\n                  \"default_empty_key only supports integral types; supply an \"\n                  \"explicit EmptyKey for other key types\");\n\n    static constexpr unsigned char memset_byte =\n        std::is_unsigned_v<Key> ? 0xFFu : 0x80u;\n\n    static constexpr Key value = []() {\n        using U = std::make_unsigned_t<Key>;\n        U bits = 0;\n        for (std::size_t i = 0; i < sizeof(Key); ++i) {\n            bits = static_cast<U>(bits << 8) | static_cast<U>(memset_byte);\n        }\n        return static_cast<Key>(bits);\n    }();\n};\n\n// Compile-time verification for the standard integer types: every byte is\n// 0xFF for unsigned, 0x80 for signed.\nstatic_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint8_t >::value == 0xFFu);\nstatic_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint16_t>::value == 0xFFFFu);\nstatic_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint32_t>::value == 0xFFFFFFFFu);\nstatic_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint64_t>::value == 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFull);\nstatic_assert(std::bit_cast<std::uint8_t >(default_empty_key<std::int8_t >::value) == 0x80u);\nstatic_assert(std::bit_cast<std::uint16_t>(default_empty_key<std::int16_t>::value) == 0x8080u);\nstatic_assert(std::bit_cast<std::uint32_t>(default_empty_key<std::int32_t>::value) == 0x80808080u);\nstatic_assert(std::bit_cast<std::uint64_t>(default_empty_key<std::int64_t>::value) == 0x8080808080808080ull);",
```json
  "new_string": "// Reserved value signalling an empty slot. Users may not insert this key.\n//\n// Default is the all-bits-set representation of `Key` — every byte is 0xFF,\n// so `value` is the maximum representable value (e.g. 0xFFFFFFFFu for\n// uint32_t). The all-ones bit pattern lets `HashTable` initialise its\n// slot array with a single cudaMemset(0xFF, ...) at construction.\n//\n// Compiles for: unsigned integral types only. For signed integers,\n// floating-point types, scoped enums, or user-defined structs, supply an\n// explicit `EmptyKey` template argument to `HashTable` (and likely your\n// own `Hash` functor too). We deliberately don't provide a default for\n// signed types because no single repeated byte pattern gives a \"good\"\n// signed sentinel: 0xFF gives -1 (a common legitimate key); 0x80 gives\n// type-dependent values that are only INT_MIN for int8_t; INT_MIN itself\n// isn't a repeated-byte pattern. Leaving the choice to the user avoids\n// committing to any of those compromises.\n//\n// The trait exposes both `value` (the key value itself) and `memset_byte`\n// (the byte that, repeated, produces that value). `HashTable`'s\n// constructor uses `memset_byte` to init the slot array with one\n// cudaMemset call.\ntemplate <class Key>\nstruct default_empty_key {\n    static_assert(std::is_unsigned_v<Key>,\n                  \"default_empty_key only provides a default for unsigned \"\n                  \"integral types; supply an explicit EmptyKey for signed \"\n                  \"or other key types\");\n\n    static constexpr unsigned char memset_byte = 0xFFu;\n    static constexpr Key           value       = static_cast<Key>(~Key{});\n};\n\n// Compile-time verification for the supported integer types.\nstatic_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint8_t >::value == 0xFFu);\nstatic_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint16_t>::value == 0xFFFFu);\nstatic_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint32_t>::value == 0xFFFFFFFFu);\nstatic_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint64_t>::value == 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFull);"
}
```

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T19:48:00.239Z

Spell-check noise on `initialise`, `cuda`, `Memset`, ignoring.

Simplification done in [include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh):

- `default_empty_key` now `static_assert`s on `std::is_unsigned_v<Key>` and provides defaults only for unsigned integer types. `memset_byte = 0xFFu` is now a plain literal; `value = static_cast<Key>(~Key{})` is back to the simple form, no constexpr lambda.
- The 8 namespace-scope `static_assert`s become 4 (just the unsigned widths).
- Removed `<limits>` from includes — no longer needed.
- Comment rewritten to explain why we don't default for signed (no good repeated-byte sentinel exists), pointing users at the explicit `EmptyKey` override path.

Net: less code, fewer template branches, fewer asserts, no design choice forced on signed-key users.

```
95	// Primary template: dispatch on sizeof(Key). Defined only for 4- and 8-byte
96	// keys, matching the supported slot widths.
97	template <class Key, std::size_t = sizeof(Key)>
98	struct default_hash;
99	
100	template <class Key>
101	struct default_hash<Key, 4> {
102	    __host__ __device__ inline std::uint32_t operator()(Key key) const noexcept {
103	        return detail::fmix32(std::bit_cast<std::uint32_t>(key));
104	    }
105	};
106	
107	template <class Key>
108	struct default_hash<Key, 8> {
109	    __host__ __device__ inline std::uint64_t operator()(Key key) const noexcept {
110	        return detail::fmix64(std::bit_cast<std::uint64_t>(key));
111	    }
112	};
113	
114	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
115	// HashTable: host-side owner of the device-resident slot array.
116	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
117	
118	template <
119	    class Key,
120	    class Value,
121	    class Hash         = default_hash<Key>,
122	    Key   EmptyKey     = default_empty_key<Key>::value,
123	    int   CacheLineBytes = 128,
124	    int   WarpSize     = 32
125	>
126	class HashTable {
127	public:
128	    using key_type   = Key;
129	    using value_type = Value;
130	    using hasher     = Hash;
131	
132	    // Packed (key, value) slot. One Slot is the unit of an atomic CAS.
133	    struct Slot {
134	        Key   key;
135	        Value value;
136	    };
137	
138	    // ---- Architecture-derived constants ----
139	
140	    static constexpr Key empty_key        = EmptyKey;
141	    static constexpr int slot_bytes       = sizeof(Slot);
142	    static constexpr int cache_line_bytes = CacheLineBytes;
143	    static constexpr int warp_size        = WarpSize;
144	    static constexpr int bucket_size      = CacheLineBytes / slot_bytes;
145	    static constexpr int tile_size        = bucket_size;
146	    static constexpr int tiles_per_warp   = WarpSize / bucket_size;
147	
148	    // Slot / cache-line invariants.
149	    static_assert(slot_bytes == 8 || slot_bytes == 16,
150	                  "slot must be 8 or 16 bytes (a 64-bit or 128-bit packed "
151	                  "key+value pair)");
152	    // Temporary: v1 implements only 64-bit slots, which use a single
153	    // atomicCAS<uint64_t>. Remove this assertion once 128-bit slots are
154	    // supported (needed for 64-bit keys with 64-bit values, requiring
155	    // 128-bit CAS on sm_70+).
156	    static_assert(slot_bytes == 8,
157	                  "only 64-bit slots are currently supported");
158	    static_assert(slot_bytes <= CacheLineBytes,
159	                  "slot does not fit in a cache line");
160	    static_assert(CacheLineBytes % slot_bytes == 0,
161	                  "cache line must be a whole multiple of slot size");
162	
163	    // Tile / bucket invariants. tile_size == bucket_size by construction; the
164	    // cooperative_groups::thread_block_tile requirements drive these.
165	    static_assert(tile_size > 0,
166	                  "tile size must be positive");
167	    static_assert((tile_size & (tile_size - 1)) == 0,
168	                  "tile size must be a power of two "
169	                  "(required by cooperative_groups::tiled_partition)");
170	    static_assert(tile_size <= WarpSize,
171	                  "tile size must not exceed warp size");
172	    static_assert(WarpSize % bucket_size == 0,
173	                  "warp size must be a whole multiple of bucket size");
174	
175	    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
176	    // View: lightweight device-side handle. Trivially copyable; pass by value
177	    // into user kernels. Holds only a device pointer and a couple of integers.
178	    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
179	    class View {
180	    public:
181	        // Cooperative single-key insert.
182	        // Called by a tile of exactly tile_size threads in lock-step. Returns
183	        // true on success, false if the table is full (probe length cap hit).
184	        template <class Tile>
185	        __device__ bool insert(const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value);
186	
187	        // Cooperative single-key lookup.
188	        // Returns true and writes the stored value to `out_value` if the key
189	        // is present; returns false otherwise.
190	        template <class Tile>
191	        __device__ bool get(const Tile& tile, Key key, Value& out_value) const;
192	
193	        __host__ __device__ std::size_t capacity() const noexcept { return capacity_; }
194	
195	    private:
196	        friend class HashTable;
197	
198	        Slot*       slots_         = nullptr;
199	        std::size_t capacity_      = 0;   // power of two
200	        std::size_t capacity_mask_ = 0;   // capacity_ - 1, for modulo via AND
201	        Hash        hash_{};
202	    };
203	
204	    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
205	    // Host-side resource management.
206	    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
207	
208	    // Allocates a slot array on the current device sized for at least
209	    // `min_capacity` slots, rounded up to the next power of two, and
210	    // initialises every slot to (empty_key, _).
211	    explicit HashTable(std::size_t min_capacity);
212	
213	    ~HashTable();
214	
215	    HashTable(const HashTable&)            = delete;
216	    HashTable& operator=(const HashTable&) = delete;
217	    HashTable(HashTable&&) noexcept;
218	    HashTable& operator=(HashTable&&) noexcept;
219	
220	    // Returns a device-side handle for use inside user kernels.
221	    View view() const noexcept;
222	
223	    // Actual capacity (input capacity rounded up to a power of two).
224	    std::size_t capacity() const noexcept { return capacity_; }
```

</details>
