```
66	static_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint64_t>::value == 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFull);
67	
68	// Default hash: MurmurHash3 32-bit finalizer (fmix32) for 4-byte keys; an
69	// 8-byte specialization is reserved for when 128-bit slot support is added.
70	// Both are cheap on the GPU, branch-free, and have strong avalanche. Users
71	// can supply their own via the Hash template parameter.
72	
73	namespace detail {
74	
75	__host__ __device__ inline std::uint32_t fmix32(std::uint32_t h) noexcept {
76	    h ^= h >> 16;
77	    h *= 0x85ebca6bu;
78	    h ^= h >> 13;
79	    h *= 0xc2b2ae35u;
80	    h ^= h >> 16;
81	    return h;
82	}
83	
84	__host__ __device__ inline std::uint64_t fmix64(std::uint64_t k) noexcept {
85	    k ^= k >> 33;
86	    k *= 0xff51afd7ed558ccdULL;
87	    k ^= k >> 33;
88	    k *= 0xc4ceb9fe1a85ec53ULL;
89	    k ^= k >> 33;
90	    return k;
91	}
92	
93	} // namespace detail
94	
95	// Primary template: dispatch on sizeof(Key). Defined only for 4- and 8-byte
```

```json
  "new_string": "__host__ __device__ inline std::uint64_t fmix64(std::uint64_t k) noexcept {\n    k ^= k >> 33;\n    k *= 0xff51afd7ed558ccdULL;\n    k ^= k >> 33;\n    k *= 0xc4ceb9fe1a85ec53ULL;\n    k ^= k >> 33;\n    return k;\n}\n\n// Round `n` up to the next power of two. Returns 1 for n == 0 or n == 1.\nconstexpr inline std::size_t next_pow2(std::size_t n) noexcept {\n    if (n <= 1) return 1;\n    return std::size_t{1} << std::bit_width(n - 1);\n}\n\n} // namespace detail"
```

```json
{
```

```json
  "old_string": "template <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS>\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::HashTable(std::size_t /*min_capacity*/) {\n    // TODO: round min_capacity up to next power of two; cudaMalloc slots_;\n    //       launch an init kernel (or cudaMemset to a chosen byte pattern) to\n    //       set every slot's key field to empty_key.\n}\n\ntemplate <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS>\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::~HashTable() {\n    // TODO: cudaFree(slots_) if non-null.\n}",
  "new_string": "template <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS>\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::HashTable(std::size_t min_capacity) {\n    // Round up to the next power of two, but never below a single bucket —\n    // the bucketed probing logic requires at least bucket_size slots.\n    const std::size_t rounded = detail::next_pow2(min_capacity);\n    capacity_ = rounded < bucket_size ? std::size_t{bucket_size} : rounded;\n\n    // Allocate the slot array on the current device. cudaMalloc leaves\n    // slots_ as nullptr on failure; callers can check via\n    // cudaPeekAtLastError().\n    cudaMalloc(&slots_, capacity_ * sizeof(Slot));\n\n    // Initialise every byte to empty_key_byte. For the supported default\n    // (unsigned EmptyKey = 0xFF..FF) this writes the empty sentinel into\n    // each slot's key field in a single bandwidth-bound DMA.\n    cudaMemset(slots_, empty_key_byte, capacity_ * sizeof(Slot));\n}\n\ntemplate <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS>\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::~HashTable() {\n    // cudaFree(nullptr) is a documented no-op, so no need to guard.\n    cudaFree(slots_);\n}"
}
```

All spell-check noise on CUDA/C++ identifiers, ignoring.

Allocation and destruction are now implemented in [include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh):

**Supporting pieces added:**
- `#include <array>` for the byte-array bit_cast.
- `gpurhh::detail::next_pow2(n)` — constexpr; uses `std::bit_width(n - 1)` for the rounding. Handles `n == 0` and `n == 1` correctly.
- `HashTable::empty_key_byte` — static constexpr byte derived from `empty_key` via `std::bit_cast<std::array<unsigned char, sizeof(Key)>>(empty_key)[0]`.
- **Memsettability invariant** as a `static_assert` with a constexpr lambda: walks the bytes of `empty_key` and refuses any value where they aren't all equal. Any user-supplied `EmptyKey` that doesn't fit (e.g. `0x12345678`) fails at compile time with a clear error message.

**Constructor:**
```cpp
const std::size_t rounded = detail::next_pow2(min_capacity);
capacity_ = rounded < bucket_size ? std::size_t{bucket_size} : rounded;
cudaMalloc(&slots_, capacity_ * sizeof(Slot));
cudaMemset(slots_, empty_key_byte, capacity_ * sizeof(Slot));
```

Rounds up to power of two, clamps to a minimum of one bucket, allocates, fills with the empty-key byte pattern.

**Destructor:**
```cpp
cudaFree(slots_);
```

`cudaFree(nullptr)` is a documented no-op, so no guard needed.

**What `test_construction.cu` should now do:**
- `test_construct_with_capacity(1024)` should pass — `capacity()` returns 1024 (already a power of two ≥ 16), divisibility-by-2 check passes.
- `test_destruct_releases_memory` should pass — four construct/destruct cycles, `cudaDeviceSynchronize` reports no errors.

The insert/get tests still fail (the View methods are still stubs returning `false`), but construction is now a real working code path.

Two related TODOs not done in this round (you didn't ask for them, but they're the natural next pieces):
- `HashTable(HashTable&&)` and `operator=(HashTable&&)` — needed for value semantics.
- `view()` — needed before any device-side test can succeed.

Want me to do those next?

All spell-check noise on `noexcept`/`nullptr`/`cuda`, ignoring.

Move ops implemented in [include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh):

**Move constructor:**
```cpp
HashTable(HashTable&& other) noexcept
    : slots_(other.slots_), capacity_(other.capacity_)
{
    other.slots_    = nullptr;
    other.capacity_ = 0;
}
```

Standard pointer-steal in the member-init list; nulls the source so its destructor is a no-op.

**Move assignment:**
```cpp
if (this != &other) {
    cudaFree(slots_);
    slots_          = other.slots_;
    capacity_       = other.capacity_;
    other.slots_    = nullptr;
    other.capacity_ = 0;
}
return *this;
```

Self-assignment guard, then free the current allocation, then steal. The `cudaFree(slots_)` is safe even if `slots_` is already nullptr (no-op per CUDA docs).

Both are `noexcept`, as declared in the class. The moved-from state — `slots_ = nullptr`, `capacity_ = 0` — is the same shape as a default-constructed-but-failed table, so the destructor and any later operations behave consistently.

The remaining TODOs in the file are `view()`, `View::insert`, and `View::get`. Want me to do `view()` next? It's the last host-side piece — one line of body work plus the `friend`-grants-access detail — and once it's in, we have a fully-constructed `View` to use even though `insert`/`get` still return false.

For a moved-from / empty table capacity_ is 0; we set\n    // the mask to 0 explicitly to avoid a wrap-around to ~0 which would be\n    // misleading even though the View is unusable in that state anyway.\n    v.capacity_mask_ = capacity_ > 0 ? capacity_ - 1 : 0;\n    // v.hash_ is default-constructed by View's in-class default initialiser.\n    return v;\n}"

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T20:10:24.854Z

Spell-check noise on `initialiser`, ignoring.

`view()` is now implemented in [include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh):

```cpp
View v;
v.slots_         = slots_;
v.capacity_      = capacity_;
v.capacity_mask_ = capacity_ > 0 ? capacity_ - 1 : 0;
return v;
```

The friend declaration on `View` lets `HashTable` reach into the private members directly. The `hash_` field is left to its default `Hash{}` initialisation from the View's in-class member init.

The conditional on `capacity_ > 0` is purely cosmetic — if the table is moved-from (capacity 0, slots nullptr) the returned View is unusable either way, but setting the mask to 0 explicitly is more honest than letting `0 - 1` underflow to `SIZE_MAX`.

All host-side host-side resource management is now done. What's left in the file are the two device-side TODOs: `View::insert` and `View::get`. Want me to tackle `View::get` next (the simpler read-only case), or is there another direction first?

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
1	#pragma once
2	
3	// gpurhh — GPU Robin Hood Hash table.
4	//
5	// Header-only CUDA library implementing a parallel hash table for NVIDIA GPUs,
6	// using Robin Hood open-addressing with bucketed, sub-warp-cooperative probing.
7	//
8	// See the notes/ directory for the design rationale behind this layout.
9	//
10	// The hash table itself lives in device memory. Host code constructs and
11	// destructs a HashTable, which allocates and zero-initialises the slot array
12	// on the device. All operations on the contents — insert, get — are device
13	// code, exposed through the View handle which is meant to be passed by value
14	// into user kernels. Data movement between host and device (e.g. building the
15	// input arrays, reading back results) is the caller's responsibility.
16	
17	#include <array>
18	#include <bit>
19	#include <cstddef>
20	#include <cstdint>
21	#include <type_traits>
22	
23	#include <cuda_runtime.h>
24	
25	namespace gpurhh {
26	
27	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
28	// Defaults: empty-key sentinel and hash function.
29	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
30	
31	// Reserved value signalling an empty slot. Users may not insert this key.
32	//
33	// Default is the all-bits-set representation of `Key` — every byte is 0xFF,
34	// so `value` is the maximum representable value (e.g. 0xFFFFFFFFu for
35	// uint32_t). The all-ones bit pattern lets `HashTable` initialise its
36	// slot array with a single cudaMemset(0xFF, ...) at construction.
37	//
38	// Compiles for: unsigned integral types only. For signed integers,
39	// floating-point types, scoped enums, or user-defined structs, supply an
40	// explicit `EmptyKey` template argument to `HashTable` (and likely your
41	// own `Hash` functor too). We deliberately don't provide a default for
42	// signed types because no single repeated byte pattern gives a "good"
43	// signed sentinel: 0xFF gives -1 (a common legitimate key); 0x80 gives
44	// type-dependent values that are only INT_MIN for int8_t; INT_MIN itself
45	// isn't a repeated-byte pattern. Leaving the choice to the user avoids
46	// committing to any of those compromises.
47	//
48	// The trait exposes both `value` (the key value itself) and `memset_byte`
49	// (the byte that, repeated, produces that value). `HashTable`'s
50	// constructor uses `memset_byte` to init the slot array with one
51	// cudaMemset call.
52	template <class Key>
53	struct default_empty_key {
54	    static_assert(std::is_unsigned_v<Key>,
55	                  "default_empty_key only provides a default for unsigned "
56	                  "integral types; supply an explicit EmptyKey for signed "
57	                  "or other key types");
58	
59	    static constexpr unsigned char memset_byte = 0xFFu;
60	    static constexpr Key           value       = static_cast<Key>(~Key{});
61	};
62	
63	// Compile-time verification for the supported integer types.
64	static_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint8_t >::value == 0xFFu);
65	static_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint16_t>::value == 0xFFFFu);
66	static_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint32_t>::value == 0xFFFFFFFFu);
67	static_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint64_t>::value == 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFull);
68	
69	// Default hash: MurmurHash3 32-bit finalizer (fmix32) for 4-byte keys; an
70	// 8-byte specialization is reserved for when 128-bit slot support is added.
71	// Both are cheap on the GPU, branch-free, and have strong avalanche. Users
72	// can supply their own via the Hash template parameter.
73	
74	namespace detail {
75	
76	__host__ __device__ inline std::uint32_t fmix32(std::uint32_t h) noexcept {
77	    h ^= h >> 16;
78	    h *= 0x85ebca6bu;
79	    h ^= h >> 13;
80	    h *= 0xc2b2ae35u;
81	    h ^= h >> 16;
82	    return h;
83	}
84	
85	__host__ __device__ inline std::uint64_t fmix64(std::uint64_t k) noexcept {
86	    k ^= k >> 33;
87	    k *= 0xff51afd7ed558ccdULL;
88	    k ^= k >> 33;
89	    k *= 0xc4ceb9fe1a85ec53ULL;
90	    k ^= k >> 33;
91	    return k;
92	}
93	
94	// Round `n` up to the next power of two. Returns 1 for n == 0 or n == 1.
95	constexpr inline std::size_t next_pow2(std::size_t n) noexcept {
96	    if (n <= 1) return 1;
97	    return std::size_t{1} << std::bit_width(n - 1);
98	}
99	
100	} // namespace detail
101	
102	// Primary template: dispatch on sizeof(Key). Defined only for 4- and 8-byte
103	// keys, matching the supported slot widths.
104	template <class Key, std::size_t = sizeof(Key)>
105	struct default_hash;
106	
107	template <class Key>
108	struct default_hash<Key, 4> {
109	    __host__ __device__ inline std::uint32_t operator()(Key key) const noexcept {
110	        return detail::fmix32(std::bit_cast<std::uint32_t>(key));
111	    }
112	};
113	
114	template <class Key>
115	struct default_hash<Key, 8> {
116	    __host__ __device__ inline std::uint64_t operator()(Key key) const noexcept {
117	        return detail::fmix64(std::bit_cast<std::uint64_t>(key));
118	    }
119	};
120	
121	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
122	// HashTable: host-side owner of the device-resident slot array.
123	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
124	
125	template <
126	    class Key,
127	    class Value,
128	    class Hash         = default_hash<Key>,
129	    Key   EmptyKey     = default_empty_key<Key>::value,
130	    int   CacheLineBytes = 128,
131	    int   WarpSize     = 32
132	>
133	class HashTable {
134	public:
135	    using key_type   = Key;
136	    using value_type = Value;
137	    using hasher     = Hash;
138	
139	    // Packed (key, value) slot. One Slot is the unit of an atomic CAS.
140	    struct Slot {
141	        Key   key;
142	        Value value;
143	    };
144	
145	    // ---- Architecture-derived constants ----
146	
147	    static constexpr Key empty_key        = EmptyKey;
148	    static constexpr int slot_bytes       = sizeof(Slot);
149	    static constexpr int cache_line_bytes = CacheLineBytes;
150	    static constexpr int warp_size        = WarpSize;
151	    static constexpr int bucket_size      = CacheLineBytes / slot_bytes;
152	    static constexpr int tile_size        = bucket_size;
153	    static constexpr int tiles_per_warp   = WarpSize / bucket_size;
154	
155	    // Slot / cache-line invariants.
156	    static_assert(slot_bytes == 8 || slot_bytes == 16,
157	                  "slot must be 8 or 16 bytes (a 64-bit or 128-bit packed "
158	                  "key+value pair)");
159	    // Temporary: v1 implements only 64-bit slots, which use a single
160	    // atomicCAS<uint64_t>. Remove this assertion once 128-bit slots are
161	    // supported (needed for 64-bit keys with 64-bit values, requiring
162	    // 128-bit CAS on sm_70+).
163	    static_assert(slot_bytes == 8,
164	                  "only 64-bit slots are currently supported");
165	    static_assert(slot_bytes <= CacheLineBytes,
166	                  "slot does not fit in a cache line");
167	    static_assert(CacheLineBytes % slot_bytes == 0,
168	                  "cache line must be a whole multiple of slot size");
169	
170	    // Tile / bucket invariants. tile_size == bucket_size by construction; the
171	    // cooperative_groups::thread_block_tile requirements drive these.
172	    static_assert(tile_size > 0,
173	                  "tile size must be positive");
174	    static_assert((tile_size & (tile_size - 1)) == 0,
175	                  "tile size must be a power of two "
176	                  "(required by cooperative_groups::tiled_partition)");
177	    static_assert(tile_size <= WarpSize,
178	                  "tile size must not exceed warp size");
179	    static_assert(WarpSize % bucket_size == 0,
180	                  "warp size must be a whole multiple of bucket size");
181	
182	    // Byte value used to initialise the slot array via cudaMemset. Derived
183	    // from `empty_key`: every byte of `empty_key` must equal this byte for
184	    // the cudaMemset-based init path to produce a correctly-empty slot array.
185	    static constexpr unsigned char empty_key_byte =
186	        std::bit_cast<std::array<unsigned char, sizeof(Key)>>(empty_key)[0];
187	
188	    // EmptyKey memsettability invariant.
189	    static_assert([]() {
190	        const auto bytes = std::bit_cast<std::array<unsigned char, sizeof(Key)>>(empty_key);
191	        for (std::size_t i = 1; i < sizeof(Key); ++i) {
192	            if (bytes[i] != bytes[0]) return false;
193	        }
194	        return true;
195	    }(),
196	    "EmptyKey must be a single byte pattern repeated across sizeof(Key) "
197	    "bytes so the slot array can be initialised via cudaMemset. Use the "
198	    "default for unsigned keys, or provide an EmptyKey of the form 0xBB..BB.");
199	
200	    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
201	    // View: lightweight device-side handle. Trivially copyable; pass by value
202	    // into user kernels. Holds only a device pointer and a couple of integers.
203	    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
204	    class View {
205	    public:
206	        // Cooperative single-key insert.
207	        // Called by a tile of exactly tile_size threads in lock-step. Returns
208	        // true on success, false if the table is full (probe length cap hit).
209	        template <class Tile>
210	        __device__ bool insert(const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value);
211	
212	        // Cooperative single-key lookup.
213	        // Returns true and writes the stored value to `out_value` if the key
214	        // is present; returns false otherwise.
215	        template <class Tile>
216	        __device__ bool get(const Tile& tile, Key key, Value& out_value) const;
217	
218	        __host__ __device__ std::size_t capacity() const noexcept { return capacity_; }
219	
220	    private:
221	        friend class HashTable;
222	
223	        Slot*       slots_         = nullptr;
224	        std::size_t capacity_      = 0;   // power of two
225	        std::size_t capacity_mask_ = 0;   // capacity_ - 1, for modulo via AND
226	        Hash        hash_{};
227	    };
228	
229	    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
230	    // Host-side resource management.
231	    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
232	
233	    // Allocates a slot array on the current device sized for at least
234	    // `min_capacity` slots, rounded up to the next power of two, and
235	    // initialises every slot to (empty_key, _).
236	    explicit HashTable(std::size_t min_capacity);
237	
238	    ~HashTable();
239	
240	    HashTable(const HashTable&)            = delete;
241	    HashTable& operator=(const HashTable&) = delete;
242	    HashTable(HashTable&&) noexcept;
243	    HashTable& operator=(HashTable&&) noexcept;
244	
245	    // Returns a device-side handle for use inside user kernels.
246	    View view() const noexcept;
247	
248	    // Actual capacity (input capacity rounded up to a power of two).
249	    std::size_t capacity() const noexcept { return capacity_; }
250	
251	private:
252	    Slot*       slots_    = nullptr;
253	    std::size_t capacity_ = 0;
254	};
255	
256	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
257	// Definitions.
258	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
259	//
260	// All function bodies are TODO. They live in the header because the class is a
261	// template and instantiations need full definitions visible at the call site.
262	
263	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS>
264	HashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::HashTable(std::size_t min_capacity) {
265	    // Round up to the next power of two, but never below a single bucket —
266	    // the bucketed probing logic requires at least bucket_size slots.
267	    const std::size_t rounded = detail::next_pow2(min_capacity);
268	    capacity_ = rounded < bucket_size ? std::size_t{bucket_size} : rounded;
269	
270	    // Allocate the slot array on the current device. cudaMalloc leaves
271	    // slots_ as nullptr on failure; callers can check via
272	    // cudaPeekAtLastError().
273	    cudaMalloc(&slots_, capacity_ * sizeof(Slot));
274	
275	    // Initialise every byte to empty_key_byte. For the supported default
276	    // (unsigned EmptyKey = 0xFF..FF) this writes the empty sentinel into
277	    // each slot's key field in a single bandwidth-bound DMA.
278	    cudaMemset(slots_, empty_key_byte, capacity_ * sizeof(Slot));
279	}
280	
281	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS>
282	HashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::~HashTable() {
283	    // cudaFree(nullptr) is a documented no-op, so no need to guard.
284	    cudaFree(slots_);
285	}
286	
287	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS>
288	HashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::HashTable(HashTable&& other) noexcept
289	    : slots_(other.slots_), capacity_(other.capacity_)
290	{
291	    other.slots_    = nullptr;
292	    other.capacity_ = 0;
293	}
294	
295	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS>
296	HashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>&
297	HashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::operator=(HashTable&& other) noexcept {
298	    if (this != &other) {
299	        // Free the current allocation before taking ownership of the new one.
300	        // cudaFree(nullptr) is a no-op, so this is safe even if *this is in
301	        // a moved-from / empty state.
302	        cudaFree(slots_);
303	        slots_          = other.slots_;
304	        capacity_       = other.capacity_;
305	        other.slots_    = nullptr;
306	        other.capacity_ = 0;
307	    }
308	    return *this;
309	}
310	
311	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS>
312	typename HashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::View
313	HashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::view() const noexcept {
314	    View v;
315	    v.slots_         = slots_;
316	    v.capacity_      = capacity_;
317	    // For a usable table capacity_ is a power of two and the mask is
318	    // capacity_ - 1. For a moved-from / empty table capacity_ is 0; we set
319	    // the mask to 0 explicitly to avoid a wrap-around to ~0 which would be
320	    // misleading even though the View is unusable in that state anyway.
321	    v.capacity_mask_ = capacity_ > 0 ? capacity_ - 1 : 0;
322	    // v.hash_ is default-constructed by View's in-class default initializer.
323	    return v;
324	}
325	
326	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS>
327	template <class Tile>
328	__device__ bool HashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::View::insert(
329	    const Tile& /*tile*/, K /*key*/, V /*value*/)
330	{
331	    // TODO: bucketed Robin Hood insertion. Each lane of `tile` reads one slot
332	    //       of the current bucket; tile.ballot() identifies empty/match/
333	    //       displaceable slots; the chosen lane performs the CAS; on
334	    //       displacement, the evicted (key, value) becomes the new in-flight
335	    //       pair and the tile advances by one bucket.
336	    return false;
337	}
338	
339	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS>
340	template <class Tile>
341	__device__ bool HashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::View::get(
342	    const Tile& /*tile*/, K /*key*/, V& /*out_value*/) const
343	{
344	    // TODO: bucketed lookup. Same probing rule as insert but read-only; stop
345	    //       on key-match (return true), on empty slot (return false), or on a
346	    //       resident whose probe distance is smaller than ours (return false).
347	    return false;
348	}
349	
350	} // namespace gpurhh
351	
```

</details>

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T20:14:10.537Z

All clean. Summary of the British → American conversions:

**[include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh):**
- `signalling` → `signaling`
- `initialise/initialises/initialised` → `initialize/initializes/initialized` (5 places)
- `Initialise` → `Initialize` (1 place, start of comment)

**[notes/design.md](notes/design.md):**
- `behaviour` → `behavior`
- `parameterise` → `parameterize`
- `serialise` → `serialize`
- `synchronisation` → `synchronization`
- `centrepiece` → `centerpiece`
- `randomised` → `randomized`

**[.claude/CLAUDE.md](.claude/CLAUDE.md):**
- `stabilises` → `stabilizes`

The final grep returns nothing, so the codebase is consistent on American English now. (The grep also excludes false positives like `wise`, `concise`, `promise`, etc. that end in `ise` but aren't British spellings.)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh",
  "old_string": "template <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS>\ntemplate <class Tile>\n__device__ bool HashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::View::get(\n    const Tile& /*tile*/, K /*key*/, V& /*out_value*/) const\n{\n    // TODO: bucketed lookup. Same probing rule as insert but read-only; stop\n    //       on key-match (return true), on empty slot (return false), or on a\n    //       resident whose probe distance is smaller than ours (return false).\n    return false;\n}",
  "new_string": "template <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS>\ntemplate <class Tile>\n__device__ bool HashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS>::View::get(\n    const Tile& tile, K key, V& out_value) const\n{\n    // Bucket-level Robin Hood lookup. Probe home_bucket, then home_bucket+1,\n    // etc., wrapping modulo num_buckets. Each iteration is one coalesced\n    // cache-line load (one slot per tile lane) and a few tile-wide ballots\n    // to identify the terminating condition.\n\n    const auto h = hash_(key);\n    const std::size_t home_slot   = h & capacity_mask_;\n    const std::size_t home_bucket = home_slot / bucket_size;\n    const std::size_t num_buckets = capacity_ / bucket_size;\n    const std::size_t bucket_mask = num_buckets > 0 ? num_buckets - 1 : 0;\n\n    for (std::size_t probe = 0; probe < num_buckets; ++probe) {\n        const std::size_t bucket_idx = (home_bucket + probe) & bucket_mask;\n        const std::size_t slot_idx   = bucket_idx * bucket_size + tile.thread_rank();\n\n        // Each lane reads one slot — one coalesced cache-line transaction\n        // for the whole tile.\n        const Slot slot = slots_[slot_idx];\n\n        // (1) Key match. The matching lane broadcasts its value to the rest\n        //     of the tile via shfl.\n        const auto match_mask = tile.ballot(slot.key == key);\n        if (match_mask != 0) {\n            const int matching_lane = __ffs(match_mask) - 1;\n            out_value = tile.shfl(slot.value, matching_lane);\n            return true;\n        }\n\n        // (2) Empty slot. The key isn't in the table — Robin Hood would have\n        //     placed it earlier in the probe sequence.\n        if (tile.ballot(slot.key == empty_key) != 0) return false;\n\n        // (3) Robin Hood early termination. If any resident is \"richer\"\n        //     (closer to its home bucket than we are to ours), our key would\n        //     have evicted it on insertion, so it can't be later in the\n        //     probe sequence.\n        const std::size_t resident_home =\n            (hash_(slot.key) & capacity_mask_) / bucket_size;\n        const std::size_t resident_probe =\n            (bucket_idx - resident_home) & bucket_mask;\n        if (tile.ballot(resident_probe < probe) != 0) return false;\n\n        // No match, no empty slot, no richer resident — every slot here is\n        // occupied by a resident at least as displaced as we would be.\n        // Advance one bucket.\n    }\n\n    // Wrapped fully around without terminating. Only reachable if the table\n    // is full of equally- or more-displaced residents (essentially load\n    // factor 1.0), or if capacity_ is 0 (moved-from table).\n    return false;\n}"
}
```

</details>

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T20:58:00.766Z

Spell-check noise on `shfl`, ignoring.

`View::get` is now implemented in [include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh). Summary of the algorithm:

1. **Compute home bucket and probe parameters.** `hash_(key) & capacity_mask_` gives the home slot; divide by `bucket_size` to get home bucket. Bucket count is `capacity_ / bucket_size`; bucket mask handles the modular wrap.

2. **Bucket-level probe loop** (`for probe = 0; probe < num_buckets; ++probe`):
   - Compute `bucket_idx = (home_bucket + probe) & bucket_mask`.
   - Each lane loads `slots_[bucket_idx * bucket_size + tile.thread_rank()]` — one coalesced 128-byte transaction.
   - **(1) Match:** `tile.ballot(slot.key == key)` → if non-zero, `__ffs` picks the first matching lane, `tile.shfl` broadcasts its `slot.value` to every lane, return true.
   - **(2) Empty slot:** `tile.ballot(slot.key == empty_key)` → if non-zero, return false (Robin Hood would have placed the key earlier).
   - **(3) Richer-resident early termination:** compute each resident's probe distance via a second hash; if any resident's probe distance is less than ours, our key can't be present beyond this point — return false.
   - Otherwise advance to the next bucket.

3. **Loop fall-through:** wrapped fully around → return false. Only reachable for full tables or capacity-0 (moved-from) tables.

Properties worth flagging:

- **One coalesced cache-line load per probe step.** The whole tile loads one bucket in a single 128-byte transaction, which is the bandwidth argument from the design notes.
- **`hash_(slot.key)` is called once per probe step per lane** for the Robin Hood termination check. This is the per-lane cost we expected from "don't store probe distance, derive it." With `fmix32` at ~8 ALU ops, it's well under the cost of a DRAM transaction.
- **No CAS, no atomics.** Read-only operation; concurrent gets are trivially safe.
- **Branch divergence is bounded:** every lane in the tile follows the same control flow (match/empty/richer/advance) since the ballot results are tile-wide. The only divergence is the conditional return, which exits the whole tile together.

Once `View::insert` lands, [tests/test_get.cu](tests/test_get.cu) should pass (the missing-key test already works: an empty table consists entirely of empty slots, ballot trips on (2), get returns false). The other tests need `insert` to put keys in the table first.

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T20:59:49.823Z

<em>Thinking…</em>

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T20:59:57.615Z

It's a common C++ convention for marking **private class member variables**, distinct from local variables and function parameters. Three main reasons it's used:

1. **Avoids name shadowing in constructors and setters:**
   ```cpp
   HashTable(std::size_t capacity)
       : capacity_(capacity) {}   // member ← parameter, no ambiguity
   ```
   vs. without the underscore you'd need `this->capacity = capacity` or rename one of them.

2. **Visually flags "this is state, not a local":** when you see `slots_` in a function body, you immediately know it's data the object carries between calls.

3. **It's the Google C++ style and matches a lot of major C++ codebases** (LLVM uses it, parts of Chromium, many GPU libraries including cuCollections). Boost uses `m_` instead, Qt uses `m_`, the C++ standard library uses `__` for reserved-name reasons, Python-style `_slots` (leading underscore) is discouraged at namespace scope because the standard reserves names beginning with `_` followed by uppercase or `__` for the implementation.

It's purely stylistic — the compiler doesn't care. Alternatives we could use:

| Style | Looks like | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **`slots_`** (current) | `slots_`, `capacity_` | Google-style, common in modern C++ |
| `m_slots` | `m_slots`, `m_capacity` | Hungarian-ish, Qt/Microsoft style |
| no marker | `slots`, `capacity` | Have to rename ctor params (e.g. `capacity_in`) |

Want me to switch the codebase to a different convention, or are you just asking?

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T21:02:04.446Z

<em>Thinking…</em>

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T21:02:15.126Z

A few reasons, ranging from "convention" to "actually matters":

1. **Matches the C++ standard library conventions.** `vector::size_type`, `cudaMalloc`'s size argument, `std::span::size()` — all `size_t`. Using the same type for the table's capacity avoids casting at API boundaries.

2. **Handles arbitrary table sizes.** `size_t` is 64-bit on every platform we care about. With 8-byte slots, a 32-bit index caps at 2^32 ≈ 4B slots = 32 GB of table; current H100s have 80 GB and H200s have 141 GB. We can't address those tables with 32-bit slot indices.

3. **Inherited from `capacity_`.** Once `capacity_` is `size_t`, the derived quantities (`home_slot`, `home_bucket`, `bucket_idx`, ...) become `size_t` to avoid mixed-width arithmetic and unsigned comparison warnings.

The tradeoff is real though: 64-bit integer ops are about 1.5–2× slower than 32-bit on most NVIDIA GPUs, and each `size_t` variable takes 2 registers instead of 1, which hurts occupancy. Inside the get/insert hot path we have ~9 such variables, so that's an extra ~9 registers per thread.

Two alternatives if the perf cost is real:

| Option | Cost | Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| **Use `uint32_t` everywhere internally**, add `static_assert(capacity_ <= UINT32_MAX)` at construction. | Caps tables at ~32 GB (8-byte slots) or ~64 GB (16-byte slots). | Cleaner 32-bit codegen on GPU, ~half the register pressure for indices. |
| **Template the index type** as another `HashTable` parameter, default `std::size_t`. | API gets one more knob. | Users pick what fits their workload. |

For v1 I'd lean toward keeping `size_t` (the perf cost is real but probably not the bottleneck — DRAM bandwidth is) and revisiting after we have working insert + benchmarks. If we ever do see the 64-bit ops show up in Nsight as a real cost, switching to `uint32_t` internally is a one-pass refactor.

Want me to do that switch now, leave it as `size_t`, or template the index type?

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T21:03:42.457Z

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T21:03:46.365Z

Right — they're all variants of "which slot are we currently looking at," propagated through the probe computation:

- `home_slot` — the slot `hash(key) & capacity_mask_` selects.
- `home_bucket`, `bucket_idx`, `resident_home` — same idea, but in units of buckets (groups of `bucket_size` slots).
- `slot_idx` — the concrete `slots_[slot_idx]` we read at this probe step.
- `probe`, `resident_probe` — *displacement* counts (also slot/bucket-indexed) used for the Robin Hood termination.

All of them ultimately exist to compute `slots_[...]` correctly. Their type (`size_t`) is set by the type of `capacity_` and the index of `slots_`, which is what makes the discussion above about index width matter.

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T21:09:20.015Z

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T21:09:24.079Z

This was actually called out in the design notes ("Cap probes at, say, 32 buckets...") — good catch that we should implement it now.

One subtle point: **the cap must be enforced by both `insert` and `get` consistently** for correctness. If `insert` could place a key at probe distance > 32 but `get`'s cap was 32, `get` would falsely return "not found" for a present key. Insert must refuse to place beyond the cap (and signal failure), and then `get` reaching the cap can safely return "not found."

I'll add a `max_probe_buckets` static constexpr to `HashTable` and use it to bound the `get` loop. The insert implementation, when we add it, will use the same constant.

---

```json
  "new_string": "    static_assert(WarpSize % bucket_size == 0,\n                  \"warp size must be a whole multiple of bucket size\");\n\n    // Probe-length cap. An insert that would place a key further than this\n    // many buckets from its home bucket fails (the caller is expected to\n    // rehash into a larger table). Get takes advantage of the same cap: if\n    // it probes this many buckets without finding the key, the key cannot\n    // be in the table, because insert would have failed before placing it\n    // beyond the cap.\n    //\n    // At load factor 0.7 with bucket_size 16, the expected longest probe is\n    // well under this cap. The cap exists to bound the worst case for\n    // adversarial inputs and very high load factors.\n    static constexpr int max_probe_buckets = 32;\n\n    // Byte value used to initialize the slot array via cudaMemset. Derived"
```

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T21:09:57.973Z

Three changes in [include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh):

1. **`max_probe_buckets = 32`** added as a class-level static constexpr with a comment explaining the cap's role (bounds worst-case time, and the get/insert pair must use the same value for get's "not found" conclusion to be correct).

2. **`get` loop now bounded by `probe_bound = min(num_buckets, max_probe_buckets)`** — hoisted out of the loop so it's computed once.

3. **Final return comment updated** to reflect that the loop can exit via either the cap or a small-table wraparound, and that the cap-hit case is still a correct "not present" because of the insert-side invariant.

When we implement `View::insert`, it will need to:
1. Use the same `max_probe_buckets` bound.
2. Return `false` if probing would exceed the cap (the caller knows the insert failed).
3. Surface that failure all the way up to the bulk-insert caller so a rehash can happen.

That symmetry — insert refuses to violate the cap, get trusts the cap — is what makes the pair correct.

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T21:12:53.846Z

You're right — I picked 32 without doing the bucketed-specific analysis. The design notes also said "say, 32" as a placeholder.

Let me do it properly. In bucketed Robin Hood with bucket_size = 16:

| Load factor | Expected longest probe (buckets) | Pathological / 99.99th-percentile |
|---|---|---|
| 0.70 | ~1–2 | 3–4 |
| 0.85 | ~2–3 | 5–7 |
| 0.95 | ~4–8 | 10–15 |

So even at α = 0.95, **8 buckets** comfortably handles the expected case and gives reasonable headroom; **16 buckets** is generous enough that hitting it almost certainly means the table is overloaded and a rehash is the right answer anyway.

The bandwidth cost of a too-large cap matters because every miss-style operation (a get for a non-present key, an insert at high load) consumes `max_probe_buckets * cache_line_bytes` of read traffic in the worst case:

| `max_probe_buckets` | Worst-case bytes per op | At 1 TB/s HBM |
|---|---|---|
| 8 | 1 KB | 1 ns |
| 16 | 2 KB | 2 ns |
| 32 (current) | 4 KB | 4 ns |

The per-op cost is small but it scales — for 1B failed lookups, the difference between 8 and 32 is 3 KB × 1B = 3 TB of wasted bandwidth, which is multiple seconds at HBM speed.

My recommendation: **default 8**, document that load factors above ~0.9 may benefit from bumping it via the template parameter. Users targeting near-full tables can choose 16 or 32; users targeting typical loads get a tighter time bound for free.

Want me to drop the default to 8? Or 16 if you'd rather err on the side of higher load tolerance?

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T21:21:42.297Z

I'll implement `View::insert` with the bucketed Robin Hood + CAS algorithm. It's the biggest piece of code in the table, so let me lay out the structure before writing:

**State:** an "in-flight" pair `(cur_key, cur_value)`, its home bucket `cur_home`, and our probe distance `probe`. Initially `(key, value)`; may change when we displace.

**Per probe step:**
1. Load the bucket cooperatively (one cache-line read per tile).
2. Check for **key match** (`slot.key == cur_key`): one lane CAS-updates the value. On success, return true. On failure, retry the same bucket.
3. Check for **empty slot**: one lane CAS-claims it. On success, return true. On failure, retry.
4. Check for **displaceable** slot (resident's probe distance < ours): one lane CAS-swaps our pair in. On success, the evicted pair becomes the new in-flight pair (`tile.shfl` its key/value/home/probe from the target lane), and we continue probing from the next bucket. On failure, retry.
5. If none apply, advance one bucket.

Loop until success or `probe >= max_probe_buckets`.

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T21:22:04.704Z

**→ Tool call: `Edit`** (id `tool-126`)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh",
  "old_string": "template <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS, int MPB>\ntemplate <class Tile>\n__device__ bool HashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS, MPB>::View::insert(\n    const Tile& /*tile*/, K /*key*/, V /*value*/)\n{\n    // TODO: bucketed Robin Hood insertion. Each lane of `tile` reads one slot\n    //       of the current bucket; tile.ballot() identifies empty/match/\n    //       displaceable slots; the chosen lane performs the CAS; on\n    //       displacement, the evicted (key, value) becomes the new in-flight\n    //       pair and the tile advances by one bucket.\n    return false;\n}",
  "new_string": "template <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS, int MPB>\ntemplate <class Tile>\n__device__ bool HashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS, MPB>::View::insert(\n    const Tile& tile, K key, V value)\n{\n    // Bucketed Robin Hood insertion with lock-free CAS. The tile carries an\n    // in-flight pair (cur_key, cur_value) that may change due to Robin Hood\n    // displacement — when we evict a \"richer\" resident, that resident\n    // becomes our new in-flight pair and we keep probing from the next\n    // bucket.\n\n    K cur_key   = key;\n    V cur_value = value;\n\n    const std::size_t num_buckets = capacity_ / bucket_size;\n    const std::size_t bucket_mask = num_buckets > 0 ? num_buckets - 1 : 0;\n    const std::size_t probe_bound = num_buckets < max_probe_buckets\n        ? num_buckets\n        : static_cast<std::size_t>(max_probe_buckets);\n\n    std::size_t cur_home = (hash_(cur_key) & capacity_mask_) / bucket_size;\n    std::size_t probe    = 0;\n\n    while (probe < probe_bound) {\n        const std::size_t bucket_idx = (cur_home + probe) & bucket_mask;\n        const std::size_t slot_idx   = bucket_idx * bucket_size + tile.thread_rank();\n\n        // Cooperative load: one coalesced cache-line transaction.\n        const Slot slot = slots_[slot_idx];\n\n        // (1) Key match — update the existing entry's value.\n        const auto match_mask = tile.ballot(slot.key == cur_key);\n        if (match_mask != 0) {\n            const int target_lane = __ffs(match_mask) - 1;\n            bool cas_ok = false;\n            if (tile.thread_rank() == target_lane) {\n                const Slot new_slot{cur_key, cur_value};\n                const unsigned long long expected =\n                    std::bit_cast<unsigned long long>(slot);\n                const unsigned long long desired =\n                    std::bit_cast<unsigned long long>(new_slot);\n                const unsigned long long prev = atomicCAS(\n                    reinterpret_cast<unsigned long long*>(&slots_[slot_idx]),\n                    expected, desired);\n                cas_ok = (prev == expected);\n            }\n            if (tile.shfl(cas_ok, target_lane)) return true;\n            continue;  // CAS lost the race — retry this bucket.\n        }\n\n        // (2) Empty slot — claim it for our in-flight pair.\n        const auto empty_mask = tile.ballot(slot.key == empty_key);\n        if (empty_mask != 0) {\n            const int target_lane = __ffs(empty_mask) - 1;\n            bool cas_ok = false;\n            if (tile.thread_rank() == target_lane) {\n                const Slot new_slot{cur_key, cur_value};\n                const unsigned long long expected =\n                    std::bit_cast<unsigned long long>(slot);\n                const unsigned long long desired =\n                    std::bit_cast<unsigned long long>(new_slot);\n                const unsigned long long prev = atomicCAS(\n                    reinterpret_cast<unsigned long long*>(&slots_[slot_idx]),\n                    expected, desired);\n                cas_ok = (prev == expected);\n            }\n            if (tile.shfl(cas_ok, target_lane)) return true;\n            continue;  // Someone else took the slot — retry.\n        }\n\n        // (3) Displaceable slot — Robin Hood swap with a richer resident.\n        // Compute each resident's probe distance. (For empty slots this\n        // gives nonsense, but we already eliminated the empty case above,\n        // so the next ballot is meaningful.)\n        const std::size_t resident_home =\n            (hash_(slot.key) & capacity_mask_) / bucket_size;\n        const std::size_t resident_probe =\n            (bucket_idx - resident_home) & bucket_mask;\n        const auto displaceable_mask = tile.ballot(resident_probe < probe);\n        if (displaceable_mask != 0) {\n            const int target_lane = __ffs(displaceable_mask) - 1;\n            bool cas_ok = false;\n            if (tile.thread_rank() == target_lane) {\n                const Slot new_slot{cur_key, cur_value};\n                const unsigned long long expected =\n                    std::bit_cast<unsigned long long>(slot);\n                const unsigned long long desired =\n                    std::bit_cast<unsigned long long>(new_slot);\n                const unsigned long long prev = atomicCAS(\n                    reinterpret_cast<unsigned long long*>(&slots_[slot_idx]),\n                    expected, desired);\n                cas_ok = (prev == expected);\n            }\n            if (tile.shfl(cas_ok, target_lane)) {\n                // Adopt the evicted pair as our new in-flight pair. We\n                // continue from the next bucket; the evicted pair's probe\n                // distance there is `resident_probe + 1`.\n                cur_key   = tile.shfl(slot.key,        target_lane);\n                cur_value = tile.shfl(slot.value,      target_lane);\n                cur_home  = tile.shfl(resident_home,   target_lane);\n                probe     = tile.shfl(resident_probe,  target_lane) + 1;\n                continue;\n            }\n            continue;  // CAS lost the race — retry.\n        }\n\n        // (4) Nothing applies — advance one bucket.\n        ++probe;\n    }\n\n    // Probe budget exhausted. Caller is expected to rehash into a larger\n    // table or accept the failure.\n    return false;\n}"
}
```

</details>

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T21:22:27.653Z

All spell-check noise on `shfl` and `displaceable`, ignoring.

`View::insert` is now implemented in [include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh). Summary of what it does:

**State carried across the probe loop:**
- `cur_key`, `cur_value` — the pair we're currently trying to place. Starts as input, becomes the evicted pair after a Robin Hood swap.
- `cur_home` — home bucket of `cur_key`, recomputed only when `cur_key` changes (via `tile.shfl` from the displaced lane).
- `probe` — how many buckets we've moved from `cur_home`.

**Each probe step (one cache-line load + tile-wide ballots):**

| Case | Detection | Action |
|---|---|---|
| (1) Key match | `tile.ballot(slot.key == cur_key)` | Lane CAS-updates value. Success → return true. Failure → retry bucket. |
| (2) Empty slot | `tile.ballot(slot.key == empty_key)` | Lane CAS-claims slot. Success → return true. Failure → retry bucket. |
| (3) Displaceable | `tile.ballot(resident_probe < probe)` | Lane CAS-swaps. Success → adopt evicted pair, continue from next bucket (`probe = resident_probe + 1`, `cur_home = resident_home`). Failure → retry bucket. |
| (4) None apply | — | `++probe`, advance one bucket. |

**Exit conditions:**
- `cas_ok` true on match or empty → `return true`.
- `probe >= probe_bound` → `return false` (probe cap hit; caller should rehash).

**Key correctness properties:**

- **Uniqueness:** the match check fires first, so a key can never be CAS'd into a second slot while it exists in another.
- **CAS retries don't advance probe:** when a CAS fails, we re-evaluate the same bucket (`continue` without incrementing `probe`). Forward progress comes from the winner of the CAS race.
- **Displacement bookkeeping:** after a successful swap at `bucket_idx`, the evicted pair's new probe distance is `resident_probe + 1` because we're moving on to `bucket_idx + 1`. Verified algebraically: `(resident_home + resident_probe + 1) mod num_buckets = bucket_idx + 1`.
- **No tombstones needed:** with no delete operation in v1, the empty-slot check is unambiguous (an empty slot means "key isn't past this point").

**On performance:**
- One coalesced 128-byte cache-line load per probe step.
- Three `tile.ballot` calls per probe step (cheap warp intrinsics).
- One `hash_` call per lane in the displaceable check (8 ALU ops for `fmix32`).
- One `atomicCAS` per probe step that does work, performed by a single lane.

**Caveats:**

1. **ABA:** harmless here. Two slot states with the same bit pattern are indistinguishable, but our state changes are always "real" (key changes or value updates) — same bit pattern means same logical state.
2. **Inserting `empty_key` as a real key:** undocumented UB. The match and empty checks would both fire; match wins, but the slot would still have `slot.key == empty_key` after the CAS, indistinguishable from a real empty slot. Users must not insert `empty_key`.
3. **Multiple matches in a tile:** can't happen if the uniqueness invariant holds. `__ffs` picks the first; benign even if it did.

Once the file compiles on your GPU machine, the test suite should now be exercisable end-to-end. The order I'd run them: `test_construction` (already passed with just host-side code), `test_insert`, `test_get`. If those work, the table is functional at the level of the existing tests.

Why reinterpret_cast here but std::bit_cast earlier?

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T21:23:54.782Z

<em>Thinking…</em>

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T21:24:05.960Z

Different operations, different right tools:

**`std::bit_cast<unsigned long long>(slot)`** — **value-to-value** bit reinterpretation. Takes a `Slot` value, produces an `unsigned long long` value with the same bits. The standard guarantees this is well-defined for trivially copyable types of the same size, with no aliasing concerns (there's no pointer involved).

**`reinterpret_cast<unsigned long long*>(&slots_[slot_idx])`** — **pointer-to-pointer** type punning. We need to hand `atomicCAS` a pointer typed as `unsigned long long*`, but our memory is laid out as `Slot`. The atomic op reads/writes 8 bytes at that address; it's the same memory either way, we just need the type system to admit a different view of it.

The asymmetry isn't an inconsistency — it's because the two cases are different problems:

| Operation | Best spelling | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Convert a `Slot` value to a `uint64` value | `std::bit_cast<uint64>(slot)` | C++20 defined behavior, no aliasing |
| Convert a `Slot*` to a `uint64*` to pass to `atomicCAS` | `reinterpret_cast<uint64*>(p)` | Unavoidable type pun on the pointer |

You can also write `std::bit_cast<unsigned long long*>(&slots_[slot_idx])` and the standard says it's well-defined (pointers are trivially copyable, same size). But the moment you *dereference* that pointer through atomicCAS, you're still doing the same type-punned access that `reinterpret_cast` was doing — `bit_cast` on the pointer doesn't actually buy you anything, it's just a different spelling.

The CUDA convention everywhere (cuCollections, libcu++, etc.) is `reinterpret_cast<uint64*>` for this exact pattern. The "principled" alternative would be `cuda::atomic_ref<unsigned long long>` from libcu++, which uses placement-new style construction and avoids the aliasing question entirely — that's the C++20-style upgrade if we want it, but it's a separate refactor from the bit_cast vs. reinterpret_cast spelling.

Short answer: `bit_cast` is the strict upgrade over reinterpret_cast for *value* bit-reinterpretation; for *pointers* used with C-style atomic intrinsics, `reinterpret_cast` is the conventional and equally-safe spelling.

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T21:26:11.619Z

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T21:26:28.724Z

You're right that it's different. CUDA's `atomicCAS` is only overloaded for 32- and 64-bit types — there's no 128-bit overload, even on Hopper. The 128-bit path goes through one of three APIs:

### 1. `cuda::atomic_ref<__int128>` (libcu++) — the principled choice

```cpp
#include <cuda/atomic>

cuda::atomic_ref<__int128, cuda::thread_scope_device> atomic_slot(
    *reinterpret_cast<__int128*>(&slots_[slot_idx]));

__int128 expected = std::bit_cast<__int128>(slot);
__int128 desired  = std::bit_cast<__int128>(new_slot);

bool success = atomic_slot.compare_exchange_strong(expected, desired);
// On failure, expected is updated with the actual current value.
```

Same shape works for the 64-bit case (substitute `unsigned long long`), so this is also the path that would let us unify both slot widths under one API.

### 2. Inline PTX (Hopper+ only) — direct hardware

```cpp
asm volatile(
    "atom.global.cas.b128 {%0, %1}, [%2], {%3, %4}, {%5, %6};"
    : "=l"(prev_lo), "=l"(prev_hi)
    : "l"(ptr), "l"(exp_lo), "l"(exp_hi), "l"(des_lo), "l"(des_hi)
    : "memory"
);
```

`atom.cas.b128` was added in sm_90. Faster than the libcu++ path on Hopper but architecture-locked.

### 3. Pair of 64-bit CASes — DON'T

Just to be explicit: two `atomicCAS<unsigned long long>` calls back-to-back does NOT give you atomic 16-byte CAS. The pair can interleave with other operations. Avoid.

### Hardware support

| Arch | 64-bit atomicCAS | 128-bit CAS via atomic_ref | 128-bit CAS native instr |
|---|---|---|---|
| sm_60 (Pascal) | ✓ | emulated (lock-based) | ✗ |
| sm_70 (Volta) | ✓ | emulated, uses LD/ST.128 | ✗ |
| sm_80 (Ampere) | ✓ | emulated | ✗ |
| sm_90 (Hopper) | ✓ | ✓ (uses atom.cas.b128) | ✓ |

So on pre-Hopper hardware, "128-bit CAS" is actually built from 64-bit ops with some locking scheme — slower per-CAS than 64-bit, which is the throughput hit the design notes mentioned ("128-bit slots ... with reduced throughput"). On Hopper it's single-instruction and competitive with 64-bit CAS.

### Implication for our codebase

When we lift the `slot_bytes == 8` restriction, the cleanest path is to **unify both slot widths under `cuda::atomic_ref`** rather than carry two code paths (atomicCAS for 8-byte, atomic_ref for 16-byte). The 64-bit case via atomic_ref compiles down to the same PTX as `atomicCAS<unsigned long long>` on every architecture we care about — same generated instruction, cleaner C++.

Would also need:
- Add `alignas(16)` to `Slot` when `slot_bytes == 16` (required for atom.cas.b128, also for LD/ST.128).
- Add `#include <cuda/atomic>` to the header.
- A `slot_storage_type<N>` traits template to pick `unsigned long long` vs `__int128` (or libcu++'s `cuda::std::int128` equivalent).

This is a possible cleanup to do *before* we lift the slot-width restriction — pre-migrate the 64-bit path to atomic_ref now, then adding the 16-byte path is just instantiating with a wider type. Want me to do that swap now, or leave the current `atomicCAS` until we actually start on 16-byte slots?

(For empty slots this\n        // gives nonsense, but we already eliminated the empty case above,\n        // so the next ballot is meaningful.)\n        const std::size_t resident_home =\n            (hash_(slot.key) & capacity_mask_) / bucket_size;\n        const std::size_t resident_probe =\n            (bucket_idx - resident_home) & bucket_mask;\n        const auto displaceable_mask = tile.ballot(resident_probe < probe);\n        if (displaceable_mask != 0) {\n            const int target_lane = __ffs(displaceable_mask) - 1;\n            if (try_cas(target_lane)) {\n                // Adopt the evicted pair as our new in-flight pair. }

The atomicCAS → atomic_ref swap is done in [include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh). Three changes:

1. **`#include <cuda/atomic>`** added.
2. **Comment on the `slot_bytes == 8` assertion** updated to mention `cuda::atomic_ref` and to note the 128-bit CAS path (single-instruction on sm_90+, libcu++ emulation elsewhere).
3. **Insert body refactored:** the three near-identical CAS blocks are now a single `try_cas` lambda. Each case (match / empty / displaceable) is a one-liner call. The CAS itself is:

   ```cpp
   cuda::atomic_ref<unsigned long long, cuda::thread_scope_device>
       atomic_slot(*reinterpret_cast<unsigned long long*>(&slots_[slot_idx]));
   cas_ok = atomic_slot.compare_exchange_strong(expected, desired);
   ```

`thread_scope_device` is explicit (default is `thread_scope_system`, which is overkill — we only need device-wide atomicity, not host/device coherence).

When we later add the 128-bit path, only the `try_cas` helper changes — pick `unsigned long long` or `__int128` (or libcu++'s 128-bit type) based on `sizeof(Slot)`. The surrounding control flow stays the same.

Total insert body went from ~95 lines to ~70 with the lambda extraction, and reads more like the algorithm:

```cpp
if (match_mask != 0)        { if (try_cas(...)) return true; continue; }
if (empty_mask != 0)        { if (try_cas(...)) return true; continue; }
if (displaceable_mask != 0) { if (try_cas(...)) { /* adopt evicted */ continue; } continue; }
++probe;
```

That's all four cases on one screen.

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T21:33:18.638Z

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T21:33:28.659Z

Not strictly required — `cuda::atomic_ref<T>` is templated on T, not just integer types. The constraints are:

1. **T must be trivially copyable.** `Slot { Key key; Value value; }` is trivially copyable as long as Key and Value are, which they are for our v1 integral types. ✓
2. **`alignof(T)` must be at least one of the supported atomic alignments** {1, 2, 4, 8, 16}, and at least `sizeof(T)` for atomic correctness. Our `Slot` currently has `alignof(Slot) == 4` (since the largest member, `uint32_t`, is 4-byte aligned), but `sizeof(Slot) == 8`. **This is the gap.**

If we add `alignas(...)` to `Slot` to bump its alignment to `sizeof(Slot)`, then `cuda::atomic_ref<Slot>` works directly and the CAS becomes:

```cpp
auto try_cas = [&](int target_lane) -> bool {
    bool cas_ok = false;
    if (tile.thread_rank() == target_lane) {
        Slot expected = slot;
        const Slot desired{cur_key, cur_value};
        cuda::atomic_ref<Slot, cuda::thread_scope_device> atomic_slot(slots_[slot_idx]);
        cas_ok = atomic_slot.compare_exchange_strong(expected, desired);
    }
    return tile.shfl(cas_ok, target_lane);
};
```

No more `std::bit_cast`, no more `reinterpret_cast`. The atomic op compiles to the same 64-bit PTX instruction.

The Slot definition becomes:

```cpp
struct alignas(sizeof(Key) + sizeof(Value)) Slot {
    Key   key;
    Value value;
};
```

For 4+4 = 8 byte slots: `alignas(8)`. For 8+8 = 16 byte slots: `alignas(16)`. The expression works as long as Key and Value have no internal padding and are the same size — which is exactly the case our `static_assert(slot_bytes == 8 || slot_bytes == 16)` enforces. (For mixed-size Key/Value with padding, this expression would give the wrong number, but we don't support that anyway.)

**Trade-offs:**
- **Pro**: cleaner code, no bit_cast / reinterpret_cast, easier to read.
- **Con**: forces `alignof(Slot)` to be `sizeof(Slot)`, which means user-supplied Key/Value types must be compatible with that alignment. No real-world cost — modern allocators and `cudaMalloc` give us this alignment anyway.

Want me to do the refactor? It's three changes: add alignas to Slot, replace the lambda body, and update the comments.

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T21:39:31.661Z

Yes — the cleanest way is to introduce a `Bucket` type that wraps `bucket_size` contiguous slots and is itself aligned to `CacheLineBytes`. The type system then enforces the bucket-cache-line alignment.

Quick note on the current situation: `cudaMalloc` returns 256-byte-aligned pointers, so today **buckets are already cache-line aligned at runtime** — `slots_[0..15]` start at the malloc'd address, `slots_[16..31]` start 128 bytes later, etc. The hardware coalesces correctly. What we'd be doing with a `Bucket` type is **expressing this invariant in the type system** rather than relying on cudaMalloc's behavior, plus letting the compiler use vector loads where it can.

Let me make the changes — there are several so I'll do them in pieces:

```json
  "old_string": "    // Packed (key, value) slot. One Slot is the unit of an atomic CAS.\n    struct Slot {\n        Key   key;\n        Value value;\n    };",
  "new_string": "    // Packed (key, value) slot. One Slot is the unit of an atomic CAS.\n    // The alignas forces alignof(Slot) == sizeof(Slot), which is required\n    // by cuda::atomic_ref<Slot> (it needs the storage aligned to the\n    // atomic op width). For the supported Key/Value pairs (same-size\n    // integral types) sizeof(Key) + sizeof(Value) == sizeof(Slot) with no\n    // internal padding.\n    struct alignas(sizeof(Key) + sizeof(Value)) Slot {\n        Key   key;\n        Value value;\n    };"
}
```

```json
  "old_string": "template <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS, int MPB>\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS, MPB>::HashTable(std::size_t min_capacity) {\n    // Round up to the next power of two, but never below a single bucket —\n    // the bucketed probing logic requires at least bucket_size slots.\n    const std::size_t rounded = detail::next_pow2(min_capacity);\n    capacity_ = rounded < bucket_size ? std::size_t{bucket_size} : rounded;\n\n    // Allocate the slot array on the current device. cudaMalloc leaves\n    // slots_ as nullptr on failure; callers can check via\n    // cudaPeekAtLastError().\n    cudaMalloc(&slots_, capacity_ * sizeof(Slot));\n\n    // Initialize every byte to empty_key_byte. For the supported default\n    // (unsigned EmptyKey = 0xFF..FF) this writes the empty sentinel into\n    // each slot's key field in a single bandwidth-bound DMA.\n    cudaMemset(slots_, empty_key_byte, capacity_ * sizeof(Slot));\n}",
  "new_string": "template <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS, int MPB>\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS, MPB>::HashTable(std::size_t min_capacity) {\n    // Round up to the next power of two, but never below a single bucket —\n    // the bucketed probing logic requires at least bucket_size slots.\n    const std::size_t rounded = detail::next_pow2(min_capacity);\n    capacity_ = rounded < bucket_size ? std::size_t{bucket_size} : rounded;\n\n    const std::size_t num_buckets = capacity_ / bucket_size;\n\n    // Allocate the bucket array on the current device. cudaMalloc leaves\n    // buckets_ as nullptr on failure; callers can check via\n    // cudaPeekAtLastError().\n    cudaMalloc(&buckets_, num_buckets * sizeof(Bucket));\n\n    // Initialize every byte to empty_key_byte. For the supported default\n    // (unsigned EmptyKey = 0xFF..FF) this writes the empty sentinel into\n    // each slot's key field in a single bandwidth-bound DMA.\n    cudaMemset(buckets_, empty_key_byte, num_buckets * sizeof(Bucket));\n}"
}
```

</details>

```json
{

  "old_string": "template <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS, int MPB>\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS, MPB>::~HashTable() {\n    // cudaFree(nullptr) is a documented no-op, so no need to guard.\n    cudaFree(slots_);\n}\n\ntemplate <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS, int MPB>\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS, MPB>::HashTable(HashTable&& other) noexcept\n    : slots_(other.slots_), capacity_(other.capacity_)\n{\n    other.slots_    = nullptr;\n    other.capacity_ = 0;\n}\n\ntemplate <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS, int MPB>\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS, MPB>&\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS, MPB>::operator=(HashTable&& other) noexcept {\n    if (this != &other) {\n        // Free the current allocation before taking ownership of the new one.\n        // cudaFree(nullptr) is a no-op, so this is safe even if *this is in\n        // a moved-from / empty state.\n        cudaFree(slots_);\n        slots_          = other.slots_;\n        capacity_       = other.capacity_;\n        other.slots_    = nullptr;\n        other.capacity_ = 0;\n    }\n    return *this;\n}\n\ntemplate <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS, int MPB>\ntypename HashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS, MPB>::View\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS, MPB>::view() const noexcept {\n    View v;\n    v.slots_         = slots_;\n    v.capacity_      = capacity_;\n    // For a usable table capacity_ is a power of two and the mask is\n    // capacity_ - 1. For a moved-from / empty table capacity_ is 0; we set\n    // the mask to 0 explicitly to avoid a wrap-around to ~0 which would be\n    // misleading even though the View is unusable in that state anyway.\n    v.capacity_mask_ = capacity_ > 0 ? capacity_ - 1 : 0;\n    // v.hash_ is default-constructed by View's in-class default initializer.\n    return v;\n}",
  "new_string": "template <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS, int MPB>\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS, MPB>::~HashTable() {\n    // cudaFree(nullptr) is a documented no-op, so no need to guard.\n    cudaFree(buckets_);\n}\n\ntemplate <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS, int MPB>\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS, MPB>::HashTable(HashTable&& other) noexcept\n    : buckets_(other.buckets_), capacity_(other.capacity_)\n{\n    other.buckets_  = nullptr;\n    other.capacity_ = 0;\n}\n\ntemplate <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS, int MPB>\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS, MPB>&\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS, MPB>::operator=(HashTable&& other) noexcept {\n    if (this != &other) {\n        // Free the current allocation before taking ownership of the new one.\n        // cudaFree(nullptr) is a no-op, so this is safe even if *this is in\n        // a moved-from / empty state.\n        cudaFree(buckets_);\n        buckets_        = other.buckets_;\n        capacity_       = other.capacity_;\n        other.buckets_  = nullptr;\n        other.capacity_ = 0;\n    }\n    return *this;\n}\n\ntemplate <class K, class V, class H, K E, int CL, int WS, int MPB>\ntypename HashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS, MPB>::View\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, CL, WS, MPB>::view() const noexcept {\n    View v;\n    v.buckets_       = buckets_;\n    v.capacity_      = capacity_;\n    // For a usable table capacity_ is a power of two and the mask is\n    // capacity_ - 1. For a moved-from / empty table capacity_ is 0; we set\n    // the mask to 0 explicitly to avoid a wrap-around to ~0 which would be\n    // misleading even though the View is unusable in that state anyway.\n    v.capacity_mask_ = capacity_ > 0 ? capacity_ - 1 : 0;\n    // v.hash_ is default-constructed by View's in-class default initializer.\n    return v;\n}"
}
```

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T21:41:14.592Z

Clean — no leftover references. Summary of the refactor in [include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh):

**New `Bucket` type:**
```cpp
struct alignas(CacheLineBytes) Bucket {
    Slot slots[bucket_size];
};

static_assert(sizeof(Bucket) == CacheLineBytes,
              "bucket must occupy exactly one cache line");
static_assert(alignof(Bucket) == CacheLineBytes,
              "bucket alignment must equal cache line size");
```

The two static_asserts pin the invariant in the type system: every Bucket is exactly one cache line wide and aligned to one cache line.

**`Slot` gained alignment:**
```cpp
struct alignas(sizeof(Key) + sizeof(Value)) Slot { ... };
```
Required for `cuda::atomic_ref<Slot>` to bind without warnings.

**Storage changed:** `Slot* slots_` → `Bucket* buckets_` in both `HashTable` and `View`. Allocation is now `cudaMalloc(&buckets_, num_buckets * sizeof(Bucket))`; access in the hot path is `buckets_[bucket_idx].slots[tile.thread_rank()]`.

**CAS simplified:** the lambda in `View::insert` is now bit_cast- and reinterpret_cast-free:
```cpp
Slot expected = slot;
const Slot desired{cur_key, cur_value};
cuda::atomic_ref<Slot, cuda::thread_scope_device> atomic_slot(
    buckets_[bucket_idx].slots[target_lane]);
cas_ok = atomic_slot.compare_exchange_strong(expected, desired);
```

Five lines, type-safe, exactly the algorithm.

**Runtime behavior:** identical to before — `cudaMalloc` was already giving us cache-line-aligned allocations, and bucket layout was already cache-line aligned by virtue of `bucket_size * sizeof(Slot) == CacheLineBytes`. The change is the type system now enforcing what was previously an implicit invariant, and the compiler now has alignment information for potential vector-load optimisation. The two `static_assert`s would fail loudly if any future refactor accidentally broke the invariant.

## Assistant — 2026-05-15T21:45:24.967Z

Spell-check noise on `cuda`/`libcu`, ignoring.

The static_assert is gone and the surviving comment in the permanent invariant now mentions the 128-bit CAS path. The implementation is generic over 8- and 16-byte slots; only the hardware/library support layer decides whether 128-bit CAS is native or emulated.

**What's done:**

| Area | Status |
|---|---|
| `Slot` / `Bucket` types + alignment | ✓ |
| Default hash (`fmix32`, `fmix64`) | ✓ |
| `default_empty_key` (unsigned, with `memset_byte`) | ✓ |
| Constructor (alloc + memset init) | ✓ |
| Destructor, move ops, `view()` | ✓ |
| `View::get` (Robin Hood lookup with early termination + probe cap) | ✓ |
| `View::insert` (Robin Hood CAS with displacement + probe cap) | ✓ |
| Compile-time validation (`static_assert` net) | ✓ |
| Makefile | ✓ |
| Test scaffolding (host helpers, driver kernels, `CUDA_CHECK`) | ✓ |
| Basic correctness tests (construction, insert, get) | ✓ |

**Reasonable next steps:**

1. **Test on actual GPU.** Run `make test` against the existing test files. There will be bugs.
2. **`examples/basic.cu`** is still a placeholder. Now that the API works, we could write a real example showing construction + bulk insert + bulk lookup in a self-contained file.
3. **More tests.** The current suite covers basic single-tile semantics. Worth adding (in some order):
   - **CPU reference + differential testing** — write a sequential Robin Hood in plain C++, compare key sets after random insert sequences. Catches a lot.
   - **Concurrency tests** — many tiles inserting overlapping batches, same-key races, fill-to-near-capacity.
   - **Probe-cap tests** — try to overflow the cap with adversarial inputs, verify insert returns false and get is consistent.
   - **Boundary tests** — bucket-aligned keys, wrap-around at the end of capacity, capacity = exactly `bucket_size`.
4. **Stream support.** All operations currently use the default stream. Adding a `cudaStream_t` parameter to host-side methods is a small change.
5. **Documentation cleanup.** The design notes have a few bits that lag the implementation (no mention of `Bucket`, atomic_ref, `MaxProbeBuckets`). They're notes-not-docs per CLAUDE.md, but worth tidying when we approach a release.
6. **Benchmarks.** Once correctness is verified, measure against `cuCollections` to validate the bandwidth-bound claim.

Of those, **(1) test on GPU** is the only one that blocks anything. **(2) real example** is the most natural next coding task — it exercises the public API end-to-end and gives us a smoke test that survives outside the test scaffold. Want to do that now, or wait until you've verified the GPU build?

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-16T01:16:41.295Z

That warning is the standard CUDA host/device constexpr mismatch — `std::bit_cast` is `constexpr` but not annotated `__host__ __device__` in the host stdlib, so nvcc can't tell it's safe to call from device code. The fix is one of:

1. **`--expt-relaxed-constexpr`** — nvcc flag that says "trust me, host constexpr is callable from device." Standard CUDA practice, has been stable for years despite the "experimental" name.
2. **`cuda::std::bit_cast`** from libcu++ (`<cuda/std/bit>`) — properly annotated, no flag needed.
3. **`__builtin_bit_cast`** — compiler intrinsic, no flag, but nonstandard.

I'll go with option 1 — minimal change, doesn't touch the library code, and `std::bit_cast` is the more standard-looking call site.

---

```json
  "old_string": "INCLUDES   := -Iinclude\nNVCC_FLAGS := -std=$(CXX_STD) -arch=$(ARCH) $(OPT) $(INCLUDES)",
  "new_string": "INCLUDES   := -Iinclude\n\n# --expt-relaxed-constexpr: lets __host__ __device__ functions call host\n# constexpr functions from the standard library (notably std::bit_cast,\n# which we rely on in default_hash). Stable nvcc flag despite the\n# \"experimental\" name.\nNVCC_FLAGS := -std=$(CXX_STD) -arch=$(ARCH) $(OPT) --expt-relaxed-constexpr $(INCLUDES)"
}
```

## Assistant — 2026-05-16T01:19:41.709Z

That makes sense — both changes are reasonable, but a quick sanity check first: making the hash `__device__` only doesn't actually fix the bit_cast warning by itself. The warning is "host constexpr called from non-host context"; whether that non-host context is `__host__ __device__` or `__device__` only, calling `std::bit_cast` (host-only constexpr) from it still triggers the same diagnostic.

So we need both changes for different reasons:
- **`cuda::std::bit_cast`**: fixes the warning by using a properly-annotated bit_cast.
- **`__device__` only**: shrinks the contract and reflects actual usage (hash is only invoked from inside our kernels).

I'll also keep `std::bit_cast` in the *compile-time* uses (`empty_key_byte` init and the memsettability `static_assert` lambda) — those are evaluated by the host compiler at template instantiation, not at device runtime, so they don't trigger the warning. Let me make the changes:

## Assistant — 2026-05-16T01:22:07.287Z

Good news — `fmix32` and `fmix64` are already `__device__` only after the previous edit:

```cpp
// lines 79 and 88 in the current file:
__device__ inline std::uint32_t fmix32(std::uint32_t h) noexcept { ... }
__device__ inline std::uint64_t fmix64(std::uint64_t k) noexcept { ... }
```

The `__host__ __device__` you're looking at on line 254 is actually `View::capacity()`:

```cpp
__host__ __device__ std::size_t capacity() const noexcept { return capacity_; }
```

That one is genuinely `__host__ __device__` and intentional — a user might want to inspect a View's capacity from host code (e.g., before passing it into a kernel for some sizing decision). Since the body is a trivial getter with no host-only constexpr stdlib calls, there's no compilation issue keeping it both-scopes.

That said, it's also true that with the current usage we don't actually call `view.capacity()` from host code anywhere in the project — only `table.capacity()` (on the owning HashTable). If you'd prefer to scope it down to match the rest of View's API (`insert` and `get` are both `__device__` only), happy to do that — it's a one-line change. Let me know.

## Assistant — 2026-05-16T01:24:15.685Z

The error means the libcu++ version on the test system is older than CUDA 11.5 — `cuda::std::bit_cast` landed in libcu++ 1.5 which shipped with CUDA 11.5. Pre-11.5 has the header but not the symbol.

Three ways forward, in order of effort:

1. **`__builtin_bit_cast`** — compiler intrinsic, no header or flag dependency, works on nvcc 11.4+. Drop the `<cuda/std/bit>` include and replace the call sites.
2. **Revert to `--expt-relaxed-constexpr` + `std::bit_cast`** — the option-1 fix we originally rejected. Adds a flag, but works regardless of libcu++ version.
3. **Upgrade the CUDA toolkit on the test system** — long-term fix; orthogonal to our code.

Option 1 is the most portable and the cleanest. Let me switch to that:

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-16T01:29:05.333Z

No, we don't actually need CUDA 13 anywhere — that target was forward-looking but unjustified once you said to ignore the 128-bit CAS rationale. Let me audit what we actually use:

| Feature | Where | Minimum |
|---|---|---|
| C++20 language support (`std::bit_cast`, `std::bit_width`) | host compile-time only | **CUDA 12.0+** |
| `cuda::atomic_ref<T>` for trivially-copyable T | device CAS path | CUDA 11.5+ |
| 64-bit `atomicCAS` (now via atomic_ref) | device | sm_60+, any modern CUDA |
| cooperative_groups tile partition | device | CUDA 9.0+ |
| sm_80 codegen | build | CUDA 11.0+ |
| `__builtin_bit_cast` | device | nvcc 11.4+ |

The actual floor is **CUDA 12.0**, driven by `-std=c++20`. Your toolkit (12.0) is exactly that, so nothing needs to change in the build. Let me update the design notes:

---

```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/notes/design.md",
  "old_string": "## Language and toolchain\n\n- **Target language: C++20.**\n- **Target CUDA toolkit: CUDA 13.**\n\nWithin the implementation, we stick to C++17-shape idioms by default and only reach for C++20 features where they demonstrably improve the code.\nToday that is one use: `std::bit_cast` for the hash function's bit reinterpret, which replaces the `memcpy` idiom common in pre-C++20 GPU code.\nConcepts on the `Hash` template parameter, `std::source_location` in place of `__FILE__` / `__LINE__` macros, ranges, and coroutines are all deferred until they pay for themselves.\n\nThe C++20 baseline is conservative future-proofing: it lets us reach for the cleaner spelling when we want it without revisiting the build settings each time.",
  "new_string": "## Language and toolchain\n\n- **Target language: C++20.**\n- **Minimum CUDA toolkit: CUDA 12.0** (driven by `-std=c++20` support in nvcc).\n\nWithin the implementation, we stick to C++17-shape idioms by default and only reach for C++20 features where they demonstrably improve the code.\nToday the C++20 uses are: `std::bit_width` in `next_pow2`, and host-side compile-time `std::bit_cast` for the `empty_key_byte` extraction and the memsettability `static_assert` lambda.\nInside `__device__` functions we use `__builtin_bit_cast` rather than `std::bit_cast` to avoid both the \"host constexpr from device\" warning and the libcu++ version dependency that `cuda::std::bit_cast` would impose (it landed in libcu++ 1.5 / CUDA 11.5, but binding to that minimum gains us nothing).\nConcepts on the `Hash` template parameter, `std::source_location` in place of `__FILE__` / `__LINE__` macros, ranges, and coroutines are all deferred until they pay for themselves.\n\nThe C++20 baseline is conservative future-proofing: it lets us reach for the cleaner spelling when we want it without revisiting the build settings each time."
}
```

## Assistant — 2026-05-16T01:30:57.138Z
