## Assistant — 2026-05-16T01:31:08.760Z

Our codebase is **already 128-bit CAS compatible** — the static_assert restriction is gone, `cuda::atomic_ref<Slot>` is templated on Slot regardless of size, and the alignment is correct (`alignas(sizeof(Key)+sizeof(Value))` gives 16 for 8+8). To exercise the 128-bit path you'd just instantiate the table with 8-byte key + 8-byte value:

```cpp
using Table64 = gpurhh::HashTable<std::uint64_t, std::uint64_t>;
```

which would give:
- `sizeof(Slot)` = 16, `alignof(Slot)` = 16.
- `bucket_size` = `CacheLineBytes / sizeof(Slot)` = 128 / 16 = **8** slots per bucket.
- `tile_size` = 8, `tiles_per_warp` = 4.

What it actually runs on:

| GPU arch | 128-bit CAS path | Performance |
|---|---|---|
| sm_60 (Pascal) and earlier | libcu++ lock-table emulation | Slow, serialized via global mutex |
| sm_70 (Volta) to sm_89 (Ada) | libcu++ lock-table emulation | Slow under contention |
| sm_90 (Hopper) and up | Native `atom.cas.b128` | Roughly competitive with 64-bit CAS |

On your **sm_80** (Ampere): the libcu++ implementation falls back to a lock-based scheme. It works correctly, but each 128-bit CAS is ~10× slower than a 64-bit CAS under contention. That's the throughput hit the design notes alluded to.

Things that don't need changes:
- The insert / get algorithms — already generic over slot size.
- The bucket layout — already cache-line aligned, smaller bucket_size is fine.
- The tile-cooperative kernels in `tests/common.cuh` — they pick up `Table::tile_size` automatically.

Things you might want for testing it:
- A new test file `tests/test_insert64.cu` (or similar) that uses `HashTable<uint64_t, uint64_t>`. The test bodies could be near-copies of the existing ones with the type swap.
- Or just templatize the existing `common.cuh` over the Table type — but that's a bigger refactor for unclear benefit if the goal is just to verify 128-bit CAS compiles and runs.

So in summary: **nothing in our code blocks 128-bit slots.** What it requires is libcu++ ≥ 1.5 (you have it) and sm_70+ hardware (you have it). The cost is perf, not correctness, on pre-Hopper.

Want me to add a 64-bit-key test file as a smoke test for the 128-bit CAS path?

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-16T02:16:58.685Z

That's a PATH issue rather than a code one — your shell can't find `nvcc`. After a CUDA install/upgrade the binary often lands in `/usr/local/cuda-13.2/bin/` but the symlink `/usr/local/cuda` or your shell's `PATH` may not be updated.

Three quick ways to point at it:

```bash
# 1. Pass NVCC explicitly on the make command (Makefile already honors this):
make NVCC=/usr/local/cuda-13.2/bin/nvcc test_construction

# 2. Export PATH for this shell session:
export PATH=/usr/local/cuda-13.2/bin:$PATH
make test_construction

# 3. Confirm nvcc is reachable and which version it is:
which nvcc
nvcc --version
```

If `which nvcc` returns nothing, option 1 or 2 is the fix. If it returns a path but a *different* version (e.g., still 12.0), the new install didn't replace the symlink — option 1 is the safest in that case so you're sure you're using 13.2.

Once you've located it, also worth doing:
```bash
ls /usr/local/cuda-13.2/include/cuda/std/bit
```
to confirm libcu++'s `<cuda/std/bit>` actually exists in the new install before re-trying the build.

Both edits done in [include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh):

- `#include <cuda/std/bit>` is back.
- Both `default_hash` specializations are back to `cuda::std::bit_cast<T>(key)`.

Worth recording for future reference: in **CUDA 13.x** this diagnostic is now a hard error rather than a warning, so the choice between `cuda::std::bit_cast` / `__builtin_bit_cast` / `--expt-relaxed-constexpr` isn't optional anymore — picking one is required for any `__device__` code that does bit-reinterpretation. `cuda::std::bit_cast` is the cleanest of the three on a system that has a recent libcu++.

Worth updating the notes' minimum-CUDA line — given that we now know our `cuda::std::bit_cast` use forces libcu++ 1.5+, and the test system needed 13.x to make it work — let me know if you'd like that bumped from "12.0" to something more honest, or if you'd rather leave it pending more data.

50	    }
51	}
52	
53	// For each input key, writes (value, 1) on hit and (_, 0) on miss. We assume
54	// view.get broadcasts the located value to every lane in the tile, and have
55	// lane 0 perform the store to keep the store count to one per key.
56	__global__ inline void get_kernel(View view,
57	                                  const std::uint32_t* keys,
58	                                  std::uint32_t* values_out,
59	                                  int* found_out,
60	                                  std::size_t n)
61	{
62	    auto block = cg::this_thread_block();
63	    auto tile  = cg::tiled_partition<Table::tile_size>(block);
64	
65	    const std::size_t tiles_per_block = blockDim.x / Table::tile_size;
66	    const std::size_t tile_id =
67	        blockIdx.x * tiles_per_block + threadIdx.x / Table::tile_size;
68	    const std::size_t total_tiles = gridDim.x * tiles_per_block;
69	
70	    for (std::size_t i = tile_id; i < n; i += total_tiles) {
71	        std::uint32_t v = 0;
72	        const bool ok = view.get(tile, keys[i], v);
73	        if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) {
74	            values_out[i] = ok ? v : 0;
75	            found_out[i]  = ok ? 1 : 0;
76	        }
77	    }
78	}
79	
80	// --- Host-side driver helpers -------------------------------------------
81	
82	inline void bulk_insert(Table& table,
83	                        const std::uint32_t* h_keys,
84	                        const std::uint32_t* h_values,
85	                        std::size_t n)
86	{
87	    std::uint32_t *d_keys = nullptr, *d_values = nullptr;
88	    cudaMalloc(&d_keys,   n * sizeof(std::uint32_t)) >> CUDA_CHECK;
89	    cudaMalloc(&d_values, n * sizeof(std::uint32_t)) >> CUDA_CHECK;
90	    cudaMemcpy(d_keys,   h_keys,   n * sizeof(std::uint32_t),
91	               cudaMemcpyHostToDevice) >> CUDA_CHECK;
92	    cudaMemcpy(d_values, h_values, n * sizeof(std::uint32_t),
93	               cudaMemcpyHostToDevice) >> CUDA_CHECK;
94	
95	    const int tiles_per_block = kBlockSize / Table::tile_size;
96	    const int grid_size =
97	        static_cast<int>((n + tiles_per_block - 1) / tiles_per_block);
98	    insert_kernel<<<grid_size, kBlockSize>>>(table.view(), d_keys, d_values, n);
99	    cudaGetLastError()      >> CUDA_CHECK;
```

## Assistant — 2026-05-16T02:24:31.826Z

`inline` removed from both `__global__ insert_kernel` and `__global__ get_kernel` in [tests/common.cuh](tests/common.cuh). The host-side `bulk_insert` and `bulk_find` keep their `inline` — that's the meaningful case (they're regular host functions in a header that may be included from multiple TUs).

This works as long as we stick to one-test-per-executable, which is what the current build assumes. If we ever combined tests into one binary, the duplicate `__global__` definitions would conflict — at that point we'd put the kernels in an anonymous namespace or as static helpers, but no need to do that preemptively.

```
1	# Makefile for gpurhh — header-only GPU Robin Hood hash table.
2	#
3	# Builds tests and examples against the public header in include/. There is
4	# no library to compile (header-only). Each .cu file under tests/ and
5	# examples/ becomes its own executable in build/.
6	#
7	# Common overrides:
8	#   make NVCC=/path/to/nvcc
9	#   make ARCH=sm_75
10	#   make CXX_STD=c++17
11	#   make OPT="-O0 -g"
12	
13	NVCC    ?= nvcc
14	CXX_STD ?= c++20
15	ARCH    ?= sm_89
16	OPT     ?= -O2
17	
18	INCLUDES   := -Iinclude
19	NVCC_FLAGS := -std=$(CXX_STD) -arch=$(ARCH) $(OPT) $(INCLUDES)
20	
21	BUILD := build
22	
23	TEST_SRCS    := $(wildcard tests/test_*.cu)
24	TEST_BINS    := $(patsubst tests/%.cu,$(BUILD)/tests/%,$(TEST_SRCS))
25	
26	EXAMPLE_SRCS := $(wildcard examples/*.cu)
27	EXAMPLE_BINS := $(patsubst examples/%.cu,$(BUILD)/examples/%,$(EXAMPLE_SRCS))
28	
29	# Treat any header change as a reason to rebuild everything. Adequate for a
30	# small project; revisit if compile times become an issue.
31	ALL_HEADERS := $(shell find include tests -name "*.cuh" 2>/dev/null)
32	
33	.PHONY: all tests examples test clean
34	
35	all: tests examples
36	
37	tests:    $(TEST_BINS)
38	examples: $(EXAMPLE_BINS)
39	
40	test: $(TEST_BINS)
41		@set -e; for t in $(TEST_BINS); do echo "==> $$t"; "$$t"; done
42	
43	$(BUILD)/tests/%: tests/%.cu $(ALL_HEADERS)
44		@mkdir -p $(BUILD)/tests
45		$(NVCC) $(NVCC_FLAGS) $< -o $@
46	
47	$(BUILD)/examples/%: examples/%.cu $(ALL_HEADERS)
48		@mkdir -p $(BUILD)/examples
49		$(NVCC) $(NVCC_FLAGS) $< -o $@
50	
51	clean:
52		rm -rf $(BUILD)
53	
```

</details>

Each .cu file under tests/ and\n# examples/ becomes its own executable in build/.\n#\n# Common overrides:\n#   make NVCC=/path/to/nvcc\n#   make ARCH=sm_75\n#   make CXX_STD=c++17\n#   make OPT=\"-O0 -g\"\n\nNVCC    ?= nvcc\nCXX_STD ?= c++20\nARCH    ?= sm_89\nOPT     ?= -O2\n\nINCLUDES   := -Iinclude\nNVCC_FLAGS := -std=$(CXX_STD) -arch=$(ARCH) $(OPT) $(INCLUDES)",
  "new_string": "# Makefile for gpurhh — header-only GPU Robin Hood hash table.\n#\n# Builds tests and examples against the public header in include/. There is\n# no library to compile (header-only). Each .cu file under tests/ and\n# examples/ becomes its own executable in build/.\n#\n# Build modes (pick at invocation time):\n#   make            unoptimized build (-O0); fastest rebuild, the default\n#   make release    optimized build (-O2)\n#   make debug      unoptimized + host/device debug info (-O0 -G -g)\n#\n# Common overrides:\n#   make NVCC=/path/to/nvcc\n#   make ARCH=sm_75\n#   make CXX_STD=c++17\n#   make OPT=\"-O3 -lineinfo\"   # bypass the mode selection entirely\n\nNVCC    ?= nvcc\nCXX_STD ?= c++20\nARCH    ?= sm_89\n\n# Pick optimization flags based on the goal that was passed on the command\n# line. Falls through to -O0 for plain `make` or any non-mode goal.\nifneq (,$(filter release,$(MAKECMDGOALS)))\n    OPT ?= -O2\nelse ifneq (,$(filter debug,$(MAKECMDGOALS)))\n    OPT ?= -O0 -G -g\nelse\n    OPT ?= -O0\nendif\n\nINCLUDES   := -Iinclude\nNVCC_FLAGS := -std=$(CXX_STD) -arch=$(ARCH) $(OPT) $(INCLUDES)"
}
```

</details>

## Assistant — 2026-05-16T02:28:54.572Z

Two changes to [Makefile](Makefile):

1. **OPT is now goal-driven.** Three modes:
   - `make` (or any non-mode goal like `make test_construction`) → `-O0`
   - `make release` → `-O2`
   - `make debug` → `-O0 -G -g` (`-G` is nvcc's device-side debug info; `-g` is host-side)

   The selection uses `$(filter ...,$(MAKECMDGOALS))` so it just looks at the goals you pass on the command line. Manual `make OPT="..."` overrides still work because of `?=` plus command-line precedence.

2. **`release` and `debug` phony targets** added that depend on `all`. So `make release` builds everything with `-O2`, `make debug` does the same with `-O0 -G -g`.

**Note on caching:** because `build/` is shared across modes and only depends on source-file timestamps, switching between modes won't trigger a rebuild unless sources changed. If you want a clean switch, run `make clean release` (or `make clean debug`). I could add per-mode build directories (`build/release/`, `build/debug/`) to fix this — let me know if you'd prefer that.

## User — 2026-05-16T02:32:13.570Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-166`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
1	# GPU Robin Hood Hash Table — Design Brainstorm
2	
3	## Goals
4	
5	- **Header-only CUDA library.**
6	  A single `.cuh` (or a small set) that users `#include` and template-instantiate for their key/value types.
7	  No separate compilation of the table itself.
8	- **Massively parallel insertion.**
9	  A batch of `N` keys is inserted by `N` (or more) GPU threads cooperatively.
10	  Worst-case behavior must remain bounded even under heavy contention.
11	- **Memory-bandwidth bound.**
12	  On large tables, the table is much bigger than the L2 cache, so performance is determined by how many useful bytes each DRAM transaction delivers.
13	  The aim is to come as close to peak HBM bandwidth as the access pattern allows.
14	
15	## Language and toolchain
16	
17	- **Target language: C++20.**
18	- **Minimum CUDA toolkit: CUDA 12.0** (driven by `-std=c++20` support in nvcc).
19	
20	Within the implementation, we stick to C++17-shape idioms by default and only reach for C++20 features where they demonstrably improve the code.
21	Today the C++20 uses are: `std::bit_width` in `next_pow2`, and host-side compile-time `std::bit_cast` for the `empty_key_byte` extraction and the memsettability `static_assert` lambda.
22	Inside `__device__` functions we use `__builtin_bit_cast` rather than `std::bit_cast` to avoid both the "host constexpr from device" warning and the libcu++ version dependency that `cuda::std::bit_cast` would impose (it landed in libcu++ 1.5 / CUDA 11.5, but binding to that minimum gains us nothing).
23	Concepts on the `Hash` template parameter, `std::source_location` in place of `__FILE__` / `__LINE__` macros, ranges, and coroutines are all deferred until they pay for themselves.
24	
25	The C++20 baseline is conservative future-proofing: it lets us reach for the cleaner spelling when we want it without revisiting the build settings each time.
26	
27	## Why Robin Hood?
28	
29	Open-addressing with the invariant *"the entry in slot `i` whose probe distance is smaller gets evicted by an entry whose probe distance is larger"* gives:
30	
31	- A tight, low-variance distribution of probe distances → small worst-case lookup length even at load factors of 0.85–0.9.
32	- A natural early-exit rule for lookups (stop as soon as the slot's probe distance is smaller than the probe distance of the key we're searching for).
33	- Backward-shift deletion that avoids tombstones.
34	
35	These properties matter even more on a GPU than on a CPU because the warp's tail latency is set by the longest probe sequence among its 32 lanes.
36	
37	## Slot layout
38	
39	A slot is the unit of an atomic update.
40	The simplest design that admits lock-free CAS on a single machine word is:
41	
42	```
43	struct Slot {                       // 8 bytes total — fits in atomicCAS<uint64_t>
44	    uint32_t key;
45	    uint32_t value;                 // or a 32-bit payload / pointer index
46	};
47	```
48	
49	Empty slots use a reserved sentinel key (e.g. `0xFFFFFFFF`); users are forbidden from inserting that key.
50	For 64-bit keys or larger payloads we have two options:
51	
52	1. **128-bit slots** with `__int128` CAS via `atomicCAS` on `unsigned long long` pairs — supported on sm_70+ but with reduced throughput.
53	2. **Separate parallel arrays** (`keys[]` and `values[]`) with the key array being the source of truth for CAS, and values being written only by the thread that successfully claimed the slot.
54	   This halves the bytes-per-CAS but requires care: a finder must re-read the key after reading the value to make sure the slot wasn't reused mid-read.
55	
56	We will start with the 64-bit packed slot and parameterize later.
57	
58	**Probe distance.**
59	We do *not* store probe distance in the slot.
60	It is derivable as `(slot_index - hash(key)) mod capacity`.
61	Storing it would cost bits we'd rather give to the payload, and the derivation is a single subtract once we've read the slot.
62	
63	## Capacity, hash function, load factor
64	
65	- Capacity is a power of two so `mod` becomes a mask.
66	  Users specify a target load factor (default 0.7); we round capacity up to `next_pow2(N / load_factor)`.
67	- Hash function is a template parameter; default is a finalizer-style mixer (e.g. the splitmix64/Murmur3 finalizer) that is cheap on GPU and decorrelates structured keys well.
68	- Robin Hood tolerates higher load than linear probing, but on a GPU the warp tail-latency argument pushes us back toward 0.7–0.8 rather than 0.9.
69	
70	## Parallel insertion algorithm
71	
72	Each thread owns one `(key, value)` pair.
73	The thread walks the probe sequence and, at every slot, performs one of three actions:
74	
75	1. **Empty slot, CAS succeeds.** Done.
76	2. **Same key, CAS succeeds.** This is an update; done.
77	3. **Occupied by another key.**
78	   Compare probe distances.
79	   If the resident is "richer" (its probe distance from *its* home is smaller than ours from *our* home), we attempt to CAS our pair into the slot.
80	   On success, we adopt the displaced pair and continue probing from this slot.
81	   If the resident is "poorer" or equal, we move on.
82	
83	A CAS failure is treated as "the world changed under us": we re-read the slot and re-evaluate without advancing the probe index.
84	This is the standard lock-free retry.
85	
86	### Why this is correct under contention
87	
88	The Robin Hood invariant is maintained by every individual CAS because each CAS only swaps in a pair with probe distance strictly greater than the one already in the slot (or the slot was empty).
89	Two concurrent inserts targeting the same slot serialize through CAS, and whichever loses retries with the freshest state.
90	There is no global ordering requirement: as long as every write respects the local invariant, the final table is a valid Robin Hood layout for *some* permutation of the input.
91	
92	Caveat: the *value* portion of an update (case 2) needs care.
93	If two threads insert the same key concurrently and the user wants "last write wins" semantics, we need an additional CAS loop on the value, or we tolerate non-deterministic resolution.
94	
95	## Achieving bandwidth saturation
96	
97	The 64-bit slot is small relative to a 128-byte L2 cache line: one transaction brings 16 slots.
98	The bandwidth strategy follows from this:
99	
100	- **Warp-cooperative probing.**
101	  Within a warp, threads whose home slots land in the same cache line should service their probes from one load, not 32 loads.
102	  We have the warp ballot/shuffle primitives to do this cheaply.
103	- **Bucketed probing.**
104	  Treat the table as an array of *buckets* of 16 slots (one cache line).
105	  A probe inspects an entire bucket at a time — one coalesced 128-byte load — and uses warp intrinsics (`__ballot_sync`, `__match_any_sync`) to find the candidate slot.
106	  Insertion still uses CAS on a single 8-byte slot inside the bucket, but the read traffic per probe is exactly one cache line.
107	  This is the same idea as `bucketed_cuckoo` and Bucketized Cuckoo Hashing.
108	- **Probe length capping.**
109	  Cap probes at, say, 32 buckets.
110	  If a thread overruns the cap during insertion, mark the batch as failed and rehash.
111	  With Robin Hood at 0.7 load this happens with vanishing probability for reasonable `N`.
112	- **Avoid divergence.**
113	  All threads in a warp execute the same probe loop.
114	  The "I'm done, others aren't" case is handled with masked operations rather than early-return-then-divergence.
115	
116	If we get this right, an insert of `N` keys at load factor 0.7 should cost approximately `(1/(1-α)) * sizeof(slot)` bytes per insert in expectation, which is the information-theoretic floor for an open-addressed table with this layout.
117	
118	## Work decomposition: how insertions map onto the GPU hierarchy
119	
120	Before discussing parameters, we need to commit to a unit of work — what GPU resource cooperates on a single key insertion.
121	There are four candidates:
122	
123	| Mapping              | Threads / key | Probe is one coalesced load? | Keys in flight / warp | Wasted lanes / probe |
124	| -------------------- | ------------- | ---------------------------- | --------------------- | -------------------- |
125	| Thread per key       | 1             | No — 32 unrelated lines      | 32                    | 0                    |
126	| **Sub-warp tile**    | **B**         | **Yes — exactly one line**   | **32 / B**            | **0 (if B ∣ 32)**    |
127	| Warp per key         | 32            | Yes, but ≤ B lanes used      | 1                     | 32 − B               |
128	| Block per key        | 128–1024      | Yes, but lanes mostly idle   | ≪ 1                   | huge                 |
129	
130	- **Thread-per-key** is the naive choice and exactly the case our bandwidth strategy is designed to avoid.
131	  Each warp issues 32 cache-line loads per probe step instead of one, and we lose any coalescing benefit.
132	  Fatal for our stated goal.
133	- **Block-per-key** is too coarse: there is no useful work for 128+ threads on a single key.
134	- **Warp-per-key** is the classic "warp-cooperative hashing" design but wastes lanes whenever the bucket is narrower than the warp.
135	  With 8-byte slots and a 128-byte line on NVIDIA, the bucket holds 16 slots, so half of every warp sits idle.
136	- **Sub-warp tile per key**, with tile width equal to the bucket width, is the one design where *one tile-probe = one cache-line transaction* with no idle lanes.
137	  This is the design we adopt.
138	
139	### The structure that follows
140	
141	1. A **bucket** is a contiguous, cache-line-aligned run of `B` slots.
142	2. A **cooperative tile** of `B` threads (`cooperative_groups::thread_block_tile<B>`) owns one in-flight key at a time.
143	   Each tile lane holds one slot of the current bucket in a register; one bucket probe is one coalesced load.
144	3. **Tiles per warp** = `WarpSize / B`.
145	   For NVIDIA with 8-byte slots that is 2.
146	4. **Block size** is purely an occupancy knob — some multiple of `WarpSize`, tuned empirically.
147	5. **Work assignment** is a tile-strided loop: tile index `t` (across the whole grid) handles input keys `t, t+T, t+2T, …` where `T` is the total number of resident tiles.
148	   Naturally load-balanced and robust to input skew.
149	
150	### Per-tile probe step
151	
152	On each probe:
153	
154	1. The `B` lanes cooperatively load one bucket — each lane reads one slot, fully coalesced.
155	2. `tile.ballot(...)` over a predicate identifies empty slots, key matches, or Robin-Hood-displaceable slots in a single warp intrinsic.
156	3. The lane corresponding to the chosen slot performs the CAS; the outcome is broadcast to the rest of the tile with `tile.shfl(...)`.
157	4. On displacement, the evicted `(key, value)` becomes the tile's new in-flight pair and the tile advances by one bucket.
158	
159	Every step is a warp-intrinsic operation on a single cache line, which is what makes the bandwidth argument hold.
160	
161	### Parameters: what is forced by the architecture, what is free
162	
163	Once we commit to the sub-warp tile design, almost every constant is *derived* from the hardware, not chosen:
164	
165	| Parameter         | Value (default)              | Source                                   |
166	| ----------------- | ---------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
167	| `SlotBytes`       | 8                            | Key/value packing choice                 |
168	| `CacheLineBytes`  | 128 (NVIDIA)                 | Hardware                                 |
169	| `BucketSize`      | `CacheLineBytes / SlotBytes` | **Derived**                              |
170	| `WarpSize`        | 32 (NVIDIA)                  | Hardware                                 |
171	| `TileSize`        | `BucketSize`                 | **Derived** (the central design choice)  |
172	| `TilesPerWarp`    | `WarpSize / BucketSize`      | **Derived**                              |
173	| `BlockSize`       | tuned (e.g. 128 or 256)      | Occupancy — empirical                    |
174	| `TargetLoadFactor`| tuned (e.g. 0.7)             | Robin Hood quality — empirical           |
175	
176	So the only genuinely free knobs are `BlockSize` and `TargetLoadFactor`.
177	The rest should be expressed in the code as compile-time constants derived from `SlotBytes`, `CacheLineBytes`, and `WarpSize`, with the latter two being template parameters of the table (with sensible defaults) rather than hard-coded.
178	
179	### Why the abstraction matters — an AMD aside (out of scope for v1)
180	
181	AMD GPUs (CDNA, RDNA) have a 64-byte cache line and a 64-thread wavefront.
182	With 8-byte slots:
183	
184	- NVIDIA: `BucketSize` = 128 / 8 = 16, `TilesPerWarp` = 32 / 16 = **2**.
185	- AMD:    `BucketSize` =  64 / 8 =  8, `TilesPerWarp` = 64 /  8 = **8**.
186	
187	The same algorithm runs unchanged; only the derived constants differ.
188	We are not targeting AMD, but it is the reason we want `CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` to be parameters of the table type rather than hard-coded `128` and `32`.
189	Anything that ends up baked in as a literal in the implementation is a portability bug waiting to happen.
190	
191	## Lookup and deletion
192	
193	- **Lookup.**
194	  Same probing rule, no CAS.
195	  Stop as soon as we find the key, an empty slot, or a resident whose probe distance is smaller than ours.
196	  Lookup is fully read-only and trivially parallel — no synchronization required.
197	- **Deletion.**
198	  Backward-shift: starting from the deleted slot, while the next slot is non-empty and its probe distance is positive, shift it back by one.
199	  On a concurrent GPU this is the hardest operation to make correct without locks.
200	  **Initial scope: defer deletion**, support insert + lookup only, and add it once we have benchmarks in place.
201	
202	## API sketch
203	
204	```cpp
205	namespace gpurhh {
206	
207	template <class Key,
208	          class Value,
209	          class Hash = default_hash<Key>,
210	          Key   EmptyKey = Key{} - Key{1}>            // sentinel
211	class HashTable {
212	public:
213	    // Host-side construction allocates device memory.
214	    explicit HashTable(std::size_t capacity);
215	
216	    // Bulk operations. Stream-aware, non-owning input buffers.
217	    void insert(const Key* keys, const Value* values,
218	                std::size_t n, cudaStream_t stream = 0);
219	
220	    void get(const Key* keys, Value* values_out,
221	             std::size_t n, cudaStream_t stream = 0) const;
222	
223	    // A device-side, copyable handle for use inside user kernels.
224	    struct View {
225	        __device__ bool  insert(Key k, Value v);
226	        __device__ bool  get   (Key k, Value& out) const;
227	    };
228	    View view();
229	
230	private:
231	    Slot*       slots_;
232	    std::size_t capacity_;
233	};
234	
235	} // namespace gpurhh
236	```
237	
238	The `View` is the centerpiece for header-only use: a user kernel that already has its keys in registers can call `view.insert(...)` directly without a separate kernel launch.
239	
240	## Open questions / things to settle before coding
241	
242	1. **Slot width.**
243	   Start with 64-bit packed `(uint32 key, uint32 value)`, or go straight to a 128-bit slot for 64-bit keys?
244	   The 64-bit case is the canonical micro-benchmark and is what most published GPU hash tables use.
245	   Note that changing this also changes the derived `BucketSize`.
246	2. **Failure semantics on full table.**
247	   Return a count of failed inserts, or `assert`?
248	   A bulk-insert API can naturally return the number of survivors and let the caller resize.
249	3. **Resize.**
250	   Out of scope for v1.
251	   The table is allocated at a fixed capacity.
252	   Users that need growth allocate a bigger one and re-insert.
253	4. **Multi-GPU.**
254	   Out of scope for v1.
255	5. **Portability across vendors.**
256	   Out of scope for v1 but informs the abstractions — see the AMD aside above.
257	   `CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` should be table-level parameters, not magic numbers in the implementation.
258	6. **Testing.**
259	   A small CPU reference implementation (sequential Robin Hood) plus a randomized differential test against the GPU implementation, plus stress tests at 0.5/0.7/0.9 load.
260	
261	## Suggested next steps
262	
263	1. Sketch the `Slot`, hash, and probe primitives in a single `.cuh`.
264	2. Implement the insert kernel without bucketing, just to validate correctness and the CAS loop.
265	3. Add the bucketed (cache-line-aligned) variant and benchmark against the naive one.
266	4. Add `get`, then benchmark against `cuCollections` / `WarpCore` as external baselines.
267	
```

</details>

---

**→ Tool call: `Edit`** (id `tool-167`)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/notes/design.md",
  "old_string": "## Language and toolchain\n\n- **Target language: C++20.**\n- **Minimum CUDA toolkit: CUDA 12.0** (driven by `-std=c++20` support in nvcc).\n\nWithin the implementation, we stick to C++17-shape idioms by default and only reach for C++20 features where they demonstrably improve the code.\nToday the C++20 uses are: `std::bit_width` in `next_pow2`, and host-side compile-time `std::bit_cast` for the `empty_key_byte` extraction and the memsettability `static_assert` lambda.\nInside `__device__` functions we use `__builtin_bit_cast` rather than `std::bit_cast` to avoid both the \"host constexpr from device\" warning and the libcu++ version dependency that `cuda::std::bit_cast` would impose (it landed in libcu++ 1.5 / CUDA 11.5, but binding to that minimum gains us nothing).\nConcepts on the `Hash` template parameter, `std::source_location` in place of `__FILE__` / `__LINE__` macros, ranges, and coroutines are all deferred until they pay for themselves.\n\nThe C++20 baseline is conservative future-proofing: it lets us reach for the cleaner spelling when we want it without revisiting the build settings each time.",
  "new_string": "## Language and toolchain\n\n- **Target language: C++20.**\n- **Target CUDA toolkit: CUDA 13.x.**\n  Driven by two requirements: `-std=c++20` support in nvcc (CUDA 12.0+) and `cuda::std::bit_cast` in libcu++ (CUDA 13.0 was the first version where this combination worked cleanly on the test system).\n\nWithin the implementation, we stick to C++17-shape idioms by default and only reach for C++20 features where they demonstrably improve the code.\nThe C++20 uses today are:\n\n- `std::bit_width` in `next_pow2` (host-side, compile-time).\n- Host-side compile-time `std::bit_cast` for the `empty_key_byte` extraction and the memsettability `static_assert` lambda.\n- Device-side `cuda::std::bit_cast` (libcu++) for the runtime bit-reinterpret in `default_hash`.\n  The libcu++ version is mandatory here because `std::bit_cast` is host-only `constexpr` in libstdc++; calling it from a `__device__` function is a hard error in CUDA 13.x.\n\nConcepts on the `Hash` template parameter, `std::source_location` in place of `__FILE__` / `__LINE__` macros, ranges, and coroutines are all deferred until they pay for themselves.\n\nThe C++20 baseline is conservative future-proofing: it lets us reach for the cleaner spelling when we want it without revisiting the build settings each time."
}
```

</details>

## Assistant — 2026-05-16T02:33:16.065Z

**→ Tool call: `Edit`** (id `tool-168`)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/notes/design.md",
  "old_string": "A slot is the unit of an atomic update.\nThe simplest design that admits lock-free CAS on a single machine word is:\n\n```\nstruct Slot {                       // 8 bytes total — fits in atomicCAS<uint64_t>\n    uint32_t key;\n    uint32_t value;                 // or a 32-bit payload / pointer index\n};\n```\n\nEmpty slots use a reserved sentinel key (e.g. `0xFFFFFFFF`); users are forbidden from inserting that key.\nFor 64-bit keys or larger payloads we have two options:\n\n1. **128-bit slots** with `__int128` CAS via `atomicCAS` on `unsigned long long` pairs — supported on sm_70+ but with reduced throughput.\n2. **Separate parallel arrays** (`keys[]` and `values[]`) with the key array being the source of truth for CAS, and values being written only by the thread that successfully claimed the slot.\n   This halves the bytes-per-CAS but requires care: a finder must re-read the key after reading the value to make sure the slot wasn't reused mid-read.\n\nWe will start with the 64-bit packed slot and parameterize later.",
  "new_string": "A slot is the unit of an atomic update.\nThe simplest design that admits lock-free CAS on a single machine word is:\n\n```\nstruct alignas(sizeof(Key) + sizeof(Value)) Slot {\n    Key   key;       // typically uint32_t or uint64_t\n    Value value;     // typically the same width as Key\n};\n```\n\nEmpty slots use a reserved sentinel key (default: all-bits-set for unsigned integer Key); users are forbidden from inserting that key.\nThe packed slot supports two widths:\n\n1. **64-bit slots** (4-byte key + 4-byte value): atomic CAS via `cuda::atomic_ref<Slot>`, which on every NVIDIA arch compiles down to a 64-bit `atom.cas`. Fast.\n2. **128-bit slots** (8-byte key + 8-byte value): atomic CAS still goes through `cuda::atomic_ref<Slot>`; on sm_90+ this is the single-instruction `atom.cas.b128`, on sm_70–sm_89 libcu++ emulates with a lock-table fallback. Functional everywhere, but the emulated path is significantly slower than 64-bit CAS under contention.\n\nWe use the same control flow for both widths — the only thing that changes is which atomic instruction `cuda::atomic_ref::compare_exchange_strong` resolves to. A parallel-arrays layout (separate keys[] and values[]) is rejected: it halves the per-CAS bytes but at the cost of an awkward second-read-the-key step for finders that complicates correctness."
}
```

</details>

{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/notes/design.md",
  "old_string": "## API sketch\n\n```cpp\nnamespace gpurhh {\n\ntemplate <class Key,\n          class Value,\n          class Hash = default_hash<Key>,\n          Key   EmptyKey = Key{} - Key{1}>            // sentinel\nclass HashTable {\npublic:\n    // Host-side construction allocates device memory.\n    explicit HashTable(std::size_t capacity);\n\n    // Bulk operations. Stream-aware, non-owning input buffers.\n    void insert(const Key* keys, const Value* values,\n                std::size_t n, cudaStream_t stream = 0);\n\n    void get(const Key* keys, Value* values_out,\n             std::size_t n, cudaStream_t stream = 0) const;\n\n    // A device-side, copyable handle for use inside user kernels.\n    struct View {\n        __device__ bool  insert(Key k, Value v);\n        __device__ bool  get   (Key k, Value& out) const;\n    };\n    View view();\n\nprivate:\n    Slot*       slots_;\n    std::size_t capacity_;\n};\n\n} // namespace gpurhh\n```\n\nThe `View` is the centerpiece for header-only use: a user kernel that already has its keys in registers can call `view.insert(...)` directly without a separate kernel launch.",
  "new_string": "## API sketch\n\n```cpp\nnamespace gpurhh {\n\ntemplate <class Key,\n          class Value,\n          class Hash             = default_hash<Key>,\n          Key   EmptyKey         = default_empty_key<Key>::key,\n          int   CacheLineBytes   = 128,\n          int   WarpSize         = 32,\n          int   MaxProbeBuckets  = 8>\nclass HashTable {\npublic:\n    // Host-side: allocate and initialize the device-resident slot array.\n    explicit HashTable(std::size_t min_capacity);\n    ~HashTable();\n\n    // Non-copyable, movable.\n    HashTable(HashTable&&) noexcept;\n    HashTable& operator=(HashTable&&) noexcept;\n\n    // Device-side, copyable handle. Trivially passed by value into kernels.\n    class View {\n    public:\n        template <class Tile>\n        __device__ bool insert(const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value);\n\n        template <class Tile>\n        __device__ bool get(const Tile& tile, Key key, Value& out) const;\n    };\n\n    View view() const noexcept;\n    std::size_t capacity() const noexcept;  // power of two, in slots\n};\n\n} // namespace gpurhh\n```\n\nThe `View` is the centerpiece for header-only use: a user kernel that already has its keys in registers can call `view.insert(tile, ...)` directly.\nBulk host-side operations are deliberately *not* part of the table — callers manage their device buffers and write their own driver kernels, which calls `view.insert` / `view.get` cooperatively from a tile of `tile_size` threads.\nThe tests in `tests/common.cuh` show the canonical pattern."
}
```

</details>

Sketch the `Slot`, hash, and probe primitives in a single `.cuh`.\n2. Implement the insert kernel without bucketing, just to validate correctness and the CAS loop.\n3. Add the bucketed (cache-line-aligned) variant and benchmark against the naive one.\n4. Add `get`, then benchmark against `cuCollections` / `WarpCore` as external baselines.",
  "new_string": "## Open questions / things to settle\n\n1. **Slot width.**\n   Both 64-bit (4+4 byte) and 128-bit (8+8 byte) packings are supported via `cuda::atomic_ref<Slot>`.\n   The 64-bit case is what current tests exercise; the 128-bit case compiles and links but uses libcu++'s lock-emulated 128-bit CAS on pre-Hopper GPUs, which is significantly slower than 64-bit CAS.\n   Open: validate the 128-bit path on a Hopper system, and benchmark.\n2. **Failure semantics on full table.**\n   `View::insert` currently returns `false` when the probe-length cap is hit.\n   Open: whether a bulk operation should return a survivor count or just a failure flag — depends on whether we add bulk operations to the API at all.\n3. **Resize.**\n   Out of scope for v1.\n   The table is allocated at a fixed capacity.\n   Users that need growth allocate a bigger one and re-insert.\n4. **Multi-GPU.**\n   Out of scope for v1.\n5. **Portability across vendors.**\n   Out of scope for v1 but informs the abstractions — see the AMD aside above.\n   `CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` are table-level parameters, not magic numbers in the implementation.\n6. **Testing.**\n   Basic correctness tests exist (construction, single-tile insert, single-tile get).\n   Still missing: a CPU reference for randomized differential testing; concurrency stress tests at varying load factors; probe-cap stress tests; benchmarks against `cuCollections` / `WarpCore`.\n\n## Suggested next steps\n\n1. Add stress tests for concurrency and load-factor edges.\n5. Benchmark against `cuCollections` / `WarpCore` to validate the bandwidth-bound claim."

  "old_string": "- **Probe length capping.**\n  Cap probes at, say, 32 buckets.\n  If a thread overruns the cap during insertion, mark the batch as failed and rehash.\n  With Robin Hood at 0.7 load this happens with vanishing probability for reasonable `N`.",
  "new_string": "- **Probe length capping.**\n  Exposed as a `MaxProbeBuckets` template parameter (default 8).\n  An insert that would place a key past the cap fails (the caller is expected to rehash); a get that probes the cap without finding the key returns \"not found\" (correct because of the insert-side cap).\n  With Robin Hood at load factor ~0.85 and `bucket_size` 16, the expected longest probe is well under the default cap; the cap protects against adversarial inputs and very high load factors."
}
```

```
1	# CLAUDE.md
2	
3	Project context for Claude Code.
4	This file is loaded automatically at the start of every conversation in this repository.
5	
6	## What this project is
7	
8	A header-only CUDA library implementing a parallel hash table for NVIDIA GPUs, using Robin Hood open-addressing with bucketed (cache-line-aligned), sub-warp-cooperative probing.
9	The design target is bandwidth-bound performance on large tables.
10	
11	## Repository layout
12	
13	- `include/gpurhh/` — public headers.
14	  This is what users `#include` after adding `include/` to their include path.
15	  The library is header-only; everything ships from here.
16	- `tests/` — tests built against the public header.
17	  Each `test_*.cu` file is compiled as its own executable with its own `main`, scoped to one topic (a public method, a trait, etc.).
18	  Shared driver kernels, CUDA error-check operator, and host-side bulk helpers live in `tests/common.cuh`; small CUDA utilities like `CUDA_CHECK` live in `tests/utils.cuh`.
19	- `examples/` — small standalone programs demonstrating use of the library.
20	  Currently a placeholder; will be filled in once `View::insert` and `View::get` are implemented.
21	- `notes/` — design notes, written for humans.
22	  These files are the canonical record of *why* the implementation looks the way it does.
23	  They are intended to eventually become the project's documentation, but they are not documentation yet — they are working notes that capture decisions as we make them.
24	  Read these before touching code.
25	- `Makefile` — builds tests and examples with `nvcc`.
26	  `make test` builds and runs all tests; `make clean` wipes `build/`. Defaults to C++20 and `sm_80`; override via env vars (`make ARCH=sm_75 CXX_STD=c++17`).
27	
28	When new design decisions are made or revised, update the relevant note in `notes/` rather than scattering rationale across commit messages or code comments.
29	
30	## Conventions for working in this repo
31	
32	- **Notes are working documents that will become documentation.**
33	  Keep them prose-first and structured.
34	  Avoid jargon shorthand that only makes sense inside one conversation, since these will eventually be read by people who weren't in the room.
35	- **Architecture-derived constants are derived, not chosen.**
36	  `BucketSize`, `TileSize`, `TilesPerWarp` are all functions of `SlotBytes`, `CacheLineBytes`, and `WarpSize`.
37	  The latter two should be table-level template parameters, not literals embedded in the implementation.
38	
39	## Out of scope (for now)
40	
41	- Deletion (insert + lookup first).
42	- Resize / dynamic growth.
43	- Multi-GPU.
44	- AMD / non-NVIDIA backends — but the abstractions should not preclude them.
45	
```

</details>

## User — 2026-05-16T03:30:42.366Z

<ide_selection>The user selected the lines 461 to 461 from [repo]/include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh:
const

This may or may not be related to the current task.</ide_selection>

Why is there a second const in ::View::get(..

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-16T03:30:54.299Z

<em>Thinking…</em>

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-16T03:31:00.956Z

Two `const`s in different positions doing two unrelated things:

```cpp
template <class Tile>
__device__ bool get(const Tile& tile, Key key, Value& out_value) const;
//                  ^^^^^                                       ^^^^^
//                  (1)                                         (2)
```

**(1) `const Tile&`** — the parameter is a const reference. We don't mutate the tile object inside `get`; passing it as `const Tile&` documents that and lets the compiler reject any accidental mutation. (The cooperative-group methods we call on it — `ballot`, `shfl`, `thread_rank` — are themselves const member functions of `thread_block_tile`, so this binds correctly.)

**(2) Trailing `const`** — this makes `get` itself a const member function: it promises not to modify `*this` (the View). Since `View::get` is a read-only operation, the slots/capacity/hash members are not written. Without this `const`, you couldn't call `get` on a `const View&`, and the compiler couldn't enforce that `get` is actually read-only.

For comparison, `View::insert` has only the first `const`, not the trailing one — because `insert` does modify the table memory through `slots_`. Even though `slots_` itself isn't reassigned, the pointed-to slots are mutated, and we don't want to lie about that with a `const` qualifier.

Both `const`s are doing real work; neither is redundant.

---

## User — 2026-05-16T03:31:38.757Z

Does it matter that the underlying arrays might get mutated by another kernel running in parallel?

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-16T03:32:04.117Z

<em>Thinking…</em>

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-16T03:32:15.845Z

Good distinction to draw out. The `const` says nothing about whether the *underlying slot memory* can be mutated — it only constrains what `get` does to `*this` (the View object).

**On the const question:** the View has a `Bucket*` pointer member. `const View` means the pointer can't be reassigned, but the bytes the pointer points to can still be mutated by anything that has the same address. This is the same as `std::vector::operator[] const` returning a non-const reference to elements — the vector is const, the elements aren't. So `View::get const` is correctly saying "I don't write through this View," even though other threads are writing through their own copies of the View at the same time.

**On the concurrency question — which I think is what you're really asking:** yes, this does matter, but it's a *separate* concern from const-correctness. A `get` running concurrently with `insert`s on the same table can:

1. **Miss a key that was just inserted** — `get`'s scan visited the home bucket before the insert's CAS landed there. False negative.
2. **Miss a key whose Robin Hood displacement is in flight** — `get` reads slot S which had key K, but K has just been displaced to a later slot. By the time `get` would catch K at the new position, K's old slot may show as occupied by a different key or empty, and `get` may terminate early. Another false negative.

Neither produces *corruption*: the table itself stays consistent and the Robin Hood invariant holds at every slot. But `get` is only guaranteed to be correct relative to a "snapshot" of the table that exists at no specific moment.

For our usage that's fine because:

- Our tests serialize: `bulk_insert(...)` followed by `bulk_find(...)` with a `cudaDeviceSynchronize()` between them, so no insert/get overlap.
- A workload that interleaves inserts and gets across kernel launches gets serialization for free through stream ordering.
- Within a single kernel that mixes operations from different tiles, false-negative-`get` is the actual semantic.

If we ever wanted *linearizable* get-during-insert semantics, we'd need per-slot versioning or retry-on-displacement logic in `get`, with measurable performance cost. Worth noting in the design notes as a known property — want me to add it?

---

We assume\n// view.get broadcasts the located value to every lane in the tile, and have\n// lane 0 perform the store to keep the store count to one per key.\n__global__ void get_kernel(View view,\n                                  const std::uint32_t* keys,\n                                  std::uint32_t* values_out,\n                                  int* found_out,\n                                  std::size_t n)\n{\n    auto block = cg::this_thread_block();\n    auto tile  = cg::tiled_partition<Table::tile_size>(block);\n\n    const std::size_t tiles_per_block = blockDim.x / Table::tile_size;\n    const std::size_t tile_id =\n        blockIdx.x * tiles_per_block + threadIdx.x / Table::tile_size;\n    const std::size_t total_tiles = gridDim.x * tiles_per_block;\n\n    for (std::size_t i = tile_id; i < n; i += total_tiles) {\n        std::uint32_t v = 0;\n        const bool ok = view.get(tile, keys[i], v);\n        if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) {\n            values_out[i] = ok ? v : 0;\n            found_out[i]  = ok ? 1 : 0;\n        }\n    }\n}",

## Assistant — 2026-05-16T17:21:38.995Z

API change complete. Summary:

**[include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh):**
- Added `#include <cuda/std/optional>`.
- `View::get` declaration: `bool get(const Tile&, Key, Value&) const` → `cuda::std::optional<Value> get(const Tile&, Key) const`.
- Definition body: the success path returns `cuda::std::optional<V>{tile.shfl(slot.value, matching_lane)}`; the three failure paths return `{}` (empty optional via braced init).
- Doc comments updated to reflect the new contract.

**[tests/common.cuh](tests/common.cuh):**
- `get_kernel` now does `const auto result = view.get(tile, keys[i]); ... values_out[i] = result.value_or(0); found_out[i] = result.has_value() ? 1 : 0;`. Tests using `bulk_get` need no changes — its external `(values, found)` array API is preserved at the host-side level.

Concurrency semantics note worth flagging (you may want this in the design notes if you care):

`cuda::std::optional<V>` is a value type returned by-value. Each lane in the tile runs the same control flow, hits the same return statement, and constructs an optional from the same broadcast value. There's no shared state. So the optional return preserves the property that every lane sees the same result — the cooperative semantics are unchanged from the bool + out-param version.

## Assistant — 2026-05-16T17:23:57.191Z

`return {};` works because the return type deduces to `cuda::std::optional<V>` and brace-init produces an empty optional, but it relies on context. The explicit canonical way is `cuda::std::nullopt` — it's the sentinel value designed to mean "no value" unambiguously.

## Assistant — 2026-05-16T17:29:10.602Z

## Assistant — 2026-05-16T17:29:19.671Z

Correct — under the hood `cuda::std::optional<V>` is roughly:

```cpp
struct optional<V> {
    V    value;       // (alignment may move this)
    bool has_value;
};
```

so it's an 8-byte struct for `Value = uint32_t` (4 bytes data + 1 byte bool + 3 bytes padding). Same total information as `bool` + writing through a `Value&` reference.

**Code-gen comparison:**

| | Old API (bool + out-ref) | New API (optional return) |
|---|---|---|
| Inside `get`, on hit | write value to `*out_value`, return `true` | construct optional with value, return it |
| Caller | reads bool and local `v` | reads optional's two fields |
| Registers used | ~1–2 (return value, plus `v` in caller's stack/reg) | ~1–2 (optional return packed) |
| Branches | 1 on the bool | 1 on `has_value()` |

The compiler inlines `has_value()` and `value_or()` to direct field reads/branches, so the resulting PTX is essentially identical to the old version. nvcc's return-value optimization handles 8-byte structs as register returns on every ABI we care about.

**One caveat worth flagging:** for very large `Value` types (think 32-byte structs or larger), returning by value can be more expensive than writing through a reference, because the return must traverse the calling convention's return path. For small Value types (≤ 16 bytes — our supported cases), they're equivalent. If we ever wanted to support large value types, that's the moment the bool + out-ref API might be marginally cheaper, but at that point a reference to a `Slot` or careful in-register packing would be the better answer anyway.

So for the current and any plausible near-term use: zero perf cost, cleaner API.

## Assistant — 2026-05-16T17:40:09.317Z

Good plan. Let me sketch the concrete approach for get, then brainstorm for insert.

## Get tests — concrete plan

**Two enabling changes to the library:**

1. **Add `Bucket* data() noexcept` to `HashTable`** so tests can `cudaMemcpy` host-built state directly into the device-side bucket array. Standard "container.data()" idiom. Returning the raw pointer signals "you're poking the storage directly; you own correctness."

2. **Use an `IdentityHash` in the test files** instead of `default_hash`:
   ```cpp
   struct IdentityHash {
       __device__ std::uint32_t operator()(std::uint32_t k) const noexcept { return k; }
   };
   using TestTable = gpurhh::HashTable<std::uint32_t, std::uint32_t, IdentityHash>;
   ```
   With identity hash, key `K` has home slot `K & mask`, home bucket `(K & mask) / bucket_size`. Completely predictable, so we can hand-construct any valid table state.

**Test scenarios (one test per scenario), all running on a small table — say `capacity = 64`, so `bucket_size = 16`, `num_buckets = 4`:**

- **Hit in home bucket**: Place `(5, 50)` at slot 5. `get(5)` should return `{50}`.
- **Hit in second bucket**: Fill bucket 0 with keys 0..15 (all home-bucket 0). Place `(64, 6400)` at slot 16 (bucket 1, but its home is bucket 0 — a "displaced" entry). `get(64)` should probe past bucket 0 and find it. Returns `{6400}`.
- **Miss via empty slot in home bucket**: Empty table. `get(5)` returns nullopt.
- **Miss via empty slot in later bucket**: Bucket 0 full of keys 0..15, bucket 1 empty. `get(64)` should probe to bucket 1, see empty, return nullopt.
- **Miss via richer resident**: Bucket 0 full of keys 0..15 (home 0). Bucket 1 full of keys 16..31 (home 1, probe distance 0). `get(64)` (home 0) probes bucket 0 (no hit), advances to bucket 1, finds residents with probe 0 < ours (1) → Robin Hood early termination, returns nullopt.
- **Miss in fully-empty table**: Just verify the simplest negative path.

These run via a tiny one-tile-one-block helper kernel (`get_one_kernel`) that wraps a single `view.get` call. No need for the full `bulk_get` machinery.

**File organization suggestion:** keep `test_get.cu` (current end-to-end tests using `bulk_insert` + `bulk_get`) and add `test_get_state.cu` (new, manually-set state). The two exercise different layers.

---

## Insert tests — brainstorm

The "ground truth" question is the hard part. Four options I see:

| Option | What it checks | Cost | Coverage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A. Hand-computed sequences** | Insert a specific small sequence into a small table, check exact slot positions | Low setup; brittle to algorithm changes | Narrow but precise |
| **B. Robin Hood invariant scan** | After any insert, walk the table and verify the RH invariant holds (every occupied slot is at probe distance ≥ what the inserted-here resident would have had) | Low setup; reusable across tests | Broad correctness without committing to specific layouts |
| **C. CPU reference** | Write a sequential Robin Hood in host C++, run both, compare sets | Medium setup (one extra ~50-line file); flexibility | Strong — catches semantic divergence at any scale |
| **D. Property test** | Random inputs, check {keys present} = {keys inserted}, {get(k)} = {value last inserted for k} | Medium setup; non-deterministic but powerful | Strong on properties, weak on layout |

**My recommendation: A + B together**, with C added later if needed.

- **A handles the "what should it look like?" question** by reducing to small, hand-traceable cases.
  With IdentityHash and a 64-slot table, "insert keys 0..15 then key 64" has an obvious expected layout: keys 0..15 fill bucket 0 in order, key 64 displaces nothing (bucket 0 is full of equally-poor residents at probe 0; key 64 also has probe 0; no displacement), so key 64 lands in slot 16 with probe 1. We can hand-write the expected `Slot[]` array and compare.

- **B provides the safety net for cases where exact layout is hard to predict** (e.g., inserts where displacement chains get long).
  An invariant scanner is one function that runs after every test:
  ```cpp
  void assert_robin_hood_invariant(const std::vector<Slot>& state) {
      for (every occupied slot s at index i):
          home_bucket = hash(s.key) / bucket_size
          probe_distance = (i / bucket_size - home_bucket) mod num_buckets
          assert: probe_distance < max_probe_buckets
          assert: every slot in buckets at distance 0..probe_distance-1 from home_bucket is occupied
                  (i.e., the slot couldn't have been placed earlier)
  }
  ```
  Reusable across every insert test for free.

- **C (CPU reference) is the heavy artillery** — best for randomized differential testing once we want broad confidence. We don't need it yet for "a few inserts with collisions"; the hand-computed cases + invariant scan cover that. We'd add C when we want to randomize.

**Concrete near-full scenario using A + B:**

- Small table: `capacity = 32`, `bucket_size = 16`, `num_buckets = 2`. Two buckets total.
- Insert 30 keys with overlapping home buckets, designed to force displacement.
- Hand-compute the expected `Slot[32]` after the inserts.
- After running on GPU, `cudaMemcpy` state back, compare to expected array; also run the invariant scanner.
- Final case: try to insert a 33rd key (table at 30/32 = 0.94 load), check whether it succeeds or fails as expected.

**Question for you before I commit:** does A + B match what you wanted, or would you rather start with C (CPU reference) for stronger coverage from day one? My instinct says A + B is the right next step — it's lighter, gets you concrete layout testing immediately, and the invariant scanner is reusable infrastructure. C is the natural follow-up when we want randomized stress tests, which is a separate later question.

---

{
  "file_path": "[repo]/tests/test_combined.cu",
  "content": "// End-to-end tests combining gpurhh::HashTable::View::insert and View::get.\n//\n// These tests verify the public API behaves correctly when insert and get\n// are used together, without inspecting internal state. Tests for insert\n// and get individually live in test_insert.cu and test_get.cu, which seed\n// the table directly via cudaMemcpy and inspect its state.\n\n#include \"common.cuh\"\n\nnamespace {\n\nvoid test_insert_and_get_single_key() {\n    Table table(1024);\n    const std::uint32_t key   = 42;\n    const std::uint32_t value = 100;\n    bulk_insert(table, &key, &value, 1);\n\n    std::uint32_t got_value = 0;\n    int got_found = 0;\n    bulk_get(table, &key, &got_value, &got_found, 1);\n    assert(got_found == 1);\n    assert(got_value == value);\n}\n\nvoid test_insert_and_get_many_distinct_keys() {\n    Table table(1024);\n    constexpr std::size_t N = 100;\n    std::uint32_t keys[N], values[N];\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < N; ++i) {\n        keys[i]   = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i + 1);\n        values[i] = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i + 1000);\n    }\n    bulk_insert(table, keys, values, N);\n\n    std::uint32_t got_value[N] = {};\n    int got_found[N] = {};\n    bulk_get(table, keys, got_value, got_found, N);\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < N; ++i) {\n        assert(got_found[i] == 1);\n        assert(got_value[i] == values[i]);\n    }\n}\n\nvoid test_duplicate_key_updates_value() {\n    Table table(1024);\n    const std::uint32_t key = 7;\n    const std::uint32_t v1  = 111;\n    const std::uint32_t v2  = 222;\n    bulk_insert(table, &key, &v1, 1);\n    bulk_insert(table, &key, &v2, 1);\n\n    std::uint32_t got_value = 0;\n    int got_found = 0;\n    bulk_get(table, &key, &got_value, &got_found, 1);\n    assert(got_found == 1);\n    assert(got_value == v2);\n}\n\nvoid test_get_missing_key() {\n    Table table(1024);\n    const std::uint32_t key = 9999;\n    std::uint32_t got_value = 12345;   // poison\n    int got_found = 1;                 // poison\n    bulk_get(table, &key, &got_value, &got_found, 1);\n    assert(got_found == 0);\n}\n\nvoid test_get_strided_subset() {\n    Table table(1024);\n    constexpr std::size_t N = 50;\n    std::uint32_t keys[N], values[N];\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < N; ++i) {\n        keys[i]   = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i + 1);\n        values[i] = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(2 * i + 1);\n    }\n    bulk_insert(table, keys, values, N);\n\n    constexpr std::size_t M = 10;\n    std::uint32_t query[M], got_value[M];\n    int got_found[M];\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < M; ++i) query[i] = keys[5 * i];\n    bulk_get(table, query, got_value, got_found, M);\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < M; ++i) {\n        assert(got_found[i] == 1);\n        assert(got_value[i] == values[5 * i]);\n    }\n}\n\n} // namespace\n\nint main() {\n    test_insert_and_get_single_key();\n    test_insert_and_get_many_distinct_keys();\n    test_duplicate_key_updates_value();\n    test_get_missing_key();\n    test_get_strided_subset();\n    std::printf(\"test_combined: all tests passed.\\n\");\n    return 0;\n}\n"
}
```

</details>

{
  "file_path": "[repo]/tests/test_get.cu",
  "content": "// Isolated tests for gpurhh::HashTable::View::get.\n//\n// We use an IdentityHash so that key K maps deterministically to home\n// slot (K & capacity_mask) and home bucket (K & capacity_mask) / bucket_size.\n// This lets us hand-build any valid Robin Hood state on the host, write\n// it directly into the table via cudaMemcpy, and then verify that get\n// returns the expected result — all without invoking View::insert.\n\n#include \"common.cuh\"\n\n#include <vector>\n\nnamespace {\n\n// Identity hash for predictable bucket placement.\nstruct IdentityHash {\n    __device__ std::uint32_t operator()(std::uint32_t k) const noexcept { return k; }\n};\n\nusing TestTable = gpurhh::HashTable<std::uint32_t, std::uint32_t, IdentityHash>;\nusing TestView  = TestTable::View;\n\n// Single-tile kernel: one block, one tile, looks up one key.\n__global__ void get_one_kernel(TestView view, std::uint32_t key,\n                               std::uint32_t* value_out, int* found_out) {\n    auto block = cg::this_thread_block();\n    auto tile  = cg::tiled_partition<TestTable::tile_size>(block);\n    const auto result = view.get(tile, key);\n    if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) {\n        *value_out = result.value_or(0);\n        *found_out = result.has_value() ? 1 : 0;\n    }\n}\n\n// --- Host-side helpers ---------------------------------------------------\n\n// Construct an all-empty state with `capacity` slots.\nstd::vector<TestTable::Slot> empty_state(std::size_t capacity) {\n    return std::vector<TestTable::Slot>(capacity, {TestTable::empty_key, 0});\n}\n\n// cudaMemcpy a host-built state directly into the table's bucket array.\n// The flat Slot[] layout matches Bucket[N] layout because bucket_size *\n// sizeof(Slot) == sizeof(Bucket) with no internal padding.\nvoid set_state(TestTable& table, const std::vector<TestTable::Slot>& host_slots) {\n    assert(host_slots.size() == table.capacity());\n    cudaMemcpy(table.data(), host_slots.data(),\n               host_slots.size() * sizeof(TestTable::Slot),\n               cudaMemcpyHostToDevice) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n}\n\nstruct GetResult { bool found; std::uint32_t value; };\n\n// Look up one key and return the result.\nGetResult get_one(TestTable& table, std::uint32_t key) {\n    std::uint32_t* d_value = nullptr;\n    int* d_found = nullptr;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_value, sizeof(std::uint32_t)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_found, sizeof(int))           >> CUDA_CHECK;\n\n    get_one_kernel<<<1, TestTable::tile_size>>>(table.view(), key, d_value, d_found);\n    cudaGetLastError()      >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaDeviceSynchronize() >> CUDA_CHECK;\n\n    std::uint32_t value = 0;\n    int found = 0;\n    cudaMemcpy(&value, d_value, sizeof(std::uint32_t), cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMemcpy(&found, d_found, sizeof(int),           cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n\n    cudaFree(d_value);\n    cudaFree(d_found);\n    return {found != 0, value};\n}\n\n// --- Tests ---------------------------------------------------------------\n\n// Small table for all tests: capacity = 64, bucket_size = 16, num_buckets = 4.\nconstexpr std::size_t kCapacity = 64;\n\nvoid test_hit_in_home_bucket() {\n    // Place (5, 50) at slot 5 — its home slot. Lookup should find it\n    // immediately in the first probe.\n    TestTable table(kCapacity);\n    auto state = empty_state(kCapacity);\n    state[5] = {5, 50};\n    set_state(table, state);\n\n    auto r = get_one(table, 5);\n    assert(r.found);\n    assert(r.value == 50);\n}\n\nvoid test_hit_in_second_bucket() {\n    // Fill bucket 0 with keys 0..15 (all home bucket 0). Place key 64\n    // (also home bucket 0, since 64 & 63 == 0) in slot 16 — i.e., bucket 1.\n    // Get(64) should probe bucket 0 (no hit, no empty, no richer because\n    // every resident has probe 0 == ours) and advance to bucket 1.\n    TestTable table(kCapacity);\n    auto state = empty_state(kCapacity);\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < TestTable::bucket_size; ++i) {\n        state[i] = {static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i),\n                    static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i * 10)};\n    }\n    state[TestTable::bucket_size] = {64, 6400};  // slot 16 = bucket 1, lane 0\n    set_state(table, state);\n\n    auto r = get_one(table, 64);\n    assert(r.found);\n    assert(r.value == 6400);\n}\n\nvoid test_miss_in_empty_table() {\n    TestTable table(kCapacity);\n    set_state(table, empty_state(kCapacity));\n\n    auto r = get_one(table, 42);\n    assert(!r.found);\n}\n\nvoid test_miss_via_empty_slot_in_home_bucket() {\n    // Bucket 0 partly occupied (slots 0, 1 only); slots 2..15 empty.\n    // Lookup of key 5 sees its home bucket and finds an empty slot in the\n    // ballot — Robin Hood would have placed it at one of those empties,\n    // so it isn't in the table.\n    TestTable table(kCapacity);\n    auto state = empty_state(kCapacity);\n    state[0] = {0, 100};\n    state[1] = {1, 101};\n    set_state(table, state);\n\n    auto r = get_one(table, 5);\n    assert(!r.found);\n}\n\nvoid test_miss_via_richer_resident() {\n    // Bucket 0 full of keys 0..15 (home bucket 0, probe 0).\n    // Bucket 1 full of keys 16..31 (home bucket 1, probe 0).\n    // Lookup of key 64 (home bucket 0): no match in bucket 0, no empty,\n    // residents all have probe 0 == ours so no richer-termination yet.\n    // Advance to bucket 1 (our probe now 1): residents have probe 0 < ours.\n    // Richer-resident termination fires → return nullopt.\n    TestTable table(kCapacity);\n    auto state = empty_state(kCapacity);\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < TestTable::bucket_size; ++i) {\n        state[i] = {static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i), 0};\n    }\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < TestTable::bucket_size; ++i) {\n        const std::uint32_t k = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(TestTable::bucket_size + i);\n        state[TestTable::bucket_size + i] = {k, 0};\n    }\n    set_state(table, state);\n\n    auto r = get_one(table, 64);\n    assert(!r.found);\n}\n\n} // namespace\n\nint main() {\n    test_hit_in_home_bucket();\n    test_hit_in_second_bucket();\n    test_miss_in_empty_table();\n    test_miss_via_empty_slot_in_home_bucket();\n    test_miss_via_richer_resident();\n    std::printf(\"test_get: all tests passed.\\n\");\n    return 0;\n}\n"
}
```

## Assistant — 2026-05-16T17:59:56.065Z

**→ Tool call: `Write`** (id `tool-186`)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "file_path": "[repo]/tests/test_insert.cu",
  "content": "// Isolated tests for gpurhh::HashTable::View::insert.\n//\n// We use an IdentityHash so that key K maps deterministically to home\n// slot (K & capacity_mask) and home bucket (K & capacity_mask) / bucket_size.\n// Each test:\n//   - Optionally seeds a pre-state via cudaMemcpy (so the test runs against\n//     a controlled starting layout, independent of insert correctness).\n//   - Runs one or more inserts.\n//   - Reads the table state back and either:\n//       (A) compares to a hand-computed expected layout, or\n//       (B) calls assert_robin_hood_invariant() to verify the structural\n//           invariant holds across the whole table.\n// Tests use both (A) and (B) — (A) for cases where the parallel insertion\n// outcome is hand-predictable, (B) as a safety net everywhere else.\n\n#include \"common.cuh\"\n\n#include <algorithm>\n#include <vector>\n\nnamespace {\n\n// Identity hash for predictable bucket placement.\nstruct IdentityHash {\n    __device__ std::uint32_t operator()(std::uint32_t k) const noexcept { return k; }\n};\n\nusing TestTable = gpurhh::HashTable<std::uint32_t, std::uint32_t, IdentityHash>;\nusing TestView  = TestTable::View;\n\n// Insertion kernel: one tile per (key, value) pair, tile-strided over n.\n__global__ void insert_many_kernel(TestView view,\n                                   const std::uint32_t* keys,\n                                   const std::uint32_t* values,\n                                   std::size_t n) {\n    auto block = cg::this_thread_block();\n    auto tile  = cg::tiled_partition<TestTable::tile_size>(block);\n    const std::size_t tiles_per_block = blockDim.x / TestTable::tile_size;\n    const std::size_t tile_id = blockIdx.x * tiles_per_block +\n                                 threadIdx.x / TestTable::tile_size;\n    const std::size_t total_tiles = gridDim.x * tiles_per_block;\n    for (std::size_t i = tile_id; i < n; i += total_tiles) {\n        view.insert(tile, keys[i], values[i]);\n    }\n}\n\n// --- Host-side helpers ---------------------------------------------------\n\nstd::vector<TestTable::Slot> empty_state(std::size_t capacity) {\n    return std::vector<TestTable::Slot>(capacity, {TestTable::empty_key, 0});\n}\n\nvoid set_state(TestTable& table, const std::vector<TestTable::Slot>& host_slots) {\n    assert(host_slots.size() == table.capacity());\n    cudaMemcpy(table.data(), host_slots.data(),\n               host_slots.size() * sizeof(TestTable::Slot),\n               cudaMemcpyHostToDevice) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n}\n\nstd::vector<TestTable::Slot> read_state(const TestTable& table) {\n    std::vector<TestTable::Slot> result(table.capacity());\n    cudaMemcpy(result.data(), table.data(),\n               result.size() * sizeof(TestTable::Slot),\n               cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    return result;\n}\n\n// Run a batch of inserts.\nvoid do_insert(TestTable& table,\n               const std::vector<std::uint32_t>& keys,\n               const std::vector<std::uint32_t>& values) {\n    assert(keys.size() == values.size());\n    if (keys.empty()) return;\n\n    std::uint32_t *d_keys = nullptr, *d_values = nullptr;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_keys,   keys.size()   * sizeof(std::uint32_t)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_values, values.size() * sizeof(std::uint32_t)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMemcpy(d_keys,   keys.data(),   keys.size()   * sizeof(std::uint32_t),\n               cudaMemcpyHostToDevice) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMemcpy(d_values, values.data(), values.size() * sizeof(std::uint32_t),\n               cudaMemcpyHostToDevice) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n\n    constexpr int block_size = 128;\n    const int tiles_per_block = block_size / TestTable::tile_size;\n    const int grid_size =\n        static_cast<int>((keys.size() + tiles_per_block - 1) / tiles_per_block);\n    insert_many_kernel<<<grid_size, block_size>>>(\n        table.view(), d_keys, d_values, keys.size());\n    cudaGetLastError()      >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaDeviceSynchronize() >> CUDA_CHECK;\n\n    cudaFree(d_keys);\n    cudaFree(d_values);\n}\n\n// Robin Hood invariant: for every occupied slot at index i, the resident's\n// home bucket H_R satisfies (i / bucket_size - H_R) mod num_buckets ≤ probe\n// cap, AND every bucket strictly between H_R and i / bucket_size is fully\n// occupied (no empty slot). The second condition is the key invariant: an\n// empty slot at distance < the resident's actual probe would mean the\n// resident should have been placed there instead.\nvoid assert_robin_hood_invariant(const std::vector<TestTable::Slot>& state) {\n    constexpr auto B = static_cast<std::size_t>(TestTable::bucket_size);\n    const auto num_buckets = state.size() / B;\n    const auto cap_mask = state.size() - 1;\n\n    // Pre-compute which buckets contain at least one empty slot.\n    std::vector<bool> bucket_has_empty(num_buckets, false);\n    for (std::size_t b = 0; b < num_buckets; ++b) {\n        for (std::size_t s = 0; s < B; ++s) {\n            if (state[b * B + s].key == TestTable::empty_key) {\n                bucket_has_empty[b] = true;\n                break;\n            }\n        }\n    }\n\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < state.size(); ++i) {\n        if (state[i].key == TestTable::empty_key) continue;\n\n        const std::size_t home_bucket = (state[i].key & cap_mask) / B;\n        const std::size_t this_bucket = i / B;\n        const std::size_t probe =\n            (this_bucket + num_buckets - home_bucket) % num_buckets;\n\n        // Probe distance must be within the cap.\n        assert(probe < static_cast<std::size_t>(TestTable::max_probe_buckets));\n\n        // Every bucket strictly between home and this one must be fully\n        // occupied; otherwise the resident should have been placed earlier.\n        for (std::size_t step = 0; step < probe; ++step) {\n            const std::size_t b = (home_bucket + step) % num_buckets;\n            assert(!bucket_has_empty[b] &&\n                   \"Robin Hood invariant violated: an earlier bucket has an \"\n                   \"empty slot that this resident could have occupied\");\n        }\n    }\n}\n\n// --- Tests ---------------------------------------------------------------\n\n// Small table: capacity 64, bucket_size 16, num_buckets 4.\nconstexpr std::size_t kCapacity = 64;\n\n// (A) single key inserted into empty table lands at its home slot.\nvoid test_insert_single_key_into_empty_table() {\n    TestTable table(kCapacity);\n    do_insert(table, {5}, {50});\n    auto state = read_state(table);\n\n    assert(state[5].key == 5);\n    assert(state[5].value == 50);\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < kCapacity; ++i) {\n        if (i != 5) assert(state[i].key == TestTable::empty_key);\n    }\n    assert_robin_hood_invariant(state);\n}\n\n// (A) keys with distinct home buckets all land at their home slots.\nvoid test_insert_keys_into_distinct_home_buckets() {\n    TestTable table(kCapacity);\n    do_insert(table, {0, 16, 32, 48}, {100, 200, 300, 400});\n    auto state = read_state(table);\n\n    assert(state[0].key  ==  0 && state[0].value  == 100);\n    assert(state[16].key == 16 && state[16].value == 200);\n    assert(state[32].key == 32 && state[32].value == 300);\n    assert(state[48].key == 48 && state[48].value == 400);\n    assert_robin_hood_invariant(state);\n}\n\n// (B) fill bucket 0 with 16 home-0 keys. No displacement: every key has\n// probe 0. Bucket 1..3 should be untouched. The exact (key → slot) mapping\n// within bucket 0 is non-deterministic under concurrent insertion, so we\n// check set membership rather than positions.\nvoid test_insert_fills_home_bucket() {\n    TestTable table(kCapacity);\n    std::vector<std::uint32_t> keys(16), values(16);\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < 16; ++i) {\n        keys[i]   = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i);\n        values[i] = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i * 10);\n    }\n    do_insert(table, keys, values);\n    auto state = read_state(table);\n\n    std::vector<bool> seen(16, false);\n    for (std::size_t s = 0; s < 16; ++s) {\n        const auto& slot = state[s];\n        assert(slot.key != TestTable::empty_key);\n        assert(slot.key < 16);\n        assert(slot.value == slot.key * 10);\n        seen[slot.key] = true;\n    }\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < 16; ++i) assert(seen[i]);\n\n    for (std::size_t i = 16; i < kCapacity; ++i) {\n        assert(state[i].key == TestTable::empty_key);\n    }\n    assert_robin_hood_invariant(state);\n}\n\n// (A) deterministic displacement: start from a pre-state where bucket 0 is\n// full of home-0 keys (0..15) AND bucket 1 is full of home-1 keys (16..31).\n// Then insert key 64 (home bucket 0). It must walk bucket 0 with no\n// progress (no match, no empty, equal probe), advance to bucket 1, find a\n// richer resident (lane 0 / slot 16, key 16, probe 0 < ours 1), CAS it\n// out, take key 16 as the new in-flight pair, advance to bucket 2 (key 16\n// has home bucket 1, evicted probe 0 + 1 = 1 → bucket 1+1=2), and land in\n// bucket 2's first empty slot (lane 0 → slot 32).\nvoid test_insert_displaces_richer_resident() {\n    TestTable table(kCapacity);\n    auto state = empty_state(kCapacity);\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < 32; ++i) {\n        state[i] = {static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i),\n                    static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i * 10)};\n    }\n    set_state(table, state);\n\n    do_insert(table, {64}, {640});\n    auto after = read_state(table);\n\n    // Key 64 displaced key 16 at slot 16.\n    assert(after[16].key   == 64);\n    assert(after[16].value == 640);\n\n    // Key 16 landed in slot 32 (bucket 2 lane 0), unchanged value.\n    assert(after[32].key   == 16);\n    assert(after[32].value == 160);\n\n    // Keys 0..15 unchanged.\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < 16; ++i) {\n        assert(after[i].key   == i);\n        assert(after[i].value == i * 10);\n    }\n    // Keys 17..31 unchanged (slot 16 was overwritten, the rest weren't).\n    for (std::size_t i = 17; i < 32; ++i) {\n        assert(after[i].key   == i);\n        assert(after[i].value == i * 10);\n    }\n    // Bucket 2 slots 33..47 and all of bucket 3 should still be empty.\n    for (std::size_t i = 33; i < kCapacity; ++i) {\n        assert(after[i].key == TestTable::empty_key);\n    }\n\n    assert_robin_hood_invariant(after);\n}\n\n// (B) near-full table: 45 keys = load factor 0.7. Keys 1..45 have home\n// buckets 0, 1, or 2 (no key in bucket 3); each home bucket has at most 16\n// keys, so no displacement *out of* any bucket is forced — but several\n// bucket-0 and bucket-2 keys may still land at non-home slots within their\n// home bucket. Verify that all 45 keys are present and the invariant\n// holds.\nvoid test_insert_near_full() {\n    TestTable table(kCapacity);\n    constexpr std::size_t N = 45;\n    std::vector<std::uint32_t> keys(N), values(N);\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < N; ++i) {\n        keys[i]   = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i + 1);\n        values[i] = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i * 7);\n    }\n    do_insert(table, keys, values);\n    auto state = read_state(table);\n\n    std::vector<std::uint32_t> keys_seen;\n    for (const auto& slot : state) {\n        if (slot.key != TestTable::empty_key) keys_seen.push_back(slot.key);\n    }\n    std::sort(keys_seen.begin(), keys_seen.end());\n\n    std::vector<std::uint32_t> expected(N);\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < N; ++i) expected[i] = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(i + 1);\n    assert(keys_seen == expected);\n\n    assert_robin_hood_invariant(state);\n}\n\n} // namespace\n\nint main() {\n    test_insert_single_key_into_empty_table();\n    test_insert_keys_into_distinct_home_buckets();\n    test_insert_fills_home_bucket();\n    test_insert_displaces_richer_resident();\n    test_insert_near_full();\n    std::printf(\"test_insert: all tests passed.\\n\");\n    return 0;\n}\n"
}
```

</details>
