<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
1	#pragma once
2	
3	// gpurhh — GPU Robin Hood Hash table.
4	//
5	// Header-only CUDA library implementing a parallel hash table for NVIDIA GPUs,
6	// using Robin Hood open-addressing with bucketed, sub-warp-cooperative probing.
7	//
8	// See the notes/ directory for the design rationale behind this layout.
9	//
10	// The hash table itself lives in device memory. Host code constructs and
11	// destructs a HashTable, which allocates and zero-initializes the slot array
12	// on the device. All operations on the contents — insert, get — are device
13	// code, exposed through the View handle which is meant to be passed by value
14	// into user kernels. Data movement between host and device (e.g. building the
15	// input arrays, reading back results) is the caller's responsibility.
16	
17	#include <array>
18	#include <bit>
19	#include <cstddef>
20	#include <cstdint>
21	#include <cstdio>
22	#include <type_traits>
23	#include <vector>
24	
25	#include <cooperative_groups.h>
26	#include <cuda/atomic>
27	#include <cuda/std/bit>
28	#include <cuda/std/optional>
29	#include <cuda_runtime.h>
30	
31	namespace gpurhh {
32	
33	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
34	// Defaults: empty-key sentinel and hash function.
35	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
36	
37	// Reserved value signaling an empty slot. Users may not insert this key.
38	//
39	// Default is the all-bits-set representation of `Key` — every byte is 0xFF,
40	// so `value` is the maximum representable value (e.g. 0xFFFFFFFFu for
41	// uint32_t). The all-ones bit pattern lets `HashTable` initialize its
42	// slot array with a single cudaMemset(0xFF, ...) at construction.
43	//
44	// Compiles for: unsigned integral types only. For signed integers,
45	// floating-point types, scoped enums, or user-defined structs, supply an
46	// explicit `EmptyKey` template argument to `HashTable` (and likely your
47	// own `Hash` functor too). We deliberately don't provide a default for
48	// signed types because no single repeated byte pattern gives a "good"
49	// signed sentinel: 0xFF gives -1 (a common legitimate key); 0x80 gives
50	// type-dependent values that are only INT_MIN for int8_t; INT_MIN itself
51	// isn't a repeated-byte pattern. Leaving the choice to the user avoids
52	// committing to any of those compromises.
53	//
54	// The trait exposes both `key` (the empty-key value itself) and
55	// `memset_byte` (the byte that, repeated, produces that value).
56	// `HashTable`'s constructor uses `memset_byte` to init the slot array
57	// with one cudaMemset call.
58	template <class Key>
59	struct default_empty_key {
60	    static_assert(std::is_unsigned_v<Key>,
61	                  "default_empty_key only provides a default for unsigned "
62	                  "integral types; supply an explicit EmptyKey for signed "
63	                  "or other key types");
64	
65	    static constexpr unsigned char memset_byte = 0xFFu;
66	    static constexpr Key           key         = static_cast<Key>(~Key{});
67	};
68	
69	// Compile-time verification for the supported integer types.
70	static_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint8_t >::key == 0xFFu);
71	static_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint16_t>::key == 0xFFFFu);
72	static_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint32_t>::key == 0xFFFFFFFFu);
73	static_assert(default_empty_key<std::uint64_t>::key == 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFull);
74	
75	// Default hash: MurmurHash3 32-bit finalizer (fmix32) for 4-byte keys; an
76	// 8-byte specialization is reserved for when 128-bit slot support is added.
77	// Both are cheap on the GPU, branch-free, and have strong avalanche. Users
78	// can supply their own via the Hash template parameter.
79	
80	namespace detail {
81	
82	__device__ inline std::uint32_t fmix32(std::uint32_t h) noexcept {
83	    h ^= h >> 16;
84	    h *= 0x85ebca6bu;
85	    h ^= h >> 13;
86	    h *= 0xc2b2ae35u;
87	    h ^= h >> 16;
88	    return h;
89	}
90	
91	__device__ inline std::uint64_t fmix64(std::uint64_t k) noexcept {
92	    k ^= k >> 33;
93	    k *= 0xff51afd7ed558ccdULL;
94	    k ^= k >> 33;
95	    k *= 0xc4ceb9fe1a85ec53ULL;
96	    k ^= k >> 33;
97	    return k;
98	}
99	
100	// Round `n` up to the next power of two. Returns 1 for n == 0 or n == 1.
101	constexpr inline std::size_t next_pow2(std::size_t n) noexcept {
102	    if (n <= 1) return 1;
103	    return std::size_t{1} << std::bit_width(n - 1);
104	}
105	
106	} // namespace detail
107	
108	// Primary template: dispatch on sizeof(Key). Defined only for 4- and 8-byte
109	// keys, matching the supported slot widths.
110	template <class Key, std::size_t = sizeof(Key)>
111	struct default_hash;
112	
113	template <class Key>
114	struct default_hash<Key, 4> {
115	    __device__ inline std::uint32_t operator()(Key key) const noexcept {
116	        return detail::fmix32(cuda::std::bit_cast<std::uint32_t>(key));
117	    }
118	};
119	
120	template <class Key>
121	struct default_hash<Key, 8> {
122	    __device__ inline std::uint64_t operator()(Key key) const noexcept {
123	        return detail::fmix64(cuda::std::bit_cast<std::uint64_t>(key));
124	    }
125	};
126	
127	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
128	// Reduction operators applied by View::insert when an existing key is hit.
129	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
130	//
131	// Called as `reduce(existing_value, incoming_value)`; the returned value is
132	// stored in the slot via the same atomic CAS that the insertion would do.
133	// Stateless and trivially copyable so they sit inside a View by value.
134	
135	struct replace_op {
136	    template <class T>
137	    __device__ T operator()(T /*existing*/, T incoming) const noexcept {
138	        return incoming;
139	    }
140	};
141	
142	struct sum_op {
143	    template <class T>
144	    __device__ T operator()(T existing, T incoming) const noexcept {
145	        return existing + incoming;
146	    }
147	};
148	
149	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
150	// HashTable: host-side owner of the device-resident slot array.
151	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
152	
153	template <
154	    class Key,
155	    class Value,
156	    class Hash             = default_hash<Key>,
157	    Key   EmptyKey         = default_empty_key<Key>::key,
158	    class Reduction        = replace_op,
159	    int   CacheLineBytes   = 128,
160	    int   WarpSize         = 32,
161	    int   MaxProbeBuckets  = 8
162	>
163	class HashTable {
164	public:
165	    using key_type   = Key;
166	    using value_type = Value;
167	    using hasher     = Hash;
168	
169	    // Packed (key, value) slot. One Slot is the unit of an atomic CAS.
170	    // The alignas forces alignof(Slot) == sizeof(Slot), which is required
171	    // by cuda::atomic_ref<Slot> (it needs the storage aligned to the
172	    // atomic op width). For the supported Key/Value pairs (same-size
173	    // integral types) sizeof(Key) + sizeof(Value) == sizeof(Slot) with no
174	    // internal padding.
175	    struct alignas(sizeof(Key) + sizeof(Value)) Slot {
176	        Key   key;
177	        Value value;
178	    };
179	
180	    // ---- Architecture-derived constants ----
181	
182	    static constexpr Key empty_key        = EmptyKey;
183	    static constexpr int slot_bytes       = sizeof(Slot);
184	    static constexpr int cache_line_bytes = CacheLineBytes;
185	    static constexpr int warp_size        = WarpSize;
186	    static constexpr int bucket_size      = CacheLineBytes / slot_bytes;
187	    static constexpr int tile_size        = bucket_size;
188	    static constexpr int tiles_per_warp   = WarpSize / bucket_size;
189	
190	    // Slot / cache-line invariants.
191	    static_assert(slot_bytes == 8 || slot_bytes == 16,
192	                  "slot must be 8 or 16 bytes (a 64-bit or 128-bit packed "
193	                  "key+value pair). The 16-byte case uses 128-bit CAS via "
194	                  "cuda::atomic_ref — single-instruction on sm_90+, "
195	                  "emulated by libcu++ on earlier architectures.");
196	    static_assert(slot_bytes <= CacheLineBytes,
197	                  "slot does not fit in a cache line");
198	    static_assert(CacheLineBytes % slot_bytes == 0,
199	                  "cache line must be a whole multiple of slot size");
200	
201	    // Tile / bucket invariants. tile_size == bucket_size by construction; the
202	    // cooperative_groups::thread_block_tile requirements drive these.
203	    static_assert(tile_size > 0,
204	                  "tile size must be positive");
205	    static_assert((tile_size & (tile_size - 1)) == 0,
206	                  "tile size must be a power of two "
207	                  "(required by cooperative_groups::tiled_partition)");
208	    static_assert(tile_size <= WarpSize,
209	                  "tile size must not exceed warp size");
210	    static_assert(WarpSize % bucket_size == 0,
211	                  "warp size must be a whole multiple of bucket size");
212	
213	    // A bucket is a contiguous run of `bucket_size` slots aligned to one
214	    // cache line. The alignas guarantees that loading a bucket via a
215	    // cooperative tile produces a single coalesced cache-line transaction
216	    // (the bucket can never straddle two cache lines).
217	    struct alignas(CacheLineBytes) Bucket {
218	        Slot slots[bucket_size];
219	    };
220	
221	    static_assert(sizeof(Bucket) == CacheLineBytes,
222	                  "bucket must occupy exactly one cache line");
223	    static_assert(alignof(Bucket) == CacheLineBytes,
224	                  "bucket alignment must equal cache line size");
225	
226	    // Probe-length cap. An insert that would place a key further than this
227	    // many buckets from its home bucket fails (the caller is expected to
228	    // rehash into a larger table). Get takes advantage of the same cap: if
229	    // it probes this many buckets without finding the key, the key cannot
230	    // be in the table, because insert would have failed before placing it
231	    // beyond the cap.
232	    //
233	    // The default of 8 comfortably handles load factors up to ~0.9 with
234	    // bucket_size 16, where the expected longest probe is well under the
235	    // cap. The cap exists to bound the worst case for adversarial inputs
236	    // and very high load factors. Users targeting load factors above ~0.9
237	    // can override via the `MaxProbeBuckets` template parameter; lowering
238	    // it tightens the time bound at the cost of higher insert-failure
239	    // probability.
240	    static constexpr int max_probe_buckets = MaxProbeBuckets;
241	    static_assert(max_probe_buckets > 0,
242	                  "max_probe_buckets must be positive");
243	
244	    // Byte value used to initialize the slot array via cudaMemset. Derived
245	    // from `empty_key`: every byte of `empty_key` must equal this byte for
246	    // the cudaMemset-based init path to produce a correctly-empty slot array.
247	    static constexpr unsigned char empty_key_byte =
248	        std::bit_cast<std::array<unsigned char, sizeof(Key)>>(empty_key)[0];
249	
250	    // EmptyKey memsettability invariant.
251	    static_assert([]() {
252	        const auto bytes = std::bit_cast<std::array<unsigned char, sizeof(Key)>>(empty_key);
253	        for (std::size_t i = 1; i < sizeof(Key); ++i) {
254	            if (bytes[i] != bytes[0]) return false;
255	        }
256	        return true;
257	    }(),
258	    "EmptyKey must be a single byte pattern repeated across sizeof(Key) "
259	    "bytes so the slot array can be initialized via cudaMemset. Use the "
260	    "default for unsigned keys, or provide an EmptyKey of the form 0xBB..BB.");
261	
262	    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
263	    // View: lightweight device-side handle. Trivially copyable; pass by value
264	    // into user kernels. Holds only a device pointer and a couple of integers.
265	    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
266	    class View {
267	    public:
268	        // Cooperative single-key insert.
269	        // Called by a tile of exactly tile_size threads in lock-step. Returns
270	        // true on success, false if the table is full (probe length cap hit).
271	        template <class Tile>
272	        __device__ bool insert(const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value);
273	
274	        // Cooperative single-key lookup. Returns the stored value if the
275	        // key is present, or an empty optional otherwise. Every lane in the
276	        // tile receives the same result.
277	        template <class Tile>
278	        __device__ cuda::std::optional<Value> get(const Tile& tile, Key key) const;
279	
280	        __host__ __device__ std::size_t capacity() const noexcept { return capacity_; }
281	
282	    private:
283	        friend class HashTable;
284	
285	        Bucket*     buckets_       = nullptr;
286	        std::size_t capacity_      = 0;   // power of two, in slots
287	        std::size_t capacity_mask_ = 0;   // capacity_ - 1, for modulo via AND
288	        Hash        hash_{};
289	        Reduction   reduce_{};
290	    };
291	
292	    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
293	    // Host-side resource management.
294	    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
295	
296	    // Allocates a slot array on the current device sized for at least
297	    // `min_capacity` slots, rounded up to the next power of two, and
298	    // initializes every slot to (empty_key, _).
299	    explicit HashTable(std::size_t min_capacity);
300	
301	    ~HashTable();
302	
303	    HashTable(const HashTable&)            = delete;
304	    HashTable& operator=(const HashTable&) = delete;
305	    HashTable(HashTable&&) noexcept;
306	    HashTable& operator=(HashTable&&) noexcept;
307	
308	    // Returns a device-side handle for use inside user kernels.
309	    View view() const noexcept;
310	
311	    // Actual capacity (input capacity rounded up to a power of two).
312	    std::size_t capacity() const noexcept { return capacity_; }
313	
314	#ifdef GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS
315	    // Direct access to the underlying device-resident bucket array, of
316	    // length `capacity() / bucket_size`. Only available when the including
317	    // translation unit defines GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS before this
318	    // header is included. Provided for tests and diagnostics that need to
319	    // inspect or seed table state directly via cudaMemcpy. Bypasses the
320	    // table's concurrency contract — there is no synchronization against
321	    // concurrent insert / get from other kernels.
322	    Bucket*       data() noexcept       { return buckets_; }
323	    const Bucket* data() const noexcept { return buckets_; }
324	#endif
325	
326	    // Pretty-print slots in [start, stop) to stdout, with bucket
327	    // boundaries marked. Provided for debugging and diagnostics; copies
328	    // (stop - start) slots from device to host and is not synchronized
329	    // against concurrent insert / get from other kernels.
330	    void print_slots(std::size_t start, std::size_t stop) const;
331	
332	private:
333	    Bucket*     buckets_  = nullptr;
334	    std::size_t capacity_ = 0;  // in slots
335	};
336	
337	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
338	// Definitions.
339	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
340	//
341	// Function bodies live in the header because the class is a template and
342	// instantiations need full definitions visible at the call site. Host-side
343	// methods (constructor, destructor, move ops, view) are implemented; the
344	// device-side View::insert and View::get bodies are still TODO.
345	
346	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>
347	HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::HashTable(std::size_t min_capacity) {
348	    // Round up to the next power of two, but never below a single bucket —
349	    // the bucketed probing logic requires at least bucket_size slots.
350	    const std::size_t rounded = detail::next_pow2(min_capacity);
351	    capacity_ = rounded < bucket_size ? std::size_t{bucket_size} : rounded;
352	
353	    const std::size_t num_buckets = capacity_ / bucket_size;
354	
355	    // Allocate the bucket array on the current device. cudaMalloc leaves
356	    // buckets_ as nullptr on failure; callers can check via
357	    // cudaPeekAtLastError().
358	    cudaMalloc(&buckets_, num_buckets * sizeof(Bucket));
359	
360	    // Initialize every byte to empty_key_byte. For the supported default
361	    // (unsigned EmptyKey = 0xFF..FF) this writes the empty sentinel into
362	    // each slot's key field in a single bandwidth-bound DMA.
363	    cudaMemset(buckets_, empty_key_byte, num_buckets * sizeof(Bucket));
364	}
365	
366	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>
367	HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::~HashTable() {
368	    // cudaFree(nullptr) is a documented no-op, so no need to guard.
369	    cudaFree(buckets_);
370	}
371	
372	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>
373	HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::HashTable(HashTable&& other) noexcept
374	    : buckets_(other.buckets_), capacity_(other.capacity_)
375	{
376	    other.buckets_  = nullptr;
377	    other.capacity_ = 0;
378	}
379	
380	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>
381	HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>&
382	HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::operator=(HashTable&& other) noexcept {
383	    if (this != &other) {
384	        // Free the current allocation before taking ownership of the new one.
385	        // cudaFree(nullptr) is a no-op, so this is safe even if *this is in
386	        // a moved-from / empty state.
387	        cudaFree(buckets_);
388	        buckets_        = other.buckets_;
389	        capacity_       = other.capacity_;
390	        other.buckets_  = nullptr;
391	        other.capacity_ = 0;
392	    }
393	    return *this;
394	}
395	
396	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>
397	typename HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::View
398	HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::view() const noexcept {
399	    View v;
400	    v.buckets_       = buckets_;
401	    v.capacity_      = capacity_;
402	    // For a usable table capacity_ is a power of two and the mask is
403	    // capacity_ - 1. For a moved-from / empty table capacity_ is 0; we set
404	    // the mask to 0 explicitly to avoid a wrap-around to ~0 which would be
405	    // misleading even though the View is unusable in that state anyway.
406	    v.capacity_mask_ = capacity_ > 0 ? capacity_ - 1 : 0;
407	    // v.hash_ and v.reduce_ are default-constructed by View's in-class
408	    // default initializers.
409	    return v;
410	}
411	
412	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>
413	void HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::print_slots(
414	    std::size_t start, std::size_t stop) const
415	{
416	    if (start >= stop || stop > capacity_) {
417	        std::printf("print_slots: invalid range [%zu, %zu) (capacity = %zu)\n",
418	                    start, stop, capacity_);
419	        return;
420	    }
421	
422	    // Copy the requested slot range to host. The Bucket array is laid out
423	    // such that reinterpreting it as a flat Slot[] is byte-equivalent —
424	    // bucket_size * sizeof(Slot) == sizeof(Bucket) with no internal padding.
425	    const std::size_t n = stop - start;
426	    std::vector<Slot> host_slots(n);
427	    cudaMemcpy(host_slots.data(),
428	               reinterpret_cast<const Slot*>(buckets_) + start,
429	               n * sizeof(Slot),
430	               cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost);
431	
432	    std::printf("gpurhh::HashTable slots [%zu, %zu) "
433	                "(capacity = %zu, bucket_size = %d):\n",
434	                start, stop, capacity_, bucket_size);
435	
436	    constexpr std::size_t B = static_cast<std::size_t>(bucket_size);
437	    std::size_t current_bucket = static_cast<std::size_t>(-1);
438	    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
439	        const std::size_t slot_idx   = start + i;
440	        const std::size_t bucket_idx = slot_idx / B;
441	        if (bucket_idx != current_bucket) {
442	            std::printf("  bucket %zu:\n", bucket_idx);
443	            current_bucket = bucket_idx;
444	        }
445	
446	        const Slot& slot = host_slots[i];
447	        std::printf("    slot %zu: ", slot_idx);
448	        if (slot.key == empty_key) {
449	            std::printf("empty\n");
450	        } else if constexpr (sizeof(K) == 4 && sizeof(V) == 4) {
451	            std::printf("key=0x%08x value=0x%08x\n",
452	                        static_cast<unsigned int>(slot.key),
453	                        static_cast<unsigned int>(slot.value));
454	        } else {
455	            std::printf("key=0x%016llx value=0x%016llx\n",
456	                        static_cast<unsigned long long>(slot.key),
457	                        static_cast<unsigned long long>(slot.value));
458	        }
459	    }
460	}
461	
462	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>
463	template <class Tile>
464	__device__ bool HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::View::insert(
465	    const Tile& tile, K key, V value)
466	{
467	    // Bucketed Robin Hood insertion with lock-free CAS. The tile carries an
468	    // in-flight pair (cur_key, cur_value) that may change due to Robin Hood
469	    // displacement — when we evict a "richer" resident, that resident
470	    // becomes our new in-flight pair and we keep probing from the next
471	    // bucket.
472	
473	    K cur_key   = key;
474	    V cur_value = value;
475	
476	    const std::size_t num_buckets = capacity_ / bucket_size;
477	    const std::size_t bucket_mask = num_buckets > 0 ? num_buckets - 1 : 0;
478	    const std::size_t probe_bound = num_buckets < max_probe_buckets
479	        ? num_buckets
480	        : static_cast<std::size_t>(max_probe_buckets);
481	
482	    std::size_t cur_home = (hash_(cur_key) & capacity_mask_) / bucket_size;
483	    std::size_t probe    = 0;
484	
485	    while (probe < probe_bound) {
486	        const std::size_t bucket_idx = (cur_home + probe) & bucket_mask;
487	
488	        // Cooperative load: one coalesced cache-line transaction. Each
489	        // lane reads one slot of the bucket (lane i reads slots[i]).
490	        const Slot slot = buckets_[bucket_idx].slots[tile.thread_rank()];
491	
492	        // Helper: `target_lane` performs an atomic CAS to replace its slot
493	        // (whose current contents we have in `slot`) with the in-flight
494	        // pair. On the match case the new value is computed as
495	        // `reduce_(existing, incoming)`; on the empty / displaceable cases
496	        // the new value is just the incoming value. Returns the broadcast
497	        // result of the CAS to every lane.
498	        auto try_cas = [&](int target_lane, bool apply_reduction) -> bool {
499	            bool cas_ok = false;
500	            if (tile.thread_rank() == target_lane) {
501	                const V to_store = apply_reduction
502	                    ? reduce_(slot.value, cur_value)
503	                    : cur_value;
504	                Slot expected = slot;
505	                const Slot desired{cur_key, to_store};
506	                cuda::atomic_ref<Slot, cuda::thread_scope_device> atomic_slot(
507	                    buckets_[bucket_idx].slots[target_lane]);
508	                cas_ok = atomic_slot.compare_exchange_strong(expected, desired);
509	            }
510	            return tile.shfl(cas_ok, target_lane);
511	        };
512	
513	        // (1) Key match — combine the in-flight value with the existing one
514	        //     via the Reduction operator (default: replace).
515	        const auto match_mask = tile.ballot(slot.key == cur_key);
516	        if (match_mask != 0) {
517	            if (try_cas(__ffs(match_mask) - 1, /*apply_reduction=*/true)) return true;
518	            continue;  // CAS lost the race — retry this bucket.
519	        }
520	
521	        // (2) Empty slot — claim it for our in-flight pair.
522	        const auto empty_mask = tile.ballot(slot.key == empty_key);
523	        if (empty_mask != 0) {
524	            if (try_cas(__ffs(empty_mask) - 1, /*apply_reduction=*/false)) return true;
525	            continue;  // Someone else took the slot — retry.
526	        }
527	
528	        // (3) Displaceable slot — Robin Hood swap with a richer resident.
529	        // Compute each resident's probe distance. (For empty slots this
530	        // gives nonsense, but we already eliminated the empty case above,
531	        // so the next ballot is meaningful.)
532	        const std::size_t resident_home =
533	            (hash_(slot.key) & capacity_mask_) / bucket_size;
534	        const std::size_t resident_probe =
535	            (bucket_idx - resident_home) & bucket_mask;
536	        const auto displaceable_mask = tile.ballot(resident_probe < probe);
537	        if (displaceable_mask != 0) {
538	            const int target_lane = __ffs(displaceable_mask) - 1;
539	            if (try_cas(target_lane, /*apply_reduction=*/false)) {
540	                // Adopt the evicted pair as our new in-flight pair. We
541	                // continue from the next bucket; the evicted pair's probe
542	                // distance there is `resident_probe + 1`.
543	                cur_key   = tile.shfl(slot.key,        target_lane);
544	                cur_value = tile.shfl(slot.value,      target_lane);
545	                cur_home  = tile.shfl(resident_home,   target_lane);
546	                probe     = tile.shfl(resident_probe,  target_lane) + 1;
547	                continue;
548	            }
549	            continue;  // CAS lost the race — retry.
550	        }
551	
552	        // (4) Nothing applies — advance one bucket.
553	        ++probe;
554	    }
555	
556	    // Probe budget exhausted. Caller is expected to rehash into a larger
557	    // table or accept the failure.
558	    return false;
559	}
560	
561	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>
562	template <class Tile>
563	__device__ cuda::std::optional<V>
564	HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::View::get(const Tile& tile, K key) const
565	{
566	    // Bucket-level Robin Hood lookup. Probe home_bucket, then home_bucket+1,
567	    // etc., wrapping modulo num_buckets. Each iteration is one coalesced
568	    // cache-line load (one slot per tile lane) and a few tile-wide ballots
569	    // to identify the terminating condition.
570	
571	    const auto h = hash_(key);
572	    const std::size_t home_slot   = h & capacity_mask_;
573	    const std::size_t home_bucket = home_slot / bucket_size;
574	    const std::size_t num_buckets = capacity_ / bucket_size;
575	    const std::size_t bucket_mask = num_buckets > 0 ? num_buckets - 1 : 0;
576	
577	    // Probe at most max_probe_buckets buckets, or every bucket in the table
578	    // if it's smaller than the cap.
579	    const std::size_t probe_bound = num_buckets < max_probe_buckets
580	        ? num_buckets
581	        : static_cast<std::size_t>(max_probe_buckets);
582	
583	    for (std::size_t probe = 0; probe < probe_bound; ++probe) {
584	        const std::size_t bucket_idx = (home_bucket + probe) & bucket_mask;
585	
586	        // Each lane reads one slot — one coalesced cache-line transaction
587	        // for the whole tile.
588	        const Slot slot = buckets_[bucket_idx].slots[tile.thread_rank()];
589	
590	        // (1) Key match. The matching lane broadcasts its value to the rest
591	        //     of the tile via shfl; each lane wraps the broadcast result in
592	        //     an optional and returns it (every lane returns the same one).
593	        const auto match_mask = tile.ballot(slot.key == key);
594	        if (match_mask != 0) {
595	            const int matching_lane = __ffs(match_mask) - 1;
596	            return cuda::std::optional<V>{tile.shfl(slot.value, matching_lane)};
597	        }
598	
599	        // (2) Empty slot. The key isn't in the table — Robin Hood would have
600	        //     placed it earlier in the probe sequence.
601	        if (tile.ballot(slot.key == empty_key) != 0) return cuda::std::nullopt;
602	
603	        // (3) Robin Hood early termination. If any resident is "richer"
604	        //     (closer to its home bucket than we are to ours), our key would
605	        //     have evicted it on insertion, so it can't be later in the
606	        //     probe sequence.
607	        const std::size_t resident_home =
608	            (hash_(slot.key) & capacity_mask_) / bucket_size;
609	        const std::size_t resident_probe =
610	            (bucket_idx - resident_home) & bucket_mask;
611	        if (tile.ballot(resident_probe < probe) != 0) return cuda::std::nullopt;
612	
613	        // No match, no empty slot, no richer resident — every slot here is
614	        // occupied by a resident at least as displaced as we would be.
615	        // Advance one bucket.
616	    }
617	
618	    // Either hit the probe cap, or wrapped fully around in a small table,
619	    // without finding the key, an empty slot, or a richer resident. The
620	    // insert invariant guarantees the key cannot be at probe distance
621	    // greater than max_probe_buckets, so this is a definite "not present".
622	    return cuda::std::nullopt;
623	}
624	
625	} // namespace gpurhh
626	
```

</details>

All operations\n// here copy bucket contents to host via cudaMemcpy and print to stdout;\n// none of this is synchronized against concurrent device-side activity.\n\n#include <gpurhh/hash_table.cuh>\n\n#include <cstddef>\n#include <cstdio>\n#include <vector>\n\n#include <cuda_runtime.h>\n\nnamespace gpurhh {\n\n// Pretty-print slots in [start, stop) of `table` to stdout, with bucket\n// boundaries marked. Copies (stop - start) slots from device to host and\n// is not synchronized against concurrent insert / get from other kernels.\ntemplate <class Table>\ninline void print_slots(const Table& table, std::size_t start, std::size_t stop) {\n    using Slot = typename Table::Slot;\n    using Key  = typename Table::key_type;\n    using V    = typename Table::value_type;\n\n    const std::size_t capacity = table.capacity();\n    if (start >= stop || stop > capacity) {\n        std::printf(\"print_slots: invalid range [%zu, %zu) (capacity = %zu)\\n\",\n                    start, stop, capacity);\n        return;\n    }\n\n    // The Bucket array is laid out such that reinterpreting it as a flat\n    // Slot[] is byte-equivalent — bucket_size * sizeof(Slot) == sizeof(Bucket)\n    // with no internal padding.\n    const std::size_t n = stop - start;\n    std::vector<Slot> host_slots(n);\n    cudaMemcpy(host_slots.data(),\n               reinterpret_cast<const Slot*>(table.data()) + start,\n               n * sizeof(Slot),\n               cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost);\n\n    std::printf(\"gpurhh::HashTable slots [%zu, %zu) \"\n                \"(capacity = %zu, bucket_size = %d):\\n\",\n                start, stop, capacity, Table::bucket_size);\n\n    constexpr std::size_t B = static_cast<std::size_t>(Table::bucket_size);\n    std::size_t current_bucket = static_cast<std::size_t>(-1);\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < n; ++i) {\n        const std::size_t slot_idx   = start + i;\n        const std::size_t bucket_idx = slot_idx / B;\n        if (bucket_idx != current_bucket) {\n            std::printf(\"  bucket %zu:\\n\", bucket_idx);\n            current_bucket = bucket_idx;\n        }\n\n        const Slot& slot = host_slots[i];\n        std::printf(\"    slot %zu: \", slot_idx);\n        if (slot.key == Table::empty_key) {\n            std::printf(\"empty\\n\");\n        } else if constexpr (sizeof(Key) == 4 && sizeof(V) == 4) {\n            std::printf(\"key=0x%08x value=0x%08x\\n\",\n                        static_cast<unsigned int>(slot.key),\n                        static_cast<unsigned int>(slot.value));\n        } else {\n            std::printf(\"key=0x%016llx value=0x%016llx\\n\",\n                        static_cast<unsigned long long>(slot.key),\n                        static_cast<unsigned long long>(slot.value));\n        }\n    }\n}\n\n} // namespace gpurhh\n"

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T19:21:48.840Z

**→ Tool call: `Edit`** (id `tool-287`)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh",
  "old_string": "template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>\nvoid HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::print_slots(\n    std::size_t start, std::size_t stop) const\n{\n    if (start >= stop || stop > capacity_) {\n        std::printf(\"print_slots: invalid range [%zu, %zu) (capacity = %zu)\\n\",\n                    start, stop, capacity_);\n        return;\n    }\n\n    // Copy the requested slot range to host. The Bucket array is laid out\n    // such that reinterpreting it as a flat Slot[] is byte-equivalent —\n    // bucket_size * sizeof(Slot) == sizeof(Bucket) with no internal padding.\n    const std::size_t n = stop - start;\n    std::vector<Slot> host_slots(n);\n    cudaMemcpy(host_slots.data(),\n               reinterpret_cast<const Slot*>(buckets_) + start,\n               n * sizeof(Slot),\n               cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost);\n\n    std::printf(\"gpurhh::HashTable slots [%zu, %zu) \"\n                \"(capacity = %zu, bucket_size = %d):\\n\",\n                start, stop, capacity_, bucket_size);\n\n    constexpr std::size_t B = static_cast<std::size_t>(bucket_size);\n    std::size_t current_bucket = static_cast<std::size_t>(-1);\n    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < n; ++i) {\n        const std::size_t slot_idx   = start + i;\n        const std::size_t bucket_idx = slot_idx / B;\n        if (bucket_idx != current_bucket) {\n            std::printf(\"  bucket %zu:\\n\", bucket_idx);\n            current_bucket = bucket_idx;\n        }\n\n        const Slot& slot = host_slots[i];\n        std::printf(\"    slot %zu: \", slot_idx);\n        if (slot.key == empty_key) {\n            std::printf(\"empty\\n\");\n        } else if constexpr (sizeof(K) == 4 && sizeof(V) == 4) {\n            std::printf(\"key=0x%08x value=0x%08x\\n\",\n                        static_cast<unsigned int>(slot.key),\n                        static_cast<unsigned int>(slot.value));\n        } else {\n            std::printf(\"key=0x%016llx value=0x%016llx\\n\",\n                        static_cast<unsigned long long>(slot.key),\n                        static_cast<unsigned long long>(slot.value));\n        }\n    }\n}\n\ntemplate <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>",
  "new_string": "template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>"
}

289	
290	The `View` is the centerpiece for header-only use: a user kernel that already has its keys in registers calls `view.insert(tile, ...)` or `view.get(tile, ...)` directly.
291	Bulk host-side operations are deliberately *not* part of the table — callers manage their device buffers and write their own driver kernels.
292	The tests in `tests/kernels.cuh` and `tests/isolated.cuh` show the canonical pattern; `examples/basic.cu` is a minimal standalone walkthrough.
293	
294	## Testing
295	
296	The test suite lives in `tests/`, with each `test_*.cu` compiled into its own executable.
297	
298	- **`test_construction.cu`** — construct / destruct, move semantics, edge sizes (capacity == bucket_size, capacity below bucket_size), moved-from state behavior.
299	- **`test_combined.cu`** — end-to-end insert + get round-trips through the public API.
```

## User — 2026-05-17T19:54:06.094Z

<ide_opened_file>The user opened the file [repo]/include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh in the IDE. This may or may not be related to the current task.</ide_opened_file>

Okay, now back to the benchmarks. We want to keep this reasonable simple to start.

I agree with your take that the two key things to measure are:
- How many ops per second
- Memory bandwidth utilization

We want this for both get and insert separately. Let's do insert starting from an empty table, and get starting from a full table, unless you think one of these isn't a good candidate for the simplest reasonably realistic test.

Regarding the parameters:
- Uniform random keys with some arbitrary values and summation-based reduction makes sense to me
- Parameterizing by load factor makes sense to me, this will be a hyperparameter, we will also need to check for insert failure
- Let's do 32-bit unsigned keys/values. The design requires slots to be either 64 or 128 bits
- Launch shape is also a hyperparameter
- 1-2 warmups runs, then averaging over 16 runs seems reasonable, 5 seems a bit small
- We want to benchmark the DRAM case, so there should be only one table and it should be pretty large. One could also consider settings where we want many small tables running in parallel, but then one starts to wonder whether table parameters should live in shared memory instead of on the host, whether we need on-GPU resizing, and other more complex stuff. Let's leave this out of scope for now.

As a baseline, it'd be great to benchmark against anything included by default in CUDA, assuming it supports a comparable API. I believe the hash table there uses a completely different design which in my opinion is far less clean than Robin Hood, but it will also likely be more optimized compared to the current implementation. It would be interesting to see how that translates to performance.

We can dump results into a CSV, and then read it in via a plotting framework later. 

Are there any other questions to consider before starting?

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T19:55:02.379Z

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T19:55:28.595Z

Mostly clear, but a few things worth pinning down before I write any code.

## 1. The "CUDA baseline" — there isn't one

CUDA itself ships no hash table. CUB, Thrust, and libcu++ have primitives but no map. NVIDIA's first-party header-only library is **cuCollections** (`cuco`), but it lives at `github.com/NVIDIA/cuCollections`, not in the toolkit. It's the natural apples-to-apples comparison — also bucketed open-addressing, also tile-cooperative, also `View`-style API — but it would need to be vendored (git submodule or pinned tag) into the repo.

**Question:** is bringing cuCollections in as a submodule acceptable? If yes, I'll add `bench_cuco_insert.cu` and `bench_cuco_get.cu` as separate binaries emitting the same CSV schema. If you'd rather not pull in a dependency yet, we benchmark against ourselves only and revisit the baseline later.

## 2. Get benchmark — hit/miss mix

"Gets from a full table" can mean 100%-hit (query the keys we inserted) or 100%-miss (query disjoint keys), and they exercise different code paths — the miss path has to probe further on average because it terminates on empty slot or Robin Hood early-exit, not on a key match. Realistic workloads are mostly hits.

**Default:** 100% hit. Cheap to add a `--miss-rate` flag later if curves are surprising, but I'd start with one number.

## 3. The bandwidth number we report is a floor

`ops/sec × sizeof(Bucket) = ops/sec × 128 B` assumes one bucket touched per op. At higher load factors and on the miss path, the actual count is > 1, so the reported "% of peak" is a *lower bound* on real bandwidth consumed. I'll call this out in the CSV header / printed output so we don't accidentally claim "only 60% of peak, we're leaving perf on the table" when in fact bytes-per-op is higher than we credited.

Probe-counter instrumentation (atomic increment on every bucket load) is doable but perturbs the timing; better as a separate diagnostic mode later if needed.

## 4. Insert failures — handling

At load factors ≤ 0.9 with uniform-random keys, the probe cap (8 buckets ≈ 128 slot positions) should never trigger. We'll still track failures via a device-side atomic counter and write the count into the CSV.

**Policy:** if the insert-benchmark sees *any* failures, we record them and continue (the row will show the failure count, and the throughput number is honest about how many ops succeeded). For the get-benchmark pre-fill, a failure is more serious — it makes "100% hit" a lie. I'd abort the run with a clear error and ask the user to lower the load factor.

## 5. Peak bandwidth — where it comes from

For "% of peak" reporting: simplest is to print effective GB/s only and let you divide offline against your card's known peak. If you'd like the benchmark to do the math, I can pull `cudaDeviceProp.memoryBusWidth × memoryClockRate × 2 / 8`, but the theoretical peak for GDDR6X is notoriously different from the achievable peak — empirical "copy kernel" peak is more honest. I'd default to **printing GB/s and not claiming a peak fraction**, and you can normalize offline against a known number for your card.

## 6. CSV — one row per repetition

Each binary emits one row per (config, rep) tuple, with `rep` as a column. Aggregation (min / median / mean over the 16 reps) belongs in the plotting script. Proposed columns:

```
library, workload, slot_bytes, capacity, load_factor, block_size,
n_ops, seed, rep, time_ms, ops_per_sec, gbps_effective, insert_failures
```

`library` ∈ {`gpurhh`, `cuco`} (only matters if we add the baseline).
`workload` ∈ {`insert`, `get`}.
A `--header` flag emits just the header line so the sweep driver can write it once.

## 7. Launch shape — what to sweep

Block size as a hyperparameter is fine, but the sweep is small: multiples of `tile_size` (16) — so {64, 128, 256, 512, 1024}. I'd cross-product it with load factor; the full grid for one workload is ~5 block sizes × 7 load factors × 16 reps = 560 rows. Cheap. Same for get.

---

Summary of decisions I'd like your read on, in priority order:

1. **cuCollections as the baseline — yes / no / later?** (Biggest scope question.)
2. **Get benchmark default = 100% hit. OK?**
3. **CSV schema above — anything missing / unwanted?**
4. **Block-size sweep across {64,128,256,512,1024} crossed with load factor — OK, or sweep them independently?**

If those four are settled, I'll start with the gpurhh insert + get binaries and the sweep driver; cuCollections (if approved) follows as a parallel pair of binaries.

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T20:04:34.422Z

A few real things came up.

## A. The two-independent-arrays idea — math doesn't quite work out

Worth doing the arithmetic on what hit rate that actually gives. With the headline workload (1 GiB table, 32-bit keys, capacity = 2^28 slots), N keys at load factor F:

$$
P(\text{hit}) = \frac{N}{2^{32}} = \frac{F \cdot 2^{28}}{2^{32}} = \frac{F}{16}
$$

So at F = 0.5 the natural hit rate is ~3%, and even at F = 1.0 it's only ~6%. The two-arrays setup with full-range `uint32` keys is essentially an **almost-pure-miss workload**. That's a perfectly fine thing to measure (the miss path is the interesting worst case for Robin Hood early-exit), but it's not really a "balanced mix" — it's a "mostly misses, occasional accidental hit."

If you want hit rate that scales with load factor in a "feels realistic" way (hit_rate ≈ load_factor, which is what you'd see if get traffic and insert traffic are drawn from the same population and the table covers that population), the cleanest setup is:

- **Insert keys**: an `fmix32` permutation of `{0..N-1}` — N distinct keys in a controlled range.
- **Get keys**: an independent `fmix32` permutation of `{0..capacity-1}` — drawn uniformly from the *table-sized* range.

Then `P(hit) = N / capacity = F`. Same two-streams idea; the only change is the range each stream is drawn from.

**Three options, ranked by what I'd recommend:**

1. **Insert ∈ {0..N-1}, get ∈ {0..capacity-1}** — hit rate ≈ load factor. Most natural / "balanced."
2. **Both ∈ uint32 full range** — your original idea; ~F/16 hit rate, very miss-heavy. Fine if you actually want miss-dominated.
3. **Explicit `--hit-rate p` knob with two disjoint key pools** — most controlled but more code.

I'd go with (1). Let me know if you'd rather have (2) or (3).

## B. The "For get," sentence got cut off

You wrote "For insert, this seems reasonable. For get," and stopped. What did you want to say about block-size sweep for the get benchmark? My default would be to use the same {64, 128, 256, 512, 1024} × load-factor grid for both, but I'd rather not guess.

## C. CSV schema — proposal to bash on

Wide format, one row per (config, rep) tuple. Empty cells for columns that don't apply to a given workload.

```
library, workload, slot_bytes, capacity, load_factor, block_size,
n_ops, seed, rep, time_ms, ops_per_sec, gbps_effective,
insert_failures, hits, misses, gpu_name, sm_arch
```

Per-column:

- `library` — `gpurhh` always, for now. Holds the slot open for a future `cuco` column.
- `workload` — `insert`, `get`, or `memcpy_d2d` / `memcpy_kernel` for the bandwidth baseline.
- `slot_bytes` — 8 today, 16 if we add 64-bit slots later.
- `capacity` — actual rounded-up slot count.
- `block_size` — threads per CUDA block.
- `n_ops` — number of operations the kernel executed.
- `rep` — 0-indexed repetition number; warmups are *not* written to the CSV.
- `time_ms`, `ops_per_sec` — primary throughput numbers.
- `gbps_effective` — `n_ops × sizeof(Bucket) / time` — the floor estimate. The plotting script can divide this against the memcpy peak.
- `insert_failures` — empty for `get` rows.
- `hits`, `misses` — empty for `insert` rows. Cheap to track via two atomic counters; useful for sanity-checking the workload (e.g., "did we actually get the hit rate we expected?").
- `gpu_name`, `sm_arch` — environment context, repeated on every row (CSV is meant to be concatenated across machines).

Things I deliberately left out:
- Driver / CUDA version — sidecar `run_info.txt` written once, not per row.
- Wall-clock timestamp — not useful for analysis.
- Aggregated stats (min/median/mean) — that's the plotting script's job.

**Questions on the schema:**
- Anything missing you'd want to slice by later?
- Anything extra that's not pulling its weight?
- Are you OK with environment info (`gpu_name`, `sm_arch`) repeated per row, or would you prefer a sidecar?

## D. Memcpy baseline — structure

I'd make this a separate `bench_memcpy.cu` binary that emits rows with `workload=memcpy_d2d` and `workload=memcpy_kernel` in the same CSV schema. Run it once per machine; the plot reads it the same way it reads the gpurhh rows and uses the values as the "achievable peak" reference line.

Two flavors worth measuring:
- `cudaMemcpyAsync(... DeviceToDevice ...)` — the toolkit's reference.
- A trivial `__global__ void copy(T* dst, const T* src, size_t n)` kernel — what a hand-rolled bandwidth-bound kernel achieves.

These usually land within a few percent of each other but the kernel version is more directly comparable to ours (same launch overhead, same arithmetic-free body).

---

If you can answer A (option 1/2/3), B (cut-off sentence), and C (CSV columns OK or what to change), I think we have enough to start writing the gpurhh insert + get benchmarks.

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T20:11:37.710Z

A. Yes, that's a clean version. But it's worth working through what it implies, because there's a real coupling to flag.

If keys ∈ Uniform(0, N) and we attempt `n_ops` insertions:

- Expected **distinct** inserted keys: $N(1 - e^{-n_\text{ops}/N})$.
- Expected **slot occupancy** of the table: distinct / capacity.
- Expected **hit rate** for a get drawn from the same Uniform(0, N): distinct / N.

Let me parameterize this with α = N / capacity (the user's "not too small, not too large" knob) and F = n_ops / capacity (the "input load factor" we sweep over):

| α   | F = 0.5: occupancy / hit rate | F = 1.0: occupancy / hit rate | F = 2.0: occupancy / hit rate |
| --- | ----------------------------- | ----------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| 1   | 0.39 / 0.39                   | 0.63 / 0.63                   | 0.86 / 0.86                   |
| 2   | 0.44 / 0.22                   | 0.79 / 0.39                   | 1.73 (impossible)             |
| 0.5 | 0.32 / 0.63                   | 0.43 / 0.86                   | 0.49 / 0.98                   |

Two things fall out:

1. **Input load factor F = n_ops / capacity is not the same as occupancy.** With α = 1 and F = 0.5, we *attempt* half-capacity-worth of inserts but only end up with ~39% occupancy because of collisions (which sum-reduce, so they're not failures — they're just no new slot). The plot's x-axis is whichever of these we sweep. I'd vote x-axis = **input load factor F** (the natural knob), and we report effective occupancy as a derived column in the CSV so the analysis side can re-plot against actual occupancy if needed.

2. **Single α can't simultaneously give high occupancy and high hit rate.** They're directly coupled through α. With α = 1 we get hit rate = occupancy (nice symmetry, balanced regime). With α = 2 we get higher peak occupancy (≈0.79 at F=1) but lower hit rate (≈0.39). With α = 0.5 we get high hit rates (~0.86 at F=1) but capped occupancy (~0.43, because most slots' worth of keys don't exist in the input space).

My recommendation: **α = 1 as the default**, sweep F over {0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0}. This gives a clean diagonal where hit rate ≈ occupancy, reaches ~86% occupancy at F=2, and gets us into the "Robin Hood under pressure" regime that the design is meant to handle.

If you'd rather sweep α too, we can — but I'd start with α fixed and only sweep it if curves are surprising.

## B. Get setup — falls out automatically

With the scheme above, the get benchmark is just:

1. Fill the table by inserting `F × capacity` keys ~ Uniform(0, α × capacity) (this is the gpurhh-insert workload run once, untimed, with stream A).
2. Draw `F × capacity` get keys ~ Uniform(0, α × capacity), **independently** (stream B with a different seed). Hit rate is approximately the table's occupancy.
3. Same block-size grid as insert: {64, 128, 256, 512, 1024}.

So the get sweep is the same shape as the insert sweep; we'd reuse the pre-filled table across the 16 reps and across block sizes for a given F.

## New things that came out of this

A few small ones — confirm or push back:

1. **Capacity:** I was assuming the headline run is at ~1 GiB total table allocation (= 2²⁷ slots, ~134M keys). Sound right, or want a different default? `--capacity-bytes` as an override is easy either way.

2. **Where keys live:** Insert and get key arrays staged in device memory before the timed kernel — generated by a tiny pre-kernel using `fmix32(seed + i) % N`. The timed kernel reads them as a flat array. This keeps PRNG work out of the timing window and matches what a real user would have (a buffer of keys to insert).

3. **Effective occupancy in the CSV:** add a column `effective_occupancy` (= distinct inserts / capacity) so we don't have to compute it offline from `n_ops` and `α`. Cheap — counted by an atomic incremented on every successful empty-slot CAS in the insert kernel. Worth it?

4. **`α` in the CSV:** also probably worth a column, given it controls the workload shape and we may want to sweep it later.

5. **Insert failure semantics:** with α = 1 and uniform keys, even at F = 2.0 (~86% occupancy), Robin Hood with cap 8 should not fail. If it does, that's a real finding worth flagging in the CSV — I'd still record it rather than abort. Sound right?

If those land, I'll start writing.

Three swept variables + repetitions:

1. **Workload** — `insert`, `get` (2 values)
2. **F** (input load factor) — {0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0} (7 values)
3. **Block size** — {64, 128, 256, 512, 1024} (5 values)
4. **Rep** — 16 timed reps per cell

Fixed for now: α = 1, slot_bytes = 8, capacity (= 1 GiB), seed.

Total: 2 × 7 × 5 × 16 = **1120 timed runs**. Each is sub-second, so total wall-clock is on the order of minutes, not hours.

Plot dimensionality matches: x-axis = F, color/line = block size, one panel per workload. Adding α later (if we sweep it) would need a fourth dimension via faceting — still tractable but it's where complexity starts to grow.

If you want to trim further before starting, the cheapest cut is **block size**: pick one reasonable default (256) and not sweep it on the first run. That gets us down to 2 × 7 × 16 = 224 runs and one fewer axis on the plot. Block size very rarely moves throughput by more than ~10% for bandwidth-bound kernels, so it's a defensible omission for the v1 benchmark; we can add it back once the rest of the curves are in hand.

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T20:19:02.454Z

Fair point. At α = 1:

| F    | Expected occupancy |
| ---- | ------------------ |
| 0.1  | ~0.10              |
| 0.25 | ~0.22              |
| 0.5  | ~0.39              |
| 1.0  | ~0.63              |
| 1.5  | ~0.78              |
| 2.0  | ~0.86              |
| 3.0  | ~0.95              |

F ≤ 0.25 lives in a regime where probes are almost always length 1 and Robin Hood never does any displacement work — the points will sit on top of each other on the throughput plot. The interesting regime is F ≥ 0.5 (where collisions start) and especially F ≥ 1.5 (where probe distances grow and Robin Hood actually earns its keep).

Suggested tighter grid: **{0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0}** — 5 values. One uncongested anchor (0.5), a moderate point (1.0), and three points in the high-pressure regime (1.5, 2.0, 3.0). Drops the two trivial values, keeps enough resolution to draw a smooth curve, and lets us see the cliff (if there is one) as occupancy approaches saturation.

Total runs with this and the full block-size sweep: 2 × 5 × 5 × 16 = **800**. Without block-size sweep: 160.

Want me to go with {0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0}, or trim further?

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T20:22:25.284Z

A handful of small implementation details to lock in, then we're good to start:

1. **Get output**: the get kernel needs somewhere to put the looked-up values. Simplest is a `uint32_t* output` buffer with one slot per get op; lane 0 of each tile writes `result.value_or(0xFFFFFFFF)`. That's the right shape for what a real user does, and the 4 B/op write is negligible against the 128 B/op bucket read for the bandwidth metric. Output buffer is ~1.5 GiB at F = 3.0 on a 1 GiB table — fine on a 24 GiB card, worth flagging.

2. **Hit/miss counters**: cheapest reliable option is a single global `atomicAdd` from tile-lane-0 on the hit case (one atomic per op, not per thread). Adds a small perturbation but gives empirical hit rate in the CSV for sanity-checking against the theoretical `1 - e^(-F/α)`. Alternative is to skip the counter and trust the theory — I'd rather pay the tiny cost and have the number.

3. **Insert failures at F = 3.0**: the default `MaxProbeBuckets = 8` (≈ 128 slot positions in the bucketed setting) should be fine at ~0.95 occupancy with uniform random keys, but it's borderline. If F = 3.0 starts reporting nonzero `insert_failures`, that's a finding — we record it and revisit (either raise the cap or trim F to 2.5).

4. **Pre-fill is shared across reps and across the block-size sweep for get**: same (capacity, F) tuple → one pre-fill, then 5 block sizes × 16 reps = 80 timed get kernels against the same table state. Worth structuring the sweep driver that way to avoid 79 unnecessary pre-fills.

5. **`run_info` sidecar**: easiest done by the sweep script via `nvidia-smi --query-gpu=name,driver_version,memory.total --format=csv` plus `uname -a` and `nvcc --version` to a `run_info.txt` next to the CSV. The C++ binaries stay clean.

6. **Memory budget for the biggest run** (F = 3.0, 1 GiB table): ~1 GiB table + ~1.5 GiB insert keys + ~1.5 GiB get keys + ~1.5 GiB get output ≈ 5.5 GiB. Comfortable on a 24 GiB card; if you happen to be on a smaller card, the cap is F ≈ 2.0.

No blockers I can see beyond these. Ready to start writing the three binaries (`bench_insert.cu`, `bench_get.cu`, `bench_memcpy.cu`), the sweep driver, and the Makefile addition?

Ideally, we'd return this element from the insert method, so that whatever might happen next in the upstream kernel - for example, putting it into a buffer in order to go back to the host and rebuild a new, larger hash table - can handle it. Do we actually do this now? It would be good to guarantee that actual keys cannot get lost even when failures occur.

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T20:33:40.365Z

<em>Thinking…</em>

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T20:33:52.809Z

Good catch. Confirmed — the current implementation does drop the leftover.

Walking through [include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh:462-559](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh#L462-L559): when a displacement succeeds, the kernel atomically swaps our in-flight `(cur_key, cur_value)` into the bucket slot, then re-broadcasts the evicted resident's `(key, value, home, probe)` to every lane as the new in-flight pair via `tile.shfl(...)`. From that point on, the originally-passed-in `(key, value)` is already in the table and the in-flight pair is some unrelated victim. If probing exhausts without that victim ever finding a home, we `return false` and the victim is gone.

The fix is straightforward and the cost is essentially zero — `cur_key` / `cur_value` are already in registers on every lane.

## Proposed change

Replace `bool` with a small return struct. Two shapes worth considering:

**Option A — minimal, only what's needed for the leftover guarantee:**

```cpp
struct InsertResult {
    bool  ok;              // true on success (placed or reduced); false on probe-cap failure
    Key   leftover_key;    // meaningful only when ok == false
    Value leftover_value;  // meaningful only when ok == false
};
```

**Option B — also distinguishes "placed a new entry" from "reduced into an existing one":**

```cpp
enum class InsertStatus : unsigned char {
    Inserted,   // claimed an empty slot (and possibly displaced residents along the way)
    Reduced,    // merged into an existing slot via the Reduction operator
    Failed,     // probe-cap exhausted; leftover_* hold the in-flight pair
};

struct InsertResult {
    InsertStatus status;
    Key   leftover_key;
    Value leftover_value;
};
```

The Inserted/Reduced distinction is exactly what the benchmark's `effective_occupancy` column needs (counts Inserted, not Reduced), and lets users do per-insert callsite logic without needing a global atomic. The cost in the kernel is one extra branch / flag — negligible.

I'd lean toward **B** for that reason — it's only a couple more lines in the implementation and saves the benchmark a perturbing atomic. But A is the minimal fix to the actual bug you raised.

## Callers that change

- `examples/basic.cu` — currently ignores the return value; would compile after the change but should probably assert ok.
- `tests/kernels.cuh::bulk_insert` and `insert_kernel` — used by `test_combined.cu`; currently ignores return value.
- `tests/isolated.cuh::do_insert_with_outcomes` — already tracks an outcomes array; just adapts to `InsertResult`.
- All the test files that use these helpers — minor.

A or B?

I'll go with `cuda::std::optional<Slot>` — `Slot` is already public, has the right two fields, and reads cleanly. Yes, the optional's "present" meaning is inverted from `get` (here, present = failure, since success returns nothing), but that's an honest reflection of the semantics. Callers that need\n        // to avoid data loss should buffer the returned Slot and retry it\n        // elsewhere (typically by rebuilding a larger table).\n        template <class Tile>\n        __device__ cuda::std::optional<Slot> insert(\n            const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value);"
}

  "old_string": "        // (1) Key match — combine the in-flight value with the existing one\n        //     via the Reduction operator (default: replace).\n        const auto match_mask = tile.ballot(slot.key == cur_key);\n        if (match_mask != 0) {\n            if (try_cas(__ffs(match_mask) - 1, /*apply_reduction=*/true)) return true;\n            continue;  // CAS lost the race — retry this bucket.\n        }\n\n        // (2) Empty slot — claim it for our in-flight pair.\n        const auto empty_mask = tile.ballot(slot.key == empty_key);\n        if (empty_mask != 0) {\n            if (try_cas(__ffs(empty_mask) - 1, /*apply_reduction=*/false)) return true;\n            continue;  // Someone else took the slot — retry.\n        }",
  "new_string": "        // (1) Key match — combine the in-flight value with the existing one\n        //     via the Reduction operator (default: replace).\n        const auto match_mask = tile.ballot(slot.key == cur_key);\n        if (match_mask != 0) {\n            if (try_cas(__ffs(match_mask) - 1, /*apply_reduction=*/true))\n                return cuda::std::nullopt;\n            continue;  // CAS lost the race — retry this bucket.\n        }\n\n        // (2) Empty slot — claim it for our in-flight pair.\n        const auto empty_mask = tile.ballot(slot.key == empty_key);\n        if (empty_mask != 0) {\n            if (try_cas(__ffs(empty_mask) - 1, /*apply_reduction=*/false))\n                return cuda::std::nullopt;\n            continue;  // Someone else took the slot — retry.\n        }"
}
```

## User — 2026-05-17T20:40:24.347Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-308`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
175	4. **Block size** is purely an occupancy knob — some multiple of `WarpSize`, tuned empirically.
176	5. **Work assignment** is a tile-strided loop: tile index `t` (across the whole grid) handles input keys `t, t+T, t+2T, …` where `T` is the total number of resident tiles.
177	   Naturally load-balanced and robust to input skew.
178	
179	### Per-tile probe step
180	
181	On each probe:
182	
183	1. The `B` lanes cooperatively load one bucket — each lane reads one slot, fully coalesced.
184	2. `tile.ballot(...)` over a predicate identifies empty slots, key matches, or Robin-Hood-displaceable slots in a single warp intrinsic.
185	3. The lane corresponding to the chosen slot performs the CAS; the outcome is broadcast to the rest of the tile with `tile.shfl(...)`.
186	4. On displacement, the evicted `(key, value)` becomes the tile's new in-flight pair and the tile advances by one bucket.
187	
188	Every step is a warp-intrinsic operation on a single cache line, which is what makes the bandwidth argument hold.
189	
190	### Parameters: what is forced by the architecture, what is free
191	
192	Once we commit to the sub-warp tile design, almost every constant is *derived* from the hardware, not chosen:
193	
194	| Parameter          | Value (default)              | Source                                   |
195	| ------------------ | ---------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
196	| `SlotBytes`        | 8 (4+4) or 16 (8+8)          | Key/value packing choice                 |
197	| `CacheLineBytes`   | 128 (NVIDIA)                 | Hardware                                 |
198	| `BucketSize`       | `CacheLineBytes / SlotBytes` | **Derived**                              |
199	| `WarpSize`         | 32 (NVIDIA)                  | Hardware                                 |
200	| `TileSize`         | `BucketSize`                 | **Derived** (the central design choice)  |
201	| `TilesPerWarp`     | `WarpSize / BucketSize`      | **Derived**                              |
202	| `BlockSize`        | tuned (e.g. 128 or 256)      | Occupancy — empirical                    |
203	| `TargetLoadFactor` | tuned (e.g. 0.7)             | Robin Hood quality — empirical           |
204	| `MaxProbeBuckets`  | 8                            | Worst-case time vs. insert-failure rate  |
205	
206	So the only genuinely free knobs are `BlockSize`, `TargetLoadFactor`, and `MaxProbeBuckets`.
207	The rest should be expressed in the code as compile-time constants derived from `SlotBytes`, `CacheLineBytes`, and `WarpSize`, with the latter two being template parameters of the table (with sensible defaults) rather than hard-coded.
208	
209	### Why the abstraction matters — an AMD aside (out of scope for v1)
210	
211	AMD GPUs (CDNA, RDNA) have a 64-byte cache line and a 64-thread wavefront.
212	With 8-byte slots:
213	
214	- NVIDIA: `BucketSize` = 128 / 8 = 16, `TilesPerWarp` = 32 / 16 = **2**.
215	- AMD:    `BucketSize` =  64 / 8 =  8, `TilesPerWarp` = 64 /  8 = **8**.
216	
217	The same algorithm runs unchanged; only the derived constants differ.
218	We are not targeting AMD, but it is the reason we want `CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` to be parameters of the table type rather than hard-coded `128` and `32`.
219	Anything that ends up baked in as a literal in the implementation is a portability bug waiting to happen.
220	
221	## Lookup
222	
223	`View::get` follows the same probing rule as insert, read-only, returning `cuda::std::optional<Value>` — populated on hit, empty on miss. The probe terminates as soon as it sees one of:
224	
225	- A key match → return `{value}`.
226	- An empty slot → return `nullopt`. Robin Hood would have placed the key before this point.
227	- A resident "richer" than ours (smaller probe distance) → return `nullopt`. Our key would have evicted that resident if it were in the table, contradiction.
228	- The probe cap → return `nullopt`. The insert-side cap means the key cannot live further than this.
229	
230	Lookups are read-only and trivially parallel: concurrent lookups never race each other.
231	
232	**Concurrent get during insert is not linearizable.** A get running simultaneously with an insert can return `nullopt` for a key that is about to be inserted, or for a key in the middle of being displaced. Linearizability would require per-slot versioning. Most workloads sync between insert and get phases.
233	
234	## Deletion
235	
236	Not supported. The natural design is backward-shift deletion, but a correct lock-free implementation is significantly harder than insert/lookup, and the use case (this is an append-only table for most consumers) hasn't pushed us to do it.
237	
238	## Public API
239	
240	```cpp
241	namespace gpurhh {
242	
243	template <class Key,
244	          class Value,
245	          class Hash             = default_hash<Key>,
246	          Key   EmptyKey         = default_empty_key<Key>::key,
247	          class Reduction        = replace_op,
248	          int   CacheLineBytes   = 128,
249	          int   WarpSize         = 32,
```

</details>

68:It is derivable as `(slot_index - hash(key)) mod capacity`.
74:  Users specify a target load factor (default 0.7); we round capacity up to `next_pow2(N / load_factor)`.
112:A CAS failure is treated as "the world changed under us": we re-read the slot and re-evaluate without advancing the probe index.
137:- **Probe length capping.**
139:  An insert that would place a key past the cap fails (the caller is expected to rehash); a get that probes the cap without finding the key returns "not found" (correct because of the insert-side cap).
140:  With Robin Hood at load factor ~0.85 and `bucket_size` 16, the expected longest probe is well under the default cap; the cap protects against adversarial inputs and very high load factors.
204:| `MaxProbeBuckets`  | 8                            | Worst-case time vs. insert-failure rate  |

263:        // Returns nullopt on success; on probe-cap failure returns the

306:- **`test_construction.cu`** — construct / destruct, move semantics, edge sizes (capacity == bucket_size, capacity below bucket_size), moved-from state behavior.

321:The test suite runs about 34 tests across ~1100 lines of code, against ~350 lines of library code. ## User — 2026-05-17T20:40:41.977Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-311`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
130	  Within a warp, threads whose home slots land in the same cache line should service their probes from one load, not 32 loads.
131	  We have the warp ballot/shuffle primitives to do this cheaply.
132	- **Bucketed probing.**
133	  Treat the table as an array of *buckets* of 16 slots (one cache line).
134	  A probe inspects an entire bucket at a time — one coalesced 128-byte load — and uses warp intrinsics (`__ballot_sync`, `__match_any_sync`) to find the candidate slot.
135	  Insertion still uses CAS on a single 8-byte slot inside the bucket, but the read traffic per probe is exactly one cache line.
136	  This is the same idea as `bucketed_cuckoo` and Bucketized Cuckoo Hashing.
137	- **Probe length capping.**
138	  Exposed as a `MaxProbeBuckets` template parameter (default 8).
139	  An insert that would place a key past the cap fails (the caller is expected to rehash); a get that probes the cap without finding the key returns "not found" (correct because of the insert-side cap).
140	  With Robin Hood at load factor ~0.85 and `bucket_size` 16, the expected longest probe is well under the default cap; the cap protects against adversarial inputs and very high load factors.
141	- **Avoid divergence.**
142	  All threads in a warp execute the same probe loop.
143	  The "I'm done, others aren't" case is handled with masked operations rather than early-return-then-divergence.
144	
```

</details>

  "new_string": "- **Probe length capping.**\n  Exposed as a `MaxProbeBuckets` template parameter (default 8).\n  An insert that would place a key past the cap fails (the caller is expected to rehash); a get that probes the cap without finding the key returns \"not found\" (correct because of the insert-side cap).\n  With Robin Hood at load factor ~0.85 and `bucket_size` 16, the expected longest probe is well under the default cap; the cap protects against adversarial inputs and very high load factors.\n  Crucially, a failing insert hands the leftover `(key, value)` back to the caller via the return value rather than dropping it on the floor.\n  This matters because the leftover may not be the original input: if Robin Hood displacements happened during the probe, the originally-passed-in pair is already in the table and the leftover is the most-recently-evicted victim.\n  Returning it is what makes the operation lossless under failure — callers can buffer leftovers and replay them into a rebuilt larger table."
}
```

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T20:41:00.319Z

Summary of the API change:

- [include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh): `View::insert` now returns `cuda::std::optional<Slot>`. `nullopt` on success; on probe-cap failure, the optional contains the leftover `Slot` — either the original `(key, value)` if no displacement happened, or the last evicted victim if one did. Either way, no key is silently lost. The two success paths return `nullopt`, the exhausted-probe path returns `Slot{cur_key, cur_value}`.
- [tests/isolated.cuh:145](tests/isolated.cuh#L145): `insert_many_with_outcomes_kernel` now reads `leftover.has_value()` instead of a bool. Other call sites (`tests/kernels.cuh`, `examples/basic.cu`, the other helper in `isolated.cuh`) ignore the return value and continue to compile unchanged.
- [notes/design.md](notes/design.md): public-API sketch updated; the probe-cap section now explains the lossless-failure guarantee and *why* the leftover is sometimes a victim rather than the original input.

This is a soft compile break for any external code that was binding the return to `bool`. Worth flagging if you have anything outside this repo using the API.

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T20:44:12.826Z

Short list — most are propose-and-move-on:

## 1. CLI surface (proposed; flag if you want changes)

```
./bench_insert --capacity-bytes 1073741824   # 1 GiB; rounded up to next pow2 of slots
               --load-factor 1.0             # F = n_ops / capacity (input load factor)
               --block-size 256
               --warmups 2
               --reps 16
               --seed 42
               --tag run1                    # free-form label that lands in CSV
               [--header]                    # print CSV header and exit

./bench_get      same flags, plus an independent --get-seed (so insert and get use disjoint streams)
./bench_memcpy   --bytes 1073741824 --reps 16 --warmups 2 [--header]
```

`--capacity-bytes` (vs. raw slot count) since "1 GiB table" is more natural than "2²⁷ slots."

## 2. Output destination

CSV to **stdout**, diagnostic chatter (e.g. "warmup 1/2 done", "insert_failures=0") to **stderr**. Sweep script redirects stdout to a single CSV file and the header is emitted once via `--header`. No `--output FILE` flag — keeps the binaries minimal and the sweep script in charge of file paths.

## 3. Bandwidth metric — explicit, not implicit

To make the floor estimate honest in the CSV: add `bytes_per_op` as a column (= `sizeof(Bucket)` = 128 for our current config), so `gbps_effective = n_ops × bytes_per_op / time_ms / 1e6`. Anyone reading the CSV later knows exactly what was assumed and can recompute against the memcpy peak.

## 4. Insert failure handling — count, don't record

`View::insert` now returns leftover Slots, but recording them all would need a ~1.5 GiB device buffer at F = 3. For v1: a single device-side `uint64_t` counter, incremented per `leftover.has_value()`, with a separate counter for `successful_inserts` derived as `n_ops - failures`. Counter goes in CSV; failed-key recovery is a feature for users to use, not something the benchmark exercises beyond counting.

## 5. Atomic counters — modern API

For the hit counter and the failure counter, use `cuda::atomic_ref<std::uint64_t, cuda::thread_scope_device>` and `.fetch_add(1, cuda::memory_order_relaxed)`. Same library facility we already use for `cuda::atomic_ref<Slot>` in the insert path; no legacy `atomicAdd` intrinsics.

## 6. `run_info` sidecar — proposed contents

```
git_sha:        $(git rev-parse HEAD)
git_dirty:      yes|no
nvcc_version:   $(nvcc --version | tail -n1)
uname:          $(uname -srm)
nvidia_smi:     $(nvidia-smi --query-gpu=name,driver_version,memory.total --format=csv,noheader)
```

Git SHA is the one I'd most want to have when looking at a CSV six months from now. Generated by the sweep script before the runs start.

## 7. Layout in the repo

```
benchmarks/
  common.cuh          shared: BENCH_CHECK macro, fmix32 key generator, CUDA event timing helper
  bench_insert.cu
  bench_get.cu
  bench_memcpy.cu
  sweep.sh            sweep driver; writes bench_results/{csv,run_info}
Makefile              new `bench` target paralleling `tests`/`examples`
```

`benchmarks/common.cuh` is independent of `tests/tests.cuh` — the two test/bench scopes shouldn't depend on each other.

---

That's everything I can think of. If 1–7 all look fine, I'll start writing. The natural order is `common.cuh` → `bench_memcpy.cu` (simplest, validates the harness) → `bench_insert.cu` → `bench_get.cu` → `Makefile` → `sweep.sh`.

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T20:51:01.140Z

All clear. Two small implementation choices that follow from #2 that I'll lock in unless you'd rather flip them:

- **One CSV per binary** in the output dir — `insert.csv`, `get.csv`, `memcpy.csv` — rather than a single combined `results.csv`. Each binary appends to its own file (writing the header if the file is new), no inter-binary contention, easy to delete and re-run one workload. The plot script reads all three.
- **`--header` flag goes away**: since the binary owns its CSV file, it can detect empty/new and write the header itself. No separate header-only mode needed.

CLI shape becomes:

```
./benchmark_insert --capacity-bytes 1073741824 --load-factor 1.0 \
                   --block-size 256 --warmups 2 --reps 16 --seed 42 \
                   --tag run1 --output-dir bench_results/2026-05-17/
```

File layout:
```
benchmarks/
  common.cuh
  benchmark_insert.cu
  benchmark_get.cu
  benchmark_memcpy.cu
  sweep.sh
Makefile                  # `make benchmarks` builds, `make benchmark` runs
```

Starting in the order: `common.cuh` → `benchmark_memcpy.cu` (simplest, validates the timing harness) → `benchmark_insert.cu` → `benchmark_get.cu` → Makefile → `sweep.sh`. Will pause after the first two if anything in the shape feels off.
