```json
{
  "file_path": "[repo]/benchmarks/common.cuh",
  "content": "#pragma once\n\n// Shared benchmark infrastructure: CUDA error checking, event-based\n// timing, append-mode CSV file writer with header-on-first-write, and\n// deterministic uniform key generation. Independent of the test suite —\n// benchmarks should not pull in <tests/...>.\n\n#include <cuda_runtime.h>\n\n#include <cstddef>\n#include <cstdint>\n#include <cstdio>\n#include <cstdlib>\n#include <filesystem>\n#include <fstream>\n#include <iostream>\n#include <string>\n\nnamespace bench {\n\n// CUDA error checking, postfix-operator style:\n//   cudaMalloc(&p, n) >> BENCH_CHECK;\n// Source location is captured via __FILE__ / __LINE__ at the call site.\nstruct CheckLoc { const char* file; int line; };\n\ninline void operator>>(cudaError_t err, CheckLoc loc) {\n    if (err != cudaSuccess) {\n        std::fprintf(stderr, \"[CUDA] %s:%d — %s\\n\",\n                     loc.file, loc.line, cudaGetErrorString(err));\n        std::exit(1);\n    }\n}\n\n#define BENCH_CHECK ::bench::CheckLoc{__FILE__, __LINE__}\n\n// CUDA event timer. begin() records the start event; end_ms() records\n// the stop event, synchronizes on it, and returns elapsed milliseconds.\nclass EventTimer {\npublic:\n    EventTimer() {\n        cudaEventCreate(&start_) >> BENCH_CHECK;\n        cudaEventCreate(&stop_)  >> BENCH_CHECK;\n    }\n    ~EventTimer() {\n        cudaEventDestroy(start_);\n        cudaEventDestroy(stop_);\n    }\n    EventTimer(const EventTimer&)            = delete;\n    EventTimer& operator=(const EventTimer&) = delete;\n\n    void begin() { cudaEventRecord(start_) >> BENCH_CHECK; }\n    float end_ms() {\n        cudaEventRecord(stop_) >> BENCH_CHECK;\n        cudaEventSynchronize(stop_) >> BENCH_CHECK;\n        float ms;\n        cudaEventElapsedTime(&ms, start_, stop_) >> BENCH_CHECK;\n        return ms;\n    }\n\nprivate:\n    cudaEvent_t start_{};\n    cudaEvent_t stop_{};\n};\n\n// CSV writer: appends rows to a file in the output directory, and also\n// echoes every row (and the header) to stdout for live visibility.\n//\n// If the target file is new or empty when constructed, the header line\n// is written to the file first; if not, the file is appended to without\n// a fresh header (so a sweep over many invocations builds one CSV).\nclass CsvWriter {\npublic:\n    CsvWriter(const std::filesystem::path& path, std::string header)\n        : header_(std::move(header))\n    {\n        if (!path.parent_path().empty()) {\n            std::filesystem::create_directories(path.parent_path());\n        }\n        const bool needs_header = !std::filesystem::exists(path)\n                                || std::filesystem::file_size(path) == 0;\n        file_.open(path, std::ios::app);\n        if (!file_) {\n            std::fprintf(stderr, \"Failed to open %s for writing\\n\",\n                         path.string().c_str());\n            std::exit(1);\n        }\n        if (needs_header) {\n            file_ << header_ << \"\\n\";\n            file_.flush();\n        }\n        // Echo header once per invocation, regardless of file state, so\n        // stdout is self-documenting even when appending to an existing\n        // CSV from the sweep driver.\n        std::cout << header_ << \"\\n\";\n    }\n\n    void write_row(const std::string& row) {\n        file_ << row << \"\\n\";\n        file_.flush();\n        std::cout << row << \"\\n\";\n        std::cout.flush();\n    }\n\nprivate:\n    std::string   header_;\n    std::ofstream file_;\n};\n\n// Device-side fmix32 finalizer. Same mixer the library uses as its\n// default hash; reused here as a cheap PRNG over the iteration index.\n__host__ __device__ inline std::uint32_t fmix32(std::uint32_t h) {\n    h ^= h >> 16;\n    h *= 0x85ebca6bu;\n    h ^= h >> 13;\n    h *= 0xc2b2ae35u;\n    h ^= h >> 16;\n    return h;\n}\n\n// Fill out[0..n) with fmix32(seed + i) mod range. For `range` a power\n// of two (the case here, since key range = α × capacity with capacity\n// a power of two), the modulus yields a uniform distribution.\n__global__ inline void fill_uniform_keys_kernel(\n    std::uint32_t* out, std::size_t n,\n    std::uint32_t seed, std::uint32_t range)\n{\n    const std::size_t tid = blockIdx.x * std::size_t{blockDim.x} + threadIdx.x;\n    if (tid >= n) return;\n    out[tid] = fmix32(static_cast<std::uint32_t>(tid) + seed) % range;\n}\n\ninline void fill_uniform_keys(\n    std::uint32_t* d_out, std::size_t n,\n    std::uint32_t seed, std::uint32_t range)\n{\n    constexpr int block = 256;\n    const int grid = static_cast<int>((n + block - 1) / block);\n    fill_uniform_keys_kernel<<<grid, block>>>(d_out, n, seed, range);\n    cudaGetLastError() >> BENCH_CHECK;\n}\n\n// Round `n` up to the next power of two. Returns 1 for n == 0 or n == 1.\nconstexpr inline std::size_t next_pow2(std::size_t n) noexcept {\n    if (n <= 1) return 1;\n    std::size_t p = 1;\n    while (p < n) p <<= 1;\n    return p;\n}\n\n} // namespace bench\n"
}
```

</details>

```json
{
  "file_path": "[repo]/benchmarks/benchmark_memcpy.cu",
  "content": "// Memcpy baseline benchmark.\n//\n// Measures device-to-device memory bandwidth via two flavors:\n//   - workload=memcpy_d2d    — cudaMemcpyAsync(..., DeviceToDevice)\n//   - workload=memcpy_kernel — a trivial uint4-aligned copy kernel\n// Both report effective GB/s assuming two bytes of DRAM traffic per byte\n// of payload (one read plus one write). Used as a peak-bandwidth\n// reference line for the insert / get benchmarks.\n\n#include \"common.cuh\"\n\n#include <cuda_runtime.h>\n\n#include <cstddef>\n#include <cstdint>\n#include <cstdio>\n#include <filesystem>\n#include <iomanip>\n#include <sstream>\n#include <string>\n\nnamespace {\n\nstruct Args {\n    std::size_t bytes      = std::size_t{1} << 30;  // 1 GiB\n    int         warmups    = 2;\n    int         reps       = 16;\n    std::uint32_t seed     = 42;\n    std::string tag        = \"\";\n    std::filesystem::path output_dir;\n};\n\n[[noreturn]] void die_usage(const char* prog) {\n    std::fprintf(stderr,\n        \"usage: %s --output-dir DIR [options]\\n\"\n        \"  --bytes N         Buffer size in bytes (default 1<<30; rounded down to 16 B)\\n\"\n        \"  --warmups N       Untimed warmup runs per workload (default 2)\\n\"\n        \"  --reps N          Timed runs per workload (default 16)\\n\"\n        \"  --seed N          PRNG seed for the source buffer fill (default 42)\\n\"\n        \"  --tag STR         Free-form label written to every CSV row\\n\"\n        \"  --output-dir DIR  Required. memcpy.csv is appended to here.\\n\",\n        prog);\n    std::exit(1);\n}\n\nArgs parse_args(int argc, char** argv) {\n    Args a;\n    for (int i = 1; i < argc; ++i) {\n        std::string flag = argv[i];\n        auto get_val = [&]() -> std::string {\n            if (i + 1 >= argc) {\n                std::fprintf(stderr, \"Missing value for %s\\n\", flag.c_str());\n                die_usage(argv[0]);\n            }\n            return argv[++i];\n        };\n        if      (flag == \"--bytes\")      a.bytes      = std::stoull(get_val());\n        else if (flag == \"--warmups\")    a.warmups    = std::stoi(get_val());\n        else if (flag == \"--reps\")       a.reps       = std::stoi(get_val());\n        else if (flag == \"--seed\")       a.seed       = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(std::stoul(get_val()));\n        else if (flag == \"--tag\")        a.tag        = get_val();\n        else if (flag == \"--output-dir\") a.output_dir = get_val();\n        else { std::fprintf(stderr, \"Unknown flag: %s\\n\", flag.c_str()); die_usage(argv[0]); }\n    }\n    if (a.output_dir.empty()) die_usage(argv[0]);\n    a.bytes -= a.bytes % sizeof(uint4);  // round down for vectorized copy\n    if (a.bytes == 0) {\n        std::fprintf(stderr, \"--bytes must be at least %zu\\n\", sizeof(uint4));\n        std::exit(1);\n    }\n    return a;\n}\n\n__global__ void copy_kernel(uint4* dst, const uint4* src, std::size_t n) {\n    const std::size_t tid = blockIdx.x * std::size_t{blockDim.x} + threadIdx.x;\n    if (tid < n) dst[tid] = src[tid];\n}\n\nstd::string format_row(const std::string& workload, std::size_t bytes,\n                       int rep, const std::string& tag,\n                       float time_ms, double gbps)\n{\n    std::ostringstream s;\n    s.precision(9);\n    s << \"cuda,\"\n      << workload << \",\"\n      << bytes << \",\"\n      << rep << \",\"\n      << tag << \",\"\n      << time_ms << \",\"\n      << gbps;\n    return s.str();\n}\n\n} // namespace\n\nint main(int argc, char** argv) {\n    const Args args = parse_args(argc, argv);\n\n    const std::size_t bytes = args.bytes;\n    const std::size_t n_u32 = bytes / sizeof(std::uint32_t);\n    const std::size_t n_u4  = bytes / sizeof(uint4);\n\n    std::fprintf(stderr,\n        \"[memcpy] bytes=%zu warmups=%d reps=%d seed=%u tag=\\\"%s\\\"\\n\",\n        bytes, args.warmups, args.reps, args.seed, args.tag.c_str());\n\n    std::uint32_t* d_src = nullptr;\n    std::uint32_t* d_dst = nullptr;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_src, bytes) >> BENCH_CHECK;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_dst, bytes) >> BENCH_CHECK;\n\n    // Fill the source buffer with non-trivial bytes so the kernel\n    // doesn't accidentally measure a zero-page fast path.\n    bench::fill_uniform_keys(d_src, n_u32, args.seed,\n                             std::uint32_t{1} << 31);\n    cudaMemset(d_dst, 0, bytes) >> BENCH_CHECK;\n    cudaDeviceSynchronize() >> BENCH_CHECK;\n\n    bench::CsvWriter csv(args.output_dir / \"memcpy.csv\",\n        \"library,workload,bytes,rep,tag,time_ms,gbps_effective\");\n\n    bench::EventTimer timer;\n\n    auto run = [&](const char* workload, auto&& launch) {\n        for (int w = 0; w < args.warmups; ++w) launch();\n        cudaDeviceSynchronize() >> BENCH_CHECK;\n        for (int r = 0; r < args.reps; ++r) {\n            timer.begin();\n            launch();\n            const float ms = timer.end_ms();\n            // Two bytes of DRAM traffic per byte of payload.\n            const double gbps = 2.0 * static_cast<double>(bytes)\n                              / (static_cast<double>(ms) * 1.0e6);\n            csv.write_row(format_row(workload, bytes, r, args.tag, ms, gbps));\n        }\n    };\n\n    run(\"memcpy_d2d\", [&]() {\n        cudaMemcpyAsync(d_dst, d_src, bytes, cudaMemcpyDeviceToDevice)\n            >> BENCH_CHECK;\n    });\n\n    constexpr int block = 256;\n    const int grid = static_cast<int>((n_u4 + block - 1) / block);\n    run(\"memcpy_kernel\", [&]() {\n        copy_kernel<<<grid, block>>>(\n            reinterpret_cast<uint4*>(d_dst),\n            reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(d_src),\n            n_u4);\n        cudaGetLastError() >> BENCH_CHECK;\n    });\n\n    cudaFree(d_src);\n    cudaFree(d_dst);\n    return 0;\n}\n"
}
```

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T20:55:38.196Z

Paused after the first two as planned. What's there:

- [benchmarks/common.cuh](benchmarks/common.cuh): `BENCH_CHECK` postfix CUDA error checker, `EventTimer`, `CsvWriter` (append + header-if-new + stdout echo), device-side `fmix32`, `fill_uniform_keys` for `Uniform(0, N)` key generation, `next_pow2`.
- [benchmarks/benchmark_memcpy.cu](benchmarks/benchmark_memcpy.cu): times two workloads — `cudaMemcpyAsync(..., D2D)` and a trivial `uint4`-vectorized copy kernel — with the agreed warmup + rep pattern. CSV at `<output-dir>/memcpy.csv` with columns `library, workload, bytes, rep, tag, time_ms, gbps_effective`.

One design choice worth flagging before I write the insert/get binaries:

**Bandwidth metric for the memcpy reference uses 2× the buffer size per op** (one read + one write of every byte). This is the standard convention for copy benchmarks and matches what tools like `bandwidthTest` report. The insert/get binaries will use a different convention: `n_ops × sizeof(Bucket)`, which counts one cache-line read per op (the floor estimate we discussed). The two numbers will both be in the `gbps_effective` column but conceptually represent different things — memcpy is "actual peak DRAM," insert/get is "lower bound on actual traffic." We'll need to remember that when plotting the comparison.

Want me to proceed to `benchmark_insert.cu` next, or push back on the metric mismatch first?

---

A few notes on your implementation.

- Instead of CsvWriter, CSVWriter will read nicer
- Let's copy-paste the existing code from the tests instead of writing a bespoke BENCH_CHECK with slightly different syntax. Usually copy-paste is not great but in this case it is justified.
- Instead of re-implementing fmix32 and fmix64, let's mark this specific function as __host__ __device__ in the header, letting us reuse it. We can do similarly for next_pow2, except that's constexpr so the attributes are not needed. I think this may come up in the tests too.
- I believe previously there was an error saying __global__ cannot be inline - something worth considering.

Then, let's discuss metrics.

Identical in shape to the macro\n// in <tests/tests.cuh>; copy-pasted rather than shared because the benchmark\n// suite intentionally does not depend on test infrastructure.\n//\n//     cudaMalloc(&p, n) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n// ----------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\nstruct CudaCheckLoc {\n    const char* file;\n    int         line;\n};\n\ninline void operator>>(cudaError_t e, CudaCheckLoc loc) {\n    if (e != cudaSuccess) {\n        std::fprintf(stderr, \"CUDA error at %s:%d: %s\\n\",\n                     loc.file, loc.line, cudaGetErrorString(e));\n        std::exit(1);\n    }\n}\n\n#define CUDA_CHECK CudaCheckLoc{__FILE__, __LINE__}\n\nnamespace bench {\n\n// CUDA event timer. begin() records the start event; }\n    float end_ms() {\n        cudaEventRecord(stop_) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n        cudaEventSynchronize(stop_) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n        float ms;\n        cudaEventElapsedTime(&ms, start_, stop_) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n        return ms;\n    }\n\nprivate:\n    cudaEvent_t start_{};\n    cudaEvent_t stop_{};\n};\n\n// CSV writer: appends rows to a file in the output directory, and also\n// echoes every row (plus the header) to stdout for live visibility.\n//\n// If the target file is new or empty when constructed, the header line\n// is written to the file first; if not, the file is appended to without\n// a fresh header (so a sweep over many invocations builds one CSV).\nclass CSVWriter {\npublic:\n    CSVWriter(const std::filesystem::path& path, std::string header)\n        : header_(std::move(header))\n    {\n        if (!path.parent_path().empty()) {\n            std::filesystem::create_directories(path.parent_path());\n        }\n        const bool needs_header = !std::filesystem::exists(path)\n                                || std::filesystem::file_size(path) == 0;\n        file_.open(path, std::ios::app);\n        if (!file_) {\n            std::fprintf(stderr, \"Failed to open %s for writing\\n\",\n                         path.string().c_str());\n            std::exit(1);\n        }\n        if (needs_header) {\n            file_ << header_ << \"\\n\";\n            file_.flush();\n        }\n        // Echo header once per invocation, regardless of file state, so\n        // stdout is self-documenting even when appending to an existing\n        // CSV from the sweep driver.\n        std::cout << header_ << \"\\n\";\n    }\n\n    void write_row(const std::string& row) {\n        file_ << row << \"\\n\";\n        file_.flush();\n        std::cout << row << \"\\n\";\n        std::cout.flush();\n    }\n\nprivate:\n    std::string   header_;\n    std::ofstream file_;\n};\n\n// Fill out[0..n) with fmix32(seed + i) mod range. For `range` a power\n// of two (the case here, since key range = α × capacity with capacity\n// a power of two), the modulus yields a uniform distribution.\n//\n// Not marked inline: __global__ functions cannot be inline, but each\n// benchmark binary is a single translation unit that includes this\n// header exactly once, so there is no multiple-definition concern.\n__global__ void fill_uniform_keys_kernel(\n    std::uint32_t* out, std::size_t n,\n    std::uint32_t seed, std::uint32_t range)\n{\n    const std::size_t tid = blockIdx.x * std::size_t{blockDim.x} + threadIdx.x;\n    if (tid >= n) return;\n    out[tid] = gpurhh::detail::fmix32(static_cast<std::uint32_t>(tid) + seed) % range;\n}\n\ninline void fill_uniform_keys(\n    std::uint32_t* d_out, std::size_t n,\n    std::uint32_t seed, std::uint32_t range)\n{\n    constexpr int block = 256;\n    const int grid = static_cast<int>((n + block - 1) / block);\n    fill_uniform_keys_kernel<<<grid, block>>>(d_out, n, seed, range);\n    cudaGetLastError() >> CUDA_CHECK;\n}\n\n} // namespace bench\n"
```json
}
```

</details>

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T21:05:22.307Z

Two different things, in two senses — **what bytes are counted**, and **whether it's exact or a floor**.

## What each one counts

**memcpy (`2 × bytes / time`)**

Counts every byte that crosses the DRAM↔SM boundary. A device-to-device copy of N bytes reads N bytes from DRAM and writes N bytes to DRAM, so total DRAM traffic is `2N`. The "2×" is not a fudge factor — it's the actual physical traffic.

**insert / get (`n_ops × sizeof(Bucket) / time`)**

Counts the bare minimum *one cache-line read per operation*. It assumes:
- Exactly one bucket-load per op (no displacement chains on insert, no extended probing on get/miss).
- Zero traffic for writes (CAS on insert, output buffer on get, atomic counters).

The write traffic for our case is small (a 4-byte get-output per op, an 8-byte CAS on a successful insert) — adding it explicitly would change the number by maybe a few percent. The probe-count assumption is the bigger one: at high load factor or on the miss path, the *real* average bucket-loads per op is > 1.

## Semantic difference

| | memcpy | insert / get |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Direction of traffic | reads + writes | reads only |
| Probe count assumed | n/a | exactly 1 per op |
| Relationship to actual DRAM traffic | **exact** | **lower bound** |

So:

- memcpy bandwidth = "this is how much DRAM bandwidth the device can actually deliver, end to end."
- insert/get bandwidth = "this is the bandwidth our operations need *at minimum*; actual DRAM traffic during the kernel is at least this much, likely more."

## Implication when we compare

If we plot both lines together and memcpy_kernel comes out at e.g. 900 GB/s and `bench_insert` at 600 GB/s, we **cannot** conclude "the hash table is 67% efficient." What we can say is:

> The hash table achieves at least 600 GB/s of *useful-probe-traffic*. The actual DRAM traffic during the kernel is some larger number (≥ 600 GB/s × average_probe_count). The device's known ceiling is 900 GB/s.

If we want a number we can directly compare to the memcpy peak, we have two paths:

1. **Instrument probe count** with an atomic counter in the kernel (one increment per bucket-load), then report `n_ops × avg_probes × sizeof(Bucket) / time` — the actual DRAM traffic. Has a perturbation cost (an extra atomic per probe) — but at high load factor with many probes per op, that perturbation might be ≤ the noise we already have.
2. **Use Nsight Compute** to read the DRAM throughput counter directly. No perturbation; not part of the benchmark binary; harder to wire into the CSV.

For v1 I'd leave the floor estimate in place and label the column accordingly (e.g. `gbps_floor` instead of `gbps_effective` — the latter overstates the precision). If the curves are surprising, option 1 is what to add next.

Does that match your mental model, or were you asking about a different aspect of the difference?

```
64	        cudaEventSynchronize(stop_) >> CUDA_CHECK;

83	    CSVWriter(const std::filesystem::path& path, std::string header)

85	    {

87	            std::filesystem::create_directories(path.parent_path());
88	        }
89	        const bool needs_header = !std::filesystem::exists(path)
90	                                || std::filesystem::file_size(path) == 0;
91	        file_.open(path, std::ios::app);
92	        if (!file_) {
93	            std::fprintf(stderr, "Failed to open %s for writing\n",
94	                         path.string().c_str());
95	            std::exit(1);
96	        }
97	        if (needs_header) {
98	            file_ << header_ << "\n";
99	            file_.flush();
100	        }
101	        // Echo header once per invocation, regardless of file state, so
102	        // stdout is self-documenting even when appending to an existing
103	        // CSV from the sweep driver.
104	        std::cout << header_ << "\n";
105	    }
106

108	        file_ << row << "\n";

111	        std::cout.flush();
112	    }
113

122	//
123	// Not marked inline: __global__ functions cannot be inline, but each
124	// benchmark binary is a single translation unit that includes this
125	// header exactly once, so there is no multiple-definition concern.
126	__global__ void fill_uniform_keys_kernel(
127	    std::uint32_t* out, std::size_t n,
128	    std::uint32_t seed, std::uint32_t range)
129	{
130	    const std::size_t tid = blockIdx.x * std::size_t{blockDim.x} + threadIdx.x;
131	    if (tid >= n) return;
132	    out[tid] = gpurhh::detail::fmix32(static_cast<std::uint32_t>(tid) + seed) % range;
133	}
134	
135	inline void fill_uniform_keys(
136	    std::uint32_t* d_out, std::size_t n,
137	    std::uint32_t seed, std::uint32_t range)
138	{
139	    constexpr int block = 256;
140	    const int grid = static_cast<int>((n + block - 1) / block);
141	    fill_uniform_keys_kernel<<<grid, block>>>(d_out, n, seed, range);
142	    cudaGetLastError() >> CUDA_CHECK;
143	}
```

## User — 2026-05-17T21:09:42.686Z

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T21:09:53.653Z

<em>Thinking…</em>

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T21:10:04.452Z

Yes, that's exactly the gap. To say it more precisely:

**What we want:** bytes of DRAM traffic per second (= memory bandwidth).
**What we can measure cheaply:** completed ops, elapsed time.
**Bridge:** an assumption about bytes-per-op.

The bridge is the lossy part. We're assuming bytes-per-op = `sizeof(Bucket)` = one cache line, but actual bytes-per-op = `avg_probe_count × sizeof(Bucket)`, and `avg_probe_count ≥ 1`. So:

$$
\text{reported gbps} \;\le\; \text{actual DRAM gbps used by the kernel}
$$

The gap widens exactly where you'd expect it to:

- **Low load factor (F = 0.5):** most ops finish in one probe; `avg_probe_count` ≈ 1.05–1.1. Reported gbps is within ~10% of actual.
- **High load factor (F = 2.0, 3.0):** ~86% / 95% occupancy. Probe chains and Robin Hood displacements push `avg_probe_count` materially above 1 (probably 2–4 at the cliff). Reported gbps could be 2–4× *under* the actual DRAM use.
- **Get on the miss path:** every miss walks until it either sees an empty slot, a richer resident, or hits the cap. At high occupancy, this is consistently > 1 bucket.

So the rule of reading the plot is:
- If the reported gbps is **near memcpy peak** → we're saturating bandwidth (the actual is at least the reported).
- If the reported gbps is **well below memcpy peak** → we can't tell from this number alone whether we're inefficient, or just doing more work per op than the floor assumes. The diagnostic is to add a probe counter and recover `avg_probe_count`.

A second consequence: the *shape* of the curve will be distorted at high F. Throughput in ops/sec genuinely drops as probes lengthen (correct), but the reported gbps will drop *less* (or even rise, if probe count grows faster than ops/sec falls) because actual DRAM work per op is going up. So "gbps_floor vs F" is not a monotone proxy for "DRAM efficiency vs F."

If we add the probe counter later, the difference between the two curves *is* the average probe count — useful data on its own.

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T21:12:26.666Z

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T21:12:47.352Z

Two separate things — let me take them in order.

## Nsight Compute as a spot-check tool

`ncu` (Nsight Compute, ships with the CUDA toolkit) reads hardware performance counters directly, including:

- `dram__bytes_read.sum`, `dram__bytes_write.sum` — actual bytes that crossed the DRAM↔GPU boundary, per kernel launch.
- `dram__throughput.avg.pct_of_peak_sustained_elapsed` — achieved DRAM throughput as % of the hardware peak.
- `lts__t_bytes` and friends — L2 traffic, useful for separating L2-resident from DRAM-bound regimes.

Workflow: build the benchmark binaries the normal way, then invoke them under `ncu`:

```
ncu --metrics dram__throughput.avg.pct_of_peak_sustained_elapsed,dram__bytes_read.sum,dram__bytes_write.sum \
    ./benchmark_insert --capacity-bytes 1073741824 --load-factor 2.0 ...
```

ncu replays each kernel several times to harvest counters in batches, so a profiled run is 5–20× slower than an unprofiled run. The output is per-kernel-launch, not per-op, and the format isn't CSV-friendly — needs a wrapper to fold it into our schema.

So ncu's natural role is *not* the per-rep number that lands in the CSV; it's the **independent ground-truth check** we run on a handful of representative (workload, F) points to confirm the floor metric isn't lying to us. Run it on the F = 3.0 case, see the real DRAM number, compare to the reported floor, and we'll know how much we're undercounting at the cliff.

I'd recommend keeping ncu as a manual sanity-check step (a section in `notes/` or a `bench/profile.sh` that wraps a single config) rather than wiring it into the main sweep.

## Patching get/insert from outside — no clean way

C++ genuinely has no in-language equivalent of monkey-patching or aspect-oriented hooks. Header-only templates are particularly resistant because every call site gets its own inlined copy, so there's nothing to interpose on. The realistic options all require library code changes:

1. **Template-parameter observer** — add a defaulted template parameter to `insert`/`get` whose call site fires on each probe iteration. Default is a no-op functor that the compiler eliminates, so non-instrumented callers pay zero cost. Roughly:
   ```cpp
   struct no_op_observer { __device__ void probe() {} };

   template <class Tile, class Observer = no_op_observer>
   __device__ cuda::std::optional<Slot> insert(
       const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value, Observer obs = {});
   ```
   In the loop, `obs.probe();` once per iteration. The benchmark's kernel constructs a `probe_counter_observer` that holds a pointer to a global atomic and increments it.

2. **`#ifdef` guard** — `#ifdef GPURHH_INSTRUMENT_PROBES` blocks inside `insert`/`get` that do the atomic. The benchmark TU defines the macro before including the header. Forks the build (instrumented vs not) but is dead simple.

Of the two, **option 1** is what I'd reach for. It's a one-time, additive API change that costs zero overhead in normal builds, doesn't fork compilation, and lets us extend with more counters later (probe count, CAS retries, displacement count) without growing the surface. Option 2 is fewer characters but introduces a build mode we'd have to maintain.

Beyond those, the only things that come close to "downstream patching" — function pointers, `std::function` indirection, linker shims — all need to be designed in upfront and aren't suitable for header-only inlined templates anyway.

## What I'd actually do

1. Land the benchmarks with the floor metric and the column labeled honestly (`gbps_floor`, not `gbps_effective`).
2. Use `ncu` on the high-F cases once we have curves, to validate the picture.
3. If we still want the in-binary number to be accurate at high F, add the template observer (option 1) and a `probe_count` column to the CSV — small, additive change.

This way we don't pay for instrumentation until we've actually seen we need it.

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T21:18:34.225Z

Like this approach — it's the minimum-overhead version of the same idea, and it stays in the spirit of `GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS` (gated, opt-in, no surface for non-bench users).

One detail to nail down: **"every global memory call" should mean "every bucket read," not "every thread-load."**

The hardware coalesces the 16 lane reads of consecutive 8-byte slots into **one** 128-byte DRAM transaction. So the bandwidth-meaningful count is *probe iterations*, not lane-thread loads. We want one increment per bucket fetch — done by lane 0 of the tile — not 16. That's also what makes the multiplication `total_probes × sizeof(Bucket)` come out as bytes-of-DRAM-traffic, directly comparable to the memcpy peak.

Counter type: `unsigned long long` (= 64 bits), which is the largest with atomic support on all NVIDIA compute capabilities since 3.5. Saturation isn't a concern (1 GiB / F=3 / avg ~3 probes ≈ 10⁹ increments, well under 2⁶⁴).

Signature:

```cpp
template <class Tile>
__device__ cuda::std::optional<Slot> insert(
    const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value
#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
    , unsigned long long* probe_counter
#endif
);
```

Inside the loop, right after the bucket read:

```cpp
const Slot slot = buckets_[bucket_idx].slots[tile.thread_rank()];
#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) {
    cuda::atomic_ref<unsigned long long, cuda::thread_scope_device>
        c(*probe_counter);
    c.fetch_add(1, cuda::memory_order_relaxed);
}
#endif
```

Two side effects worth flagging upfront:

1. **CAS retries naturally re-enter the loop**, so a failed CAS that re-reads the same bucket is counted as a second probe. That's the right thing — it's a real DRAM transaction.
2. **Every call site that wants the counter must wrap the arg in a matching `#ifdef`** — same shape as the `GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS` pattern. Just one extra knob downstream callers need to be aware of (in our case, only the benchmark TUs).

Three small choices left:

- **Counter granularity**: one shared counter per kernel, or one per op? Shared is dramatically simpler (one allocation, one atomic location) and lets us compute `avg_probes = total_probes / n_ops`. Per-op would let us look at the distribution, but adds a 1.5 GiB output array. **Shared.**
- **Separate counters for insert vs get?** We run them in separate kernels, separate CSV files. **Single counter, reused per kernel.**
- **What lands in the CSV**: I'd add `total_probes` as a column. The plotting script can derive `avg_probes` and the corrected bandwidth (`total_probes × bytes_per_op / time`).

Sound good? If yes, I'll thread it through `View::insert` and `View::get`, and update the two benchmark binaries (once they exist) to define the macro and pass the counter.

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T21:29:16.718Z

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T21:29:40.441Z

Three things, in order.

## Memory pressure — yes, this is real and bigger than you might expect

Back-of-envelope: a 1 GiB run at F = 2 with average probe count ~2 fires roughly **5 × 10⁸ atomic increments** on a single global location. Atomics on one location serialize through the L2 atomic unit at ~1 ns each, which is ~500 ms of pure atomic work — on a kernel that should naturally run in ~100 ms. So a naive single-counter scheme is ~5× the kernel's real runtime, which not only distorts the timing but actively competes for the very bandwidth we're trying to measure. Useless.

The standard fix is to never let the per-probe atomic touch global memory at all:

- Each calling tile carries a **register-resident counter** (an `unsigned long long`).
- Inside `View::insert`/`View::get`, lane 0 increments that register counter on each probe. No atomic needed — it's thread-local.
- At the end of the user kernel (after the per-tile work loop), lane 0 does **one** `atomicAdd` to the global counter with the per-tile total.

That collapses the global-atomic count from "per probe" to "per tile, at end of kernel." With a properly grid-strided launch (say 8K blocks × 16 tiles), that's ~130K atomics total — order of microseconds, well below kernel time.

API consequence: the conditional parameter is a **reference / pointer to a thread-local counter** (caller's responsibility to allocate, accumulate, and flush to global), not a pointer to a global. The library never touches global memory for instrumentation — the user kernel does the global atomic exactly once at end-of-work.

```cpp
template <class Tile>
__device__ cuda::std::optional<Slot> insert(
    const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value
#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
    , unsigned long long& probe_count
#endif
);
```

Inside the loop:
```cpp
#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) ++probe_count;
#endif
```

Caller (benchmark kernel) side:
```cpp
unsigned long long my_probes = 0;            // per-thread register
for (... grid-stride op loop ...) {
    view.insert(tile, key, value, my_probes);
}
if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) {
    atomicAdd(global_probe_counter, my_probes);  // one atomic per tile
}
```

## Per-counter scope

Agreed — the header takes a counter, and "what counter" is the benchmark's problem. Insert and get are separate binaries, separate kernels, separate counters; we allocate fresh, zero it, and read it back per kernel launch.

## What we're counting — probes only, and why that's enough

The most rigorous accounting would be:

| Source                             | When             | Bytes      | Fraction of probe cost |
| ---------------------------------- | ---------------- | ---------- | ---------------------- |
| Bucket read (1 cache line)         | every probe      | 128        | 100% (reference)       |
| CAS write on success               | insert, once/op  | 8          | ~6%                    |
| Get output write to result buffer  | get, once/op     | 4          | ~3%                    |

The bucket reads completely dominate. The 8-byte CAS write is, in practice, into a cache line that's already resident from the read on the same iteration — its DRAM cost is mostly absorbed into the eventual writeback rather than a fresh transaction. The 4-byte output buffer write is similarly small.

I'd count **only probes**, label the metric honestly (e.g. `gbps_probes`), and either:
- ignore the 3–6% write contribution as smaller than measurement noise across reps, or
- add an analytic constant to the GB/s computation (`n_ops × write_bytes_per_op`) — the user kernel knows `n_ops` and which workload it is, so this is a host-side post-multiplication, not a kernel cost.

I'd start with the first (just probes, document the omission), and revisit if we ever see ncu's true number disagreeing by more than a few percent.

Sound right? If so I'll:
1. Add the `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` macro guard in [include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh), with the reference parameter and the lane-0-increment.
2. Hold the benchmark-side wiring until the insert/get binaries get written — we'll thread the local counter through their kernels then.

3. How does write granularity behave? We can't read less than 128 bytes - is there something analogous for writes? We also need to think about what happens on CAS failures? ## Assistant — 2026-05-17T21:39:17.749Z

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T21:39:41.534Z

## 1. Clarifying the API: register, not array

`probe_count` is **one register-resident `unsigned long long` per CUDA thread**, not an array. Each thread has its own copy as a local variable; only lane 0 of each tile writes to it (the other 15 lanes have a copy that stays zero, which is fine — they're cheap unused registers). No global memory is touched during the loop at all.

That's why no atomic is needed during probing: a register is thread-local by definition, no other thread can see it, so no synchronization is required. The single atomic at end-of-kernel is just to fold all the per-tile totals into one global number — that's ~ #tiles atomics, which at ~130K is microseconds total.

You're right that there's also a valid "array per tile in global memory, no atomics" design. Worth laying out side by side:

| Approach                         | Per-probe cost              | End-of-kernel cost           | Extra memory                |
| -------------------------------- | --------------------------- | ---------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| Register + final atomic per tile | ~1 cycle (register ++)      | ~130K atomics, microseconds  | 0                           |
| Array slot per tile, no atomic   | ~50–100 cycles (cached R-M-W) | 0 (host sums after copy back) | ~1 MiB (#tiles × 8 B)       |

The array approach is essentially "free at the end" but every probe has to do a cached global memory read-modify-write, which is hundreds of cycles even when the line is hot in L1. The register approach has zero global memory traffic during probing — the increment compiles to one or two integer instructions. Both are well below the 500 ms catastrophe of a single shared global counter, but register is the better default. And the "nuisance" of the end-of-kernel aggregation is literally five lines in the user kernel:

```cpp
unsigned long long my_probes = 0;
for (...) view.insert(tile, k, v, my_probes);
if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) atomicAdd(global_counter, my_probes);
```

So unless you'd specifically prefer the array variant, I'd recommend the register version.

## 2. Write granularity and CAS

NVIDIA DRAM transactions are read/write sector-granular at 32 bytes; full cache-line accesses are 128 B = 4 sectors. So **writes can be smaller than reads** — an 8-byte CAS does not have to write a whole cache line. What it actually does in practice:

1. **Read brings the cache line into L1 / L2** at the start of the probe iteration (this is the 128 B transaction we've been counting).
2. **Ballots, shuffles, the CAS itself** all run while that line is still resident. These are warp intrinsics — single-digit nanoseconds — far below the cache eviction timescale. The line stays in L1 / L2 the whole time.
3. **CAS failure**: the instruction reads the slot (from L1), compares, fails, *no write occurs*. The line stays clean. Zero DRAM cost.
4. **CAS success**: the slot is updated in L1 / L2. The line becomes dirty. *No DRAM traffic at this moment* — the write is absorbed into the cache, exactly as you suspected. The DRAM write happens later, asynchronously, when the dirty line gets evicted (typically at full cache-line granularity, so the 8-byte CAS produces a 128 B write-back when its line is evicted).

So the total DRAM write traffic over the lifetime of the kernel is approximately:

$$
\text{DRAM writes} \approx (\text{unique modified buckets evicted from L2}) \times 128\,\text{B}
$$

For inserts at F = 1.0 (≈ 63% occupancy), unique modified buckets ≈ ~63% of the bucket array. That works out to ~0.63 GiB of DRAM writes against ~25 GiB of DRAM reads (134M ops × avg ~1.5 probes × 128 B). Writes are 2–3% of reads — well below measurement noise.

For gets, zero writes to the table. The only writes are the 4-byte output buffer per op, in a separate 1.5 GiB-or-so allocation that the cache also absorbs and partially writes back. Same order of magnitude as the insert-side writes, similarly negligible.

The summary the question converges to: **count probes only, don't count CAS writes, and don't worry about CAS failures.** The cache mechanics absolutely do what you assumed — the ballots and shuffles are microscopic relative to the eviction timeframe, the modified line stays hot until our work moves on. Writes contribute a few percent to total DRAM traffic and are dominated by the probe-read term.

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T21:43:31.406Z

Got it — I had the wrong granularity in mind. So the design is: a local counter declared at the top of `View::insert`/`View::get`, incremented on each probe, and written out once via a passed-in pointer at the end of the call. No atomics anywhere because the caller arranges for each call to write to its own unique slot.

```cpp
template <class Tile>
__device__ cuda::std::optional<Slot> insert(
    const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value
#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
    , unsigned long long* probe_count_out
#endif
)
{
    unsigned long long probes = 0;
    while (probe < probe_bound) {
        if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) ++probes;
        const Slot slot = buckets_[...].slots[...];
        // ... probing as before ...
    }
    if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) *probe_count_out = probes;
    return ...;
}
```

Caller:
```cpp
for (op_id in grid-stride) {
    view.insert(tile, keys[op_id], values[op_id], &probe_counts[op_id]);
}
```

Each call writes to a distinct slot → no atomic, no contention.

## What it costs

**Memory.** Array of `n_ops` slots. At 1 GiB table / F = 2, n_ops ≈ 268M:
- `uint64_t` → ~2 GiB.
- `uint16_t` → ~512 MiB. Plenty of range — probe count per op is bounded by `max_probe_buckets × CAS_retries`, well within 65535 even adversarially.

Worth dropping to `uint16_t`; we lose nothing meaningful and save 1.5 GiB.

**Write traffic during the kernel.** ~512 MiB of writes (uint16) or ~2 GiB (uint64). With grid-stride and lane-0 writing, the 16 tiles in a block hit 16 consecutive slots → coalesced single-cache-line transactions. At ~500 GB/s peak write bandwidth: roughly 1 ms (uint16) to 4 ms (uint64) of DRAM write time. Against a kernel that naturally runs ~25 ms, that's a **1–4% perturbation** — much better than the 500× catastrophe of the single global atomic.

**Bonus.** This gives us the per-op probe distribution for free. We can plot histograms, look at p99 / max, see how the cliff at high F manifests in tail probes vs. average probes. The register-plus-one-atomic alternative I proposed earlier collapses everything to a single total — useful, but less data.

## Aggregation

Two paths to fold `probe_counts` into a single number:
1. **Host-side sum.** After the kernel, `cudaMemcpy` the array back and `std::accumulate`. With uint16 / 512 MiB, the memcpy is ~1 ms and the sum is ~100 ms on host — adds wall-clock but not GPU time.
2. **Reduce kernel.** A second kernel that sums in parallel on GPU. ~1 ms. Use `cub::DeviceReduce::Sum` or a hand-rolled reduction.

For the bandwidth metric we only need the sum, but if we want to keep the distribution we'd copy the raw array anyway. Simplest: copy back, sum on host, optionally write the distribution to a sidecar file (`probe_dist_insert_F2.0.bin` or similar) for later analysis.

## A few small picks left

- **Counter type**: `uint16_t` (recommended) or stay with `uint64_t`?
- **Same parameter on `View::get`?** Same shape, same `unsigned ___* probe_count_out`, lane 0 writes at end. Symmetrical.
- **Aggregation default**: keep the raw array and write it to a sidecar, vs. throw it away after summing? I'd keep it — it's the whole point of going per-op.

If those land, I'll add the `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` guard to `View::insert` and `View::get`, and the benchmark binaries will wire up the array when they get written.

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T21:48:49.813Z

Mostly yes, with one clarification on the granularity and one honesty about a third option.

**Granularity correction.** What we actually designed is **per-op** (= per-call), not per-tile: a fresh local counter inside each call to `View::insert`/`get`, with the call's caller passing a unique array slot to write into. With a grid-stride loop, each tile makes many calls, each writing to its own different slot. So the array has `n_ops` entries, not `n_tiles` — that's what gives us the per-op distribution. (Per-tile would aggregate inside one tile across all the ops it handled, which we could do too, but loses the per-op view.)

**Accuracy vs the naive atomic — yes, dramatically.** A single global atomic incremented on every probe serializes ~10⁸–10⁹ ops through the L2 atomic unit and inflates kernel runtime by ~5×, contaminating exactly the bandwidth measurement we're trying to make. The per-op-slot approach trades that for ~1–4 ms of well-coalesced cache-line writes against a ~25 ms kernel — a 1–4% perturbation instead of a 5× one. Plus we get the distribution.

**Honesty: there's a third option that's even less perturbing.** Register-resident counter per thread, accumulated across the tile's entire grid-stride loop, with **one** `atomicAdd` per tile at end-of-kernel. That's ~130K atomics total (microseconds), versus the per-op approach's ~1–4 ms of writes. Strictly less perturbation than per-op. **But** it collapses everything to a single total — no distribution, no max, no p99.

So the real trade-off is:

| Approach | Perturbation | What we learn |
| --- | --- | --- |
| One global atomic per probe | ~500 ms (5× kernel time) | total only |
| Register + one atomic per tile at end | microseconds | total only |
| Per-op slot, no atomics | ~1–4 ms (uint16 / uint64) | total + full distribution |

Both the bottom two are accurate enough for our purposes. The per-op approach buys distribution data at the cost of a small additional write-traffic perturbation. If the distribution data is something you want — and at high F it probably is, because tail behavior is what Robin Hood is supposed to control — the per-op slot is the right call. If we only care about the total, register-plus-one-atomic is a hair cheaper but throws away information.

I'd go per-op for the distribution. Want me to lock that in?

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T21:55:15.831Z

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T21:55:30.794Z

Your decomposition is right, and I was muddling things by collapsing your axis 2 with a separate granularity question. Let me lay it out cleanly.

**Axis 1 (settled): accumulate in registers inside the function, write once at return.** Per-probe global writes are nowhere near worth it — 50–100 cycles per probe each. A register increment is one cycle. Clear win.

**Axis 2 (your question): where does the per-call count end up?**

- **(a) Single global counter, atomic add at return** — every call does an atomic on one location. With `n_ops` ≈ 10⁸, that serializes ~10⁸ atomics through the L2 atomic unit. Hundreds of milliseconds of contention. Bad.
- **(b) Per-something array, no atomics** — every call writes to a slot only it touches. No contention, well-coalesced writes. Good.

You're right that (b) is the win here. The trade-off in axis 2 is fundamentally "is the atomic contention worth eating to save the array allocation?" — and the answer is unambiguously no.

---

Where I added confusion is by sliding in a **third axis** without flagging it: in (b), **how many slots in the array?**

- **(b-tile) `n_tiles` slots** — function returns the per-call count to the caller; the caller's kernel maintains a per-tile register accumulator across all its grid-stride iterations, writes once to its tile's slot at end of kernel. Memory: ~1 MiB. Writes during kernel: ~130K total. Perturbation: microseconds. Gives only a per-tile total (which, summed, is the grand total).
- **(b-op) `n_ops` slots** — function writes its count straight to a per-call slot. Memory: 512 MiB (uint16) to 2 GiB (uint64). Writes during kernel: `n_ops`. Perturbation: 1–4 ms. Gives the full per-op probe distribution.

This is what I was previously calling "per-op vs per-tile" — it lives orthogonal to your axis 2 (both are no-atomic array storage). The only thing it buys you is **resolution of the output**: a single grand total vs. a per-op histogram. (b-op) costs more memory and a small extra write perturbation; in exchange you can plot p99 / max / tail behavior, which is the kind of data that earns its keep when Robin Hood starts to struggle at F = 2–3.

So the full picture is:

| Approach | Atomics | Mem | Perturbation | Output |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| (a) Single global atomic | n_ops on one loc | 8 B | hundreds of ms | total only |
| (b-tile) n_tiles array | none | ~1 MiB | µs | total + per-tile |
| (b-op) n_ops array | none | 512 MiB – 2 GiB | 1–4 ms | total + per-op distribution |

Nothing else hiding in there as far as I can see. The remaining call is between (b-tile) and (b-op) — same accuracy story (both demolish the atomic), the only question is whether the per-op distribution is worth the memory.

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T22:04:51.698Z

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T22:05:00.192Z

Great question — the distinction only exists because of how we launch the kernel.

Two patterns are possible:

**One tile per op (no grid-stride).** Launch a grid with exactly `n_ops / tiles_per_block` blocks. Each tile handles one op, full stop. Here `n_tiles == n_ops` and there's no meaningful difference between the two granularities. The "(b-tile) array" and "(b-op) array" become the same array.

**Grid-stride loop.** Launch a fixed, modest-sized grid (e.g. 8K blocks) and have each tile iterate over many ops:

```cpp
for (std::size_t op_id = tile_id; op_id < n_ops; op_id += total_tiles) {
    view.insert(tile, keys[op_id], values[op_id]);
}
```

Each tile handles `n_ops / total_tiles` ops over its lifetime. With 8K blocks × 16 tiles/block = 128K tiles and 268M ops, that's ~2K ops per tile. **Here `n_tiles ≪ n_ops`**, and the granularity choice becomes real:
- `n_tiles` array slots ⇒ ~1 MiB, each slot accumulates ~2K ops' worth of probes.
- `n_ops` array slots ⇒ 512 MiB+, one slot per individual op.

The grid-stride pattern is the standard idiom on NVIDIA because:
- Launching 16M blocks (the no-grid-stride alternative for n_ops = 268M) wastes scheduler bookkeeping.
- A fixed-size grid sized to the device's SM count autotunes to whatever GPU you're on without changing the kernel.
- Load is naturally balanced — slow tiles just process fewer iterations.

The existing tests in this repo already use grid-stride (e.g. `for (std::size_t i = tile_id; i < n; i += total_tiles)` in `tests/isolated.cuh`), so I was implicitly assuming the benchmarks would too. If we instead went one-tile-per-op, axis 3 collapses and we just have one "array of `n_ops`" — but with the launch overhead concern above.

So the third axis only exists if we use grid-stride, which we should.

## User — 2026-05-17T22:10:38.710Z

How are tile ids allocated - are they unique over the whole grid, or within blocks?

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T22:10:51.037Z

<em>Thinking…</em>

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T22:10:56.194Z

CUDA doesn't give you a "tile ID" — cooperative groups only exposes `tile.thread_rank()` (a thread's rank within its tile, 0..tile_size-1). The tile ID, at whatever granularity you want, is something you compute from `threadIdx` and `blockIdx`.

For a tile within a block:
```cpp
const std::size_t tile_in_block = threadIdx.x / Table::tile_size;
// range: 0 .. tiles_per_block - 1
```

For a tile across the whole grid:
```cpp
const std::size_t tiles_per_block = blockDim.x / Table::tile_size;
const std::size_t tile_in_grid =
    blockIdx.x * tiles_per_block + threadIdx.x / Table::tile_size;
// range: 0 .. total_tiles_in_grid - 1
```

The existing code in [tests/isolated.cuh:140-143](tests/isolated.cuh#L140-L143) uses exactly this grid-global form. For our per-tile probe array, indexing with `tile_in_grid` is what gives every tile its own unique slot across the whole launch. Block-local IDs would alias across blocks — wrong if the array has one entry per tile in the grid, right only if the array were per-block-and-merged-later.

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T22:12:02.157Z

No — one kernel launch, with the loop inside the kernel. The "grid-stride" refers to the loop body's stride, not to the launch pattern.

```cpp
__global__ void insert_kernel(View view, const Key* keys, ..., std::size_t n_ops)
{
    auto block = cg::this_thread_block();
    auto tile  = cg::tiled_partition<Table::tile_size>(block);

    const std::size_t tiles_per_block = blockDim.x / Table::tile_size;
    const std::size_t tile_id     = blockIdx.x * tiles_per_block
                                  + threadIdx.x / Table::tile_size;
    const std::size_t total_tiles = gridDim.x * tiles_per_block;

    // Each tile picks up ops with stride `total_tiles`. One kernel launch
    // covers all n_ops; tiles loop here as needed.
    for (std::size_t op_id = tile_id; op_id < n_ops; op_id += total_tiles) {
        view.insert(tile, keys[op_id], values[op_id]);
    }
}
```

So:
- One `<<<grid, block>>>` launch.
- Grid size is fixed at something device-shaped (e.g. enough blocks to fill the SMs a few times over — common choice is `4 × num_SMs` blocks, or simply `min(some_cap, ceil(n_ops / tiles_per_block))`).
- Each tile's `for` loop runs `n_ops / total_tiles` iterations on average.
- Tiles drop out as the loop variable exceeds `n_ops`; load is naturally balanced.

The benefits over "launch one tile per op":
- Fewer blocks to schedule (avoids the 16M-blocks-for-268M-ops situation).
- Each tile reuses its already-loaded register state across many ops — cheaper than the spin-up cost of a fresh block.
- Same kernel binary works on any GPU; the launch shape is the only thing you tune.

So in the n_tiles-array storage, each tile writes once **at the end of its single grid-stride loop** (i.e. once per kernel launch), to its unique slot indexed by `tile_id`.

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T22:12:47.788Z

Exactly. Each tile is a long-lived worker for the duration of the kernel launch: it grabs op N, processes it; grabs op N + total_tiles, processes it; and so on until it runs out of ops to claim. The tile's local state (register-resident probe counter, etc.) persists across all those iterations, which is what makes the per-tile-array storage practical — one slot written once at the end of the loop, regardless of how many ops the tile handled.

The contrast with one-tile-per-op is essentially "tile per worker" vs. "tile per work item." Grid-stride is the former.

---

Let's do per-tile recording. We can actually still get some distributional information, it'll just be averaged over the reads in each tile, but not across times.

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T22:16:32.321Z

Adding the counter parameter to `View::insert` and `View::get` now. Using `unsigned long long&` (reference, per-tile accumulator passed in by the caller). Each binary opts in by defining `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` before including the header — same pattern as `GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS`.

```
260	    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
261	    // View: lightweight device-side handle. Trivially copyable; pass by value
262	    // into user kernels. Holds only a device pointer and a couple of integers.
263	    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
264	    class View {
265	    public:
266	        // Cooperative single-key insert.
267	        // Called by a tile of exactly tile_size threads in lock-step. Returns
268	        // nullopt on success (the in-flight pair was placed in an empty slot
269	        // or merged into an existing slot via the Reduction operator).
270	        //
271	        // On probe-cap failure returns the leftover Slot that could not be
272	        // placed. Note this is not necessarily the (key, value) originally
273	        // passed in: if the insertion triggered Robin Hood displacements
274	        // before failing, the returned Slot is the most-recently-evicted
275	        // victim — the originally-passed-in pair is already in the table,
276	        // and it is the victim that is now unreachable. Callers that need
277	        // to avoid data loss should buffer the returned Slot and retry it
278	        // elsewhere (typically by rebuilding a larger table).
279	        template <class Tile>
280	        __device__ cuda::std::optional<Slot> insert(
281	            const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value);
282	
283	        // Cooperative single-key lookup. Returns the stored value if the
284	        // key is present, or an empty optional otherwise. Every lane in the
285	        // tile receives the same result.
286	        template <class Tile>
287	        __device__ cuda::std::optional<Value> get(const Tile& tile, Key key) const;
288	
289	        __host__ __device__ std::size_t capacity() const noexcept { return capacity_; }
290	
291	    private:
292	        friend class HashTable;
293	
294	        Bucket*     buckets_       = nullptr;
```

```
418	
419	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>
420	typename HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::View
421	HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::view() const noexcept {
422	    View v;
423	    v.buckets_       = buckets_;
424	    v.capacity_      = capacity_;
425	    // For a usable table capacity_ is a power of two and the mask is
426	    // capacity_ - 1. For a moved-from / empty table capacity_ is 0; we set
427	    // the mask to 0 explicitly to avoid a wrap-around to ~0 which would be
428	    // misleading even though the View is unusable in that state anyway.
429	    v.capacity_mask_ = capacity_ > 0 ? capacity_ - 1 : 0;
430	    // v.hash_ and v.reduce_ are default-constructed by View's in-class
431	    // default initializers.
432	    return v;
433	}
434	
435	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>
436	template <class Tile>
437	__device__ auto HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::View::insert(
438	    const Tile& tile, K key, V value)
439	    -> cuda::std::optional<Slot>
440	{
441	    // Bucketed Robin Hood insertion with lock-free CAS. The tile carries an
442	    // in-flight pair (cur_key, cur_value) that may change due to Robin Hood
443	    // displacement — when we evict a "richer" resident, that resident
444	    // becomes our new in-flight pair and we keep probing from the next
445	    // bucket.
446	
447	    K cur_key   = key;
448	    V cur_value = value;
449	
450	    const std::size_t num_buckets = capacity_ / bucket_size;
451	    const std::size_t bucket_mask = num_buckets > 0 ? num_buckets - 1 : 0;
452	    const std::size_t probe_bound = num_buckets < max_probe_buckets
453	        ? num_buckets
454	        : static_cast<std::size_t>(max_probe_buckets);
455	
456	    std::size_t cur_home = (hash_(cur_key) & capacity_mask_) / bucket_size;
457	    std::size_t probe    = 0;
458	
459	    while (probe < probe_bound) {
460	        const std::size_t bucket_idx = (cur_home + probe) & bucket_mask;
461	
462	        // Cooperative load: one coalesced cache-line transaction. Each
463	        // lane reads one slot of the bucket (lane i reads slots[i]).
464	        const Slot slot = buckets_[bucket_idx].slots[tile.thread_rank()];
465	
466	        // Helper: `target_lane` performs an atomic CAS to replace its slot
467	        // (whose current contents we have in `slot`) with the in-flight
468	        // pair. On the match case the new value is computed as
469	        // `reduce_(existing, incoming)`; on the empty / displaceable cases
470	        // the new value is just the incoming value. Returns the broadcast
471	        // result of the CAS to every lane.
472	        auto try_cas = [&](int target_lane, bool apply_reduction) -> bool {
473	            bool cas_ok = false;
474	            if (tile.thread_rank() == target_lane) {
475	                const V to_store = apply_reduction
476	                    ? reduce_(slot.value, cur_value)
477	                    : cur_value;
478	                Slot expected = slot;
479	                const Slot desired{cur_key, to_store};
480	                cuda::atomic_ref<Slot, cuda::thread_scope_device> atomic_slot(
481	                    buckets_[bucket_idx].slots[target_lane]);
482	                cas_ok = atomic_slot.compare_exchange_strong(expected, desired);
483	            }
484	            return tile.shfl(cas_ok, target_lane);
485	        };
486	
487	        // (1) Key match — combine the in-flight value with the existing one
488	        //     via the Reduction operator (default: replace).
489	        const auto match_mask = tile.ballot(slot.key == cur_key);
490	        if (match_mask != 0) {
491	            if (try_cas(__ffs(match_mask) - 1, /*apply_reduction=*/true))
492	                return cuda::std::nullopt;
493	            continue;  // CAS lost the race — retry this bucket.
494	        }
495	
496	        // (2) Empty slot — claim it for our in-flight pair.
497	        const auto empty_mask = tile.ballot(slot.key == empty_key);
498	        if (empty_mask != 0) {
499	            if (try_cas(__ffs(empty_mask) - 1, /*apply_reduction=*/false))
500	                return cuda::std::nullopt;
501	            continue;  // Someone else took the slot — retry.
502	        }
503	
504	        // (3) Displaceable slot — Robin Hood swap with a richer resident.
505	        // Compute each resident's probe distance. (For empty slots this
506	        // gives nonsense, but we already eliminated the empty case above,
507	        // so the next ballot is meaningful.)
508	        const std::size_t resident_home =
509	            (hash_(slot.key) & capacity_mask_) / bucket_size;
510	        const std::size_t resident_probe =
511	            (bucket_idx - resident_home) & bucket_mask;
512	        const auto displaceable_mask = tile.ballot(resident_probe < probe);
513	        if (displaceable_mask != 0) {
514	            const int target_lane = __ffs(displaceable_mask) - 1;
515	            if (try_cas(target_lane, /*apply_reduction=*/false)) {
516	                // Adopt the evicted pair as our new in-flight pair. We
517	                // continue from the next bucket; the evicted pair's probe
518	                // distance there is `resident_probe + 1`.
519	                cur_key   = tile.shfl(slot.key,        target_lane);
520	                cur_value = tile.shfl(slot.value,      target_lane);
521	                cur_home  = tile.shfl(resident_home,   target_lane);
522	                probe     = tile.shfl(resident_probe,  target_lane) + 1;
523	                continue;
524	            }
525	            continue;  // CAS lost the race — retry.
526	        }
527	
528	        // (4) Nothing applies — advance one bucket.
529	        ++probe;
530	    }
531	
532	    // Probe budget exhausted. Hand the in-flight pair back so the caller
533	    // can buffer it and rehash into a larger table. This is the original
534	    // (key, value) if no displacement happened, or the most-recently-
535	    // evicted victim otherwise — either way, returning it is what keeps
536	    // the operation lossless.
537	    return Slot{cur_key, cur_value};
538	}
539	
540	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>
541	template <class Tile>
542	__device__ cuda::std::optional<V>
543	HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::View::get(const Tile& tile, K key) const
544	{
545	    // Bucket-level Robin Hood lookup. Probe home_bucket, then home_bucket+1,
546	    // etc., wrapping modulo num_buckets. Each iteration is one coalesced
547	    // cache-line load (one slot per tile lane) and a few tile-wide ballots
```

</details>

```
540	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>
541	template <class Tile>
542	__device__ cuda::std::optional<V>
543	HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::View::get(const Tile& tile, K key) const
544	{
545	    // Bucket-level Robin Hood lookup. Probe home_bucket, then home_bucket+1,
546	    // etc., wrapping modulo num_buckets. Each iteration is one coalesced
547	    // cache-line load (one slot per tile lane) and a few tile-wide ballots
548	    // to identify the terminating condition.
549	
550	    const auto h = hash_(key);
551	    const std::size_t home_slot   = h & capacity_mask_;
552	    const std::size_t home_bucket = home_slot / bucket_size;
553	    const std::size_t num_buckets = capacity_ / bucket_size;
554	    const std::size_t bucket_mask = num_buckets > 0 ? num_buckets - 1 : 0;
555	
556	    // Probe at most max_probe_buckets buckets, or every bucket in the table
557	    // if it's smaller than the cap.
558	    const std::size_t probe_bound = num_buckets < max_probe_buckets
559	        ? num_buckets
560	        : static_cast<std::size_t>(max_probe_buckets);
561	
562	    for (std::size_t probe = 0; probe < probe_bound; ++probe) {
563	        const std::size_t bucket_idx = (home_bucket + probe) & bucket_mask;
564	
565	        // Each lane reads one slot — one coalesced cache-line transaction
566	        // for the whole tile.
567	        const Slot slot = buckets_[bucket_idx].slots[tile.thread_rank()];
568	
569	        // (1) Key match. The matching lane broadcasts its value to the rest
570	        //     of the tile via shfl; each lane wraps the broadcast result in
571	        //     an optional and returns it (every lane returns the same one).
572	        const auto match_mask = tile.ballot(slot.key == key);
573	        if (match_mask != 0) {
574	            const int matching_lane = __ffs(match_mask) - 1;
575	            return cuda::std::optional<V>{tile.shfl(slot.value, matching_lane)};
576	        }
577	
578	        // (2) Empty slot. The key isn't in the table — Robin Hood would have
579	        //     placed it earlier in the probe sequence.
580	        if (tile.ballot(slot.key == empty_key) != 0) return cuda::std::nullopt;
581	
582	        // (3) Robin Hood early termination. If any resident is "richer"
583	        //     (closer to its home bucket than we are to ours), our key would
584	        //     have evicted it on insertion, so it can't be later in the
585	        //     probe sequence.
586	        const std::size_t resident_home =
587	            (hash_(slot.key) & capacity_mask_) / bucket_size;
588	        const std::size_t resident_probe =
589	            (bucket_idx - resident_home) & bucket_mask;
590	        if (tile.ballot(resident_probe < probe) != 0) return cuda::std::nullopt;
591	
592	        // No match, no empty slot, no richer resident — every slot here is
593	        // occupied by a resident at least as displaced as we would be.
594	        // Advance one bucket.
595	    }
596	
597	    // Either hit the probe cap, or wrapped fully around in a small table,
598	    // without finding the key, an empty slot, or a richer resident. The
599	    // insert invariant guarantees the key cannot be at probe distance
600	    // greater than max_probe_buckets, so this is a definite "not present".
601	    return cuda::std::nullopt;
602	}
603	
604	} // namespace gpurhh
605	
```

## Assistant — 2026-05-17T22:17:41.867Z

All four edits in, only spell-check noise. The wiring is complete on the library side:

- [`View::insert`](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh) declaration + definition take an optional `unsigned long long& probe_count` parameter under `#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS`. The probe loop increments it (lane 0 only) once per bucket-load — including CAS-retry re-reads, since those are real DRAM transactions.
- [`View::get`](include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh) symmetric.
- Comments on both declarations explain the per-tile-accumulator contract: caller passes a register variable, library increments via reference, caller flushes once to a per-tile global slot at end of kernel.

Default builds (tests, examples) see no API change — without the macro, the parameter doesn't exist. Benchmark binaries opt in by defining `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` before including `<gpurhh/hash_table.cuh>`.

Next step is `benchmark_insert.cu`. Want me to proceed?

```json
{
  "file_path": "[repo]/benchmarks/benchmark_insert.cu",
  "content": "// Insert benchmark for gpurhh.\n//\n// Measures insert throughput and effective DRAM bandwidth as a function\n// of input load factor F = n_ops / capacity. Workload:\n//   - Key type uint32_t, value type uint32_t, sum-based reduction.\n//   - Keys ~ Uniform(0, key_range) with key_range = capacity (α = 1).\n//   - Single ~1 GiB table, DRAM-resident.\n//\n// Per timed rep:\n//   1. Empty the table via cudaMemset(0xFF) (untimed).\n//   2. Zero the per-tile probe and failure counters (untimed).\n//   3. Time one kernel that processes n_ops insert ops in a grid-stride loop.\n//   4. Sum per-tile counters on host; write a CSV row.\n\n// Opt in to the gpurhh benchmark counter API and the internal data()\n// accessor (needed to memset the bucket array between reps without\n// destroying / reconstructing the table). Both macros must be defined\n// before any gpurhh header inclusion.\n#define GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS    1\n#define GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS 1\n\n#include \"benchmarks.cuh\"\n\n#include <cooperative_groups.h>\n#include <cuda_runtime.h>\n\n#include <cstddef>\n#include <cstdint>\n#include <cstdio>\n#include <cstdlib>\n#include <filesystem>\n#include <numeric>\n#include <sstream>\n#include <string>\n#include <vector>\n\nnamespace cg = cooperative_groups;\n\nusing Table = gpurhh::HashTable<\n    std::uint32_t,\n    std::uint32_t,\n    gpurhh::default_hash<std::uint32_t>,\n    gpurhh::default_empty_key<std::uint32_t>::key,\n    gpurhh::sum_op>;\nusing View = Table::View;\n\nnamespace {\n\nstruct Args {\n    std::size_t   capacity_bytes = std::size_t{1} << 30;  // 1 GiB\n    double        load_factor    = 1.0;                    // F = n_ops / capacity\n    int           block_size     = 256;\n    int           warmups        = 2;\n    int           reps           = 16;\n    std::uint32_t seed           = 42;\n    std::string   tag            = \"\";\n    std::filesystem::path output_dir;\n};\n\n[[noreturn]] void die_usage(const char* prog) {\n    std::fprintf(stderr,\n        \"usage: %s --output-dir DIR [options]\\n\"\n        \"  --capacity-bytes N  Target table size in bytes (default 1<<30; rounded up)\\n\"\n        \"  --load-factor F     n_ops as a fraction of capacity (default 1.0)\\n\"\n        \"  --block-size N      Threads per CUDA block; must be a multiple of tile_size (default 256)\\n\"\n        \"  --warmups N         Untimed warmup reps (default 2)\\n\"\n        \"  --reps N            Timed reps (default 16)\\n\"\n        \"  --seed N            PRNG seed for key generation (default 42)\\n\"\n        \"  --tag STR           Free-form label written to every CSV row\\n\"\n        \"  --output-dir DIR    Required. insert.csv is appended to here.\\n\",\n        prog);\n    std::exit(1);\n}\n\nArgs parse_args(int argc, char** argv) {\n    Args a;\n    for (int i = 1; i < argc; ++i) {\n        std::string flag = argv[i];\n        auto get_val = [&]() -> std::string {\n            if (i + 1 >= argc) {\n                std::fprintf(stderr, \"Missing value for %s\\n\", flag.c_str());\n                die_usage(argv[0]);\n            }\n            return argv[++i];\n        };\n        if      (flag == \"--capacity-bytes\") a.capacity_bytes = std::stoull(get_val());\n        else if (flag == \"--load-factor\")    a.load_factor    = std::stod(get_val());\n        else if (flag == \"--block-size\")     a.block_size     = std::stoi(get_val());\n        else if (flag == \"--warmups\")        a.warmups        = std::stoi(get_val());\n        else if (flag == \"--reps\")           a.reps           = std::stoi(get_val());\n        else if (flag == \"--seed\")           a.seed           = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(std::stoul(get_val()));\n        else if (flag == \"--tag\")            a.tag            = get_val();\n        else if (flag == \"--output-dir\")     a.output_dir     = get_val();\n        else { std::fprintf(stderr, \"Unknown flag: %s\\n\", flag.c_str()); die_usage(argv[0]); }\n    }\n    if (a.output_dir.empty())                        die_usage(argv[0]);\n    if (a.load_factor <= 0.0)                        { std::fprintf(stderr, \"--load-factor must be > 0\\n\"); std::exit(1); }\n    if (a.block_size % Table::tile_size != 0)        { std::fprintf(stderr, \"--block-size must be a multiple of tile_size (%d)\\n\", Table::tile_size); std::exit(1); }\n    if (a.block_size <= 0 || a.block_size > 1024)    { std::fprintf(stderr, \"--block-size out of range\\n\"); std::exit(1); }\n    return a;\n}\n\n__global__ void insert_kernel(\n    View view,\n    const std::uint32_t* __restrict__ keys,\n    std::size_t n_ops,\n    unsigned long long* __restrict__ per_tile_probes,\n    unsigned long long* __restrict__ per_tile_failures)\n{\n    auto block = cg::this_thread_block();\n    auto tile  = cg::tiled_partition<Table::tile_size>(block);\n\n    const std::size_t tiles_per_block = blockDim.x / Table::tile_size;\n    const std::size_t tile_id =\n        blockIdx.x * tiles_per_block + threadIdx.x / Table::tile_size;\n    const std::size_t total_tiles =\n        std::size_t{gridDim.x} * tiles_per_block;\n\n    unsigned long long my_probes   = 0;\n    unsigned long long my_failures = 0;\n\n    for (std::size_t op_id = tile_id; op_id < n_ops; op_id += total_tiles) {\n        const std::uint32_t k = keys[op_id];\n        // Derive value from key by hashing again. With sum_op, value content\n        // affects the sum's correctness but not throughput; we just need\n        // non-trivial bytes per op.\n        const std::uint32_t v = gpurhh::detail::fmix32(k);\n\n        const auto leftover = view.insert(tile, k, v, my_probes);\n        if (leftover.has_value() && tile.thread_rank() == 0) {\n            ++my_failures;\n        }\n    }\n\n    if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) {\n        per_tile_probes  [tile_id] = my_probes;\n        per_tile_failures[tile_id] = my_failures;\n    }\n}\n\nstd::string format_row(\n    std::size_t slot_bytes, std::size_t capacity, std::size_t capacity_bytes,\n    std::size_t key_range, double alpha, double load_factor,\n    int block_size, std::size_t n_ops, std::size_t bytes_per_op,\n    std::uint32_t seed, int rep, const std::string& tag,\n    float time_ms, double ops_per_sec, double gbps_floor, double gbps_corrected,\n    unsigned long long total_probes, unsigned long long total_failures)\n{\n    std::ostringstream s;\n    s.precision(9);\n    s << \"gpurhh,insert,\"\n      << slot_bytes      << \",\"\n      << capacity        << \",\"\n      << capacity_bytes  << \",\"\n      << key_range       << \",\"\n      << alpha           << \",\"\n      << load_factor     << \",\"\n      << block_size      << \",\"\n      << n_ops           << \",\"\n      << bytes_per_op    << \",\"\n      << seed            << \",\"\n      << rep             << \",\"\n      << tag             << \",\"\n      << time_ms         << \",\"\n      << ops_per_sec     << \",\"\n      << gbps_floor      << \",\"\n      << gbps_corrected  << \",\"\n      << total_probes    << \",\"\n      << total_failures;\n    return s.str();\n}\n\n} // namespace\n\nint main(int argc, char** argv) {\n    const Args args = parse_args(argc, argv);\n\n    // --- table sizing ---\n    constexpr std::size_t slot_bytes   = sizeof(Table::Slot);    // 8\n    constexpr std::size_t bytes_per_op = sizeof(Table::Bucket);  // 128\n    const std::size_t min_slots = args.capacity_bytes / slot_bytes;\n    Table table(min_slots);\n    const std::size_t capacity       = table.capacity();\n    const std::size_t capacity_bytes = capacity * slot_bytes;\n    // α = 1: key range matches capacity. Cast to uint32 — capacity must\n    // fit, since key type is uint32_t.\n    const std::uint32_t key_range = static_cast<std::uint32_t>(capacity);\n    const std::size_t   n_ops     =\n        static_cast<std::size_t>(args.load_factor * static_cast<double>(capacity));\n\n    if (n_ops == 0) {\n        std::fprintf(stderr, \"Computed n_ops = 0; raise --load-factor or --capacity-bytes\\n\");\n        std::exit(1);\n    }\n\n    std::fprintf(stderr,\n        \"[insert] capacity=%zu slots (%zu B), F=%.3f, n_ops=%zu, \"\n        \"block_size=%d, warmups=%d, reps=%d, seed=%u, tag=\\\"%s\\\"\\n\",\n        capacity, capacity_bytes, args.load_factor, n_ops,\n        args.block_size, args.warmups, args.reps, args.seed, args.tag.c_str());\n\n    // --- input buffer ---\n    std::uint32_t* d_keys = nullptr;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_keys, n_ops * sizeof(std::uint32_t)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    fill_uniform_keys(d_keys, n_ops, args.seed, key_range);\n\n    // --- launch shape ---\n    const int tiles_per_block = args.block_size / Table::tile_size;\n    int device  = 0;\n    int num_sms = 0;\n    cudaGetDevice(&device) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaDeviceGetAttribute(&num_sms, cudaDevAttrMultiProcessorCount, device) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    // 8 blocks per SM keeps every SM well-occupied without inflating the\n    // grid past the point of useful concurrency.\n    const int grid_size = num_sms * 8;\n    const std::size_t n_tiles =\n        std::size_t{grid_size} * static_cast<std::size_t>(tiles_per_block);\n\n    // --- per-tile counter arrays ---\n    unsigned long long* d_probes   = nullptr;\n    unsigned long long* d_failures = nullptr;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_probes,   n_tiles * sizeof(unsigned long long)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_failures, n_tiles * sizeof(unsigned long long)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    std::vector<unsigned long long> h_probes  (n_tiles);\n    std::vector<unsigned long long> h_failures(n_tiles);\n\n    CSVWriter csv(args.output_dir / \"insert.csv\",\n        \"library,workload,slot_bytes,capacity,capacity_bytes,\"\n        \"key_range,alpha,load_factor,block_size,n_ops,bytes_per_op,\"\n        \"seed,rep,tag,time_ms,ops_per_sec,gbps_floor,gbps_corrected,\"\n        \"total_probes,total_failures\");\n\n    EventTimer timer;\n\n    auto reset_table = [&]() {\n        const std::size_t num_buckets = capacity / Table::bucket_size;\n        cudaMemset(table.data(), 0xFF,\n                   num_buckets * sizeof(Table::Bucket)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    };\n    auto reset_counters = [&]() {\n        cudaMemset(d_probes,   0, n_tiles * sizeof(unsigned long long)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n        cudaMemset(d_failures, 0, n_tiles * sizeof(unsigned long long)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    };\n    auto launch = [&]() {\n        insert_kernel<<<grid_size, args.block_size>>>(\n            table.view(), d_keys, n_ops, d_probes, d_failures);\n        cudaGetLastError() >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    };\n\n    // --- warmups ---\n    for (int w = 0; w < args.warmups; ++w) {\n        reset_table();\n        reset_counters();\n        launch();\n    }\n    cudaDeviceSynchronize() >> CUDA_CHECK;\n\n    // --- timed reps ---\n    for (int r = 0; r < args.reps; ++r) {\n        reset_table();\n        reset_counters();\n        timer.begin();\n        launch();\n        const float ms = timer.end_ms();\n\n        cudaMemcpy(h_probes.data(),   d_probes,\n                   n_tiles * sizeof(unsigned long long),\n                   cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n        cudaMemcpy(h_failures.data(), d_failures,\n                   n_tiles * sizeof(unsigned long long),\n                   cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n        const unsigned long long total_probes =\n            std::accumulate(h_probes.begin(), h_probes.end(), 0ull);\n        const unsigned long long total_failures =\n            std::accumulate(h_failures.begin(), h_failures.end(), 0ull);\n\n        const double ops_per_sec =\n            static_cast<double>(n_ops) / (static_cast<double>(ms) * 1.0e-3);\n        const double gbps_floor =\n            static_cast<double>(n_ops) * static_cast<double>(bytes_per_op)\n                / (static_cast<double>(ms) * 1.0e6);\n        const double gbps_corrected =\n            static_cast<double>(total_probes) * static_cast<double>(bytes_per_op)\n                / (static_cast<double>(ms) * 1.0e6);\n\n        csv.write_row(format_row(\n            slot_bytes, capacity, capacity_bytes,\n            std::size_t{key_range}, /*alpha=*/1.0, args.load_factor,\n            args.block_size, n_ops, bytes_per_op,\n            args.seed, r, args.tag,\n            ms, ops_per_sec, gbps_floor, gbps_corrected,\n            total_probes, total_failures));\n    }\n\n    cudaFree(d_keys);\n    cudaFree(d_probes);\n    cudaFree(d_failures);\n    return 0;\n}\n"
}
```
