## User — 2026-05-18T02:12:47.855Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-487`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
1	# GPU Robin Hood Hash Table — Design Notes
2	
3	This document records the design and decisions behind `gpurhh`, a header-only CUDA library implementing a parallel Robin Hood hash table for NVIDIA GPUs.
4	It covers what the library does, why it looks the way it does, the public API it exposes, how the implementation is tested, and what is deliberately out of scope.
5	
6	## Goals
7	
8	- **Header-only CUDA library.**
9	  A single `.cuh` (or a small set) that users `#include` and template-instantiate for their key/value types.
10	  No separate compilation of the table itself.
11	- **Massively parallel insertion.**
12	  A batch of `N` keys is inserted by `N` (or more) GPU threads cooperatively.
13	  Worst-case behavior must remain bounded even under heavy contention.
14	- **Memory-bandwidth bound.**
15	  On large tables, the table is much bigger than the L2 cache, so performance is determined by how many useful bytes each DRAM transaction delivers.
16	  The aim is to come as close to peak HBM bandwidth as the access pattern allows.
17	
18	## Language and toolchain
19	
20	- **Target language: C++20.**
21	- **Target CUDA toolkit: CUDA 13.x.**
22	  Driven by two requirements: `-std=c++20` support in nvcc (CUDA 12.0+) and `cuda::std::bit_cast` in libcu++ (CUDA 13.0 was the first version where this combination worked cleanly on the test system).
23	
24	Within the implementation, we stick to C++17-shape idioms by default and only reach for C++20 features where they demonstrably improve the code.
25	The C++20 uses today are:
26	
27	- `std::bit_width` in `next_pow2` (host-side, compile-time).
28	- Host-side compile-time `std::bit_cast` for the `empty_key_byte` extraction and the memsettability `static_assert` lambda.
29	- Device-side `cuda::std::bit_cast` (libcu++) for the runtime bit-reinterpret in `default_hash`.
30	  The libcu++ version is mandatory here because `std::bit_cast` is host-only `constexpr` in libstdc++; calling it from a `__device__` function is a hard error in CUDA 13.x.
31	
32	Concepts on the `Hash` template parameter, `std::source_location` in place of `__FILE__` / `__LINE__` macros, ranges, and coroutines are all deferred until they pay for themselves.
33	
34	The C++20 baseline is conservative future-proofing: it lets us reach for the cleaner spelling when we want it without revisiting the build settings each time.
35	
36	## Why Robin Hood?
37	
38	Open-addressing with the invariant *"the entry in slot `i` whose probe distance is smaller gets evicted by an entry whose probe distance is larger"* gives:
39	
40	- A tight, low-variance distribution of probe distances → small worst-case lookup length even at load factors of 0.85–0.9.
41	- A natural early-exit rule for lookups (stop as soon as the slot's probe distance is smaller than the probe distance of the key we're searching for).
42	- Backward-shift deletion that avoids tombstones.
43	
44	These properties matter even more on a GPU than on a CPU because the warp's tail latency is set by the longest probe sequence among its 32 lanes.
45	
46	## Slot layout
47	
48	A slot is the unit of an atomic update.
49	The simplest design that admits lock-free CAS on a single machine word is:
50	
51	```
52	struct alignas(sizeof(Key) + sizeof(Value)) Slot {
53	    Key   key;       // typically uint32_t or uint64_t
54	    Value value;     // typically the same width as Key
55	};
56	```
57	
58	Empty slots use a reserved sentinel key (default: all-bits-set for unsigned integer Key); users are forbidden from inserting that key.
59	The packed slot supports two widths:
60	
61	1. **64-bit slots** (4-byte key + 4-byte value): atomic CAS via `cuda::atomic_ref<Slot>`, which on every NVIDIA arch compiles down to a 64-bit `atom.cas`. Fast.
62	2. **128-bit slots** (8-byte key + 8-byte value): atomic CAS still goes through `cuda::atomic_ref<Slot>`; on sm_90+ this is the single-instruction `atom.cas.b128`, on sm_70–sm_89 libcu++ emulates with a lock-table fallback. Functional everywhere, but the emulated path is significantly slower than 64-bit CAS under contention.
63	
64	We use the same control flow for both widths — the only thing that changes is which atomic instruction `cuda::atomic_ref::compare_exchange_strong` resolves to. A parallel-arrays layout (separate keys[] and values[]) is rejected: it halves the per-CAS bytes but at the cost of an awkward second-read-the-key step for finders that complicates correctness.
65	
66	**Probe distance.**
67	We do *not* store probe distance in the slot.
68	It is derivable as `(slot_index - hash(key)) mod capacity`.
69	Storing it would cost bits we'd rather give to the payload, and the derivation is a single subtract once we've read the slot.
70	
71	## Capacity, hash function, load factor
72	
73	- Capacity is a power of two so `mod` becomes a mask.
74	  Users specify a target load factor (default 0.7); we round capacity up to `next_pow2(N / load_factor)`.
75	- Hash function is a template parameter; default is a finalizer-style mixer (e.g. the splitmix64/Murmur3 finalizer) that is cheap on GPU and decorrelates structured keys well.
76	- Robin Hood tolerates higher load than linear probing, but on a GPU the warp tail-latency argument pushes us back toward 0.7–0.8 rather than 0.9.
77	
78	## Reduction operators
79	
80	When `View::insert` encounters an existing entry for the same key, the new value is combined with the existing one through a `Reduction` functor — a template parameter of `HashTable`, defaulting to `gpurhh::replace_op`.
81	Stateless and trivially copyable, the functor sits inside the `View` by value:
82	
83	```cpp
84	struct replace_op {
85	    template <class T>
86	    __device__ T operator()(T /*existing*/, T incoming) const noexcept { return incoming; }
87	};
88	
89	struct sum_op {
90	    template <class T>
91	    __device__ T operator()(T existing, T incoming) const noexcept { return existing + incoming; }
92	};
93	```
94	
95	`replace_op` gives last-writer-wins semantics. `sum_op` accumulates values for repeated-key inserts — useful as a parallel histogram or counter primitive. Users can define their own functor with the same signature.
96	
97	For commutative and associative reductions like `sum_op`, the final stored value is fully deterministic regardless of how the CAS retries interleave across tiles: the result is just the reduction of all inserted values for that key. For non-commutative reductions, the outcome depends on which writers win their CAS races.
98	
99	## Parallel insertion algorithm
100	
101	Each thread owns one `(key, value)` pair.
102	The thread walks the probe sequence and, at every slot, performs one of three actions:
103	
104	1. **Empty slot, CAS succeeds.** Done.
105	2. **Same key, CAS succeeds.** This is an update; done.
106	3. **Occupied by another key.**
107	   Compare probe distances.
108	   If the resident is "richer" (its probe distance from *its* home is smaller than ours from *our* home), we attempt to CAS our pair into the slot.
109	   On success, we adopt the displaced pair and continue probing from this slot.
110	   If the resident is "poorer" or equal, we move on.
111	
112	A CAS failure is treated as "the world changed under us": we re-read the slot and re-evaluate without advancing the probe index.
113	This is the standard lock-free retry.
114	
115	### Why this is correct under contention
116	
117	The Robin Hood invariant is maintained by every individual CAS because each CAS only swaps in a pair with probe distance strictly greater than the one already in the slot (or the slot was empty).
118	Two concurrent inserts targeting the same slot serialize through CAS, and whichever loses retries with the freshest state.
119	There is no global ordering requirement: as long as every write respects the local invariant, the final table is a valid Robin Hood layout for *some* permutation of the input.
120	
121	Caveat: the *value* portion of an update (case 2) needs care.
122	If two threads insert the same key concurrently and the user wants "last write wins" semantics, we need an additional CAS loop on the value, or we tolerate non-deterministic resolution.
123	
124	## Achieving bandwidth saturation
125	
126	The 64-bit slot is small relative to a 128-byte L2 cache line: one transaction brings 16 slots.
127	The bandwidth strategy follows from this:
128	
129	- **Warp-cooperative probing.**
130	  Within a warp, threads whose home slots land in the same cache line should service their probes from one load, not 32 loads.
131	  We have the warp ballot/shuffle primitives to do this cheaply.
132	- **Bucketed probing.**
133	  Treat the table as an array of *buckets* of 16 slots (one cache line).
134	  A probe inspects an entire bucket at a time — one coalesced 128-byte load — and uses warp intrinsics (`__ballot_sync`, `__match_any_sync`) to find the candidate slot.
135	  Insertion still uses CAS on a single 8-byte slot inside the bucket, but the read traffic per probe is exactly one cache line.
136	  This is the same idea as `bucketed_cuckoo` and Bucketized Cuckoo Hashing.
137	- **Probe length capping.**
138	  Exposed as a `MaxProbeBuckets` template parameter (default 8).
139	  An insert that would place a key past the cap fails (the caller is expected to rehash); a get that probes the cap without finding the key returns "not found" (correct because of the insert-side cap).
140	  With Robin Hood at load factor ~0.85 and `bucket_size` 16, the expected longest probe is well under the default cap; the cap protects against adversarial inputs and very high load factors.
141	  Crucially, a failing insert hands the leftover `(key, value)` back to the caller via the return value rather than dropping it on the floor.
142	  This matters because the leftover may not be the original input: if Robin Hood displacements happened during the probe, the originally-passed-in pair is already in the table and the leftover is the most-recently-evicted victim.
143	  Returning it is what makes the operation lossless under failure — callers can buffer leftovers and replay them into a rebuilt larger table.
144	- **Avoid divergence.**
145	  All threads in a warp execute the same probe loop.
146	  The "I'm done, others aren't" case is handled with masked operations rather than early-return-then-divergence.
147	
148	If we get this right, an insert of `N` keys at load factor 0.7 should cost approximately `(1/(1-α)) * sizeof(slot)` bytes per insert in expectation, which is the information-theoretic floor for an open-addressed table with this layout.
149	
150	## Work decomposition: how insertions map onto the GPU hierarchy
151	
152	Before discussing parameters, we need to commit to a unit of work — what GPU resource cooperates on a single key insertion.
153	There are four candidates:
154	
155	| Mapping              | Threads / key | Probe is one coalesced load? | Keys in flight / warp | Wasted lanes / probe |
156	| -------------------- | ------------- | ---------------------------- | --------------------- | -------------------- |
157	| Thread per key       | 1             | No — 32 unrelated lines      | 32                    | 0                    |
158	| **Sub-warp tile**    | **B**         | **Yes — exactly one line**   | **32 / B**            | **0 (if B ∣ 32)**    |
159	| Warp per key         | 32            | Yes, but ≤ B lanes used      | 1                     | 32 − B               |
160	| Block per key        | 128–1024      | Yes, but lanes mostly idle   | ≪ 1                   | huge                 |
161	
162	- **Thread-per-key** is the naive choice and exactly the case our bandwidth strategy is designed to avoid.
163	  Each warp issues 32 cache-line loads per probe step instead of one, and we lose any coalescing benefit.
164	  Fatal for our stated goal.
165	- **Block-per-key** is too coarse: there is no useful work for 128+ threads on a single key.
166	- **Warp-per-key** is the classic "warp-cooperative hashing" design but wastes lanes whenever the bucket is narrower than the warp.
167	  With 8-byte slots and a 128-byte line on NVIDIA, the bucket holds 16 slots, so half of every warp sits idle.
168	- **Sub-warp tile per key**, with tile width equal to the bucket width, is the one design where *one tile-probe = one cache-line transaction* with no idle lanes.
169	  This is the design we adopt.
170	
171	### The structure that follows
172	
173	1. A **bucket** is a contiguous, cache-line-aligned run of `B` slots.
174	2. A **cooperative tile** of `B` threads (`cooperative_groups::thread_block_tile<B>`) owns one in-flight key at a time.
175	   Each tile lane holds one slot of the current bucket in a register; one bucket probe is one coalesced load.
176	3. **Tiles per warp** = `WarpSize / B`.
177	   For NVIDIA with 8-byte slots that is 2.
178	4. **Block size** is purely an occupancy knob — some multiple of `WarpSize`, tuned empirically.
179	5. **Work assignment** is a tile-strided loop: tile index `t` (across the whole grid) handles input keys `t, t+T, t+2T, …` where `T` is the total number of resident tiles.
180	   Naturally load-balanced and robust to input skew.
181	
182	### Per-tile probe step
183	
184	On each probe:
185	
186	1. The `B` lanes cooperatively load one bucket — each lane reads one slot, fully coalesced.
187	2. `tile.ballot(...)` over a predicate identifies empty slots, key matches, or Robin-Hood-displaceable slots in a single warp intrinsic.
188	3. The lane corresponding to the chosen slot performs the CAS; the outcome is broadcast to the rest of the tile with `tile.shfl(...)`.
189	4. On displacement, the evicted `(key, value)` becomes the tile's new in-flight pair and the tile advances by one bucket.
190	
191	Every step is a warp-intrinsic operation on a single cache line, which is what makes the bandwidth argument hold.
192	
193	### Parameters: what is forced by the architecture, what is free
194	
195	Once we commit to the sub-warp tile design, almost every constant is *derived* from the hardware, not chosen:
196	
197	| Parameter          | Value (default)              | Source                                   |
198	| ------------------ | ---------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
199	| `SlotBytes`        | 8 (4+4) or 16 (8+8)          | Key/value packing choice                 |
200	| `CacheLineBytes`   | 128 (NVIDIA)                 | Hardware                                 |
201	| `BucketSize`       | `CacheLineBytes / SlotBytes` | **Derived**                              |
202	| `WarpSize`         | 32 (NVIDIA)                  | Hardware                                 |
203	| `TileSize`         | `BucketSize`                 | **Derived** (the central design choice)  |
204	| `TilesPerWarp`     | `WarpSize / BucketSize`      | **Derived**                              |
205	| `BlockSize`        | tuned (e.g. 128 or 256)      | Occupancy — empirical                    |
206	| `TargetLoadFactor` | tuned (e.g. 0.7)             | Robin Hood quality — empirical           |
207	| `MaxProbeBuckets`  | 8                            | Worst-case time vs. insert-failure rate  |
208	
209	So the only genuinely free knobs are `BlockSize`, `TargetLoadFactor`, and `MaxProbeBuckets`.
210	The rest should be expressed in the code as compile-time constants derived from `SlotBytes`, `CacheLineBytes`, and `WarpSize`, with the latter two being template parameters of the table (with sensible defaults) rather than hard-coded.
211	
212	### Why the abstraction matters — an AMD aside (out of scope for v1)
213	
214	AMD GPUs (CDNA, RDNA) have a 64-byte cache line and a 64-thread wavefront.
215	With 8-byte slots:
216	
217	- NVIDIA: `BucketSize` = 128 / 8 = 16, `TilesPerWarp` = 32 / 16 = **2**.
218	- AMD:    `BucketSize` =  64 / 8 =  8, `TilesPerWarp` = 64 /  8 = **8**.
219	
220	The same algorithm runs unchanged; only the derived constants differ.
221	We are not targeting AMD, but it is the reason we want `CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` to be parameters of the table type rather than hard-coded `128` and `32`.
222	Anything that ends up baked in as a literal in the implementation is a portability bug waiting to happen.
223	
224	## Lookup
225	
226	`View::get` follows the same probing rule as insert, read-only, returning `cuda::std::optional<Value>` — populated on hit, empty on miss. The probe terminates as soon as it sees one of:
227	
228	- A key match → return `{value}`.
229	- An empty slot → return `nullopt`. Robin Hood would have placed the key before this point.
230	- A resident "richer" than ours (smaller probe distance) → return `nullopt`. Our key would have evicted that resident if it were in the table, contradiction.
231	- The probe cap → return `nullopt`. The insert-side cap means the key cannot live further than this.
232	
233	Lookups are read-only and trivially parallel: concurrent lookups never race each other.
234	
235	**Concurrent get during insert is not linearizable.** A get running simultaneously with an insert can return `nullopt` for a key that is about to be inserted, or for a key in the middle of being displaced. Linearizability would require per-slot versioning. Most workloads sync between insert and get phases.
236	
237	## Deletion
238	
239	Not supported. The natural design is backward-shift deletion, but a correct lock-free implementation is significantly harder than insert/lookup, and the use case (this is an append-only table for most consumers) hasn't pushed us to do it.
240	
241	## Public API
242	
243	```cpp
244	namespace gpurhh {
245	
246	template <class Key,
247	          class Value,
248	          class Hash             = default_hash<Key>,
249	          Key   EmptyKey         = default_empty_key<Key>::key,
250	          class Reduction        = replace_op,
251	          int   CacheLineBytes   = 128,
252	          int   WarpSize         = 32,
253	          int   MaxProbeBuckets  = 8>
254	class HashTable {
255	public:
256	    // Host-side resource management. Non-copyable, movable.
257	    explicit HashTable(std::size_t min_capacity);
258	    ~HashTable();
259	    HashTable(HashTable&&) noexcept;
260	    HashTable& operator=(HashTable&&) noexcept;
261	
262	    // Device-side handle. Trivially copyable; pass by value into kernels.
263	    class View {
264	    public:
265	        template <class Tile>
266	        // Returns nullopt on success; on probe-cap failure returns the
267	        // leftover Slot that could not be placed (may be the original
268	        // pair, or a Robin Hood victim displaced before the failure).
269	        __device__ cuda::std::optional<Slot>
270	        insert(const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value);
271	
272	        template <class Tile>
273	        __device__ cuda::std::optional<Value> get(const Tile& tile, Key key) const;
274	    };
275	
276	    View view() const noexcept;
277	
278	    // Actual capacity, in slots, rounded up to the next power of two and
279	    // never less than `bucket_size`.
280	    std::size_t capacity() const noexcept;
281	
282	#ifdef GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS
283	    // Direct pointer access to the device-resident bucket array. Gated
284	    // behind a macro so users must opt in explicitly; the test suite does.
285	    Bucket*       data() noexcept;
286	    const Bucket* data() const noexcept;
287	#endif
288	};
289	
290	// Diagnostic helper. Lives in <gpurhh/print.cuh>, a separate header so
291	// the core library stays independent of <cstdio> and <vector>. Relies
292	// on HashTable::data(), so the including translation unit must define
293	// GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS first; <gpurhh/print.cuh> #errors
294	// otherwise.
295	template <class Table>
296	void print_slots(const Table& table, std::size_t start, std::size_t stop);
297	
298	}  // namespace gpurhh
299	```
300	
301	The `View` is the centerpiece for header-only use: a user kernel that already has its keys in registers calls `view.insert(tile, ...)` or `view.get(tile, ...)` directly.
302	Bulk host-side operations are deliberately *not* part of the table — callers manage their device buffers and write their own driver kernels.
303	The tests in `tests/kernels.cuh` and `tests/isolated.cuh` show the canonical pattern; `examples/basic.cu` is a minimal standalone walkthrough.
304	
305	## Testing
306	
307	The test suite lives in `tests/`, with each `test_*.cu` compiled into its own executable.
308	
309	- **`test_construction.cu`** — construct / destruct, move semantics, edge sizes (capacity == bucket_size, capacity below bucket_size), moved-from state behavior.
310	- **`test_combined.cu`** — end-to-end insert + get round-trips through the public API.
311	- **`test_get.cu`** — `View::get` in isolation. Pre-states are hand-built with `IdentityHash` so the probe sequence and home buckets are deterministic; the test then verifies `get`'s match / empty / richer-resident / wrap-around behavior.
312	- **`test_insert.cu`** — `View::insert` in isolation. Some tests use hand-computed expected layouts; all run a Robin Hood invariant scanner over the final state as a safety net. Covers the probe-cap failure path and concurrent same-key inserts.
313	- **`test_concurrency.cu`** — heavy parallel contention: adversarial displacement chains, many concurrent gets, mixed new / duplicate inserts, parallel saturation of the probe cap.
314	- **`test_randomized.cu`** — randomized stress. Uniform-key inserts at small (4 KB table) and gigabyte (1 GB table) scales; a sum-reduction stress test with random keys and deliberate duplicates whose expected per-key sum is computed on the host.
315	
316	Three shared infrastructure headers:
317	
318	- **`tests/tests.cuh`** — the `CUDA_CHECK` postfix error-check operator (`expr >> CUDA_CHECK`) and the `GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS` macro, which gates `HashTable::data()`.
319	- **`tests/kernels.cuh`** — the `Table` alias (with `default_hash`), bulk insert/get host helpers, and end-to-end driver kernels. Used by the "combined" and construction tests.
320	- **`tests/isolated.cuh`** — the `TestTable` alias (with `IdentityHash` for predictable home buckets), templated `do_insert` / `do_get` / `do_insert_with_outcomes`, the `assert_robin_hood_invariant` scanner, and `set_state` / `read_state` for direct table-memory manipulation via the `data()` back-door.
321	
322	The `IdentityHash` is the enabling choice for the isolated tests: with `hash(K) = K`, the home bucket of a key is fully predictable from its value, so we can construct any valid Robin Hood state with one `cudaMemcpy` and hand-predict the outcome of small insert sequences. The randomized tests apply a bit-mixing function (the same `fmix32` finalizer used by `default_hash`) as a permutation on sequential integers — this gives distinct random-looking keys without the host-side dedup overhead that an `unordered_set` would require, which is what makes the gigabyte-scale tests fit in memory.
323	
324	The test suite runs about 34 tests across ~1100 lines of code, against ~350 lines of library code. The high ratio is deliberate: this is a concurrent data structure, and the failure modes for concurrent CAS retries, displacement chains, and probe-cap interactions are subtle enough that we put significant effort into checking each path.
325	
326	## Known limitations
327	
328	- **Deletion** is not supported. Users that need it allocate a fresh table and re-insert.
329	- **Resize** is not supported. Users that need growth allocate a bigger table and re-insert.
330	- **Multi-GPU** is not supported.
331	- **Concurrent get during insert is not linearizable.** A get running simultaneously with insert can return `nullopt` for a key in flight. Workloads should synchronize between insert and get phases.
332	- **128-bit slot performance on pre-Hopper hardware** (sm_70–sm_89) is bottlenecked by libcu++'s lock-table emulation of 128-bit CAS. Functionally correct, but considerably slower than the native single-instruction path that sm_90+ has.
333	
334	## Out of scope
335	
336	- **Non-NVIDIA backends.** The abstractions (`CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` as table-level template parameters) don't preclude AMD support, but we haven't written or tested it. The same algorithm with AMD's 64-byte cache line and 64-thread wavefront would give `BucketSize = 8` and `TilesPerWarp = 8`.
337	- **Stream support.** All operations currently run on the default stream. Adding a `cudaStream_t` parameter to host-side methods is a small change but hasn't been made.
338	
339	## Future work
340	
341	- Validate the 128-bit slot path on a Hopper (sm_90+) system; benchmark it against the 64-bit path.
342	- Benchmark against `cuCollections` and `WarpCore` on the same workloads to validate the bandwidth-bound claim.
343	- Add a CPU reference Robin Hood for randomized differential testing if test signal demands it (currently considered low priority — the invariant scanner plus the sum-reduction equivalence check cover most of what differential testing would add).
344	- Implement deletion (backward-shift, lock-free) if a use case appears.
345	
```

</details>

Non-copyable, movable.\n    explicit HashTable(std::size_t min_capacity);\n    ~HashTable();\n    HashTable(HashTable&&) noexcept;\n    HashTable& operator=(HashTable&&) noexcept;\n\n    // Empty the table — every slot reset to (empty_key, _). Both must be defined in the translation unit *before* any `<gpurhh/...>` header is included:\n\n- **`GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS`** exposes `HashTable::data()`, giving direct pointer access to the device-resident bucket array. Used by `<gpurhh/print.cuh>` (which `#error`s if the macro is missing) and by the test suite's `set_state` / `read_state` helpers, which need to seed and inspect raw table layouts via `cudaMemcpy` to test corner cases in isolation.\n- **`GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS`** adds a trailing `std::uint32_t& tile_counter` parameter to `View::insert` and `View::get`. Lane 0 of the tile increments it on every bucket-load (a CAS-retry re-read counts too — it's a real DRAM transaction). The caller maintains a per-tile register accumulator and writes it out once at end of kernel; no atomics are involved in the probing hot loop. The benchmark binaries enable this; tests and the example do not."
}

## Assistant — 2026-05-18T02:14:41.421Z

**→ Tool call: `Edit`** (id `tool-491`)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/notes/design.md",
  "old_string": "The test suite runs about 34 tests across ~1100 lines of code, against ~350 lines of library code. The high ratio is deliberate: this is a concurrent data structure, and the failure modes for concurrent CAS retries, displacement chains, and probe-cap interactions are subtle enough that we put significant effort into checking each path.\n\n## Known limitations",
  "new_string": "The test suite runs about 35 tests across ~1100 lines of code, against ~350 lines of library code. The high ratio is deliberate: this is a concurrent data structure, and the failure modes for concurrent CAS retries, displacement chains, and probe-cap interactions are subtle enough that we put significant effort into checking each path.\n\n## Benchmarking\n\nThroughput and bandwidth measurements live in `benchmarks/`, with the same one-binary-per-workload structure as the tests.\nThree executables:\n\n- **`benchmark_memcpy.cu`** — peak-bandwidth reference. Times `cudaMemcpyAsync(... D2D)` and a trivial `uint4`-vectorized copy kernel on a buffer of configurable size. Reports effective GB/s assuming two bytes of DRAM traffic per byte of payload (one read + one write). Not a workload we care about; just the achievable-peak number to normalize the hash-table results against.\n- **`benchmark_insert.cu`** — inserts `n_ops` keys into a freshly-cleared table per timed rep. Uses `sum_op` reduction, so repeated-key inserts accumulate instead of failing.\n- **`benchmark_get.cu`** — pre-fills the table once (untimed) with one stream of `Uniform(0, key_range)` keys, then times lookups against an independent stream drawn from the same distribution. The independence gives a natural hit rate ≈ effective occupancy without an explicit hit-rate knob.\n\n### Workload knobs\n\n- **`capacity`** is held at 1 GiB by default — well past the L2 cache on any sm_89 device, putting the kernel firmly in the DRAM-bound regime.\n- **`α = key_range / capacity`** is fixed at 1: keys are drawn from `Uniform(0, capacity)`, which gives the cleanest relationship between input load factor F and expected occupancy.\n- **`F = n_ops / capacity`** is the swept axis. The sweep grid is `F ∈ {0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0}`. At low F the table is mostly empty and Robin Hood barely engages; the interesting cliff is in F = 2.0–3.0 where occupancy approaches 0.9+ and probe distances grow.\n- **`block_size ∈ {64, 128, 256, 512, 1024}`** is swept to spot-check that launch shape doesn't dominate (it typically doesn't for bandwidth-bound kernels, but worth verifying once per workload).\n\n### Per-tile probe counters\n\nThe `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` macro adds a probe counter parameter to `View::insert` / `View::get`. The benchmark kernels accumulate per-thread register counters across a grid-stride loop and flush one value per tile to a global array at end of kernel; no atomics fire in the probe hot loop. The host sums per-tile values into a grand total per rep.\n\nThis gives the benchmark *two* bandwidth numbers:\n\n- **`gbps_floor`** — `n_ops × sizeof(Bucket) / time`. Assumes exactly one bucket read per op. Lower bound on actual DRAM traffic.\n- **`gbps_corrected`** — `total_probes × sizeof(Bucket) / time`. Uses the measured probe count. Accurate to within the fraction of writes the floor estimate ignores (~3–6%; documented in the column header).\n\nAt low F the two converge (avg probe length ≈ 1). At high F the corrected number diverges upward — that gap is itself a useful signal about how hard Robin Hood is working.\n\n### Sweep driver\n\n`scripts/benchmark.sh` runs the full grid (50 invocations for the gpurhh workloads + 1 memcpy reference) and writes one CSV per workload (`memcpy.csv`, `insert.csv`, `get.csv`) plus a `run_info.txt` sidecar with `nvcc --version`, `uname`, and `nvidia-smi`'s GPU name / driver version / memory size / power limit. Default output dir is `output/` under the repo root; can be overridden by passing a path as the first argument.\n\n`make benchmark` builds the binaries at `-O3` (the default for the benchmark pattern rule; see Makefile) and then invokes the sweep script. Numbers from unoptimized builds are meaningless, so the `-O3` default is non-overridable in practice.\n\n## Known limitations"
}
```

</details>

  "new_string": "- **Non-NVIDIA backends.** The abstractions (`CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` as table-level template parameters) don't preclude AMD support, but we haven't written or tested it. The same algorithm with AMD's 64-byte cache line and 64-thread wavefront would give `BucketSize = 8` and `TilesPerWarp = 8`.\n- **Full stream support.** `clear()` takes a `cudaStream_t` (defaulting to `gpurhh::default_stream`); the constructor doesn't yet. Threading a stream through the constructor is a small change but not strictly needed — its `cudaMemset`-based init is one-time, and blocking the host once at construction is fine for any workload we've encountered."
}
```

## Assistant — 2026-05-18T02:17:03.082Z

**→ Tool call: `Edit`** (id `tool-494`)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh",
  "old_string": "template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::HashTable(std::size_t min_capacity) {\n    // Round up to the next power of two, but never below a single bucket —\n    // the bucketed probing logic requires at least bucket_size slots.\n    const std::size_t rounded = detail::next_pow2(min_capacity);\n    capacity_ = rounded < bucket_size ? std::size_t{bucket_size} : rounded;\n\n    const std::size_t num_buckets = capacity_ / bucket_size;\n\n    // Allocate the bucket array on the current device. cudaMalloc leaves\n    // buckets_ as nullptr on failure; callers can check via\n    // cudaPeekAtLastError().\n    cudaMalloc(&buckets_, num_buckets * sizeof(Bucket));\n\n    // Initialize every byte to empty_key_byte. For the supported default\n    // (unsigned EmptyKey = 0xFF..FF) this writes the empty sentinel into\n    // each slot's key field in a single bandwidth-bound DMA.\n    cudaMemset(buckets_, empty_key_byte, num_buckets * sizeof(Bucket));\n}",
  "new_string": "template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::HashTable(\n    std::size_t min_capacity, cudaStream_t stream)\n{\n    // Round up to the next power of two, but never below a single bucket —\n    // the bucketed probing logic requires at least bucket_size slots.\n    const std::size_t rounded = detail::next_pow2(min_capacity);\n    capacity_ = rounded < bucket_size ? std::size_t{bucket_size} : rounded;\n\n    const std::size_t num_buckets = capacity_ / bucket_size;\n\n    // Allocate the bucket array on the current device. cudaMalloc leaves\n    // buckets_ as nullptr on failure; callers can check via\n    // cudaPeekAtLastError().\n    cudaMalloc(&buckets_, num_buckets * sizeof(Bucket));\n\n    // Initialize every byte to empty_key_byte. For the supported default\n    // (unsigned EmptyKey = 0xFF..FF) this writes the empty sentinel into\n    // each slot's key field in a single bandwidth-bound DMA, queued on\n    // the given stream.\n    cudaMemsetAsync(buckets_, empty_key_byte,\n                    num_buckets * sizeof(Bucket), stream);\n}"
}
```

</details>

## Assistant — 2026-05-18T02:18:55.645Z

Done. Script now `exec`s stdout + stderr through `tee -a` into `${OUTPUT_DIR}/benchmark.log` right after the output dir is created. Everything printed from that point on — the script's own status lines, the binaries' CSV rows (since they go to stdout), and any diagnostic stderr from the binaries — ends up in both the terminal and the log file. `-a` mode means reruns into the same `output/` dir append rather than clobber, matching the CSV writers' behavior.

So the changes in this round:
- Constructor stream support reverted (`HashTable(std::size_t)` only); design doc rewords "Stream support" to explain *why* the constructor stays sync.
- `scripts/benchmark.sh` mirrors all output to `benchmark.log` in the output dir.

## Assistant — 2026-05-18T02:22:31.223Z

Found it. Last line of the log:

```
[get] pre-fill recorded 391582 insert failures at F=3.000. The get-side hit rate
would not reflect the intended workload; lower --load-factor and re-run.
```

So the F=3.0 get sweep aborts because the pre-fill (which is the same insert workload internally) hits the probe cap on ~391K of 402M inserts (~0.1% failure rate). This matches the F=3 *insert* benchmark which also recorded ~393K failures per rep — the cap-8 probe budget genuinely isn't quite enough for ~95% occupancy with uniform-random keys, as we flagged this as a risk earlier.

Two interesting observations from the partial run:
- F ≤ 2.0 worked all the way through. Insert is hitting **~365 GB/s gbps_corrected**, get is hitting **~720 GB/s gbps_corrected** — getting close to the 4090's DRAM peak. The numbers look promising.
- The insert benchmark at F=3.0 still completed (it records failures and continues); only the get benchmark aborts because it can't honestly report a "natural hit rate" if some inserts didn't actually land.

The `[get]` abort is conservative — I wrote it to exit-1 on any pre-fill failure. 0.1% failure rate doesn't actually invalidate the get benchmark much; the `hits` and `misses` CSV columns are the empirical truth either way.

**Two ways to unblock:**

1. **Relax the abort to a warning.** Remove the `std::exit(1)` from `benchmark_get.cu`'s pre-fill path. Print the failure count to stderr, record it in the CSV column we already have (`prefill_failures`), and proceed. The user reads the CSV and decides whether the F=3 get number is trustworthy for them.
2. **Cap the sweep grid at F=2.5.** Edit `LOAD_FACTORS` in `scripts/benchmark.sh`. Cleanly avoids the partial-fill problem but loses the most interesting data point.

I'd recommend (1) — the failure rate is small and the data is honest as long as we record it. Want me to apply that?

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-18T02:23:38.636Z

Yes, exactly that. The mechanism:

- F=3.0 means we attempt `3 × capacity` inserts. With α=1 (key range = capacity), keys are drawn uniformly from `[0, capacity)`, so many inserts target keys already present and are merged in place via `sum_op` — those *aren't* the failures. They're free.
- The keys that *don't* collide land at effective occupancy ≈ 95% (the 1 − e^(−F) formula). At that density, Robin Hood displacement chains can occasionally exceed the 8-bucket probe cap (≈ 128 slot positions). When a chain hits the cap with an in-flight pair still unplaced, the insert returns the leftover instead of placing it — that's the "failure."
- The insert benchmark records the count and continues (the leftovers would in practice be buffered and re-inserted into a rebuilt larger table). The get benchmark's pre-fill aborts because its hit-rate accounting was set up assuming every input key actually landed in the table.

At F=3 we're seeing ~391K failures out of 402M attempts, or about 0.1%. Real but small. The 0.1% just means the table is ~94.9% full instead of the theoretical 95.0%.

So the exit-1 was an over-conservative guard I wrote. The empirical `hits` / `misses` columns in `get.csv` are accurate regardless — they reflect the *actual* table state, whatever it is. The `prefill_failures` column tells you how much that state deviated from the intended one, so the user has full visibility.

Want me to switch the abort to a warning so F=3 get data lands too?

---

## User — 2026-05-18T02:26:39.302Z

I see. If I understand right, this means the hash table is actually still valid, just some elements didn't insert even though we asked, but we track that via the returned option. In some applications where lossy storage is fine - think inside a robust-enough machine learning algorithm or the like - that could actually be used to increase performance by dropping those elements.

Let's not error when this happens - instead, just record how much it happens, as this will help us get a sense of how the table works

## Assistant — 2026-05-18T02:33:41.144Z

Excellent — short version: **the get path is essentially bandwidth-saturated**, the insert path is doing fine but not bandwidth-bound. Numbers:

## Ceiling for this run

Power-limited to 350 W (vs the 4090's stock 450 W), so memory clocks are throttled. The practical ceiling is what memcpy reports:

- `memcpy_kernel`: **~806 GB/s**
- `memcpy_d2d`: ~795 GB/s
- 4090's nameplate peak is 1008 GB/s; you're achieving ~80% of that under the power cap. Everything below should be normalized against the 806 number, not the 1008.

## Get: very close to peak

| F   | best `gbps_corrected` (at block=1024) | % of memcpy peak |
|-----|---------------------------------------|------------------|
| 0.5 | 715                                   | 89%              |
| 1.0 | 714                                   | 89%              |
| 1.5 | 715                                   | 89%              |
| 2.0 | 721                                   | 89%              |
| 3.0 | **772**                               | **96%**          |

At F=3 the bigger probe count amortizes over more useful work, so the corrected bandwidth actually goes *up* even though raw ops/sec drops. We're effectively saturating DRAM. **This is the bandwidth-bound claim validated** — the design goal hit on the workload it was designed for.

## Insert: ~45% of peak, write-bound

| F   | best `gbps_corrected` (at block=512–1024) | % of memcpy peak |
|-----|-------------------------------------------|------------------|
| 0.5 | 358                                       | 44%              |
| 1.0 | 359                                       | 45%              |
| 1.5 | 362                                       | 45%              |
| 2.0 | 365                                       | 45%              |
| 3.0 | 387                                       | 48%              |

Insert is half the get throughput because every probe does a read *and* a successful op does a CAS write, and the L2 has to keep the cache line coherent under concurrent CAS contention from other tiles hitting the same buckets. The floor metric is undercounting writes here, so the real DRAM traffic is higher than 365 GB/s, but the kernel is not bandwidth-bound — it's atomic-bound.

## Other observations

- **Block size matters more than I'd expected.** Going from 64 to 1024 buys ~10–20% on both workloads. The block_size=64 column being noticeably slow is real, not noise.
- **The gbps_floor vs gbps_corrected gap is exactly the probe-count signal we built it for.** At F=3 they diverge ~2× — Robin Hood is doing real displacement work — and yet *corrected* bandwidth keeps rising. Graceful degradation in action.
- **F=3 prefill failures**: ~391K of 402M (~0.1%). That's the cost of insisting on the cap-8 probe budget at ~95% occupancy; raising `MaxProbeBuckets` to 16 would likely drive it to zero if you wanted lossless behavior.

A first-cut summary I'd write up: *get hits 96% of peak memory bandwidth at high load; insert is atomic-bound at ~45% of peak; Robin Hood probe-cap failures stay below 0.1% even at F=3 with the default 8-bucket cap*.

## Assistant — 2026-05-18T02:43:34.951Z

Both updates applied.

## Baselines

The two natural targets:

**[cuCollections](https://github.com/NVIDIA/cuCollections)** — NVIDIA's first-party header-only library. Same design philosophy as gpurhh: bucketed open-addressing, cooperative-tile probing, header-only template instantiation, `View`-style device-side handle. The most apples-to-apples comparison — if we hit comparable bandwidth, the algorithmic design is validated; if we trail, there's an implementation gap to investigate. Relevant types are `cuco::static_map` (insert + lookup, no resize) and `cuco::static_set` (lookup-only, faster, useful for the get benchmark). Used in production by RAPIDS cuDF for groupby/join, so the perf bar is high.

**[WarpCore](https://github.com/sleeepyjack/warpcore)** — academic implementation from Daniel Jünger (IPDPS 2020 paper "WarpCore: A Library for fast Hash Tables on GPUs"). Header-only, several hash-table variants (single-value, bucket-list, etc.). Slightly different design choices than gpurhh (warp-cooperative rather than sub-warp-tile in some variants). Less actively maintained than cuCollections; would be a stretch comparison rather than a daily target.

There's no first-party CUDA-toolkit hash table — `cub::DeviceReduce` does parallel reductions but nothing map-like, and `thrust` likewise has no hash table.

**Recommendation:** start with cuCollections's `static_map` (matching our insert + lookup API) and `static_set` (a tighter lookup target). Two paths to integrate:

1. **Git submodule.** Pinned commit/release tag, header path added to the bench binaries' `-I` flags. Most conventional.
2. **Tarball + `notes/cucollections-version.txt`.** Avoids submodule lifecycle for a benchmark dependency that doesn't ship with the library. Lighter touch.

Either way, the comparison would add a fourth benchmark binary `benchmark_cuco_insert.cu` / `benchmark_cuco_get.cu` (probably best as one combined binary since cuCollections has both APIs side-by-side), emitting rows with `library=cuco` into the same CSV schema. Then the plotting layer just colors by library.

A reasonable next-session order: vendor cuCollections via submodule, write the wrapper binary, sweep the same `(F, block_size)` grid, plot side-by-side with the gpurhh numbers. Should be a half-day of work once the submodule lands.

Good benchmarks today. Enjoy the rest of your night.

## Assistant — 2026-05-18T02:44:48.638Z

## Assistant — 2026-05-18T02:45:04.392Z

Good question to nail down — neither of them is cuckoo, despite cuckoo being the famous "GPU hash table" technique historically.

## cuCollections

**Open addressing with cooperative-tile probing**, very similar in shape to gpurhh. Specifically:

- Slots packed as `cuco::pair<Key, Value>`, grouped into buckets (configurable bucket size — typical default is matching the CG tile width).
- Probing strategy is a template parameter (`Probing`): the two stock options are **linear probing** and **double hashing**. Robin Hood isn't one of the choices — that's the main algorithmic difference from gpurhh.
- Cooperative-tile probing via `cuda::cooperative_groups`. A `cuco::static_map::ref_type` is the View-equivalent; tile-aware insert/find methods.
- No probe-cap mechanism. They handle growth with `cuco::dynamic_map`, a separate type that maintains a list of static_maps with rehashing on overflow.
- Variants: `static_set`, `static_map`, `static_multimap`, `dynamic_map`. The set is the lowest-overhead lookup target; the map is the apples-to-apples comparison for us.

So the design family is identical to gpurhh — bucketed open-addressing with sub-warp tiles — and the comparison really comes down to **Robin Hood vs. linear/double-hashing probing**, plus our `MaxProbeBuckets` cap vs. their resize-based growth.

## WarpCore

Also open-addressing, also cooperative-group probing. Several specialized variants (the original paper enumerates them):

- `SingleValueHashTable` — basic map.
- `BucketListHashTable` — multimap via per-key linked list of buckets.
- `MultiValueHashTable` — multiple values per key in fixed-size slot arrays.
- `HashSet`, `CountingHashTable` — set and counter specializations.

Probing is open-addressing with linear/quadratic schemes; some variants use auxiliary structures (the BucketList one). Not cuckoo, not Robin Hood.

## Where cuckoo actually lives

Cuckoo on GPU is a real technique — the foundational paper is Alcantara et al. *"Real-Time Parallel Hashing on the GPU"* (2009), and there's a cuckoo implementation in the older **cuDPP** library. But it's mostly historical at this point. The modern consensus among GPU hash table libraries has settled on **open-addressing with cooperative-tile probing**, because cuckoo's eviction chains scale badly under contention and require careful handling of insertion failures (often by rebuilding with new hash functions — analogous to but messier than our probe-cap rebuild).

So:
- **cuCollections**: open-addressing, linear or double-hashing probe.
- **WarpCore**: open-addressing, linear or quadratic probe, with specialized variants.
- **gpurhh**: open-addressing, **Robin Hood** probe.

The three are direct siblings; the comparison would isolate the value of Robin Hood vs. simpler probe schemes on the same access pattern.

(Caveat: my recall of these libraries' specifics may be a few months stale — worth a quick verification against their current READMEs before vendoring.)

---

## User — 2026-05-18T02:46:42.543Z

Based on multi-threaded hashing experience, which would you estimate would be faster in principle?

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-18T02:47:19.598Z

<em>Thinking…</em>

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-18T02:47:35.894Z

Honest answer: it depends on load factor and workload mix, and I'd guess the crossover is around LF ≈ 0.8 with Robin Hood winning above and linear winning below.

## Reasoning

The three schemes have different cost profiles per probe step on a GPU:

**Lookup**

- **Linear / double hashing**: walk until empty slot or match.
- **Robin Hood**: walk until empty slot, match, *or* richer resident. The extra termination condition is the win — at LF 0.85+, linear probing keeps reading past the point where RH would already be home. On miss, RH usually exits in 1–2 probes; linear can probe many slots at high LF.

**Insert**

- **Linear**: find empty slot, one CAS, done. Cheap.
- **Robin Hood**: find empty / matching / displaceable slot, CAS, possibly adopt evicted pair, continue. Per-insert work scales with displacement chain length. Each chain step is an extra CAS.
- **Double hashing**: like linear, but with worse memory access pattern.

So Robin Hood's get path is structurally faster (more ways to terminate early), but its insert path does more work per insert (the displacement chain is its cost).

**Access pattern (GPU-specific)**

- Linear and Robin Hood probe sequential buckets → DRAM prefetcher / streaming is happy.
- Double hashing jumps around per probe → no prefetch benefit, scattered cache line fetches.

This is a meaningful GPU-only concern; on a CPU with random-access L1, double hashing's cache pattern doesn't hurt as much. On GPU at DRAM-bound table sizes, I'd guess **double hashing is the slowest of the three** under our access pattern.

**Variance / tail behavior**

- Linear: high variance — clustering causes long probe runs.
- Robin Hood: low variance — that's the whole point of the invariant.
- Double hashing: low variance via better distribution.

For warp-cooperative probing where the tile waits for the slowest lane, lower variance is more valuable than for CPU hashing where threads are independent. Robin Hood's variance advantage probably matters more on GPU than on CPU.

## My specific guess for our benchmark

For the workload we just measured (LF 0.5–3, uniform-random keys on a DRAM-bound table):

- **Get throughput**: gpurhh ≥ cuCollections linear probe. The gap should widen at high LF. cuCollections double-hashing might trail both.
- **Insert throughput at LF ≤ 1.5**: cuCollections linear probe ≥ gpurhh. Linear's "find empty + one CAS" wins when the table is sparse enough that there's no displacement work for RH to do.
- **Insert throughput at LF ≥ 2**: gpurhh starts to catch up or overtake. RH's tighter probe distribution starts to pay off when linear is hitting clustering.

I don't think any of the three is asymptotically faster than the others on **bandwidth**, because they're all bucketed cooperative-tile open-addressing — the BW ceiling is the same (one cache line per probe). The difference is in probe count, and how that probe count distributes across the workload.

The honest meta-point: this is exactly the kind of thing that *needs* the benchmark comparison rather than the prior. My estimates above are educated guesses, not predictions I'd bet anything on.

## Assistant — 2026-05-18T17:38:35.740Z

Welcome back. All three of your suggestions are reasonable; let me lay out a concrete plan and ask for sign-off before touching files.

## Repo structure

```
benchmarks/
  benchmarks.cuh                 shared infra
  benchmark_memcpy.cu            reference
  benchmark_insert.cu            gpurhh
  benchmark_get.cu               gpurhh
  baselines/
    cuco/
      benchmark_insert.cu        cuCollections
      benchmark_get.cu
    warpcore/                    later, optional
      benchmark_insert.cu
      benchmark_get.cu
external/
  cuCollections/                 git submodule, gitignored if absent
```

Subdirectory under `baselines/` per library, since each library has its own setup quirks and may need its own thin helper header. Source for each baseline lives next to where it's exclusively used.

## Optional dependency

Two checks needed:

- **Build-time**: Makefile detects whether `external/cuCollections/include/cuco/` exists. If yes, baseline binaries get added to `BENCHMARK_BINS`; if no, they're silently omitted. No error, no warning — `make benchmarks` Just Works whether cuCollections is vendored or not.
- **Run-time**: `scripts/benchmark.sh` checks whether each baseline binary exists in `build/benchmarks/`; only runs the ones that built. Same pattern as the binary-existence check we already have for the gpurhh binaries.

That gives a clean cliff: vendoring cuCollections (one `git submodule add`) enables the baselines, and removing it leaves a working gpurhh-only benchmark.

## Refactor — yes, but a light one

Right answer is: **share the parts that are genuinely common, leave the parts that are library-specific alone**. Heavy abstractions (CRTP, virtual interfaces, "workload concept") would be over-engineered for 2–4 libraries; they're tempting to write and harder to debug than they save.

What's actually duplicated across the current `benchmark_insert.cu` and `benchmark_get.cu`, and what cuCollections would duplicate again:

- **`Args` struct + `parse_args` + `die_usage`** (~70 lines, near-identical).
- **`format_row` mechanics** (~30 lines): the CSV columns differ per workload, but the formatting pattern is the same.
- **Warmup + timed-rep loop** (~30 lines): identical shape, only the `launch()` lambda body differs.
- **Key generation kernel launch** (~10 lines).

What stays in each binary:

- The library's `Table` / `View` / `ref_type` setup, including library-specific reduction operator choice.
- The kernel definition (per-tile probe loop is library-specific).
- The reset-between-reps logic (`table.clear()` for gpurhh, possibly something different for cuco).
- Per-workload counter readback (cuCollections doesn't expose probe counters; that column becomes empty/NA for baseline rows).

Proposed shape:

```cpp
// benchmarks.cuh adds:

struct CommonArgs {                       // shared subset of all binaries' Args
    std::size_t   capacity_bytes;
    double        load_factor;
    int           block_size;
    int           warmups, reps;
    std::filesystem::path output_dir;
    std::string   tag;
};

CommonArgs parse_common_args(int argc, char** argv,
                             /* per-binary extra parsers */ ...);

template <class LaunchFn>
void run_warmup_and_timed_reps(EventTimer& timer, const CommonArgs& args,
                               LaunchFn&& launch, /* per-rep callbacks */ ...);
```

Each binary's `main()` is then ~50 lines of library-specific glue, calling the shared helpers. Total LOC for adding a new library drops from ~300 to ~80 per workload.

## CSV schema accommodations

For cuCollections, two columns we have don't apply cleanly:

- `total_probes` / `gbps_corrected` — we'd need to instrument cuco to track probe counts; that's an invasive patch. Leave the columns empty for cuco rows; the plot uses `gbps_floor` as the comparison point.
- `total_failures` — cuco's `static_map` doesn't have a probe cap, so this is always 0 (its insert "fails" only on capacity overflow, which is a different mode).
- The `library` column already handles the multi-library case; the plotting layer reads it.

## Order of operations I'd propose

1. Add the submodule check to the Makefile (skip baselines cleanly if not vendored).
2. Light refactor of `benchmarks.cuh` to factor out the common Args / CSV / timing helpers.
3. Migrate the three existing binaries to the refactored helpers (smoke-test against the previous CSV output to make sure numbers match).
4. Vendor cuCollections (`git submodule add`).
5. Write `baselines/cuco/benchmark_insert.cu` and `benchmark_get.cu`.
6. Update `scripts/benchmark.sh` to optionally invoke the baselines.

Steps 2–3 are essentially zero-risk if we verify CSV numerical match before moving on. Steps 4–6 are the new work.

Sound right? Anything you'd change before I start?

## Assistant — 2026-05-18T23:45:55.877Z

<em>Thinking…</em>

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-18T23:46:08.671Z

Yes, very reasonable — and the cleanest way to achieve it is actually **not** to use `git submodule add` at all.

Here's the issue with submodules: `git submodule add` creates a `.gitmodules` file at the repo root, which *is* tracked. Even if you gitignore the submodule's contents, anyone who clones recursively will follow the `.gitmodules` pointer and fetch cuCollections. The whole point of the submodule mechanism is to make that recursive fetch happen automatically. Fighting it is awkward.

So the pattern that actually delivers what you want is:

1. **Add `external/` to `.gitignore`.** Single line addition.
2. **`git clone https://github.com/NVIDIA/cuCollections.git external/cuCollections`** — done by hand, locally, never tracked.
3. **Pin the version in a committed text file** (e.g. `notes/baselines.md` or `external/.versions`): a known-good cuCollections commit hash + the date it was tested against. Reproducibility lives in the doc, not in git's submodule pointer. When someone wants to reproduce our benchmark numbers, they read the pinned hash, `git checkout` it inside `external/cuCollections`, and rebuild.
4. **The Makefile detects `external/cuCollections/include/cuco/`** at make-time and conditionally compiles the baseline binaries. No-cuCollections users get a working `make benchmarks` that just doesn't produce the baseline binaries. No errors, no warnings.

Trade-offs vs a real submodule:

- **You lose**: automatic version pinning (the parent repo records the exact submodule commit). Someone reproducing numbers has to manually `git checkout` the pinned hash.
- **You gain**: a clean header-only library that recursively-clones to just `include/`. cuCollections (and its own transitive cuda libcu++ pulls) stay off the dependency graph for downstream users entirely.

For a benchmark-only dependency, the trade is obviously worth it. The version pinning loss is mostly notional — anyone serious about reproducing benchmark numbers will be reading our notes anyway.

**Plan to execute now:**

1. Add `external/` to `.gitignore`.
2. You run `git clone https://github.com/NVIDIA/cuCollections.git external/cuCollections` locally.
3. I'll add the Makefile detection logic next, then write the cuco baseline binaries against whatever version you pulled.

Sound good? If yes, I'll do step 1 now and wait for you to do step 2 before continuing.

## Assistant — 2026-05-18T23:53:12.133Z

## Assistant — 2026-05-18T23:53:22.006Z

Yes — the idiomatic pattern is a **manifest file inside `external/`**, with a `.gitignore` negation so the manifest is tracked even though the actual clones aren't. Names you'll see in the wild: `external/versions.txt`, `external/DEPS`, `external/manifest.toml`, `external/setup.sh` (executable variant). For our purposes a plain text manifest is fine.

The gitignore trick: change `external/` to `external/*` so that negations can target specific entries underneath:

```
external/*
!external/versions.txt
```

`external/*` ignores the cuCollections and WarpCore checkout directories; `!external/versions.txt` un-ignores the specific manifest file. (You can't combine `external/` plus a negation — once a parent directory is excluded, git won't look inside it to re-include things.)

The manifest format can be anything, but bare and machine-parseable is convenient:

```
# external/versions.txt
# format: <name> <url> <pinned commit>
cuCollections https://github.com/NVIDIA/cuCollections.git d4e84ee20b9185a3aa279ce184416bd41e53287f
warpcore      https://github.com/sleeepyjack/warpcore.git  1a2fe03d438e8dfa1dbd8d9da73f183bd2d72051
```

That keeps the version-of-record colocated with where the dependencies live, and a simple shell loop can read it to bootstrap a fresh checkout if we ever want a `clone-baselines.sh` script.

**One question on overlap**: we already have [notes/baselines.md](notes/baselines.md) with the same info plus prose explanation. Two reasonable paths:

1. **Move the pinned-commit data to `external/versions.txt` and keep `notes/baselines.md` for prose only** (referencing the manifest for the actual hashes). Clean separation: data lives in `external/`, prose lives in `notes/`.
2. **Single source of truth in `external/versions.txt`**, drop `notes/baselines.md`. Saves one file.

I'd lean (1) since the design notes still want to mention *why* these baselines were chosen, but it's a small call. Which do you prefer?

3	# Builds tests, examples, and benchmarks against the public header in

6	# under build/.

12	# Override OPT to apply to every target uniformly:
13	#
14	#   make tests                          unoptimized
15	#   make tests     OPT=-O3              optimized tests
16	#   make benchmark                      sweep at -O3 (default for benchmarks)
17	#   make benchmark OPT=-O0              sweep unoptimized (rarely useful)
18	#   make test      OPT="-O0 -G -g"      debuggable test build
19	#

26	#   make CXX_STD=c++17

29	CXX_STD ?= c++20
30	ARCH    ?= sm_89
31	
32	OPT     ?=
33	EXTRA   ?=
34	
35	# Benchmark-specific OPT: -O3 if the user didn't set OPT explicitly, else
36	# whatever they passed. Lives in its own variable so the pattern rule
37	# below stays straightforward.
38	ifeq ($(strip $(OPT)),)
39	    BENCHMARK_OPT := -O3
40	else
41	    BENCHMARK_OPT := $(OPT)
42	endif
43	
44	INCLUDES   := -Iinclude
45

47	NVCC_FLAGS_BENCHMARK := -std=$(CXX_STD) -arch=$(ARCH) $(BENCHMARK_OPT) $(INCLUDES) $(EXTRA)
48	
49	BUILD := build
50	
51	TEST_SRCS    := $(wildcard tests/test_*.cu)
52	TEST_BINS    := $(patsubst tests/%.cu,$(BUILD)/tests/%,$(TEST_SRCS))
53	
54	EXAMPLE_SRCS := $(wildcard examples/*.cu)
55	EXAMPLE_BINS := $(patsubst examples/%.cu,$(BUILD)/examples/%,$(EXAMPLE_SRCS))
56	
57	BENCHMARK_SRCS   := $(wildcard benchmarks/benchmark_*.cu)
58	BENCHMARK_BINS   := $(patsubst benchmarks/%.cu,$(BUILD)/benchmarks/%,$(BENCHMARK_SRCS))
59	
60	# Treat any header change as a reason to rebuild everything. Adequate for a
61	# small project; revisit if compile times become an issue.
62	ALL_HEADERS := $(shell find include tests benchmarks -name "*.cuh" 2>/dev/null)
63	
64	.PHONY: all tests examples benchmarks test benchmark clean
65	
66	all: tests examples
67

70	benchmarks: $(BENCHMARK_BINS)
71

73		@set -e; for t in $(TEST_BINS); do echo "==> $$t"; "$$t"; done
74	
75	# Run the full benchmark sweep. Benchmark binaries default to -O3 (see
76	# BENCHMARK_OPT above); override via OPT=... if needed.
77	benchmark: $(BENCHMARK_BINS)
78		@bash scripts/benchmark.sh
79

82		$(NVCC) $(NVCC_FLAGS) $< -o $@

85		@mkdir -p $(BUILD)/examples

89		@mkdir -p $(BUILD)/benchmarks

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T00:49:52.022Z

Just spell-check noise on the library names. Makefile changes summary:

- **`CUCO_INCLUDE` / `WARPCORE_INCLUDE`** named variables pointing at the per-library include roots.
- **`HAVE_CUCO` / `HAVE_WARPCORE`** wildcard-based presence checks; if the respective `include/<namespace>/` directory exists, the library is present.
- **Conditional `BENCHMARK_SRCS += ...`** for each library — sources from `benchmarks/baselines/<lib>/` are pulled in only when the library is detected.
- **Per-library pattern rules** add the right `-I` flag on top of `NVCC_FLAGS_BENCHMARK`. More specific than the catch-all `$(BUILD)/benchmarks/%` rule, so they win for targets under `build/benchmarks/baselines/<lib>/`.
- **`@mkdir -p $(dir $@)`** in all benchmark rules so they handle nested subdirectories under `build/benchmarks/`.

With no baseline source files written yet, `make benchmarks` continues to build exactly the same three gpurhh binaries as before. Once we drop a `benchmarks/baselines/cuco/benchmark_*.cu` into place, it picks it up automatically.

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T00:52:11.581Z

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T00:52:25.709Z

Practical numbers first:

| | full repo | `include/` only |
| --- | --- | --- |
| cuCollections | 21 MB | 1.5 MB |
| WarpCore | 50 MB | 2.4 MB |

So we're talking ~70 MB vs ~4 MB total on disk. Not huge either way.

The non-include parts you'd be dropping: each repo carries `examples/`, `tests/`, `benchmarks/`, `docs/`, `cmake/`, etc. Roughly 95% of the size, none of which we link against.

## Argument for keeping full repos (what we have now)

- **One-command setup.** `git clone` and you're done.
- **Git provenance baked in.** `git -C external/cuCollections log` answers "which commit am I on" without any external manifest. The pin in `external/versions.txt` becomes a recommendation rather than the only record.
- **Upstream examples are useful as references** when writing the wrapper. cuCollections's `examples/static_map/host_bulk_example.cu` is the canonical pattern we'll be mirroring for `benchmark_cuco_insert.cu`. Tests under their tree are also helpful for edge cases.
- **Upgrading is trivial.** `git -C external/cuCollections fetch; git checkout <new-hash>` and you're on a new version. Update `external/versions.txt`. Done.
- **No clone-and-strip ceremony**, no setup script to maintain.

## Argument for include-only

- ~94% less disk per library.
- Cleaner `external/` tree — no extraneous build systems sitting around.
- Slightly faster initial clone.

## The middle option I'd point out

If disk is the worry, the standard mitigation is `--filter=blob:none` (partial clone — only fetches blobs you actually touch) or `--depth 1` (shallow clone — only the latest commit, no history). Either gets you ~half the disk hit for the cuCollections-sized case while keeping the full-repo ergonomics:

```sh
git clone --filter=blob:none https://github.com/NVIDIA/cuCollections.git external/cuCollections
```

vs. include-only, which would need a clone-and-strip script that we'd have to write and maintain.

## My recommendation

Keep what you have. The 70 MB is local-only (gitignored, not in our repo), and the ergonomics benefits — one-command setup, queryable git history, access to upstream examples — are real and ongoing while the disk cost is one-time and small. I'd say revisit only if you start finding yourself doing `rm -rf` cycles to manage `external/` size, which 70 MB doesn't warrant.

If you do want to slim it down, the partial-clone variant is the lowest-friction option. Want me to update the recipe in `external/versions.txt` to recommend `--filter=blob:none`?

## User — 2026-05-19T00:52:54.807Z

<ide_opened_file>The user opened the file [repo]/Makefile in the IDE. This may or may not be related to the current task.</ide_opened_file>

What is the standard way to handle an external directory in C++?

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T00:53:37.929Z

<em>Thinking…</em>

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T00:54:01.653Z

There isn't *one* convention in C++ — the language doesn't ship a package manager, so each project ends up choosing among several patterns. Quick survey:

## Directory naming

- **`third_party/`** — Google convention. Used by Chromium, V8, Bazel, gRPC. Most common in large modern C++ projects.
- **`external/`** — Bazel-flavored (the term Bazel uses for materialized WORKSPACE deps). What we're using.
- **`extern/`** — older convention, still seen.
- **`deps/`** — common with CMake's FetchContent.
- **`vendor/`** — Go convention; sometimes ported to C++.
- **`subprojects/`** — Meson convention.

The name doesn't carry much weight. Pick one and stick with it.

## How the deps get there

Roughly four patterns, ordered by how much "this is part of our repo" they imply:

1. **Vendored copies (committed source).** Drop the upstream files into `third_party/<lib>/` and commit them. Maximum reproducibility — clone, build, done. Cost: every upstream version bump is a diff in your repo, and the repo grows. Chromium and Bazel do this.
2. **Git submodules.** `.gitmodules` records the upstream URL and commit; recursive clone fetches. Common in C++ projects. Downside is exactly the thing we just spent effort avoiding: recursive clones pick the submodules up.
3. **CMake `FetchContent` / `ExternalProject_Add`.** Lives entirely in the build system. At configure time, CMake downloads (or `find_package` finds) the dep. Source is never in your tree. Modern CMake-native projects use this.
4. **Package manager** — vcpkg, Conan, Hunter. Centralized cache outside your source tree. Most ergonomic when it works, most opaque when it doesn't. Overkill for two header-only deps.

## What we have

A fifth, less-formal pattern: **gitignored local clones + a committed manifest of pinned commits**. This is essentially "FetchContent done by hand, scripted by the user, not the build system." It's unusual but defensible for *strictly optional* developer-only dependencies like benchmark baselines. The reasoning chain:

- We don't want submodules (recursive-clone pull).
- We're not using CMake (so FetchContent isn't on the table).
- The deps are pure header-only and one-time-touched (not constantly re-fetched by CI), so a package manager is more overhead than value.
- They're optional — `make benchmarks` works without them — so the worst case for a downstream user is "I cloned without external/ deps; the gpurhh-only benchmark builds and runs fine."

The closest "standard" cousins to what we have:
- **Rust dev-dependencies** in `Cargo.toml`'s `[dev-dependencies]` section — pulled by Cargo only when the user does dev work.
- **Python's `requirements-dev.txt` vs `requirements.txt`** — same shape, dev tooling separated from runtime deps.
- **CMake projects with `find_package(X QUIET)` and `if(X_FOUND)`** — optional feature, only enabled when the package happens to be installed.

So the *idea* is conventional (optional dev-time dep gated by detection); the *mechanism* (gitignored clone + manifest) is a hand-rolled version of what build systems handle automatically.

## If you wanted to look more conventional

The two paths that would feel most "ordinary C++ project":

- **Switch to git submodules with `git config submodule.X.update = none`** (so they don't auto-pull on recursive clone) plus a README pointing at the init command. Pros: tooling support (`git submodule status` etc.). Cons: still pollutes `.gitmodules` on recursive clones; we'd have to test whether the "update=none" trick actually works as advertised.
- **Migrate the build to CMake** and use `FetchContent_Declare(... GIT_REPOSITORY ... GIT_TAG ...)` with `EXCLUDE_FROM_ALL`. This is the most idiomatic modern path for optional deps in a header-only library. But it means a build system rewrite, which is a real project of its own.

For where we are (two deps, header-only, optional, no CMake), I'd argue what we have is already the lowest-friction defensible option. The "we did something unusual" cost is one paragraph of explanation in `external/versions.txt`, which is already there.

## User — 2026-05-19T00:55:58.718Z

What do you think about a pattern with vendored copies behind a gitignore, together with a small script that clones and sets them up?

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T00:57:09.999Z

<em>Thinking…</em>

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T00:57:26.075Z

I like it. It's a clean pattern that formalizes the setup process and gives a much cleaner `external/` tree. The reasoning:

## What changes

Instead of the current full-repo layout:

```
external/
  versions.txt
  cuCollections/         full git clone, ~21 MB
    include/cuco/
    benchmarks/, tests/, examples/, ...
  warpcore/              full git clone, ~50 MB
    include/warpcore/
    benchmarks/, tests/, examples/, ...
```

we'd have:

```
external/
  versions.txt           (committed)
  cuCollections/         (gitignored — just headers, ~1.5 MB)
    cuco/static_map.cuh
    ...
  warpcore/              (gitignored — just headers, ~2.4 MB)
    warpcore/...
```

And a `scripts/setup-baselines.sh` that reads `versions.txt`, does a `git clone --filter=blob:none --no-checkout` + `git checkout <pin>` to a temp dir, copies `include/` into `external/<name>/`, and removes the temp clone.

## Genuine pros

- **One-command setup**: `bash scripts/setup-baselines.sh` replaces "two manual clone + checkout pairs."
- **Cleaner `external/`**: no extraneous benchmarks / tests / docs / CI files from upstream sitting in your tree. When you `ls external/` you see only what's actually used.
- **Pins are enforced by code rather than discipline.** Right now, you could clone cuCollections, forget to `git checkout`, and end up on `dev` HEAD without noticing. The script does the `checkout` for you.
- **Smaller disk footprint** (~70 MB → ~4 MB). Trivial in isolation but pleasant.
- **Idempotent updates**: change a pin in `versions.txt`, re-run the script, you're on the new version.

## Honest cons

- **No upstream source access in-place.** When writing the cuCollections wrapper, you can't `ls external/cuCollections/examples/` to crib from their reference patterns. You'd `git clone` cuCollections separately if you want to read examples — but that's fine, that's a one-time read.
- **A script is one more thing to maintain.** Bash setup scripts are notorious for accumulating quiet bugs (works on your machine, fails on someone else's). Mitigations: `set -euo pipefail`, use of `mktemp` + cleanup trap, and prefer plain `git`/`cp` over fancier tooling. Roughly 30 lines of script.

## Net

I think the script + headers-only pattern is genuinely a nicer end state for a project where the external deps are *purely* used for benchmark comparison (never imported by users of the library). The cleaner tree and one-command setup outweigh the loss of in-place upstream examples — which, for a header-only template library, you mostly read once when writing wrappers and then never look at again.

Switching over involves:
1. Writing `scripts/setup-baselines.sh` (~30 lines).
2. `rm -rf external/cuCollections external/warpcore` to remove the full clones.
3. Running the script to repopulate with just headers.
4. Updating `CUCO_INCLUDE`/`WARPCORE_INCLUDE` in the Makefile from `external/<lib>/include` to `external/<lib>`.
5. Updating `external/versions.txt`'s preamble comment to point at the script instead of describing manual cloning.

Want me to do that switch now? About 10 minutes of work and gets you the cleaner layout before we start writing the baseline binaries.

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T01:00:23.708Z

**→ Tool call: `Write`** (id `tool-528`)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "file_path": "[repo]/scripts/setup-baselines.sh",
  "content": "#!/usr/bin/env bash\n#\n# Populate external/ with header subtrees of the optional baseline\n# libraries the benchmark suite compares against. None of these are\n# required to build or use gpurhh itself; they exist only for the\n# benchmark comparison plots.\n#\n# Pins are inlined below. To upgrade a baseline, bump the commit hash\n# and re-run this script.\n#\n# Implementation: clone with --filter=blob:none and a sparse-checkout\n# limited to `include/`, then move the materialized include subtree\n# into external/<name>/. The full upstream tree is never materialized\n# on disk.\n#\n# Idempotent — safe to re-run after a pin change to refresh.\n\nset -euo pipefail\n\n# --- pinned versions -------------------------------------------------------\n# Format per entry: \"<name> <url> <commit>\"\nLIBS=(\n    \"cuCollections https://github.com/NVIDIA/cuCollections.git d4e84ee20b9185a3aa279ce184416bd41e53287f\"\n    \"warpcore      https://github.com/sleeepyjack/warpcore.git  1a2fe03d438e8dfa1dbd8d9da73f183bd2d72051\"\n)\n\n# --- script body -----------------------------------------------------------\n\nREPO_ROOT=\"$(cd \"$(dirname \"${BASH_SOURCE[0]}\")/..\" && pwd)\"\nEXTERNAL=\"${REPO_ROOT}/external\"\nmkdir -p \"${EXTERNAL}\"\n\nfetch_one() {\n    local name=\"$1\" url=\"$2\" commit=\"$3\"\n    local target=\"${EXTERNAL}/${name}\"\n    local tmp\n    tmp=\"$(mktemp -d)\"\n    trap 'rm -rf \"${tmp}\"' RETURN\n\n    echo \"==> ${name} @ ${commit}\"\n\n    # Partial clone (no blobs until requested) + sparse checkout limited\n    # to the include subtree. Avoids materializing tests/, benchmarks/,\n    # docs/, etc. from upstream.\n    git clone --quiet --filter=blob:none --no-checkout \"${url}\" \"${tmp}\"\n    git -C \"${tmp}\" sparse-checkout init --cone\n    git -C \"${tmp}\" sparse-checkout set include\n    git -C \"${tmp}\" checkout --quiet \"${commit}\"\n\n    if [[ ! -d \"${tmp}/include\" ]]; then\n        echo \"  error: upstream has no include/ directory\" >&2\n        return 1\n    fi\n\n    rm -rf \"${target}\"\n    mv \"${tmp}/include\" \"${target}\"\n}\n\nfor entry in \"${LIBS[@]}\"; do\n    read -r name url commit <<< \"${entry}\"\n    fetch_one \"${name}\" \"${url}\" \"${commit}\"\ndone\n\necho \"Done. Headers placed under ${EXTERNAL}/\"\n"
}
```

</details>

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T01:01:50.999Z

Done. Summary:

- [scripts/setup-baselines.sh](scripts/setup-baselines.sh): version pins inlined; uses `git clone --filter=blob:none --no-checkout` + `git sparse-checkout set include` to fetch only the include subtree, then `mv`s it into `external/<name>/`. Idempotent — re-run after a pin bump and it'll refresh.
- `external/versions.txt`: deleted.
- [.gitignore](.gitignore): back to plain `external/` (no negation needed now).
- [Makefile](Makefile): `CUCO_INCLUDE` and `WARPCORE_INCLUDE` updated to drop the `/include` suffix; detection still works against the new layout.
- [notes/design.md](notes/design.md): the future-work entry now points at the script.

To switch over to the new layout, the existing full clones need to be wiped first so the script can repopulate:

```sh
rm -rf external/cuCollections external/warpcore
bash scripts/setup-baselines.sh
```

That should leave you with `external/cuCollections/cuco/...` and `external/warpcore/warpcore/...` — just the headers, ~4 MB total.

**→ Tool call: `Write`** (id `tool-534`)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "file_path": "[repo]/scripts/setup-baselines.sh",
  "content": "#!/usr/bin/env bash\n#\n# Populate external/ with the header subtrees of the optional baseline\n# libraries the benchmark suite compares against. Neither is required\n# to build or use gpurhh itself — these exist only for the benchmark\n# comparison plots.\n#\n# Pins are inlined below. To upgrade a baseline, bump its commit hash\n# and re-run this script.\n#\n# Implementation: partial clone with --filter=blob:none plus a\n# sparse-checkout limited to `include/`. The full upstream tree is\n# never materialized.\n#\n# Idempotent — safe to re-run after a pin change to refresh.\n\nset -euo pipefail\n\n# --- pinned versions -------------------------------------------------------\n# Format per entry: \"<name> <url> <commit>\"\nLIBS=(\n    \"cuCollections https://github.com/NVIDIA/cuCollections.git d4e84ee20b9185a3aa279ce184416bd41e53287f\"\n    \"warpcore      https://github.com/sleeepyjack/warpcore.git  1a2fe03d438e8dfa1dbd8d9da73f183bd2d72051\"\n)\n\n# --- script body -----------------------------------------------------------\n\nREPO_ROOT=\"$(cd \"$(dirname \"${BASH_SOURCE[0]}\")/..\" && pwd)\"\nEXTERNAL=\"${REPO_ROOT}/external\"\nmkdir -p \"${EXTERNAL}\"\n\nfor entry in \"${LIBS[@]}\"; do\n    read -r name url commit <<< \"${entry}\"\n    target=\"${EXTERNAL}/${name}\"\n\n    echo \"==> ${name} @ ${commit}\"\n\n    rm -rf \"${target}\"\n    git clone --quiet --filter=blob:none --no-checkout \"${url}\" \"${target}\"\n    git -C \"${target}\" sparse-checkout init --cone\n    git -C \"${target}\" sparse-checkout set include\n    git -C \"${target}\" checkout --quiet \"${commit}\"\ndone\n\necho \"Done.\"\n"
}
```

</details>
