```json
  "file_path": "[repo]/benchmarks/baselines/warpcore/benchmark_get.cu",
  "old_string": "std::string format_row(\n    std::size_t slot_bytes, std::size_t capacity, std::size_t capacity_bytes,\n    std::size_t key_range, double alpha, double load_factor,\n    int block_size, std::size_t n_ops, std::size_t bytes_per_op,\n    std::uint32_t insert_seed, std::uint32_t get_seed,\n    int rep, const std::string& tag,\n    float time_ms, double ops_per_sec, double gbps_floor,\n    std::uint64_t total_hits, std::uint64_t total_misses)\n{\n    std::ostringstream s;\n    s.precision(9);\n    s << \"warpcore,get,\"\n      << slot_bytes      << \",\"\n      << capacity        << \",\"\n      << capacity_bytes  << \",\"\n      << key_range       << \",\"\n      << alpha           << \",\"\n      << load_factor     << \",\"\n      << block_size      << \",\"\n      << n_ops           << \",\"\n      << bytes_per_op    << \",\"\n      << insert_seed     << \",\"\n      << get_seed        << \",\"\n      << rep             << \",\"\n      << tag             << \",\"\n      << time_ms         << \",\"\n      << ops_per_sec     << \",\"\n      << gbps_floor      << \",\"\n      << \",\"             // gbps_corrected\n      << \",\"             // total_probes\n      << total_hits      << \",\"\n      << total_misses    << \",\"\n      << \"\";             // prefill_failures\n    return s.str();\n}",
  "new_string": "std::string format_row(\n    std::size_t slot_bytes, std::size_t capacity, std::size_t capacity_bytes,\n    std::size_t key_range, double alpha, double load_factor,\n    int block_size, std::size_t n_ops, std::size_t bytes_per_op,\n    std::uint32_t insert_seed, std::uint32_t get_seed,\n    int rep, const std::string& tag,\n    float time_ms, double ops_per_sec, double gbps_floor)\n{\n    std::ostringstream s;\n    s.precision(9);\n    s << \"warpcore,get,\"\n      << slot_bytes      << \",\"\n      << capacity        << \",\"\n      << capacity_bytes  << \",\"\n      << key_range       << \",\"\n      << alpha           << \",\"\n      << load_factor     << \",\"\n      << block_size      << \",\"\n      << n_ops           << \",\"\n      << bytes_per_op    << \",\"\n      << insert_seed     << \",\"\n      << get_seed        << \",\"\n      << rep             << \",\"\n      << tag             << \",\"\n      << time_ms         << \",\"\n      << ops_per_sec     << \",\"\n      << gbps_floor      << \",\"\n      << \",\"             // gbps_corrected\n      << \",\"             // total_probes\n      << \",\"             // total_hits   (StatusHandler wire-up not done)\n      << \",\"             // total_misses\n      << \"\";             // prefill_failures\n    return s.str();\n}"
}
```

```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/benchmarks/baselines/warpcore/benchmark_get.cu",
  "old_string": "    EventTimer timer;\n\n    // Same caching pattern as the cuco baseline: compute hit/miss counts\n    // once on the first rep, reuse for subsequent rows.\n    std::vector<Value> h_out(n_ops);\n    std::uint64_t cached_hits   = 0;\n    std::uint64_t cached_misses = 0;\n    bool counted = false;\n    const Value empty_value_sentinel = Table::empty_value();\n\n    run_benchmark_loop(args.warmups, args.reps, timer,\n        /*setup=*/  []() {},\n        /*launch=*/ [&]() {\n            table.retrieve(d_get_keys, n_ops, d_out);\n        },\n        /*after=*/  [&](int rep, float ms) {\n            if (!counted) {\n                cudaMemcpy(h_out.data(), d_out, n_ops * sizeof(Value),\n                           cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n                for (std::size_t i = 0; i < n_ops; ++i) {\n                    if (h_out[i] == empty_value_sentinel) ++cached_misses;\n                    else                                  ++cached_hits;\n                }\n                counted = true;\n            }\n            const double ops_per_sec =\n                static_cast<double>(n_ops) / (static_cast<double>(ms) * 1.0e-3);\n            const double gbps_floor =\n                static_cast<double>(n_ops) * static_cast<double>(bytes_per_op)\n                    / (static_cast<double>(ms) * 1.0e6);\n            csv.write_row(format_row(\n                slot_bytes, capacity, capacity_bytes,\n                std::size_t{key_range}, /*alpha=*/1.0, args.load_factor,\n                args.block_size, n_ops, bytes_per_op,\n                args.insert_seed, args.get_seed, rep, args.tag,\n                ms, ops_per_sec, gbps_floor,\n                cached_hits, cached_misses));\n        });",
  "new_string": "    EventTimer timer;\n\n    run_benchmark_loop(args.warmups, args.reps, timer,\n        /*setup=*/  []() {},\n        /*launch=*/ [&]() {\n            table.retrieve(d_get_keys, n_ops, d_out);\n        },\n        /*after=*/  [&](int rep, float ms) {\n            const double ops_per_sec =\n                static_cast<double>(n_ops) / (static_cast<double>(ms) * 1.0e-3);\n            const double gbps_floor =\n                static_cast<double>(n_ops) * static_cast<double>(bytes_per_op)\n                    / (static_cast<double>(ms) * 1.0e6);\n            csv.write_row(format_row(\n                slot_bytes, capacity, capacity_bytes,\n                std::size_t{key_range}, /*alpha=*/1.0, args.load_factor,\n                args.block_size, n_ops, bytes_per_op,\n                args.insert_seed, args.get_seed, rep, args.tag,\n                ms, ops_per_sec, gbps_floor));\n        });"
```

```
3:# Builds tests, examples, and benchmarks against the public header in
5:# under tests/, examples/, and benchmarks/ becomes its own executable
9:#   - tests / examples: empty (nvcc's default; fastest to compile)
10:#   - benchmarks:       -O3 (unoptimized throughput numbers are meaningless)
14:#   make tests                          unoptimized
15:#   make tests     OPT=-O3              optimized tests
16:#   make benchmark                      sweep at -O3 (default for benchmarks)
17:#   make benchmark OPT=-O0              sweep unoptimized (rarely useful)
18:#   make test      OPT="-O0 -G -g"      debuggable test build
21:# one-off additions (e.g. `make tests EXTRA=-lineinfo`).
51:TEST_SRCS    := $(wildcard tests/test_*.cu)
52:TEST_BINS    := $(patsubst tests/%.cu,$(BUILD)/tests/%,$(TEST_SRCS))
57:BENCHMARK_SRCS   := $(wildcard benchmarks/benchmark_*.cu)
63:# `make benchmarks` produces just the gpurhh binaries.
70:    BENCHMARK_SRCS += $(wildcard benchmarks/baselines/cuco/benchmark_*.cu)
73:    BENCHMARK_SRCS += $(wildcard benchmarks/baselines/warpcore/benchmark_*.cu)
76:BENCHMARK_BINS   := $(patsubst benchmarks/%.cu,$(BUILD)/benchmarks/%,$(BENCHMARK_SRCS))
80:ALL_HEADERS := $(shell find include tests benchmarks -name "*.cuh" 2>/dev/null)
82:.PHONY: all tests examples benchmarks test benchmark benchmark-with-baselines clean
84:all: tests examples
86:tests:      $(TEST_BINS)
88:benchmarks: $(BENCHMARK_BINS)
90:test: $(TEST_BINS)
93:# Run the full benchmark sweep. Benchmark binaries default to -O3 (see
95:benchmark: $(BENCHMARK_BINS)
96:	@bash scripts/benchmark.sh
98:# Same as `benchmark`, plus the baseline sweeps for any external
103:benchmark-with-baselines: $(BENCHMARK_BINS)
104:	@bash scripts/benchmark.sh --with-baselines
106:$(BUILD)/tests/%: tests/%.cu $(ALL_HEADERS)
107:	@mkdir -p $(BUILD)/tests
114:$(BUILD)/benchmarks/%: benchmarks/%.cu $(ALL_HEADERS)
118:# Baseline pattern rule — more specific than the catch-all benchmark
119:# rule above, so it wins for targets under build/benchmarks/baselines/.
124:$(BUILD)/benchmarks/baselines/%: benchmarks/baselines/%.cu $(ALL_HEADERS)
```

</details>

```json
  "new_string": "`make benchmarks` builds the binaries at `-O3` (the default for the benchmark pattern rule; see Makefile); the sweep itself is launched directly from `scripts/benchmark.sh` rather than a make target, since benchmark runs are heavy and infrequent compared with the build / test inner loop. ## Assistant — 2026-05-19T01:56:45.348Z
```

Done. Make targets removed:
- `benchmark` and `benchmark-with-baselines` gone.
- `.PHONY` trimmed accordingly.
- Header comment block updated to explain the asymmetry (test runs via make because it's an inner-loop activity; benchmarks run via the standalone script because they're heavy and infrequent).
- [notes/design.md](notes/design.md) Benchmarking section updated to point at the script rather than the deleted make target.

Workflow now:
- `make benchmarks` — build the binaries (at `-O3` automatically).
- `bash scripts/benchmark.sh` — run the gpurhh sweep.
- `bash scripts/benchmark.sh --with-baselines` — run gpurhh + baselines.

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T01:59:22.279Z

Done. Behavior matrix:

| Flags | gpurhh memcpy + sweep | Baselines |
| --- | --- | --- |
| (none) | yes | no |
| `--with-baselines` | yes | yes |
| `--only-baselines` | no | yes |
| both | error (mutually exclusive) | — |

So `bash scripts/benchmark.sh --only-baselines output/2026-05-18_02-27-54/` appends just the cuco/warpcore rows into an existing gpurhh run's `insert.csv` / `get.csv` (and skips re-running memcpy). `--help` lists both options.

```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/scripts/benchmark.sh",
  "old_string": "WITH_BASELINES=0\nONLY_BASELINES=0\nPOSITIONAL=()\nwhile [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do\n    case \"$1\" in\n        --with-baselines) WITH_BASELINES=1; shift ;;\n        --only-baselines) ONLY_BASELINES=1; shift ;;\n        --help|-h)\n            sed -n '2,23p' \"$0\" | sed 's/^# //; s/^#//'\n            exit 0\n            ;;\n        --*)\n            echo \"Unknown flag: $1\" >&2\n            echo \"Try '$0 --help' for usage.\" >&2\n            exit 1\n            ;;\n        *)\n            POSITIONAL+=(\"$1\")\n            shift\n            ;;\n    esac\ndone\nset -- \"${POSITIONAL[@]}\"\n\nif [[ \"${WITH_BASELINES}\" -eq 1 && \"${ONLY_BASELINES}\" -eq 1 ]]; then\n    echo \"--with-baselines and --only-baselines are mutually exclusive\" >&2\n    exit 1\nfi",
  "new_string": "print_usage() {\n    sed -n '2,30p' \"$0\" | sed 's/^# //; s/^#//'\n}\n\nNO_BASELINES=0\nWITH_BASELINES=0\nONLY_BASELINES=0\nPOSITIONAL=()\nwhile [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do\n    case \"$1\" in\n        --no-baselines)   NO_BASELINES=1;   shift ;;\n        --with-baselines) WITH_BASELINES=1; shift ;;\n        --only-baselines) ONLY_BASELINES=1; shift ;;\n        --help|-h)\n            print_usage\n            exit 0\n            ;;\n        --*)\n            echo \"Unknown flag: $1\" >&2\n            echo \"Try '$0 --help' for usage.\" >&2\n            exit 1\n            ;;\n        *)\n            POSITIONAL+=(\"$1\")\n            shift\n            ;;\n    esac\ndone\nset -- \"${POSITIONAL[@]}\"\n\nmodes_set=$((NO_BASELINES + WITH_BASELINES + ONLY_BASELINES))\nif [[ ${modes_set} -eq 0 ]]; then\n    print_usage >&2\n    exit 1\nfi\nif [[ ${modes_set} -gt 1 ]]; then\n    echo \"Pick at most one of --no-baselines, --with-baselines, --only-baselines\" >&2\n    exit 1\nfi"
}
```

```json
  "old_string": "# Usage:\n#     scripts/benchmark.sh (--no-baselines | --with-baselines | --only-baselines) [output-dir]\n#\n# One mode flag is required (no default — benchmark runs are heavy\n# enough that we'd rather force the choice than have you typo your way\n# into one). Passing no flags prints this usage and exits.\n#\n#   --no-baselines      Run the gpurhh sweep (memcpy reference + insert\n#                       + get); skip any external baselines.\n#   --with-baselines    Run the gpurhh sweep, then also any baseline\n#                       libraries whose binaries were built. Baseline\n#                       rows append to the same insert.csv / get.csv;\n#                       the `library` column distinguishes them.\n#   --only-baselines    Skip the gpurhh sweep entirely; run baselines\n#                       only.   "new_string": "# Usage:\n#     scripts/benchmark.sh <one-or-more-libraries> [output-dir]\n#\n# Pick at least one library to run. Combining flags runs each\n# selected library's sweep in turn. Passing no flag prints this usage\n# and exits — no default, since benchmark runs are heavy.\n#\n#   --gpurhh    Run the gpurhh sweep (memcpy reference + insert + get).\n#   --cuco      Run the cuCollections baseline (must be set up via\n#               scripts/setup-baselines.sh).\n#   --warpcore  Run the WarpCore baseline (likewise).\n#   --all       Shortcut for \"every library whose binaries built\";\n#               missing baselines are skipped with a warning rather\n#               than erroring.\n#\n# Library-specific flags are strict — they error if the corresponding\n# binaries weren't built. Use --all if you want lenient \"run what's\n# there\" behavior. All rows land in the same insert.csv / get.csv;\n# the `library` column distinguishes them."
}
```

```
65	fi

67	REPO_ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/.." && pwd)"
68	BUILD_DIR="${REPO_ROOT}/build/benchmarks"
69	
70	if [[ $# -ge 1 ]]; then
71	    OUTPUT_DIR="$1"
72	else
73	    OUTPUT_DIR="${REPO_ROOT}/output/$(date +%Y-%m-%d_%H-%M-%S)"
74	fi
75	mkdir -p "${OUTPUT_DIR}"
76	
77	# Mirror stdout + stderr to a log file in the output dir.
78	LOG_FILE="${OUTPUT_DIR}/benchmark.log"
79	exec > >(tee -a "${LOG_FILE}") 2>&1
80	
81	# Make sure the gpurhh binaries are built (unless we're skipping them
82	# entirely with --only-baselines, in which case the baseline existence
83	# check further down handles diagnostics).
84	if [[ "${ONLY_BASELINES}" -eq 0 ]]; then
85	    for bin in benchmark_memcpy benchmark_insert benchmark_get; do
86	        if [[ ! -x "${BUILD_DIR}/${bin}" ]]; then
87	            echo "Missing executable: ${BUILD_DIR}/${bin}" >&2
88	            echo "Build first with:   make benchmarks" >&2
89	            exit 1
90	        fi
91	    done
92	fi
93	
94	echo "==> Writing CSV output and run_info to ${OUTPUT_DIR}"
95	
96	{
97	    echo "date:        $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)"
98	    echo "uname:       $(uname -srm)"
99	    echo "nvcc:        $(nvcc --version 2>/dev/null | tail -n1 || echo 'not found')"
100	    echo "nvidia-smi:  $(nvidia-smi --query-gpu=name,driver_version,memory.total,power.limit --format=csv,noheader 2>/dev/null || echo 'not found')"
101	} > "${OUTPUT_DIR}/run_info.txt"
102	
103	# --- sweep grid (gpurhh) ---------------------------------------------------
104	#
105	# Capacity is held fixed at 1 GiB (DRAM-resident, well past the 4090's
106	# 72 MB L2). α is implicitly 1 (key range = capacity). F sweeps over
107	# the regime where Robin Hood actually has work to do; block_size
108	# sweeps to spot-check whether launch shape moves the number.
109	CAPACITY_BYTES=$((1024 * 1024 * 1024))   # 1 GiB
110	LOAD_FACTORS=(0.5 1.0 1.5 2.0 3.0)
111	BLOCK_SIZES=(64 128 256 512 1024)
112	TAG="sweep"
113	
114	if [[ "${ONLY_BASELINES}" -eq 0 ]]; then
115	    # --- memcpy peak-bandwidth reference --------------------------------------
116	    echo "==> memcpy baseline (${CAPACITY_BYTES} B)"
117	    "${BUILD_DIR}/benchmark_memcpy" \
118	        --output-dir "${OUTPUT_DIR}" \
119	        --bytes "${CAPACITY_BYTES}" \
120	        --tag "${TAG}"
121	
122	    # --- gpurhh insert and get sweeps -----------------------------------------
123	    for f in "${LOAD_FACTORS[@]}"; do
124	        for b in "${BLOCK_SIZES[@]}"; do
125	            echo "==> insert F=${f} block=${b}"
126	            "${BUILD_DIR}/benchmark_insert" \
127	                --output-dir "${OUTPUT_DIR}" \
128	                --capacity-bytes "${CAPACITY_BYTES}" \
129	                --load-factor "${f}" \
130	                --block-size "${b}" \
131	                --tag "${TAG}"
132	
133	            echo "==> get    F=${f} block=${b}"
134	            "${BUILD_DIR}/benchmark_get" \
135	                --output-dir "${OUTPUT_DIR}" \
136	                --capacity-bytes "${CAPACITY_BYTES}" \
137	                --load-factor "${f}" \
138	                --block-size "${b}" \
139	                --tag "${TAG}"
140	        done
141	    done
142	fi
143	
144	# --- baselines (with --with-baselines or --only-baselines) ----------------
145	if [[ "${WITH_BASELINES}" -eq 1 || "${ONLY_BASELINES}" -eq 1 ]]; then
146	    ran_any=0
147	
148	    # cuCollections: fixed-capacity static_map; F sweep stops short of 1.0.
149	    # cuco's bulk API picks its own launch; block_size is not swept.
150	    CUCO_DIR="${BUILD_DIR}/baselines/cuco"
151	    if [[ -x "${CUCO_DIR}/benchmark_insert" && -x "${CUCO_DIR}/benchmark_get" ]]; then
152	        CUCO_LOAD_FACTORS=(0.5 0.7 0.85 0.95)
153	        for f in "${CUCO_LOAD_FACTORS[@]}"; do
154	            echo "==> cuco insert F=${f}"
155	            "${CUCO_DIR}/benchmark_insert" \
156	                --output-dir "${OUTPUT_DIR}" \
157	                --capacity-bytes "${CAPACITY_BYTES}" \
158	                --load-factor "${f}" \
159	                --tag "${TAG}"
160	
161	            echo "==> cuco get    F=${f}"
162	            "${CUCO_DIR}/benchmark_get" \
163	                --output-dir "${OUTPUT_DIR}" \
164	                --capacity-bytes "${CAPACITY_BYTES}" \
165	                --load-factor "${f}" \
166	                --tag "${TAG}"
167	        done
168	        ran_any=1
169	    fi
170	
171	    # WarpCore: same shape as cuco — fixed capacity, F < 1, no block sweep.
172	    WARPCORE_DIR="${BUILD_DIR}/baselines/warpcore"
173	    if [[ -x "${WARPCORE_DIR}/benchmark_insert" && -x "${WARPCORE_DIR}/benchmark_get" ]]; then
174	        WARPCORE_LOAD_FACTORS=(0.5 0.7 0.85 0.95)
175	        for f in "${WARPCORE_LOAD_FACTORS[@]}"; do
176	            echo "==> warpcore insert F=${f}"
177	            "${WARPCORE_DIR}/benchmark_insert" \
178	                --output-dir "${OUTPUT_DIR}" \
179	                --capacity-bytes "${CAPACITY_BYTES}" \
180	                --load-factor "${f}" \
181	                --tag "${TAG}"
182	
183	            echo "==> warpcore get    F=${f}"
184	            "${WARPCORE_DIR}/benchmark_get" \
185	                --output-dir "${OUTPUT_DIR}" \
186	                --capacity-bytes "${CAPACITY_BYTES}" \
187	                --load-factor "${f}" \
188	                --tag "${TAG}"
189	        done
190	        ran_any=1
191	    fi
192	
193	    if [[ "${ran_any}" -eq 0 ]]; then
194	        echo "==> Baselines requested but no baseline binaries found under" >&2
```

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/scripts/benchmark.sh",
  "old_string": "# Make sure the gpurhh binaries are built (unless we're skipping them\n# entirely with --only-baselines, in which case the baseline existence\n# check further down handles diagnostics).\nif [[ \"${ONLY_BASELINES}\" -eq 0 ]]; then\n    for bin in benchmark_memcpy benchmark_insert benchmark_get; do\n        if [[ ! -x \"${BUILD_DIR}/${bin}\" ]]; then\n            echo \"Missing executable: ${BUILD_DIR}/${bin}\" >&2\n            echo \"Build first with:   make benchmarks\" >&2\n            exit 1\n        fi\n    done\nfi",
  "new_string": "# Per-library binary existence check. For an explicit library flag,\n# missing binaries are an error; for --all (LENIENT=1) we drop the\n# library silently with a notice and continue.\nrequire_or_drop() {\n    local label=\"$1\"; shift\n    for bin in \"$@\"; do\n        if [[ ! -x \"${bin}\" ]]; then\n            if [[ \"${LENIENT}\" -eq 1 ]]; then\n                echo \"==> ${label} binaries not built; skipping (--all is lenient)\" >&2\n                return 1\n            fi\n            echo \"Missing executable: ${bin}\" >&2\n            echo \"Build first with:   make benchmarks  (and scripts/setup-baselines.sh for baselines)\" >&2\n            exit 1\n        fi\n    done\n    return 0\n}\n\nif [[ \"${RUN_GPURHH}\" -eq 1 ]]; then\n    require_or_drop \"gpurhh\" \\\n        \"${BUILD_DIR}/benchmark_memcpy\" \\\n        \"${BUILD_DIR}/benchmark_insert\" \\\n        \"${BUILD_DIR}/benchmark_get\" \\\n        || RUN_GPURHH=0\nfi\nif [[ \"${RUN_CUCO}\" -eq 1 ]]; then\n    require_or_drop \"cuco\" \\\n        \"${BUILD_DIR}/baselines/cuco/benchmark_insert\" \\\n        \"${BUILD_DIR}/baselines/cuco/benchmark_get\" \\\n        || RUN_CUCO=0\nfi\nif [[ \"${RUN_WARPCORE}\" -eq 1 ]]; then\n    require_or_drop \"warpcore\" \\\n        \"${BUILD_DIR}/baselines/warpcore/benchmark_insert\" \\\n        \"${BUILD_DIR}/baselines/warpcore/benchmark_get\" \\\n        || RUN_WARPCORE=0\nfi\n\nif [[ $((RUN_GPURHH + RUN_CUCO + RUN_WARPCORE)) -eq 0 ]]; then\n    echo \"Nothing to run.\" >&2\n    exit 1\nfi"
}
```

</details>

then\n        CUCO_LOAD_FACTORS=(0.5 0.7 0.85 0.95)\n        for f in \"${CUCO_LOAD_FACTORS[@]}\"; do\n            echo \"==> cuco insert F=${f}\"\n            \"${CUCO_DIR}/benchmark_insert\" \\\n                --output-dir \"${OUTPUT_DIR}\" \\\n                --capacity-bytes \"${CAPACITY_BYTES}\" \\\n                --load-factor \"${f}\" \\\n                --tag \"${TAG}\"\n\n            echo \"==> cuco get    F=${f}\"\n            \"${CUCO_DIR}/benchmark_get\" \\\n                --output-dir \"${OUTPUT_DIR}\" \\\n                --capacity-bytes \"${CAPACITY_BYTES}\" \\\n                --load-factor \"${f}\" \\\n                --tag \"${TAG}\"\n        done\n        ran_any=1\n    fi\n\n    # WarpCore: same shape as cuco — fixed capacity, F < 1, no block sweep.\n    WARPCORE_DIR=\"${BUILD_DIR}/baselines/warpcore\"\n    if [[ -x \"${WARPCORE_DIR}/benchmark_insert\" && -x \"${WARPCORE_DIR}/benchmark_get\" ]]; then\n        WARPCORE_LOAD_FACTORS=(0.5 0.7 0.85 0.95)\n        for f in \"${WARPCORE_LOAD_FACTORS[@]}\"; do\n            echo \"==> warpcore insert F=${f}\"\n            \"${WARPCORE_DIR}/benchmark_insert\" \\\n                --output-dir \"${OUTPUT_DIR}\" \\\n                --capacity-bytes \"${CAPACITY_BYTES}\" \\\n                --load-factor \"${f}\" \\\n                --tag \"${TAG}\"\n\n            echo \"==> warpcore get    F=${f}\"\n            \"${WARPCORE_DIR}/benchmark_get\" \\\n                --output-dir \"${OUTPUT_DIR}\" \\\n                --capacity-bytes \"${CAPACITY_BYTES}\" \\\n                --load-factor \"${f}\" \\\n                --tag \"${TAG}\"\n        done\n        ran_any=1\n    fi\n\n    if [[ \"${ran_any}\" -eq 0 ]]; then\n        echo \"==> Baselines requested but no baseline binaries found under\" >&2\n        echo \"    ${BUILD_DIR}/baselines/.\" >&2\n        echo \"    Run scripts/setup-baselines.sh and 'make benchmarks' to enable.\" >&2\n    fi\nfi",
```json
  "new_string": "# --- gpurhh (memcpy reference + insert + get sweeps) ---------------------\nif [[ \"${RUN_GPURHH}\" -eq 1 ]]; then\n    echo \"==> memcpy baseline (${CAPACITY_BYTES} B)\"\n    \"${BUILD_DIR}/benchmark_memcpy\" \\\n        --output-dir \"${OUTPUT_DIR}\" \\\n        --bytes \"${CAPACITY_BYTES}\" \\\n        --tag \"${TAG}\"\n\n    for f in \"${LOAD_FACTORS[@]}\"; do\n        for b in \"${BLOCK_SIZES[@]}\"; do\n            echo \"==> gpurhh insert F=${f} block=${b}\"\n            \"${BUILD_DIR}/benchmark_insert\" \\\n                --output-dir \"${OUTPUT_DIR}\" \\\n                --capacity-bytes \"${CAPACITY_BYTES}\" \\\n                --load-factor \"${f}\" \\\n                --block-size \"${b}\" \\\n                --tag \"${TAG}\"\n\n            echo \"==> gpurhh get    F=${f} block=${b}\"\n            \"${BUILD_DIR}/benchmark_get\" \\\n                --output-dir \"${OUTPUT_DIR}\" \\\n                --capacity-bytes \"${CAPACITY_BYTES}\" \\\n                --load-factor \"${f}\" \\\n                --block-size \"${b}\" \\\n                --tag \"${TAG}\"\n        done\n    done\nfi\n\n# --- cuCollections (fixed-capacity static_map; ## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:02:41.117Z
```

Done. The script now exposes one flag per library, with `--all` as a lenient shorthand. Behavior:

| Invocation | Runs |
| --- | --- |
| `bash scripts/benchmark.sh` | prints usage to stderr, exits 1 |
| `--gpurhh` | memcpy + gpurhh insert/get sweep |
| `--cuco` | cuco insert/get sweep only |
| `--warpcore` | warpcore insert/get sweep only |
| `--gpurhh --cuco` | gpurhh + cuco |
| `--all` | every library whose binaries are present |
| `--help` | prints usage to stdout, exits 0 |

Per-library flags are **strict** — `--cuco` without cuco binaries built errors with "Missing executable: …". `--all` is **lenient** — drops missing libraries with a notice and proceeds with what's there. The strict/lenient distinction means typos / forgotten setup steps surface immediately when you're targeting a specific library, while `--all` does the obvious thing.

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:05:25.740Z

All clean. Every cell in every workload matches the pre-refactor numbers within ~0.3%:

- **Memcpy reference**: 1.000–1.001 ratio. Identical.
- **Insert sweep** (25 cells): 0.999–1.002. All within run-to-run noise.
- **Get sweep** (25 cells): 0.997–1.003. Slightly more variance only at F=3, which is expected — that's the load-factor cliff where probe-cap retries kick in and timing is naturally noisier.

Nothing's drifted in a direction that suggests a regression (no cell shifted by more than ~3 parts in 1000, no systematic bias up or down, no cells missing). The `LaunchShape` extraction and the `run_benchmark_loop` template produce byte-identical kernel launches to the inline versions — the only thing that moved was the host-side scaffolding around them.

Refactor is verified clean. Safe to add the baseline comparison rows now.

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "command": "python3 <<'PY'\nimport csv\nfrom collections import defaultdict\n\ndef mean_by(path, library_filter=None):\n    by = defaultdict(list)\n    with open(path) as f:\n        for r in csv.DictReader(f):\n            if library_filter and r[\"library\"] != library_filter: continue\n            lf = float(r[\"load_factor\"])\n            v = float(r[\"gbps_floor\"])\n            by[lf].append(v)\n    return {k: sum(v)/len(v) for k,v in by.items()}\n\n# Memcpy peak for normalization context.\nmem_gbps = []\nwith open(\"memcpy.csv\") as f:\n    for r in csv.DictReader(f):\n        if r[\"workload\"] == \"memcpy_kernel\":\n            mem_gbps.append(float(r[\"gbps_effective\"]))\npeak = sum(mem_gbps)/len(mem_gbps)\nprint(f\"memcpy_kernel peak: {peak:.1f} GB/s\\n\")\n\nfor fname in [\"insert.csv\", \"get.csv\"]:\n    print(f\"=== {fname} : mean gbps_floor, averaged over block_size ===\")\n    print(f\"{'F':>5}  {'gpurhh':>8}  {'cuco':>8}  {'warpcore':>8}    pct of memcpy\")\n    gp = mean_by(fname, \"gpurhh\")\n    cu = mean_by(fname, \"cuco\")\n    wc = mean_by(fname, \"warpcore\")\n    factors = sorted(set(gp)|set(cu)|set(wc))\n    for f in factors:\n        def cell(d, k): return f\"{d[k]:8.1f}\" if k in d else \"      --\"\n        def pct(d, k):\n            if k not in d: return \"  --  \"\n            return f\"{100*d[k]/peak:5.1f}%\"\n        print(f\"{f:>5.2f}  {cell(gp,f)}  {cell(cu,f)}  {cell(wc,f)}    \"\n              f\"gp {pct(gp,f)}  cuco {pct(cu,f)}  wc {pct(wc,f)}\")\n    print()\nPY",
  "description": "Side-by-side mean throughput across libraries"
}
```

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:10:04.106Z

The `gbps_floor` comparison is **not apples-to-apples** across libraries — cuco shows 113% of memcpy peak which is physically impossible. Why: `gbps_floor = n_ops × 128 / time` assumes "1 op = 1 cache line." That holds for gpurhh's bucketed tile-cooperative probing (one tile = one 128-byte bucket per probe), but cuco's `static_map` defaults to **linear probing without bucketing** — each probe is one 8-byte slot, with adjacent slots sharing cache lines, so the *actual* bytes-per-op are much less than 128. cuco moves less DRAM per logical op, which makes its floor metric look inflated.

The fair cross-library comparison is **ops/sec**. Let me pull those.

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:10:09.569Z

**→ Tool call: `Bash`** (id `tool-632`)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "command": "python3 <<'PY'\nimport csv\nfrom collections import defaultdict\n\ndef mean_by(path, library):\n    by = defaultdict(list)\n    with open(path) as f:\n        for r in csv.DictReader(f):\n            if r[\"library\"] != library: continue\n            by[float(r[\"load_factor\"])].append(float(r[\"ops_per_sec\"]))\n    return {k: sum(v)/len(v) for k,v in by.items()}\n\nfor fname in [\"insert.csv\", \"get.csv\"]:\n    print(f\"=== {fname} : mean ops/sec (millions), averaged over block_size ===\")\n    print(f\"{'F':>5}  {'gpurhh':>8}  {'cuco':>8}  {'warpcore':>8}    cuco/gpurhh  wc/gpurhh\")\n    gp = mean_by(fname, \"gpurhh\")\n    cu = mean_by(fname, \"cuco\")\n    wc = mean_by(fname, \"warpcore\")\n    factors = sorted(set(gp)|set(cu)|set(wc))\n    for f in factors:\n        def cell(d, k): return f\"{d[k]/1e6:8.1f}\" if k in d else \"      --\"\n        ratio_c = f\"{cu[f]/gp[f]:5.2f}x\" if f in cu and f in gp else \"   --\"\n        ratio_w = f\"{wc[f]/gp[f]:5.2f}x\" if f in wc and f in gp else \"   --\"\n        print(f\"{f:>5.2f}  {cell(gp,f)}  {cell(cu,f)}  {cell(wc,f)}      {ratio_c}     {ratio_w}\")\n    print()\nPY",
  "description": "ops/sec across libraries"
}
```

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:10:56.667Z

OK, the picture is much clearer with ops/sec. Honest summary:

## At F=0.5 (only overlap of all three)

| Workload | gpurhh | cuco | warpcore |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| Insert (B ops/sec) | 2.76 | **3.67** (1.33×) | 2.80 (1.01×) |
| Get (B ops/sec) | 5.41 | **7.14** (1.32×) | 5.73 (1.06×) |

cuco is **~32% faster** on both workloads in raw ops/sec. WarpCore is roughly even on insert, ~6% faster on get.

## What this means

The "% of memcpy peak" framing isn't fair across libraries with different probe sizes. cuco's default `static_map` is non-bucketed linear probing — each probe is one 8-byte slot, and adjacent threads in a warp share cache lines, so cuco's *actual* DRAM bytes per logical op are far below the 128 B that the `gbps_floor` formula assumes. That's why its floor metric came out at 113% of memcpy peak — the floor formula was wrong for cuco's design.

In **bytes-per-op** terms, cuco genuinely moves less DRAM per insert/get than gpurhh does. In **bytes-per-second** terms, both libraries are limited by other things (atomic contention for insert, kernel-launch overhead for get, etc.) — not pure DRAM throughput. The gpurhh "we hit 96% of memcpy peak" claim from earlier was real but specific to gpurhh's `gbps_corrected` metric (which used measured probe counts). It's not a "we beat cuco at bandwidth" claim, and we shouldn't have been comparing the two as if it were.

## What gpurhh is paying for

The ~32% gap reflects what Robin Hood adds vs. plain linear probing:

- **Per-probe work**: extra arithmetic to compute resident probe distances, the displacement-chain logic, the early-exit conditions on get.
- **CAS pressure**: a successful insert can trigger multiple displacement-chain CASs. cuco's plain linear probe does one CAS per insert on the empty slot it finds.
- **Reduction support**: `sum_op` adds a branch in the insert hot path even when the reduction is never actually fired (most uniform-random inserts).

The Robin Hood pitch is **probe distance variance and worst-case bound**, not raw throughput. At F ≤ 0.95 with uniform-random keys, linear probing's average probe count is fine and cuco gets to skip all our extra logic. The win arrives only when load factor pushes the worst case.

## Where gpurhh actually wins

cuco's `static_map` is **fixed-capacity** and physically cannot run F > 0.95. gpurhh keeps running and the workload changes meaningfully:

| F | gpurhh insert (B ops/sec) | gpurhh get (B ops/sec) |
| --- | ---: | ---: |
| 0.5 | 2.76 | 5.41 |
| 1.0 | 2.74 | 5.36 |
| 1.5 | 2.70 | 5.20 |
| 2.0 | 2.56 | 4.86 |
| 3.0 | **1.43** | **1.78** |

Up through F=2.0 (≈86% effective occupancy with α=1), the degradation is < 10% — that's the Robin Hood claim landing. At F=3.0 (~95% occupancy) there's a cliff, which is where the probe-cap-8 starts triggering failures and the workload is partly "displacements that go nowhere."

## Net read

- **Pure throughput at moderate load factor**: cuco wins clearly. gpurhh trails by ~30%.
- **Graceful degradation past capacity / dropping behavior**: only gpurhh attempts it; cuco simply can't run those configs.
- **Bandwidth saturation as a design goal**: validated for gpurhh's get at high F (the 96% of peak result from before, via the corrected metric). Doesn't translate into a head-to-head throughput win because cuco's per-op design is leaner.

This is a real and useful finding: gpurhh's Robin Hood machinery costs ~30% throughput vs cuco's linear probe in the regime cuco supports, and earns its keep in the regime cuco doesn't. Worth being honest about in the design doc, when we get to writing the results section.

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:12:41.427Z

Yes, basically — with one important refinement: "worst case" actually splits into a few different scenarios, and Robin Hood's wins aren't all in the same place.

## What the benchmark caught

Our workload is the **ideal case for linear probing**:

- Uniform-random keys → no primary clustering pathology.
- Load factor F ≤ 0.95 → linear probing's probe-length explosion hasn't kicked in yet.
- Measuring average throughput → no tail-latency signal.
- Fixed-capacity test → no graceful-overflow comparison.

Linear probing's per-op cost is structurally smaller (no probe-distance computation, no displacement chain, no early-exit logic), so in this regime it wins ~30%. That's a real cost of Robin Hood — its constant-factor overhead is paid every op, not just the contended ones.

## Where Robin Hood's value lives

In rough order of how visible it would be on a benchmark:

1. **Tail latency / probe-distance variance.** Robin Hood's whole pitch is "the worst probe length isn't far above the average." Linear probing's worst case at LF = 0.9 is dramatically worse than its mean. For p99 / p999 / max latency, RH wins; for mean, linear wins. Our benchmark only measures mean (averaged over millions of ops per rep) and so misses this entirely.
2. **Adversarial or skewed keys.** Linear probing's "primary clustering" exists because adjacent slots reuse each other's lookup work — great when keys are uniformly distributed, terrible when keys hash to clustered home positions. Robin Hood's invariant keeps the layout balanced regardless of input distribution. Real workloads with Zipfian access or with a hash function that mixes poorly look very different here.
3. **Very high load factor.** Linear probing's mean probe length grows like 1/(1-α)² — quadratic in remaining-capacity. Robin Hood's grows like log(1/(1-α)) — much shallower curve. At F = 0.98 the two would diverge noticeably; we never get to test this because cuco's `static_map` is fixed-capacity at exactly F ≤ 1.
4. **Behavior under overflow.** gpurhh has a probe cap and returns leftovers via the optional; the caller decides what to do. Linear probing in a fixed-capacity table at F → 1 just hangs (or rebuilds, depending on implementation). At F = 3 (force overflow) only gpurhh is even attempting to do useful work.

## What our benchmark didn't isolate

The 32% gap isn't a clean "cost of Robin Hood probing" measurement, because cuco and gpurhh differ in more than just probing scheme:

- **Slot layout**: gpurhh's bucketed (16 slots/cache line, all read at once) vs. cuco's non-bucketed (1 slot/probe).
- **Reduction operator**: gpurhh's `sum_op` adds an extra branch in the hot path that fires rarely on uniform keys but is paid every op.
- **Kernel launch shape**: cuco's bulk API picks its own; gpurhh's is parameterized.
- **Counter instrumentation**: ours has the lane-0-counter branch under `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS`, cuco doesn't (and we left those columns empty for cuco).

The ~30% could plausibly be half RH overhead and half "we instrument more / dispatch more per op." Pure-RH-cost-of-probing would need a fairer comparison kernel, which is more work than warrants right now.

## Net read for your design choice

If gpurhh's target audience is "people who care about tail latency and behavior near or past capacity," the Robin Hood machinery earns its keep. If the target is "fastest possible uniform-random insert/get throughput on a fixed-capacity table at LF ≤ 0.9," our benchmark says cuco wins and that's where to go.

The honest design-doc summary: **Robin Hood costs constant factor overhead; pays it back in worst-case bounds, tail behavior, and high-load resilience. None of those show up in mean-ops-per-second on uniform random keys** — that benchmark is the one Robin Hood designs *agreed* to lose, in exchange for the guarantees that matter on workloads we didn't test.

If at the end, the whole computation keeps waiting. Where it lands is random, but it seems unlikely it should just get amoritized out by running many keys. What do you think? ## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:16:54.130Z

<em>Thinking…</em>

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:17:18.760Z

Let me push back on the placement intuition first, then the bigger CPU vs GPU question.

## Placement within a tile's work — actually doesn't matter

Your premise was that if a long probe lands "at the beginning" of a tile's grid-stride loop it gets hidden, and "at the end" stalls the kernel. The grid-stride structure means each tile's total time is `sum(time_per_op_i)` over the ~2000 ops it processes — addition is commutative, so when the tail-op happens inside that sum doesn't affect the tile's total. The kernel finishes when the slowest tile finishes, and the slowest tile is whichever one got the worst luck across its 2000 ops.

So the right framing is: did any tile get hit by enough tail-ops to push its sum above the others'? With ~130K tiles × ~2000 ops each, that's a `max-of-130K-means` problem. By CLT, the max is only a few standard deviations above the mean of `2000 × mean_op_time` — and that standard deviation shrinks like 1/√2000. So tail latency at the per-op level *does* mostly amortize on GPU, not because of "hiding" but because each tile sees so many ops.

## Why GPU should care about tail latency less than CPU

Concrete reasons, in order of how clean the argument is:

1. **GPUs are structurally batch processors.** A typical CPU hash table call is `map.get(k)` — one key, response time = single-op latency. The tail of the single-op distribution *is* what the user feels. A typical GPU call is `bulk_get(keys, n)` with `n` in the millions — wall time = sum-of-ops within the slowest tile. Mean per-op dominates almost everything else.

2. **Cooperative-tile probing already eats one source of variance.** With our design (16 lanes cooperating on one op), within-warp probe-distance variance is *zero* — every lane is on the same probe walk, finishing together. If we used "one thread per op" (which is the analog of a CPU thread doing one lookup), then 32 ops with different probe distances would execute in SIMT lockstep and the warp would wait for the worst lane. *That's* where tail variance bites GPUs hard, and it's exactly the design we deliberately rejected. We pre-paid the variance-reduction cost in the launch shape, so Robin Hood's variance-reduction job has less marginal benefit here than it would in a thread-per-op design.

3. **Per-op tail is bounded already by `MaxProbeBuckets`.** With cap-8, no single op probes more than 128 slot positions. Linear probing in cuco doesn't have this cap (it has no probe cap — it just probes until empty), so cuco's tail IS unbounded in principle even though its mean is low. We don't see it in the benchmark because uniform-random keys at F ≤ 0.95 keep the tail well-behaved, but in adversarial conditions cuco's worst case can be very long while ours can't exceed cap-8.

## Where GPU tail latency DOES bite

For completeness:

- **Small batches.** If your kernel does 100 ops, one tail-op is 1% of work — visible. As batch shrinks toward 1, the GPU degenerates toward CPU semantics.
- **Heterogeneous workloads** where different ops have very different costs and SIMT is exposed. Not our case.
- **Latency-critical interactive paths.** Inference systems sometimes do "one query per kernel" for low latency; tail matters there. Not our benchmark setup.

## What this means for the design's claims

Honest read: the GPU + cooperative-tile design choice we already made eats most of the GPU-specific tail-latency benefit Robin Hood could have provided. The Robin Hood machinery is still doing real work — bounded worst case, graceful overflow, even mean probe length at high LF — but the *specific* "tail latency variance" axis is less visible on GPU than on CPU, exactly because the GPU machinery (SIMT cooperation, large batches) already does some of that variance smoothing for free.

So the cleanest design-doc framing might be: "Robin Hood's GPU benefit is **resilience at high load factor and under adversarial keys**, not tail latency per se. The cooperative-tile structure already handles the within-warp variance that Robin Hood would otherwise be eating on CPU. The remaining wins are about the *shape* of the degradation curve at F → 1, not the variance at moderate F."

That's the workload our benchmark should be hitting next, if we want to actually show Robin Hood paying off: either push F past 0.95 with cuco somehow (use `dynamic_map` which resizes?), or test with skewed/adversarial keys.

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:21:56.142Z

<em>Thinking…</em>

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:22:18.496Z

You're right, I oversimplified. The correct picture has more layers.

## The actual GPU scheduling model

On a 4090 with 128 SMs, each SM can hold roughly 8 resident blocks simultaneously, so ~1024 blocks run "in parallel" at any moment. With our 8K-block launch, only 1/8 of the work is active at once; the rest queues. The scheduler is greedy — whenever a block completes, the freed SM slot grabs the next queued block. So the kernel's actual execution looks like ~1024 *streams* of blocks, each stream processing 8-ish blocks sequentially on its slot.

The kernel ends when the last block finishes, which is **max over streams of (sum of block times in that stream)**. Not max over tiles.

## Where tail latency lands in this model

Each tile sees ~125 ops (grid-stride). Each block has 16 tiles running concurrently (different warps, same SM). Each stream sees ~8 blocks sequentially. There are ~1024 streams.

Stacked tail-smoothing:

1. **125 ops per tile** — a tail-op's effect is ~1/125 of the tile's sum, well-averaged.
2. **16 tiles per block** — block time is max over 16 tile-sums, so within-block worst-case is a few σ above tile mean.
3. **8 blocks per stream** — stream time is sum of 8 block maxes. Adds 8 block-time-maxes; variance shrinks by √8 relative to a single block.
4. **Max over 1024 streams** — the kernel waits for the slowest stream, which sits ~√(2 ln 1024) ≈ 3.7 σ above the mean stream time.

So *yes*, a tail-op contributes to kernel wall time — but the contribution is smoothed by ~√(125 × 8) ≈ 31× relative to its raw size, then re-amplified by the max-over-1024-streams factor of ~3.7. Net effect: a single Δt tail-op in our setup might add maybe Δt / 8 to the kernel time, not the full Δt.

And critically: **our `MaxProbeBuckets = 8` caps Δt absolutely.** No probe can be more than 8 cache-line reads, so the per-op upper bound is bounded by design. Linear probing without a cap (cuco's `static_map`) has no such bound — its Δt is in principle the entire table walk. Our benchmark workload (uniform random keys at F ≤ 0.95) keeps cuco's actual tail tame, but the *guarantee* is something only we have.

## Revised CPU vs GPU framing

The clean reason GPU tail concern is structurally lower than CPU's is this stack of variance-reducing layers, which don't exist on CPU:

| | CPU (single-thread batch) | GPU (our cooperative-tile design) |
|---|---|---|
| Op-level variance | direct | absorbed into a tile-sum of ~125 ops |
| Per-thread variance | direct | absorbed into a per-block max of 16 tiles |
| Per-block variance | direct | absorbed into a per-stream sum of ~8 blocks |
| Parallelism scaling | sequential | max-of-N-streams cost grows like √log(N) |

CPU's "tail of one op = visible latency" only collapses to GPU's "tail of one op = √(125 × 8) fraction of visible latency" because we have so many ops, so many tiles, so many streams.

## Where this leaves Robin Hood

Robin Hood's value on GPU isn't "shrink the tail of individual ops in a way that shows up in kernel time," because the scheduling cascade already does most of that smoothing. It's:

1. **Bound the worst case at all** (the cap exists, the leftover is returned losslessly). Linear probing without a cap has no guarantee.
2. **Keep degradation graceful as F → 1.** Linear probing's mean probe length grows like 1/(1-α)² while RH's is ~log; the difference is small at F = 0.85 (our cuco-comparison ceiling) but large at F = 0.99.
3. **Resist adversarial / skewed input distributions.** Even on GPU, if a hash function clusters keys, linear probing's primary clustering hurts mean probe length, not just variance. RH stays balanced.

So you're right that we picked a design good for the wrong axis of the benchmark — the tail-latency-on-GPU axis is structurally smoothed by the layers above. The Robin-Hood-pays-off scenarios are *high LF* and *bad-input-distribution* scenarios, neither of which our current workload hits.

## User — 2026-05-19T02:23:53.558Z

Skewed input distribution - this would only show up if, somehow, the input keys don't get hashed to something approximately uniform?

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:24:26.162Z

<em>Thinking…</em>

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:24:45.735Z

Yes, you've spotted a real over-claim of mine. Let me be more honest about it.

## The hash function eats most of the skew

With a high-quality hash like `fmix32` (or `splitmix64` for 64-bit), the empirical input distribution is essentially irrelevant to the *home bucket* distribution. fmix32 has strong avalanche — flipping one input bit changes ~50% of output bits — so any input distribution gets mixed into a uniform-looking distribution at the bucket level. Even structured inputs like sequential integers, ASCII strings, or pointer-aligned addresses come out looking uniform after the mix.

So the clustering that remains in our setup is **birthday-paradox finite-sample clustering**, not input-driven clustering. That's exactly what "uniform random keys" simulates — N independent uniform draws into K buckets, with the natural Poisson-distributed bucket counts. Robin Hood's variance benefit at moderate F is dealing with that natural finite-sample noise, which our benchmark already exercises.

In other words: the benchmark workload we're already running is the *right* one for testing how RH handles realistic clustering, *assuming the hash function is good*. Which fmix32 is.

## Where the "adversarial input" framing actually holds

Three narrow scenarios:

1. **User supplies a bad custom hash.** Identity hash, weak mixer, hash that ignores high bits. Out of our control — we publish `default_hash` and recommend it. If users pick something worse, RH protects them. But that's a "we make user mistakes less painful" story, not a "we beat cuco on real workloads."

2. **Hash-collision adversary.** `fmix32` is *unkeyed* and a known function. An attacker who knows it can construct N keys whose `fmix32(k) mod 2^27` all collide. They'd dump all those keys into one home bucket, defeating linear probing entirely and stressing even RH. Real security-relevant for things like network hash maps facing untrusted input; not a concern for typical numerical / scientific GPU workloads.

3. **Pre-hashed inputs that bypass our hasher.** If a caller has keys that are *already* hash outputs from a different hash with worse mixing, our `default_hash` re-mixing helps less. Rare.

For typical use — scientific workloads, ML embeddings, joins, group-bys, etc. — the hash function genuinely solves the input-skew problem and our benchmark workload reflects the realistic case.

## What's left as Robin Hood's actual advantage

Stripping the over-claim, the honest list:

1. **Behavior at very high load factor.** Linear probing's mean probe count goes like 1/(1−α)² — explodes near α = 1. Robin Hood's stays manageable until very close to saturation. Our benchmark *should* show this if we got past F = 0.95, but cuco's `static_map` won't run there.

2. **Bounded worst case per op.** With the cap-8 budget, no single op can exceed 128 slot positions of probing. cuco's `static_map` has no such cap — it'll keep probing. Matters for absolute time guarantees (real-time systems) or for the lossy-storage workloads you mentioned earlier (drop a bounded fraction, never block).

3. **Lossless overflow.** `View::insert` returns the leftover slot when it gives up; cuco silently fails (or hangs, depending on capacity). Matters when callers want to rebuild rather than silently lose data.

That's it. The "adversarial inputs" angle was me reaching — with a good hash, it doesn't apply to typical workloads. The actual case for Robin Hood on GPU is **what happens past F = 0.95**, which our benchmark and our baselines can't directly compare.

## User — 2026-05-19T02:26:30.484Z

Why won't cuco still run?

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:27:15.628Z

<em>Thinking…</em>

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:27:34.369Z

You're right to push back. "Won't run" was overstated. Let me be precise about what actually happens.

## What cuco does past F = 0.95

cuco's `static_map` doesn't have a hard load-factor limit baked in — the 0.95 cap is a *benchmark-script* choice I made, not a cuco constraint. The actual failure modes:

- **0.95 < F < 1.0**: still runs, but slowly. With linear probing, mean probe length is ~1/(1−α)². At α = 0.95 it's ~400 probes per op; at α = 0.99 it's ~10000. The kernel finishes but takes orders of magnitude longer per op.
- **F ≈ 1.0**: the last few inserts have to probe nearly the entire table to find the one or two remaining empty slots. Throughput collapses but the kernel still terminates.
- **F > 1.0**: there's no slot for some inserts. cuco's `insert_async` walks the probe sequence; without an empty slot anywhere, it would loop indefinitely unless cuco's implementation detects "we've probed all slots, give up" and bails. I'd have to read the source to know exactly — most modern implementations have some form of cycle detection or step cap, but the exact behavior at F > 1 is implementation-dependent.

## Why I capped at 0.95 anyway

Two practical reasons rather than "cuco refuses":

1. **Apples-to-apples regime.** gpurhh runs F up to 3 by *attempting* 3× capacity worth of inserts and *dropping* ~0.1% via the probe cap. cuco running at F = 1.05 would try to insert 5% more items than it has slots, with no probe cap — either it loops, or it silently drops, but in either case the comparison isn't matched: cuco is doing a structurally different thing in the "overfull" regime.

2. **Pathological runtimes.** At F ≥ 0.99, cuco's per-op time grows to thousands of probes. A single benchmark cell might take 10s of minutes instead of seconds. Doesn't break anything, but the sweep time blows up.

## What would actually be informative

Three honest options for a high-F cuco comparison, in order of cleanness:

1. **Run cuco at F ∈ {0.97, 0.98, 0.99}** — confirms the predicted 1/(1−α)² explosion in mean probe length and shows the slope at which throughput collapses. We can compare gpurhh's gentle degradation directly against cuco's cliff. Adds ~30 minutes of sweep time per F.

2. **Use cuco's `dynamic_map`** for the F > 1 regime — this is the cuco type *designed* for overflow, with automatic resize. Different perf characteristics (each resize is expensive) but actually answers "what does cuco do when you ask it to hold more than its capacity." Closer to gpurhh's "rebuild on leftover" pattern in spirit.

3. **Test cuco at matched memory budget instead of matched capacity** — give cuco enough slots to comfortably fit n_ops at F = 0.7 (say). Then "load factor" on cuco's side is 0.7, on gpurhh's side is 3.0, but both are using the same total memory. Cleaner apples-to-apples for "what's the throughput at 1 GiB budget when you need to fit N items?", at the cost of giving cuco a built-in advantage.

I'd lean toward (1) as the first follow-up — it tests cuco in its own comfort zone (still fixed capacity, no overflow), just close to the limit, and the comparison stays meaningful. Worth a separate sweep if you want to see the cliff before deciding what to publish.

Sound right? If yes I can adjust the cuco F grid in the script and you can run a focused high-F sweep.

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:29:29.230Z

Sharp cliff. Failures stay exactly zero up through F = 2.0, then jump to ~0.1% at F = 3.0:

| F | n_ops | mean failures | fail rate |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| 0.5 | 67M | 0 | 0 |
| 1.0 | 134M | 0 | 0 |
| 1.5 | 201M | 0 | 0 |
| 2.0 | 268M | 0 | 0 |
| 3.0 | 403M | ~387,000 | ~9.6 × 10⁻⁴ |

The right x-axis is **effective occupancy** (not input F), since with α = 1 and uniform random keys, collisions absorb work and the table doesn't fill linearly with input volume. The mapping is `occupancy ≈ 1 − exp(−F)`:

- F = 2.0 → occupancy ≈ 0.865
- F = 3.0 → occupancy ≈ 0.950
- F = 4.0 → occupancy ≈ 0.982 (we don't sweep this)

So the probe cap of 8 (≈ 128 slot positions) is fine up through **~86% occupancy** and starts failing somewhere between 86% and 95%. Worth noting: cuco's F = 0.95 comparison point — which we treated as the "high load" of its regime — corresponds to ~95% occupancy on cuco's side, comparable to gpurhh's F = 3.0 on occupancy. We just got there via different definitions of "load factor."

For a comparison that's actually aligned on physical density, **gpurhh at F = 3.0 vs cuco at F = 0.95** is the right pair — and that's where gpurhh starts to pay its tax (0.1% failures, the F = 3 cliff in throughput we saw earlier).

So the honest picture: with cap-8, gpurhh comfortably handles up to ~86% occupancy with zero failures. Past that, you trade lossless behavior for graceful throughput degradation, and the cliff lands somewhere around 90%. If we wanted true lossless behavior at 95% occupancy and beyond, raising `MaxProbeBuckets` to 16 or 32 would push the failure floor up further (at the cost of a worse worst-case latency bound).

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:31:04.694Z

Recap and a correction — I was sloppy about cuco's behavior earlier.

## F

`F = n_ops / capacity` — the ratio of *attempted* inserts to total slot count. With `--capacity-bytes 1 GiB` and 8-byte slots, `capacity = 2^27 ≈ 134M`. At F = 0.5 we attempt 67M inserts; at F = 3.0 we attempt 403M.

F is **input volume**, not table occupancy. Keys are drawn from `Uniform(0, capacity)` (α = 1), so with finitely many distinct values, many inserts have duplicate keys. The mapping between attempts and slots-actually-filled is:

```
occupancy ≈ 1 − exp(−F)
```

→ F = 1.0 fills ~63% of slots, F = 2.0 fills ~86%, F = 3.0 fills ~95%. We never literally exceed capacity in terms of distinct keys, no matter how high F goes.

## Why cuco doesn't fundamentally need F ≤ 1

You were right to question this — my earlier claim was wrong. cuco *can* run at F > 1 in our setup. Here's the actual story:

Both libraries handle duplicate-key attempts:
- **gpurhh** with `sum_op` merges duplicates into the existing slot via the reduction.
- **cuco `static_map`** treats duplicates as no-ops (first writer wins; subsequent inserts of the same key find the matching slot and skip).

Distinct keys are bounded by the draw range (= capacity for α = 1), so the *number of slots actually consumed* never exceeds capacity, regardless of F. There's no capacity overflow.

What I was actually worried about was **throughput collapse**, not overflow. cuco's `static_map` uses linear probing with no probe cap. Mean probe length grows as ~1/(1−occupancy). For our occupancies:

| F | occupancy | linear mean probe |
| --- | ---: | ---: |
| 0.5 | 0.39 | ~1.6 |
| 0.95 | 0.61 | ~2.6 |
| 1.0 | 0.63 | ~2.7 |
| 2.0 | 0.865 | ~7.4 |
| 3.0 | 0.95 | ~20 |
| 4.0 | 0.98 | ~50 |

So cuco at F = 3 isn't "won't run" — it's "does ~8× more probe work per op than at F = 0.95, in addition to wasted work for duplicate-no-op probes." Slower but completes. Probably tractable for a benchmark sweep.

The script's `[cuco] --load-factor > 0.95 may overflow static_map capacity` warning is misleading. It won't overflow — it'll just slow down. I should fix that wording.

## Why gpurhh degrades less than cuco does at the same occupancy

- **Probe cap-8**: gpurhh's per-op probe is bounded absolutely. cuco's grows like 1/(1−occupancy).
- **Robin Hood**: tighter probe-distance distribution, so the mean grows like log(1/(1−occupancy)) instead of 1/(1−occupancy)².
- **Bucketed (16 slots/probe)**: each probe inspects 16 candidates at once, so we're doing fewer probe iterations to find a match.

At F = 0.95 occupancy (= gpurhh's F = 3.0), gpurhh's ~387K failures and 1.43 B ops/sec is its degradation profile. cuco at the same occupancy would have its own profile, with no probe cap but unbounded individual probe lengths.

## Worth running

We could extend cuco's F grid up to 2.0 or 3.0 — should add maybe ~15–30 minutes to a sweep. That would let us plot **throughput vs occupancy** with both libraries on the same axis up to 95% occupancy, which is the comparison we actually want. Want me to update the sweep grid + fix the misleading warning, and you can run that next?

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:34:30.479Z

Good catch. Let me enumerate them systematically. Marking each as **(A)** essential to fix for fair comparison, **(B)** worth knowing about, or **(C)** intrinsic to the comparison (this *is* the thing being measured).

## Per-op semantic asymmetries

**(A) Reduction operator on insert.** This is the one you flagged.
- gpurhh: `sum_op` — duplicate-key inserts add the new value into the existing slot via CAS. Costs an extra ALU op + a (possibly retrying) CAS per duplicate-key probe.
- cuco `static_map`: no reduction. First-writer-wins; duplicate-key inserts find the slot, see the key matches, no-op. No CAS, no add.
- warpcore `SingleValueHashTable`: same as cuco — first-writer-wins, duplicate inserts are no-ops.

So gpurhh is doing strictly more work per duplicate-key insert. With our `Uniform(0, capacity)` key distribution at F=2.0, the duplicate-key fraction is ~38% of attempts. That's a real load gpurhh is shouldering and the baselines aren't.

**Fix:** switch gpurhh's benchmark `Table` to use `replace_op` (or even drop the reduction template entirely and let it default to `replace_op`). Re-run the gpurhh sweep.

## Internal-design asymmetries that are part of the comparison

**(C) Probing scheme.** Robin Hood (gpurhh) vs linear probing (cuco) vs double hashing (warpcore). This *is* what we're trying to compare; not a confound.

**(C) Bucket size + cooperative-tile width.** gpurhh: 16-slot buckets, 16-thread tiles. cuco: 1-slot probes, 1-thread per op (I think). warpcore: 8-thread cooperative groups. These differ from each other and from gpurhh in ways that affect bytes/op and SIMT behavior — they're consequences of each library's design choices, not confounds.

## Asymmetries that probably move numbers a little

**(B) Hash function.** gpurhh uses `fmix32`. cuco's default is its own `cuco::default_hash_function` (a Murmur variant IIRC). warpcore uses `warpcore::hashers::MurmurHash`. All are good mixers; per-call cycle counts differ by a few ALU ops. Sub-percent on throughput likely.

**(B) Counter instrumentation in the hot loop.** gpurhh's kernel has `if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) ++tile_counter;` inside the probe loop under `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS`. cuco/warpcore don't pay this. Lane-0 branch + register inc per probe — small but real. **Fix:** for the comparison runs specifically, build gpurhh without `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` defined (i.e., omit that `#define` line from `benchmarks.cuh` for a comparison build). Or just acknowledge that gpurhh's reported numbers include this overhead and the baselines don't.

**(B) Value derivation pathway.** gpurhh and cuco compute `value = fmix32(key)` inline in the kernel (transform iterator for cuco). warpcore needs a pre-staged `d_values` buffer (its bulk API takes raw key + value pointers, not transform iterators), so we run a `derive_values` kernel beforehand AND warpcore's insert kernel does a second DRAM read of the values. That extra read isn't part of the kernel we time, but the *kernel itself* loads from `d_values` whereas the other two compute in-register. This penalizes warpcore by some small amount.

**(B) Pre-fill semantics for get.** Same hit-rate after pre-fill across all three (depends only on which keys made it, not on stored values), so the get benchmark itself is aligned. But the pre-fill *runtimes* differ in the same ways the insert benchmark's runtimes do; if anyone cares about pre-fill time, those numbers aren't apples-to-apples either.

## Asymmetries we already aligned

- **Capacity** (slots): identical across libraries. All three round our `2^27` to itself.
- **Slot byte size**: 8 bytes (uint32 + uint32) for all three.
- **Key range**: `Uniform(0, capacity)` for all three.
- **Stream**: all default stream.
- **Compute / sync model**: all bulk-call APIs from the host, all wait via `cudaEventSynchronize`.

## Recommended changes

In order of impact:

1. **Switch gpurhh to `replace_op`** for the baseline comparison sweep. This is the biggest legitimately-fixable asymmetry. We keep `sum_op` available for users that want reduction semantics, but the comparison benchmarks should use `replace_op`.
2. **Drop `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` for comparison runs.** Either build a counter-free variant, or move `gbps_corrected` reporting to a separate dedicated kernel and keep the comparison kernel clean.
3. Document the warpcore-specific extra `d_values` DRAM read; treat its number as conservatively slow.

Want me to apply (1) and (2)? After that we'd rerun the comparison sweep and the cuco/gpurhh gap should narrow somewhat — probably 5–15% of the 32%, leaving us with a more honest read of "Robin Hood + bucket + cooperative-tile + counter overhead vs. linear-probe-no-bucket."

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:41:38.088Z

Verified: **warpcore's default probing length is `~uint64(0) - 1024 ≈ 1.8×10¹⁹`** — effectively infinite. cuco's `insert_async` has no probing-length parameter at all (also uncapped). So yes, gpurhh at cap-8 is the only one walking with a cap, and removing it is the right fairness fix.

## Plan for the two-binary split

**Structure:**

```
benchmarks/
  benchmark_insert.cu              comparison version (no counters, replace_op, uncapped)
  benchmark_get.cu                 comparison version (no counters)
  benchmark_insert_diagnostic.cu   counter version (sum_op, cap-8, full CSV)
  benchmark_get_diagnostic.cu      counter version
  benchmarks.cuh                   shared infra — no longer defines GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
```

Each `_diagnostic.cu` defines `#define GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS 1` at the top before `#include "benchmarks.cuh"`; the comparison `.cu` files don't. CSV files split too: comparison writes to `insert.csv` / `get.csv` (matches cuco/warpcore schema, no probe/failure columns); diagnostic writes to `insert_diagnostic.csv` / `get_diagnostic.csv` with the full gpurhh-specific schema.

Code-sharing: I lean *against* aggressive header-sharing across the two variants — each binary is ~250 lines, the diagnostic is a strict superset of the comparison's needs, and a clean per-binary `main()` reads better than `#ifdef`'d shared body. Maintenance cost across 4 binaries is acceptable for this scope; if we ever add a 3rd variant we'd reconsider.

## Equalize occupancy as the cross-library axis

Right framing: **plot throughput vs effective occupancy, not vs F**.

Implementation: add `--key-range N` to each binary, defaulting to `capacity` (= α = 1 today). Each binary stays parameterized on F = n_ops / capacity, but the script picks (F, key_range) pairs that target specific effective occupancies. For α = 1 the mapping is `occupancy ≈ 1 − exp(−F)`; with α as a free knob the mapping is `α × (1 − exp(−F/α))`.

For the sweep, four target occupancies span the regime we care about: `{0.5, 0.7, 0.85, 0.95}`. Each library gets the (F, α) pair that hits its target — gpurhh and the baselines all run the same effective occupancy. We don't need exotic α values to start; α = 1 throughout is the cleanest first pass and matches what we already collected. We can introduce other α values later for "stress linear probing" experiments.

Two variants worth adding once the core comparison is solid:

- **Higher α (e.g., α = 4)**: key_range > capacity → fewer collisions → effective occupancy can reach 0.99+. This is the regime where linear probing's primary clustering really bites.
- **Lower α (e.g., α = 0.25)**: key_range < capacity → heavy collision rate → tests "many duplicate inserts" path. With replace_op vs no-op, this is fair; with sum_op (diagnostic), it stresses gpurhh harder.

## Uncap gpurhh for the comparison binary

`MaxProbeBuckets` is a non-type template parameter on `HashTable`. The comparison binary's `Table` typedef sets it to a value large enough that cap-never-triggers:

```cpp
// effectively uncapped for comparison runs
using Table = gpurhh::HashTable<
    std::uint32_t, std::uint32_t,
    gpurhh::default_hash<std::uint32_t>,
    gpurhh::default_empty_key<std::uint32_t>::key,
    gpurhh::replace_op,
    /*CacheLineBytes=*/128,
    /*WarpSize=*/32,
    /*MaxProbeBuckets=*/(1 << 20)>;
```

`1 << 20 = 1M buckets` is comfortably larger than `capacity / bucket_size` for any benchmark configuration we'd run. Cap never trips. The library compiles fine — `MaxProbeBuckets` is just a constant in the probe-loop bound.

The diagnostic binary keeps cap-8 (or whatever you want to study).

## Order of operations

1. Drop `#define GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` from `benchmarks.cuh`.
2. Edit existing `benchmark_insert.cu` / `benchmark_get.cu` to become the *comparison* versions: replace_op, MaxProbeBuckets=1<<20, no counters, lean CSV. Output goes to `insert.csv` / `get.csv` alongside cuco/warpcore rows.
3. Create `benchmark_insert_diagnostic.cu` / `benchmark_get_diagnostic.cu` from copies of what's currently there — full counters, sum_op, cap-8, separate CSV.
4. Add `--key-range` flag to all four (and to the cuco/warpcore binaries) — defaulting to `capacity`.
5. Update Makefile to discover the new diagnostic files automatically (wildcard already does).
6. Update `scripts/benchmark.sh`: the `--gpurhh` flag runs both comparison and diagnostic variants by default; document an option for "just one of them" if useful.

That gets us to apples-to-apples comparison runs + separate diagnostic data, and unblocks a follow-up sweep with broader cuco/warpcore F coverage (since they're not cap-limited).

Want me to execute? Step (2) is the biggest behavioral change — once that's done and re-sweeped, the gpurhh-vs-cuco gap should narrow meaningfully, and we'll have a much cleaner read on whether Robin Hood-with-bucketing's intrinsic per-op cost is high or low vs. linear-probing-without-bucketing.

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:45:24.838Z

Naming change noted — `_diagnostic` → `_counters`. On the parameterization, let me be explicit, because looking back I've been waving hands at three different things and calling all of them "load factor."

## What each variable means

| | Symbol / flag | Definition | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Input volume** | `F = n_ops / capacity` (CLI: `--load-factor`) | How many inserts we *attempt*, as a fraction of total slot count. | This is what the sweep iterates over. |
| **Key-range ratio** | `α = key_range / capacity` (CLI: `--key-range` ÷ capacity) | What range we draw keys from, as a fraction of capacity. Today fixed at 1. | Controls the collision rate at any given F. |
| **Effective occupancy** | `occ ≈ α × (1 − exp(−F/α))` | Fraction of slots actually populated after the inserts. | *Derived*, not specified. Reported in CSV. |
| **Capacity** | `--capacity-bytes` | Total table size in slots. Fixed at 1 GiB / 8 B = 2²⁷ slots. | Fixed across the sweep. |
| **Block size** | `--block-size` | CUDA launch block size for gpurhh kernels. | Only meaningful for gpurhh; cuco/warpcore pick their own internally. |
| **Reps/warmups/seed** | — | Run repetition controls. | Fixed. |

## What gets swept in the comparison

For the **comparison** binaries (the apples-to-apples one):

- **Swept**: F. We use `F ∈ {0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0}` for *both* gpurhh and the baselines. (Right now cuco/warpcore stop at F=0.95 — that was my over-conservative choice; should expand to match gpurhh once we uncap and verify the baselines complete.)
- **Fixed**: α = 1, capacity = 1 GiB, seed, warmups, reps.
- **Per-library**: gpurhh also sweeps block_size; cuco/warpcore don't expose one.
- **Reported per row**: ops/sec, gbps_floor, plus the *derived* effective occupancy (so the plotting layer can put it on a common axis).

Since α is fixed at 1, F and effective occupancy are one-to-one: `occ = 1 − exp(−F)`. So `F = 2.0` always means `occ ≈ 86%` for any library. The reason occupancy matters as a column is just so the plot's x-axis is comparable across configurations — if we later run a sweep with `α = 4`, the F=2 column there means a *different* occupancy, and we'd want to compare on the occupancy axis, not the F axis.

## What gets swept in the counters binaries

Same as comparison, but the CSV gains `total_probes`, `total_failures`, `gbps_corrected`, and we keep `MaxProbeBuckets = 8` to study the probe-cap behavior. Reduction operator stays `sum_op` here so the diagnostic still exercises the reduction path (which is part of the design under study).

## Possible follow-on sweeps (separate from the main comparison)

These vary α and would be added later:

- **α > 1** (e.g., 2 or 4): same F, but more key-space → fewer collisions → higher effective occupancy. Punishes linear probing's primary clustering.
- **α < 1** (e.g., 0.25): tiny key-space → many duplicates → stresses no-op / reduction paths heavily. Hits cuco harder than gpurhh-with-replace_op because cuco still has to walk to the matching key for every duplicate.

These would *not* run in the default comparison sweep — they're targeted "stress tests" we'd opt into.

## So: clean answer

The thing you sweep is **F**. Everything else (occupancy, ops/sec, bandwidth) is reported but derived. α and capacity are fixed at "α = 1, 1 GiB" unless we explicitly opt into a follow-on stress sweep.

Does that match your mental model, or is there still something unclear?

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:47:58.492Z

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:48:17.861Z

You're right — this is cleaner. Let me confirm everything we care about derives from those three raw quantities.

## Three knobs, everything else derived

Inputs (raw, no implicit math):
- `cap` — table capacity (slots, or bytes / slot_bytes)
- `N` — upper bound of the Uniform draws (call this `key_range` in code)
- `n_ops` — number of draws to attempt

Workload metrics that fall out:

| Metric | Formula | Why we care |
| --- | --- | --- |
| input load factor (was F) | `n_ops / cap` | Ratio of attempts to slots; reported in CSV |
| key-range ratio (was α) | `N / cap` | Tells you the density of the input keyspace |
| expected distinct keys | `N × (1 − exp(−n_ops/N))` | How many slots get populated (with replace_op or no-op on duplicates) |
| expected occupancy | `[N × (1 − exp(−n_ops/N))] / cap` | The cross-library comparison axis |
| expected hit rate (for get) | ≈ expected occupancy | When get keys are drawn from same Uniform(0,N) |
| mean probe length (RH) | ≈ ln(1/(1 − occupancy)) | Theoretical expectation |
| mean probe length (linear) | ≈ 1/(1 − occupancy) | Theoretical expectation |

Everything plot-axis-worthy comes out of `{cap, N, n_ops}` plus measured `time_ms`. No CLI flag should need to be a ratio or a derived quantity.

## Things that *aren't* derivable (legitimately separate flags)

- `block_size` (gpurhh only — launch-shape knob, orthogonal to the workload).
- `seed` (insert_seed + get_seed for get binary).
- `warmups`, `reps` (run-control).

These stay as their own flags because they're not workload definitions.

## Proposed CLI shape

```
benchmark_insert         --capacity-bytes N           (or --capacity in slots)
                         --key-range N                ← new, was implicit (= capacity)
                         --n-ops N                    ← new, replaces --load-factor
                         --block-size N
                         --warmups N --reps N
                         --seed N
                         --tag STR
                         --output-dir DIR

benchmark_get            ... same plus --insert-seed / --get-seed
```

`--load-factor` goes away entirely. The CSV still reports the derived ratios (`load_factor`, `alpha`, `expected_occupancy`) as columns for plotting convenience, but they're computed by the binary, not parameters of it.

## Script side

`scripts/benchmark.sh` keeps using ergonomic multipliers internally (where humans think in terms of "0.5× capacity worth of inserts") but passes raw `n_ops` to the binaries:

```bash
CAPACITY_BYTES=$((1024 * 1024 * 1024))
SLOT_BYTES=8
CAPACITY_SLOTS=$(( CAPACITY_BYTES / SLOT_BYTES ))
KEY_RANGE=$CAPACITY_SLOTS                    # default α = 1

for mul in 0.5 1.0 1.5 2.0 3.0; do
    n_ops=$(python3 -c "print(int($mul * $CAPACITY_SLOTS))")
    "${BIN}" --capacity-bytes $CAPACITY_BYTES \
             --key-range $KEY_RANGE \
             --n-ops $n_ops \
             ...
done
```

That keeps the human-facing sweep grid in familiar multiplier form while making the binary itself agnostic to ratios.

## Plan

1. Rename `_diagnostic` → `_counters` in the file naming you mentioned earlier.
2. Switch all four gpurhh-side binaries (comparison + counters, for insert + get) plus the cuco/warpcore binaries to the new `{--capacity-bytes, --key-range, --n-ops}` parameterization. Drop `--load-factor`.
3. CSV keeps reporting `load_factor`, `alpha`, `expected_occupancy` as derived columns — same plotting workflow.
4. `scripts/benchmark.sh` switches to computing `n_ops` from multipliers internally and passing raw values.
5. While at it, do the `replace_op` + uncapped `MaxProbeBuckets` switch for the comparison binary.

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:51:06.849Z

## Assistant — 2026-05-19T02:51:24.470Z

Good call — same units for all three makes the raw-variable picture cleaner. So:

- **`capacity`** — number of slots in the table.
- **`key_range`** — the N in Uniform(0, N).
- **`n_ops`** — number of draws to attempt.

All counts. The CLI changes from `--capacity-bytes N` to `--capacity N`; if the user wants to think in bytes they divide by `slot_bytes` themselves (or we add `--capacity-bytes` as a convenience alias that converts).

## Definitions of derived variables

Every metric in our analysis is expressible from these three (plus measurements):

| Variable | Definition | What it tells you |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `load_factor` | `n_ops / capacity` | Old "F". Pure ratio — how many inserts attempted per slot. |
| `alpha` | `key_range / capacity` | Old "α". Pure ratio — how "wide" the key space is vs the table. |
| `expected_distinct_keys` | `key_range × (1 − exp(−n_ops/key_range))` | The number of *unique* values you actually drew (birthday-paradox formula). |
| `expected_occupancy` | `expected_distinct_keys / capacity` | Fraction of slots that end up populated. Equivalent: `alpha × (1 − exp(−load_factor/alpha))`. |
| `expected_hit_rate` | `1 − exp(−n_ops/key_range)` | For the get benchmark, probability that a fresh draw lands on a key already in the table. Equals `expected_distinct_keys / key_range`. Doesn't depend on capacity. |

A couple of library-side constants come along for the ride and are also worth reporting (they're fixed by the type instantiation, not derived from the three raw vars):

| Variable | Definition | What it tells you |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `slot_bytes` | `sizeof(Slot)` | 8 for our `<uint32, uint32>` instantiation. |
| `bytes_per_op` | `sizeof(Bucket)` for gpurhh, 128 across the comparison | Reference value for `gbps_floor = n_ops × bytes_per_op / time`. |
| `capacity_bytes` | `capacity × slot_bytes` | Just for memory-context reporting. |

The handy invariants:

- `load_factor` and `alpha` are pure-ratio names for `n_ops/capacity` and `key_range/capacity`. They don't introduce new information; they re-package the raw triple in normalized form.
- For α = 1 (our current sweep), `expected_occupancy = 1 − exp(−load_factor)` and `expected_hit_rate = 1 − exp(−load_factor)` — same value. That's just the special case where they collapse.
- For α ≠ 1: occupancy and hit_rate diverge. Hit rate is purely a function of `n_ops / key_range`. Occupancy is hit rate × `key_range/capacity`.

## Proposed CSV schema

Columns roughly ordered identification → raw workload → library constants → derived → measurements → counter-binary extras:

```
library, workload, tag, rep, seed, get_seed,
capacity, key_range, n_ops, block_size,
slot_bytes, capacity_bytes, bytes_per_op,
load_factor, alpha, expected_occupancy, expected_hit_rate,
time_ms, ops_per_sec, gbps_floor,
gbps_corrected, total_probes, total_hits, total_misses, total_failures
```

The first four sections are identical across all binaries (gpurhh comparison, gpurhh counters, cuco, warpcore). The last row (`gbps_corrected` through `total_failures`) is filled only by the gpurhh counters binary; everyone else leaves those cells empty. `get_seed` is filled only by the get binaries; insert binaries leave it empty. `block_size` is filled only by gpurhh (cuco/warpcore have no exposed block size — leave it empty for them).

That gives one CSV schema across libraries, all the raw variables explicit, and the derived metrics ready for plotting without the analysis script having to re-derive them.

Sound right? If so, I'll implement.
