<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/benchmarks/timing/baselines/warpcore/benchmark_insert.cu",
  "old_string": "    // d_keys: refreshed per rep in setup.\n    // d_values: zeroed once below. Apples-to-apples with gpurhh / cuco\n    // — the warpcore insert kernel pays the d_values input read, and\n    // under replace_op semantics the value content is irrelevant.\n    Key*   d_keys   = nullptr;\n    Value* d_values = nullptr;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_keys,   args.n_ops * sizeof(Key))   >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_values, args.n_ops * sizeof(Value)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMemset(d_values, 0, args.n_ops * sizeof(Value)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    UniformKeyGenerator gen(args.seed);\n\n    Table table(args.capacity);\n\n    Recorder rec(args.output_dir / \"insert.csv\",\n        \"library,workload,tag,rep,seed,capacity,n_ops,block_size,\"\n        \"slot_bytes,bytes_per_op,time_ms\");\n\n    EventTimer timer;\n\n    run_benchmark_loop(args.warmups, args.reps, timer,\n        /*setup=*/  [&]() {\n            table.init();\n            fill_uniform_keys(d_keys, args.n_ops, gen);\n        },\n        /*launch=*/ [&]() { table.insert(d_keys, d_values, args.n_ops); },\n        /*after=*/  [&](int rep, float ms) {\n            rec.write_row(format_row(\n                args.capacity, args.n_ops,\n                slot_bytes, bytes_per_op,\n                args.seed, rep, args.tag, ms));\n        });\n\n    cudaFree(d_keys);\n    cudaFree(d_values);\n    return 0;\n}",
  "new_string": "    // d_keys:   refreshed per rep in setup.\n    // d_values: zeroed once below. Apples-to-apples with gpurhh / cuco\n    //           — the warpcore insert kernel pays the d_values input\n    //           read, and under replace_op semantics the value content\n    //           is irrelevant.\n    // d_sorted: scratch copy of d_keys for sort + unique drop counting.\n    Key*   d_keys   = nullptr;\n    Value* d_values = nullptr;\n    Key*   d_sorted = nullptr;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_keys,   args.n_ops * sizeof(Key))   >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_values, args.n_ops * sizeof(Value)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMalloc(&d_sorted, args.n_ops * sizeof(Key))   >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    cudaMemset(d_values, 0, args.n_ops * sizeof(Value)) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n    UniformKeyGenerator gen(args.seed);\n\n    Table table(args.capacity);\n\n    Recorder rec(args.output_dir / \"insert.csv\",\n        \"library,workload,tag,rep,seed,capacity,n_ops,block_size,\"\n        \"slot_bytes,bytes_per_op,time_ms,drops\");\n\n    EventTimer timer;\n\n    std::size_t n_unique = 0;\n\n    run_benchmark_loop(args.warmups, args.reps, timer,\n        /*setup=*/  [&]() {\n            table.init();\n            fill_uniform_keys(d_keys, args.n_ops, gen);\n            cudaMemcpy(d_sorted, d_keys, args.n_ops * sizeof(Key),\n                       cudaMemcpyDeviceToDevice) >> CUDA_CHECK;\n            thrust::sort(thrust::device, d_sorted, d_sorted + args.n_ops);\n            auto end = thrust::unique(thrust::device, d_sorted,\n                                      d_sorted + args.n_ops);\n            n_unique = end - d_sorted;\n        },\n        /*launch=*/ [&]() { table.insert(d_keys, d_values, args.n_ops); },\n        /*after=*/  [&](int rep, float ms) {\n            // warpcore's size() is synchronous w.r.t. the stream argument.\n            const std::size_t occupied = table.size();\n            const std::size_t drops =\n                n_unique > occupied ? n_unique - occupied : 0;\n            rec.write_row(format_row(\n                args.capacity, args.n_ops,\n                slot_bytes, bytes_per_op,\n                args.seed, rep, args.tag, ms, drops));\n        });\n\n    cudaFree(d_keys);\n    cudaFree(d_values);\n    cudaFree(d_sorted);\n    return 0;\n}"
}
```

</details>

160	SLOT_BYTES=8
161	CAPACITY=$(( (1024 * 1024 * 1024) / SLOT_BYTES ))   # 1 GiB / 8 = 2^27
162	BLOCK_SIZES=(64 128 256 512 1024)
163	TAG="sweep"
164	
165	# Each binary invocation runs $REPS timed reps, with the keys refilled
166	# from the continuing cuRAND stream before every rep. Statistically
167	# equivalent to $REPS independent seeds but with all CUDA / table /
168	# generator setup paid once instead of $REPS times.
169	REPS=16
170	SEED=1
171	
172	# n_ops multipliers: f * capacity. We compute the raw integers once so
173	# the rest of the script passes them as-is. Multipliers for gpurhh
174	# (which can run past 1.0) and for cuco/warpcore (capped at < 1.0 to
175	# stay below their fixed-capacity limits).

194	        --tag "${TAG}"

202	                --n-ops "${n_ops}" \

206	                --tag "${TAG}"
207

209	            "${TIMING_BIN}/benchmark_get" \
210	                --output-dir "${TIMING_OUT}" \
211	                --capacity "${CAPACITY}" \
212	                --n-ops "${n_ops}" \
213	                --block-size "${b}" \
214	                --reps "${REPS}" \
215	                --seed "${SEED}" \
216	                --tag "${TAG}"
217	        done
218	    done

Multipliers for gpurhh\n# (which can run past 1.0) and for cuco/warpcore (capped at < 1.0 to\n# stay below their fixed-capacity limits).\nGPURHH_NOPS=()\nfor mul in 0.5 1.0 1.5 2.0 3.0; do\n    GPURHH_NOPS+=($(python3 -c \"print(int($mul * $CAPACITY))\"))\ndone\nBASELINE_NOPS=()\nfor mul in 0.5 0.7 0.85 0.95; do\n    BASELINE_NOPS+=($(python3 -c \"print(int($mul * $CAPACITY))\"))\ndone",
  "new_string": "# n_ops multipliers: f * capacity, shared across all three libraries.\n# All inserts have bounded probe sequences (gpurhh: MaxProbeBuckets cap;\n# cuco/warpcore: probe up to capacity then give up), so we can run every\n# library at every F point. At F > 1 we measure \"how does each library\n# degrade once over-subscribed\" — both throughput and drop count are in\n# the CSV, so the timing data and the failure data are both honest.\nNOPS=()\nfor mul in 0.5 0.7 0.85 0.95 1.0 1.5 2.0 3.0; do\n    NOPS+=($(python3 -c \"print(int($mul * $CAPACITY))\"))\ndone"
}

## Assistant — 2026-05-20T22:36:41.915Z

Done. Summary:

**`benchmarks/timing/benchmark_insert.cu` (gpurhh)**:
- `#define GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS` at top so `table.data()` is callable.
- New `d_sorted` device buffer (n_ops × uint32) for the unique counting.
- In setup: copy `d_keys → d_sorted`, `thrust::sort`, `thrust::unique`, capture `n_unique`.
- In after: `reinterpret_cast<const Slot*>(table.data())` and call `count_occupied_slots(slots, capacity, Table::empty_key)`. Derive `drops = n_unique - occupied`.
- CSV gains a `drops` column at the end.
- Updated header comment to describe the new pipeline.

**`benchmarks/timing/baselines/cuco/benchmark_insert.cu`**: same sort+unique flow in setup; uses `map.size()` (built-in, synchronous) in after; `drops` column added.

**`benchmarks/timing/baselines/warpcore/benchmark_insert.cu`**: same; uses `table.size()`; `drops` column added.

**`scripts/benchmark.sh`**: merged `GPURHH_NOPS` and `BASELINE_NOPS` into a single `NOPS=(0.5 0.7 0.85 0.95 1.0 1.5 2.0 3.0)` array, used by every library. Updated comment to explain that all three are runnable across the full F range because each has bounded probing — the data now tells both the throughput story and the failure-count story for every library at every F point.

When you run this, the timing/insert.csv will have entries from all three libraries at all 8 F points, with `time_ms` and `drops` columns to compare on both axes.

1	#pragma once

3	// gpurhh — printing helpers for HashTable.
4	//
5	// Pretty-prints HashTable internal state for debugging and diagnostics.
6	// Lives in a separate header so the core library is independent of
7	// <cstdio> and <vector>, and so that pulling print support in is opt-in.
8	//
9	// Relies on HashTable::data(), which is gated behind
10	// GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS: that macro must be defined in the
11	// translation unit before any gpurhh header is included. All operations
12	// here copy bucket contents to host via cudaMemcpy and print to stdout;
13	// none of this is synchronized against concurrent device-side activity.
14	//
15	// We cannot define the macro here ourselves: <gpurhh/hash_table.cuh>
16	// uses `#pragma once`, so by the time control reaches this header it
17	// may already have been processed (without the macro set, leaving
18	// data() invisible). Instead we require the user to define the macro
19	// up-front, and fail compilation early with a clear message otherwise.
20	
21	#ifndef GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS
22	#error "<gpurhh/print.cuh> requires GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS to be defined before any gpurhh header is included"
23	#endif
24	
25	#include <gpurhh/hash_table.cuh>

30

1	#pragma once
2	
3	// Small CUDA utilities shared across tests (and, eventually, examples).
4	//
5	// CUDA_CHECK provides a postfix error-check operator:
6	//
7	//     cudaMalloc(&p, n) >> CUDA_CHECK;
8	//
9	// The macro captures the call site so errors are reported with file:line and
10	// individual call sites do not need to carry a context string.
11	
12	// Opt into the gpurhh testing back-door. This must be defined before any
13	// inclusion of <gpurhh/hash_table.cuh> in this translation unit, so that
14	// HashTable::data() is exposed for direct memory inspection / seeding.
15	#define GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS 1
16	
17	#include <cstdio>
18	#include <cstdlib>
19	
20	#include <cuda_runtime.h>
21	
22	struct CudaCheckLoc {
23	    const char* file;
24	    int         line;
25	};

1	// Minimal example of using gpurhh.
2	//
3	// Constructs a HashTable on the host, launches a small kernel to insert a
4	// handful of (key, value) pairs, launches a second small kernel to look
5	// them back up, prints the round-trip, and finally pretty-prints the
6	// table's memory layout.
7	
8	// Pull in the diagnostic pretty-printer at the end of the round-trip. It
9	// touches the table's internal bucket array, so we opt into the gated
10	// data() accessor before including any gpurhh header.
11	#define GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS 1
12	
13	#include <gpurhh/hash_table.cuh>
14	#include <gpurhh/print.cuh>
15	
16	#include <cooperative_groups.h>
17	#include <cuda_runtime.h>
18	
19	#include <cstdint>
20	#include <cstdio>
```

</details>

```
5	// end-to-end bulk path used in kernels.cuh.
6	//
7	// The defining choice here is the IdentityHash: with hash(K) = K, every
8	// key's home bucket is fully predictable from its value, which lets tests
9	// hand-build or hand-predict any valid Robin Hood state. The header also
10	// provides:
11	//
12	//   - set_state / read_state — direct cudaMemcpy into / out of the table's
13	//     internal bucket array (requires GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS, set
14	//     by tests.cuh).
15	//   - insert_many / run_insert — tile-strided insert and host-side
16	//     launcher.
17	//   - assert_robin_hood_invariant — structural check usable as a safety
18	//     net after any sequence of inserts.
19	
20	#include <cassert>
21	#include <cstdint>
22	
23	#include <cooperative_groups.h>
24	#include <cuda_runtime.h>
25	
26	// tests.cuh must precede the gpurhh header — it sets the
27	// GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS macro that exposes HashTable::data().
28	#include "tests.cuh"
29	#include <gpurhh/hash_table.cuh>
30	
31	#include <optional>
32	#include <vector>
33	
34	namespace cg = cooperative_groups;
35	
36	// Identity hash for predictable bucket placement: hash(K) = K, so home
37	// slot = K & capacity_mask and home bucket = home_slot / bucket_size.
38	struct IdentityHash {
39	    __device__ std::uint32_t operator()(std::uint32_t k) const noexcept { return k; }
40	};
41	
42	// Default table type for the isolated tests. The Reduction is left at the
43	// `replace_op` default; tests that want a different reduction declare their
44	// own alias (see test_randomized.cu).
```

</details>

## User — 2026-05-20T22:40:48.613Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-984`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
318	
319	    View view() const noexcept;
320	
321	    // Actual capacity, in slots, rounded up to the next power of two and
322	    // never less than `bucket_size`.
323	    std::size_t capacity() const noexcept;
324	
325	#ifdef GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS
326	    // Direct pointer access to the device-resident bucket array. Gated
327	    // behind a macro so users must opt in explicitly; the test suite does.
328	    Bucket*       data() noexcept;
329	    const Bucket* data() const noexcept;
330	#endif
331	};
332	
333	// Diagnostic helper. Lives in <gpurhh/print.cuh>, a separate header so
334	// the core library stays independent of <cstdio> and <vector>. Relies
335	// on HashTable::data(), so the including translation unit must define
336	// GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS first; <gpurhh/print.cuh> #errors
337	// otherwise.
338	template <class Table>
339	void print_slots(const Table& table, std::size_t start, std::size_t stop);
340	
341	}  // namespace gpurhh
342	```
343	
344	### Opt-in macros
345	
346	Two macros gate functionality that the core library doesn't expose by default. Both must be defined in the translation unit *before* any `<gpurhh/...>` header is included:
347	
348	- **`GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS`** exposes `HashTable::data()`, giving direct pointer access to the device-resident bucket array. Used by `<gpurhh/print.cuh>` (which `#error`s if the macro is missing) and by the test suite's `set_state` / `read_state` helpers, which need to seed and inspect raw table layouts via `cudaMemcpy` to test corner cases in isolation.
349	- **`GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS`** adds a trailing `std::uint32_t& tile_counter` parameter to `View::insert` and `View::get`. Lane 0 of the tile increments it on every bucket-load (a CAS-retry re-read counts too — it's a real DRAM transaction). The caller maintains a per-tile register accumulator and writes it out once at end of kernel; no atomics are involved in the probing hot loop. The benchmark binaries enable this; tests and the example do not.
350	
351	The `View` is the centerpiece for header-only use: a user kernel that already has its keys in registers calls `view.insert(tile, ...)` or `view.get(tile, ...)` directly.
352	Bulk host-side operations are deliberately *not* part of the table — callers manage their device buffers and write their own driver kernels.
353	The tests in `tests/kernels.cuh` and `tests/isolated.cuh` show the canonical pattern; `examples/basic.cu` is a minimal standalone walkthrough.
354	
355	## Testing
356	
357	The test suite lives in `tests/`, with each `test_*.cu` compiled into its own executable.
358	
359	- **`test_construction.cu`** — construct / destruct, move semantics, edge sizes (capacity == bucket_size, capacity below bucket_size), moved-from state behavior, and `clear()` returning the bucket array to the empty-byte pattern.
360	- **`test_combined.cu`** — end-to-end insert + get round-trips through the public API.
361	- **`test_get.cu`** — `View::get` in isolation. Pre-states are hand-built with `IdentityHash` so the probe sequence and home buckets are deterministic; the test then verifies `get`'s match / empty / richer-resident / wrap-around behavior.
362	- **`test_insert.cu`** — `View::insert` in isolation. Some tests use hand-computed expected layouts; all run a Robin Hood invariant scanner over the final state as a safety net. Covers the probe-cap failure path and concurrent same-key inserts.
363	- **`test_concurrency.cu`** — heavy parallel contention: adversarial displacement chains, many concurrent gets, mixed new / duplicate inserts, parallel saturation of the probe cap.
364	- **`test_randomized.cu`** — randomized stress. Uniform-key inserts at small (4 KB table) and gigabyte (1 GB table) scales; a sum-reduction stress test with random keys and deliberate duplicates whose expected per-key sum is computed on the host.
365	
366	Three shared infrastructure headers:
367	
368	- **`tests/tests.cuh`** — the `CUDA_CHECK` postfix error-check operator (`expr >> CUDA_CHECK`) and the `GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS` macro, which gates `HashTable::data()`.
369	- **`tests/kernels.cuh`** — the `Table` alias (with `default_hash`), bulk insert/get host helpers, and end-to-end driver kernels. Used by the "combined" and construction tests.
370	- **`tests/isolated.cuh`** — the `TestTable` alias (with `IdentityHash` for predictable home buckets), templated `run_insert` / `run_get` / `run_get_one` / `run_insert_with_outcomes`, the `assert_robin_hood_invariant` scanner, and `set_state` / `read_state` for direct table-memory manipulation via the `data()` back-door.
371	
372	The `IdentityHash` is the enabling choice for the isolated tests: with `hash(K) = K`, the home bucket of a key is fully predictable from its value, so we can construct any valid Robin Hood state with one `cudaMemcpy` and hand-predict the outcome of small insert sequences. The randomized tests apply a bit-mixing function (the same `fmix32` finalizer used by `default_hash`) as a permutation on sequential integers — this gives distinct random-looking keys without the host-side dedup overhead that an `unordered_set` would require, which is what makes the gigabyte-scale tests fit in memory.
```

</details>

## Assistant — 2026-05-20T22:41:38.397Z

`GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS` is gone everywhere:

- **`include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh`**: `data()` is now unconditionally declared. The doc comment retains the "bypasses the table's concurrency contract" warning so the semantics are still flagged.
- **`include/gpurhh/print.cuh`**: dropped the `#ifndef ... #error` guard and the multi-paragraph preamble explaining the macro dance. The header now just `#include`s `<gpurhh/hash_table.cuh>` and relies on `data()` directly.
- **`tests/tests.cuh`**, **`examples/basic.cu`**, **`benchmarks/timing/benchmark_insert.cu`**: removed the `#define` lines and the surrounding explanatory comments.
- **`tests/kernels.cuh`**, **`tests/isolated.cuh`**: dropped the "tests.cuh must precede the gpurhh header" comments and the `data()`-back-door references in the file-level docstring.
- **`notes/design.md`**: removed the `#ifdef GPURHH_ENABLE_INTERNAL_ACCESS` from the API sketch, deleted the `print.cuh` `#error` note, simplified "Opt-in macros" from two macros to one (only `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` remains), and updated the test-infrastructure listing.

Net effect: one less concept for users to learn. `data()` is just there with a clear comment about what it does and what it doesn't promise.

## Assistant — 2026-05-20T22:53:51.653Z

Good instinct. Let me sketch what this would look like and check it matches what you have in mind, since "table constructor" might have a slight wrinkle — the counter is fundamentally per-tile (lives in the kernel as a register), not per-table.

**Proposed design:**

```cpp
// In gpurhh namespace, hash_table.cuh
#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
struct TileCounter {
    std::uint32_t value_ = 0;
    __device__ void          bump()  noexcept { ++value_; }
    __device__ void          clear() noexcept { value_ = 0; }
    __device__ std::uint32_t read()  const noexcept { return value_; }
};
#else
struct TileCounter {
    __device__ void bump()  const noexcept {}
    __device__ void clear() const noexcept {}
    // No read() — nothing to read. Calls fail to compile in that
    // branch, which is what we want.
};
#endif
```

`View::insert` and `View::get` each get one overload pair — three-arg version forwards to a four-arg version with a default-constructed `TileCounter`:

```cpp
template <class Tile>
__device__ cuda::std::optional<Slot>
insert(const Tile& tile, K key, V value) {
    TileCounter dummy;
    return insert(tile, key, value, dummy);
}

template <class Tile>
__device__ cuda::std::optional<Slot>
insert(const Tile& tile, K key, V value, TileCounter& counter) {
    for (...) {
        if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) counter.bump();
        ...
    }
}
```

**At call sites:**

- Tests, examples, timing benchmarks: keep using `view.insert(tile, k, v)` — unchanged. The temporary `TileCounter` is empty when the macro is off, so the compiler elides it entirely. Identical machine code to today.
- Memory benchmarks: declare `TileCounter c;` as a kernel register, pass it through, read at end of kernel:
  ```cpp
  TileCounter c;
  for (op_id ...) view.insert(tile, k, v, c);
  if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) per_tile_probes[tile_id] = c.read();
  ```

**Where the `#ifdef` ends up:**

1. One in `hash_table.cuh` — the `TileCounter` definition (the `#ifdef/#else` block above).
2. Implicitly one at the memory-benchmark call sites that use `.read()` — those translation units have to define the macro to compile, but that's a `#define`, not an `#ifdef`, so it's a single line at the top of the file.

So the `#ifdef`s that were sprinkled through the probe loop bodies and the `View::insert`/`View::get` signatures all collapse to one block. Probe-loop bodies become unconditional `counter.bump()` calls. Signatures gain a fourth parameter but in a way that's invisible to anyone using the three-arg form.

**Verifying same machine code:**

- Macro off → `TileCounter` is empty → `sizeof(TileCounter) == 0` (or 1 for ABI but optimized away) → `bump()`/`clear()` are no-op functions → compiler inlines and elides → identical PTX to today.
- Macro on → `TileCounter` holds a `uint32_t` → `bump()` is a register increment → matches today's `++tile_counter` pattern, with the difference being it's wrapped in a method.

The one observable behavior difference is that the timing benchmark code path always constructs a temporary `TileCounter` even when macro is off — but since the type is empty, this construction has zero cost (no memory traffic, no instructions).

Matches your intent? If yes I'll implement; if you meant something different by "table constructor" I want to make sure I'm not building the wrong thing.

270	    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
271	    class View {
272	    public:
273	        // Cooperative single-key insert.
274	        // Called by a tile of exactly tile_size threads in lock-step. Returns
275	        // nullopt on success (the in-flight pair was placed in an empty slot
276	        // or merged into an existing slot via the Reduction operator).
277	        //
278	        // On probe-cap failure returns the leftover Slot that could not be
279	        // placed. Note this is not necessarily the (key, value) originally
280	        // passed in: if the insertion triggered Robin Hood displacements
281	        // before failing, the returned Slot is the most-recently-evicted
282	        // victim — the originally-passed-in pair is already in the table,
283	        // and it is the victim that is now unreachable. Callers that need
284	        // to avoid data loss should buffer the returned Slot and retry it
285	        // elsewhere (typically by rebuilding a larger table).
286	        //
287	        // When GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS is defined, takes an additional
288	        // trailing reference parameter `tile_counter`. Lane 0 of the tile
289	        // increments it once per bucket read (i.e. once per probe loop
290	        // iteration, including CAS-retry re-reads of the same bucket).
291	        // The caller is expected to pass a per-tile register accumulator
292	        // and flush it to a per-tile global slot at end of kernel — no
293	        // atomic is involved in the increment.
294	        template <class Tile>
295	        __device__ cuda::std::optional<Slot> insert(
296	            const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value
297	#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
298	            , std::uint32_t& tile_counter
299	#endif
300	        );
301	
302	        // Cooperative single-key lookup. Returns the stored value if the
303	        // key is present, or an empty optional otherwise. Every lane in the
304	        // tile receives the same result.
305	        //
306	        // The `tile_counter` parameter under GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
307	        // behaves the same way as in `insert`.
308	        template <class Tile>
309	        __device__ cuda::std::optional<Value> get(
310	            const Tile& tile, Key key
311	#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
312	            , std::uint32_t& tile_counter
313	#endif
314	        ) const;
315	
316	        __host__ __device__ std::size_t capacity() const noexcept { return capacity_; }
317	
318	    private:
319	        friend class HashTable;
320	
321	        Bucket*     buckets_       = nullptr;
322	        std::size_t capacity_      = 0;   // power of two, in slots
323	        std::size_t capacity_mask_ = 0;   // capacity_ - 1, for modulo via AND
324	        Hash        hash_{};
325	        Reduction   reduce_{};
326	    };
327	
328	    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
329	    // Host-side resource management.
330	    // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
331	
332	    // Allocates a slot array on the current device sized for at least
333	    // `min_capacity` slots, rounded up to the next power of two, and
334	    // initializes every slot to (empty_key, _). Synchronous: cudaMalloc
335	    // already blocks the host, so there is nothing to be gained from
336	    // routing the slot-init memset through a stream.
337	    explicit HashTable(std::size_t min_capacity);
338	
339	    ~HashTable();
340	
341	    HashTable(const HashTable&)            = delete;
342	    HashTable& operator=(const HashTable&) = delete;
343	    HashTable(HashTable&&) noexcept;
344	    HashTable& operator=(HashTable&&) noexcept;
```

451	                    num_buckets * sizeof(Bucket), stream);

453	
454	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>
455	template <class Tile>
456	__device__ auto HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::View::insert(
457	    const Tile& tile, K key, V value
458	#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
459	    , std::uint32_t& tile_counter
460	#endif
461	)
462	    -> cuda::std::optional<Slot>
463	{
464	    // Bucketed Robin Hood insertion with lock-free CAS. The tile carries an
465	    // in-flight pair (cur_key, cur_value) that may change due to Robin Hood
466	    // displacement — when we evict a "richer" resident, that resident
467	    // becomes our new in-flight pair and we keep probing from the next
468	    // bucket.
469	
470	    K cur_key   = key;
471	    V cur_value = value;
472	
473	    const std::size_t num_buckets = capacity_ / bucket_size;
474	    const std::size_t bucket_mask = num_buckets > 0 ? num_buckets - 1 : 0;
475	    const std::size_t probe_bound = num_buckets < max_probe_buckets
476	        ? num_buckets
477	        : static_cast<std::size_t>(max_probe_buckets);
478	
479	    std::size_t cur_home = (hash_(cur_key) & capacity_mask_) / bucket_size;
480	    std::size_t probe    = 0;
481	
482	    while (probe < probe_bound) {
483	        const std::size_t bucket_idx = (cur_home + probe) & bucket_mask;
484	
485	#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
486	        // Count one bucket-load per probe iteration. CAS-retry `continue`s
487	        // re-enter the loop and re-read the same bucket — those count too,
488	        // because they are real DRAM transactions.
489	        if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) ++tile_counter;
490	#endif
491	
492	        // Cooperative load: one coalesced cache-line transaction. Each
493	        // lane reads one slot of the bucket (lane i reads slots[i]).
494	        const Slot slot = buckets_[bucket_idx].slots[tile.thread_rank()];
495	
496	        // Helper: `target_lane` performs an atomic CAS to replace its slot
497	        // (whose current contents we have in `slot`) with the in-flight
498	        // pair. On the match case the new value is computed as
499	        // `reduce_(existing, incoming)`; on the empty / displaceable cases
500	        // the new value is just the incoming value. Returns the broadcast
501	        // result of the CAS to every lane.
502	        auto try_cas = [&](int target_lane, bool apply_reduction) -> bool {
503	            bool cas_ok = false;
504	            if (tile.thread_rank() == target_lane) {
505	                const V to_store = apply_reduction
506	                    ? reduce_(slot.value, cur_value)
507	                    : cur_value;
508	                Slot expected = slot;
509	                const Slot desired{cur_key, to_store};
510	                cuda::atomic_ref<Slot, cuda::thread_scope_device> atomic_slot(
511	                    buckets_[bucket_idx].slots[target_lane]);
512	                cas_ok = atomic_slot.compare_exchange_strong(expected, desired);
513	            }
514	            return tile.shfl(cas_ok, target_lane);
515	        };
516	
517	        // (1) Key match — combine the in-flight value with the existing one
518	        //     via the Reduction operator (default: replace).
519	        const auto match_mask = tile.ballot(slot.key == cur_key);
520	        if (match_mask != 0) {
521	            if (try_cas(__ffs(match_mask) - 1, /*apply_reduction=*/true))
522	                return cuda::std::nullopt;
523	            continue;  // CAS lost the race — retry this bucket.
524	        }
525	
526	        // (2) Empty slot — claim it for our in-flight pair.
527	        const auto empty_mask = tile.ballot(slot.key == empty_key);
528	        if (empty_mask != 0) {
529	            if (try_cas(__ffs(empty_mask) - 1, /*apply_reduction=*/false))
530	                return cuda::std::nullopt;
531	            continue;  // Someone else took the slot — retry.
532	        }
533	
534	        // (3) Displaceable slot — Robin Hood swap with a richer resident.
535	        // Compute each resident's probe distance. (For empty slots this
536	        // gives nonsense, but we already eliminated the empty case above,
537	        // so the next ballot is meaningful.)
538	        const std::size_t resident_home =
539	            (hash_(slot.key) & capacity_mask_) / bucket_size;
540	        const std::size_t resident_probe =
541	            (bucket_idx - resident_home) & bucket_mask;
542	        const auto displaceable_mask = tile.ballot(resident_probe < probe);
543	        if (displaceable_mask != 0) {
544	            const int target_lane = __ffs(displaceable_mask) - 1;
545	            if (try_cas(target_lane, /*apply_reduction=*/false)) {
546	                // Adopt the evicted pair as our new in-flight pair. We
547	                // continue from the next bucket; the evicted pair's probe
548	                // distance there is `resident_probe + 1`.
549	                cur_key   = tile.shfl(slot.key,        target_lane);
550	                cur_value = tile.shfl(slot.value,      target_lane);
551	                cur_home  = tile.shfl(resident_home,   target_lane);
552	                probe     = tile.shfl(resident_probe,  target_lane) + 1;
553	                continue;
554	            }
555	            continue;  // CAS lost the race — retry.
556	        }
557	
558	        // (4) Nothing applies — advance one bucket.
559	        ++probe;
560	    }
561	
562	    // Probe budget exhausted. Hand the in-flight pair back so the caller
563	    // can buffer it and rehash into a larger table. This is the original
564	    // (key, value) if no displacement happened, or the most-recently-
565	    // evicted victim otherwise — either way, returning it is what keeps
566	    // the operation lossless.
567	    return Slot{cur_key, cur_value};
568	}
569	
570	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>
571	template <class Tile>
572	__device__ cuda::std::optional<V>
573	HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::View::get(
574	    const Tile& tile, K key
575	#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
576	    , std::uint32_t& tile_counter
577	#endif
578	) const
579	{
580	    // Bucket-level Robin Hood lookup. Probe home_bucket, then home_bucket+1,
581	    // etc., wrapping modulo num_buckets. Each iteration is one coalesced
582	    // cache-line load (one slot per tile lane) and a few tile-wide ballots
583	    // to identify the terminating condition.
584	
585	    const auto h = hash_(key);
586	    const std::size_t home_slot   = h & capacity_mask_;
587	    const std::size_t home_bucket = home_slot / bucket_size;
588	    const std::size_t num_buckets = capacity_ / bucket_size;
589	    const std::size_t bucket_mask = num_buckets > 0 ? num_buckets - 1 : 0;
590	
591	    // Probe at most max_probe_buckets buckets, or every bucket in the table
592	    // if it's smaller than the cap.
593	    const std::size_t probe_bound = num_buckets < max_probe_buckets
594	        ? num_buckets
595	        : static_cast<std::size_t>(max_probe_buckets);
596	
597	    for (std::size_t probe = 0; probe < probe_bound; ++probe) {
598	        const std::size_t bucket_idx = (home_bucket + probe) & bucket_mask;
599	
600	#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
601	        // One bucket-load per probe iteration.
602	        if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) ++tile_counter;
603	#endif
604	
605	        // Each lane reads one slot — one coalesced cache-line transaction
606	        // for the whole tile.
607	        const Slot slot = buckets_[bucket_idx].slots[tile.thread_rank()];
608	
609	        // (1) Key match. The matching lane broadcasts its value to the rest
610	        //     of the tile via shfl; each lane wraps the broadcast result in
611	        //     an optional and returns it (every lane returns the same one).
612	        const auto match_mask = tile.ballot(slot.key == key);
613	        if (match_mask != 0) {
614	            const int matching_lane = __ffs(match_mask) - 1;
615	            return cuda::std::optional<V>{tile.shfl(slot.value, matching_lane)};
616	        }
617	
618	        // (2) Empty slot. The key isn't in the table — Robin Hood would have
619	        //     placed it earlier in the probe sequence.
620	        if (tile.ballot(slot.key == empty_key) != 0) return cuda::std::nullopt;
621	
622	        // (3) Robin Hood early termination. If any resident is "richer"
623	        //     (closer to its home bucket than we are to ours), our key would
624	        //     have evicted it on insertion, so it can't be later in the
```

  "old_string": "inline constexpr cudaStream_t default_stream = 0;\n\n// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------\n// HashTable: host-side owner of the device-resident slot array.\n// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------",
  "new_string": "inline constexpr cudaStream_t default_stream = 0;\n\n// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------\n// TileCounter: optional probe-count instrumentation passed into View::insert\n// and View::get. Without GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS the type is empty and all\n// of its methods are no-ops, so bump() calls in the probe hot loop fold to\n// nothing at compile time and the table's normal users (tests, examples,\n// timing benchmarks) pay zero overhead. With the macro defined, the type\n// holds a `std::uint32_t` register-resident counter and exposes `read()`\n// for the benchmark binaries that want to flush it out at end of kernel.\n//\n// The intent is one TileCounter per cooperative tile, allocated as a kernel\n// register at the start of the timed loop and (when the macro is on) read\n// out once before the kernel returns.\n// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------\n#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS\nstruct TileCounter {\n    std::uint32_t value_ = 0;\n    __device__ void          bump()  noexcept { ++value_; }\n    __device__ void          clear() noexcept { value_ = 0; }\n    __device__ std::uint32_t read()  const noexcept { return value_; }\n};\n#else\nstruct TileCounter {\n    __device__ void bump()  const noexcept {}\n    __device__ void clear() const noexcept {}\n    // No `read()` — there is no counter to read. Calls fail to compile in\n    // this branch, which is what we want: a binary that asks for counter\n    // values must opt into GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS.\n};\n#endif\n\n// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------\n// HashTable: host-side owner of the device-resident slot array.\n// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------"
}

  "new_string": "template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>\ntemplate <class Tile>\n__device__ cuda::std::optional<V>\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::View::get(\n    const Tile& tile, K key) const\n{\n    TileCounter dummy;\n    return get(tile, key, dummy);\n}\n\ntemplate <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>\ntemplate <class Tile>\n__device__ cuda::std::optional<V>\nHashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::View::get(\n    const Tile& tile, K key, TileCounter& counter) const\n{\n    // Bucket-level Robin Hood lookup. Probe home_bucket, then home_bucket+1,\n    // etc., wrapping modulo num_buckets. Each iteration is one coalesced\n    // cache-line load (one slot per tile lane) and a few tile-wide ballots\n    // to identify the terminating condition.\n\n    const auto h = hash_(key);\n    const std::size_t home_slot   = h & capacity_mask_;\n    const std::size_t home_bucket = home_slot / bucket_size;\n    const std::size_t num_buckets = capacity_ / bucket_size;\n    const std::size_t bucket_mask = num_buckets > 0 ? num_buckets - 1 : 0;\n\n    // Probe at most max_probe_buckets buckets, or every bucket in the table\n    // if it's smaller than the cap.\n    const std::size_t probe_bound = num_buckets < max_probe_buckets\n        ? num_buckets\n        : static_cast<std::size_t>(max_probe_buckets);\n\n    for (std::size_t probe = 0; probe < probe_bound; ++probe) {\n        const std::size_t bucket_idx = (home_bucket + probe) & bucket_mask;\n\n        // One bucket-load per probe iteration. No-op when\n        // GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS is undefined.\n        if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) counter.bump();"
}
```

</details>

107	std::string format_row(

132	int main(int argc, char** argv) {
133	    const Args args = parse_args(argc, argv);
134	
135	    constexpr std::size_t slot_bytes   = sizeof(Table::Slot);
136	    constexpr std::size_t bytes_per_op = sizeof(Table::Bucket);
137	
138	    Table table(args.capacity);
139	    const std::size_t capacity = table.capacity();
140	
141	    std::fprintf(stderr,
142	        "[memory insert] capacity=%zu n_ops=%zu "
143	        "block_size=%d warmups=%d reps=%d seed=%u tag=\"%s\"\n",
144	        capacity, args.n_ops,
145	        args.block_size, args.warmups, args.reps, args.seed, args.tag.c_str());
146	
147	    std::uint32_t* d_keys = nullptr;
148	    cudaMalloc(&d_keys, args.n_ops * sizeof(std::uint32_t)) >> CUDA_CHECK;
149	    UniformKeyGenerator gen(args.seed);
150	
151	    const auto shape = compute_launch_shape(args.block_size, Table::tile_size);
152	    const std::size_t n_tiles = shape.n_tiles;
153	
154	    std::uint32_t* d_probes   = nullptr;
155	    std::uint32_t* d_failures = nullptr;
156	    cudaMalloc(&d_probes,   n_tiles * sizeof(std::uint32_t)) >> CUDA_CHECK;
157	    cudaMalloc(&d_failures, n_tiles * sizeof(std::uint32_t)) >> CUDA_CHECK;
158	    std::vector<std::uint32_t> h_probes  (n_tiles);
159	    std::vector<std::uint32_t> h_failures(n_tiles);
160	
161	    Recorder rec(args.output_dir / "insert.csv",
162	        "library,workload,tag,rep,seed,capacity,n_ops,block_size,"
163	        "slot_bytes,bytes_per_op,time_ms,total_probes,total_failures");
164	
165	    EventTimer timer;
166	
167	    run_benchmark_loop(args.warmups, args.reps, timer,
168	        /*setup=*/  [&]() {
169	            table.clear();
170	            fill_uniform_keys(d_keys, args.n_ops, gen);
171	            cudaMemset(d_probes,   0, n_tiles * sizeof(std::uint32_t)) >> CUDA_CHECK;
172	            cudaMemset(d_failures, 0, n_tiles * sizeof(std::uint32_t)) >> CUDA_CHECK;
173	        },
174	        /*launch=*/ [&]() {
175	            insert<<<shape.grid_size, args.block_size>>>(
176	                table.view(), d_keys, args.n_ops, d_probes, d_failures);
177	            cudaGetLastError() >> CUDA_CHECK;
178	        },
179	        /*after=*/  [&](int rep, float ms) {
180	            cudaMemcpy(h_probes.data(),   d_probes,
181	                       n_tiles * sizeof(std::uint32_t),
182	                       cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost) >> CUDA_CHECK;
183	            cudaMemcpy(h_failures.data(), d_failures,
184	                       n_tiles * sizeof(std::uint32_t),
185	                       cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost) >> CUDA_CHECK;
186	            const std::uint64_t total_probes =
187	                std::accumulate(h_probes.begin(), h_probes.end(), std::uint64_t{0});
188	            const std::uint64_t total_failures =
189	                std::accumulate(h_failures.begin(), h_failures.end(), std::uint64_t{0});
190	            rec.write_row(format_row(
191	                capacity, args.n_ops,
192	                args.block_size, slot_bytes, bytes_per_op,
193	                args.seed, rep, args.tag,
194	                ms, total_probes, total_failures));
195	        });
196	
197	    cudaFree(d_keys);
198	    cudaFree(d_probes);
199	    cudaFree(d_failures);
200	    return 0;
201	}
202

## User — 2026-05-20T22:57:24.153Z

297:        // The `tile_counter` parameter appears only when
298:        // GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS is defined. Lane 0 of the tile
305:#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
306:            , std::uint32_t& tile_counter
313:#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
314:            , std::uint32_t& tile_counter
347:- **`GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS`** adds a trailing `std::uint32_t& tile_counter` parameter to `View::insert` and `View::get`. Lane 0 of the tile increments it on every bucket-load (a CAS-retry re-read counts too — it's a real DRAM transaction). The caller maintains a per-tile register accumulator and writes it out once at end of kernel; no atomics are involved in the probing hot loop. The memory-utilization benchmark binaries enable this; the timing benchmarks, tests, and the example do not.
396:The `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` macro adds a probe counter parameter to `View::insert` / `View::get`. The benchmark kernels accumulate per-thread register counters across a grid-stride loop and flush one value per tile to a global array at end of kernel; no atomics fire in the probe hot loop. The host sums per-tile values into a grand total per rep.
```

</details>

290	    // Device-side handle. Trivially copyable; pass by value into kernels.
291	    class View {
292	    public:
293	        // Returns nullopt on success; on probe-cap failure returns the
294	        // leftover Slot that could not be placed (may be the original
295	        // pair, or a Robin Hood victim displaced before the failure).
296	        //
297	        // The `tile_counter` parameter appears only when
298	        // GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS is defined. Lane 0 of the tile
299	        // increments it on each bucket-load; the caller is expected
300	        // to pass a per-tile register accumulator and flush it to a
301	        // per-tile global slot at end of kernel.
302	        template <class Tile>
303	        __device__ cuda::std::optional<Slot>
304	        insert(const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value
305	#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
306	            , std::uint32_t& tile_counter
307	#endif
308	        );
309	
310	        template <class Tile>
311	        __device__ cuda::std::optional<Value>
312	        get(const Tile& tile, Key key
313	#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
314	            , std::uint32_t& tile_counter
315	#endif
316	        ) const;
317	    };
318	
319	    View view() const noexcept;
320	
321	    // Actual capacity, in slots, rounded up to the next power of two and
322	    // never less than `bucket_size`.
323	    std::size_t capacity() const noexcept;
324	
325	    // Direct pointer access to the device-resident bucket array.
326	    // Provided for tests, diagnostics, and benchmark instrumentation —
327	    // the `data()` name (matching the `std::vector::data` convention)
328	    // and the comment in the header are enough warning that callers
329	    // are bypassing the table's concurrency contract.
330	    Bucket*       data() noexcept;
331	    const Bucket* data() const noexcept;
332	};
333	
334	// Diagnostic helper. Lives in <gpurhh/print.cuh>, a separate header so
335	// the core library stays independent of <cstdio> and <vector>. Reads
336	// the bucket array via HashTable::data().
337	template <class Table>
338	void print_slots(const Table& table, std::size_t start, std::size_t stop);
339	
340	}  // namespace gpurhh
341	```
342	
343	### Opt-in macros
344	
345	One macro gates functionality that the core library doesn't expose by default. It must be defined in the translation unit *before* any `<gpurhh/...>` header is included:
346	
347	- **`GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS`** adds a trailing `std::uint32_t& tile_counter` parameter to `View::insert` and `View::get`. Lane 0 of the tile increments it on every bucket-load (a CAS-retry re-read counts too — it's a real DRAM transaction). The caller maintains a per-tile register accumulator and writes it out once at end of kernel; no atomics are involved in the probing hot loop. The memory-utilization benchmark binaries enable this; the timing benchmarks, tests, and the example do not.
348	
349	The `View` is the centerpiece for header-only use: a user kernel that already has its keys in registers calls `view.insert(tile, ...)` or `view.get(tile, ...)` directly.
350	Bulk host-side operations are deliberately *not* part of the table — callers manage their device buffers and write their own driver kernels.
351	The tests in `tests/kernels.cuh` and `tests/isolated.cuh` show the canonical pattern; `examples/basic.cu` is a minimal standalone walkthrough.
352	
```

</details>

With the macro undefined, `TileCounter` is an empty struct with no-op `bump()` and `clear()` methods — `view.insert(tile, k, v, counter)` calls in the probe hot loop compile to identical code as if the counter weren't there at all. With the macro defined, `TileCounter` holds a `std::uint32_t` and exposes `read()` so the benchmark binaries can flush it out at end of kernel. The probe loops in `View::insert` and `View::get` always call `counter.bump()` regardless — the dispatch is in the type, not in `#ifdef`s sprinkled through the methods. Memory-utilization benchmark binaries enable this; the timing benchmarks, tests, and the example do not."

## User — 2026-05-20T22:57:59.733Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1012`)

```
393	### Per-tile probe counters
394	
395	The `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` macro adds a probe counter parameter to `View::insert` / `View::get`. The benchmark kernels accumulate per-thread register counters across a grid-stride loop and flush one value per tile to a global array at end of kernel; no atomics fire in the probe hot loop. The host sums per-tile values into a grand total per rep.
396	
397	This gives the benchmark *two* bandwidth numbers:
398	
399	- **`gbps_floor`** — `n_ops × sizeof(Bucket) / time`. Assumes exactly one bucket read per op. Lower bound on actual DRAM traffic.
400	- **`gbps_corrected`** — `total_probes × sizeof(Bucket) / time`. Uses the measured probe count. Accurate to within the fraction of writes the floor estimate ignores (~3–6%; documented in the column header).
401	
402	At low F the two converge (avg probe length ≈ 1). At high F the corrected number diverges upward — that gap is itself a useful signal about how hard Robin Hood is working.
```

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-20T22:58:35.546Z

One `#ifdef`/`#endif` pair in the entire library, gating the two `TileCounter` shapes. No `tile_counter` references anywhere.

Summary of changes:

**`include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh`**:
- Added `gpurhh::TileCounter` class with two definitions selected by `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS`:
  - Off: empty struct, `bump()` and `clear()` are `const noexcept` no-ops; no `read()`.
  - On: holds `std::uint32_t value_`, with `bump()`, `clear()`, and `read()`.
- `View::insert` and `View::get` each now have two overloads: a three-arg "I don't care about counts" version that default-constructs a throwaway counter and forwards to the four-arg version that takes `TileCounter&`.
- All probe-loop bodies use unconditional `if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) counter.bump();` — no more `#ifdef` blocks inside the methods.

**`benchmarks/memory/benchmark_insert.cu`**: kernel now declares `gpurhh::TileCounter counter;` and reads `counter.read()` at end. Same machine code, cleaner source.

**`benchmarks/memory/benchmark_get.cu`**: same pattern for the get kernel. The pre-fill kernel uses the no-counter overload `view.insert(tile, k, v)` since the count isn't wanted there.

**`notes/design.md`**: rewrote the API sketch to show the two-overload pattern and the `TileCounter` design; revised the "Opt-in macros" entry and the "Per-tile probe counters" subsection to describe the empty-vs-real type dispatch.

Compile output is identical to before — the empty-struct overload elides at every call site that doesn't define the macro. The library now has exactly one `#ifdef` for the counter feature, where before it had five.

## Assistant — 2026-05-20T23:00:47.335Z

You're right. I added the no-counter overload to keep `view.insert(tile, k, v)` unchanged for tests/examples/timing benchmarks, but the cost of "must declare one `gpurhh::TileCounter counter;` per kernel" is tiny — and it makes the API more honest about what the method takes.

Single overload pattern:

```cpp
template <class Tile>
__device__ cuda::std::optional<Slot>
insert(const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value, TileCounter& counter);

template <class Tile>
__device__ cuda::std::optional<Value>
get(const Tile& tile, Key key, TileCounter& counter) const;
```

Every kernel that uses `view.insert` / `view.get` declares a `gpurhh::TileCounter counter;` at the top and passes it through. Counter is empty when `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` is undefined, so the cost is exactly zero — just one extra local that compiles to nothing.

Call sites that need updating (one-line addition each, plus a fourth arg in the existing call):

- `tests/kernels.cuh` — bulk insert / get driver kernels
- `tests/isolated.cuh` — `insert_many`, `run_get`, `run_get_one`, `run_insert_with_outcomes`
- `examples/basic.cu` — the example's insert and get kernels
- `benchmarks/timing/benchmark_insert.cu`, `benchmark_get.cu` — insert/get kernels
- The pre-fill kernel in `benchmarks/timing/benchmark_get.cu`

The memory benchmarks already pass a counter — no change there.

- Each thread calls an increment method (rename of bump) in the kernels, passing in its index so that it knows which array to increment

## Assistant — 2026-05-20T23:07:07.125Z

Let me sketch what I think you're proposing to make sure I've got it — and flag two open questions:

**My reading:**

1. `TileCounter` (when macro on) holds a `std::uint32_t*` device array, one entry per tile.
2. `HashTable`'s constructor allocates and zeroes that array; the destructor frees it. Memory benchmark no longer allocates `d_probes` itself.
3. `View` carries a pointer to the counter array.
4. `View::insert` / `View::get` internally call `counter.increment(tile_id)` on every probe iteration, where `tile_id` is computed inside the method from CUDA built-ins (`blockIdx.x * tiles_per_block + threadIdx.x / tile_size`).
5. **The public API returns to `view.insert(tile, k, v)` and `view.get(tile, k)`** — no counter argument visible to callers. Tests/examples are completely unchanged.
6. `HashTable` exposes some accessor (e.g. `counters()` or `probe_counts()`) that returns the device pointer, gated by the macro. Memory benchmark reads from there after the kernel.

When macro is off: `TileCounter` is empty, no array, `increment()` is a no-op, no constructor allocation, no accessor method. Zero overhead, zero footprint, no API surface change.

**This is genuinely cleaner — counter goes from "argument plumbed through every callsite" to "invisible side channel owned by the table." But two open questions before I implement:**

**(a) Sizing the counter array.** The table's constructor doesn't know how many tiles the user's kernel will launch. Options:

- **`(b1)`** Pass `max_tiles` (or `max_grid_threads`) as an extra HashTable constructor parameter. Cleanest, but every constructor call has to think about it. When macro off, the parameter is ignored (or doesn't exist).
- **`(b2)`** Allocate one slot per *bucket* — `num_buckets = capacity / bucket_size`. For our default capacity that's 2^23 ≈ 8 M slots = 32 MB. Way more than needed for ~few-thousand actual tiles, but it's a fixed function of capacity that the table already knows. Slight memory waste, no extra API surface.
- **`(b3)`** Lazy: `HashTable` exposes `prepare_counters(n_tiles)` that the benchmark calls before the timed kernel. Adds an API.

I'd lean **`(b2)`** — wastes some memory but keeps the API zero-overhead.

**(b) `tile_id` computation inside `View::insert`/`get`.** The probe loop needs to do `blockIdx.x * tiles_per_block + threadIdx.x / tile_size` once per call (or hoist outside the loop). When macro is off, this entire computation compiles away because `increment` is a no-op and its argument is unused. When macro is on, it's a handful of integer ops added to each `view.insert` call.

Want me to go with this design, and **`(b2)`** for sizing? If so I'll implement.

## Assistant — 2026-05-20T23:09:24.178Z

Yes — return the probe count by value. The counter lives in `view.insert`'s local register, gets returned as part of the result, and the caller chooses to accumulate it (also in a register) or ignore it. No global-memory traffic, no parameter plumbing.

```cpp
struct InsertResult {
    cuda::std::optional<Slot> leftover;
    std::uint32_t             probes;
};

struct GetResult {
    cuda::std::optional<Value> value;
    std::uint32_t              probes;
};

template <class Tile>
__device__ InsertResult insert(const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value);

template <class Tile>
__device__ GetResult get(const Tile& tile, Key key) const;
```

Inside `insert`:

```cpp
std::uint32_t my_probes = 0;
// ... probe loop ...
if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) ++my_probes;     // unconditional, register-only
// ...
return {leftover, my_probes};
```

**Memory benchmark** keeps probes in its own kernel register:

```cpp
std::uint32_t my_probes = 0;
for (op_id ...) {
    const auto r = view.insert(tile, k, v);
    if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) {
        my_probes += r.probes;
        if (r.leftover.has_value()) ++my_failures;
    }
}
if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) per_tile_probes[tile_id] = my_probes;
```

**Tests / examples / timing benchmarks** just read the field they care about:

```cpp
const auto r = view.insert(tile, k, v);
if (r.leftover.has_value()) /* probe-cap failure */ ;
// r.probes ignored — dead-stripped by the optimizer
```

**Why this keeps registers in registers:**
- `view.insert`'s `my_probes` is a function-local register, never touched by global memory.
- The return is by value; the struct fits in a couple of registers (a 32-bit Slot's worth for `leftover.value_or` payload plus a 32-bit count). The compiler passes it back through the ABI's register-return path, not through memory.
- The caller's accumulator `my_probes` is also a register. The `+= r.probes` is a register-to-register add.

**Why it's zero-cost when callers don't read `.probes`:**
- `view.insert` is a `__device__` template — always inlined into the calling kernel.
- After inlining, the local `my_probes` and its increments are just instructions in the kernel's scope.
- Standard dead-code elimination: if the kernel never reads the value, the optimizer removes the increments.
- For belt-and-suspenders, we could `#ifdef` the increment line, but I'd argue we don't need to — NVCC at `-O3` handles this case reliably.

**What disappears entirely:**
- `TileCounter` type — gone.
- `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` macro — gone.
- The `#ifdef` in `hash_table.cuh` — gone.
- Macro-conditional struct shapes, opt-in surfaces in design.md, the `#define` in memory benchmarks — all gone.

The one user-visible change is the return type: `optional<Slot>` → `InsertResult{ optional<Slot>, uint32_t }`. Callers swap `r.has_value()` for `r.leftover.has_value()` at their existing call sites.

Want me to implement?

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-20T23:12:20.002Z

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-20T23:12:27.489Z

You're right — what you want is aspect-oriented instrumentation, and C++ genuinely doesn't have it. No language-level "wrap this method with an observer" without changing the method's API. The closest things C++ offers are:

- **Templates**: change the type at the call site (multiple instantiations).
- **Macros**: change the source at preprocessing time.
- **Function pointers / `std::function`**: runtime indirection with overhead.
- **Compiler-specific attributes** (e.g. `__attribute__((instrument_function))`): change the codegen, but capture only entry/exit, not loop iterations.

None of those are a "method hook." Every option for getting probe counts out of `View::insert` either:
- adds an argument (current state — `TileCounter&`),
- changes the return type (returns-struct-with-probes), or
- goes through global memory (counter-array-in-table).

The current design is honestly the least bad of these. The `TileCounter` argument is the smallest API perturbation (one extra parameter, of an empty type when the macro is off), and it preserves register-locality.

My recommendation: **leave the current design in place**. If you want a small additional simplification, dropping the 3-arg overload (so callers always declare a `gpurhh::TileCounter counter;` in their kernel) is reasonable — but if you'd rather not perturb tests/examples/timing benchmarks, the current two-overload version is fine too.

Want to keep things as they are, or drop the no-counter overload?

---

## User — 2026-05-20T23:13:34.799Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1019`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
        // passed in: if the insertion triggered Robin Hood displacements
        // before failing, the returned Slot is the most-recently-evicted
        // victim — the originally-passed-in pair is already in the table,
        // and it is the victim that is now unreachable. Callers that need
        // to avoid data loss should buffer the returned Slot and retry it
        // elsewhere (typically by rebuilding a larger table).
        //
        // When GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS is defined, takes an additional
        // trailing reference parameter `tile_counter`. Lane 0 of the tile
        // increments it once per bucket read (i.e. once per probe loop
        // iteration, including CAS-retry re-reads of the same bucket).
        // The caller is expected to pass a per-tile register accumulator
        // and flush it to a per-tile global slot at end of kernel — no
        // atomic is involved in the increment.
        template <class Tile>
        __device__ cuda::std::optional<Slot> insert(
            const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value
#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
            , std::uint32_t& tile_counter
#endif
        );

        // Cooperative single-key lookup. Returns the stored value if the
        // key is present, or an empty optional otherwise. Every lane in the
        // tile receives the same result.
        //
        // The `tile_counter` parameter under GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
        // behaves the same way as in `insert`.
        template <class Tile>
        __device__ cuda::std::optional<Value> get(
            const Tile& tile, Key key
#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
            , std::uint32_t& tile_counter
#endif
        ) const;

        __host__ __device__ std::size_t capacity() const noexcept { return capacity_; }

    private:
        friend class HashTable;
```

</details>

{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh",
  "old_string": "inline constexpr cudaStream_t default_stream = 0;\n\n// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------\n// TileCounter: optional probe-count instrumentation passed into View::insert\n// and View::get. Without GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS the type is empty and all\n// of its methods are no-ops, so bump() calls in the probe hot loop fold to\n// nothing at compile time and the table's normal users (tests, examples,\n// timing benchmarks) pay zero overhead. With the macro defined, the type\n// holds a `std::uint32_t` register-resident counter and exposes `read()`\n// for the benchmark binaries that want to flush it out at end of kernel.\n//\n// The intent is one TileCounter per cooperative tile, allocated as a kernel\n// register at the start of the timed loop and (when the macro is on) read\n// out once before the kernel returns.\n// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------\n#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS\nstruct TileCounter {\n    std::uint32_t value_ = 0;\n    __device__ void          bump()  noexcept { ++value_; }\n    __device__ void          clear() noexcept { value_ = 0; }\n    __device__ std::uint32_t read()  const noexcept { return value_; }\n};\n#else\nstruct TileCounter {\n    __device__ void bump()  const noexcept {}\n    __device__ void clear() const noexcept {}\n    // No `read()` — there is no counter to read. Calls fail to compile in\n    // this branch, which is what we want: a binary that asks for counter\n    // values must opt into GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS.\n};\n#endif\n\n// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------\n// HashTable: host-side owner of the device-resident slot array.\n// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------",
  "new_string": "inline constexpr cudaStream_t default_stream = 0;\n\n// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------\n// HashTable: host-side owner of the device-resident slot array.\n// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------"
}
```

</details>

{

  "old_string": "    // Device-side handle. Trivially copyable; pass by value into kernels.\n    class View {\n    public:\n        // Returns nullopt on success; on probe-cap failure returns the\n        // leftover Slot that could not be placed (may be the original\n        // pair, or a Robin Hood victim displaced before the failure).\n        //\n        // The four-arg overload takes a `TileCounter&` (see below).\n        // The three-arg overload is the common case and default-\n        // constructs a throwaway counter, so callers that don't care\n        // about probe counts write `view.insert(tile, k, v)` as usual.\n        template <class Tile>\n        __device__ cuda::std::optional<Slot>\n        insert(const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value);\n\n        template <class Tile>\n        __device__ cuda::std::optional<Slot>\n        insert(const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value, TileCounter& counter);\n\n        template <class Tile>\n        __device__ cuda::std::optional<Value>\n        get(const Tile& tile, Key key) const;\n\n        template <class Tile>\n        __device__ cuda::std::optional<Value>\n        get(const Tile& tile, Key key, TileCounter& counter) const;\n    };",
  "new_string": "    // Device-side handle. Trivially copyable; pass by value into kernels.\n    class View {\n    public:\n        // Returns nullopt on success; on probe-cap failure returns the\n        // leftover Slot that could not be placed (may be the original\n        // pair, or a Robin Hood victim displaced before the failure).\n        //\n        // The `tile_counter` parameter appears only when\n        // GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS is defined. Lane 0 of the tile\n        // increments it on each bucket-load; the caller is expected\n        // to pass a per-tile register accumulator and flush it to a\n        // per-tile global slot at end of kernel.\n        template <class Tile>\n        __device__ cuda::std::optional<Slot>\n        insert(const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value\n#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS\n            , std::uint32_t& tile_counter\n#endif\n        );\n\n        template <class Tile>\n        __device__ cuda::std::optional<Value>\n        get(const Tile& tile, Key key\n#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS\n            , std::uint32_t& tile_counter\n#endif\n        ) const;\n    };"
}
```

</details>

  "old_string": "- **`GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS`** changes the shape of the `gpurhh::TileCounter` type. With the macro undefined, `TileCounter` is an empty struct with no-op `bump()` and `clear()` methods — `view.insert(tile, k, v, counter)` calls in the probe hot loop compile to identical code as if the counter weren't there at all. With the macro defined, `TileCounter` holds a `std::uint32_t` and exposes `read()` so the benchmark binaries can flush it out at end of kernel. The probe loops in `View::insert` and `View::get` always call `counter.bump()` regardless — the dispatch is in the type, not in `#ifdef`s sprinkled through the methods. Memory-utilization benchmark binaries enable this; the timing benchmarks, tests, and the example do not.",
  "new_string": "- **`GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS`** adds a trailing `std::uint32_t& tile_counter` parameter to `View::insert` and `View::get`. Lane 0 of the tile increments it on every bucket-load (a CAS-retry re-read counts too — it's a real DRAM transaction). The caller maintains a per-tile register accumulator and writes it out once at end of kernel; no atomics are involved in the probing hot loop. The memory-utilization benchmark binaries enable this; the timing benchmarks, tests, and the example do not."
}
```

</details>

## Assistant — 2026-05-20T23:16:26.986Z

Reverted. The TileCounter abstraction is gone everywhere:

- **`include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh`**: back to the original `#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` pattern — gated parameter in the View::insert/get signatures (twice each: declaration + definition), gated `++tile_counter` lines in the two probe loops.

- **`benchmarks/memory/benchmark_get.cu`**: pre-fill kernel restored to `std::uint32_t scratch_probes = 0;`; get kernel restored to `std::uint32_t my_probes = 0;` accumulated via `view.get(tile, k, my_probes)`.
- **`notes/design.md`**: API sketch, Opt-in macros section, and Per-tile probe counters subsection all back to describing the `tile_counter` argument pattern.

Back to the previously working `#ifdef`-based design with the counter as an explicit `std::uint32_t&` parameter when the macro is on.

## Assistant — 2026-05-20T23:58:29.396Z

Sketching this out — yes, this lands at a cleaner place than the current scattered `#ifdef`s. Here's what it would look like:

**One `#ifdef` block at the top of `gpurhh` namespace** — defines (1) a result struct, (2) the two type aliases, (3) a small `pack()` helper:

```cpp
#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS

template <class T>
struct ResultWithProbes {
    cuda::std::optional<T> value;
    std::uint32_t          probes;
};
template <class Slot>  using InsertResult = ResultWithProbes<Slot>;
template <class V>     using GetResult    = ResultWithProbes<V>;

template <class T>
__device__ inline auto pack(cuda::std::optional<T> v, std::uint32_t p) {
    return ResultWithProbes<T>{v, p};
}

#else

template <class Slot>  using InsertResult = cuda::std::optional<Slot>;
template <class V>     using GetResult    = cuda::std::optional<V>;

template <class T>
__device__ inline auto pack(cuda::std::optional<T> v, std::uint32_t /*p*/) {
    return v;
}

#endif
```

**`View::insert` / `View::get`** then become unconditional — no `#ifdef`s in the body:

```cpp
template <class Tile>
__device__ InsertResult<Slot> insert(const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value) {
    std::uint32_t probes = 0;

    while (probe < probe_bound) {
        const std::size_t bucket_idx = (cur_home + probe) & bucket_mask;
        if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) ++probes;        // unconditional

        // ... probe-loop body ...

        if (match_found_cas_ok) return pack<Slot>(cuda::std::nullopt, probes);
        if (empty_cas_ok)       return pack<Slot>(cuda::std::nullopt, probes);
        // ...
    }
    return pack<Slot>(Slot{cur_key, cur_value}, probes);
}
```

Same shape for `get`. Both signatures lose their conditional parameter; their return type is the alias, which is `optional<X>` when the macro is off (so the optimizer dead-strips `++probes` and `pack` becomes the identity).

**Call sites:**

```cpp
// tests / examples / timing — unchanged. InsertResult is optional<Slot> for them.
const auto leftover = view.insert(tile, k, v);
if (leftover.has_value()) /* probe-cap failure */ ;

// memory benchmark — destructured access.
const auto r = view.insert(tile, k, v);
if (r.value.has_value() && tile.thread_rank() == 0) ++my_failures;
if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) my_probes += r.probes;
```

**`#ifdef` count:**
- Today: 6 (signature + body for each of `insert`/`get`, twice — declaration + definition + body).
- Proposed: 1 block.

**Two open questions** before committing to this:

1. **Trust the optimizer to dead-strip `++probes` when macro is off?** Inlining + DCE should handle it for `__device__` templates at `-O3`, but it's not 100% guaranteed. If you want a hard guarantee, we add 2 more `#ifdef`s around the increment lines (one per method). That brings the total to 3 — still half of today's 6.

2. **Call-site shape change for the memory benchmark.** Currently it does `view.insert(tile, k, v, my_probes)` with a counter passed as a parameter. Under this design it does `auto r = view.insert(tile, k, v); my_probes += r.probes;`. Slightly more typing per loop iteration but no more parameter-list `#ifdef` in the method signature. Same machine code.

I'd lean toward the belt-and-suspenders 3-`#ifdef` version to keep "zero overhead when off" as a guaranteed property, not an optimizer-dependent one. Want to go with that?

## Assistant — 2026-05-21T04:19:14.635Z

Yes, this is the cleanest of all the options we've discussed. A bare preprocessor macro is exactly what this kind of "ergonomic ifdef" calls for — and unlike the type-alias / return-shape rewrites, semantics stay 1:1 with what's already working.

Concretely:

```cpp
#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
#define IF_GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS(...) __VA_ARGS__
#else
#define IF_GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS(...)
#endif
```

Then the four scattered conditional sites collapse to one-liners:

**Declarations** (in the View class):
```cpp
template <class Tile>
__device__ cuda::std::optional<Slot> insert(
    const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value
    IF_GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS(, std::uint32_t& tile_counter)
);

template <class Tile>
__device__ cuda::std::optional<Value> get(
    const Tile& tile, Key key
    IF_GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS(, std::uint32_t& tile_counter)
) const;
```

**Definitions** — same pattern.

**Probe-loop body**:
```cpp
IF_GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS(
    // Count one bucket-load per probe iteration. CAS-retry `continue`s
    // re-enter the loop and re-read the same bucket — those count too,
    // because they are real DRAM transactions.
    if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) ++tile_counter;
)
```

**Count of `#ifdef`s in `hash_table.cuh`**:
- Today: 6 (scattered through declaration/definition/body × insert/get).
- Proposed: 1, only at the top of the file where the macro itself is defined.

**Memory benchmark binaries**: unchanged. They still `#define GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS 1` at the top, still pass `my_probes` as the trailing parameter to `view.insert` / `view.get`. The macro is purely an in-header cosmetic.

**Tradeoffs to consider:**

1. **One extra macro name in scope.** `IF_GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` is verbose but unambiguous, and it's scoped with the `GPURHH_` prefix matching the library's convention. We could `#undef` it at the end of `hash_table.cuh` to avoid leaking it into user TUs — small additional discipline, but worth doing since it's purely internal to the header.

2. **Macros are macros.** Anyone reading `IF_GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS(, std::uint32_t& counter)` has to know the macro pattern. But it's a standard idiom (Linux kernel uses `IF_ENABLED(...)`, BSDs have `__P((...))`), so any C++ engineer recognizes it.

3. **Comma in argument list works fine.** `__VA_ARGS__` captures the leading comma; modern preprocessors (C++11+) handle this cleanly. No GNU `##__VA_ARGS__` tricks needed.

Want me to implement?

---

455	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>
456	void HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::clear(cudaStream_t stream) {
457	    const std::size_t num_buckets = capacity_ / bucket_size;
458	    cudaMemsetAsync(buckets_, empty_key_byte,
459	                    num_buckets * sizeof(Bucket), stream);
460	}
461	
462	template <class K, class V, class H, K E, class R, int CL, int WS, int MPB>
463	template <class Tile>
464	__device__ auto HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::View::insert(
465	    const Tile& tile, K key, V value
466	#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
467	    , std::uint32_t& tile_counter
468	#endif
469	)
470	    -> cuda::std::optional<Slot>
471	{
472	    // Bucketed Robin Hood insertion with lock-free CAS. The tile carries an
473	    // in-flight pair (cur_key, cur_value) that may change due to Robin Hood
474	    // displacement — when we evict a "richer" resident, that resident
475	    // becomes our new in-flight pair and we keep probing from the next
476	    // bucket.
477	
478	    K cur_key   = key;
479	    V cur_value = value;
480	
481	    const std::size_t num_buckets = capacity_ / bucket_size;
482	    const std::size_t bucket_mask = num_buckets > 0 ? num_buckets - 1 : 0;
483	    const std::size_t probe_bound = num_buckets < max_probe_buckets
484	        ? num_buckets
485	        : static_cast<std::size_t>(max_probe_buckets);
486	
487	    std::size_t cur_home = (hash_(cur_key) & capacity_mask_) / bucket_size;
488	    std::size_t probe    = 0;
489	
490	    while (probe < probe_bound) {
491	        const std::size_t bucket_idx = (cur_home + probe) & bucket_mask;

575	template <class Tile>
576	__device__ cuda::std::optional<V>
577	HashTable<K, V, H, E, R, CL, WS, MPB>::View::get(
578	    const Tile& tile, K key
579	#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
580	    , std::uint32_t& tile_counter
581	#endif
582	) const
583	{
584	    // Bucket-level Robin Hood lookup. Probe home_bucket, then home_bucket+1,
585	    // etc., wrapping modulo num_buckets. Each iteration is one coalesced
586	    // cache-line load (one slot per tile lane) and a few tile-wide ballots
587	    // to identify the terminating condition.
588	
589	    const auto h = hash_(key);
590	    const std::size_t home_slot   = h & capacity_mask_;
591	    const std::size_t home_bucket = home_slot / bucket_size;
592	    const std::size_t num_buckets = capacity_ / bucket_size;
593	    const std::size_t bucket_mask = num_buckets > 0 ? num_buckets - 1 : 0;
594	
595	    // Probe at most max_probe_buckets buckets, or every bucket in the table
596	    // if it's smaller than the cap.
597	    const std::size_t probe_bound = num_buckets < max_probe_buckets
598	        ? num_buckets
599	        : static_cast<std::size_t>(max_probe_buckets);
600	
601	    for (std::size_t probe = 0; probe < probe_bound; ++probe) {
602	        const std::size_t bucket_idx = (home_bucket + probe) & bucket_mask;
603	
604	#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS

## Assistant — 2026-05-21T04:21:53.322Z

Done. `hash_table.cuh` now has exactly two preprocessor blocks: the `IF_GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` macro definition at the top (one `#ifdef/#else/#endif`) and the matching `#undef` at the bottom. The four previously scattered sites — two signatures and two probe-loop increments — are each now a single-line macro call:

```cpp
IF_GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS(, std::uint32_t& tile_counter)
IF_GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS(if (tile.thread_rank() == 0) ++tile_counter;)
```

Semantics are identical to the previous `#ifdef` design — same parameter, same probe-bump behaviour, same machine code with or without the macro defined. Memory benchmark binaries unchanged: they still `#define GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS 1` and pass their `my_probes` register through.

The `#undef` at the bottom keeps the macro from leaking into translation units that include the header.

## Assistant — 2026-05-21T04:30:35.199Z
