1	# CLAUDE.md
2	
3	Project context for Claude Code.
4	This file is loaded automatically at the start of every conversation in this repository.
5	
6	## What this project is
7	
8	A header-only CUDA library implementing a parallel hash table for NVIDIA GPUs, using Robin Hood open-addressing with bucketed (cache-line-aligned), sub-warp-cooperative probing.
9	The design target is bandwidth-bound performance on large tables.
10	
11	## Repository layout
12	
13	- `include/gpurhh/` — public headers.
14	  This is what users `#include` after adding `include/` to their include path.
15	  The library is header-only; everything ships from here.
16	- `tests/` — tests built against the public header.
17	  Each `test_*.cu` file is compiled as its own executable with its own `main`, scoped to one topic (a public method, a trait, etc.).
18	  Shared driver kernels, table aliases, and host-side bulk helpers live in `tests/kernels.cuh`; the CUDA error-check operator and other test-wide infrastructure (including the macro that exposes `HashTable::data()`) live in `tests/tests.cuh`.
19	- `examples/` — small standalone programs demonstrating use of the library.
20	  Currently a placeholder; to be filled in with a real construct + bulk insert + bulk lookup demo.
21	- `notes/` — design notes, written for humans.
22	  These files are the canonical record of *why* the implementation looks the way it does.
23	  They are intended to eventually become the project's documentation, but they are not documentation yet — they are working notes that capture decisions as we make them.
24	  Read these before touching code.
25	- `Makefile` — builds tests and examples with `nvcc`. Defaults to C++20, `sm_89`, and no optimization.
26	  Build modes: `make` (no `-O`), `make release` (`-O3`), `make debug` (`-O0 -G -g`).
27	  Other useful targets: `make test` builds and runs all tests, `make clean` wipes `build/`. Override flags via env vars (`make ARCH=sm_90 CXX_STD=c++17`).
28	
29	When new design decisions are made or revised, update the relevant note in `notes/` rather than scattering rationale across commit messages or code comments.
30	
31	## Conventions for working in this repo
32	
33	- **Notes are working documents that will become documentation.**
34	  Keep them prose-first and structured.
35	  Avoid jargon shorthand that only makes sense inside one conversation, since these will eventually be read by people who weren't in the room.
36	- **Architecture-derived constants are derived, not chosen.**
37	  `BucketSize`, `TileSize`, `TilesPerWarp` are all functions of `SlotBytes`, `CacheLineBytes`, and `WarpSize`.
38	  The latter two should be table-level template parameters, not literals embedded in the implementation.
39	
40	## Out of scope (for now)
41	
42	- Deletion (insert + lookup first).
43	- Resize / dynamic growth.
44	- Multi-GPU.
45	- AMD / non-NVIDIA backends — but the abstractions should not preclude them.
46	
```

</details>

## User — 2026-05-27T02:31:57.537Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1252`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
1	# GPU Robin Hood Hash Table — Design Notes
2	
3	This document records the design and decisions behind `gpurhh`, a header-only CUDA library implementing a parallel Robin Hood hash table for NVIDIA GPUs.
4	It covers what the library does, why it looks the way it does, the public API it exposes, how the implementation is tested, and what is deliberately out of scope.
5	
6	## Goals
7	
8	- **Header-only CUDA library.**
9	  A single `.cuh` (or a small set) that users `#include` and template-instantiate for their key/value types.
10	  No separate compilation of the table itself.
11	- **Massively parallel insertion.**
12	  A batch of `N` keys is inserted by `N` (or more) GPU threads cooperatively.
13	  Worst-case behavior must remain bounded even under heavy contention.
14	- **Memory-bandwidth bound.**
15	  On large tables, the table is much bigger than the L2 cache, so performance is determined by how many useful bytes each DRAM transaction delivers.
16	  The aim is to come as close to peak HBM bandwidth as the access pattern allows.
17	
18	## Language and toolchain
19	
20	- **Target language: C++20.**
21	- **Target CUDA toolkit: CUDA 13.x.**
22	  Driven by two requirements: `-std=c++20` support in nvcc (CUDA 12.0+) and `cuda::std::bit_cast` in libcu++ (CUDA 13.0 was the first version where this combination worked cleanly on the test system).
23	
24	Within the implementation, we stick to C++17-shape idioms by default and only reach for C++20 features where they demonstrably improve the code.
25	The C++20 uses today are:
26	
27	- `std::bit_width` in `next_pow2` (host-side, compile-time).
28	- Host-side compile-time `std::bit_cast` for the `empty_key_byte` extraction and the memsettability `static_assert` lambda.
29	- Device-side `cuda::std::bit_cast` (libcu++) for the runtime bit-reinterpret in `default_hash`.
30	  The libcu++ version is mandatory here because `std::bit_cast` is host-only `constexpr` in libstdc++; calling it from a `__device__` function is a hard error in CUDA 13.x.
31	
32	Concepts on the `Hash` template parameter, `std::source_location` in place of `__FILE__` / `__LINE__` macros, ranges, and coroutines are all deferred until they pay for themselves.
33	
34	The C++20 baseline is conservative future-proofing: it lets us reach for the cleaner spelling when we want it without revisiting the build settings each time.
35	
36	## Why Robin Hood?
37	
38	Open-addressing with the invariant *"the entry in slot `i` whose probe distance is smaller gets evicted by an entry whose probe distance is larger"* gives:
39	
40	- A tight, low-variance distribution of probe distances → small worst-case lookup length even at load factors of 0.85–0.9.
41	- A natural early-exit rule for lookups (stop as soon as the slot's probe distance is smaller than the probe distance of the key we're searching for).
42	- Backward-shift deletion that avoids tombstones.
43	
44	These properties matter even more on a GPU than on a CPU because the warp's tail latency is set by the longest probe sequence among its 32 lanes.
45	
46	## Slot layout
47	
48	A slot is the unit of an atomic update.
49	The simplest design that admits lock-free CAS on a single machine word is:
50	
51	```
52	struct alignas(sizeof(Key) + sizeof(Value)) Slot {
53	    Key   key;       // typically uint32_t or uint64_t
54	    Value value;     // typically the same width as Key
55	};
56	```
57	
58	Empty slots use a reserved sentinel key (default: all-bits-set for unsigned integer Key); users are forbidden from inserting that key.
59	The packed slot supports two widths:
60	
61	1. **64-bit slots** (4-byte key + 4-byte value): atomic CAS via `cuda::atomic_ref<Slot>`, which on every NVIDIA arch compiles down to a 64-bit `atom.cas`. Fast.
62	2. **128-bit slots** (8-byte key + 8-byte value): atomic CAS still goes through `cuda::atomic_ref<Slot>`; on sm_90+ this is the single-instruction `atom.cas.b128`, on sm_70–sm_89 libcu++ emulates with a lock-table fallback. Functional everywhere, but the emulated path is significantly slower than 64-bit CAS under contention.
63	
64	We use the same control flow for both widths — the only thing that changes is which atomic instruction `cuda::atomic_ref::compare_exchange_strong` resolves to. A parallel-arrays layout (separate keys[] and values[]) is rejected: it halves the per-CAS bytes but at the cost of an awkward second-read-the-key step for finders that complicates correctness.
65	
66	**Probe distance.**
67	We do *not* store probe distance in the slot.
68	It is derivable as `(slot_index - hash(key)) mod capacity`.
69	Storing it would cost bits we'd rather give to the payload, and the derivation is a single subtract once we've read the slot.
70	
71	## Capacity, hash function, load factor
72	
73	- Capacity is a power of two so `mod` becomes a mask.
74	  Users specify a target load factor (default 0.7); we round capacity up to `next_pow2(N / load_factor)`.
75	- Hash function is a template parameter; default is a finalizer-style mixer (e.g. the splitmix64/Murmur3 finalizer) that is cheap on GPU and decorrelates structured keys well.
76	- Robin Hood tolerates higher load than linear probing, but on a GPU the warp tail-latency argument pushes us back toward 0.7–0.8 rather than 0.9.
77	
78	### Where the power-of-two assumption is load-bearing
79	
80	The "capacity is a power of two" constraint is not just a convenience — it's wired into multiple hot-path operations. Anyone touching these sites should know that breaking the invariant would require switching every `& mask` back to a `% capacity`, which is materially slower on GPU (multi-cycle integer divmod vs. a single AND).
81	
82	In `include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh`:
83	
84	- **`HashTable` constructor**: `next_pow2(min_capacity)` rounds the user-supplied capacity up; `capacity_mask_ = capacity_ - 1` is cached for use across the hot paths. Capacity can grow by up to 2× over what the user asked for. The trade-off is a one-cycle mask vs. a multi-cycle modulus on every probe step.
85	- **Home-slot computation** (insert and get): `home_slot = hash(key) & capacity_mask_` (and `home_bucket = home_slot / bucket_size`). Replacing `&` with `%` would also require capacity_mask_ to be replaced by the raw capacity, doubling the mod cost on every probe.
86	- **Probe wrap-around**: `bucket_idx = (home_bucket + probe) & bucket_mask`, where `bucket_mask = num_buckets - 1`. Since `num_buckets = capacity / bucket_size` and both are powers of two, the bucket count is also pow-2 and admits a mask.
87	- **Probe-distance derivation** (used inside the Robin Hood swap check): `resident_probe = (bucket_idx - resident_home) & bucket_mask` — this handles wrap-around correctly precisely because `bucket_mask = num_buckets - 1`.
88	- **Tile size and warp size**: independently asserted pow-2 via `static_assert((tile_size & (tile_size - 1)) == 0, ...)`. Used by `tiles_per_block = block_size / tile_size` and by `cg::tiled_partition<tile_size>`, which itself requires a pow-2 tile size.
89	
90	In benchmarks (`benchmarks/benchmarks.cuh`):
91	
92	- **Key generation** (`map_to_range`): folds cuRAND bits to `[0, range)` via `x & (range - 1)` when range is a power of two, falling back to `x % range` otherwise. The sweep driver always uses `key_range = capacity`, so the mask path is taken in practice.
93	
94	A subtler dependency lives in the **default hash function**. With a pow-2 mask, only the low bits of `hash(key)` reach `capacity_mask_` — the high bits are thrown away. If a user supplied an identity hash on structured keys (e.g. dense integer IDs), low-bit clustering would map directly into slot clustering and probe lengths would explode. The default `default_hash<uint32_t>` is `fmix32`, a finalizer-style mixer that spreads bits well, so the low bits of `hash(k)` are pseudo-uniform even when keys are not. Users supplying their own `Hash` should preserve this property; passing a non-mixing hash with a pow-2 capacity is the textbook way to break a fast hash table.
95	
96	## Reduction operators
97	
98	When `View::insert` encounters an existing entry for the same key, the new value is combined with the existing one through a `Reduction` functor — a template parameter of `HashTable`, defaulting to `gpurhh::replace_op`.
99	Stateless and trivially copyable, the functor sits inside the `View` by value:
100	
101	```cpp
102	struct replace_op {
103	    template <class T>
104	    __device__ T operator()(T /*existing*/, T incoming) const noexcept { return incoming; }
105	};
106	
107	struct sum_op {
108	    template <class T>
109	    __device__ T operator()(T existing, T incoming) const noexcept { return existing + incoming; }
110	};
111	```
112	
113	`replace_op` gives last-writer-wins semantics. `sum_op` accumulates values for repeated-key inserts — useful as a parallel histogram or counter primitive. Users can define their own functor with the same signature.
114	
115	For commutative and associative reductions like `sum_op`, the final stored value is fully deterministic regardless of how the CAS retries interleave across tiles: the result is just the reduction of all inserted values for that key. For non-commutative reductions, the outcome depends on which writers win their CAS races.
116	
117	## Parallel insertion algorithm
118	
119	Each thread owns one `(key, value)` pair.
120	The thread walks the probe sequence and, at every slot, performs one of three actions:
121	
122	1. **Empty slot, CAS succeeds.** Done.
123	2. **Same key, CAS succeeds.** This is an update; done.
124	3. **Occupied by another key.**
125	   Compare probe distances.
126	   If the resident is "richer" (its probe distance from *its* home is smaller than ours from *our* home), we attempt to CAS our pair into the slot.
127	   On success, we adopt the displaced pair and continue probing from this slot.
128	   If the resident is "poorer" or equal, we move on.
129	
130	A CAS failure is treated as "the world changed under us": we re-read the slot and re-evaluate without advancing the probe index.
131	This is the standard lock-free retry.
132	
133	### Why this is correct under contention
134	
135	The Robin Hood invariant is maintained by every individual CAS because each CAS only swaps in a pair with probe distance strictly greater than the one already in the slot (or the slot was empty).
136	Two concurrent inserts targeting the same slot serialize through CAS, and whichever loses retries with the freshest state.
137	There is no global ordering requirement: as long as every write respects the local invariant, the final table is a valid Robin Hood layout for *some* permutation of the input.
138	
139	Caveat: the *value* portion of an update (case 2) needs care.
140	If two threads insert the same key concurrently and the user wants "last write wins" semantics, we need an additional CAS loop on the value, or we tolerate non-deterministic resolution.
141	
142	## Achieving bandwidth saturation
143	
144	The 64-bit slot is small relative to a 128-byte L2 cache line: one transaction brings 16 slots.
145	The bandwidth strategy follows from this:
146	
147	- **Warp-cooperative probing.**
148	  Within a warp, threads whose home slots land in the same cache line should service their probes from one load, not 32 loads.
149	  We have the warp ballot/shuffle primitives to do this cheaply.
150	- **Bucketed probing.**
151	  Treat the table as an array of *buckets* of 16 slots (one cache line).
152	  A probe inspects an entire bucket at a time — one coalesced 128-byte load — and uses warp intrinsics (`__ballot_sync`, `__match_any_sync`) to find the candidate slot.
153	  Insertion still uses CAS on a single 8-byte slot inside the bucket, but the read traffic per probe is exactly one cache line.
154	  This is the same idea as `bucketed_cuckoo` and Bucketized Cuckoo Hashing.
155	- **Probe length capping.**
156	  Exposed as a `MaxProbeBuckets` template parameter (default 8).
157	  An insert that would place a key past the cap fails (the caller is expected to rehash); a get that probes the cap without finding the key returns "not found" (correct because of the insert-side cap).
158	  With Robin Hood at load factor ~0.85 and `bucket_size` 16, the expected longest probe is well under the default cap; the cap protects against adversarial inputs and very high load factors.
159	  Empirically, on uniform-random keys with α=1, the default cap-8 budget handles input load factor F ≤ 2 (≈ 80% slot occupancy) with zero failures, and starts losing about 0.1% of inserts at F = 3 (≈ 95% occupancy) — a real but small number that callers can either tolerate, drop (cf. lossy-storage workloads), or recover by buffering the leftover Slots and replaying into a larger table.
160	  Crucially, a failing insert hands the leftover `(key, value)` back to the caller via the return value rather than dropping it on the floor.
161	  This matters because the leftover may not be the original input: if Robin Hood displacements happened during the probe, the originally-passed-in pair is already in the table and the leftover is the most-recently-evicted victim.
162	  Returning it is what makes the operation lossless under failure — callers can buffer leftovers and replay them into a rebuilt larger table.
163	- **Avoid divergence.**
164	  All threads in a warp execute the same probe loop.
165	  The "I'm done, others aren't" case is handled with masked operations rather than early-return-then-divergence.
166	
167	If we get this right, an insert of `N` keys at load factor 0.7 should cost approximately `(1/(1-α)) * sizeof(slot)` bytes per insert in expectation, which is the information-theoretic floor for an open-addressed table with this layout.
168	
169	## Work decomposition: how insertions map onto the GPU hierarchy
170	
171	Before discussing parameters, we need to commit to a unit of work — what GPU resource cooperates on a single key insertion.
172	There are four candidates:
173	
174	| Mapping              | Threads / key | Probe is one coalesced load? | Keys in flight / warp | Wasted lanes / probe |
175	| -------------------- | ------------- | ---------------------------- | --------------------- | -------------------- |
176	| Thread per key       | 1             | No — 32 unrelated lines      | 32                    | 0                    |
177	| **Sub-warp tile**    | **B**         | **Yes — exactly one line**   | **32 / B**            | **0 (if B ∣ 32)**    |
178	| Warp per key         | 32            | Yes, but ≤ B lanes used      | 1                     | 32 − B               |
179	| Block per key        | 128–1024      | Yes, but lanes mostly idle   | ≪ 1                   | huge                 |
180	
181	- **Thread-per-key** is the naive choice and exactly the case our bandwidth strategy is designed to avoid.
182	  Each warp issues 32 cache-line loads per probe step instead of one, and we lose any coalescing benefit.
183	  Fatal for our stated goal.
184	- **Block-per-key** is too coarse: there is no useful work for 128+ threads on a single key.
185	- **Warp-per-key** is the classic "warp-cooperative hashing" design but wastes lanes whenever the bucket is narrower than the warp.
186	  With 8-byte slots and a 128-byte line on NVIDIA, the bucket holds 16 slots, so half of every warp sits idle.
187	- **Sub-warp tile per key**, with tile width equal to the bucket width, is the one design where *one tile-probe = one cache-line transaction* with no idle lanes.
188	  This is the design we adopt.
189	
190	### The structure that follows
191	
192	1. A **bucket** is a contiguous, cache-line-aligned run of `B` slots.
193	2. A **cooperative tile** of `B` threads (`cooperative_groups::thread_block_tile<B>`) owns one in-flight key at a time.
194	   Each tile lane holds one slot of the current bucket in a register; one bucket probe is one coalesced load.
195	3. **Tiles per warp** = `WarpSize / B`.
196	   For NVIDIA with 8-byte slots that is 2.
197	4. **Block size** is purely an occupancy knob — some multiple of `WarpSize`, tuned empirically.
198	5. **Work assignment** is a tile-strided loop: tile index `t` (across the whole grid) handles input keys `t, t+T, t+2T, …` where `T` is the total number of resident tiles.
199	   Naturally load-balanced and robust to input skew.
200	
201	### Per-tile probe step
202	
203	On each probe:
204	
205	1. The `B` lanes cooperatively load one bucket — each lane reads one slot, fully coalesced.
206	2. `tile.ballot(...)` over a predicate identifies empty slots, key matches, or Robin-Hood-displaceable slots in a single warp intrinsic.
207	3. The lane corresponding to the chosen slot performs the CAS; the outcome is broadcast to the rest of the tile with `tile.shfl(...)`.
208	4. On displacement, the evicted `(key, value)` becomes the tile's new in-flight pair and the tile advances by one bucket.
209	
210	Every step is a warp-intrinsic operation on a single cache line, which is what makes the bandwidth argument hold.
211	
212	### Parameters: what is forced by the architecture, what is free
213	
214	Once we commit to the sub-warp tile design, almost every constant is *derived* from the hardware, not chosen:
215	
216	| Parameter          | Value (default)              | Source                                   |
217	| ------------------ | ---------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
218	| `SlotBytes`        | 8 (4+4) or 16 (8+8)          | Key/value packing choice                 |
219	| `CacheLineBytes`   | 128 (NVIDIA)                 | Hardware                                 |
220	| `BucketSize`       | `CacheLineBytes / SlotBytes` | **Derived**                              |
221	| `WarpSize`         | 32 (NVIDIA)                  | Hardware                                 |
222	| `TileSize`         | `BucketSize`                 | **Derived** (the central design choice)  |
223	| `TilesPerWarp`     | `WarpSize / BucketSize`      | **Derived**                              |
224	| `BlockSize`        | tuned (e.g. 128 or 256)      | Occupancy — empirical                    |
225	| `TargetLoadFactor` | tuned (e.g. 0.7)             | Robin Hood quality — empirical           |
226	| `MaxProbeBuckets`  | 8                            | Worst-case time vs. insert-failure rate  |
227	
228	So the only genuinely free knobs are `BlockSize`, `TargetLoadFactor`, and `MaxProbeBuckets`.
229	The rest should be expressed in the code as compile-time constants derived from `SlotBytes`, `CacheLineBytes`, and `WarpSize`, with the latter two being template parameters of the table (with sensible defaults) rather than hard-coded.
230	
231	### Why the abstraction matters — an AMD aside (out of scope for v1)
232	
233	AMD GPUs (CDNA, RDNA) have a 64-byte cache line and a 64-thread wavefront.
234	With 8-byte slots:
235	
236	- NVIDIA: `BucketSize` = 128 / 8 = 16, `TilesPerWarp` = 32 / 16 = **2**.
237	- AMD:    `BucketSize` =  64 / 8 =  8, `TilesPerWarp` = 64 /  8 = **8**.
238	
239	The same algorithm runs unchanged; only the derived constants differ.
240	We are not targeting AMD, but it is the reason we want `CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` to be parameters of the table type rather than hard-coded `128` and `32`.
241	Anything that ends up baked in as a literal in the implementation is a portability bug waiting to happen.
242	
243	## Lookup
244	
245	`View::get` follows the same probing rule as insert, read-only, returning `cuda::std::optional<Value>` — populated on hit, empty on miss. The probe terminates as soon as it sees one of:
246	
247	- A key match → return `{value}`.
248	- An empty slot → return `nullopt`. Robin Hood would have placed the key before this point.
249	- A resident "richer" than ours (smaller probe distance) → return `nullopt`. Our key would have evicted that resident if it were in the table, contradiction.
250	- The probe cap → return `nullopt`. The insert-side cap means the key cannot live further than this.
251	
252	Lookups are read-only and trivially parallel: concurrent lookups never race each other.
253	
254	**Concurrent get during insert is not linearizable.** A get running simultaneously with an insert can return `nullopt` for a key that is about to be inserted, or for a key in the middle of being displaced. Linearizability would require per-slot versioning. Most workloads sync between insert and get phases.
255	
256	## Deletion
257	
258	Not supported. The natural design is backward-shift deletion, but a correct lock-free implementation is significantly harder than insert/lookup, and the use case (this is an append-only table for most consumers) hasn't pushed us to do it.
259	
260	## Public API
261	
262	```cpp
263	namespace gpurhh {
264	
265	// Named alias for the CUDA runtime's default stream. Used as the
266	// default argument for host-side methods that issue async work.
267	inline constexpr cudaStream_t default_stream = 0;
268	
269	template <class Key,
270	          class Value,
271	          class Hash             = default_hash<Key>,
272	          Key   EmptyKey         = default_empty_key<Key>::key,
273	          class Reduction        = replace_op,
274	          int   CacheLineBytes   = 128,
275	          int   WarpSize         = 32,
276	          int   MaxProbeBuckets  = 8>
277	class HashTable {
278	public:
279	    // Host-side resource management. Non-copyable, movable.
280	    explicit HashTable(std::size_t min_capacity);
281	    ~HashTable();
282	    HashTable(HashTable&&) noexcept;
283	    HashTable& operator=(HashTable&&) noexcept;
284	
285	    // Empty the table — every slot reset to (empty_key, _). Async on
286	    // the given stream; caller is responsible for synchronizing before
287	    // any operation that depends on the cleared state.
288	    void clear(cudaStream_t stream = default_stream);
289	
290	    // Device-side handle. Trivially copyable; pass by value into kernels.
291	    class View {
292	    public:
293	        // Returns nullopt on success; on probe-cap failure returns the
294	        // leftover Slot that could not be placed (may be the original
295	        // pair, or a Robin Hood victim displaced before the failure).
296	        //
297	        // The `tile_counter` parameter appears only when
298	        // GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS is defined. Lane 0 of the tile
299	        // increments it on each bucket-load; the caller is expected
300	        // to pass a per-tile register accumulator and flush it to a
301	        // per-tile global slot at end of kernel.
302	        template <class Tile>
303	        __device__ cuda::std::optional<Slot>
304	        insert(const Tile& tile, Key key, Value value
305	#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
306	            , std::uint32_t& tile_counter
307	#endif
308	        );
309	
310	        template <class Tile>
311	        __device__ cuda::std::optional<Value>
312	        get(const Tile& tile, Key key
313	#ifdef GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS
314	            , std::uint32_t& tile_counter
315	#endif
316	        ) const;
317	    };
318	
319	    View view() const noexcept;
320	
321	    // Actual capacity, in slots, rounded up to the next power of two and
322	    // never less than `bucket_size`.
323	    std::size_t capacity() const noexcept;
324	
325	    // Direct pointer access to the device-resident bucket array.
326	    // Provided for tests, diagnostics, and benchmark instrumentation —
327	    // the `data()` name (matching the `std::vector::data` convention)
328	    // and the comment in the header are enough warning that callers
329	    // are bypassing the table's concurrency contract.
330	    Bucket*       data() noexcept;
331	    const Bucket* data() const noexcept;
332	};
333	
334	// Diagnostic helper. Lives in <gpurhh/print.cuh>, a separate header so
335	// the core library stays independent of <cstdio> and <vector>. Reads
336	// the bucket array via HashTable::data().
337	template <class Table>
338	void print_slots(const Table& table, std::size_t start, std::size_t stop);
339	
340	}  // namespace gpurhh
341	```
342	
343	### Opt-in macros
344	
345	One macro gates functionality that the core library doesn't expose by default. It must be defined in the translation unit *before* any `<gpurhh/...>` header is included:
346	
347	- **`GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS`** adds a trailing `std::uint32_t& tile_counter` parameter to `View::insert` and `View::get`. Lane 0 of the tile increments it on every bucket-load (a CAS-retry re-read counts too — it's a real DRAM transaction). The caller maintains a per-tile register accumulator and writes it out once at end of kernel; no atomics are involved in the probing hot loop. The memory-bandwidth benchmark binaries enable this; the timing benchmarks, tests, and the example do not.
348	
349	The `View` is the centerpiece for header-only use: a user kernel that already has its keys in registers calls `view.insert(tile, ...)` or `view.get(tile, ...)` directly.
350	Bulk host-side operations are deliberately *not* part of the table — callers manage their device buffers and write their own driver kernels.
351	The tests in `tests/kernels.cuh` and `tests/isolated.cuh` show the canonical pattern; `examples/basic.cu` is a minimal standalone walkthrough.
352	
353	## Testing
354	
355	The test suite lives in `tests/`, with each `test_*.cu` compiled into its own executable.
356	
357	- **`test_construction.cu`** — construct / destruct, move semantics, edge sizes (capacity == bucket_size, capacity below bucket_size), moved-from state behavior, and `clear()` returning the bucket array to the empty-byte pattern.
358	- **`test_combined.cu`** — end-to-end insert + get round-trips through the public API.
359	- **`test_get.cu`** — `View::get` in isolation. Pre-states are hand-built with `IdentityHash` so the probe sequence and home buckets are deterministic; the test then verifies `get`'s match / empty / richer-resident / wrap-around behavior.
360	- **`test_insert.cu`** — `View::insert` in isolation. Some tests use hand-computed expected layouts; all run a Robin Hood invariant scanner over the final state as a safety net. Covers the probe-cap failure path and concurrent same-key inserts.
361	- **`test_concurrency.cu`** — heavy parallel contention: adversarial displacement chains, many concurrent gets, mixed new / duplicate inserts, parallel saturation of the probe cap.
362	- **`test_randomized.cu`** — randomized stress. Uniform-key inserts at small (4 KB table) and gigabyte (1 GB table) scales; a sum-reduction stress test with random keys and deliberate duplicates whose expected per-key sum is computed on the host.
363	
364	Three shared infrastructure headers:
365	
366	- **`tests/tests.cuh`** — the `CUDA_CHECK` postfix error-check operator (`expr >> CUDA_CHECK`).
367	- **`tests/kernels.cuh`** — the `Table` alias (with `default_hash`), bulk insert/get host helpers, and end-to-end driver kernels. Used by the "combined" and construction tests.
368	- **`tests/isolated.cuh`** — the `TestTable` alias (with `IdentityHash` for predictable home buckets), templated `run_insert` / `run_get` / `run_get_one` / `run_insert_with_outcomes`, the `assert_robin_hood_invariant` scanner, and `set_state` / `read_state` for direct table-memory manipulation via `HashTable::data()`.
369	
370	The `IdentityHash` is the enabling choice for the isolated tests: with `hash(K) = K`, the home bucket of a key is fully predictable from its value, so we can construct any valid Robin Hood state with one `cudaMemcpy` and hand-predict the outcome of small insert sequences. The randomized tests apply a bit-mixing function (the same `fmix32` finalizer used by `default_hash`) as a permutation on sequential integers — this gives distinct random-looking keys without the host-side dedup overhead that an `unordered_set` would require, which is what makes the gigabyte-scale tests fit in memory.
371	
372	The test suite runs about 35 tests across ~1100 lines of code, against ~350 lines of library code. The high ratio is deliberate: this is a concurrent data structure, and the failure modes for concurrent CAS retries, displacement chains, and probe-cap interactions are subtle enough that we put significant effort into checking each path.
373	
374	## Benchmarking
375	
376	Throughput and instrumentation measurements live in `benchmarks/`, split into two studies with their own directory subtree and their own CSV output tree.
377	
378	- **`benchmarks/timing/`** — apples-to-apples kernel throughput against optional baselines (`cuCollections::static_map`, `WarpCore::SingleValueHashTable`). gpurhh's binaries here use `replace_op` reduction and `MaxProbeBuckets = 1 << 20` (effectively uncapped), matching the baselines' unbounded-probe semantics. Two executables: `benchmark_insert`, `benchmark_get`. Baseline equivalents live under `benchmarks/timing/baselines/{cuco,cuco_dh,warpcore}/` and append to the same per-workload CSVs; the `library` column distinguishes their rows. The `cuco/` baseline uses cuCollections's default linear probing (CG=4); the `cuco_dh/` baseline reuses the same headers but specializes `static_map` with `double_hashing<8, ...>`, isolating the probing-scheme variable within a single library so the family-vs-implementation axis is testable separately.
379	- **`benchmarks/memory_bandwidth/`** — the bandwidth study. `benchmark_memcpy` provides the empirical DRAM ceiling; `benchmark_insert` and `benchmark_get` are gpurhh's design under instrumentation (`sum_op` reduction, default `MaxProbeBuckets = 8`, per-tile probe / failure / hit counters via `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS`). The CSVs from this study let you compute precise per-op DRAM traffic — `total_probes × sizeof(Bucket) / time_ms` — and compare it against memcpy's ceiling. No library baselines, since neither cuco nor warpcore exposes the per-probe counters needed for the bandwidth analysis.
380	
381	The split exists because the two studies want fundamentally different configurations: timing needs the most permissive probe behavior to make per-library performance comparable; the bandwidth study wants the design's actual default (capped probes, real reduction work) so we can read the probe / failure / hit characteristics back out and convert them into bandwidth numbers.
382	
383	`benchmark_memcpy` is the peak-bandwidth reference. Times `cudaMemcpyAsync(... D2D)` and a trivial `uint4`-vectorized copy kernel on a buffer of configurable size. The CSV stores `dram_bytes = 2 * payload_bytes` (one read of src + one write of dst), so the downstream bandwidth computation is simply `dram_bytes / time_ms` — and this is directly comparable to the hash-table benchmarks' `total_probes × sizeof(Bucket) / time_ms`, since both metrics are bytes through the DRAM controller per second. Recording the 2× factor at write time rather than relying on downstream scripts to apply it keeps the convention impossible to misuse in analysis.
384	
385	### Workload knobs
386	
387	- **`capacity`** is held at 1 GiB by default — well past the L2 cache on any sm_89 device, putting the kernel firmly in the DRAM-bound regime.
388	- **Key distribution** is raw `Uniform(0, 2^32)` — every uint32 is equally likely, no clamping. With capacity = 2^27, this gives `α = key_range / capacity ≈ 32`: keys are effectively unique. This is the collision-resolution regime where table designs actually differ; at much smaller α the table fills very slowly with duplicates and linear probing's pathological clustering never engages.
389	- **`F = n_ops / capacity`** is the swept axis. The grid is `F ∈ {0.5, 0.7, 0.85, 0.95, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0}`. With α ≈ 32, effective occupancy ≈ F up to ~0.97, so F = 0.9–1.0 is the high-occupancy regime where Robin Hood's variance reduction matters; F > 1 over-subscribes the table (more unique keys than slots) and the sweep measures how each library degrades — both throughput (`time_ms`) and how many of those keys actually landed (`n_unique` and `drops`).
390	- **`block_size ∈ {64, 128, 256, 512, 1024}`** is swept for the gpurhh binaries to spot-check that launch shape doesn't dominate. cuco / warpcore pick their own internal launch shapes.
391	
392	### Experiment phases
393	
394	Every insert benchmark structures its work via `run_benchmark_loop`, which carves each rep into three callbacks. **Only the middle phase (`launch`) sits inside the timing window** — `EventTimer::begin()` / `end_ms()` bracket exactly that callback's content, nothing more.
395	
396	**Once at startup (untimed, before the loop).**
397	- Allocate `d_keys`, `d_values`, and `d_sorted` device buffers, each `n_ops × uint32`.
398	- `cudaMemset(d_values, 0, ...)` once. Under `replace_op` the value content doesn't affect kernel work, so a zero buffer suffices; gpurhh, cuco, and warpcore all do this so the kernel pays an apples-to-apples `d_values` read.
399	- Construct the table (`gpurhh::HashTable`, `cuco::static_map`, or `warpcore::SingleValueHashTable`).
400	- Construct a single `UniformKeyGenerator` (cuRAND Philox4_32_10) seeded with `--seed`.
401	- For cuco: build a `thrust::counting_iterator → cuco::pair{d_keys[i], d_values[i]}` transform iterator for the bulk-insert call.
402	
403	**`setup` callback (untimed, runs before each warmup and each timed rep).**
404	- Empty the table (`table.clear()` / `map.clear_async()` / `table.init()`).
405	- Generate fresh input keys: `fill_uniform_keys(d_keys, n_ops, gen)` advances the cuRAND stream by `n_ops` draws. Across reps the generator state continues, so the 16 reps see iid draws rather than 16 timings of one frozen input — statistically equivalent to 16 independent seeds but with CUDA / table / generator setup paid once.
406	- Count distinct inputs for drop accounting: copy `d_keys → d_sorted`, `thrust::sort`, `thrust::unique`, record `n_unique`. This is the ground-truth denominator for the drops measurement: at α ≈ 32 most inputs are unique but ~1–2% are birthday-collision duplicates, and we don't want input duplicates to show up as drops.
407	
408	**`launch` callback (timed window).**
409	- One bulk insert: gpurhh's grid-stride insert kernel, cuco's `map.insert_async(pair_begin, pair_begin + n_ops)`, or warpcore's `table.insert(d_keys, d_values, n_ops)`. That's it.
410	
411	**`after` callback (untimed, runs after each timed rep).**
412	- Count occupied slots in the table:
413	  - gpurhh: `count_occupied_slots(reinterpret_cast<Slot*>(table.data()), capacity, empty_key)` — a `thrust::count_if` scan in `benchmarks.cuh`. We use the table's `data()` accessor to view the bucket storage as a flat slot array.
414	  - cuco: `map.size()` (synchronous, built-in).
415	  - warpcore: `table.size()` (synchronous, built-in).
416	- Compute `drops = n_unique - occupied`. This is the count of keys the library tried to insert and couldn't fit, with input duplicates already factored out via `n_unique`.
417	- Emit one CSV row with `time_ms`, `n_unique`, `drops`, and the configuration columns.
418	
419	The get benchmark follows the same phase structure with one difference: the pre-fill is one-shot (untimed, before the loop) rather than per-rep, since pre-fill input variability is a second-order effect for the get throughput we're measuring. Only the get-key stream is refreshed in `setup` per rep.
420	
421	### Per-tile probe / failure / hit counters
422	
423	The `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` macro (see ## Public API → Opt-in macros) adds a trailing `std::uint32_t&` parameter to `View::insert` / `View::get`. The memory-benchmark kernels declare a per-tile register accumulator, pass it through every `view.insert` / `view.get` call in the grid-stride loop, and flush one value per tile to a global array at end of kernel. No atomics fire in the probe hot loop. The host sums per-tile values into a grand total per rep.
424	
425	The memory-bandwidth CSVs (`memory_bandwidth/insert.csv`, `memory_bandwidth/get.csv`) add `total_probes` and `total_failures` (insert) or `total_probes`, `total_hits`, `total_misses` (get) on top of the timing CSV schema. Downstream analysis derives metrics like average probe length (`total_probes / n_ops`) and bandwidth-corrected throughput (`total_probes × sizeof(Bucket) / time_ms`), the latter being directly comparable to the memcpy ceiling in the same directory.
426	
427	At low F the corrected and uncorrected throughput numbers converge (average probe length ≈ 1). At high F the corrected number diverges upward — that gap is itself a useful signal about how hard Robin Hood is working.
428	
429	### Sweep driver
430	
431	`scripts/benchmark.sh` runs the full grid. Flags select which study to run:
432	
433	- `--timing` — gpurhh's timing sweep (insert + get).
434	- `--memory-bandwidth` — the bandwidth sweep (memcpy ceiling + counter-instrumented gpurhh insert + get).
435	- `--cuco` — cuCollections baseline, default linear probing (timing only).
436	- `--cuco-dh` — cuCollections baseline re-specialized with double hashing (timing only).
437	- `--warpcore` — WarpCore baseline (timing only).
438	- `--all` — every flag above; missing binaries are skipped with a notice rather than erroring.
439	
440	Output goes to `output/<timestamp>/timing/{insert,get}.csv` and `output/<timestamp>/memory_bandwidth/{memcpy,insert,get}.csv`, with a `run_info.txt` sidecar (`nvcc --version`, `uname`, `nvidia-smi` GPU summary) and a `benchmark.log` transcript at the top of the output dir. The seed is fixed at 1 across the sweep; `reps = 16` per invocation, with the cuRAND stream refreshing keys before every rep so each rep is an independent input draw.
441	
442	`make benchmarks` builds the binaries at `-O3` (the default for the benchmark pattern rule). The sweep is launched separately from `scripts/benchmark.sh` because runs are heavy. Numbers from unoptimized builds are meaningless, so the `-O3` default is effectively non-overridable.
443	
444	## Known limitations
445	
446	- **Deletion** is not supported. Users that need it allocate a fresh table and re-insert.
447	- **Resize** is not supported. Users that need growth allocate a bigger table and re-insert.
448	- **Multi-GPU** is not supported.
449	- **Concurrent get during insert is not linearizable.** A get running simultaneously with insert can return `nullopt` for a key in flight. Workloads should synchronize between insert and get phases.
450	- **128-bit slot performance on pre-Hopper hardware** (sm_70–sm_89) is bottlenecked by libcu++'s lock-table emulation of 128-bit CAS. Functionally correct, but considerably slower than the native single-instruction path that sm_90+ has.
451	
452	## Out of scope
453	
454	- **Non-NVIDIA backends.** The abstractions (`CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` as table-level template parameters) don't preclude AMD support, but we haven't written or tested it. The same algorithm with AMD's 64-byte cache line and 64-thread wavefront would give `BucketSize = 8` and `TilesPerWarp = 8`.
455	- **Full stream support.** `clear()` takes a `cudaStream_t` (defaulting to `gpurhh::default_stream`). The constructor does not — `cudaMalloc` is synchronous and dominates the construction cost, so threading a stream through the slot-init memset would buy nothing while leaving the constructor's overall behavior confusingly half-async. The destructor doesn't take a stream either; destruction is a host-side lifecycle event, and the caller is expected to synchronize relevant streams before letting the table go out of scope.
456	
457	## Future work
458	
459	- Validate the 128-bit slot path on a Hopper (sm_90+) system; benchmark it against the 64-bit path.
460	- Benchmark against `cuCollections` and `WarpCore` on the same workloads to validate the bandwidth-bound claim. Pinned commits for both are inlined in `scripts/setup-baselines.sh`, which clones each upstream with a sparse-checkout of just the `include/` subtree into `external/<lib>/`. `external/` itself is gitignored, so downstream users of `gpurhh` who recursively clone aren't forced to pull benchmark-only dependencies.
461	- Add a CPU reference Robin Hood for randomized differential testing if test signal demands it (currently considered low priority — the invariant scanner plus the sum-reduction equivalence check cover most of what differential testing would add).
462	- Implement deletion (backward-shift, lock-free) if a use case appears.
463	
```

</details>

{

Defaults to C++20, `sm_89`, and no optimization.\n  Build modes: `make` (no `-O`), `make release` (`-O3`), `make debug` (`-O0 -G -g`).\n  Other useful targets: `make test` builds and runs all tests, `make clean` wipes `build/`. Override flags via env vars (`make ARCH=sm_90 CXX_STD=c++17`).",
  "new_string": "- `tests/` — tests built against the public header.\n  Each `test_*.cu` file is compiled as its own executable with its own `main`, scoped to one topic (a public method, a trait, etc.).\n  Shared driver kernels, table aliases, and host-side bulk helpers live in `tests/kernels.cuh`; the CUDA error-check operator lives in `tests/tests.cuh`; `tests/isolated.cuh` provides the `IdentityHash`-based fixture that lets tests hand-build and verify Robin Hood states.\n- `examples/` — small standalone programs demonstrating use of the library.\n  `examples/basic.cu` is a self-contained construct + bulk insert + bulk lookup + pretty-print walkthrough.\n- `benchmarks/` — split into `timing/` (apples-to-apples library throughput vs. cuCollections and WarpCore) and `memory_bandwidth/` (counter-instrumented gpurhh study + memcpy ceiling reference).\n  Run via `scripts/benchmark.sh`; output lands under `output/<timestamp>/`.\n  `external/` (gitignored) holds the sparse-checkout of baseline headers; populate with `scripts/setup-baselines.sh`.\n- `notes/` — design notes, written for humans.\n  These files are the canonical record of *why* the implementation looks the way it does.\n  They are intended to eventually become the project's documentation, but they are not documentation yet — they are working notes that capture decisions as we make them.\n  Read these before touching code.\n- `Makefile` — builds tests, examples, and benchmarks with `nvcc`. Defaults to C++20, `sm_89`, and no optimization for tests/examples; `-O3` for benchmarks (since unoptimized throughput numbers are meaningless).\n  Targets: `make` (= `make all` — tests + examples + benchmarks), `make tests`, `make examples`, `make benchmarks`, `make test` (build + run all tests), `make clean`.\n  Override flags via env vars (`make ARCH=sm_90 CXX_STD=c++17 OPT=-O3`); benchmark binaries always build at `-O3` regardless of `OPT`."
}
```

</details>

  "new_string": "## Capacity, hash function, load factor\n\n- Capacity is a power of two so `mod` becomes a mask.\n  The constructor takes `min_capacity` (in slots) and rounds up to the next power of two, never below `bucket_size`. Users targeting a specific load factor compute the slot count themselves (e.g. `min_capacity = ceil(N / target_load_factor)` for an expected `N` insertions).\n- Hash function is a template parameter; default is a finalizer-style mixer (e.g. the splitmix64/Murmur3 finalizer) that is cheap on GPU and decorrelates structured keys well.\n- Robin Hood tolerates higher load than linear probing, but on a GPU the warp tail-latency argument pushes the practical sweet spot back toward 0.7–0.8 rather than 0.9."
}

**2. The probe-length-capping paragraph** references an α=1 empirical claim that's no longer the sweep configuration:

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-27T02:34:55.205Z

**→ Tool call: `Edit`** (id `tool-1260`)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/notes/design.md",
  "old_string": "- **Probe length capping.**\n  Exposed as a `MaxProbeBuckets` template parameter (default 8).\n  An insert that would place a key past the cap fails (the caller is expected to rehash); a get that probes the cap without finding the key returns \"not found\" (correct because of the insert-side cap).\n  With Robin Hood at load factor ~0.85 and `bucket_size` 16, the expected longest probe is well under the default cap; the cap protects against adversarial inputs and very high load factors.\n  Empirically, on uniform-random keys with α=1, the default cap-8 budget handles input load factor F ≤ 2 (≈ 80% slot occupancy) with zero failures, and starts losing about 0.1% of inserts at F = 3 (≈ 95% occupancy) — a real but small number that callers can either tolerate, drop (cf. lossy-storage workloads), or recover by buffering the leftover Slots and replaying into a larger table.\n  Crucially, a failing insert hands the leftover `(key, value)` back to the caller via the return value rather than dropping it on the floor.\n  This matters because the leftover may not be the original input: if Robin Hood displacements happened during the probe, the originally-passed-in pair is already in the table and the leftover is the most-recently-evicted victim.\n  Returning it is what makes the operation lossless under failure — callers can buffer leftovers and replay them into a rebuilt larger table.",
  "new_string": "- **Probe length capping.**\n  Exposed as a `MaxProbeBuckets` template parameter (default 8).\n  An insert that would place a key past the cap fails (the caller is expected to rehash); a get that probes the cap without finding the key returns \"not found\" (correct because of the insert-side cap).\n  With Robin Hood at load factor ~0.85 and `bucket_size` 16, the expected longest probe is well under the default cap; the cap protects against adversarial inputs and very high load factors.\n  Empirically, on `Uniform(0, 2^32)` uint32 keys (effectively unique inputs), the default cap-8 budget handles `F = n_ops / capacity` up to ~1 with essentially zero failures, and starts losing inserts as `F` grows past 1 into the over-subscription regime (see `benchmarks/memory_bandwidth/insert.csv`'s `total_failures` column).\n  Crucially, a failing insert hands the leftover `(key, value)` back to the caller via the return value rather than dropping it on the floor.\n  This matters because the leftover may not be the original input: if Robin Hood displacements happened during the probe, the originally-passed-in pair is already in the table and the leftover is the most-recently-evicted victim.\n  Returning it is what makes the operation lossless under failure — callers can buffer leftovers and replay them into a rebuilt larger table."
}
```

</details>

{
  "replace_all": false,

  "old_string": "- **`GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS`** adds a trailing `std::uint32_t& tile_counter` parameter to `View::insert` and `View::get`. Lane 0 of the tile increments it on every bucket-load (a CAS-retry re-read counts too — it's a real DRAM transaction). The caller maintains a per-tile register accumulator and writes it out once at end of kernel; no atomics are involved in the probing hot loop. The memory-bandwidth benchmark binaries enable this; the timing benchmarks, tests, and the example do not.",
  "new_string": "- **`GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS`** adds a trailing `std::uint32_t& tile_counter` parameter to `View::insert` and `View::get`. Lane 0 of the tile increments it on every cache-line-sized memory event: each bucket-load at the top of a probe-loop iteration (including CAS-retry re-reads of the same bucket), *and* each attempted CAS — every CAS requires the line in an exclusive state, which is an additional memory transaction beyond the cooperative load. `View::get` is read-only and only bumps on bucket loads. The caller maintains a per-tile register accumulator and writes it out once at end of kernel; no atomics fire in the hot loop. Downstream bandwidth = `counter × sizeof(Bucket) / time`. The memory-bandwidth benchmark binaries enable this; the timing benchmarks, tests, and the example do not."
}
```

</details>

**→ Tool call: `Edit`** (id `tool-1262`)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/notes/design.md",
  "old_string": "- **`F = n_ops / capacity`** is the swept axis. The grid is `F ∈ {0.5, 0.7, 0.85, 0.95, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0}`. With α ≈ 32, effective occupancy ≈ F up to ~0.97, so F = 0.9–1.0 is the high-occupancy regime where Robin Hood's variance reduction matters; F > 1 over-subscribes the table (more unique keys than slots) and the sweep measures how each library degrades — both throughput (`time_ms`) and how many of those keys actually landed (`n_unique` and `drops`).",
  "new_string": "- **`F = n_ops / capacity`** is the swept axis. The timing sweep uses `F ∈ {0.5, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 1.0}` — capped at F=1 because gpurhh's timing build sets `MaxProbeBuckets = 1 << 20` (matching the baselines' effectively-unbounded probing), and at F > 1 with α ≈ 32 each doomed insert would walk its full probe budget, turning the sweep into a multi-hour stall. A redraw guard in the timing-insert binary aborts if a cuRAND draw produces `n_unique > capacity` after a few retries. The memory-bandwidth sweep extends through `F ∈ {0.5, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0}` — with `MaxProbeBuckets = 8` (the design default) failed inserts give up cheaply, so the over-subscription regime is tractable and the failure / hit / probe counters become informative."
}
```

</details>

## Assistant — 2026-05-27T20:20:03.563Z

## Assistant — 2026-05-27T20:20:25.760Z

For this project I'd recommend **MkDocs with the Material theme**, optionally paired with **mkdoxy** for C++ API reference extraction. Three reasons:

1. **Your design notes are already markdown-first**, so they import with essentially no edits. `notes/design.md` becomes a docs page directly. The same is true for any later additions (analysis writeups, benchmark commentary, etc.).
2. **Material is the best-looking technical-docs theme of the modern crop** — admonitions, content tabs, LaTeX math via MathJax (which you'll want for the probe-length formulas, occupancy `α(1 - exp(-F/α))`, etc.), CUDA-aware code highlighting, dark mode, instant client-side search, all out of the box with zero config.
3. **GitHub Pages deployment is one command** (`mkdocs gh-deploy`) and CI integration is a 10-line YAML file.

mkdoxy is the auto-C++-reference bit: it runs Doxygen against your headers and emits markdown that MkDocs can render alongside the prose docs. You get an "API Reference" section that mirrors the comments already in `include/gpurhh/hash_table.cuh` without writing anything new — the existing docstrings on `HashTable`, `View::insert`, `View::get`, `clear`, `data()`, etc. just become rendered API pages. Skip this if you decide the design notes alone are enough; add it later if/when users want a function-by-function reference.

**The site structure I'd suggest:**

```
docs/
├── index.md                  # short pitch + quick example
├── getting-started.md        # build, include, instantiate, insert, get
├── design/                   # imported from notes/design.md, split into pages
│   ├── overview.md
│   ├── slot-layout.md
│   ├── parallel-insert.md
│   ├── work-decomposition.md
│   └── ...
├── benchmarks/
│   ├── methodology.md        # the experiment-phases section
│   └── results.md            # plots + headline numbers
└── api/                      # mkdoxy output (optional)
```

The split of `notes/design.md` into per-topic pages is purely so the sidebar TOC is navigable; the content is unchanged.

**Two alternatives worth knowing about, briefly:**

- **Sphinx + MyST** — the scientific/numerical community standard (numpy, scipy, PyTorch all use it). More powerful for long-form structured docs with cross-references, better LaTeX, more mature C++ integration via Breathe/Exhale. Heavier setup; the syntax (even with MyST) has more sharp edges than MkDocs's plain markdown. I'd pick this if you wanted a more academic-paper-shaped output, or if you ever cared about generating a PDF version.
- **mdBook** — markdown-first like MkDocs, simpler, runs as a single Rust binary. No plugin ecosystem to speak of, no built-in C++ API reference. Right pick if you want zero infrastructure burden and the design notes are the entire docs. Used by the Rust project itself and CNCF projects (e.g. Cilium's eBPF book), so the bar is high; just narrower than MkDocs.

**My honest take**: this project sits right in the sweet spot for MkDocs Material. The library is small enough that an API reference isn't urgent (the prose docs cover the conceptual surface), but mature enough that having a real site beats just-a-README. Setting it up is maybe an hour:

1. `pip install mkdocs mkdocs-material`
2. `mkdocs new .` at the repo root (creates `mkdocs.yml` and `docs/`)
3. Drop `notes/design.md` into `docs/` (possibly split into per-section pages); add a short `index.md`.
4. Configure the Material theme in `mkdocs.yml` — 20-line file.
5. `mkdocs serve` to preview locally; `mkdocs gh-deploy` to publish.

Add mkdoxy later if/when you want the API reference. Skip Sphinx unless you want the academic feel or PDF output.

Want me to draft the initial `mkdocs.yml` and a docs scaffold?

## Assistant — 2026-05-27T20:25:25.208Z

Good order — Doxygen first lets you pick a frontend later (MkDocs/mkdoxy, Sphinx/Breathe, or just Doxygen's own HTML output) without committing now.

**Concretely, the integration is four pieces:**

1. **`Doxyfile`** at the repo root. Hand-tuned for the project shape. Key choices:
   - `INPUT = include/ notes/` — pick up the headers *and* the design notes (Doxygen renders markdown files as pages out of the box).
   - `FILE_PATTERNS = *.cuh *.cu *.md` — the `.cu`/`.cuh` extensions aren't in Doxygen's defaults.
   - `RECURSIVE = YES`.
   - `EXTRACT_ALL = YES` — header-only library, every public member is interesting.
   - `JAVADOC_AUTOBRIEF = YES` — first sentence of a comment becomes the brief.
   - `USE_MDFILE_AS_MAINPAGE = notes/design.md` — landing page is the design doc.
   - `PREDEFINED = __device__= __host__= __global__= __restrict__=` — preprocess CUDA decorators away so Doxygen sees clean C++.
   - `GENERATE_LATEX = NO`, `HAVE_DOT = YES` (if you want call/inheritance diagrams; needs graphviz).
   - `OUTPUT_DIRECTORY = build/doxygen` — keeps the output out of source.
   - `PROJECT_NAME = gpurhh`, `PROJECT_BRIEF = "GPU Robin Hood hash table"`.

2. **`Makefile` target** — one line:
   ```make
   docs:
   	doxygen Doxyfile
   ```
   Add `docs/` (or `build/doxygen/`) to `.gitignore`.

3. **Comment style — light touch.** The existing comments in `hash_table.cuh` are mostly already Doxygen-readable. Doxygen picks up `//` blocks immediately preceding a declaration as documentation when `EXTRACT_ALL = YES` + `JAVADOC_AUTOBRIEF = YES`. The cases where it'll miss or misattribute are:
   - Section-banner comments (`// ---- HashTable -----`) attached to nothing.
   - Comments separated from declarations by blank lines.
   - Free-floating explanations inside function bodies (Doxygen ignores these — fine, that's the right call).
   
   I'd run Doxygen once, look at the HTML, and selectively convert a handful of `//` → `///` (or wrap with `/** */`) at the spots Doxygen misses. Maybe ~10 edits total, all in `hash_table.cuh`. No new tags needed unless we want to add `@param`/`@returns` later for richer API pages — strongly optional.

4. **GitHub Pages publishing (optional, later)** — `.github/workflows/docs.yml` that runs Doxygen on push to `main` and pushes `build/doxygen/html` to a `gh-pages` branch. ~20 lines of YAML.

**One subtle thing worth deciding upfront:**

Doxygen's `notes/design.md` rendering will work but the result is plain Doxygen-styled HTML, which is functional-but-dated-looking. If you're sure you want a Material-themed frontend later, the natural shape is:

- Doxygen produces XML in `build/doxygen/xml/` (set `GENERATE_XML = YES`).
- The XML is the input to mkdoxy (for MkDocs) or Breathe (for Sphinx).
- HTML output from Doxygen itself becomes a fallback / debugging artifact.

So I'd configure Doxygen with both `GENERATE_HTML = YES` (for direct viewing) and `GENERATE_XML = YES` (for the eventual MkDocs pipeline). Cheap to do up front; saves a config round-trip when you pick the frontend.

Want me to write the `Doxyfile`, add the `make docs` target, and adjust the handful of comments that need re-tagging? I'd skip the GitHub Actions piece for now and add it once you've picked the public frontend.

## Assistant — 2026-05-27T20:27:57.404Z

Doxygen itself is location-agnostic — it just consumes whatever directories `INPUT` lists. But there's a community convention worth knowing, plus a project-specific call we should make.

**The conventions, briefly:**

- **`docs/`** is the most common location for narrative documentation in C++ projects (cmake, llvm, boost, all the obvious ones).
- **`doc/`** is the older Unix convention; less common in new C++ projects but you still see it (qt, some autotools projects).
- **`notes/`** typically signals "internal / working notes" to a casual reader — not where they'd look for documentation.

Doxygen itself supports:
- **`@mainpage`** — a single landing page. Pointed at via `USE_MDFILE_AS_MAINPAGE = <path>`.
- **`@page`** — other narrative pages, each gets a URL. Auto-generated when Doxygen sees a markdown file in `INPUT` that *isn't* the mainpage.
- **`@section`** / `@subsection` — only relevant if you write `.dox` files in Doxygen's custom syntax. Markdown headers (`#`, `##`) just work — no need.

**My recommendation for this project:** rename `notes/` → `docs/`, point Doxygen at `docs/` for narrative content + `include/` for API extraction. Two reasons:

1. **The content has graduated.** `notes/design.md` started as "working notes that will eventually become documentation." With Doxygen integration, it *becomes* documentation. The directory name should match the new role.
2. **Discoverability.** A reader hitting the repo for the first time looks for `docs/`. Finding `notes/` first signals "internal scratch" and they might bounce.

Two sub-decisions while we're there:

**(a) Keep `design.md` as one file, or split?**

Currently `design.md` is ~460 lines spanning ~10 major sections (Goals, Slot layout, Capacity, Parallel insertion, Work decomposition, Lookup, Public API, Testing, Benchmarking, Known limitations, Out of scope, Future work). Doxygen will render this as one long page with the auto-TOC sidebar; readable but you scroll a lot.

A natural split:

```
docs/
├── index.md             # landing page (the Goals + brief intro) — USE_MDFILE_AS_MAINPAGE
├── architecture.md      # Slot layout, Work decomposition, why Robin Hood
├── algorithm.md         # Parallel insertion, Lookup, correctness
├── implementation.md    # Capacity, hash function, power-of-two assumptions
├── benchmarks.md        # Everything currently under ## Benchmarking
└── limitations.md       # Known limitations, out of scope, future work
```

Each becomes a separate page in the generated site, with Doxygen's "Related Pages" sidebar linking them. The Public API section becomes redundant once we have the auto-generated API reference from the headers — we can drop the hand-maintained API sketch entirely.

I'd lean **split it**, since we're already touching things. The hand-maintained API sketch in `design.md` is exactly the kind of content that drifts (we just spent time syncing it after several refactors) and is the strongest argument for letting Doxygen own that bit.

**(b) `@mainpage` choice.**

Either:
- A short new `docs/index.md` (project pitch, install/build, "where to go next" links) — feels more like a documentation site.
- Or just `docs/design.md` becomes the mainpage with no separate landing page — minimum churn.

Either works; the first is more discoverable, the second is less work.

**Concrete proposal:**

1. `git mv notes docs` — single rename, history preserved.
2. Either leave `docs/design.md` as-is and point `USE_MDFILE_AS_MAINPAGE = docs/design.md`, *or* split into the layout above with a new `docs/index.md` as the mainpage.
3. Update `CLAUDE.md` and any other references to `notes/`.

Want to do the bare-minimum rename (1 + 3) now and revisit splitting later, or do the full split as part of the Doxygen integration?
