<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "file_path": "[repo]/docs/benchmarks.md",
  "content": "# Benchmarks\n\nThroughput and instrumentation measurements live in `benchmarks/`, split into two studies with their own directory subtree and their own CSV output tree.\n\n- **`benchmarks/timing/`** — apples-to-apples kernel throughput against optional baselines (`cuCollections::static_map`, `WarpCore::SingleValueHashTable`).\n  gpurhh's binaries here use `replace_op` reduction and `MaxProbeBuckets = 1 << 20` (effectively uncapped), matching the baselines' unbounded-probe semantics.\n  Two executables: `benchmark_insert`, `benchmark_get`.\n  Baseline equivalents live under `benchmarks/timing/baselines/{cuco,cuco_dh,warpcore}/` and append to the same per-workload CSVs; the `library` column distinguishes their rows.\n  The `cuco/` baseline uses cuCollections's default linear probing (CG=4); the `cuco_dh/` baseline reuses the same headers but specializes `static_map` with `double_hashing<8, ...>`, isolating the probing-scheme variable within a single library so the family-vs-implementation axis is testable separately.\n- **`benchmarks/memory_bandwidth/`** — the bandwidth study.\n  `benchmark_memcpy` provides the empirical DRAM ceiling; `benchmark_insert` and `benchmark_get` are gpurhh's design under instrumentation (`sum_op` reduction, default `MaxProbeBuckets = 8`, per-tile probe / failure / hit counters via `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS`).\n  The CSVs from this study let you compute precise per-op DRAM traffic — `total_probes × sizeof(Bucket) / time_ms` — and compare it against memcpy's ceiling.\n  No library baselines, since neither cuco nor warpcore exposes the per-probe counters needed for the bandwidth analysis.\n\nThe split exists because the two studies want fundamentally different configurations: timing needs the most permissive probe behavior to make per-library performance comparable; the bandwidth study wants the design's actual default (capped probes, real reduction work) so we can read the probe / failure / hit characteristics back out and convert them into bandwidth numbers.\n\n`benchmark_memcpy` is the peak-bandwidth reference.\nTimes `cudaMemcpyAsync(... D2D)` and a trivial `uint4`-vectorized copy kernel on a buffer of configurable size.\nThe CSV stores `dram_bytes = 2 * payload_bytes` (one read of src + one write of dst), so the downstream bandwidth computation is simply `dram_bytes / time_ms` — and this is directly comparable to the hash-table benchmarks' `total_probes × sizeof(Bucket) / time_ms`, since both metrics are bytes through the DRAM controller per second.\nRecording the 2× factor at write time rather than relying on downstream scripts to apply it keeps the convention impossible to misuse in analysis.\n\n## Workload knobs\n\n- **`capacity`** is held at 1 GiB by default — well past the L2 cache on any sm_89 device, putting the kernel firmly in the DRAM-bound regime.\n- **Key distribution** is raw `Uniform(0, 2^32)` — every uint32 is equally likely, no clamping.\n  With capacity = 2^27, this gives `α = key_range / capacity ≈ 32`: keys are effectively unique.\n  This is the collision-resolution regime where table designs actually differ; at much smaller α the table fills very slowly with duplicates and linear probing's pathological clustering never engages.\n- **`F = n_ops / capacity`** is the swept axis.\n  The timing sweep uses `F ∈ {0.5, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 1.0}` — capped at F=1 because gpurhh's timing build sets `MaxProbeBuckets = 1 << 20` (matching the baselines' effectively-unbounded probing), and at F > 1 with α ≈ 32 each doomed insert would walk its full probe budget, turning the sweep into a multi-hour stall.\n  A redraw guard in the timing-insert binary aborts if a cuRAND draw produces `n_unique > capacity` after a few retries.\n  The memory-bandwidth sweep extends through `F ∈ {0.5, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0}` — with `MaxProbeBuckets = 8` (the design default) failed inserts give up cheaply, so the over-subscription regime is tractable and the failure / hit / probe counters become informative.\n- **`block_size ∈ {64, 128, 256, 512, 1024}`** is swept for the gpurhh binaries to spot-check that launch shape doesn't dominate.\n  cuco / warpcore pick their own internal launch shapes.\n\n## Experiment phases\n\nEvery insert benchmark structures its work via `run_benchmark_loop`, which carves each rep into three callbacks.\n**Only the middle phase (`launch`) sits inside the timing window** — `EventTimer::begin()` / `end_ms()` bracket exactly that callback's content, nothing more.\n\n**Once at startup (untimed, before the loop).**\n\n- Allocate `d_keys`, `d_values`, and `d_sorted` device buffers, each `n_ops × uint32`.\n- `cudaMemset(d_values, 0, ...)` once. Under `replace_op` the value content doesn't affect kernel work, so a zero buffer suffices; gpurhh, cuco, and warpcore all do this so the kernel pays an apples-to-apples `d_values` read.\n- Construct the table (`gpurhh::HashTable`, `cuco::static_map`, or `warpcore::SingleValueHashTable`).\n- Construct a single `UniformKeyGenerator` (cuRAND Philox4_32_10) seeded with `--seed`.\n- For cuco: build a `thrust::counting_iterator → cuco::pair{d_keys[i], d_values[i]}` transform iterator for the bulk-insert call.\n\n**`setup` callback (untimed, runs before each warmup and each timed rep).**\n\n- Empty the table (`table.clear()` / `map.clear_async()` / `table.init()`).\n- Generate fresh input keys: `fill_uniform_keys(d_keys, n_ops, gen)` advances the cuRAND stream by `n_ops` draws.\n  Across reps the generator state continues, so the 16 reps see iid draws rather than 16 timings of one frozen input — statistically equivalent to 16 independent seeds but with CUDA / table / generator setup paid once.\n- Count distinct inputs for drop accounting: copy `d_keys → d_sorted`, `thrust::sort`, `thrust::unique`, record `n_unique`.\n  This is the ground-truth denominator for the drops measurement: at α ≈ 32 most inputs are unique but ~1–2% are birthday-collision duplicates, and we don't want input duplicates to show up as drops.\n\n**`launch` callback (timed window).**\n\n- One bulk insert: gpurhh's grid-stride insert kernel, cuco's `map.insert_async(pair_begin, pair_begin + n_ops)`, or warpcore's `table.insert(d_keys, d_values, n_ops)`. That's it.\n\n**`after` callback (untimed, runs after each timed rep).**\n\n- Count occupied slots in the table:\n  - gpurhh: `count_occupied_slots(reinterpret_cast<Slot*>(table.data()), capacity, empty_key)` — a `thrust::count_if` scan in `benchmarks.cuh`. We use the table's `data()` accessor to view the bucket storage as a flat slot array.\n  - cuco: `map.size()` (synchronous, built-in).\n  - warpcore: `table.size()` (synchronous, built-in).\n- Compute `drops = n_unique - occupied`. This is the count of keys the library tried to insert and couldn't fit, with input duplicates already factored out via `n_unique`.\n- Emit one CSV row with `time_ms`, `n_unique`, `drops`, and the configuration columns.\n\nThe get benchmark follows the same phase structure with one difference: the pre-fill is one-shot (untimed, before the loop) rather than per-rep, since pre-fill input variability is a second-order effect for the get throughput we're measuring.\nOnly the get-key stream is refreshed in `setup` per rep.\n\n## Per-tile probe / failure / hit counters\n\nThe `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` macro (see [Usage: Opt-in macros](usage.md#opt-in-macros)) adds a trailing `std::uint32_t&` parameter to `View::insert` / `View::get`.\nThe memory-benchmark kernels declare a per-tile register accumulator, pass it through every `view.insert` / `view.get` call in the grid-stride loop, and flush one value per tile to a global array at end of kernel.\nNo atomics fire in the probe hot loop.\nThe host sums per-tile values into a grand total per rep.\n\nThe memory-bandwidth CSVs (`memory_bandwidth/insert.csv`, `memory_bandwidth/get.csv`) add `total_probes` and `total_failures` (insert) or `total_probes`, `total_hits`, `total_misses` (get) on top of the timing CSV schema.\nDownstream analysis derives metrics like average probe length (`total_probes / n_ops`) and bandwidth-corrected throughput (`total_probes × sizeof(Bucket) / time_ms`), the latter being directly comparable to the memcpy ceiling in the same directory.\n\nAt low F the corrected and uncorrected throughput numbers converge (average probe length ≈ 1).\nAt high F the corrected number diverges upward — that gap is itself a useful signal about how hard Robin Hood is working.\n\n## Sweep driver\n\n`scripts/benchmark.sh` runs the full grid. Flags select which study to run:\n\n- `--timing` — gpurhh's timing sweep (insert + get).\n- `--memory-bandwidth` — the bandwidth sweep (memcpy ceiling + counter-instrumented gpurhh insert + get).\n- `--cuco` — cuCollections baseline, default linear probing (timing only).\n- `--cuco-dh` — cuCollections baseline re-specialized with double hashing (timing only).\n- `--warpcore` — WarpCore baseline (timing only).\n- `--all` — every flag above; missing binaries are skipped with a notice rather than erroring.\n\nOutput goes to `output/<timestamp>/timing/{insert,get}.csv` and `output/<timestamp>/memory_bandwidth/{memcpy,insert,get}.csv`, with a `run_info.txt` sidecar (`nvcc --version`, `uname`, `nvidia-smi` GPU summary) and a `benchmark.log` transcript at the top of the output dir.\nThe seed is fixed at 1 across the sweep; `reps = 16` per invocation, with the cuRAND stream refreshing keys before every rep so each rep is an independent input draw.\n\n`make benchmarks` builds the binaries at `-O3` (the default for the benchmark pattern rule).\nThe sweep is launched separately from `scripts/benchmark.sh` because runs are heavy.\nNumbers from unoptimized builds are meaningless, so the `-O3` default is effectively non-overridable.\n"
}
```

</details>

## Assistant — 2026-05-27T20:36:33.699Z

**→ Tool call: `Write`** (id `tool-1277`)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "file_path": "[repo]/docs/limitations.md",
  "content": "# Limitations, scope, and future work\n\n## Known limitations\n\n- **Deletion** is not supported. Users that need it allocate a fresh table and re-insert.\n- **Resize** is not supported. Users that need growth allocate a bigger table and re-insert.\n- **Multi-GPU** is not supported.\n- **Concurrent get during insert is not linearizable.** A get running simultaneously with insert can return `nullopt` for a key in flight. Workloads should synchronize between insert and get phases.\n- **128-bit slot performance on pre-Hopper hardware** (sm_70–sm_89) is bottlenecked by libcu++'s lock-table emulation of 128-bit CAS. Functionally correct, but considerably slower than the native single-instruction path that sm_90+ has.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- **Non-NVIDIA backends.** The abstractions (`CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` as table-level template parameters) don't preclude AMD support, but we haven't written or tested it. The same algorithm with AMD's 64-byte cache line and 64-thread wavefront would give `BucketSize = 8` and `TilesPerWarp = 8`.\n- **Full stream support.** `clear()` takes a `cudaStream_t` (defaulting to `gpurhh::default_stream`). The constructor does not — `cudaMalloc` is synchronous and dominates the construction cost, so threading a stream through the slot-init memset would buy nothing while leaving the constructor's overall behavior confusingly half-async. The destructor doesn't take a stream either; destruction is a host-side lifecycle event, and the caller is expected to synchronize relevant streams before letting the table go out of scope.\n\n## Future work\n\n- Validate the 128-bit slot path on a Hopper (sm_90+) system; benchmark it against the 64-bit path.\n- Add a CPU reference Robin Hood for randomized differential testing if test signal demands it (currently considered low priority — the invariant scanner plus the sum-reduction equivalence check cover most of what differential testing would add).\n- Implement deletion (backward-shift, lock-free) if a use case appears.\n- Consider the inner-loop \"exhaust all empty / displaceable slots in the current bucket snapshot before re-reading\" optimization (warpcore-style). Sketched, prototyped, and measured to be performance-neutral at our scale (low CAS contention dominates); kept on the back burner in case the contention profile shifts.\n"
}
```

</details>

3	// gpurhh — GPU Robin Hood Hash table.
4	//
5	// Header-only CUDA library implementing a parallel hash table for NVIDIA GPUs,
6	// using Robin Hood open-addressing with bucketed, sub-warp-cooperative probing.
7	//
8	// See the notes/ directory for the design rationale behind this layout.
9	//
10	// The hash table itself lives in device memory. Host code constructs and
11	// destructs a HashTable, which allocates and zero-initializes the slot array
12	// on the device. All operations on the contents — insert, get — are device
13	// code, exposed through the View handle which is meant to be passed by value
14	// into user kernels. Data movement between host and device (e.g. building the
15	// input arrays, reading back results) is the caller's responsibility.
```

## Assistant — 2026-05-27T20:39:11.359Z

**→ Tool call: `Write`** (id `tool-1293`)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "file_path": "[repo]/README.md",
  "content": "# gpurhh — GPU Robin Hood Hash Table\n\nA header-only CUDA library implementing a parallel hash table for NVIDIA GPUs, using Robin Hood open-addressing with bucketed (cache-line-aligned), sub-warp-cooperative probing.\nThe design target is bandwidth-bound performance on large tables.\n\n## Status\n\nInsert and lookup are implemented and tested.\nDeletion, dynamic resize, and multi-GPU are deliberately out of scope for now — see [docs/limitations.md](docs/limitations.md).\n\n## Requirements\n\n- NVIDIA GPU, sm_70 or newer (64-bit slots run natively on all supported arches; 128-bit slots run natively on sm_90+ and via libcu++ lock-table fallback otherwise).\n- CUDA Toolkit 13.x (for the C++20 + libcu++ `bit_cast` combination this library relies on).\n- A C++20 host compiler compatible with that nvcc.\n\n## Layout\n\n- `include/gpurhh/` — the library headers. Add this to your include path; everything ships from here.\n- `tests/` — per-topic test executables built against the public header.\n- `examples/` — small standalone programs. `examples/basic.cu` is a self-contained construct + bulk insert + bulk lookup walkthrough.\n- `benchmarks/` — apples-to-apples timing benchmarks (with optional cuCollections and WarpCore baselines) and a bandwidth study.\n- `docs/` — design documentation (the canonical record of *why* the implementation looks the way it does).\n\n## Quick start\n\n```cpp\n#include <gpurhh/hash_table.cuh>\n\nusing Table = gpurhh::HashTable<std::uint32_t, std::uint32_t>;\n\nTable table(1u << 20);          // host-side; capacity rounded up to next pow-2\n\n__global__ void insert_kernel(Table::View view, const std::uint32_t* keys,\n                              const std::uint32_t* values, std::size_t n) {\n    namespace cg = cooperative_groups;\n    auto block = cg::this_thread_block();\n    auto tile  = cg::tiled_partition<Table::tile_size>(block);\n\n    const std::size_t tile_id     = (blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x) / Table::tile_size;\n    const std::size_t total_tiles = (gridDim.x  * blockDim.x)               / Table::tile_size;\n\n    for (std::size_t i = tile_id; i < n; i += total_tiles) {\n        view.insert(tile, keys[i], values[i]);\n    }\n}\n```\n\nSee [examples/basic.cu](examples/basic.cu) for the complete walkthrough and [docs/usage.md](docs/usage.md) for the API surface and the `View` pattern.\n\n## Building\n\nThe repository ships a single `Makefile`:\n\n- `make` — build tests, examples, and benchmarks.\n- `make tests` / `make examples` / `make benchmarks` — build one group.\n- `make test` — build and run all tests.\n- `make clean` — wipe `build/`.\n\nOverrides via env vars: `make ARCH=sm_90 CXX_STD=c++20 OPT=-O3`.\nBenchmark binaries always build at `-O3`; unoptimized throughput numbers are meaningless.\n\nOptional baselines under `external/include/` are picked up automatically; populate them with `scripts/setup-baselines.sh` if you want the cuCollections / WarpCore comparisons.\n\n## Benchmark sweeps\n\n```sh\nscripts/benchmark.sh --timing\nscripts/benchmark.sh --memory-bandwidth\nscripts/benchmark.sh --all          # everything available\n```\n\nOutput lands under `output/<timestamp>/`, with `run_info.txt` capturing `nvcc --version`, `uname`, and `nvidia-smi` for each run.\n\n## Documentation\n\nDesign and rationale live under [docs/](docs/), starting with [docs/index.md](docs/index.md).\nThe C++ API reference is produced separately by Doxygen from the headers themselves.\n\n## License\n\n[MIT](LICENSE).\n"
}
```

</details>

## User — 2026-05-27T20:45:39.424Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1295`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
1	# gpurhh — GPU Robin Hood Hash Table
2	
3	A header-only CUDA library implementing a parallel hash table for NVIDIA GPUs, using Robin Hood open-addressing with bucketed (cache-line-aligned), sub-warp-cooperative probing.
4	The design target is bandwidth-bound performance on large tables.
5	
6	## Status
7	
8	Insert and lookup are implemented and tested.
9	Deletion, dynamic resize, and multi-GPU are deliberately out of scope for now — see [docs/limitations.md](docs/limitations.md).
10	
11	## Requirements
12	
13	- NVIDIA GPU, sm_70 or newer (64-bit slots run natively on all supported arches; 128-bit slots run natively on sm_90+ and via libcu++ lock-table fallback otherwise).
14	- CUDA Toolkit 13.x (for the C++20 + libcu++ `bit_cast` combination this library relies on).
15	- A C++20 host compiler compatible with that nvcc.
16	
17	## Layout
18	
19	- `include/gpurhh/` — the library headers. Add this to your include path; everything ships from here.
20	- `tests/` — per-topic test executables built against the public header.
21	- `examples/` — small standalone programs. `examples/basic.cu` is a self-contained construct + bulk insert + bulk lookup walkthrough.
22	- `benchmarks/` — apples-to-apples timing benchmarks (with optional cuCollections and WarpCore baselines) and a bandwidth study.
23	- `docs/` — design documentation (the canonical record of *why* the implementation looks the way it does).
24	
25	## Quick start
26	
27	```cpp
28	#include <gpurhh/hash_table.cuh>
29	
30	using Table = gpurhh::HashTable<std::uint32_t, std::uint32_t>;
31	
32	Table table(1u << 20);          // host-side; capacity rounded up to next pow-2
33	
34	__global__ void insert_kernel(Table::View view, const std::uint32_t* keys,
35	                              const std::uint32_t* values, std::size_t n) {
36	    namespace cg = cooperative_groups;
37	    auto block = cg::this_thread_block();
38	    auto tile  = cg::tiled_partition<Table::tile_size>(block);
39	
40	    const std::size_t tile_id     = (blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x) / Table::tile_size;
41	    const std::size_t total_tiles = (gridDim.x  * blockDim.x)               / Table::tile_size;
42	
43	    for (std::size_t i = tile_id; i < n; i += total_tiles) {
44	        view.insert(tile, keys[i], values[i]);
45	    }
46	}
47	```
48	
49	See [examples/basic.cu](examples/basic.cu) for the complete walkthrough and [docs/usage.md](docs/usage.md) for the API surface and the `View` pattern.
50	
51	## Building
52	
53	The repository ships a single `Makefile`:
54	
55	- `make` — build tests, examples, and benchmarks.
56	- `make tests` / `make examples` / `make benchmarks` — build one group.
57	- `make test` — build and run all tests.
58	- `make clean` — wipe `build/`.
59	
60	Overrides via env vars: `make ARCH=sm_90 CXX_STD=c++20 OPT=-O3`.
61	Benchmark binaries always build at `-O3`; unoptimized throughput numbers are meaningless.
62	
63	Optional baselines under `external/include/` are picked up automatically; populate them with `scripts/setup-baselines.sh` if you want the cuCollections / WarpCore comparisons.
64	
65	## Benchmark sweeps
66	
67	```sh
68	scripts/benchmark.sh --timing
69	scripts/benchmark.sh --memory-bandwidth
70	scripts/benchmark.sh --all          # everything available
71	```
72	
73	Output lands under `output/<timestamp>/`, with `run_info.txt` capturing `nvcc --version`, `uname`, and `nvidia-smi` for each run.
74	
75	## Documentation
76	
77	Design and rationale live under [docs/](docs/), starting with [docs/index.md](docs/index.md).
78	The C++ API reference is produced separately by Doxygen from the headers themselves.
79	
80	## License
81	
82	[MIT](LICENSE).
83	
```

</details>

Add this to your include path; everything ships from here.\n- `tests/` — per-topic test executables built against the public header.\n- `examples/` — small standalone programs. `examples/basic.cu` is a self-contained construct + bulk insert + bulk lookup walkthrough.\n- `benchmarks/` — apples-to-apples timing benchmarks (with optional cuCollections and WarpCore baselines) and a bandwidth study.\n- `docs/` — design documentation (the canonical record of *why* the implementation looks the way it does).\n\n# Quick start\n\n```cpp\n#include <gpurhh/hash_table.cuh>\n\nusing Table = gpurhh::HashTable<std::uint32_t, std::uint32_t>;\n\nTable table(1u << 20);          // host-side; capacity rounded up to next pow-2\n\n__global__ void insert_kernel(Table::View view, const std::uint32_t* keys,\n                              const std::uint32_t* values, std::size_t n) {\n    namespace cg = cooperative_groups;\n    auto block = cg::this_thread_block();\n    auto tile  = cg::tiled_partition<Table::tile_size>(block);\n\n    const std::size_t tile_id     = (blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x) / Table::tile_size;\n    const std::size_t total_tiles = (gridDim.x  * blockDim.x)               / Table::tile_size;\n\n    for (std::size_t i = tile_id; i < n; i += total_tiles) {\n        view.insert(tile, keys[i], values[i]);\n    }\n}\n```\n\nSee [examples/basic.cu](examples/basic.cu) for the complete walkthrough and [docs/usage.md](docs/usage.md) for the API surface and the `View` pattern.\n\n# Building\n\nThe repository ships a single `Makefile`:\n\n- `make` — build tests, examples, and benchmarks.\n- `make tests` / `make examples` / `make benchmarks` — build one group.\n- `make test` — build and run all tests.\n- `make clean` — wipe `build/`.\n\nOverrides via env vars: `make ARCH=sm_90 CXX_STD=c++20 OPT=-O3`.\nBenchmark binaries always build at `-O3`; unoptimized throughput numbers are meaningless.\n\nOptional baselines under `external/include/` are picked up automatically; populate them with `scripts/setup-baselines.sh` if you want the cuCollections / WarpCore comparisons.\n\n# Benchmark sweeps\n\n```sh\nscripts/benchmark.sh --timing\nscripts/benchmark.sh --memory-bandwidth\nscripts/benchmark.sh --all          # everything available\n```\n\nOutput lands under `output/<timestamp>/`, with `run_info.txt` capturing `nvcc --version`, `uname`, and `nvidia-smi` for each run.\n\n# Documentation\n\nDesign and rationale live under [docs/](docs/), starting with [docs/index.md](docs/index.md).\nThe C++ API reference is produced separately by Doxygen from the headers themselves.\n\n# License\n\n[MIT](LICENSE).\n"
}

  "content": "# Doxygen configuration for gpurhh.\n#\n# Inputs:\n#   - README.md                  (used as the main page)\n#   - docs/                      (hand-written design pages)\n#   - include/gpurhh/            (the public headers — API reference)\n#\n# HTML output lands in build/docs/html/. Build with `make docs` or\n# `doxygen Doxyfile` from the repo root.\n\nPROJECT_NAME           = \"gpurhh\"\nPROJECT_BRIEF          = \"GPU Robin Hood Hash Table — header-only CUDA library\"\nOUTPUT_DIRECTORY       = build/docs\n\nINPUT                  = README.md docs include/gpurhh\nFILE_PATTERNS          = *.cuh *.md\nRECURSIVE              = YES\nUSE_MDFILE_AS_MAINPAGE = README.md\n\nEXAMPLE_PATH           = examples\nEXAMPLE_PATTERNS       = *.cu\n\n# The headers use plain // comments rather than Doxygen markup. Extract\n# everything we have and let JAVADOC_AUTOBRIEF lift the first sentence\n# of each block comment into the brief.\nEXTRACT_ALL            = YES\nEXTRACT_PRIVATE        = NO\nEXTRACT_STATIC         = YES\nJAVADOC_AUTOBRIEF      = YES\n\nMARKDOWN_SUPPORT       = YES\nAUTOLINK_SUPPORT       = YES\n\n# Treat .cuh / .cu as C++ so Doxygen parses them with the C++ frontend.\nEXTENSION_MAPPING      = cuh=C++ cu=C++\n\n# Strip CUDA attribute macros so they don't confuse the parser.\nENABLE_PREPROCESSING   = YES\nMACRO_EXPANSION        = YES\nEXPAND_ONLY_PREDEF     = YES\nPREDEFINED             = __device__= \\\n                         __host__= \\\n                         __global__= \\\n                         __forceinline__=inline \\\n                         __CUDACC__ \\\n                         __NVCC__\n\nGENERATE_HTML          = YES\nHTML_OUTPUT            = html\nGENERATE_LATEX         = NO\nGENERATE_XML           = NO\n\nQUIET                  = YES\nWARN_IF_UNDOCUMENTED   = NO\nHAVE_DOT               = NO\n"
}

## Assistant — 2026-05-27T21:12:33.237Z

Doxygen builds cleanly. Setup:

- [Doxyfile](Doxyfile) at the repo root — uses [README.md](README.md) as the main page, recursively pulls in [docs/](docs/) for the design pages and [include/gpurhh/](include/gpurhh/) for API extraction, with CUDA attribute macros (`__device__`, `__host__`, `__global__`) defined away so the parser doesn't trip on them.
- `make docs` target — runs `doxygen Doxyfile`; output lands in [build/docs/html/](build/docs/html/) (already covered by the `build/` entry in `.gitignore`).
- No warnings on this run, 91 HTML files generated; open `build/docs/html/index.html` to view.

Headers currently use plain `//` comments, so the API reference is structural (members, signatures, file links) rather than narrative. Adding `///` or `/** */` blocks on the public symbols in `hash_table.cuh` and `print.cuh` would flesh that side out — happy to do that as a follow-up if you want.

## User — 2026-05-27T21:17:02.510Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1318`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
1	# Limitations, scope, and future work
2	
3	## Known limitations
4	
5	- **Deletion** is not supported. Users that need it allocate a fresh table and re-insert.
6	- **Resize** is not supported. Users that need growth allocate a bigger table and re-insert.
7	- **Multi-GPU** is not supported.
8	- **Concurrent get during insert is not linearizable.** A get running simultaneously with insert can return `nullopt` for a key in flight. Workloads should synchronize between insert and get phases.
9	- **128-bit slot performance on pre-Hopper hardware** (sm_70–sm_89) is bottlenecked by libcu++'s lock-table emulation of 128-bit CAS. Functionally correct, but considerably slower than the native single-instruction path that sm_90+ has.
10	
11	## Out of scope
12	
13	- **Non-NVIDIA backends.** The abstractions (`CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` as table-level template parameters) don't preclude AMD support, but we haven't written or tested it. The same algorithm with AMD's 64-byte cache line and 64-thread wavefront would give `BucketSize = 8` and `TilesPerWarp = 8`.
14	- **Full stream support.** `clear()` takes a `cudaStream_t` (defaulting to `gpurhh::default_stream`). The constructor does not — `cudaMalloc` is synchronous and dominates the construction cost, so threading a stream through the slot-init memset would buy nothing while leaving the constructor's overall behavior confusingly half-async. The destructor doesn't take a stream either; destruction is a host-side lifecycle event, and the caller is expected to synchronize relevant streams before letting the table go out of scope.
15	
16	## Future work
17	
18	- Validate the 128-bit slot path on a Hopper (sm_90+) system; benchmark it against the 64-bit path.
19	- Add a CPU reference Robin Hood for randomized differential testing if test signal demands it (currently considered low priority — the invariant scanner plus the sum-reduction equivalence check cover most of what differential testing would add).
20	- Implement deletion (backward-shift, lock-free) if a use case appears.
21	- Consider the inner-loop "exhaust all empty / displaceable slots in the current bucket snapshot before re-reading" optimization (warpcore-style). Sketched, prototyped, and measured to be performance-neutral at our scale (low CAS contention dominates); kept on the back burner in case the contention profile shifts.
22	
```

</details>

---

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "file_path": "[repo]/docs/limitations.md",
  "content": "# Limitations and potential future work\n\n## Known limitations\n\n- **Deletion** is not supported. Users that need it allocate a fresh table and re-insert.\n- **Resize** is not supported. Users that need growth allocate a bigger table and re-insert.\n- **Multi-GPU** is not supported.\n- **Concurrent get during insert is not linearizable.** A get running simultaneously with insert can return `nullopt` for a key in flight. Workloads should synchronize between insert and get phases.\n- **128-bit slot performance on pre-Hopper hardware** (sm_70–sm_89) is bottlenecked by libcu++'s lock-table emulation of 128-bit CAS. Functionally correct, but considerably slower than the native single-instruction path that sm_90+ has.\n\n## Potential future work\n\n- Validate the 128-bit slot path on a Hopper (sm_90+) system; benchmark it against the 64-bit path.\n- Add a CPU reference Robin Hood for randomized differential testing if test signal demands it (currently considered low priority — the invariant scanner plus the sum-reduction equivalence check cover most of what differential testing would add).\n- Implement deletion (backward-shift, lock-free) if a use case appears.\n- Consider the inner-loop \"exhaust all empty / displaceable slots in the current bucket snapshot before re-reading\" optimization (warpcore-style). Sketched, prototyped, and measured to be performance-neutral at our scale (low CAS contention dominates); kept on the back burner in case the contention profile shifts.\n- Port to non-NVIDIA backends. The abstractions (`CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` as table-level template parameters) don't preclude AMD support, but we haven't written or tested it. The same algorithm with AMD's 64-byte cache line and 64-thread wavefront would give `BucketSize = 8` and `TilesPerWarp = 8`.\n- Thread `cudaStream_t` through the constructor and destructor. Today only `clear()` takes a stream; the constructor's `cudaMalloc` is synchronous and dominates construction cost, so threading a stream through the slot-init memset would buy nothing while leaving the constructor's overall behavior confusingly half-async. The destructor doesn't take a stream either; destruction is a host-side lifecycle event, and the caller is expected to synchronize relevant streams before letting the table go out of scope.\n"
}
```

</details>

## User — 2026-05-27T21:18:57.872Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1322`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
[repo]/docs/benchmarks.md:29:  The timing sweep uses `F ∈ {0.5, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 1.0}` — capped at F=1 because gpurhh's timing build sets `MaxProbeBuckets = 1 << 20` (matching the baselines' effectively-unbounded probing), and at F > 1 with α ≈ 32 each doomed insert would walk its full probe budget, turning the sweep into a multi-hour stall.
[repo]/docs/benchmarks.md:31:  The memory-bandwidth sweep extends through `F ∈ {0.5, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0}` — with `MaxProbeBuckets = 8` (the design default) failed inserts give up cheaply, so the over-subscription regime is tractable and the failure / hit / probe counters become informative.
[repo]/docs/implementation.md:21:## Capacity, hash function, load factor
[repo]/docs/implementation.md:25:  Users targeting a specific load factor compute the slot count themselves (e.g. `min_capacity = ceil(N / target_load_factor)` for an expected `N` insertions).
[repo]/docs/implementation.md:27:- Robin Hood tolerates higher load than linear probing, but on a GPU the warp tail-latency argument pushes the practical sweet spot back toward 0.7–0.8 rather than 0.9.
[repo]/docs/architecture.md:7:- A tight, low-variance distribution of probe distances → small worst-case lookup length even at load factors of 0.85–0.9.
[repo]/docs/architecture.md:56:  With Robin Hood at load factor ~0.85 and `bucket_size` 16, the expected longest probe is well under the default cap; the cap protects against adversarial inputs and very high load factors.
[repo]/docs/architecture.md:63:If we get this right, an insert of `N` keys at load factor 0.7 should cost approximately `(1/(1-α)) * sizeof(slot)` bytes per insert in expectation, which is the information-theoretic floor for an open-addressed table with this layout.
[repo]/docs/architecture.md:121:| `TargetLoadFactor` | tuned (e.g. 0.7)             | Robin Hood quality — empirical           |
```

</details>

## Assistant — 2026-05-27T21:20:05.968Z

All load-factor references now anchor on the 0.85–0.95 target range:

- [docs/architecture.md:7](docs/architecture.md#L7) — Robin Hood's worst-case lookup tolerance updated to 0.85–0.95.
- [docs/architecture.md:56](docs/architecture.md#L56) — probe-cap argument framed around the design's 0.85–0.95 target.
- [docs/architecture.md:63](docs/architecture.md#L63) — bandwidth formula now references load factor α generically, with a follow-up sentence pinning the 0.85–0.95 range as where the bandwidth advantage shows up.
- [docs/architecture.md:121](docs/architecture.md#L121) — `TargetLoadFactor` table entry updated to `0.85–0.95`.
- [docs/implementation.md:27](docs/implementation.md#L27) — replaced the "GPU pushes sweet spot down to 0.7–0.8" framing with the inverted positive statement that 0.85–0.95 is the target and the regime where Robin Hood outperforms simpler schemes.

The 0.5/0.75/0.85/0.95/1.0 numbers in [docs/benchmarks.md](docs/benchmarks.md) are sweep axis values rather than target load-factor claims, so I left them alone.

## User — 2026-05-27T21:33:32.585Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1335`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
1	# GPU Robin Hood Hashing
2	
3	`gpurhh` is a header-only CUDA library implementing a parallel Robin Hood hash table for NVIDIA GPUs.
4	It targets bandwidth-bound performance on large tables by combining cache-line-aligned bucketed probing with sub-warp-cooperative tiles.
5	
6	These pages are the canonical record of *why* the implementation looks the way it does — the design rationale that drove the code, not a user manual generated from headers.
7	The C++ API reference is produced separately by Doxygen from the source itself.
8	
9	## Goals
10	
11	- **Header-only CUDA library.**
12	  A single `.cuh` (or a small set) that users `#include` and template-instantiate for their key/value types.
13	  No separate compilation of the table itself.
14	- **Massively parallel insertion.**
15	  A batch of `N` keys is inserted by `N` (or more) GPU threads cooperatively.
16	  Worst-case behavior must remain bounded even under heavy contention.
17	- **Memory-bandwidth bound.**
18	  On large tables, the table is much bigger than the L2 cache, so performance is determined by how many useful bytes each DRAM transaction delivers.
19	  The aim is to come as close to peak HBM bandwidth as the access pattern allows.
20	
21	## Navigating these pages
22	
23	- [Usage](usage.md) — public API surface, the `View` pattern, and opt-in macros.
24	- [Architecture](architecture.md) — why Robin Hood, the slot layout, the bandwidth-saturation argument, and how work decomposes onto the GPU hierarchy.
25	- [Algorithm](algorithm.md) — parallel insertion, lookup, deletion, and reduction operators.
26	- [Implementation](implementation.md) — language and toolchain choices, capacity, hash function, and where the power-of-two assumption lives.
27	- [Testing](testing.md) — the test suite, shared infrastructure, and what the `IdentityHash` fixture buys us.
28	- [Benchmarks](benchmarks.md) — workload knobs, experiment phases, counter instrumentation, and the sweep driver.
29	- [Limitations](limitations.md) — known limitations and potential future work.
30	
```

</details>

---

1	# GPU Robin Hood Hashing
2	
3	`gpurhh` is a header-only CUDA library implementing a parallel Robin Hood hash table for NVIDIA GPUs.
4	It targets bandwidth-bound performance on large tables by combining cache-line-aligned bucketed probing with sub-warp-cooperative tiles.
5	
6	The C++ API reference is produced separately by Doxygen from the source itself.
7	
8	## Goals
9	
10	- **Header-only CUDA library.**
11	  A single `.cuh` (or a small set) that users `#include` and template-instantiate for their key/value types.
12	  No separate compilation of the table itself.
13	- **Massively parallel insertion and retrieval.**
14	  A batch of `N` keys is inserted by `N` (or more) GPU threads cooperatively.
15	- **Memory-bandwidth-oriented design.**
16	  On large tables, the table is much bigger than the L2 cache, so performance is determined by how many useful bytes each DRAM transaction delivers.
17	  The aim is to come as close to peak HBM bandwidth as the access pattern allows.
18	
19	## Navigating these pages
20	
21	- [Usage](usage.md) — public API surface, the `View` pattern, and opt-in macros.
22	- [Architecture](architecture.md) — why Robin Hood, the slot layout, the bandwidth-saturation argument, and how work decomposes onto the GPU hierarchy.
23	- [Algorithm](algorithm.md) — parallel insertion with potential reduction operators, and lookup.
24	- [Implementation](implementation.md) — language and toolchain choices, the power-of-two assumption on capacity, and hash function.
25	- [Testing](testing.md) — the test suite, shared infrastructure, and what the `IdentityHash` fixture buys us.
26	- [Benchmarks](benchmarks.md) — workload knobs, experiment phases, counter instrumentation, and the sweep driver.
27	- [Limitations](limitations.md) — known limitations and potential future work.
28	
```

## User — 2026-05-27T21:41:40.162Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1343`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
1	# Usage
2	
3	`gpurhh` is built around a host-side `HashTable` resource and a device-side `View` handle.
4	The host object owns the bucket storage; the `View` is a trivially-copyable handle that user kernels take by value and operate on through cooperative tiles.
5	
6	## The View pattern
7	
8	The library is deliberately header-only and lean on host-side helpers.
9	Bulk insert / bulk lookup driver kernels are *not* part of the table — callers manage their device buffers and write their own driver kernels.
10	A user kernel that already has its keys in registers calls `view.insert(tile, ...)` or `view.get(tile, ...)` directly, and that is the canonical extension point.
11	
12	The tests in `tests/kernels.cuh` and `tests/isolated.cuh` show the canonical pattern; [examples/basic.cu](../examples/basic.cu) is a minimal standalone walkthrough.
13	
14	## API surface
15	
16	The headers under `include/gpurhh/` define:
17	
18	- `gpurhh::HashTable<Key, Value, ...>` — the host-side owner.
19	  Non-copyable, movable.
20	  Constructed from a `min_capacity` (in slots) that is rounded up to the next power of two; provides `clear(stream)`, `capacity()`, and `data()` accessors, plus `view()` which produces the device handle.
21	- `gpurhh::HashTable::View` — the device handle.
22	  Exposes `__device__` `insert(tile, key, value)` returning `cuda::std::optional<Slot>` (empty on success; populated with the leftover pair on probe-cap failure) and `__device__` `get(tile, key) const` returning `cuda::std::optional<Value>`.
23	- `gpurhh::default_stream` — a named alias for the CUDA runtime's default stream, used as the default argument for host-side methods that issue async work.
24	- `gpurhh::replace_op`, `gpurhh::sum_op` — built-in reduction functors for the `Reduction` template parameter (see [Algorithm: Reduction operators](algorithm.md#reduction-operators)).
25	- `gpurhh::print_slots(table, start, stop)` — a diagnostic helper, lives in `<gpurhh/print.cuh>` as a separate header so the core library stays independent of `<cstdio>` and `<vector>`.
26	
27	The `HashTable` template parameters are `<Key, Value, Hash = default_hash<Key>, EmptyKey = default_empty_key<Key>::key, Reduction = replace_op, CacheLineBytes = 128, WarpSize = 32, MaxProbeBuckets = 8>`.
28	The cache-line and warp-size parameters are present specifically so the table is not portability-pinned to NVIDIA constants (see the [AMD aside](architecture.md#why-the-abstraction-matters--an-amd-aside-out-of-scope-for-v1)).
29	
30	The full C++ signatures, parameter lists, and per-method semantics belong in the Doxygen-generated reference rather than being maintained by hand here.
31	
32	## Insert return value: lossless failure
33	
34	`View::insert` returns `cuda::std::optional<Slot>`.
35	On success it is empty; on probe-cap failure it holds the leftover `(key, value)` pair that could not be placed.
36	
37	Crucially, this leftover may *not* be the original input.
38	If Robin Hood displacements happened during the probe, the originally-passed-in pair is already in the table and the leftover is the most-recently-evicted victim.
39	Returning it is what makes the operation *lossless* under failure — callers can buffer leftovers and replay them into a rebuilt larger table without losing any of the keys they handed in.
40	
41	## Opt-in macros
42	
43	One macro gates functionality that the core library doesn't expose by default.
44	It must be defined in the translation unit *before* any `<gpurhh/...>` header is included:
45	
46	- **`GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS`** adds a trailing `std::uint32_t& tile_counter` parameter to `View::insert` and `View::get`.
47	  Lane 0 of the tile increments it on every cache-line-sized memory event: each bucket-load at the top of a probe-loop iteration (including CAS-retry re-reads of the same bucket), *and* each attempted CAS — every CAS requires the line in an exclusive state, which is an additional memory transaction beyond the cooperative load.
48	  `View::get` is read-only and only bumps on bucket loads.
49	  The caller maintains a per-tile register accumulator and writes it out once at end of kernel; no atomics fire in the hot loop.
50	  Downstream bandwidth = `counter × sizeof(Bucket) / time`.
51	  The memory-bandwidth benchmark binaries enable this; the timing benchmarks, tests, and the example do not.
52	
```

</details>

  "new_string": "## Host API\n\nThe headers under `include/gpurhh/` define, on the host side:\n\n- `gpurhh::HashTable<Key, Value, ...>` — the host-side owner.\n  Non-copyable, movable.\n  Constructed from a `min_capacity` (in slots) that is rounded up to the next power of two; provides `clear(stream)`, `capacity()`, and `data()` accessors, plus `view()` which produces the device handle.\n- `gpurhh::replace_op`, `gpurhh::sum_op` — built-in reduction functors for the `Reduction` template parameter (see [Algorithm: Reduction operators](algorithm.md#reduction-operators)).\n- `gpurhh::print_slots(table, start, stop)` — a diagnostic helper, lives in `<gpurhh/print.cuh>` as a separate header so the core library stays independent of `<cstdio>` and `<vector>`.\n\nThe `HashTable` template parameters are `<Key, Value, Hash = default_hash<Key>, EmptyKey = default_empty_key<Key>::key, Reduction = replace_op, CacheLineBytes = 128, WarpSize = 32, MaxProbeBuckets = 8>`.\nThe cache-line and warp-size parameters are present specifically so the table is not portability-pinned to NVIDIA constants (see the [AMD aside](architecture.md#why-the-abstraction-matters--an-amd-aside-out-of-scope-for-v1)).\n\n## Device API\n\nThe device-side surface is the `View` handle, obtained host-side via `table.view()` and passed by value into user kernels.\n`View` is trivially copyable and carries only the bucket pointer and the derived capacity/mask constants — no ownership.\n\nIt exposes two cooperative-tile methods, both of which take a `cooperative_groups::thread_block_tile<TileSize>` as their first argument (the `B` lanes that share each bucket load):\n\n- `__device__ cuda::std::optional<Slot> insert(tile, key, value)` — insert a `(key, value)` pair under the table's `Reduction`.\n  Returns an empty optional on success; on probe-cap failure returns the leftover slot that could not be placed (which, after Robin Hood displacements, may be a victim rather than the original input — see [Insert return value](#insert-return-value-lossless-failure)).\n- `__device__ cuda::std::optional<Value> get(tile, key) const` — look up the value associated with `key`.\n  Returns the value on hit, empty optional on miss.\n\nBoth methods are designed to be called from inside a user-written grid-stride loop where each tile owns one in-flight key per iteration.\nThe tests in `tests/kernels.cuh` and the example in `examples/basic.cu` show the canonical loop shape.\n\nThe full C++ signatures, parameter lists, and per-method semantics belong in the Doxygen-generated reference rather than being maintained by hand here."
}
```

## User — 2026-05-27T21:44:35.770Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1346`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
26	
27	## Device API
28	
29	The device-side API is designed around the `View` handle, obtained host-side via `table.view()` and passed by value into user kernels.
30	`View` is trivially copyable and carries only the bucket pointer and the derived capacity/mask constants — no ownership.
31	
32	It exposes two cooperative-tile methods, both of which take a `cooperative_groups::thread_block_tile<TileSize>` as their first argument (the `B` lanes that share each bucket load):
33	
34	- `__device__ cuda::std::optional<Slot> insert(tile, key, value)` — insert a `(key, value)` pair under the table's `Reduction`.
35	  Returns an empty optional on success; on probe-cap failure returns the leftover slot that could not be placed (which, after Robin Hood displacements, may be a victim rather than the original input — see [Insert return value](#insert-return-value-lossless-failure)).
36	- `__device__ cuda::std::optional<Value> get(tile, key) const` — look up the value associated with `key`.
37	  Returns the value on hit, empty optional on miss.
38	
39	Both methods are designed to be called from inside a user-written grid-stride loop where each tile owns one in-flight key per iteration.
40	The tests in `tests/kernels.cuh` and the example in `examples/basic.cu` show the canonical loop shape.
```

</details>

```
Device API</h1>
<p>The device-side API is designed around the <span class="tt">View</span> handle, obtained host-side via <span class="tt">table.view()</span> and passed by value into user kernels. <span class="tt">View</span> is trivially copyable and carries only the bucket pointer and the derived capacity/mask constants — no ownership.</p>
<p>It exposes two cooperative-tile methods, both of which take a <span class="tt">cooperative_groups::thread_block_tile&lt;TileSize&gt;</span> as their first argument (the <span class="tt">B</span> lanes that share each bucket load):</p>
<ul>
<li><span class="tt">__device__ cuda::std::optional&lt;Slot&gt; insert(tile, key, value)</span> — insert a <span class="tt">(key, value)</span> pair under the table's <span class="tt">Reduction</span>. Returns an empty optional on success; on probe-cap failure returns the leftover slot that could not be placed (which, after Robin Hood displacements, may be a victim rather than the original input — see Insert return valueInsert return value</li>
</ul>
<p>).</p><ul>
<li><span class="tt">__device__ cuda::std::optional&lt;Value&gt; get(tile, key) const</span> — look up the value associated with <span class="tt">key</span>. Returns the value on hit, empty optional on miss.</li>
</ul>
<p>Both methods are designed to be called from inside a user-written grid-stride loop where each tile owns one in-flight key per iteration. The tests in <span class="tt">tests/kernels.cuh</span> and the example in <span class="tt">examples/basic.cu</span> show the canonical loop shape.</p>
```

</details>

[repo]/docs/architecture.md:29:2. **128-bit slots** (8-byte key + 8-byte value): atomic CAS still goes through `cuda::atomic_ref<Slot>`; on sm_90+ this is the single-instruction `atom.cas.b128`, on sm_70–sm_89 libcu++ emulates with a lock-table fallback. Functional everywhere, but the emulated path is significantly slower than 64-bit CAS under contention.
[repo]/docs/limitations.md:8:- **Concurrent get during insert is not linearizable.** A get running simultaneously with insert can return `nullopt` for a key in flight. Workloads should synchronize between insert and get phases.
[repo]/docs/limitations.md:9:- **128-bit slot performance on pre-Hopper hardware** (sm_70–sm_89) is bottlenecked by libcu++'s lock-table emulation of 128-bit CAS. Functionally correct, but considerably slower than the native single-instruction path that sm_90+ has.
[repo]/docs/limitations.md:13:- Validate the 128-bit slot path on a Hopper (sm_90+) system; benchmark it against the 64-bit path.
[repo]/docs/algorithm.md:64:**Concurrent get during insert is not linearizable.** A get running simultaneously with an insert can return `nullopt` for a key that is about to be inserted, or for a key in the middle of being displaced.
```

</details>

## User — 2026-05-27T21:54:05.712Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1371`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
1	# Limitations and potential future work
2	
3	## Known limitations
4	
5	- **Deletion** is not supported. Users that need it allocate a fresh table and re-insert.
6	- **Resize** is not supported. Users that need growth allocate a bigger table and re-insert.
7	- **Multi-GPU** is not supported.
8	- **Concurrent get during insert is not supported.** Workloads must synchronize between insert and get phases.
9	- **128-bit slot performance on pre-Hopper hardware** (sm_70–sm_89) is not tested and might either be very slow or possibly not work at all.
10	
11	## Potential future work
12	
13	- Validate the 128-bit slot path on a Hopper (sm_90+) system; benchmark it against the 64-bit path.
14	- Add a CPU reference Robin Hood for randomized differential testing if test signal demands it (currently considered low priority — the invariant scanner plus the sum-reduction equivalence check cover most of what differential testing would add).
15	- Implement deletion (backward-shift, lock-free) if a use case appears.
16	- Consider the inner-loop "exhaust all empty / displaceable slots in the current bucket snapshot before re-reading" optimization (warpcore-style). Sketched, prototyped, and measured to be performance-neutral at our scale (low CAS contention dominates); kept on the back burner in case the contention profile shifts.
17	- Port to non-NVIDIA backends. The abstractions (`CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` as table-level template parameters) don't preclude AMD support, but we haven't written or tested it. The same algorithm with AMD's 64-byte cache line and 64-thread wavefront would give `BucketSize = 8` and `TilesPerWarp = 8`.
18	- Thread `cudaStream_t` through the constructor and destructor. Today only `clear()` takes a stream; the constructor's `cudaMalloc` is synchronous and dominates construction cost, so threading a stream through the slot-init memset would buy nothing while leaving the constructor's overall behavior confusingly half-async. The destructor doesn't take a stream either; destruction is a host-side lifecycle event, and the caller is expected to synchronize relevant streams before letting the table go out of scope.
19	
```

</details>

## User — 2026-05-27T21:56:12.800Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1378`)

```
17	Concepts on the `Hash` template parameter, `std::source_location` in place of `__FILE__` / `__LINE__` macros, ranges, and coroutines are all deferred until they pay for themselves.
18	
19	The C++20 baseline is conservative future-proofing: it lets us reach for the cleaner spelling when we want it without revisiting the build settings each time.
20	
21	## Capacity, hash function, load factor
22	
23	- Capacity is a power of two so `mod` becomes a mask.
24	  The constructor takes `min_capacity` (in slots) and rounds up to the next power of two, never below `bucket_size`.
25	  Users targeting a specific load factor compute the slot count themselves (e.g. `min_capacity = ceil(N / target_load_factor)` for an expected `N` insertions).
26	- Hash function is a template parameter; default is a finalizer-style mixer (e.g. the splitmix64/Murmur3 finalizer) that is cheap on GPU and decorrelates structured keys well.
```

}

## User — 2026-05-27T21:57:56.316Z

```
27:### Where the power-of-two assumption is load-bearing
29:The "capacity is a power of two" constraint is not just a convenience — it's wired into multiple hot-path operations.
30:Anyone touching these sites should know that breaking the invariant would require switching every `& mask` back to a `% capacity`, which is materially slower on GPU (multi-cycle integer divmod vs. a single AND).
```

}

## User — 2026-05-27T22:55:12.732Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1388`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
1	# Algorithm
2	
3	## Reduction operators
4	
5	When `View::insert` encounters an existing entry for the same key, the new value is combined with the existing one through a `Reduction` functor — a template parameter of `HashTable`, defaulting to `gpurhh::replace_op`.
6	Stateless and trivially copyable, the functor sits inside the `View` by value:
7	
8	```cpp
9	struct replace_op {
10	    template <class T>
11	    __device__ T operator()(T /*existing*/, T incoming) const noexcept { return incoming; }
12	};
13	
14	struct sum_op {
15	    template <class T>
16	    __device__ T operator()(T existing, T incoming) const noexcept { return existing + incoming; }
17	};
18	```
19	
20	`replace_op` gives last-writer-wins semantics.
21	`sum_op` accumulates values for repeated-key inserts — useful as a parallel histogram or counter primitive.
22	Users can define their own functor with the same signature.
23	
24	For commutative and associative reductions like `sum_op`, the final stored value is fully deterministic regardless of how the CAS retries interleave across tiles: the result is just the reduction of all inserted values for that key.
25	For non-commutative reductions, the outcome depends on which writers win their CAS races.
26	
27	## Parallel insertion algorithm
28	
29	Each thread owns one `(key, value)` pair.
30	The thread walks the probe sequence and, at every slot, performs one of three actions:
31	
32	1. **Empty slot, CAS succeeds.** Done.
33	2. **Same key, CAS succeeds.** This is an update; done.
34	3. **Occupied by another key.**
35	   Compare probe distances.
36	   If the resident is "richer" (its probe distance from *its* home is smaller than ours from *our* home), we attempt to CAS our pair into the slot.
37	   On success, we adopt the displaced pair and continue probing from this slot.
38	   If the resident is "poorer" or equal, we move on.
39	
40	A CAS failure is treated as "the world changed under us": we re-read the slot and re-evaluate without advancing the probe index.
41	This is the standard lock-free retry.
42	
43	### Why this is correct under contention
44	
45	The Robin Hood invariant is maintained by every individual CAS because each CAS only swaps in a pair with probe distance strictly greater than the one already in the slot (or the slot was empty).
46	Two concurrent inserts targeting the same slot serialize through CAS, and whichever loses retries with the freshest state.
47	There is no global ordering requirement: as long as every write respects the local invariant, the final table is a valid Robin Hood layout for *some* permutation of the input.
48	
49	Caveat: the *value* portion of an update (case 2) needs care.
50	If two threads insert the same key concurrently and the user wants "last write wins" semantics, we need an additional CAS loop on the value, or we tolerate non-deterministic resolution.
51	
52	## Lookup
53	
54	`View::get` follows the same probing rule as insert, read-only, returning `cuda::std::optional<Value>` — populated on hit, empty on miss.
55	The probe terminates as soon as it sees one of:
56	
57	- A key match → return `{value}`.
58	- An empty slot → return `nullopt`. Robin Hood would have placed the key before this point.
59	- A resident "richer" than ours (smaller probe distance) → return `nullopt`. Our key would have evicted that resident if it were in the table, contradiction.
60	- The probe cap → return `nullopt`. The insert-side cap means the key cannot live further than this.
61	
62	Lookups are read-only and trivially parallel: concurrent lookups never race each other.
63	
64	**Concurrent get during insert is not supported.** A get running simultaneously with an insert can return `nullopt` for a key that is about to be inserted, or for a key in the middle of being displaced.
65	Workloads must synchronize between insert and get phases.
66	
67	## Deletion
68	
69	Not supported.
70	The natural design is backward-shift deletion, but a correct lock-free implementation is significantly harder than insert/lookup, and the use case (this is an append-only table for most consumers) hasn't pushed us to do it.
71	
```

</details>

---

{
  "file_path": "[repo]/docs/algorithm.md",
  "content": "# Algorithm\n\n## Parallel insertion algorithm\n\nEach thread owns one `(key, value)` pair.\nThe thread walks the probe sequence and, at every slot, performs one of three actions:\n\n1. **Empty slot, CAS succeeds.** Done.\n2. **Same key, CAS succeeds.** This is an update; done.\n3. **Occupied by another key.**\n   Compare probe distances.\n   If the resident is \"richer\" (its probe distance from *its* home is smaller than ours from *our* home), we attempt to CAS our pair into the slot.\n   On success, we adopt the displaced pair and continue probing from this slot.\n   If the resident is \"poorer\" or equal, we move on.\n\nA CAS failure is treated as \"the world changed under us\": we re-read the slot and re-evaluate without advancing the probe index.\nThis is the standard lock-free retry.\n\n### Why this is correct under contention\n\nThe Robin Hood invariant is maintained by every individual CAS because each CAS only swaps in a pair with probe distance strictly greater than the one already in the slot (or the slot was empty).\nTwo concurrent inserts targeting the same slot serialize through CAS, and whichever loses retries with the freshest state.\nThere is no global ordering requirement: as long as every write respects the local invariant, the final table is a valid Robin Hood layout for *some* permutation of the input.\n\nCaveat: the *value* portion of an update (case 2) needs care.\nIf two threads insert the same key concurrently and the user wants \"last write wins\" semantics, we need an additional CAS loop on the value, or we tolerate non-deterministic resolution.\n\n### Reduction operators\n\nWhen `View::insert` encounters an existing entry for the same key, the new value is combined with the existing one through a `Reduction` functor — a template parameter of `HashTable`, defaulting to `gpurhh::replace_op`.\nStateless and trivially copyable, the functor sits inside the `View` by value:\n\n```cpp\nstruct replace_op {\n    template <class T>\n    __device__ T operator()(T /*existing*/, T incoming) const noexcept { return incoming; }\n};\n\nstruct sum_op {\n    template <class T>\n    __device__ T operator()(T existing, T incoming) const noexcept { return existing + incoming; }\n};\n```\n\n`replace_op` gives last-writer-wins semantics.\n`sum_op` accumulates values for repeated-key inserts — useful as a parallel histogram or counter primitive.\nUsers can define their own functor with the same signature.\n\nFor commutative and associative reductions like `sum_op`, the final stored value is fully deterministic regardless of how the CAS retries interleave across tiles: the result is just the reduction of all inserted values for that key.\nFor non-commutative reductions, the outcome depends on which writers win their CAS races.\n\n## Lookup\n\n`View::get` follows the same probing rule as insert, read-only, returning `cuda::std::optional<Value>` — populated on hit, empty on miss.\nThe probe terminates as soon as it sees one of:\n\n- A key match → return `{value}`.\n- An empty slot → return `nullopt`. Robin Hood would have placed the key before this point.\n- A resident \"richer\" than ours (smaller probe distance) → return `nullopt`. Our key would have evicted that resident if it were in the table, contradiction.\n- The probe cap → return `nullopt`. The insert-side cap means the key cannot live further than this.\n\nLookups are read-only and trivially parallel: concurrent lookups never race each other.\n\n**Concurrent get during insert is not supported.** A get running simultaneously with an insert can return `nullopt` for a key that is about to be inserted, or for a key in the middle of being displaced.\nWorkloads must synchronize between insert and get phases.\n\n## Deletion\n\nNot supported.\nThe natural design is backward-shift deletion, but a correct lock-free implementation is significantly harder than insert/lookup, and the use case (this is an append-only table for most consumers) hasn't pushed us to do it.\n"
}
```

</details>

## User — 2026-05-27T23:03:30.291Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1407`)

```
28:1. **64-bit slots** (4-byte key + 4-byte value): atomic CAS via `cuda::atomic_ref<Slot>`, which on every NVIDIA arch compiles down to a 64-bit `atom.cas`. Fast.
29:2. **128-bit slots** (8-byte key + 8-byte value): atomic CAS still goes through `cuda::atomic_ref<Slot>`; on sm_90+ this is the single-instruction `atom.cas.b128`. On sm_70–sm_89 libcu++ falls back to lock-table emulation, but this path is untested here and may be very slow or fail outright.
```

---

5:Robin-Hood refers to open addressing with the invariant *"the entry in slot `i` whose probe distance is smaller gets evicted by an entry whose probe distance is larger"*, which gives:
7:- A tight, low-variance distribution of probe distances → small worst-case lookup length even at load factors of 0.85–0.95.
8:- A natural early-exit rule for lookups (stop as soon as the slot's probe distance is smaller than the probe distance of the key we're searching for).
34:### Probe distance

## User — 2026-05-27T23:05:33.063Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1412`)

```
34	### Probe distance
35	
36	We do *not* store probe distance in the slot.
37	It is derivable as `(slot_index - hash(key)) mod capacity`.
38	Storing it would cost bits we'd rather give to the payload, and the derivation is a single subtract once we've read the slot.
39	
40	## Achieving bandwidth saturation
41	
```

## Assistant — 2026-05-27T23:05:38.940Z

**→ Tool call: `Edit`** (id `tool-1413`)

{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/docs/architecture.md",
  "old_string": "We do *not* store probe distance in the slot.\nIt is derivable as `(slot_index - hash(key)) mod capacity`.\nStoring it would cost bits we'd rather give to the payload, and the derivation is a single subtract once we've read the slot.",
  "new_string": "We do *not* store probe distance in the slot.\nIt is derivable as `(slot_index - hash(key)) mod capacity`.\nStoring it would cost bits we'd rather give to the payload, and the derivation is a single subtract once we've read the slot.\n\nIf we ever wanted to explore a double-hashing variant, one could modify the scheme to store the probe distance as well — but this would require smaller-than-32-bit keys to keep the slot packable into a single CAS word."
}
```

---

## User — 2026-05-27T23:07:28.972Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1415`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
49	  We have the warp ballot/shuffle primitives to do this cheaply.
50	- **Bucketed probing.**
51	  Treat the table as an array of *buckets* of 16 slots (one cache line).
52	  A probe inspects an entire bucket at a time — one coalesced 128-byte load — and uses warp intrinsics (`__ballot_sync`, `__match_any_sync`) to find the candidate slot.
53	  Insertion still uses CAS on a single 8-byte slot inside the bucket, but the read traffic per probe is exactly one cache line.
54	  This is the same idea as `bucketed_cuckoo` and Bucketized Cuckoo Hashing.
55	- **Probe length capping.**
56	  Exposed as a `MaxProbeBuckets` template parameter (default 8).
57	  An insert that would place a key past the cap fails (the caller is expected to rehash); a get that probes the cap without finding the key returns "not found" (correct because of the insert-side cap).
58	  With Robin Hood at the design's target load factors of 0.85–0.95 and `bucket_size` 16, the expected longest probe is well under the default cap; the cap protects against adversarial inputs and over-subscription.
```

</details>

---

{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "[repo]/docs/architecture.md",
  "old_string": "  Insertion still uses CAS on a single 8-byte slot inside the bucket, but the read traffic per probe is exactly one cache line.",
  "new_string": "  Reads thus happen at cache-line granularity (one coalesced 128-byte load per probe), while writes are CAS on a single 8-byte slot inside the bucket — since no atomic CAS spans an entire cache line."
}
```

[repo]/docs/architecture.md:59:  Empirically, on `Uniform(0, 2^32)` uint32 keys (effectively unique inputs), the default cap-8 budget handles `F = n_ops / capacity` up to ~1 with essentially zero failures, and starts losing inserts as `F` grows past 1 into the over-subscription regime (see `benchmarks/memory_bandwidth/insert.csv`'s `total_failures` column).
[repo]/docs/benchmarks.md:31:  The memory-bandwidth sweep extends through `F ∈ {0.5, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0}` — with `MaxProbeBuckets = 8` (the design default) failed inserts give up cheaply, so the over-subscription regime is tractable and the failure / hit / probe counters become informative.
```

## User — 2026-05-27T23:09:49.431Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1424`)

```
61	- **Avoid divergence.**
62	  All threads in a warp execute the same probe loop.
63	  The "I'm done, others aren't" case is handled with masked operations rather than early-return-then-divergence.
64	
65	If we get this right, an insert of `N` keys at load factor α should cost approximately `(1/(1-α)) * sizeof(slot)` bytes per insert in expectation, which is the information-theoretic floor for an open-addressed table with this layout. At the design's target range of 0.85–0.95 the bytes/insert is 7–20× the slot size, and the bandwidth advantage over simpler schemes is most pronounced precisely here.
66	
67	## Work decomposition: how insertions map onto the GPU hierarchy
```

  "new_string": "- **Avoid divergence.**\n  All threads in a warp execute the same probe loop.\n  The \"I'm done, others aren't\" case is handled with masked operations rather than early-return-then-divergence.\n  Worth noting here that on NVIDIA hardware we cannot diverge by more than a factor of 2 thanks to sub-warp tile granularity."

## User — 2026-05-27T23:10:28.681Z

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
[repo]/docs/limitations.md:17:- Port to non-NVIDIA backends. The abstractions (`CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` as table-level template parameters) don't preclude AMD support, but we haven't written or tested it, as this would require switching to HIP, which would be quite involved. The same algorithm with AMD's 64-byte cache line and 64-thread wavefront would give `BucketSize = 8` and `TilesPerWarp = 8`.
[repo]/docs/benchmarks.md:96:Output goes to `output/<timestamp>/timing/{insert,get}.csv` and `output/<timestamp>/memory_bandwidth/{memcpy,insert,get}.csv`, with a `run_info.txt` sidecar (`nvcc --version`, `uname`, `nvidia-smi` GPU summary) and a `benchmark.log` transcript at the top of the output dir.
[repo]/docs/usage.md:25:The cache-line and warp-size parameters are present specifically so the table is not portability-pinned to NVIDIA constants (see the [AMD aside](architecture.md#why-the-abstraction-matters--an-amd-aside-out-of-scope-for-v1)).
[repo]/docs/architecture.md:28:1. **64-bit slots** (4-byte key + 4-byte value): atomic CAS via `cuda::atomic_ref<Slot>`, which on every NVIDIA arch compiles down to a 64-bit `atom.cas`.
[repo]/docs/architecture.md:64:  Worth noting here that on NVIDIA hardware we cannot diverge by more than a factor of 2 thanks to sub-warp tile granularity.
[repo]/docs/architecture.md:85:  With 8-byte slots and a 128-byte line on NVIDIA, the bucket holds 16 slots, so half of every warp sits idle.
[repo]/docs/architecture.md:95:   For NVIDIA with 8-byte slots that is 2.
[repo]/docs/architecture.md:118:| `CacheLineBytes`   | 128 (NVIDIA)                 | Hardware                                 |
[repo]/docs/architecture.md:120:| `WarpSize`         | 32 (NVIDIA)                  | Hardware                                 |
[repo]/docs/architecture.md:135:- NVIDIA: `BucketSize` = 128 / 8 = 16, `TilesPerWarp` = 32 / 16 = **2**.
[repo]/docs/index.md:3:`gpurhh` is a header-only CUDA library implementing a parallel Robin Hood hash table for NVIDIA GPUs.
[repo]/README.md:18:A header-only CUDA library implementing a parallel hash table for NVIDIA GPUs, using Robin Hood open-addressing with bucketed (cache-line-aligned), sub-warp-cooperative probing.
[repo]/README.md:28:- NVIDIA GPU, sm_70 or newer (64-bit slots run natively on all supported arches; 128-bit slots run natively on sm_90+ and via libcu++ lock-table fallback otherwise).
[repo]/README.md:88:Output lands under `output/<timestamp>/`, with `run_info.txt` capturing `nvcc --version`, `uname`, and `nvidia-smi` for each run.
```

</details>

[repo]/docs/algorithm.md:70:The natural design is backward-shift deletion, but a correct lock-free implementation is significantly harder than insert/lookup, and the use case (this is an append-only table for most consumers) hasn't pushed us to do it.
[repo]/docs/limitations.md:16:- Consider the inner-loop "exhaust all empty / displaceable slots in the current bucket snapshot before re-reading" optimization (WarpCore-style). Sketched, prototyped, and measured to be performance-neutral (if this is needed, check the git commit history) at our scale (low CAS contention dominates); kept on the back burner in case the contention profile shifts.
```

## User — 2026-05-27T23:15:00.713Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1441`)

```
82	  Fatal for our stated goal.
83	- **Block-per-key** is too coarse: there is no useful work for 128+ threads on a single key.
84	- **Warp-per-key** is the classic "warp-cooperative hashing" design but wastes lanes whenever the bucket is narrower than the warp.
85	  With 8-byte slots and a 128-byte line on NVIDIA, the bucket holds 16 slots, so half of every warp sits idle.
86	- **Sub-warp tile per key**, with tile width equal to the bucket width, is the one design where *one tile-probe = one cache-line transaction* with no idle lanes.
87	  This is the design we adopt.
88	
89	### The structure that follows
```

## User — 2026-05-27T23:15:31.846Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1445`)

```
135	- NVIDIA: `BucketSize` = 128 / 8 = 16, `TilesPerWarp` = 32 / 16 = **2**.
136	- AMD:    `BucketSize` =  64 / 8 =  8, `TilesPerWarp` = 64 /  8 = **8**.
137	
138	The same algorithm runs unchanged; only the derived constants differ.
139	We are not targeting AMD, but it is the reason we want `CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` to be parameters of the table type rather than hard-coded `128` and `32`.
140	Anything that ends up baked in as a literal in the implementation is a portability bug waiting to happen.
141	
```

---

## User — 2026-05-27T23:15:42.434Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1447`)

```
130	### Why the abstraction matters — an AMD aside (out of scope for v1)
131	
132	AMD GPUs (CDNA, RDNA) have a 64-byte cache line and a 64-thread wavefront.
133	With 8-byte slots:
134	
135	- NVIDIA: `BucketSize` = 128 / 8 = 16, `TilesPerWarp` = 32 / 16 = **2**.
136	- AMD:    `BucketSize` =  64 / 8 =  8, `TilesPerWarp` = 64 /  8 = **8**.
137	
138	The same algorithm runs unchanged; only the derived constants differ.
139	We are not targeting AMD, but this is the reason `CacheLineBytes` and `WarpSize` are parameters of the table type rather than hard-coded `128` and `32`.
140	Anything baked in as a literal in the implementation would be a portability bug waiting to happen.
141	
```

7:- **`test_get.cu`** — `View::get` in isolation. Pre-states are hand-built with `IdentityHash` so the probe sequence and home buckets are deterministic; the test then verifies `get`'s match / empty / richer-resident / wrap-around behavior.

18:The `IdentityHash` is the enabling choice for the isolated tests: with `hash(K) = K`, the home bucket of a key is fully predictable from its value, so we can construct any valid Robin Hood state with one `cudaMemcpy` and hand-predict the outcome of small insert sequences.
```

## User — 2026-05-27T23:21:53.546Z

I'm not missing a point on that one, right? It's not fitting in memory that is the problem (1GB table on ~24GB+ GPU), it's that we don't want slow deduplication making the tests annoying? We had to do some fiddling for this exact issue in the benchmarks

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-27T23:22:10.609Z

<em>Thinking…</em>

---

## Assistant — 2026-05-27T23:22:12.307Z

Right — you're not missing the point. The 1 GB table on a 24 GB+ GPU is the easy part; the slow piece is dedup on the host (a billion-key `unordered_set` insert is what kills throughput, not the GPU memory budget). The bit-mixing permutation sidesteps that by producing distinct keys by construction, so no host-side dedup is needed at all. The edit reads correctly.

---

## User — 2026-05-27T23:22:15.917Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1459`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
1	# Testing
2	
3	The test suite lives in `tests/`, with each `test_*.cu` compiled into its own executable.
4	
5	- **`test_construction.cu`** — construct / destruct, move semantics, edge sizes (capacity == bucket_size, capacity below bucket_size), moved-from state behavior, and `clear()` returning the bucket array to the empty-byte pattern.
6	- **`test_combined.cu`** — end-to-end insert + get round-trips through the public API.
7	- **`test_get.cu`** — `View::get` in isolation. Pre-states are hand-built with `IdentityHash` so the probe sequence and home buckets are deterministic; the test then verifies `get`'s match / empty / richer-resident / wrap-around behavior.
8	- **`test_insert.cu`** — `View::insert` in isolation. Some tests use hand-computed expected layouts; all run a Robin Hood invariant scanner over the final state as a safety net. Covers the probe-cap failure path and concurrent same-key inserts.
9	- **`test_concurrency.cu`** — heavy parallel contention: adversarial displacement chains, many concurrent gets, mixed new / duplicate inserts, parallel saturation of the probe cap.
10	- **`test_randomized.cu`** — randomized stress. Uniform-key inserts at small (4 KB table) and gigabyte (1 GB table) scales; a sum-reduction stress test with random keys and deliberate duplicates whose expected per-key sum is computed on the host.
11	
12	Three shared infrastructure headers:
13	
14	- **`tests/tests.cuh`** — the `CUDA_CHECK` postfix error-check operator (`expr >> CUDA_CHECK`).
15	- **`tests/kernels.cuh`** — the `Table` alias (with `default_hash`), bulk insert/get host helpers, and end-to-end driver kernels. Used by the "combined" and construction tests.
16	- **`tests/isolated.cuh`** — the `TestTable` alias (with `IdentityHash` for predictable home buckets), templated `run_insert` / `run_get` / `run_get_one` / `run_insert_with_outcomes`, the `assert_robin_hood_invariant` scanner, and `set_state` / `read_state` for direct table-memory manipulation via `HashTable::data()`.
17	
18	We use an identity hash function (`IdentityHash`) to facilitate isolated tests: with `hash(K) = K`, the home bucket of a key is fully predictable from its value, so we can construct any valid Robin Hood state with one `cudaMemcpy` and hand-predict the outcome of small insert sequences.
19	The randomized tests apply a bit-mixing function (the same `fmix32` finalizer used by `default_hash`) as a permutation on sequential integers — this gives distinct random-looking keys without the slow host-side deduplication overhead that an `unordered_set` would require, which is what makes the gigabyte-scale tests run reasonably fast.
20	
21	The test suite runs about 40 tests across ~1500 lines of test code (plus ~500 lines of shared test infrastructure under `tests.cuh` / `kernels.cuh` / `isolated.cuh`), against ~750 lines of library code under `include/gpurhh/`.
22	The high ratio is deliberate: this is a concurrent data structure, and the failure modes for concurrent CAS retries, displacement chains, and probe-cap interactions are subtle enough that we put significant effort into checking each path.
23	
```

</details>

## User — 2026-05-27T23:23:17.480Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1461`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
1	# Benchmarks
2	
3	Throughput and instrumentation measurements live in `benchmarks/`, split into two studies with their own directory subtree and their own CSV output tree.
4	
5	- **`benchmarks/timing/`** — apples-to-apples kernel throughput against optional baselines (`cuCollections::static_map`, `WarpCore::SingleValueHashTable`).
6	  gpurhh's binaries here use `replace_op` reduction and `MaxProbeBuckets = 1 << 20` (effectively uncapped), matching the baselines' unbounded-probe semantics.
7	  Two executables: `benchmark_insert`, `benchmark_get`.
8	  Baseline equivalents live under `benchmarks/timing/baselines/{cuco,cuco_dh,warpcore}/` and append to the same per-workload CSVs; the `library` column distinguishes their rows.
9	  The `cuco/` baseline uses cuCollections's default linear probing (CG=4); the `cuco_dh/` baseline reuses the same headers but specializes `static_map` with `double_hashing<8, ...>`, isolating the probing-scheme variable within a single library so the family-vs-implementation axis is testable separately.
10	- **`benchmarks/memory_bandwidth/`** — the bandwidth study.
11	  `benchmark_memcpy` provides the empirical DRAM ceiling; `benchmark_insert` and `benchmark_get` are gpurhh's design under instrumentation (`sum_op` reduction, default `MaxProbeBuckets = 8`, per-tile probe / failure / hit counters via `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS`).
12	  The CSVs from this study let you compute precise per-op DRAM traffic — `total_probes × sizeof(Bucket) / time_ms` — and compare it against memcpy's ceiling.
13	  No library baselines, since neither cuco nor warpcore exposes the per-probe counters needed for the bandwidth analysis.
14	
15	The split exists because the two studies want fundamentally different configurations: timing needs the most permissive probe behavior to make per-library performance comparable; the bandwidth study wants the design's actual default (capped probes, real reduction work) so we can read the probe / failure / hit characteristics back out and convert them into bandwidth numbers.
```

</details>
