## User — 2026-05-27T23:24:48.971Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1466`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
15	The split exists because the two studies want fundamentally different configurations: timing needs the most permissive probe behavior to make per-library performance comparable; the memory bandwidth experiment wants the design's actual default (capped probes, real reduction work) so we can read the probe / failure / hit characteristics back out and convert them into bandwidth numbers.
16	
17	`benchmark_memcpy` is the peak-bandwidth reference.
18	Times `cudaMemcpyAsync(... D2D)` and a trivial `uint4`-vectorized copy kernel on a buffer of configurable size.
19	The CSV stores `dram_bytes = 2 * payload_bytes` (one read of src + one write of dst), so the downstream bandwidth computation is simply `dram_bytes / time_ms` — and this is directly comparable to the hash-table benchmarks' `total_probes × sizeof(Bucket) / time_ms`, since both metrics are bytes through the DRAM controller per second.
20	Recording the 2× factor at write time rather than relying on downstream scripts to apply it keeps the convention impossible to misuse in analysis.
21	
22	## Workload knobs
23	
24	- **`capacity`** is held at 1 GiB by default — well past the L2 cache on any sm_89 device, putting the kernel firmly in the DRAM-bound regime.
```

</details>

10	- **`benchmarks/memory_bandwidth/`** — the memory bandwidth experiment.
11	  `benchmark_memcpy` provides the empirical DRAM ceiling, measured both via the `cudaMemcpyAsync(... D2D)` API and a minimalist `uint4`-vectorized copy kernel; `benchmark_insert` and `benchmark_get` are gpurhh's design under instrumentation (`sum_op` reduction, default `MaxProbeBuckets = 8`, per-tile probe / failure / hit counters via `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS`).
12	  The CSVs from this study let you compute precise per-op DRAM traffic — `total_probes × sizeof(Bucket) / time_ms` — and compare it against memcpy's ceiling.
13	  No library baselines, since neither cuco nor warpcore exposes the per-probe counters needed for the bandwidth analysis.
14	
15	The split exists because the two studies want fundamentally different configurations: timing needs the most permissive probe behavior to make per-library performance comparable; the memory bandwidth experiment wants the design's actual default (capped probes, real reduction work) so we can read the probe / failure / hit characteristics back out and convert them into bandwidth numbers.
16	
17	`benchmark_memcpy` is the peak-bandwidth reference.
18	Times `cudaMemcpyAsync(... D2D)` and a trivial `uint4`-vectorized copy kernel on a buffer of configurable size.
19	The CSV stores `dram_bytes = 2 * payload_bytes` (one read of src + one write of dst), so the downstream bandwidth computation is simply `dram_bytes / time_ms` — and this is directly comparable to the hash-table benchmarks' `total_probes × sizeof(Bucket) / time_ms`, since both metrics are bytes through the DRAM controller per second.
```

</details>

## User — 2026-05-27T23:29:01.694Z

30:  The timing sweep uses `F ∈ {0.5, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 1.0}` — capped at F=1 because gpurhh's timing build sets `MaxProbeBuckets = 1 << 20` (matching the baselines' effectively-unbounded probing), and at F > 1 with α ≈ 32 each doomed insert would walk its full probe budget, turning the sweep into a multi-hour stall.
```

60	- The get benchmark is analogous: a single bulk get through gpurhh's grid-stride get kernel, cuco's `map.find_async(...)`, or warpcore's `table.retrieve(...)`. The surrounding phase structure matches insert with one difference: the pre-fill is one-shot (untimed, before the loop) rather than per-rep, since pre-fill input variability is a second-order effect for the get throughput we're measuring — only the get-key stream is refreshed in `setup` per rep.

62	**`after` callback (untimed, runs after each timed rep).**

64	- Count occupied slots in the table:

70	
71	## Per-tile probe / failure / hit counters
72	
73	The `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` macro (see [Usage: Preprocessor-gated benchmark counters](usage.md#preprocessor-gated-benchmark-counters)) adds a trailing `std::uint32_t&` parameter to `View::insert` / `View::get`.
74	The memory-benchmark kernels declare a per-tile register accumulator, pass it through every `view.insert` / `view.get` call in the grid-stride loop, and flush one value per tile to a global array at end of kernel.
```

</details>

## User — 2026-05-27T23:32:50.310Z

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
    run_benchmark_loop(args.warmups, args.reps, timer,
        /*setup=*/  [&]() {
            fill_uniform_keys(d_get_keys, args.n_ops, gen);
        },
        /*launch=*/ [&]() {
            get<<<shape.grid_size, args.block_size>>>(
                table.view(), d_get_keys, d_out, args.n_ops);
            cudaGetLastError() >> CUDA_CHECK;
        },
        /*after=*/  [&](int rep, float ms) {
            rec.write_row(format_row(
                capacity, args.n_ops,
                args.block_size, slot_bytes, bytes_per_op,
                args.seed, rep, args.tag, ms));
        });

    cudaFree(d_get_keys);
    cudaFree(d_out);
    return 0;
}
```

</details>

## User — 2026-05-27T23:33:16.091Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1494`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
42	
43	- Allocate `d_keys`, `d_values`, and `d_sorted` device buffers, each `n_ops × uint32`.
44	- `cudaMemset(d_values, 0, ...)` once. Under `replace_op` the value content doesn't affect kernel work, so a zero buffer suffices; gpurhh, cuco, and warpcore all do this so the kernel pays an apples-to-apples `d_values` read.
45	- Construct the table (`gpurhh::HashTable`, `cuco::static_map`, or `warpcore::SingleValueHashTable`).
46	- Construct a single `UniformKeyGenerator` (cuRAND Philox4_32_10) seeded with `--seed`.
47	- For cuco: build a `thrust::counting_iterator → cuco::pair{d_keys[i], d_values[i]}` transform iterator for the bulk-insert call.
48	
49	**`setup` callback (untimed, runs before each warmup and each timed rep).**
50	
51	- Empty the table (`table.clear()` / `map.clear_async()` / `table.init()`).
52	- Generate fresh input keys: `fill_uniform_keys(d_keys, n_ops, gen)` advances the cuRAND stream by `n_ops` draws.
53	  Across reps the generator state continues, so the 16 reps see iid draws rather than 16 timings of one frozen input — statistically equivalent to 16 independent seeds but with CUDA / table / generator setup paid once.
54	- Count distinct inputs for drop accounting: copy `d_keys → d_sorted`, `thrust::sort`, `thrust::unique`, record `n_unique`.
55	  This is the ground-truth denominator for the drops measurement: at α ≈ 32 most inputs are unique but ~1–2% are collision duplicates (governed by the mathematics of the birthday problem), and we don't want input duplicates to show up as drops.
56	
57	**`launch` callback (timed window).**
58	
59	- One bulk insert: gpurhh's grid-stride insert kernel, cuco's `map.insert_async(pair_begin, pair_begin + n_ops)`, or warpcore's `table.insert(d_keys, d_values, n_ops)`.
60	- The get benchmark is analogous: a single bulk get through gpurhh's grid-stride get kernel, cuco's `map.find_async(...)`, or warpcore's `table.retrieve(...)`. The surrounding phase structure matches insert with one difference: the pre-fill is one-shot (untimed, before the loop) rather than per-rep, since pre-fill input variability is a second-order effect for the get throughput we're measuring — only the get-key stream is refreshed in `setup` per rep.
61	
62	**`after` callback (untimed, runs after each timed rep).**
63	
64	- Count occupied slots in the table:
65	  - gpurhh: `count_occupied_slots(reinterpret_cast<Slot*>(table.data()), capacity, empty_key)` — a `thrust::count_if` scan in `benchmarks.cuh`. We use the table's `data()` accessor to view the bucket storage as a flat slot array.
66	  - cuco: `map.size()` (synchronous, built-in).
67	  - warpcore: `table.size()` (synchronous, built-in).
68	- Compute `drops = n_unique - occupied`. This is the count of keys the library tried to insert and couldn't fit, with input duplicates already factored out via `n_unique`.
69	- Emit one CSV row with `time_ms`, `n_unique`, `drops`, and the configuration columns.
70	
71	## Per-tile probe / failure / hit counters
```

</details>

## User — 2026-05-27T23:34:12.163Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1499`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
1	# Benchmarks
2	
3	Benchmarks come in two kinds — timing and memory bandwidth — and live in `benchmarks/`, split into two studies with their own directory subtree and their own CSV output tree.
4	
5	- **`benchmarks/timing/`** — apples-to-apples kernel throughput against optional baselines (`cuCollections::static_map`, `WarpCore::SingleValueHashTable`).
6	  gpurhh's binaries here use `replace_op` reduction and `MaxProbeBuckets = 1 << 20` (effectively uncapped), matching the baselines' unbounded-probe semantics.
7	  Two executables: `benchmark_insert`, `benchmark_get`.
8	  Baseline equivalents live under `benchmarks/timing/baselines/{cuco,cuco_dh,warpcore}/` and append to the same per-workload CSVs; the `library` column distinguishes their rows.
9	  The `cuco/` baseline uses cuCollections's default linear probing (CG=4); the `cuco_dh/` baseline reuses the same headers but specializes `static_map` with `double_hashing<8, ...>`, isolating the probing-scheme variable within a single library so the family-vs-implementation axis is testable separately.
10	- **`benchmarks/memory_bandwidth/`** — the memory bandwidth experiment.
11	  `benchmark_memcpy` provides the empirical DRAM ceiling, measured both via the `cudaMemcpyAsync(... D2D)` API and a minimalist `uint4`-vectorized copy kernel; `benchmark_insert` and `benchmark_get` are gpurhh's design under instrumentation (`sum_op` reduction, default `MaxProbeBuckets = 8`, per-tile probe / failure / hit counters via `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS`).
12	  The CSVs from this study let you compute precise per-op DRAM traffic — `total_probes × sizeof(Bucket) / time_ms` — and compare it against memcpy's ceiling.
13	  This is also where we can probe occupancies above 1, which is only possible because `sum_op` collapses duplicate-key inserts into the existing slot and because the design tolerates (and records) probe-cap failures rather than dropping them silently.
14	  No library baselines, since neither cuco nor warpcore exposes the per-probe counters needed for the bandwidth analysis.
15	
16	The split exists because the two studies want fundamentally different configurations: timing needs the most permissive probe behavior to make per-library performance comparable; the memory bandwidth experiment considers a different setup with capped probes and real reduction work so we can read the probe / failure / hit characteristics back out and convert them into bandwidth numbers.
17	
18	`benchmark_memcpy` is the peak-bandwidth reference.
19	Times `cudaMemcpyAsync(... D2D)` and a trivial `uint4`-vectorized copy kernel on a buffer of configurable size.
20	The CSV stores `dram_bytes = 2 * payload_bytes` (one read of src + one write of dst), so the downstream bandwidth computation is simply `dram_bytes / time_ms` — and this is directly comparable to the hash-table benchmarks' `total_probes × sizeof(Bucket) / time_ms`, since both metrics are bytes through the DRAM controller per second.
21	Recording the 2× factor at write time rather than relying on downstream scripts to apply it keeps the convention impossible to misuse in analysis.
22	
23	## Workload hyperparameters
24	
25	- **`capacity`** is held at 1 GiB by default — well past the L2 cache on any sm_89 device, putting the kernel firmly in the DRAM-bound regime.
26	- **Key distribution** is raw `Uniform(0, 2^32)` — every uint32 is equally likely, no clamping.
27	  With capacity = 2^27, this gives `α = key_range / capacity ≈ 32`: keys are effectively unique.
28	  This is the collision-resolution regime where table designs actually differ; at much smaller α the table fills very slowly with duplicates and linear probing's pathological clustering never engages.
29	- The _load factor_ **`F = n_ops / capacity`** is the swept axis.
30	  The timing sweep uses `F ∈ {0.5, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 1.0}` — capped at F=1 because gpurhh's timing build sets `MaxProbeBuckets = 1 << 20` (matching the baselines' effectively-unbounded probing), and at F > 1 with α ≈ 32 each doomed insert would walk its full probe budget, this could cause the table to stall, as the kernel would have to loop over the whole table, which would be extremely slow.
31	  A redraw guard in the timing-insert binary aborts if a cuRAND draw produces `n_unique > capacity` after a few retries.
32	  The memory-bandwidth sweep extends through `F ∈ {0.5, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0}` — with `MaxProbeBuckets = 8` (the design default) failed inserts give up cheaply, so the over-subscription regime is tractable and the failure / hit / probe counters become informative.
33	- **`block_size ∈ {64, 128, 256, 512, 1024}`** is swept for the gpurhh binaries to spot-check that launch shape doesn't dominate.
34	  cuco / warpcore pick their own internal launch shapes.
35	
36	## Experiment phases
37	
38	Every insert benchmark structures its work via `run_benchmark_loop`, which carves each rep into three callbacks.
39	**Only the middle phase (`launch`) sits inside the timing window** — `EventTimer::begin()` / `end_ms()` bracket exactly that callback's content, nothing more.
40	
41	**Once at startup (untimed, before the loop).**
42	
43	- Allocate `d_keys`, `d_values`, and `d_sorted` device buffers, each `n_ops × uint32`.
44	- `cudaMemset(d_values, 0, ...)` once. Under `replace_op` the value content doesn't affect kernel work, so a zero buffer suffices; gpurhh, cuco, and warpcore all do this so the kernel pays an apples-to-apples `d_values` read.
45	- Construct the table (`gpurhh::HashTable`, `cuco::static_map`, or `warpcore::SingleValueHashTable`).
46	- Construct a single `UniformKeyGenerator` (cuRAND Philox4_32_10) seeded with `--seed`.
47	- For cuco: build a `thrust::counting_iterator → cuco::pair{d_keys[i], d_values[i]}` transform iterator for the bulk-insert call.
48	- For the get benchmark only: pre-fill the table once via the same bulk-insert path. Pre-fill input variability is a second-order effect for the get throughput we're measuring, so it's paid once rather than per-rep.
49	
50	**`setup` callback (untimed, runs before each warmup and each timed rep).**
51	
52	- Empty the table (`table.clear()` / `map.clear_async()` / `table.init()`) — insert only; the get benchmark keeps its pre-filled state across reps.
53	- Generate fresh input keys: `fill_uniform_keys(d_keys, n_ops, gen)` advances the cuRAND stream by `n_ops` draws.
54	  Across reps the generator state continues, so the 16 reps see iid draws rather than 16 timings of one frozen input — statistically equivalent to 16 independent seeds but with CUDA / table / generator setup paid once.
55	- Count distinct inputs for drop accounting (insert only): copy `d_keys → d_sorted`, `thrust::sort`, `thrust::unique`, record `n_unique`.
56	  This is the ground-truth denominator for the drops measurement: at α ≈ 32 most inputs are unique but ~1–2% are collision duplicates (governed by the mathematics of the birthday problem), and we don't want input duplicates to show up as drops.
57	
58	**`launch` callback (timed window).**
59	
60	- One bulk insert: gpurhh's grid-stride insert kernel, cuco's `map.insert_async(pair_begin, pair_begin + n_ops)`, or warpcore's `table.insert(d_keys, d_values, n_ops)`.
61	- The get benchmark is analogous: a single bulk get through gpurhh's grid-stride get kernel, cuco's `map.find_async(...)`, or warpcore's `table.retrieve(...)`.
62	
63	**`after` callback (untimed, runs after each timed rep).**
64	
65	- Count occupied slots in the table (insert only):
66	  - gpurhh: `count_occupied_slots(reinterpret_cast<Slot*>(table.data()), capacity, empty_key)` — a `thrust::count_if` scan in `benchmarks.cuh`. We use the table's `data()` accessor to view the bucket storage as a flat slot array.
67	  - cuco: `map.size()` (synchronous, built-in).
68	  - warpcore: `table.size()` (synchronous, built-in).
69	- Compute `drops = n_unique - occupied` (insert only). This is the count of keys the library tried to insert and couldn't fit, with input duplicates already factored out via `n_unique`.
70	- Emit one CSV row with `time_ms`, the configuration columns, and `n_unique` / `drops` on the insert side.
71	
72	## Per-tile probe / failure / hit counters
73	
74	The `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` macro (see [Usage: Preprocessor-gated benchmark counters](usage.md#preprocessor-gated-benchmark-counters)) adds a trailing `std::uint32_t&` parameter to `View::insert` / `View::get`.
75	The memory-benchmark kernels declare a per-tile register accumulator, pass it through every `view.insert` / `view.get` call in the grid-stride loop, and flush one value per tile to a global array at end of kernel.
76	No atomics fire in the probe hot loop.
77	The host sums per-tile values into a grand total per rep.
78	
79	The memory-bandwidth CSVs (`memory_bandwidth/insert.csv`, `memory_bandwidth/get.csv`) add `total_probes` and `total_failures` (insert) or `total_probes`, `total_hits`, `total_misses` (get) on top of the timing CSV schema.
80	Downstream analysis derives metrics like average probe length (`total_probes / n_ops`) and bandwidth-corrected throughput (`total_probes × sizeof(Bucket) / time_ms`), the latter being directly comparable to the memcpy ceiling in the same directory.
81	
82	At low F the corrected and uncorrected throughput numbers converge (average probe length ≈ 1).
83	At high F the corrected number diverges upward — that gap is itself a useful signal about how hard the Robin Hood machinery is working.
84	
85	## Benchmark script structure
86	
87	`scripts/benchmark.sh` runs the full grid. Flags select which study to run:
88	
89	- `--timing` — gpurhh's timing sweep (insert + get).
90	- `--memory-bandwidth` — the bandwidth sweep (memcpy ceiling + counter-instrumented gpurhh insert + get).
91	- `--cuco` — cuCollections baseline, default linear probing (timing only).
92	- `--cuco-dh` — cuCollections baseline re-specialized with double hashing (timing only).
93	- `--warpcore` — WarpCore baseline (timing only).
94	- `--all` — every flag above; missing binaries are skipped with a notice rather than erroring.
95	
96	Output goes to `output/<timestamp>/timing/{insert,get}.csv` and `output/<timestamp>/memory_bandwidth/{memcpy,insert,get}.csv`, with a `run_info.txt` sidecar (`nvcc --version`, `uname`, `nvidia-smi` GPU summary) and a `benchmark.log` transcript at the top of the output dir.
97	The seed is fixed at 1 across the sweep; `reps = 16` per invocation, with the cuRAND stream refreshing keys before every rep so each rep is an independent input draw.
98	
99	`make benchmarks` builds the binaries at `-O3` (the default for the benchmark pattern rule).
100	The sweep is launched separately from `scripts/benchmark.sh` because runs are heavy.
101	Numbers from unoptimized builds are meaningless, so the `-O3` default is effectively non-overridable.
102	
```

</details>

---

{
  "file_path": "[repo]/docs/benchmarks.md",
  "content": "# Benchmark code structure\n\n## Benchmark script structure\n\n`scripts/benchmark.sh` runs the full grid. Flags select which study to run:\n\n- `--timing` — gpurhh's timing sweep (insert + get).\n- `--memory-bandwidth` — the bandwidth sweep (memcpy ceiling + counter-instrumented gpurhh insert + get).\n- `--cuco` — cuCollections baseline, default linear probing (timing only).\n- `--cuco-dh` — cuCollections baseline re-specialized with double hashing (timing only).\n- `--warpcore` — WarpCore baseline (timing only).\n- `--all` — every flag above; missing binaries are skipped with a notice rather than erroring.\n\nOutput goes to `output/<timestamp>/timing/{insert,get}.csv` and `output/<timestamp>/memory_bandwidth/{memcpy,insert,get}.csv`, with a `run_info.txt` sidecar (`nvcc --version`, `uname`, `nvidia-smi` GPU summary) and a `benchmark.log` transcript at the top of the output dir.\nThe seed is fixed at 1 across the sweep; `reps = 16` per invocation, with the cuRAND stream refreshing keys before every rep so each rep is an independent input draw.\n\n`make benchmarks` builds the binaries at `-O3` (the default for the benchmark pattern rule).\nThe sweep is launched separately from `scripts/benchmark.sh` because runs are heavy.\nNumbers from unoptimized builds are meaningless, so the `-O3` default is effectively non-overridable.\n\n## Two benchmark studies\n\nBenchmarks come in two kinds — timing and memory bandwidth — and live in `benchmarks/`, split into two studies with their own directory subtree and their own CSV output tree.\n\n- **`benchmarks/timing/`** — apples-to-apples kernel throughput against optional baselines (`cuCollections::static_map`, `WarpCore::SingleValueHashTable`).\n  gpurhh's binaries here use `replace_op` reduction and `MaxProbeBuckets = 1 << 20` (effectively uncapped), matching the baselines' unbounded-probe semantics.\n  Two executables: `benchmark_insert`, `benchmark_get`.\n  Baseline equivalents live under `benchmarks/timing/baselines/{cuco,cuco_dh,warpcore}/` and append to the same per-workload CSVs; the `library` column distinguishes their rows.\n  The `cuco/` baseline uses cuCollections's default linear probing (CG=4); the `cuco_dh/` baseline reuses the same headers but specializes `static_map` with `double_hashing<8, ...>`, isolating the probing-scheme variable within a single library so the family-vs-implementation axis is testable separately.\n- **`benchmarks/memory_bandwidth/`** — the memory bandwidth experiment.\n  `benchmark_memcpy` provides the empirical DRAM ceiling, measured both via the `cudaMemcpyAsync(... D2D)` API and a minimalist `uint4`-vectorized copy kernel; `benchmark_insert` and `benchmark_get` are gpurhh's design under instrumentation (`sum_op` reduction, default `MaxProbeBuckets = 8`, per-tile probe / failure / hit counters via `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS`).\n  The CSVs from this study let you compute precise per-op DRAM traffic — `total_probes × sizeof(Bucket) / time_ms` — and compare it against memcpy's ceiling.\n  This is also where we can probe occupancies above 1, which is only possible because `sum_op` collapses duplicate-key inserts into the existing slot and because the design tolerates (and records) probe-cap failures rather than dropping them silently.\n  No library baselines, since neither cuco nor warpcore exposes the per-probe counters needed for the bandwidth analysis.\n\nThe split exists because the two studies want fundamentally different configurations: timing needs the most permissive probe behavior to make per-library performance comparable; the memory bandwidth experiment considers a different setup with capped probes and real reduction work so we can read the probe / failure / hit characteristics back out and convert them into bandwidth numbers.\n\n`benchmark_memcpy` is the peak-bandwidth reference.\nTimes `cudaMemcpyAsync(... D2D)` and a trivial `uint4`-vectorized copy kernel on a buffer of configurable size.\nThe CSV stores `dram_bytes = 2 * payload_bytes` (one read of src + one write of dst), so the downstream bandwidth computation is simply `dram_bytes / time_ms` — and this is directly comparable to the hash-table benchmarks' `total_probes × sizeof(Bucket) / time_ms`, since both metrics are bytes through the DRAM controller per second.\nRecording the 2× factor at write time rather than relying on downstream scripts to apply it keeps the convention impossible to misuse in analysis.\n\n## Workload hyperparameters\n\n- **`capacity`** is held at 1 GiB by default — well past the L2 cache on any sm_89 device, putting the kernel firmly in the DRAM-bound regime.\n- **Key distribution** is raw `Uniform(0, 2^32)` — every uint32 is equally likely, no clamping.\n  With capacity = 2^27, this gives `α = key_range / capacity ≈ 32`: keys are effectively unique.\n  This is the collision-resolution regime where table designs actually differ; at much smaller α the table fills very slowly with duplicates and linear probing's pathological clustering never engages.\n- The _load factor_ **`F = n_ops / capacity`** is the swept axis.\n  The timing sweep uses `F ∈ {0.5, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 1.0}` — capped at F=1 because gpurhh's timing build sets `MaxProbeBuckets = 1 << 20` (matching the baselines' effectively-unbounded probing), and at F > 1 with α ≈ 32 each doomed insert would walk its full probe budget, this could cause the table to stall, as the kernel would have to loop over the whole table, which would be extremely slow.\n  A redraw guard in the timing-insert binary aborts if a cuRAND draw produces `n_unique > capacity` after a few retries.\n  The memory-bandwidth sweep extends through `F ∈ {0.5, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0}` — with `MaxProbeBuckets = 8` (the design default) failed inserts give up cheaply, so the over-subscription regime is tractable and the failure / hit / probe counters become informative.\n- **`block_size ∈ {64, 128, 256, 512, 1024}`** is swept for the gpurhh binaries to spot-check that launch shape doesn't dominate.\n  cuco / warpcore pick their own internal launch shapes.\n\n## Experiment phases\n\nEvery insert benchmark structures its work via `run_benchmark_loop`, which carves each rep into three callbacks.\n**Only the middle phase (`launch`) sits inside the timing window** — `EventTimer::begin()` / `end_ms()` bracket exactly that callback's content, nothing more.\n\n**Once at startup (untimed, before the loop).**\n\n- Allocate `d_keys`, `d_values`, and `d_sorted` device buffers, each `n_ops × uint32`.\n- `cudaMemset(d_values, 0, ...)` once. Under `replace_op` the value content doesn't affect kernel work, so a zero buffer suffices; gpurhh, cuco, and warpcore all do this so the kernel pays an apples-to-apples `d_values` read.\n- Construct the table (`gpurhh::HashTable`, `cuco::static_map`, or `warpcore::SingleValueHashTable`).\n- Construct a single `UniformKeyGenerator` (cuRAND Philox4_32_10) seeded with `--seed`.\n- For cuco: build a `thrust::counting_iterator → cuco::pair{d_keys[i], d_values[i]}` transform iterator for the bulk-insert call.\n- For the get benchmark only: pre-fill the table once via the same bulk-insert path. Pre-fill input variability is a second-order effect for the get throughput we're measuring, so it's paid once rather than per-rep.\n\n**`setup` callback (untimed, runs before each warmup and each timed rep).**\n\n- Empty the table (`table.clear()` / `map.clear_async()` / `table.init()`) — insert only; the get benchmark keeps its pre-filled state across reps.\n- Generate fresh input keys: `fill_uniform_keys(d_keys, n_ops, gen)` advances the cuRAND stream by `n_ops` draws.\n  Across reps the generator state continues, so the 16 reps see iid draws rather than 16 timings of one frozen input — statistically equivalent to 16 independent seeds but with CUDA / table / generator setup paid once.\n- Count distinct inputs for drop accounting (insert only): copy `d_keys → d_sorted`, `thrust::sort`, `thrust::unique`, record `n_unique`.\n  This is the ground-truth denominator for the drops measurement: at α ≈ 32 most inputs are unique but ~1–2% are collision duplicates (governed by the mathematics of the birthday problem), and we don't want input duplicates to show up as drops.\n\n**`launch` callback (timed window).**\n\n- One bulk insert: gpurhh's grid-stride insert kernel, cuco's `map.insert_async(pair_begin, pair_begin + n_ops)`, or warpcore's `table.insert(d_keys, d_values, n_ops)`.\n- The get benchmark is analogous: a single bulk get through gpurhh's grid-stride get kernel, cuco's `map.find_async(...)`, or warpcore's `table.retrieve(...)`.\n\n**`after` callback (untimed, runs after each timed rep).**\n\n- Count occupied slots in the table (insert only):\n  - gpurhh: `count_occupied_slots(reinterpret_cast<Slot*>(table.data()), capacity, empty_key)` — a `thrust::count_if` scan in `benchmarks.cuh`. We use the table's `data()` accessor to view the bucket storage as a flat slot array.\n  - cuco: `map.size()` (synchronous, built-in).\n  - warpcore: `table.size()` (synchronous, built-in).\n- Compute `drops = n_unique - occupied` (insert only). This is the count of keys the library tried to insert and couldn't fit, with input duplicates already factored out via `n_unique`.\n- Emit one CSV row with `time_ms`, the configuration columns, and `n_unique` / `drops` on the insert side.\n\n## Per-tile probe / failure / hit counters\n\nThe `GPURHH_BENCHMARK_COUNTERS` macro (see [Usage: Preprocessor-gated benchmark counters](usage.md#preprocessor-gated-benchmark-counters)) adds a trailing `std::uint32_t&` parameter to `View::insert` / `View::get`.\nThe memory-benchmark kernels declare a per-tile register accumulator, pass it through every `view.insert` / `view.get` call in the grid-stride loop, and flush one value per tile to a global array at end of kernel.\nNo atomics fire in the probe hot loop.\nThe host sums per-tile values into a grand total per rep.\n\nThe memory-bandwidth CSVs (`memory_bandwidth/insert.csv`, `memory_bandwidth/get.csv`) add `total_probes` and `total_failures` (insert) or `total_probes`, `total_hits`, `total_misses` (get) on top of the timing CSV schema.\nDownstream analysis derives metrics like average probe length (`total_probes / n_ops`) and bandwidth-corrected throughput (`total_probes × sizeof(Bucket) / time_ms`), the latter being directly comparable to the memcpy ceiling in the same directory.\n\nAt low F the corrected and uncorrected throughput numbers converge (average probe length ≈ 1).\nAt high F the corrected number diverges upward — that gap is itself a useful signal about how hard the Robin Hood machinery is working.\n"
}
```

</details>

## Assistant — 2026-05-27T23:38:16.403Z

A few inconsistencies and final-pass items I'd recommend, ranked by clarity of fix:

**Clear inconsistencies to fix:**

1. **Stale "study" in [docs/benchmarks.md](docs/benchmarks.md):** we changed "bandwidth study" → "bandwidth experiment" earlier, but two leftover "study" uses remain:
   - Line 5: *"Flags select which **study** to run"* → "experiment"
   - Line 32: *"The CSVs from this **study** let you compute"* → "experiment"

2. **"warpcore" vs "WarpCore" in prose in [docs/benchmarks.md](docs/benchmarks.md):** you established "WarpCore" as the canonical capitalization earlier (limitations.md), but several prose mentions are still lowercase: lines 34, 54, 64, 80, 81, and the column label "warpcore:" on line 88. The actual `warpcore::` namespace and `baselines/warpcore/` directory should stay lowercase — only prose references need updating.

3. **Stale "sweep script" in [docs/index.md:26](docs/index.md#L26) nav line:** after the benchmarks-page restructure, the nav description still reads *"workload, experiment phases, counter instrumentation, and the sweep script"*. Could be updated to mention "running the benchmarks" or similar to match the new top-of-page section.

**Worth a look, but a judgment call:**

4. **`α` redefined in [docs/benchmarks.md:42](docs/benchmarks.md#L42):** the doc uses `F` for load factor and `α` for `key_range / capacity ≈ 32`. The standard hash-table literature uses `α` for load factor. Not wrong, but readers may double-take. You might consider renaming `α` → something like `K` (key oversampling ratio) — but only if it bothers you.

5. **"probe distance" vs "probe length"** are used somewhat interchangeably across architecture.md and algorithm.md. Both terms have specific meanings (distance from home slot vs. number of probes performed) and the doc mostly gets it right, but a closer read might tighten this.

Let me know which (if any) you'd like me to apply.

Doxygen emits some, so\n      # opt out of Jekyll.\n      - name: Disable Jekyll\n        run: touch build/docs/html/.nojekyll\n\n      - uses: actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3\n        with:\n          path: build/docs/html\n\n  deploy:\n    # Only deploy on pushes to main (and manual dispatch); PRs just build.\n    if: github.event_name != 'pull_request'\n    needs: build\n    runs-on: ubuntu-latest\n    environment:\n      name: github-pages\n      url: ${{ steps.deployment.outputs.page_url }}\n    steps:\n      - id: deployment\n        uses: actions/deploy-pages@v4\n"

## Assistant — 2026-05-27T23:42:30.142Z

Workflow added at [.github/workflows/docs.yml](.github/workflows/docs.yml).

**What it does:**

- Triggers on push to `main`, PRs (build-only), and manual dispatch. Path-filtered to skip runs when only unrelated files change.
- Installs Doxygen via apt, runs `doxygen Doxyfile`, drops a `.nojekyll` into `build/docs/html/` (so Jekyll doesn't eat the `_`-prefixed assets Doxygen generates), and uploads as a Pages artifact.
- Deploys only on `main` / manual; PRs just verify the build.

**Two things to do on the GitHub side once you push:**

1. **Enable GitHub Pages with "GitHub Actions" as the source.** In repo Settings → Pages, set "Source" to "GitHub Actions". Without this, the deploy step will fail because the `github-pages` environment can't be created.
2. **Update the Docs badge in [README.md:12](README.md#L12)** once the first deploy goes through. The hosted URL will be `https://[user].github.io/GPURobinHoodHashing/`. The current badge links to `docs/index.md` (the Markdown source), which is fine as a fallback but the rendered HTML site is the better target.

Apt's Doxygen is ~1.9.x (vs. your 1.17.0 locally); the Doxyfile only uses standard options, so it should render identically, but worth a sanity-check after the first run.

## Assistant — 2026-05-27T23:49:51.323Z

The diagnostic is just VS Code spell-check flagging "Doxyfile" — not actionable. The fix:

- Added `mkdir -p build/docs` before running Doxygen — older Doxygen (1.9.x on apt) won't auto-create nested output directories the way 1.17 does, so it bails on `OUTPUT_DIRECTORY = build/docs` if `build/` doesn't exist yet.

## User — 2026-05-27T23:52:06.237Z

**← Tool result** (for `tool-1518`)

<details><summary><code>tool result</code></summary>

```
1	# GPU Robin Hood Hashing
2	
3	`gpurhh` is a header-only CUDA library implementing a parallel Robin Hood hash table for NVIDIA GPUs.
4	It targets bandwidth-bound performance on large tables by combining cache-line-aligned bucketed probing with sub-warp-cooperative tiles.
5	
6	These Markdown design pages and the C++ API reference (extracted from the source) are bundled into a unified HTML site by Doxygen.
7	
8	## Goals
9	
10	- **Header-only CUDA library.**
11	  A single `.cuh` that users `#include` and template-instantiate for their key/value types.
12	  No separate compilation of the table itself.
13	- **Massively parallel insertion and retrieval.**
14	  A batch of `N` keys is inserted or retrieved by `N` (or more) GPU threads cooperatively.
15	- **Memory-bandwidth-oriented design.**
16	  On large tables, the table is much bigger than the L2 cache, so performance is determined by how many useful bytes each DRAM transaction delivers.
17	  The aim is to come as close to peak HBM bandwidth as the access pattern allows.
18	
19	## Navigating these pages
20	
21	- [Usage](usage.md) — public API surface, the `View` pattern, and opt-in macros.
22	- [Architecture](architecture.md) — why Robin Hood, the slot layout, the bandwidth-saturation argument, and how work decomposes onto the GPU hierarchy.
23	- [Algorithm](algorithm.md) — parallel insertion with potential reduction operators, and lookup.
24	- [Implementation](implementation.md) — language and toolchain choices, the power-of-two assumption on capacity, and hash function.
25	- [Testing](testing.md) — the test suite, shared infrastructure, and what the `IdentityHash` fixture buys us.
26	- [Benchmarks](benchmarks.md) — running the benchmarks, code structure, workload, experiment phases, and per-tile counters.
27	- [Limitations](limitations.md) — known limitations and potential future work.
28	
```

</details>

```json
{
  "command": "python3 <<'PY'\nimport json, re\n\nSRC = '[claude-project-dir]/uuid-1.jsonl'\nOUT = '[repo]/session-export.md'\n\ndef strip_tags(text):\n    # Drop IDE / system reminder tags so they don't clutter the export.\n    text = re.sub(r'<ide_opened_file>.*?</ide_opened_file>', '', text, flags=re.S)\n    text = re.sub(r'<ide_selection>.*?</ide_selection>', '', text, flags=re.S)\n    text = re.sub(r'<system-reminder>.*?</system-reminder>', '', text, flags=re.S)\n    text = re.sub(r'<command-name>.*?</command-name>', '', text, flags=re.S)\n    text = re.sub(r'<command-message>.*?</command-message>', '', text, flags=re.S)\n    text = re.sub(r'<command-args>.*?</command-args>', '', text, flags=re.S)\n    text = re.sub(r'<local-command-stdout>.*?</local-command-stdout>', '', text, flags=re.S)\n    return text.strip()\n\ndef tool_summary(block):\n    name = block.get('name', '?')\n    inp  = block.get('input', {})\n    if name == 'Bash':\n        desc = inp.get('description', '')\n        cmd  = (inp.get('command', '') or '').replace('\\n', ' ')\n        if len(cmd) > 140:\n            cmd = cmd[:140] + '…'\n        return f\"_Tool: **Bash** — {desc}_  \\n`{cmd}`\"\n    if name in ('Read', 'Edit', 'Write'):\n        fp = inp.get('file_path', '')\n        return f\"_Tool: **{name}** — `{fp}`_\"\n    if name == 'Agent':\n        return f\"_Tool: **Agent** — {inp.get('description', '')}_\"\n    if name == 'AskUserQuestion':\n        qs = inp.get('questions', [])\n        first = qs[0].get('question', '') if qs else ''\n        return f\"_Tool: **AskUserQuestion** — {first}_\"\n    return f\"_Tool: **{name}**_\"\n\nturns_written = 0\nwith open(SRC) as f, open(OUT, 'w') as out:\n    out.write('# GPURobinHoodHashing — Claude Code session export\\n\\n')\n    out.write('Conversation reconstructed from the local JSONL transcript. Tool calls are summarized; internal reasoning (`thinking` blocks) and tool results are omitted.\\n\\n---\\n\\n')\n    for line in f:\n        obj = json.loads(line)\n        if obj.get('type') not in ('user', 'assistant'):\n            continue\n        msg = obj.get('message', {})\n        role = msg.get('role')\n        content = msg.get('content', [])\n        if isinstance(content, str):\n            content = [{'type': 'text', 'text': content}]\n\n        # Collect parts\n        text_parts, tool_parts = [], []\n        for b in content:\n            if not isinstance(b, dict):\n                continue\n            bt = b.get('type')\n            if bt == 'text':\n                t = strip_tags(b.get('text', ''))\n                if t:\n                    text_parts.append(t)\n            elif bt == 'tool_use':\n                tool_parts.append(tool_summary(b))\n            # skip tool_result, thinking\n\n        if not text_parts and not tool_parts:\n            continue\n\n        # For user role, only show if there's actual text (skip pure tool-result turns)\n        if role == 'user' and not text_parts:\n            continue\n\n        header = '## User' if role == 'user' else '## Assistant'\n        out.write(header + '\\n\\n')\n        if text_parts:\n            out.write('\\n\\n'.join(text_parts) + '\\n\\n')\n        for tp in tool_parts:\n            out.write(tp + '\\n\\n')\n        out.write('---\\n\\n')\n        turns_written += 1\n\nprint(f\"Wrote {turns_written} turns to {OUT}\")\nimport os\nprint(f\"Output size: {os.path.getsize(OUT) / 1024:.1f} KB\")\nPY",
  "description": "Generate Markdown export from JSONL transcript"
}
```

</details>

}

## Assistant — 2026-05-28T00:05:00.069Z

**→ Tool call: `Bash`** (id `tool-1527`)

<details><summary><code>tool input</code></summary>

```json
{
  "command": "python3 <<'PY'\nimport json, os\n\nSRC = '[claude-project-dir]/uuid-1.jsonl'\nOUT = '[repo]/session-export-raw.md'\n\ndef fence(text, lang=''):\n    # Pick a fence that doesn't collide with content\n    n = 3\n    while ('`' * (n+1)) in (text or ''):\n        n += 1\n    bar = '`' * n\n    return f\"{bar}{lang}\\n{text}\\n{bar}\"\n\ndef render_content(block):\n    bt = block.get('type', '')\n    if bt == 'text':\n        # Preserve all tags (<system-reminder>, <ide_opened_file>, etc.) verbatim\n        return block.get('text', '')\n    if bt == 'thinking':\n        body = block.get('thinking', '') or block.get('text', '')\n        return f\"<details><summary><em>thinking</em></summary>\\n\\n{fence(body)}\\n\\n</details>\"\n    if bt == 'tool_use':\n        name  = block.get('name', '?')\n        tid   = block.get('id', '')\n        inp   = block.get('input', {})\n        inp_s = json.dumps(inp, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False)\n        return f\"**→ Tool call: `{name}`** (id `{tid}`)\\n\\n{fence(inp_s, 'json')}\"\n    if bt == 'tool_result':\n        tid = block.get('tool_use_id', '')\n        is_err = block.get('is_error', False)\n        content = block.get('content', '')\n        if isinstance(content, list):\n            parts = []\n            for c in content:\n                if isinstance(c, dict):\n                    if c.get('type') == 'text':\n                        parts.append(c.get('text', ''))\n                    elif c.get('type') == 'image':\n                        parts.append('[image omitted]')\n                    else:\n                        parts.append(json.dumps(c, ensure_ascii=False))\n                else:\n                    parts.append(str(c))\n            body = '\\n'.join(parts)\n        else:\n            body = str(content)\n        label = '← Tool result (ERROR)' if is_err else '← Tool result'\n        return f\"**{label}** (for `{tid}`)\\n\\n{fence(body)}\"\n    # Unknown block type — dump as JSON\n    return f\"_unknown content block_\\n\\n{fence(json.dumps(block, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False), 'json')}\"\n\nturns = 0\nboundaries = 0\nattachments = 0\nwith open(SRC) as f, open(OUT, 'w') as out:\n    out.write('# GPURobinHoodHashing — Claude Code session export (postprocessed)\\n\\n')\n    out.write('Full reconstruction of the local JSONL transcript including thinking blocks, tool inputs, tool outputs, system reminders, IDE tags, and compact boundaries. The companion [session-export.md](session-export.md) has a cleaned-up summary.\\n\\n---\\n\\n')\n\n    for line in f:\n        obj = json.loads(line)\n        t = obj.get('type')\n\n        if t == 'system':\n            sub = obj.get('subtype', '')\n            ts  = obj.get('timestamp', '')\n            content = obj.get('content', '')\n            out.write(f\"### System event ({sub}) — {ts}\\n\\n\")\n            cm = obj.get('compactMetadata')\n            if cm:\n                out.write(f\"_{content}_  \\nTrigger: `{cm.get('trigger')}`, pre→post tokens: {cm.get('preTokens')} → {cm.get('postTokens')}\\n\\n\")\n            else:\n                out.write(f\"_{content}_\\n\\n\")\n            out.write('---\\n\\n')\n            boundaries += 1\n            continue\n\n        if t == 'attachment':\n            att = obj.get('attachment', {})\n            atype = att.get('type', '')\n            if atype == 'deferred_tools_delta':\n                added = att.get('addedNames', [])\n                removed = att.get('removedNames', [])\n                if added or removed:\n                    out.write(f\"### Deferred tools delta\\n\\n\")\n                    if added:   out.write(f\"Added: {', '.join(f'`{a}`' for a in added)}\\n\\n\")\n                    if removed: out.write(f\"Removed: {', '.join(f'`{r}`' for r in removed)}\\n\\n\")\n                    out.write('---\\n\\n')\n                    attachments += 1\n            continue\n\n        if t not in ('user', 'assistant'):\n            continue\n\n        msg = obj.get('message', {})\n        role = msg.get('role', '?')\n        content = msg.get('content', [])\n        ts = obj.get('timestamp', '')\n        if isinstance(content, str):\n            content = [{'type': 'text', 'text': content}]\n\n        # Skip empty turns\n        if not content:\n            continue\n\n        emoji = '' if role == 'user' else ''\n        out.write(f\"## {emoji} {role.capitalize()} — {ts}\\n\\n\")\n        for block in content:\n            if isinstance(block, dict):\n                out.write(render_content(block) + '\\n\\n')\n        out.write('---\\n\\n')\n        turns += 1\n\nprint(f\"Turns: {turns}, system boundaries: {boundaries}, attachment deltas: {attachments}\")\nprint(f\"Output: {OUT}\")\nprint(f\"Size: {os.path.getsize(OUT) / (1024*1024):.2f} MB\")\nPY",
  "description": "Generate raw Markdown export with everything visible"
}
```
